Andon Labs found opening a food business is dramatically faster in Sweden than San Francisco — roughly 2 weeks of permitting in Stockholm versus ~4 months in SF — a counterintuitive regulatory-friction gap that even Claude advised offered 'really no way' to shortcut in SF.
data_point🗣 Lukas Peterssonconviction: highSwedenSan FranciscoStockholm
So what: Regulatory permitting friction, not technology, is the binding constraint on physical agent businesses in the US — a real-world bottleneck for any embodied-commerce rollout in regulated sectors.
“in in SF it's like 4 months and in Stockholm it's 2 weeks” ▶ 1:11:56
Andon Labs' explicit mission is ensuring safe deployment of real-world AI, premised on educating policymakers and the public that models are far more than chatbots — the founders argue you cannot make intelligent policy (including an AI pause) without recognizing models' autonomous capabilities.
thesis🗣 Lukas Peterssonconviction: highAndon Labs
So what: Frames the policy narrative driving demand for real-world agent evals; if regulators internalize 'agents, not chatbots,' it raises the odds of binding AI safety/deployment rules that reshape the sector.
“you can't make intelligent decisions in in society without knowing that they are way more than chatbots” ▶ 41:37
Andon Labs notes that whether US-centric, English-trained models can run businesses doesn't necessarily transfer across jurisdictions — models know US bureaucracy well but may lack the specific permit and cultural knowledge for markets like Sweden, an open question for global agent-commerce.
causal🗣 Lukas Peterssonconviction: mediumUnited StatesSweden
So what: Agent-business capability may be geographically uneven due to training-data bias toward US/English — a localization gap and risk for non-US deployment of autonomous agents.
≈ “they are very much trained on like English data and and like uscentric” paraphrase
Satya Nadella on AI: @NoPriorsPodcast x Latent Space Crossover Special at Microsoft Build 202641 min28 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
At Build 2026, Satya Nadella reframes Microsoft's AI strategy as an ecosystem/platform play built around the MAI model family, an open 'harness' (GitHub harness in Foundry), and private evals as the durable corporate IP. He argues per-user pricing gives way to consumption metering, SaaS gets unbundled and re-bundled around agents, and that Azure built more capacity in the last 15 months than its first 15 years — with 'tokens not headcount' becoming the new operational input.
MAI models & Microsoft's in-house frontier lab · 4The harness as the new platform layer · 3Private evals as corporate IP & balance-sheet token capital · 3SaaS unbundling & the end-of-software debate · 4AI pricing model evolution · 2Azure capex, data-center buildout & 'tokens not headcount' · 3Agentic coding, dev workflows & the future of engineering roles · 7AI in healthcare & education · 2
MAI models & Microsoft's in-house frontier lab 4
Microsoft has built its own MAI model family with an emphasis on a 'clean lineage' (high-quality pre-training data, careful ablation) rather than chasing benchmark scores, positioning MAI as a Microsoft-controlled alternative to relying solely on OpenAI.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftMAIOpenAIMustafa Suleyman
So what: Microsoft is reducing single-vendor model dependence; watch MAI's traction as a hedge that could shift OpenAI's leverage in the partnership and Azure's model mix.
“one of the challenges of a lot of the open weight models is they look great on one benchmark or two, but they're not great on practice” ▶ 3:10
Nadella says a 5B-parameter MAI reasoning model can 'hill climb' to beat a frontier model on a specific task after collecting traces from that frontier model, citing a Land O'Lakes demo where GPT-55 traces were distilled into a small 5B reasoning model that achieved higher performance.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMAIGPT-55Land O'LakesOpenAI
So what: Distilling small task-specialist models from frontier traces undercuts the economics of always serving large frontier models — bearish for per-token frontier revenue, bullish for cheap specialized inference.
“you took a 5B reasoning model and achieved higher” ▶ 4:49
Nadella reframes 'operating at the frontier' as time-relative: a company can use a frontier model today, collect traces, then run a cheaper specialized model that exceeds it on its own task — so the 'frontier' is per-company and per-task, not a single global model.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMAIfrontier models
So what: If 'frontier' fragments into private per-company frontiers, the moat of any one general frontier model weakens — reframes how to value model-lab differentiation.
“intelligence is log of compute kind of works” ▶ 5:46
Nadella credits the OpenAI partnership and the scaling-laws paper as the trigger for Microsoft's AI bet, and says the scaling laws 'have held' — reaffirming continued conviction in compute-driven capability gains.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highOpenAIMicrosoftscaling lawsJensen HuangNvidia
So what: Continued belief in scaling laws underwrites Microsoft's capex commitment; a public CEO reaffirmation supports the bull case for sustained compute spend.
“intelligence is log of compute kind of works” ▶ 5:46
The harness as the new platform layer 3
Microsoft is standardizing on a single 'GitHub harness' (model + data + tools loop) used across all its products — GitHub Copilot, Security Copilot, M-Dash, discovery-for-science — and is exposing it openly in Azure Foundry so customers can bring their own models, tools, and context.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftGitHubGitHub CopilotAzure FoundrySecurity CopilotM-Dash
So what: Microsoft is trying to own the agent 'harness' layer the way it owned the OS/cloud — the strategic lock-in point shifts from the model to the harness + context.
“we have the get up harness which essentially we're using across all our products. It's available in foundry” ▶ 10:06
Nadella cites M-Dash (a security product on the Microsoft harness) finding bugs/vulnerabilities at launch that an incumbent ('mythos') did not, offered as 'existence proof' that a multimodal harness can outperform model-only approaches in the real world.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highM-DashMicrosoftcybersecurity
So what: Validates harness-led security agents as a competitive wedge; watch Microsoft security ARR and incumbent security vendors facing agentic displacement.
“when it launched uh it found bugs or vulnerabilities that were not found by mythos” ▶ 10:40
Nadella says the hardest lesson of the last two years is that prepping the 'context layer' — not the model — is where the value is, requiring heavy engineering to let an agent's plan execute efficiently.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftcontext engineeringAI agents
So what: Reinforces that durable AI value accrues to whoever owns proprietary context/data plumbing, not the raw model — favors data-rich incumbents over pure model labs.
“the amount of work you need to do to prep the context layer uh such that your plan can execute in the most efficient way is where the magic is” ▶ 9:52
Private evals as corporate IP & balance-sheet token capital 3
Nadella argues a company's private evals — not its data volume — will be its biggest AI-era IP, and proposes an 'acid test': if you can swap model A for model B on your private eval and still climb, you're in control; if not, you're captive to a vendor.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftprivate evalsAI agents
So what: A concrete due-diligence test for AI vendor lock-in and for assessing a company's AI defensibility; private evals become a moat to underwrite.
“another acid test is you have an eval that's private. You're using a a model A. Can you switch it to model B and you know, climb up? If you can, then you're in control. If you can't, you're not in control.” ▶ 12:42
Nadella predicts that company-specific agents trained on the traces between humans and agents will capture tacit knowledge that was never balance-sheet-able as human capital, and should literally 'go on the balance sheet' as a new asset class.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumAI agentsMicrosoft TeamsaccountingSEC
So what: If 'token capital' / company-veteran agents become recognized intangible assets, it reshapes how AI-native firms are valued and audited.
“when a company goes says it should in fact go on to the balance sheet is how I think about it” ▶ 16:20
The network effects in AI this time come from learning from few samples (what is novel) rather than from large data volumes, so the new competitive game is protecting your private evals and traces from leaking.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAInetwork effects
So what: Shifts the data-moat thesis: scale of data matters less than proprietary, non-leaking eval/trace loops — recalibrates which 'data moats' are real.
“the network effects this time around around intelligence are such because they learn from data and not really lots of data” ▶ 12:04
SaaS unbundling & the end-of-software debate 4
Nadella argues the underlying SaaS data models (e.g., a general ledger) and business logic (e.g., Power BI semantic models) remain valuable and shouldn't be reinvented, but the SaaS packaging must be unbundled and re-bundled into agentic workflows under new business models.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highSaaSPower BIMicrosoft
So what: Tempers the 'death of SaaS' narrative — incumbents with deep data models survive; the disruption is at the packaging/pricing layer, not the data substrate.
“We now have to learn how to unbundle these things and re-bundle in new ways and discover new business models” ▶ 19:04
Nadella offers a clean make-vs-buy rule: acquire software whenever the marginal cost of building AND maintaining it yourself exceeds buying it — and stresses that maintenance (security fixes, token burn) is the under-counted cost that will pull 'agent euphoria' DIY teams back to vendors.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highSaaSenterprise softwareAI agents
So what: Predicts a reversal: enterprises rebuilding everything in-house will return to SaaS vendors within a budget cycle — bullish for flexible SaaS incumbents, bearish for the most aggressive 'rebuild it all' thesis.
“you should always acquire something if the marginal cost of building and maintaining uh something on your own is higher” ▶ 24:00
Nadella draws a historical parallel: when Exchange/SharePoint moved to the cloud, server usage went 10x-100x (subscriptions vs. buying servers), and predicts the same dynamic now with M365 'Work IQ' exposing the captive company database for agent use — with agent usage potentially exceeding human end-user usage.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft 365Work IQExchangeSharePointMicrosoft Teams
So what: Implies a large new M365 consumption-revenue vector as agents become the dominant 'users' — watch M365 monetization re-architecture and agent-usage metering.
“the number of people who will use servers in the cloud is 10x 100x” ▶ 19:35
Microsoft is re-architecting M365 infrastructure because what was built to serve a human mailbox cannot serve an agent, implying meaningful new capex/engineering to support agent-scale usage of Work IQ.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft 365Work IQ
So what: Signals incremental infrastructure investment inside the M365 stack; agent-serving architecture is a cost and a monetization opportunity.
“what I used to serve an inbox or a mailbox cannot be used to serve an agent” ▶ 20:48
AI pricing model evolution 2
Nadella predicts per-user pricing (an artifact of budget certainty) persists but must be paired with a consumption meter, and that outcome-based pricing breaks down in practice because customers reject revenue-sharing once an outcome materializes ('most people love outcomes until they have an outcome').
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highSaaS pricingMicrosoftGitHub
So what: Sets expectations against pure outcome-based pricing hype; the durable model is per-user + consumption — relevant for forecasting AI software revenue mechanics.
“most people love outcomes until they have an outcome. Because once you have an outcome it's like giving away royalty” ▶ 22:02
Microsoft recently added per-user pricing on GitHub because GitHub Copilot was originally priced per-user before the intensity of agent usage (launching thousands of agents running all day) was understood — a concrete example of pricing catching up to agentic consumption.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highGitHubGitHub CopilotMicrosoft
So what: Shows agent intensity forcing pricing redesign at Microsoft's own products; a leading indicator for how Copilot-family ARPU and metering evolve.
“GitHub co-pilot was constructed at a per user level before we understood even the intensity of usage of agents” ▶ 22:45
Azure capex, data-center buildout & 'tokens not headcount' 3
Microsoft built more Azure capacity in the last 15 months than in its first 15 years, using the same team — Nadella calls the buildout 'crazy' and unprecedented.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft Azuredata centerscapex
So what: Quantifies the extraordinary Azure capacity ramp; a direct read on sustained hyperscaler capex intensity and infrastructure demand.
“we built in the last 15 months more Azure capacity than we built in the first 15 years” ▶ 31:46
The Azure networking team (managing 500+ fiber operators / the WAN) rebuilt their function as an agentic system named 'Miles' and now ask for 'tokens, not headcount' to scale operations — a concrete example of compute substituting for labor in physical infrastructure ops.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft AzureAI agentsMiles
So what: Illustrates AI converting opex/headcount into token spend — supports the thesis that inference demand grows from internal operational automation, not just external products.
“we don't need head count. We need tokens in order to be able to manage our operation” ▶ 32:45
Nadella ties AI's 'social license' to delivering tangible local benefits — claiming data centers will lower long-term energy prices via a better grid/more energy, replenish water via closed-loop systems, and create construction jobs and tax base — and warns the industry loses 'permission' if these benefits aren't real.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highdata centersenergywaterMicrosoft
So what: Frames community/regulatory permission as a gating risk to the buildout; watch energy/water narratives and local opposition as a real constraint on hyperscaler expansion.
“if it is not, we won't have permission. It's as simple as that” ▶ 35:31
Agentic coding, dev workflows & the future of engineering roles 7
Nadella says agentic coding now works so well that Microsoft has to rebuild the IDE itself — managing 100 concurrent agent sessions transfers excessive cognitive load back to the human, requiring a new UI with a 'canvas' rather than chat-only.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highGitHub CopilotIDEAI agentsMicrosoft
So what: The IDE/dev-tooling layer is being re-platformed around agent orchestration — a competitive opening Microsoft intends to own via GitHub.
“coding is worked so well that we now have to rebuild the IDE” ▶ 7:06
Nadella predicts that within ~6 months long-running, durable autopilot agents acting with a user's delegated identity/authority will do overnight 'glue work' at scale, requiring a new IDE-like review interface to inspect what the agents did.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumAI agentsMicrosoftautopilots
So what: Points to delegated-identity, long-running agents as the next product wave — relevant for identity/security tooling and agent-management UIs.
“all through night the night there was a bunch of stuff that all these autopilots that I have working on my behalf with my delegated authority” ▶ 8:22
Nadella describes personally building long-running Foundry 'autopilot' agents — e.g., a chief-of-staff agent using Work IQ with memory stored in 'Raven' as a backend-as-a-service — that he could publish straight to Teams, citing it as trivially easy end-to-end.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAzure FoundryWork IQRavenMicrosoft TeamsScout
So what: Names the Microsoft agent stack (Foundry + Work IQ + Raven + Teams + Scout) — useful map of which Microsoft components capture agent-building value.
“store all the memory in using Raven, right? Basically at my back end as a service” ▶ 27:09
Nadella illustrates Work IQ's reach by noting an agent can read all design-meeting transcripts related to a GitHub repo and return a concrete code-change plan — a use case M365 was never previously capable of.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highWork IQMicrosoft 365GitHub
So what: Demonstrates M365's proprietary communications graph as a unique agent context source — a differentiated data moat competitors lack.
“I attended a bunch of design meetings last week related to this repo. Can you capture all that and tell me what changes I should make?” ▶ 20:11
Nadella endorses the view that engineering roles will collapse toward a few (agent managers, forward-deployed engineers, security engineers, large-scale infra) and cites LinkedIn structurally creating a 'full stack builder' discipline by merging design, PM, and front-end engineering.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumLinkedInMicrosoftsoftware engineering
So what: Signals headcount/role restructuring inside large tech orgs — relevant to labor-cost trajectories and to which engineering skills retain value.
“they did structurally change and it you know basically built up a new discipline called full stack builder” ▶ 28:08
Nadella argues infrastructure/distributed-systems talent is now critical even inside end-user app teams (e.g., the Excel team building an RL environment where a reward can be learned is one of the hardest infra problems), creating new demand for systems engineers in unexpected places.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft Excelreinforcement learningdistributed systems
So what: Building per-product RL environments is becoming core engineering work — a hidden cost/talent driver behind shipping specialized agents.
“building the RLE in which a reward can be learned is actually one of the hardest sort of infrastructure problems” ▶ 28:51
Nadella predicts the generalist role gains the most leverage in the AI era because building an app collapses into the same sentence as writing a Word doc or spreadsheet — a 'golden age for idea people with agency.'
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAIsoftwareknowledge work
So what: Implies value shifts toward generalists/founders with agency over specialists — a labor-market and productivity thesis investors can track.
“my generalist skills have gotten a higher leverage” ▶ 29:51
AI in healthcare & education 2
Nadella points to healthcare as an area where AI ROI is already happening, naming Open Evidence as an example, and frames healthcare and broad startup/large-company wealth creation as the clearest near-term AI benefits.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumOpen EvidencehealthcareAI
So what: Flags healthcare AI (and Open Evidence specifically) as a CEO-endorsed area of realized impact — a sector to watch for AI monetization.
Nadella predicts the next big startup success could be a new university or pedagogy that re-architects credentials and ties curricula to economic opportunity, arguing education has lagged despite being an obvious AI good (he cites learning from Alpha School's founders).
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumeducationAlpha SchoolAI
So what: Identifies AI-native education/credentialing as an under-penetrated opportunity — a thematic watchlist item for venture exposure.
“the next big startup and success story could be someone who builds a new university” ▶ 40:51
⚡️Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste — @AhmadAwais , CommandCode.ai41 min23 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ahmad Awais (CommandCode.ai) argues that the apparent quality gap between open models (DeepSeek V4, Kimi, MiniMax) and frontier closed models (Opus, GPT) is largely a fixable tool-calling/harness problem, not a capability gap. By deterministically repairing malformed tool calls in his coding agent, he claims cheap open models reach competitive performance — a thesis with direct implications for inference-cost economics, the value of model labs vs. harness layers, and DeepSeek's competitive positioning.
Open-model quality gap is a harness/tool-calling artifact · 6Competitive dynamics: harnesses, labs, and DeepSeek pricing · 6CommandCode scale, funding, and inference volume · 4'Taste' as a learned-context moat · 4Generalizing the repair pattern: design and security · 3
Open-model quality gap is a harness/tool-calling artifact 6
CommandCode.ai's Ahmad Awais claims the perceived weakness of open models like DeepSeek V4 in coding agents is mostly a 'tool confusion' harness problem that is deterministically fixable, not a fundamental model capability gap.
thesis🗣 Ahmad Awais (guest, CommandCode.ai founder)conviction: highDeepSeekDeepSeek V4OpusCommandCode.aiopen modelscoding agents
So what: If true, the moat of frontier closed labs in agentic coding is thinner than priced — value migrates from model weights to the harness/tooling layer and to whoever can run cheap open models well.
≈ “it can be as silly as something like this... it always ends up being a tool call hardness issue than, you know, an actual model issue” paraphrase ▶ 13:06
DeepSeek V4 Pro repeats an identical malformed tool call ~56 times on average per billion tokens when sent a Zod schema error, because (per Awais) open models trained on outputs of stronger models learn that 'whatever I am telling you is correct, so don't try to correct me.'
causal🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumDeepSeek V4 ProZodtool callingdistillation
So what: Suggests distillation-from-stronger-models leaves a systematic failure mode in agentic loops — a quantifiable inference-cost tax that third-party tooling can arbitrage away.
“DeepSeek V4 Pro has this weird alpha male energy where whatever it sends you, it thinks that that is the right thing to do” ▶ 7:42
Instead of returning errors, CommandCode deterministically repairs malformed tool-call arguments and returns the result plus a 'repair hint', after which the model's very next (third) tool call is typically corrected and behavior 'becomes super smart'.
framework🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aitool callingopen models
So what: A cheap, deterministic middleware layer can materially upgrade open-model agentic performance — the kind of capability that becomes table stakes and commoditizes a labs-only advantage.
“Instead of telling them what to do correctly, you'll first try to save them and then you will explain like why you saved them” ▶ 10:52
The same tool-confusion failure pattern was confirmed across DeepSeek, Kimi, and MiniMax, with CommandCode now maintaining ~16,000 distinct repair variations across hundreds of billions of tokens.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highDeepSeekKimiMiniMaxopen models
So what: The defect is systemic across the Chinese open-model cohort, implying a broad, repeatable arbitrage in serving open models — and a data moat for whoever accumulates the repair corpus.
“Kimi is doing exactly the same thing. Then we fixed the Kimi models and we fixed MiniMax models and now we have like 16,000 different repair... variations” ▶ 13:54
With repair logic applied, DeepSeek V4 Flash — described as 'practically completely not useful' before — becomes competitive, with one user running 70 billion tokens and 12+ hour sessions on DeepSeek via CommandCode.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highDeepSeek V4 FlashCommandCode.ai
So what: Cheap/small open models, once harness-fixed, can sustain long agentic sessions — directly attacking the per-token economics that justify premium closed-model pricing.
“this goes from a model that was practically completely not useful like DeepSeek V4 flash to something that can actually compete” ▶ 14:18
Awais claims coding agents perform worse when run with permission prompts on and better with permissions fully bypassed, attributing the degradation to the model being 'steered in the wrong direction' by the slowness/interruptions of permission blocks.
causal🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumcoding agentsagent UX
So what: Non-obvious UX/agent-design insight: human-in-the-loop friction may itself degrade model output quality, shaping how autonomous coding products are built.
“if you run any coding agent with permissions on the models are actually dumber” ▶ 14:52
Competitive dynamics: harnesses, labs, and DeepSeek pricing 6
Awais alleges Anthropic's Claude Code hides tool-call errors behind a 'control-O' UI, so users running open models through it see slowness without realizing there are 50+ tool-call failures per session — a friction he says is in Anthropic's 'vested interest' since they didn't build the harness for open models.
contrarian🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumAnthropicClaude CodeDeepSeekopen models
So what: Implies frontier labs' first-party harnesses structurally disadvantage rival open models — creating an opening for neutral, open-model-optimized harnesses.
“they hide a lot of the errors behind control O... why they don't care about open models. They didn't build their coding agent for open models” ▶ 12:23
Awais asserts Claude is markedly more lenient/robust with malformed tool calls than open models — it can recover from harness errors on its own, which open models cannot — and says this robustness is where CommandCode found product-market fit serving open models.
thesis🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highClaudeAnthropicopen modelsCommandCode.ai
So what: Tool-call robustness is a real, under-discussed frontier-model differentiator — and the gap is exactly what determines whether cheap open models are usable in production agents.
“Claude is actually really really lenient with two calls... Not the case with... open models” ▶ 27:15
Awais speculates that CommandCode's public demonstration of cheap, high-quality DeepSeek inference may have influenced DeepSeek to cut prices to showcase model quality.
causal🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: lowDeepSeekCommandCode.aiinference pricing
So what: Hints at downward price pressure in open-model inference being partly demand-side/perception-driven — relevant to inference-margin compression themes.
“I would it wouldn't be too far to say that Deep Seek saw that they can discount their prices” ▶ 17:03
DeepSeek has announced it is hiring to build its own coding agent ('DeepSeek Code'), but Awais expects it will be DeepSeek-only, leaving room for model-agnostic harnesses.
prediction🗣 Ahmad Awais / hostconviction: mediumDeepSeekDeepSeek Codecoding agents
So what: Labs moving into the harness layer (vertical integration) is a recurring pattern; single-model lock-in leaves a durable niche for neutral multi-model tools.
“they announced that we are hiring for that or whatnot... But, they will only be for Deep Seek” ▶ 39:38
CommandCode launched a $1/month 'Go' plan offering ~600 million DeepSeek tokens to prove open models are catching up, and claims to possibly be the most-used coding agent for DeepSeek inference.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumCommandCode.aiDeepSeekinference pricing
So what: Aggressive token economics signal how cheap open-model serving has become; a credible threat to per-seat/per-token pricing of incumbent coding tools.
“we launched a Go Plan with just $1 per month to you can do like 600 million tokens of Deep Seek free for pro in it” ▶ 16:43
Awais reports a model called 'Qwen 3.7 Max' (newly released, '2 or 3 days old') is already the second-most-used model on CommandCode, indicating very fast user migration toward new open/frontier models.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumQwenQwen 3.7 MaxCommandCode.ai
So what: Model loyalty among power users is low and switching is near-instant — pricing power accrues to whoever ships the best frontier model this week, not to brand.
“to 3.7 max is the second most used model on Command Code right now. It's just two or three days old” ▶ 39:44
CommandCode scale, funding, and inference volume 4
CommandCode's lineage (formerly LangBase, an AI cloud) reached 1.2 billion agent runs per month, and the coding agent now processes roughly 600 billion tokens — up ~100x from a few billion tokens/day weeks earlier.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiLangBase
So what: Concrete evidence of explosive agentic-coding inference growth — a demand tailwind for inference compute and open-model serving infrastructure.
“we are doing anywhere from 600 billion tokens right now” ▶ 14:08
CommandCode raised a $5 million seed round; investors include Tom Preston-Werner's fund (Preston-Werner Ventures) and WordPress creator Matt Mullenweg as an angel.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiTom Preston-WernerMatt MullenwegPreston-Werner Ventures
So what: Signals notable angel/operator interest in open-model-optimized coding tooling as an investable category distinct from the model labs.
“when we launched we announced our, you know, $5 million seed” ▶ 29:30
CommandCode plans to fully open-source its 6-year-old coding agent codebase, targeting an announcement at the AI Engineering conference in SF, positioning the product as 'completely hackable.'
prediction🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiopen sourceAI Engineer Conference
So what: Open-sourcing the harness commoditizes the layer further and pressures closed coding-agent products; the business must monetize elsewhere (taste, hosting, models).
“we are going to open source command code very very soon” ▶ 37:04
Awais frames coding-agent strategy via three OS analogies — OpenCode as 'Windows' (every model works), 'Pi/Linux' (build your own drivers), and CommandCode as 'Apple' (curated best open+closed models, not every model, but fully hackable).
framework🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiOpenCodecoding agents
So what: A curation-over-breadth positioning thesis; useful lens for evaluating which coding-agent businesses can sustain differentiation vs. become commodity routers.
“build it like Apple. It will have the best of the best models, both open and closed. It will not have every model, but it will be hackable” ▶ 38:51
'Taste' as a learned-context moat 4
CommandCode's 'Taste' is a meta/neuro-symbolic layer that automatically learns a developer's repeated preferences (e.g., pnpm vs npm, Vitest, TS-up) per repository and stores them as transparent, version-controlled markdown 'taste files' reviewed in every PR.
framework🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiTasteskillsagent memory
So what: Accumulated, repo-resident preference data is a potential data moat and switching cost — the durable asset when the underlying models are interchangeable.
“command code automatically learns from you on a per repository basis... it builds a library of skills” ▶ 3:58
Awais reports a study with 70+ developers showing the number of times they had to manually edit or 're-steer' the LLM dropped when using Taste-learned skill files.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumCommandCode.aiTastedeveloper productivity
So what: Quantified productivity claim supporting the thesis that learned-context layers, not raw model IQ, drive real-world coding-agent value.
“the number of times that they had to go edit files... They found that their number of edits or steers went down” ▶ 31:24
An emerging power-user pattern in CommandCode's community: build/seed a project with an expensive high-quality model (Opus, GPT 5.5) to generate a taste file, then use very cheap models to continue building against that taste file.
framework🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aiOpusGPT 5.5DeepSeekTaste
So what: A 'distill expensive taste, execute cheap' workflow that structurally reduces demand for premium-model tokens — bearish for closed-model token volume per task.
“they're building one project with a really high quality LLM like Opus or GPT 5.5... and then they're using... super cheap models to continuously build on that” ▶ 36:07
CommandCode says programmers have largely stopped writing code themselves, so it now learns 'taste' from the models' outputs rather than from humans — a stated shift in where coding signal originates.
thesis🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumCommandCode.aicoding agentsAI coding
So what: Reflects how fast human-authored code is being displaced in agentic workflows — relevant to developer-tooling TAM and where future training/feedback data comes from.
“programmers stopped writing code. So, who are we going to learn taste from... we started learning taste from models because models are writing code” ▶ 6:25
Generalizing the repair pattern: design and security 3
Awais claims ~90% of LLM 'design slop' (e.g., indigo-purple gradients) is fixable not by better models but by a 'contract gap' fix: ~24 reference docs, 10 design 'smells', and 7 surface patterns derived from interviewing designers.
thesis🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: highCommandCode.aidesignLLM design slop
So what: Same 'deterministic repair beats bigger model' thesis extended to design — reinforces that productized taste/contracts, not raw model scale, capture value.
“it's like a feels like you can fix 90% of design slop, which is not a capability gap. It's more like a contract gap” ▶ 22:24
LLMs control color far better when forced to use the OKLCH color space than HSL, so CommandCode's design skill forces OKLCH — a concrete, transferable prompt-engineering lever.
data_point🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumOKLCHHSLLLM design
So what: Granular, reproducible technique that any harness can adopt — illustrates how cheap deterministic constraints close perceived quality gaps.
“if you force an LLM to use OKLCH, they can control the colors palette really really well compared to any of other things” ▶ 21:09
CommandCode is extending the deterministic-repair approach to security, with a team member working with a security community to auto-detect the most common insecure-code patterns and apply automatic fixes.
anecdote🗣 Ahmad Awaisconviction: mediumCommandCode.aiAI securitycoding agents
So what: Signals the repair-pattern playbook generalizing into a platform (code, design, security) — a broader product/TAM thesis if it lands.
“the same problem can be applied to security as well... they can apply automatic fixes to your packages” ▶ 25:57

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 5 episode(s) · 176 insights

Is The Rally Over? Has The Pullback Now Started? | Lance Roberts102 min37 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
After 9-10 straight up weeks, Lance Roberts says a correction has technically begun (sell signal triggered, 20-day MA broken) but argues it will likely be a shallow 5-7% rotational pullback — capital sloshing from overbought semis/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality/dividend names rather than leaving the market — unless an oil-supply spike, liquidity tightening, or impaired hyperscaler capex hits forward earnings. Macro backdrop is resilient (capex + deficits + bonus-depreciation juicing growth, earnings revisions surging like a post-recession recovery), but late-cycle warning signs are mounting: record equity issuance/IPO supply, a 15-year/500%+ bull, and AI now cited as the #1 layoff reason.
Correction has technically started — shallow & rotational · 8Resilient macro & surging earnings revisions · 6Cracks in the consumer & sustainability of growth · 4Late-cycle signals: IPO/equity supply & long bull · 4Oil supply, liquidity & rug-pull risks · 5Bitcoin, credit spreads & private credit · 3Labor market split & AI displacement · 3Portfolio trades & risk management · 4
Correction has technically started — shallow & rotational 8
Lance Roberts says the S&P 500 correction has technically begun: a momentum sell signal triggered, the market is working off a severe overbought condition, and price is taking out the 20-day moving average.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Confirms near-term downside bias; traders should expect further weakness and manage risk rather than chase.
“By all intents and measures, technically speaking, yes, the correction has started.” ▶ 0:02
Roberts expects the pullback to be a rotational 'water sloshing in the bathtub' move — capital rotating out of overbought semiconductors/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality, producing only a 3-7% index decline rather than a deep 10-15% buy-the-dip correction.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500semiconductorsvalue stocksquality stocks
So what: Implies no big 'buy the dip' bargain in the index; the pain concentrates in tech while value cushions the downside.
“the market may not actually correct in the way that a lot of people are expecting” ▶ 3:37
Because technology and communications (Meta, Google, Nvidia and the semis) together make up ~50% of the S&P 500, and semiconductors alone are ~18%, any correction in those sectors mechanically drags the whole index lower even if money rotates into other sectors.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500Technology sectorCommunications sectorNvidiaMetaGooglesemiconductors
So what: Index-level declines are unavoidable given concentration; cap-weight exposure carries hidden single-sector risk.
≈ “those two sectors so communications is meta and Google and then you know your technology sectors Nvidia all the semiconductors everything else they make up 50% of the S&P 500 index” paraphrase
The rotation is already visible: on the day of taping Micron, AMD and Intel were each down ~7% while value and quality stocks rose, and the 3x semiconductor ETF is up roughly 1,000% from where this rally started — flagging how stretched the semi trade had become.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highMicronAMDIntelsemiconductors3x semiconductor ETF
So what: The unwind of the most-crowded leveraged semi trade can be violent; size and stops matter most there.
“Micron down 7%, AMD down, uh, you know, 7%, Intel's down 7% this morning” ▶ 4:35
Roberts expects roughly 7,300 on the S&P as minor support, with the 50-day MA (~7,150, a 38.2% retracement) and the 100-day MA (a 50% retracement) as the most realistic downside objectives — a 50% retracement of the rally implying about a 5-7% correction.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Gives concrete levels to watch for re-entry / risk sizing on the pullback.
“the 50-day moving average, which is currently around 7150ish, is going to be your most logical next support” ▶ 1:19:40
Roberts warns that after such a long complacent rally, even a normal 3-7% correction will feel like a 10-15% bear-market drop psychologically, and investors should avoid overreacting and selling out entirely.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Behavioral edge: pre-committing to not panic-sell a shallow dip protects against capitulating at the lows.
“It's going to feel like a 10 15% correction psychologically. So, don't overreact to the correction.” ▶ 1:20:31
Roberts frames the immediate-term setup as ~2% upside versus ~7% downside (a 3:1 to 4:1 risk/reward), which is why his firm trimmed high-beta tech mid-day and is adding to value — downside risk now far exceeds upside.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Asymmetric payoff argues for de-risking high-beta exposure now rather than reaching for the last few percent.
“the upside was maybe, you know, 2% higher versus a 7% downside” ▶ 1:21:45
The two-month rally off the April low is one of the largest two-month advances on record, which Roberts cites as an additional reason the move is exhausted and due to mean-revert.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Historically extreme momentum tends to be followed by give-back; supports taking profits.
“The size of that two-month rally is one of the largest on record.” ▶ 1:18:28
Resilient macro & surging earnings revisions 6
S&P 500 forward EPS growth estimates are being revised up toward ~30% (from below 20% a couple months ago) — a pattern of sharp upward earnings revisions that historically only occurs coming OUT of a recession (2008, dot-com, 2015-16, post-pandemic), yet is happening with no official recession.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highS&P 500earnings
So what: Either earnings momentum is real and durable, or estimates are over-extended and vulnerable to a sharp reset.
“This is something you only see coming out of recessions.” ▶ 10:19
Roberts and Taggart argue the U.S. effectively went through a 'rolling recession' that was papered over with trillions of stimulus — Roberts' ~100-input economic composite hit recession-level readings around 2023 — which makes today's V-shaped earnings recovery a normal cyclical rebound rather than an anomaly.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highU.S. economyearnings
So what: Reframes the current upswing as mid-cycle recovery, supporting near-term resilience but implying an eventual reversal.
“maybe technically we never had a recession but economically we went through an a recession” ▶ 19:09
Taggart identifies three simultaneous stimulants 'juicing' the economy: hyperscaler capex (~$800-900B in 2026, >$1T in 2027), aggressive government deficit spending, and the One Big Beautiful Bill's 100% year-one bonus depreciation incentivizing corporate plant/equipment investment.
causal🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highhyperscalersdata centersU.S. governmentfiscal policybonus depreciation
So what: These are the load-bearing pillars of growth; watch capex guidance and fiscal flows as the key macro tells.
“there there is like multiple shots of adrenaline going into this economy” ▶ 14:11
An economically-weighted ISM index (70% services / 30% manufacturing) has turned up from borderline-contraction, with ISM manufacturing back above 50 after nearly three years in contraction — confirming broad economic re-acceleration that supports the earnings recovery.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highISM manufacturingISM services
So what: Hard confirmation the recovery is broad, not just AI capex; bears betting on imminent recession face a strong tape.
“the ISM manufacturing index has been in contraction for almost three straight years. It's back into expansion territory” ▶ 19:57
Multiple independent labor sources (Revelio Labs, Bank of America payrolls estimate, ADP) all show a sharp resurgence in hiring, corroborating the BLS non-farm payrolls uptick — Taggart's point that you can dislike the government data but several private datasets tell the same story.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumBLSRevelio LabsBank of AmericaADPjobs
So what: Don't trade on data-denial; corroborated labor strength reduces near-term recession odds.
“just because you hate one indicator because it's the government... there's a lot of other indicators out there that are also telling you exactly the same thing” ▶ 22:07
Roberts' core market philosophy: the market only prices the published data, not how you feel about its accuracy — trading on shadow-stats inflation or 'fake' CPI beliefs while the market uses official CPI impairs wealth by keeping you out of rising markets.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highCPIShadowStatsBLS
So what: Discipline edge: position to the data the market actually responds to, not to a 'truer' private metric it ignores.
“Nobody cares what you think when it comes to the financial markets... The market only cares about the data that's published.” ▶ 15:06
Cracks in the consumer & sustainability of growth 4
Q1 GDP (~3%, likely revised to 2-2.5%) was driven by the ~15% business-investment component (data-center capex) while personal consumption (PCE, ~70% of GDP) actually contracted — the consumer is slowing with rising delinquencies and fading debt-fueled spending.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highGDPPCEconsumerdata centers
So what: The growth is narrowly capex-led; consumer weakness is the soft underbelly that could undercut the durability of the boom.
“personal consumption expenditures PCE which is 70% of GDP contracted. So the consumer is slowing down.” ▶ 23:51
Unit labor costs (largely wages) are declining sharply, which Roberts says will feed through to weaker consumption and drag CPI lower later in 2026 and into 2027 once the oil spike passes.
causal🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumunit labor costswagesCPIconsumer
So what: Disinflation thesis: falling wage costs point to lower future CPI prints and softer demand — bond-supportive, growth-cautionary.
≈ “unit labor costs are declining rather sharply. That's going to drag inflation lower later this year.” paraphrase
The entire bullish narrative is hostage to hyperscaler capex: if credit markets pull back and 2027 capex falls from ~$1.2T to, say, $700B, forward earnings expectations break and the whole market gets hit regardless of the rotation dynamic.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highhyperscalersdata centerscredit marketsearnings
So what: Capex financing conditions are the single biggest tail risk; monitor AI-infrastructure credit and capex guidance closely.
“if those if those get materially impaired, that's going to be a really big deal because so much of those earnings expectations are based on that money getting spent” ▶ 25:37
Roberts notes strong economic growth and strong market returns are NOT the same: you can have a decent-growth year with an underperforming market depending on valuations — a caution that bullish macro green shoots don't guarantee continued index gains.
framework🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumS&P 500GDPvaluations
So what: Decouples macro optimism from equity allocation; valuation, not GDP, drives forward returns.
“you can still have a year of pretty decent economic growth, but you could have an underperforming market” ▶ 26:44
Late-cycle signals: IPO/equity supply & long bull 4
A historic surge in equity issuance — a record IPO pipeline (SpaceX, AI/LLM names) plus secondaries like Google's reported ~$85B raise — is, in Roberts' upcoming research, a classic late-cycle supply signal that historically precedes recessionary or crash outcomes (dot-com peak, 2020 SPAC wave).
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highIPOsSpaceXGoogleequity issuance
So what: Massive new supply at premium prices needs demand to absorb it; when it can't, prices fall — a top-signal to respect.
“when you have big historic spikes in equity issuance, it tends to correspond with recessionary outcomes in in the markets” ▶ 49:14
Roberts says the wave of IPOs/issuance is insiders and private equity cashing out at premium valuations into a hot market, and he would NOT buy SpaceX on IPO day despite liking the company long-term — wait rather than pay the IPO-day premium.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highSpaceXprivate equityIPOs
So what: Avoid IPO-day premiums on hot offerings; insider selling into strength is a distribution signal.
“if I'm an insider and you got a market doing this, yeah, I'm looking to get out” ▶ 49:35
Roberts notes 2008's equity-issuance surge should be excluded from the bearish historical analog because that was desperation capital-raising to avoid bankruptcy (e.g., Goldman Sachs selling preferreds to Berkshire), not opportunistic top-ticking like today.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highGoldman SachsBerkshire Hathawayequity issuance
So what: Refines the issuance signal: today's supply is opportunistic distribution, which is the more reliable late-cycle tell.
“they were doing equity issuance to stay alive” ▶ 51:41
Roberts characterizes this as a 15-year, ~500-600% bull market — by magnitude of advancement the biggest ever — and stresses it will end, but momentum is hard to kill and it could run another 2-4 years before reverting.
thesis🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumS&P 500bull market
So what: Stay invested but vigilant; don't short a momentum bull, but recognize late-cycle positioning is warranted.
“by far, it's the biggest bull market we've ever had” ▶ 53:00
Oil supply, liquidity & rug-pull risks 5
Roberts flags oil as the most likely 'rug pull': a supply-driven spike (oil has been flat between ~$90-100 for a month) that erodes consumer confidence, rolls into earnings expectations, and triggers a broad market sell-off — the kind of event that makes capital actually leave the 'bathtub.'
causal🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumoilcrudeconsumer confidenceearnings
So what: An oil-supply shock is the cleanest path from a shallow rotation to a real index correction; watch crude as the trigger.
“there's a lot of complacency in the market... so it won't take a lot to do a rug pull” ▶ 39:46
Taggart warns oil inventories have 'passed the red line' (citing imminent interview with oil expert Jeff Currie and a corroborating WSJ article), so prices could move substantially higher despite the oil market not yet pricing the Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz risk.
prediction🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumoilJeff CurrieIranStrait of HormuzWall Street Journal
So what: Inventory drawdown is a setup for an oil squeeze; energy longs benefit, broad equities at risk if it spikes.
“there's definitely reasons to be concerned here that oil prices could actually start going substantially higher because we've kind of passed the red line in in inventories” ▶ 38:41
Roberts' RIA portfolio is long energy (Exxon Mobil, MLPs) and would benefit from higher oil; he's 'waiting for the oil market to tell me' to care, treating price action as the signal rather than front-running the inventory narrative.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumExxon MobilMLPsenergyoil
So what: Energy exposure is a hedge against the oil-spike scenario that would hurt the broad market.
“we're long energy stocks. We own Exxon Mobile. We own MLPS... I would love higher oil prices because that would play out great for the portfolio” ▶ 39:19
Liquidity / financial conditions are about as loose as they've been in a long time, sustained by bank lending surging alongside economic growth and the Fed holding lots of short-term T-bills that fund bank reserves and encourage lending — Roberts says nothing is impairing liquidity yet.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highliquidityFederal ReserveT-billsbank lending
So what: Loose liquidity is the bull's fuel; watch bank-lending slowdown, rate hikes or pricier oil as the things that would choke it.
“liquidity is the blood of the market” ▶ 46:53
Roberts says it would take the Fed hiking, rates rising and staying up, or much higher oil to tighten liquidity — and he does NOT expect the Fed to hike rates, keeping the liquidity backdrop supportive for now.
prediction🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumFederal Reserveinterest ratesliquidity
So what: Absent a Fed hike or oil spike, the liquidity tide stays in — limiting downside to a shallow correction.
“you need the Fed to hike rates... which I don't expect they will” ▶ 45:37
Bitcoin, credit spreads & private credit 3
Bitcoin has fallen sharply with retail enthusiasm gone quiet; the four explanations floated are a normal halving-style cyclical drawdown, Michael Saylor's Strategy (ex-MicroStrategy) selling a small amount of BTC, AI/semiconductors siphoning speculative capital, and Bitcoin signaling waning liquidity as a 'canary in the coal mine.'
framework🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: exploratoryBitcoinMichael SaylorStrategyMicroStrategyAIsemiconductors
So what: If Bitcoin is a liquidity tell, its weakness could foreshadow tightening even while official liquidity reads loose.
“Bitcoin's a basically a measure of liquidity and liquidity must be waning right now” ▶ 44:52
Credit spreads remain 'dead' (complacent), having only briefly widened on the oil-driven rate spike before tightening again as oil and rates fell — Roberts reads this as no stress signal from credit markets.
data_point🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highcredit spreadsinterest ratesoil
So what: Tight credit spreads remove a key recession warning; a widening would be the early sign of real trouble.
≈ “Yep. Dead.” paraphrase
Roberts argues the ~$2T private-credit market is too small to be 'the next subprime'; recent fund gating is a liquidity run, not credit impairment — e.g., the Blackstone BDC (BXSL) has ~98% collateralized loans and trades at a discount to NAV, which he frames as opportunity in the 'rubble.'
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumprivate creditBlackstoneBDCsBXSL
So what: Discounted, high-quality BDCs may be a contrarian buy; gating ≠ insolvency, but buyers must vet credit quality carefully.
“gating does not necessarily mean that there's something wrong with the underlying credits” ▶ 1:02:10
Labor market split & AI displacement 3
The labor data tells two stories: hiring is resurging but quits remain very low (signaling worker fear), the bulk of new jobs are low-wage leisure/hospitality/healthcare, and the college-graduate unemployment rate is an elevated ~5.6% — a 'low hire, no fire' market skewed to low-pay roles.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highjobsquits rateleisurehospitalityhealthcarecollege graduates
So what: The headline strength masks a soft, low-quality job mix — consumer spending power may be weaker than payrolls suggest.
“The unemployment rate for college graduates right now is like 5.6%.” ▶ 1:06:45
For the first time ever, Challenger Gray & Christmas data cites AI as the #1 reason for layoffs — and with AI deployment still in its earliest innings, Taggart warns the displacement effect compounds materially over the next 3-5 years.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highAIChallenger Gray & Christmasjobstech layoffs
So what: Structural labor displacement is now measurable; a secular headwind for entry-level employment and consumer income.
“for the first time ever um AI is being given as the number one reason for layoffs” ▶ 1:05:18
Taggart and Roberts argue AI is eroding the bottom rungs of the career ladder across every profession (bookkeeping, entry-level work), removing the development onboarding humans need — a non-obvious structural risk distinct from whole-job automation.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highAIjobsbookkeeping
So what: Long-run productivity gains come with a hollowed-out talent pipeline; watch entry-level hiring as an AI-impact gauge.
“those rungs of the ladder are getting removed and so you can't go from off the ladder to rung three or four” ▶ 1:12:19
Portfolio trades & risk management 4
Roberts' RIA executed a value rotation: trimmed high-beta tech mid-day (Broadcom and other high-flyers) earlier, then added to beaten-down Walmart and Costco — citing Costco's membership-fee model as a durable cash-flow moat Walmart lacks.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highBroadcomWalmartCostco
So what: Concrete value-rotation playbook: take profits in stretched tech, add quality consumer-staples with structural moats.
“we added to Walmart. We added to Costco” ▶ 1:22:11
Additional RIA swaps within the value/dividend sleeve: sold Procter & Gamble for Coca-Cola, and swapped a ~2% Duke Energy position for a ~3% American Electric Power position — all favoring better relative performance among defensive dividend names.
anecdote🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumProcter & GambleCoca-ColaDuke EnergyAmerican Electric Power
So what: Shows active tilt toward dividend/defensive quality as the rotation plays out; specific name-level positioning.
“we actually uh sold Proctor Gamble and bought Coke” ▶ 1:23:38
Roberts' risk-management prescription for the correction: rebalance, cut high-beta exposure, add low-beta quality dividend stocks (terrible performers the last 3 years that protect on the downside), and add short-duration Treasuries (6-month to 1-year T-bills) to lower portfolio beta.
framework🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: highTreasury billsquality stocksdividendsfixed income
So what: Actionable defensive allocation to ride out the pullback while staying invested.
“buy some, you know, six month, one-year Treasury bills. That'll help lower the beta volatility of your portfolio.” ▶ 1:20:46
On housing, Roberts says don't make a macro bet shorting subprime (don't 'be Michael Burry'); existing-home sales are at the lowest since the financial crisis and sellers are pulling listings, but a housing recession can occur without a systemic crisis or a 30% price crash.
contrarian🗣 Lance Robertsconviction: mediumhousingMichael Burrysubprimereal estate
So what: Avoid the consensus housing-crash short; instead monitor and reduce portfolio risk if concerned, and shop distressed real estate selectively.
“don't be Michael Bur and go short subprime right now” ▶ 29:28
Today's Markets Are A Digital Casino On Cocaine | Chris Irons, Quoth The Raven75 min32 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Chris Irons (Quoth the Raven) argues US equity markets are a 'permanently distorted' digital casino where the passive bid, 0DTE options gamma, and unlimited liquidity have killed fundamental active management, so he is exiting active trading and concentrating capital in gold, government bonds, emerging markets and asymmetric thematic bets (psychedelics, nuclear/SMR, cybersecurity) while warning the bond market is the key fault line that forces eventual yield-curve control.
Permanent distortion / market structure · 6Gamification of speculation · 3Macro: inflation, debt, dollar · 4Bond market as the fault line · 4Asymmetric thematic bets · 9Fundamentals vs. market reality · 3Tail risk and positioning posture · 3
Permanent distortion / market structure 6
Chris Irons argues historical valuation norms (market-cap-to-GDP, P/E) from 1920-2000 no longer apply because the Fed now prints money at every ~3% drawdown, creating a 'permanently distorted' market regime where old means are meaningless.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500Federal ReserveShiller P/Emarket cap to GDP
So what: Valuation-based shorting or mean-reversion timing is structurally unreliable; size positions for a regime that can stay distorted far longer than fundamentals justify.
“those historical means, they don't mean anything now because in 1980, we weren't printing money at every 3% draw down in markets” ▶ 10:34
The S&P set four consecutive record highs last week with negative market breadth (more decliners than advancers) on all four days, evidence the index is driven by a handful of mega-cap names rather than broad participation.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Index-level highs mask deteriorating breadth; a thinning leadership base is a classic late-cycle fragility signal to watch.
≈ “the S&P set four record highs and there were more decliners than advancers on all four days” paraphrase
Irons credits Mike Green's 'passive bid' thesis: market-cap-weighted index funds act as a price-insensitive 'giant mindless robot' buying at any valuation, which mechanically lets indices hit all-time highs even with negative breadth.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highMike Greenpassive fundsindex funds401k
So what: Inflows/outflows to passive vehicles, not fundamentals, are a primary price driver; watch fund-flow and 401k contribution dynamics for the marginal bid turning.
“Just buying at any valuation. And if you're doing that on a market weighted index, that's why you can have more decliners and advancers and set all-time highs.” ▶ 12:35
Irons describes a reflexive options mechanism where retail buying of short-dated options forces dealers/market-makers to buy the underlying stock, turning option flow into a self-reinforcing upward price driver (gamma squeeze).
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highoptions marketRobinhood0DTE options
So what: Rallies powered by forced dealer hedging rather than conviction buying can reverse violently when the option flow flips; track gamma positioning as a stability gauge.
“if you buy enough of those options, the people writing them have are forced to go in and buy the stock” ▶ 11:56
Irons declares fundamental active management 'dead, at least for right now' — citing a billionaire friend who admitted 'I just don't know how to make money' ~8 years ago, marking the end of long/short investing on fundamentals.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highhedge fundsactive management
So what: Even identifying confirmed fraud or cheap cash-generative names earns no payoff if the market won't agree; fundamental conviction must be paired with flow/momentum awareness.
“investing on fundamentals sadly is dead at least for right now” ▶ 14:42
Irons argues the leverage and forced/gamma-driven nature of the rally means any selloff will be faster and more violent than historically, potentially gapping limit-down over a few days before the Fed intervenes.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumS&P 500NASDAQFederal Reserve
So what: Tail-hedge and liquidity planning should assume a sharp non-linear drawdown rather than an orderly decline; the bounce-back catalyst is Fed action, not value buyers.
“due to that and leverage, it will snap lower a lot quicker” ▶ 1:01:38
Gamification of speculation 3
Irons frames modern markets as a 'digital casino on cocaine' — prediction markets, 24/7 crypto, pre-market options and sports betting on the same phone have collapsed the line between investing, entertainment and addiction.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highprediction marketscryptosports bettingoptions
So what: Ubiquitous speculation channels reflect and amplify froth; their proliferation is itself a late-cycle behavioral marker.
“it's a digital casino on cocaine because that is really what it's become” ▶ 0:13
Irons notes traders are now levered up to 500:1 trading single pips in crypto, equating it to betting balls-and-strikes in a baseball game — extreme retail leverage in always-on micro-speculation.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumcrypto
So what: Extreme embedded leverage in retail crypto raises systemic fragility and forced-liquidation cascade risk during volatility spikes.
“now you got guys levered 500 to one trading single pips in crypto” ▶ 25:36
Irons advises 'be right and sit tight' (get it right, sit tight) — arguing that constant accessibility leads to over-trading and that for most participants the optimal action most of the time is to do nothing.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: high
So what: Behavioral discipline (fewer, higher-conviction decisions) is presented as the dominant edge in an environment engineered to provoke constant action.
“be right and sit tight” ▶ 22:38
Macro: inflation, debt, dollar 4
Irons cites a stressed macro backdrop: inflation ~4%, PPI ~6%, and credit-card and auto-loan delinquencies at, near, or above 2008 highs, set against record-high Shiller P/E and market-cap-to-GDP.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highinflationPPIcredit cardsauto loansShiller P/E
So what: Simultaneous peak valuations and deteriorating consumer credit give the market the 'furthest to fall' it has ever had; monitor delinquency trends as a cycle tell.
“credit cards and autoloan delinquencies at or near or above 2008 highs” ▶ 42:16
Irons sees the US 'locked in a room with no exits': either the market crashes, the consumer runs out of money at ~3.5% rates, or the bond market forces a choice — and policymakers will pick the 'soft default' of more money printing and inflation rather than a hard default.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highFederal ReserveUS Treasurybond marketUS dollar
So what: Base case is debasement over default; positions hedge currency debasement (hard assets, non-dollar exposure) rather than a clean deflationary reset.
“We will take the soft default route which means printing more money and more inflation on top of inflation that we already have.” ▶ 43:41
Irons argues every round of money printing widens the wealth gap (top 1% gain faster than bottom 90%), a bipartisan policy — both Elizabeth Warren and Donald Trump are calling for lower rates — that will eventually drive social unrest.
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highElizabeth WarrenDonald TrumpFederal ReserveBitcoin
So what: Persistent debasement is structurally embedded across both parties, reinforcing the secular case for inflation hedges and hard assets.
“Every time we print money, the wealth gap widens” ▶ 1:07:37
Irons flags de-dollarization risk: the Saudi petrodollar arrangement is eroding as oil is increasingly traded in yuan and gold, undermining the durability of US reserve-currency status despite the strong narrative.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumSaudi ArabiaUS dollaryuangoldAndy SchectmanBRICS
So what: Structural reserve-currency erosion supports gold and non-dollar allocations as a multi-year theme.
“now they're they're selling and trading oil in yuan and in gold” ▶ 45:00
Bond market as the fault line 4
Irons names the bond market — specifically the 10-year chart — as the single leading indicator he watches for when 'the wheels fall off,' citing 30-year yields near multi-decade highs around 5.15% and the 10-year near 5%.
framework🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: high10-year Treasury30-year Treasurybond market
So what: Watch long-end yields and weekly auctions as the primary trigger signal; a disorderly long end is the catalyst for the broader unwind.
“If I had to just watch one chart all day every day, it' probably be like the 10-year chart.” ▶ 1:00:19
Irons predicts the Fed will be forced into yield-curve control if the long end keeps malfunctioning, and warns the Fed is trapped between a deflationary depression and a hyperinflationary outcome at the next bout of market stress.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highFederal Reservebond marketyield curve control
So what: YCC would suppress yields and accelerate currency debasement; position for financial repression and a steep negative real-rate environment.
“I think the obvious path for macro is the yield curve control” ▶ 43:06
Irons highlights bonds selling off alongside equities (rather than acting as a safe haven) as an emerging-market-style signal that investors are doubting US creditworthiness, and poses whether a war resolution with bonds still selling off would be the scariest tell.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumbond marketTreasuriesequities
So what: A broken stock-bond correlation removes the classic 60/40 hedge; rising yields amid risk-off would confirm a sovereign-credit repricing.
“that's like the kind of thing that you see in emerging market country where people are just doubting the creditworthiness of the country” ▶ 1:03:24
Irons believes that simply leaving rates at current ~3.5% levels would eventually dry up liquidity and drive consumer delinquencies higher, and that policymakers are already floating rate hikes this year.
causal🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumFederal Reserveinterest ratesconsumer credit
So what: Even a static-rate path is contractionary; the liquidity drain is the slow-burn mechanism that precedes the violent equity unwind.
Asymmetric thematic bets 9
Irons' annual watchlist of names outperformed the S&P by more than 50% last year and is beating it by more than 10% this year (equal-weighted), built around under-noticed, asymmetric ideas not covered by mainstream financial media.
data_point🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highS&P 500
So what: Track record supports his under-the-radar thematic approach; the edge is sourcing ideas before CNBC coverage rather than fundamental valuation.
≈ “Last year that list outperformed the S&P by like more than 50%. This year it's outperforming the S&P by more than 10%.” paraphrase
Irons is bullish on psychedelics as a multi-year asymmetric theme, expecting RFK Jr. as HHS Secretary and Trump executive orders to expedite FDA approvals (possibly as soon as late summer/early fall), leading to big-pharma buyouts of clinical-stage names.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highpsychedelicsPSI ETFCompass PathwaysRFK Jr.Donald TrumpFDAPfizeribogaine

≈ “I think with RFK in as Health and Human Services Secretary, and Trump signing the executive orders to kind of advance psychedelics through” paraphrase
Irons notes the PSI psychedelics ETF holds only ~$32M AUM (up from ~$20M), signaling the theme is still early and under-owned, and speculates it could run from ~$22 to $100-$150 if approvals and speculative fervor arrive.
prediction🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: exploratoryPSI ETFDRAM ETF

“it would not surprise me to see that ETF which trades at you know 22 go to 100 or go to 150” ▶ 58:35
Irons cites nuclear/small-modular-reactor (SMR) names as a theme he identified ~2.5 years early as an AI-energy offshoot, pointing to Oklo (tied to Sam Altman) running from ~$5 to ~$140 despite no meaningful revenue.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highnuclearSMROkloSam AltmanZero Hedge

≈ “when names like Ollo came out... it was trading at you know 5 or something... lo and behold, it went to 140” paraphrase ▶ 52:00
Irons favors cybersecurity (via the IHAK ETF) as an under-owned theme, arguing future wars will be fought first through fiber optics and satellites before going 'hot,' and these names are trailing the broader AI trade.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumcybersecurityIHAK ETF

“the next few wars that we fight are going to be bought you know through fiber optic cable and and satellites first before they become hot wars” ▶ 53:42
Irons recommends getting AI exposure through cheap blue-chip proxies (Microsoft ~18x, Google, Amazon, Zoom ~12-15x) that already hold stakes in OpenAI/Anthropic, rather than paying ~20-40x NAV for private funds chasing Anthropic exposure.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highMicrosoftGoogleAmazonZoomOpenAIAnthropic

≈ “Don't go out and pay 40 times NAV on some private fund that's going to get rocked.” paraphrase
Irons prefers a basket of emerging-market equities (EEM and Latin America via ILF) at P/Es of 10-20 over an S&P near ~35x at a bubble top, explicitly positioning longer gold, government bonds and emerging markets and less long US AI stocks/semiconductors.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highemerging marketsEEMILFgoldgovernment bondssemiconductorsS&P 500

≈ “would I rather own a basket of 20 countries... with price to earnings ratios between 10 and 20 than be overweight a market cap weighted S&P at whatever 35 times earnings at the top of a bubble?” paraphrase ▶ 52:46
Irons reminds listeners he called GDX (gold miners) going from ~$20 to ~$120 over two years, using it as evidence that once these under-noticed trends catch on, they move quickly.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highGDXgold minersgold
So what: Supports momentum-on-catalyst thesis for his current picks; gold-miner leverage to gold has already played out sharply.
“you're talking to the same guy who said the GDX was going to go to 100 when it was at 20 two years ago. And where did it go? Went to 120” ▶ 58:57
Irons favors consumer staples as a defensive rotation, having flagged them after two years of underperformance versus the S&P on the thesis that investors back into staples when they turn defensive.
thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumconsumer staples
So what: A defensive value rotation candidate if leadership narrows and risk appetite fades.
Fundamentals vs. market reality 3
Irons cites PayPal at ~9x earnings, gushing cash, buying back stock and owning Venmo as a name that 'should' be an obvious acquisition target, but argues it stays cheap unless private equity or the market chooses to put a bid under it.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumPayPalVenmoprivate equity

≈ “Nine times earnings, gushing cash, buying back stock, got the number one financial asset, out there, I think, in Venmo.” paraphrase
Irons says a fundamentalist friend long cash-gushing 9x-earnings stocks and short the AI bubble is getting badly hurt this year, evidence that being fundamentally right offers no protection when the market ignores fundamentals.
anecdote🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highAI stocks
So what: Shorting the AI bubble on valuation alone has been a losing trade; timing and flow dominate fundamental correctness in this regime.
≈ “short the AI bubble right now and he's getting the kicked out of him this year” paraphrase
Irons warns there are companies trading at all-time highs that he believes are committing outright fraud (earnings from off-balance-sheet entities), but argues it is irrelevant because regulators and even courts lack the financial acumen to act.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highAndrew Leftshort sellers

“You can point to a company say here's the fraud. the earnings are coming from this offbalance sheet entity. No one cares.” ▶ 14:47
Tail risk and positioning posture 3
Irons' top overall message is to take extraordinary caution, arguing the more euphoric and better things look, the more likely a violent reversal — invoking Bill Ackman's 'hell is coming' COVID call marking the bottom as evidence sentiment extremes work both ways.
contrarian🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: highBill Ackman

thesis🗣 Chris Ironsconviction: mediumJapanJGBsbond market

DEBATE: Does The Entire World Face A "Bronze Age Type" Collapse? | Brent Johnson vs Craig Tindale73 min36 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
A macro debate between Brent Johnson (Dollar Milkshake) and Craig Tindale on whether the West cedes hegemony to China. Tindale argues a financialized West has lost the material/industrial economy to China and that physical resource limits (copper, rare earths, fertilizer, electricity) make the AI/data-center buildout and current growth unsustainable for both sides — a 'Bronze Age type' collapse. Johnson counters the US won't cede dominance, that the transition triggers a stronger dollar, liquidity/sovereign-debt crises, and that the real victims are peripheral emerging markets, not the two great powers. Investment takeaway: rotate from financial assets into hard assets/commodities, refiners, agricultural commodities and re-industrialization plays; be wary of AI/data-center names hitting energy and material bottlenecks.
Financial vs material economy bifurcation (the Tindale Trap) · 4China supply-chain dependency and the West's deindustrialization · 4Currency regime change — Dollar Milkshake, CNY transactional rise, and the death of small currencies · 5Physical limits to growth — the 'Bronze Age collapse' thesis · 5AI / data-center energy and equipment bottlenecks · 4Sovereign-debt / liquidity crisis cascade and the geography of pain · 4US strategic advantages and the 'ring of power' reserve currency · 4Actionable investment themes — hard assets, agriculture, and aviation MRO · 6
Financial vs material economy bifurcation (the Tindale Trap) 4
Tindale's core framework ('the Tindale Trap') is a bifurcation between a financialized paper economy and a material/industrial economy, and the West has reached an endpoint where the material economy no longer responds to financial-economy stimulus as it did over the prior 40-50 years.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUnited StatesChinaFederal Reserve
So what: Implies monetary easing/liquidity will increasingly fail to produce real output in the West, favoring hard assets over financial assets as the policy transmission mechanism breaks.
“it's a bifocation between a financial economy and a material economy” ▶ 2:52
The US financial economy is roughly $400 trillion, of which Tindale estimates only ~1% is metals, chemicals and refined products, a ratio he argues must mean-revert toward the industrial economy.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUnited Statescommoditiesmetalschemicals
So what: Frames a multi-year rotation thesis: capital flowing out of paper assets into physical/industrial assets as the 'table turns.'
“The financial economy in the US is about $400 trillion... 1% of that at the moment is metals and chemicals and and refined products” ▶ 1:00:11
Tindale argues the financial economy has grown from roughly 8% to about 23% of the economy while Main Street de-industrialized, driven by FOMC/Fed policy acting like a 'super lobby group' that channeled wages into financial products and inflated assets via Bernanke's wealth effect.
causal🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highFederal ReserveFOMCBen Bernankehousingequities
So what: Positions Fed-driven financialization as structurally fragile and reversing, a tailwind for real assets and a headwind for inflated financial valuations.
“we've we've seen the financial economy grow from I think 8% and I think it's about 23%” ▶ 5:03
Tindale's investment posture is to cycle his own capital out of the financial economy and into the industrial economy, treating the rotation as the dominant multi-decade theme of his book 'The Return to Matter.'
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highindustrial economycommodities
So what: A clear actionable allocation signal: overweight industrial/material exposure, underweight purely financial assets.
“that's where I'm putting all all my money that that that uh that cycle into the industrial economy and out of the financial economy” ▶ 1:00:39
China supply-chain dependency and the West's deindustrialization 4
China supplies an estimated 50-98% of metals and most refined industrial inputs to the West, and the US cannot independently source titanium for F-35s or copper for data centers without Chinese permission, constituting a critical national-security dependency.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highChinatitaniumcopperrare earthsF-35data centers
So what: Re-shoring of refining and component manufacturing becomes a strategic imperative, favoring Western miners, refiners and material-tech companies addressing these dependencies.
“it can't get its titanium for the F-35s it can't get... its copper for its data centers” ▶ 4:38
India is as dependent on China as the US is, sourcing 100% of its rare earths for defense products from China, per a forthcoming Tindale essay — the supply-chain vulnerability is not US-specific.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highIndiaChinarare earthsdefense
So what: Broadens the rare-earth supply-security trade beyond the US to a global re-sourcing scramble, supporting non-Chinese rare-earth supply.
“India um you know gets 100% of its uh rare earths for defense products and... just about everything else from China” ▶ 7:02
The US gave away manufacturing to China expecting China to Westernize; that bet failed, and Johnson and Tindale agree the West now must rebuild manufacturing capacity it 'forgot how to do,' from refining to component production.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesChinamanufacturing
So what: Underwrites a structural re-industrialization capex cycle in the West — supportive of domestic industrial, refining and capital-goods plays.
“the United States gave away their manufacturing prowess to China in the hopes that China would become more western... that did not happen” ▶ 14:05
Commodity exporters like Australia sell raw materials but not refined end-products, leaving them structurally akin to a factory that ships inputs to someone else (China) for value-added refining — a vulnerability for resource-exporter currencies and economies.
causal🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highAustraliaBHPChinacommodities
So what: Suggests value-capture, and ultimately currency strength, accrues to refiners/end-producers (China) over raw-material exporters, a structural headwind for AUD-style commodity economies.
“it's akin to having a factory that um takes all the raw materials, but doesn't produce the end product” ▶ 7:34
Currency regime change — Dollar Milkshake, CNY transactional rise, and the death of small currencies 5
Tindale predicts a regime shift from a single USD reserve currency to a 'block currency' structure: the USD becomes the debt-servicing and allied-block currency (~56-58% of global debt is USD-denominated) while the CNY becomes the transactional currency for buying physical commodities like copper and rare earths that China refines.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUS dollarChinese yuanCNYcopperrare earths
