So what: Implies bifurcated FX demand: persistent USD demand for debt service even as CNY gains share in commodity settlement — watch CNY commodity-pricing milestones as a structural signal.
“the the currency that's needed to buy copper... is basically CNY... uh or rare earth because... they do most of the refining” ▶ 9:03
BHP, which had always insisted on US dollars, is now accepting CNY — cited as concrete evidence that the commodity-settlement currency is shifting toward the yuan.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highBHPCNYUS dollar
So what: A trackable data point: commodity-major acceptance of CNY is an early marker of de-dollarization in trade settlement even as USD dominates debt.
“you got examples like BHP who had always insisted on US dollars now taking CNY” ▶ 8:13
Johnson reiterates the Dollar Milkshake thesis: during the chaotic transition to a new monetary system, the dollar rises versus other fiat currencies (not necessarily versus real things like energy/commodities), and that rise triggers credit contraction, liquidity crises, currency crises and sovereign failures.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUS dollarfiat currenciessovereign bonds
So what: A long-USD-vs-fiat positioning with the expectation that dollar strength is the trigger for global liquidity/credit stress — relevant for hedging EM and peripheral-sovereign exposure.
“I expect the dollar to rise not fall... when the dollar rises versus other fiat currencies, bad things happen in markets” ▶ 22:54
Tindale predicts the end of many small/peripheral currencies rather than the end of the USD or CNY — weak currencies get absorbed into a USD-allied block via dollar swap lines (e.g. the UAE), which Tindale expects to effectively become the block's local-support and transfer mechanism.
prediction🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highUS dollarCNYUAEdollar swapsScott Bessent
So what: Implies short small-EM-currency / consolidation risk; dollar-swap inclusion becomes a survival marker for smaller economies and a tool the US Treasury wields.
“I'm not predicting the end of the USD or the CNY. I'm predicting the end of probably a lot of the others” ▶ 33:03
Both debaters expect continued dollarization (not de-dollarization) within the US-allied block, while a competing China-centric block may also coalesce, with stablecoins playing a role in extending USD reach.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumUS dollarstablecoinsChina
So what: Supports exposure to USD-linked rails (incl. stablecoins) as a vehicle of dollar entrenchment rather than betting on near-term reserve-currency replacement.
Physical limits to growth — the 'Bronze Age collapse' thesis 5
Tindale's central contrarian thesis is that this is not a US-vs-China baton change but a third outcome — a 'Bronze Age type' collapse — because global physical production capacity for key materials is insufficient for either side to build what it wants, echoing the 1973 Limits to Growth study.
contrarian🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcopperrare earthsLimits to Growth
So what: Reframes the macro debate from geopolitical winner-take-all to a supply-constrained world where scarce real resources outperform regardless of which power 'wins.'
“I think we we're we're facing a much more fundamental bronze age type uh collapse where the things that we want to do um aren't aren't sustainable” ▶ 0:04
Tindale quantifies the copper shortfall: the world needs to open about six copper mines per year to meet demand but is opening roughly one, a structural supply deficit independent of the geopolitical rivalry.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppermining
So what: A specific, durable bull case for copper prices and copper miners driven by chronic under-investment in new supply.
“we have to open six copper mines a year and refine the copper and we're opening one” ▶ 19:41
Tantalum demand is projected at roughly five times current production within five years, illustrating acute looming shortfalls across multiple critical minerals beyond copper.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: hightantalumcritical minerals
So what: Points to severe pricing/scarcity for specialty minerals like tantalum — a niche supply-security trade.
“tantelum, for instance, we need five times more tantelum in five years than we we currently produce” ▶ 19:54
Tindale frames China and the US as 'conjoined twins choking each other' in an integrated economy where China still imports ~$2.5 trillion of goods, so a successful decoupling damages both — neither power cleanly wins.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highChinaUnited States
So what: Cautions against one-sided 'China rises / West falls' or 'West wins' trades; favors assets that benefit from mutual stress (real assets, scarcity) over directional power bets.
“I compare them to... conjoined twins who are trying to choke each other that if successful they both die” ▶ 20:43
Tindale argues re-industrialization is rate-limited by physics, analogizing it to a three-legged race between Gordon Moore (bits, fast) and Einstein (atoms, physically constrained) — the material economy simply cannot scale at software speed.
framework🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highsemiconductorsindustrial economy
So what: Tempers expectations for how fast Western re-shoring and AI hardware buildout can occur; bottlenecks persist and reward incumbents/holders of scarce physical capacity.
“Gordon can go a lot faster because he's bits and bites, but Einstein can't go fast at all because he's... constrained by physics” ▶ 1:01:52
AI / data-center energy and equipment bottlenecks 4
Tindale is bearish on AI companies, arguing the data-center buildout collides with physical limits — citing a claim attributed to Jensen Huang that AI needs ~1,000x more electricity than is currently produced, which Tindale calls absurd and unmet.
contrarian🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highAIdata centersJensen HuangNvidiaelectricity
So what: A short/avoid signal on richly valued AI names whose growth assumptions ignore power and material constraints; favors power/grid and energy beneficiaries instead.
“Jensen who said it, we need a thousand times more electricity... than we're currently producing. Now, that's ridiculous” ▶ 20:21
Tindale cites Elon Musk warning that the AI industry will run out of electricity by year-end, an under-noticed comment underscoring near-term power scarcity as a constraint on AI growth.
prediction🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumElon MuskAIelectricity
So what: Reinforces a near-term electricity-bottleneck thesis: power generation/grid capacity becomes the binding constraint and the place to be positioned.
“Elon Musk... said that everyone's going to run out of electricity in the AI industry... by the end of the year” ▶ 1:00:59
Grid transformers from Siemens have a five-to-six-year lead time against a reported 148 billion euro backlog, and sulfuric-acid furnaces ('crackers') are similarly unobtainable on short notice — concrete equipment log-jams throttling electrification and refining.
data_point🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highSiemenstransformersgridsulfuric acid
So what: Identifies electrical-equipment and specialty-process-equipment makers (transformers, furnaces) as supply-constrained beneficiaries with multi-year backlogs and pricing power.
“you can't get transformers from Seammens for five to six years. They got 148 billion euro back order” ▶ 1:01:09
Tindale predicts AI will be culturally elevated to a quasi-deity and 'independent' authority (analogous to Fed independence), with governments handing more decisions to it as a control mechanism amid chaos — a managerial/technocratic overreach he ties to Trumpism as backlash and to surveillance states like Britain.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumAIFederal ReservePalantirAlex KarpUnited Kingdom
So what: Implies durable demand for AI-enabled state surveillance/control software (e.g. Palantir-type) even as broad AI valuations face material constraints — a bifurcated AI view.
Sovereign-debt / liquidity crisis cascade and the geography of pain 4
Johnson argues the supply-chain and resource problems Tindale identifies ultimately express as a currency crisis and sovereign-debt crisis — but primarily for Southeast Asian, peripheral European and some developed economies, not for the US or China.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highsovereign debtSoutheast AsiaEuropeUS dollar
So what: Directs the crisis-hedge toward peripheral sovereigns/EM rather than the core; the two great powers are expected to weather it best.
“a lot of the things that Craig has very correctly pointed out end up being a currency crisis and a sovereign debt crisis. perhaps not for the United States and China” ▶ 29:45
Johnson describes a self-reinforcing doom loop: small import-dependent countries must print local currency to buy USD-priced energy and food, which depreciates their currency further and spikes their sovereign yields, accelerating toward default.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highemerging marketsUS dollarenergyfoodsovereign bonds
So what: A mechanism for shorting/avoiding vulnerable EM sovereign debt and currencies as commodity prices rise; rising USD-priced inputs are the trigger.
“they have to then print their own currency... that ends up making the their currency fall even more... that causes their yields to jump on their their sovereign bonds” ▶ 29:13
Johnson explicitly times the potential sovereign-debt/currency crisis as anywhere from 9-12 months to 9-12 years out, declining to pin timing but stating he understands the 'plumbing' and that the transition cannot happen without great chaos.
prediction🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumsovereign debtcurrencies
So what: Wide time horizon argues for maintaining the hedge structurally rather than trading a specific catalyst date.
“I could make a case that it gets there in the next 9 to 12 months, but... I could make the case it'll take another nine to 12 years as well” ▶ 30:15
Tindale identifies the most disadvantaged economies as input-import-dependent developing nations (Bangladesh, India, Thailand and other Southeast Asian states) lacking fertilizer, LNG, herbicides and petrochemicals — not the slow-growth developed world per se.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highBangladeshIndiaThailandfertilizerLNGpetrochemicals
So what: Pinpoints input-deficit EMs as the locus of social/political instability and currency stress — relevant for EM risk screening.
US strategic advantages and the 'ring of power' reserve currency 4
Johnson argues the US holding the global reserve currency is like holding 'the ring of power' — an immense weapon usable against opponents — and that under Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant the system is shifting from a rules-based / greater-good (Keynesian) order to an 'America First' deployment of the monetary system.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUS dollarScott BessantUS TreasuryFederal Reserve
So what: Signals a more weaponized, self-interested US monetary policy with large external spillovers — bullish USD leverage, bearish for those on the wrong side of US policy.
“the United States has the global reserve currency, it is still like having the ring of power” ▶ 51:06
Asked which country to pick in a game of Risk, Johnson chooses the US for its aggregate geostrategic advantages (military, resources, reserve currency), while Tindale picks Australia, citing his own re-modeled climate scenarios showing Australia/New Zealand remaining 'wet, hot but quite survivable' versus worse outcomes elsewhere.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesAustraliaNew Zealandclimate
So what: Both converge that the resource-rich, militarily strong powers (US, China, Australia) survive best — relevant for geographic capital-safety and agricultural-land theses.
“to to me there's no question you pick the United States” ▶ 50:05
Tindale points to a strengthening US-allied block — a recently signed Quad-style agreement among Japan, the US and Australia, plus Asian states (Philippines, Korea, Japan) seeking a balance against China — as evidence the West is actively, and fairly competently, fighting back.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumQuadJapanUnited StatesAustraliaPhilippinesSouth KoreaChina
So what: An allied economic/security block is coalescing — supportive of intra-block trade, defense and supply-chain integration plays.
Tindale credits the Trump administration's economic team — Bessant at Treasury and Chris Wright on energy — as 'fairly smart' and evidence the US republic 'has a lot of fight left in it,' pushing back on naive 'China simply rises, America fades' narratives.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: mediumDonald TrumpScott BessantChris WrightUnited States
So what: Tempers outright bearish-US positioning; policy competence on currency and energy is a partial offset to structural decline.
Actionable investment themes — hard assets, agriculture, and aviation MRO 6
Tindale's actionable allocation is commodities, miners and refiners — but selectively, warning that many commodities depend on choke-point inputs like sulfuric acid used in leach mining of copper, nickel and rare earths, so miner/refiner selection must account for input dependencies.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppernickelrare earthssulfuric acidminersrefiners
So what: Buy commodity producers but screen for input-supply security (sulfuric acid, energy) rather than buying the sector indiscriminately.
“some of the commodities are beholden to things like sulfuric acid... leech... mining is is one of the first... stages of of a lot of copper mining” ▶ 58:59
Tindale flags copper and gold as 'no-brainers' and highlights an emerging cohort of Western material-technology companies building domestic refining/production capacity — including an unnamed titanium company he expects to become the US titanium provider.
thesis🗣 Craig Tindaleconviction: highcoppergoldtitaniummaterial technology
So what: Points to onshore critical-material producers (titanium, refining tech) as a growth subsector benefiting from supply-security re-shoring.
“I think we're going to be anchored in the material roots of the economy” ▶ 1:00:02
Johnson favors agricultural commodities — soybean meal, corn, wheat — on asymmetric risk/reward (limited downside, large upside) and is genuinely worried about a 2026 food shortage driven by fertilizer shortfalls (Strait of Hormuz disruption), high energy prices and a near-record El Nino.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highsoybean mealcornwheatfertilizerEl NinoStrait of Hormuz
So what: A specific long-grains/agriculture trade with favorable convexity tied to fertilizer and energy supply shocks.
“I think we have an opportunity that they're not going to go down a whole lot... but they have the potential to go up quite a bit” ▶ 1:03:22
Johnson flags a non-obvious trade in aviation MRO (maintenance, repair and overhaul): a years-long pre-existing backlog for widebody aircraft concentrated in Abu Dhabi, compounded by Middle East disruption, could force aircraft groundings and a supply constraint showing up in roughly nine months even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens tomorrow.
prediction🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumaviationMROwidebody aircraftAbu DhabiStrait of Hormuz
So what: An under-followed bottleneck idea: aviation MRO capacity and related names could benefit from grounding-driven scarcity in global air freight/travel late 2026 into 2027.
“there's an industry called MRO which stands for maintenance, repair and overhaul of airplanes... one of the major places where this takes place for widebody aircraft... Abu Dhabi” ▶ 1:04:21
Johnson cautions against over-bearishness and apocalypticism, arguing there are 'incredible opportunities' alongside the challenges and that investors should not 'check out, move to a mountain top, and come back in 10 years.'
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highmarkets
So what: A balance check: stay invested and opportunistic in real assets rather than going fully defensive/cash on the dark macro thesis.
“there's incredible opportunities, too. And I think there is a danger in being overly bearish and overly apocryphal” ▶ 1:02:36
Johnson singles out Tindale's Substack report on 'the importance of Japan to the United States' as essential reading, and both name Japan and Korea as pivotal to the US over the next few years — flagging Japan as an under-appreciated macro focus.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumJapanSouth KoreaUnited States
So what: Signals Japan/Korea as a macro theme to research for allied-block supply-chain and defense positioning.
SPECIAL REPORT: Will The Fertilizer Shortage Create A Global Food Crisis? | Bruce Sherrick63 min38 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
University of Illinois land economist Bruce Sherrick argues the Strait of Hormuz closure and tariffs delivered a fertilizer/energy PRICE shock, not a physical supply shock, for the northern hemisphere this harvest — most fertilizer was already in the ground — so he is NOT ringing a global-famine alarm for fall 2026. His real worries are: a prolonged conflict spilling into a wider regional war, food-price-driven insecurity in Africa and western parts of Asia, and depressed US commodity prices from trade rerouting. His central investment thesis is farmland as the most-fixed, inflation-hedging, equity-uncorrelated asset poised for a structural demand wave from the $126T boomer wealth transfer.
Hormuz/Tariff shock is price, not physical supply (this harvest) · 5Fertilizer supply mechanics, dependency, and inelasticity · 5Geopolitics, escalation risk, and the food-security red line · 6Trade rerouting and depressed US commodity prices · 4Second-order effects: steel, equipment, processing chain · 4Named companies and episodic fertilizer trade · 4Farmland as the structural inflation-hedge asset · 7Ag-tech productivity revolution · 3
Hormuz/Tariff shock is price, not physical supply (this harvest) 5
The Strait of Hormuz closure, through which roughly one-third of the world's seaborne fertilizer trade passes, will NOT cause a northern-hemisphere fertilizer shortage for the 2026 fall harvest because most of the required fertilizer had already shipped and was applied before the closure (a 'pig in the python' lag).
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrick (U. Illinois land economist)conviction: highStrait of Hormuzfertilizernorthern hemisphereIran
So what: Fades panic-driven longs in fertilizer/ag on a 'famine this fall' narrative; the actionable shock is price/margin, not tonnage availability, for the current cycle.
“it's sort of a pig in the python problem. It takes a long time to work something through anyhow and most of the fertilizer was already in place for the northern hemisphere planting season this year” ▶ 3:25
Much of the US corn crop's nitrogen was applied last fall as anhydrous ammonia and most dry fertilizers were already contracted and in place before spring, so 2026 northern-hemisphere planting was largely insulated from the Hormuz disruption.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnitrogenanhydrous ammoniacornUnited States
So what: The window for a physical-shortage trade in 2026 northern-hemisphere inputs has effectively closed; watch the NEXT application cycle (fall 2026 / southern-hemisphere) instead.
“much of the nitrogen application that was required for the corn crop in the US was done last fall” ▶ 12:58
Fertilizer prices rose sharply from a combination of the Hormuz closure, Canada/Mexico tariffs that pre-dated the closure, and higher energy prices that feed directly into ammonia production — but unlike the 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock there was NO offsetting rise in output/commodity prices.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfertilizerCanadaMexicotariffsnatural gasammonia
So what: Pure input-cost squeeze on farmer margins with no commodity-price tailwind — bearish for US grower net income and a margin headwind for grain-dependent processors.
“the net effect is that the input prices went up quite a bit. There's not a real material response on what you might think of as the output prices” ▶ 7:07
In 2022 the Russia-Ukraine war raised input costs but also lifted commodity prices (especially wheat, sympathetically corn and soybeans) because Black Sea grain exports were cut; the Hormuz case is different because virtually no commodities flow through Hormuz, only energy and fertilizer.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highRussiaUkraineBlack SeawheatcornsoybeansStrait of Hormuz
So what: Don't model the Hormuz event off the 2022 playbook — there is no automatic grain-price hedge for farmers this time, so the net P&L effect on growers is worse.
Higher energy prices transmit into agriculture faster and more directly than fertilizer prices, with diesel costs described as 'really really high right now,' so energy should always be paired with fertilizer when assessing the food-cost shock.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highdieselenergynatural gasfertilizer
So what: Energy is the faster-moving channel into food costs; oil/diesel price relief would ease ag input inflation more quickly than fertilizer relief.
“whenever we talk about fertilizer we should continue to say and energy because diesel costs are really really high right now as well” ▶ 15:31
Fertilizer supply mechanics, dependency, and inelasticity 5
The US imports only ~13% of its nitrogen and ~16% of its phosphate, whereas Brazil imports ~95% of nitrogen and ~72% of phosphate — and BOTH countries import essentially 100% of their potassium (potash).
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnitrogenphosphatepotassiumpotashUnited StatesBrazil
So what: Brazil is far more exposed to a fertilizer trade disruption than the US; potash is the universal import-dependency choke point for both — watch potash producers and Brazilian ag input costs.
“The US imports about 13% of its nitrogen. Brazil imports about 95%” ▶ 5:31
Fertilizer supply is highly inelastic: a new large-scale plant is a multi-year project, existing plants run near full utilization (not at 60% spare capacity), and nitrogen in particular is a near-real-time system locked to seasonal application needs.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfertilizernitrogenammonia
So what: Supply cannot ramp to backfill interrupted shipments quickly, so any prolonged disruption is a durable price tailwind for incumbent producers with available capacity.
“you can't just put in a large scale fertilizer plant in a you know, that's not even a one-year project. That's a big project” ▶ 19:33
Mined nutrients (phosphate, potash — 'dig it out of the ground and grind it up') have somewhat more flexible production than nitrogen, but nitrogen output is essentially fixed and cannot be ramped dramatically.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumphosphatepotashnitrogen
So what: Differentiates producers: mined-nutrient miners have marginally more flex; nitrogen producers benefit most from price spikes since supply is rigid.
Russian fertilizer/ammonia supply out of the Black Sea is partially but not fully restored — some ammonia pipelines and transmission channels were destroyed and have not been rebuilt, and the condition of Odessa's deep-water ports is uncertain.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumRussiaUkraineBlack SeaOdessaammonia
So what: A persistent structural reduction in global ammonia/fertilizer export capacity supports a higher baseline price floor than pre-2022.
“there were a couple of ammonia um pipelines that have not been restored” ▶ 10:06
Rationing and crop-mix shifts are the likely adjustment to tight fertilizer: US growers tend to over-apply nitrogen for a perfect year, and soybeans (a nitrogen-fixing legume) need far less nitrogen, so a squeeze pushes acreage toward soybeans and lighter input crops.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumnitrogensoybeanscornacreage
So what: A fertilizer squeeze is a relative bid for soybean over corn acreage — watch the corn/soybean acreage and price ratio as a fertilizer-stress indicator.
Geopolitics, escalation risk, and the food-security red line 6
Sherrick's biggest red line is not the next crop failing but the conflict escalating beyond a 2-3 party fight: as soon as it spills past US-only headlines into a broader regional war, he becomes 'really really worried' about a prolonged bloody conflict that disrupts energy and food.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumIranMiddle EastStrait of Hormuzenergy
So what: The tail risk to monitor is conflict widening (Gulf oil exporters drawn in), not a single bad harvest — escalation headlines are the trigger to re-risk ag/energy/food trades.
≈ “as soon as it spills out of the the US being the only headlines that we hear about day to day, then I am really really worried” paraphrase
If the Hormuz closure persists another month or two it would materially impact the SOUTHERN hemisphere's next planting cycle (~6 months out), making that the next real shortage-risk window rather than the current northern-hemisphere harvest.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzsouthern hemisphereBrazilArgentina
So what: Sets a concrete monitoring clock: a multi-month Hormuz closure is the catalyst for southern-hemisphere (Brazil/Argentina) input and crop risk in the following cycle.
“If it goes on another month or two, then it begins to really really impact southern hemisphere's next round of planting” ▶ 16:45
Real food-shortage pockets are emerging for the first time in a long time in parts of Africa and the western part of Asia that depend on imported food — driven by both physical scarcity and unaffordable prices ('you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food').
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumAfricaAsiafood securityfood inflation
So what: Localized food-insecurity / social-unrest risk (the 'Arab Spring' analog) is concentrated in import-dependent emerging markets, not developed economies — an EM political-risk signal.
“you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food” ▶ 31:05
For roughly a third of the world's population, a marginal dollar of income changes what they eat tomorrow, whereas in the US food is a low enough share of income that food inflation is barely noticed despite being a big component of the recent 3.8% inflation print.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfood inflationCPIemerging markets
So what: Food inflation's social/political impact is asymmetric and concentrated in poor importing nations — a leading indicator for EM instability, not for US consumer behavior.
“For about a third of the world's population, if you gave them each another dollar, they might change what they eat tomorrow” ▶ 31:45
Iran has already demonstrated willingness to destroy energy infrastructure (including natural gas) of neighbors, and because everything in food production depends on energy, attacks that raise energy costs erode the value/affordability of all downstream food inputs.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumIrannatural gasenergy infrastructureammonia
So what: Energy-infrastructure attacks are the transmission mechanism from geopolitics to food costs (natural gas to ammonia) — a key escalation channel to watch.
Even if the war ended and Hormuz reopened tomorrow, productivity and output would not snap back to the prior path — like a temporary smoker, the system permanently loses some of the gains and resources wasted during the disruption.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumproductivitysupply chains
So what: Reopening is not a full reversal — there's a permanent output 'gap' left on the table, so a peace-deal relief rally may overstate the recovery.
“you don't go all the way back up to the same line” ▶ 37:57
Trade rerouting and depressed US commodity prices 4
Tariffs and geopolitical conflict have already begun 'unspinning' global ag trade — e.g., China buying soybeans from Argentina/Brazil instead of the US, forcing US exporters to backfill more distant markets at much higher transportation cost borne by the seller.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highChinaArgentinaBrazilsoybeansUnited Statestariffs
So what: Structurally bearish for US soybean prices and grower margins; bullish relative positioning for South American exporters — a durable trade-reordering theme.
“when China decides to buy from Argentina and Brazil and not from the US and the US has to then go fill in backfill the rest of the world demand for soybeans to places it didn't used to sell” ▶ 17:59
Lower US commodity prices are likely to PERSIST even if China meets soybean-purchase obligations under trade agreements, because the expensive rerouting of global trade is paid for largely by the seller.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highUnited StatesChinasoybeanscommodity prices
So what: Lean bearish on US grain prices/grower income into the trade-reordering regime even on headline 'China is buying' news.
“lower prices are likely to persist in the US” ▶ 17:39
Sherrick is as worried about depressed US output prices from trade rerouting as he is about next year's crop physically not being produced, since output prices ultimately drive planting decisions.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highcommodity pricesUnited Statesplanting
So what: Reframes the dominant risk from supply-side famine to demand/price-side margin compression for US producers.
“So I'm as worried about that as I am about next year's crop not being able to be produced” ▶ 18:13
Comparative advantage means the US cannot economically replace interrupted fertilizer supply just because it has cheap, sometimes-flared natural gas — artificial trade barriers (tariffs) are 'really sloppy taxing' and natural gas is most valuable in its current uses.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highnatural gasammoniacomparative advantagetariffs
So what: Skeptical of a US fertilizer-onshoring thesis as an economic winner; tariffs raise system-wide costs rather than create durable domestic advantage.
“it's just really sloppy taxing” ▶ 21:39
Second-order effects: steel, equipment, processing chain 4
Steel and aluminum tariffs may matter more to farm economics over time than fertilizer prices because they permanently raise the cost of durable equipment — a combine that cost ~$1 million could cost ~$1.2 million, and that elevated cost lasts the machine's entire useful life.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highsteelaluminumtariffscombineJohn Deerefarm equipment
So what: A long-duration, under-appreciated cost drag on farm capex and margins — bearish for farmer economics and a margin/pricing variable for equipment makers like John Deere.
“a combine that used to cost a million might be a million too because of the increased cost of components” ▶ 35:25
Wheat itself is only ~10-15% of the cost of a loaf of bread, with transportation and packaging each a similar share, so the more processed a food and the slower the economy turns, the more all energy-linked input costs compound into the final consumer price.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highwheatbreadfood inflationpackagingtransportation
So what: Food-price inflation is driven more by energy-linked processing/transport/packaging than by raw grain prices — track energy and logistics, not just crop futures, for consumer food CPI.
“If you take wheat and say per bushel how much of the you know value of a bread a loaf of bread is represented by the input of wheat it's a really small fraction 10 15% maybe” ▶ 36:13
Plastics and other oil-derived inputs (for wrapping, cooling, freezing) mean the food system's energy exposure runs far beyond diesel — the most-processed foods carry the highest embedded energy cost and erode most when energy prices rise.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumplasticsoilenergyfood processing
So what: Highly-processed/packaged food categories are most exposed to energy-cost inflation — relevant for packaged-food company margins.
A smaller US beef herd will keep beef prices elevated until the herd is rebuilt, a dynamic largely separate from the fertilizer/energy shock but additive to food-price stickiness.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highbeefcattle herd
So what: Beef prices stay structurally high on herd-cycle dynamics independent of the Hormuz event — a separate persistent food-inflation component.
“we have a smaller beef herd right now. So beef prices will stay up until we have a bigger beef herd” ▶ 39:57
Named companies and episodic fertilizer trade 4
Nutrien and adjacent fertilizer/ag-input companies should benefit like energy companies from this shock — when competing suppliers are disrupted, the remaining producers gain, delivering at least a couple of strong quarters even if the long-run normalizes.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumNutrienfertilizerag inputs
So what: Direct long idea: fertilizer producers (Nutrien) are positioned for an episodic earnings pop from disrupted competitors — trade the episode, don't marry it.
“Will Nutrient go out of business? No. They'll have a great year.” ▶ 43:59
Fertilizer/ag-input names are 'episodic' beneficiaries — they get a pop when competing suppliers suffer, but the durable economic rents from these episodes ultimately accrue back to land (Ricardo), not to the input companies.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highNutrienfarmlandRicardoag inputs
So what: Frames fertilizer equities as tactical/episodic trades versus farmland as the structural rent-capturing asset — different holding periods and theses.
“these are um what I would call episodic” ▶ 41:29
David Hay (frequent Thoughtful Money guest) flagged Nutrien to his followers as a fertilizer/food play, and the host independently surfaces it as a name to watch into this disruption.
anecdote🗣 host, Adam Taggartconviction: mediumDavid HayNutrien
So what: Names a specific ticker (Nutrien) being circulated as the consensus fertilizer-shock beneficiary among the channel's analysts.
John Deere will not go out of business and won't be permanently impaired by this — at worst a rough year here and there — implying farm-equipment makers are durable holds through the cycle despite the steel/aluminum cost headwind.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumJohn Deerefarm equipment
So what: Equipment makers are framed as cyclical-but-durable; weakness is buyable rather than terminal.
“will John Deere go out of business? No. Will they have a rough year? Maybe now and then” ▶ 43:56
Farmland as the structural inflation-hedge asset 7
Farmland has beaten the S&P 500 over every 10-year period since 1970 with lower drawdown, while being positively correlated with inflation and negatively correlated with equities — the structural reason it 'wins' once episodic events wash out is that land is the most fixed factor of production (Ricardo).
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfarmlandS&P 500inflationRicardoFarmland LP
So what: Core thesis: farmland as a low-drawdown, inflation-hedging, equity-diversifying core holding that historically outperforms equities over rolling decades.
≈ “lower draw down than the S&P any 10-year period since 1970 you'll beat the S&P with farmland investments” paraphrase
The $126 trillion boomer 'great wealth transfer' (closer to 2050 than 2000) is driving an exploding wave of less-informed but highly-motivated demand for farmland as a tax-efficient, inflation-protected, long-term-capital-gains wealth-preservation asset across institutional, SMA, and co-mingled vehicles.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfarmlandwealth transferbaby boomersinstitutional capital
So what: A multi-decade structural demand tailwind ('a dam breaking') for farmland prices and farmland-investment vehicles — early in the cycle per Sherrick.
“we're going to transfer $126 trillion dollars to the next generation. My number one inbound inquiry right now is a less informed but more motivated uh person interested in in farmland investments” ▶ 48:56
US farmland is a ~$4 trillion sector with only ~13% debt (sector-wide leverage), and the trend toward ever-larger farms requires bigger capital bases — pointing to growing institutional financialization of land ownership.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfarmlandleverageinstitutional capital
So what: Low-leverage, large-and-growing addressable market with a financialization runway — structural setup for institutional capital inflows into farmland funds.
“the underleveraged in farmland by owners is only 13% debt in the sector it's only a $4 trillion ion dollar sector too” ▶ 50:34
Farmland prices don't fall even when farmers lose money in a given year because the asset is priced against the expectation of future income, a relationship Sherrick says 'hasn't been broken yet' and won't be in another hundred years.
framework🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfarmlandland values
So what: Farmland's price floor is set by capitalized future income, decoupling it from single-year farm P&L — the resilience case for the asset.
“farmland doesn't go down in value even when you lose money as a farmer because the expectation of the future income is what you're pricing it against” ▶ 50:50
Higher conventional fertilizer prices create a relative cost advantage for organic/sustainable farmland, because organic nitrogen sources (e.g., chicken litter, composting) don't reprice with ammonia — strengthening the case for sustainable farmland funds.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumorganic farmlandsustainable agriculturechicken litterammoniaFarmland LP
So what: Fertilizer-price spikes tilt relative economics toward organic/sustainable operations — a tailwind for sustainably-managed farmland strategies.
“You've created a relative advantage to the other production system” ▶ 46:52
Sherrick will stake his reputation that farmland is worth more in 10 years, but explicitly refuses any short-term price call ('Do I think it will be tomorrow? No') given the high economic uncertainty.
prediction🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highfarmland
So what: High conviction on the decade-out direction, none on near-term timing — farmland is positioned as a patient, long-horizon allocation.
“Do I think farmland will be worth more in 10 years? Absolutely... Do I think it will be tomorrow? No” ▶ 44:19
Sherrick believes events like this and COVID are forcing a structural reappraisal of resilience over pure efficiency, meaning the world may need 'more reserves of everything everywhere' — a permanent drag on growth but a durable bid for resilient real assets like farmland.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumresiliencesupply chainsfarmlandstrategic reserves
So what: A regime shift from efficiency to resilience favors holding excess inventory and real assets — supportive for commodities, reserves, and farmland over the long run.
“maybe that means we need more reserves of everything everywhere” ▶ 27:27
Ag-tech productivity revolution 3
Genetic improvement is the single biggest driver of agricultural productivity — US corn now yields ~250 bushels/acre where it once yielded 100, and (per former Secretary Vilsack) the US grows three times as much food as ~100 years ago with only half the inputs.
data_point🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highgeneticscorn yieldsTom Vilsackagricultural productivity
So what: Relentless yield/genetics gains are the structural reason Sherrick says Malthusian famine calls are always wrong — bullish long-run for food supply and ag-tech/seed innovators.
“that's why you grow 250 bush of corn where you used to only grow 100” ▶ 53:01
Autonomous and 'see-and-spray' technology is rapidly displacing farm labor and chemical use — nearly all tractors are now self-driving, large new sprayers selectively spray only detected weeds, and laser/robotic weeders (Blue River, others) reduce herbicide/pesticide needs, especially in organic systems.
thesis🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: highautonomous tractorssee-and-sprayBlue Riverroboticsherbicidespesticides
So what: Structural ag-tech adoption lowers labor and chemical intensity — relevant for precision-ag and autonomy plays and a secular cost-down for growers.
“All all tractors are essentially self-driving now” ▶ 52:25
Immigration/labor constraints have 'poured gasoline on the fire' of agricultural automation, accelerating robotic harvesting first in high-value, labor-intensive crops like apples and tree nuts.
causal🗣 guest, Bruce Sherrickconviction: mediumlaborimmigrationautomationapplestree nuts
So what: Labor scarcity is a catalyst accelerating ag-automation capex — a demand driver for ag-robotics and harvesting-tech firms.
“gasoline poured on the fire for that development as well” ▶ 54:43
A.I. Mania Creating Twin Bubbles In Stock Prices AND Earnings | New Harbor Financial59 min33 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
New Harbor Financial's Mike Preston and John Lloyd argue the AI rally is a 'twin bubble' in both valuations and earnings, driven by a vertical, ultra-narrow semiconductor melt-up they read as a late-stage blowoff top; they hold ~48% equity, hedge with options, and warn that at historic-extreme valuations the next decade likely delivers near-zero to negative real returns regardless of how real the AI buildout is.
AI twin-bubble thesis (valuation AND earnings) · 6Narrow market & blowoff-top mechanics · 6AI capex, the real economy & recession timing · 5IPO frenzy as late-cycle marker · 4New Harbor positioning & risk discipline · 4Fed, valuations & lost-decade returns outlook · 5Energy, metals & Bitcoin signals · 3
AI twin-bubble thesis (valuation AND earnings) 6
New Harbor believes the AI complex is a 'twin bubble' — both an overvaluation bubble (multiples on inflated forward estimates) and an earnings bubble (the forward earnings themselves are too optimistic) — even though AI is genuinely transformative like the internet.
thesis🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highAIsemiconductorsinternet/dot-com analogy
So what: If both the multiple AND the underlying earnings re-rate together, the downside in AI names compounds; investors should not assume 'real technology' protects valuations.
“We probably have a valuation and an earnings bubble. Doesn't mean these aren't real. They're not transformative, just like the internet was. But we've gotten way ahead of ourselves.” ▶ 0:00
Reported AI-related earnings quality is suspect because of third-party deals and circular financing, so a 'quality of earnings' study would likely reveal that the boom rests partly on non-organic accounting.
causal🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediumAINvidiacircular financing
So what: Circular vendor financing means a single counterparty pullback could cascade through reported revenue; watch for related-party disclosure in AI earnings.
“a lot of these um you know thirdparty deals and and circular financing all this stuff” ▶ 21:08
Large hyperscalers are beginning to rein in AI spend — Microsoft reportedly pulled back on using agentic AI for coding because it was spending too much without showing return on investment — signaling the capex narrative can flip quickly.
data_point🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediumMicrosoftagentic AIcapex
So what: Hyperscaler capex is the demand floor under semis; early signs of spending discipline are a leading indicator that semi revenue estimates could be cut.
“Microsoft um basically ska turn pulled in the reigns on their employee base using u agentic AI for coding you know because they're spending too much” ▶ 21:28
The AI rally shows late-stage bubble behavior: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang merely naming Marvell as a potential next trillion-dollar company sent that stock up ~30% in a day.
anecdote🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highNvidiaJensen HuangMarvell Technology
So what: Price moves driven by CEO soundbites rather than fundamentals are a classic blowoff-phase tell; treat such spikes as sentiment, not value.
“the next trillion dollar company is going to be I think he said Marvel technology ... and the stock went up like 30% I think in a day” ▶ 19:58
The semiconductor ETF SMH roughly tripled (from ~250-300 to over 600) in under two months, a near-vertical move New Harbor compares to the parabolic precious-metals run earlier in the year that then corrected.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston / John Lloyd (guests, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highSMHsemiconductorsprecious metals
So what: Vertical moves 'don't resolve by going sideways' — they tend to draw real pullbacks; SMH is the cleanest expression of the bubble risk to fade or hedge.
“The ETF SMH semiconductor holders went from like 250 or 300 and it shot higher than 600 in a very short period of time” ▶ 6:58
When AI narratives break, analysts reluctantly cut earnings forecasts AND compress multiples simultaneously (e.g. from 30x toward 15-20x), so the same stock gets hit twice — lower multiple on lower earnings.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highAI stocksvaluation multiplesearnings estimates
So what: Models the mechanics of a twin-bubble de-rating; the double hit explains why these unwinds are far larger than a simple multiple compression.
“Maybe it's only worth 20 or maybe 15. Oh, and by the way ... the earnings themselves ... also is being cut.” ▶ 28:44
Narrow market & blowoff-top mechanics 6
2026 YTD S&P 500 gains (~11%) are driven almost entirely by just info-tech (AI/semis) and energy, with financials (XLF stuck near 50) and healthcare/pharma (XLV) lagging — narrowness New Harbor flags as a warning sign.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highS&P 500XLFXLVenergysemiconductors
So what: Without laggard sectors broadening out, the rally is fragile; watch XLF and XLV for confirmation or for the leaders to roll over.
“Really, it's just info and energy. That's it” ▶ 6:39
Mike Preston believes this multi-decade bull cycle ends in a blowoff top rather than a normal correction, a view he only formed in the last year or two after every overvalued pullback was immediately bought in an increasingly vertical V-shaped recovery.
thesis🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highS&P 500bull market cycle
So what: Frames the base case as one final parabolic surge then a major reset — not a buy-the-dip regime indefinitely; position for an eventual cycle top.
“every single pullback, no matter how overvalued, got immediately bought in V-shaped recovery. in every V-shaped recovery was more vertical” ▶ 12:51
Preston's concrete blowoff-top trigger is the S&P 500 gaining roughly 500 points / 7-10% within a few weeks from a genuine breakout (not a V-bottom), and he believes that signal already fired when the index broke 7,000 and ran to ~7,600 in about three weeks.
prediction🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highS&P 500
So what: Gives a falsifiable marker that, by Preston's own framework, the terminal blowoff phase has likely begun; another ~1,000 S&P points is plausible if breadth expands.
“we broke out of the old high at 7,000 went right to 7500 within like 3 weeks we're sitting at 7600 here” ▶ 14:24
Major market tops typically 'fall apart from within' — indices make new highs while internal participation deteriorates and leaders abruptly roll over — so the current narrowness fits a late-top profile.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediummarket breadthS&P 500
So what: Breadth/participation indicators, not price level, are the better top-timing tools; deteriorating internals under new highs is the signal to de-risk.
“if you look at major market tops, they usually start to fall apart from within” ▶ 33:27
The S&P 500 has had 8 consecutive up weeks (~17% rally, 99th percentile) — historically rare (only ~20 times since 1950) — but Lloyd stresses these extremity readings are poor timing tools because forward returns after such streaks have averaged positive and streaks have run as long as 13 weeks.
data_point🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Counters the naive 'overbought = sell' reflex; don't short merely on extension — wait for breadth/weight-of-evidence indicators to break.
≈ “Only 20 times have there been eight consecutive weeks up in the S&P 500” paraphrase
Markets reprice at the margin, not on aggregate flows — like one neighbor selling a house 20% below Zillow resets the whole neighborhood, a small shift from buyers being less eager than sellers can crack the market without any news catalyst.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highpassive investingMike Green
So what: Explains why tops occur 'on exhaustion' with no headline; don't wait for a trigger event to de-risk a stretched, narrow market.
“It just takes on the margin buyers being somewhat less eager than sellers. That's really all it takes.” ▶ 29:22
AI capex, the real economy & recession timing 5
US data center construction spending jumped 28% YoY in April to a record ~$50B annualized, surpassing public transportation construction spending (~$49.9B) for the first time in history.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: highdata centersAI capexUS economy
So what: Quantifies how AI buildout is now a macro-scale demand source; this single line item is propping up GDP and delaying recession.
“data center construction spending has outpaced government transportation spending for the first time in history” ▶ 24:01
Taggart argues a US recession is hard to envision near-term because three spending tailwinds stack: AI capex, large federal deficit spending, and corporate capex juiced by the 'one big beautiful bill' bonus depreciation tax write-offs.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumUS economyAI capexfiscal deficitbonus depreciation
So what: Recession bears may need to push timing out years; the risk is asset-price correction independent of an economic downturn.
“it gets really hard to be able to see a recession happening anytime, you know, in the near future as long as this just tsunami of spending is going on” ▶ 24:25
Recent macro data is firming — two jobs reports showing faster job creation, expanding industrial production, and ISM manufacturing at 54 (highest reading in years) — much of which Taggart ties to AI-driven GDP growth.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumISM manufacturingjobs reportindustrial production
So what: Strong data reduces near-term Fed-easing odds and supports the 'no recession yet' case, but ties economic strength to the fragile AI cycle.
“the ISM manufacturing index, that's up at 54 now. That's the highest reading in years” ▶ 26:02
Lloyd compares AI capex to the dot-com era when IT capex as a share of GDP 'shot up like a rocket ship' then plummeted and stayed depressed for years even as the internet grew more indispensable — implying a similar capex cliff ahead.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highAI capexdot-com bubbleCiscoGDP
So what: Even with a real technology, capex is mean-reverting; the second-order hit to suppliers and GDP when AI spend normalizes is the bigger macro risk.
“it shot up like a rocket ship not unlike what we're seeing right now. And then it plummeted like a rock and stayed down for years” ▶ 27:52
Taggart argues stock prices are more dependent on AI spending than the spending is on stocks: with ~$1T of US AI spend projected this year and ~$1.2T thereafter, if actual spend comes in at only ~60% of projection, AI stocks have to reprice sharply downward.
causal🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumAI capexAI stocks
So what: Gives a quantifiable downside trigger — a capex shortfall to ~60% of plan — to watch as the leading signal for an AI-stock de-rating.
“If that only comes in at 60% of that, the stocks are going to have to repric down.” ▶ 31:12
IPO frenzy as late-cycle marker 4
A wave of gargantuan IPOs/raises is hitting the market — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI/ChatGPT, and a large Google raise (with Berkshire Hathaway reportedly taking a chunk) — which New Harbor reads as classic late-stage insiders cashing in at peak valuations.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggart (host) / Mike Preston (guest)conviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIGoogle/AlphabetBerkshire Hathaway
So what: IPO surges cluster near relative market highs (2000, 2007); the supply wave is a sentiment flag to lower exposure and tighten hedges.
“we've got anthropic coming up. Chat GPT. Um, Google just announced a massive raise and ... Berkshire Hathaway is is taking a chunk of that” ▶ 34:56
SpaceX is set to IPO at ~$135/share for roughly a $2 trillion first-day valuation (~$75B raised, ~3x the next-largest IPO, Saudi Aramco), even as Morningstar reportedly argues fair value is about half that.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highSpaceXSaudi AramcoMorningstar
So what: A $2T day-one valuation that no prior mega-cap reached at IPO signals froth; the Morningstar haircut quantifies the overpricing risk.
“SpaceX comes out with a two trillion market cap on its first day. None of these other guys did that.” ▶ 39:49
Historically the median IPO underperforms badly — down ~10% one year out, ~40% three years out — so playing IPOs requires picking the few outlier winners that drag the average positive, especially dangerous near a market top.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highIPOs
So what: Base rates argue against buying hyped IPOs like SpaceX at the offer; better to wait for a post-IPO valuation reset.
“the median IPO is down 10% ... Three years out, the median IPO stocks down 40%” ▶ 40:51
Taggart says that despite liking SpaceX as a company, he will not buy the IPO and plans to wait, expecting a high probability of acquiring it later at a materially better valuation than the IPO price.
anecdote🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumSpaceXElon Musk
So what: A 'wait for the post-IPO drawdown' stance on the year's marquee offering — a template for disciplined entry on hyped listings.
“I don't think I'm going to buy it for the IPO. I think I'm going to buy and wait” ▶ 41:33
New Harbor positioning & risk discipline 4
New Harbor keeps equity exposure capped at roughly 48% — deliberately lower than peers — and accepts underperforming a vertical market for short periods because they judge this environment too extreme to be fully invested.
framework🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highNew Harbor Financialequity allocation
So what: A concrete late-cycle allocation template: stay ~50% equity and tolerate relative lag to protect against a blowoff-top reversal.
“Our equity exposure has been roughly the same, right around 48%.” ▶ 5:22
Rather than trading in and out of positions on whipsawing indicators, New Harbor adjusts option hedges; they recently sold a July S&P 500 7,000 put at a loss when their breadth indicators flipped bullish, ready to re-add similar protection if signals turn negative.
framework🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highS&P 500put optionsoptions hedging
So what: Shows a hedging-over-rotation approach to manage whipsaw; the dropped put means they are currently less hedged and more bullish-tilted.
≈ “We had a July S&P 500 7,000 put. We sold it. We took a loss on it, but that's the cost of insurance” paraphrase
New Harbor's system relies on a 'weight of the evidence' dashboard of old-school technicals — bullish percent indices (S&P, NASDAQ, NYSE), point-and-figure breadth, relative-strength sector analysis, and moving averages — rather than fundamental valuation, which they note 'hasn't mattered.'
framework🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highbullish percent indexpoint and figurerelative strengthtechnical analysis
So what: Their actionable signals are breadth/technical, not valuation; valuation defines long-term risk, breadth defines tactical positioning.
“old school tools like point and figure charting that measure breath, bullish p bullish percents, relative strength analysis of sectors” ▶ 4:01
Lloyd warns that a turn could be either a benign sector rotation (money leaving AI into laggard value names, as happened early in the year) or a true broad reset, and he sees no obvious catalyst for laggards like financials to suddenly outperform.
prediction🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: lowfinancialsvalue stockssector rotation
So what: The fork between rotation and reset matters for whether to hide in value or de-risk entirely; lack of a financials catalyst tilts toward reset risk.
“if we start to see some of these sectors pick up and broaden out I think it could be more of a rotational event than a than a heavy reset of markets” ▶ 34:05
Fed, valuations & lost-decade returns outlook 5
If incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh signals patience — not hiking rates and betting the current inflationary impulse is transitory — Darius Dale and others think the market could bubble up further from here.
prediction🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin WarshDarius Daleinflation
So what: A dovish Warsh is the macro fuel that could extend the blowoff; Fed commentary on the inflation impulse is the key catalyst to watch.
≈ “with Kevin Worsh coming in ... if Kevin Worsh indicates that he's not going to hike rates ... the market could then bubble up from there” paraphrase ▶ 15:03
Equity valuations are at historic extremes (CAPE, Buffett indicator) that have reliably predicted poor forward returns; Goldman Sachs and John Hussman now project negligible-to-zero, and in Hussman's case negative, annualized returns over the next decade.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host) / John Lloyd (guest)conviction: highCAPEBuffett indicatorGoldman SachsJohn HussmanS&P 500
So what: Even bulge-bracket firms now forecast a 'lost decade'; retirees relying on equity returns should plan around near-zero real returns.
“investors really should expect negligible to like zero or in John Husman's case, even negative um annualized returns over the next ... decade” ▶ 54:53
Lloyd argues many 'cheapest ever' relative-value charts (utilities or financials vs the S&P) are 'chart crimes' — a sector only looks cheap because it is being compared to an S&P that has never been more expensive, not because it is actually cheap.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highutilitiesfinancialsS&P 500relative valuation
So what: Cautions against rotating into 'cheap' laggards as a safe haven — relative cheapness vs an extreme benchmark is not absolute value.
“it's kind of like a a chart crime of sorts because ... you're comparing something to another thing that's never been more expensive” ▶ 33:07
Negative-return 'lost decades' are not random Monte-Carlo coin flips but occur almost predictably from extreme starting valuations, so standard financial-planning tools using historical-average return assumptions systematically overstate retirees' odds of success.
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highMonte Carlo simulationfinancial planningvaluationsRight Capital
So what: Planners and DIY investors should haircut expected returns for the next decade given today's valuations rather than trust default tool assumptions.
“they don't just happen randomly. They happen almost predictably and reliably when we're at very extreme valuations like we are at now” ▶ 49:15
Guardrail (flexible) spending — loosening in good market years and tightening in bad ones — dramatically raises retirement plan success odds versus a rigid inflation-adjusted fixed-withdrawal assumption, which counts running short by even $1 as a 'failure.'
framework🗣 John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: highretirement planningguardrail spendingwithdrawal rate
So what: Adaptive withdrawal rules materially improve plan resilience to sequence-of-returns risk — especially valuable entering a likely low-return decade.
“if you kind of be careful how much you spend and the pattern you spend given what happens in markets you can actually increase your odds of success quite dramatically” ▶ 48:40
Energy, metals & Bitcoin signals 3
Energy stocks had a quiet early-2026 tear (helping drive the narrow S&P gains), boosted by oil prices rising on the prolonged Iran conflict and a Strait of Hormuz closure, but have been roughly flat over the past two months.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston / John Lloyd (guests, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediumenergy sectoroilIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Energy's leadership has stalled; with semis now doing the lifting, an energy re-acceleration would be one way breadth could broaden.
≈ “as oil started ... shooting up because of the the the straight of Hormuz closure um it got even more tailwind” paraphrase
Precious metals are consolidating near their correction-low levels with no change week-over-week — New Harbor frames the metals complex as a trade that currently requires patience after its earlier parabolic run.
data_point🗣 Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)conviction: mediumprecious metalsgold
So what: Metals are in a holding pattern, not a fresh signal; position sizers should expect dead time rather than chase.
“They're kind of floating along the their correction low levels. And so that's a trade that requires patience right now.” ▶ 56:28
Bitcoin has broken through notable price-support levels, opening the question of how much further it could fall — flagged by Taggart as a developing risk to revisit.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart (host)conviction: lowBitcoin
So what: A breakdown through support in Bitcoin can be an early risk-off / liquidity tell for speculative assets broadly; worth monitoring.
“we actually have seen Bitcoin break through some some pretty notable price support levels” ▶ 56:44

Anthony Pompliano 3 episode(s) · 121 insights

The Biggest Money-Making Opportunity Since Bitcoin?80 min39 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Crypto investor Andrew Kang argues humanoid robots are a 3-5 year (not 20-50 year) reality addressing the ~$40-60T human-labor market, and pitches Robo Strategy — a publicly traded, permanent-capital closed-end venture fund (trading 300-400% above NAV) — as a MicroStrategy-style vehicle to compound capital into private robotics leaders like Figure AI, Apptronik, Standard Bots and Path Robotics.
Humanoid TAM & unit economics · 6Why VCs missed it / contrarian edge · 4Figure AI thesis · 4US vs China robotics · 5Regulation & national security tailwind · 3General-purpose vs specialized & training data · 4Robo Strategy: NAV-premium vehicle & portfolio holdings · 9Labor displacement & timeline · 4
Humanoid TAM & unit economics 6
Andrew Kang sizes the humanoid robot opportunity against the total human-labor market, which he pegs at roughly 50% of GDP, or $40-60 trillion, framing humanoids as a potential multi-trillion-dollar revenue / tens-of-trillions market-cap category.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotslabor marketGDP
So what: Sets the bull-case ceiling that underwrites the entire Robo Strategy thesis; anchors how aggressively to size robotics exposure.
“It's something like 50% of GDP. It's, you know, some people put it at $40 trillion, $60 trillion.” ▶ 4:08
Kang estimates an all-in humanoid labor cost of roughly $2/hour (a one-time ~$50,000 robot amortized over multiple years plus electricity and maintenance) versus $35-40/hour fully loaded for a U.S. worker — and argues $2/hour even undercuts low-wage labor in India, the Philippines or Indonesia.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotslabor costIndiaPhilippinesIndonesia
So what: If the cost math holds, adoption is a 'no-brainer' globally, supporting a demand curve measured in millions to tens of millions of units.
“when I look at it from a per hour basis, right, ... that math gets you down to around $2 per hour” ▶ 5:23
Kang builds a bottoms-up revenue ladder for a single humanoid maker: 100K units at $50K = ~$5B revenue; 1M units = $50B; tens of millions = ~$500B — noting Amazon and Walmart each employ millions, so a million robots is 'really nothing.'
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotsAmazonWalmart
So what: Gives a concrete unit-to-revenue scaling model to value individual robot makers as they ramp production.
Kang argues humanoids trigger a Jevons-paradox demand expansion: lowering the cost of labor makes previously uneconomical tasks viable, growing the total market rather than just substituting existing labor.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumhumanoid robotsJevons paradoxlabor market
So what: Implies TAM estimates based on current labor spend understate the opportunity — a reason to fade skeptics anchored to today's labor budget.
Kang draws an Apple analogy to argue investors chronically underprice transformative tech jumps: pre-iPhone (2006) Apple was a ~$50B company and reaching $3T would have seemed absurd, yet that is where it landed.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highAppleiPhone
So what: Reframes 'the TAM is too big to be real' objection as a recurring failure mode, supporting outsized position sizing in category leaders.
Kang notes annual global robot installations are only around 500,000 units, a large chunk in China and a very small fraction in America despite extensive U.S. physical labor — implying a vast underpenetrated installed base.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highroboticsChinaUnited States
So what: Quantifies how early the adoption curve is; the gap between today's 500K/yr and the multi-million unit thesis is the runway.
“if you look at just all the amount of robots that are installed on an annual basis right now it's around 500,000 globally” ▶ 50:26
Why VCs missed it / contrarian edge 4
When Kang first looked at Figure AI in late 2023, every VC in his traditional network told him not to invest, because robotics historically produced few venture-scale winners due to long, expensive hardware iteration cycles and messy deployment.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIventure capitalrobotics
So what: The consensus 'no' is the source of the asymmetry; mispricing exists precisely because incumbent VCs stayed in their comfort zone.
“All of them told me not to invest.” ▶ 9:38
Kang's core contrarian insight is that physical-AI / physical intelligence solves the historical robotics bottleneck (intelligence), which collapses the deployment-cost problem that made past robotics uninvestable — a structural change most VCs failed to price.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highphysical intelligenceAIrobotics
So what: Identifies the specific catalyst (smart robots cut deployment cost, often larger than the robot's own cost) that re-rates the whole sector.
Kang took comfort, not alarm, from being offered Figure AI access via four stacked SPVs — initially fearing he was getting ripped off, then concluding the top VCs' absence reflected thesis-discomfort rather than a flawed deal.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumFigure AISPVventure capital
So what: A read on how to interpret unusually easy access to a hot deal: it can signal a market-wide blind spot rather than adverse selection.
Kang expects robotics to replicate the OpenAI/Anthropic vertical-takeoff dynamic, where simply concentrating capital in the top few AI companies a few years ago would have outperformed the best seed funds in the world.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicroboticsventure capital
So what: Argues the winning strategy is concentration in category leaders, not diversified seed bets — the explicit playbook for Robo Strategy.
“If you had just put all your money into the top few AI companies a few years ago, you would have outperformed the best seed funds in the world.” ▶ 52:59
Figure AI thesis 4
Kang scaled his personal Figure AI position from an initial $1M to $5M and eventually to roughly $19M, built entirely on research into founder Brett Adcock's track record and team before he had ever spoken to Adcock directly.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIBrett Adcock
So what: Signals an eight-figure conviction bet on Figure as the lead U.S. humanoid name and the anchor holding of Robo Strategy.
“I actually wanted to increase you know my initial investment from a million to 5 million eventually to around 19 million” ▶ 11:26
Kang rates founder Brett Adcock's prior companies — recruiting firm Vettery and eVTOL maker Archer (taken public via SPAC, ~$5-10B) — as the differentiator: proven talent-attraction plus a demonstrated ability to commercialize wholly new complex machinery.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIBrett AdcockVetteryArcher Aviation
So what: Frames founder pattern-matching (commercialization track record, not just PhD talent) as the key underwriting variable in humanoids.
Kang cites Figure AI's demo of robots autonomously sorting packages — first 8 hours, then extended across multiple robots to run roughly nine days straight — as a leading proof of autonomous capability few competitors have matched.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIautonomy
So what: A concrete autonomy benchmark to gauge which humanoid makers have real, deployable capability versus staged demos.
Kang likens humanoid robotics to the drone industry, where teams achieve technical breakthroughs but struggle to commercialize — and argues the winning humanoid teams now pair deep technical talent with proven business-building experience.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumhumanoid robotsdronesFigure AI
So what: A screening lens: weight commercial/operating experience alongside research talent when picking robotics winners.
US vs China robotics 5
Kang evaluates robotics companies on three competencies — high-rate manufacturing, hardware design, and AI capability — placing China ahead on manufacturing, Figure and Tesla as U.S. leaders in hardware design, and U.S. labs ahead on physical AI.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highChinaTeslaFigure AIrobotics
So what: A reusable scorecard for ranking robotics names across geographies and stages.
“I would evaluate robotics companies on three competencies. Uh one is their ability to execute on high rate manufacturing. Second one is their hardware design. Uh and the third one is their AI capabilities.” ▶ 16:46
Kang argues the U.S. holds a meaningful, underappreciated lead in physical intelligence (the 'robot brain'), and that without it a robot is 'pretty useless' — potentially making Chinese hardware makers dependent on U.S. physical-AI models.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumUnited StatesChinaphysical intelligence
So what: Locates the durable U.S. moat in AI rather than hardware, where China dominates — directs capital toward robot-brain leaders.
Kang contends technology leadership does not equal value capture: BYD outsells Tesla in cars at far lower margins and roughly one-tenth to one-twentieth of Tesla's market cap, a pattern he expects to repeat in Chinese robotics due to hyper-competition and government-encouraged crowding.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highBYDTeslaChinaEVs
So what: Warns against equating Chinese robot demos with shareholder returns; favors concentrated U.S. champions for equity upside.
“They sell more cars than Tesla, but their market cap is, you know, onetenth 120th of Tesla” ▶ 19:18
Kang positions Unitree's G1 as excellent for research/entertainment (backflips, dancing) but unable to lift 30 lb payloads in a factory, illustrating that Chinese hardware breadth doesn't yet equal industrial-grade durability.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumUnitreeG1China
So what: Differentiates demo-grade from work-grade hardware — a caution against pricing entertainment robots as industrial threats.
Kang notes China's circular JV model — local governments co-found robotics research/data-collection centers that then purchase robots from the partner company — inflates apparent sales and accelerates IP leakage across ~100+ competing firms, compressing margins.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumChinaroboticsjoint ventures
So what: Explains why Chinese robotics revenue and innovation may not translate into defensible, high-margin equity winners.
Regulation & national security tailwind 3
Kang argues robots — devices that 'see and hear everything' and are packed with telecom hardware — will be treated like Chinese EVs and Huawei/TikTok: subject to bans and tariffs that effectively wall off the U.S. market for domestic makers.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highChinaHuaweiTikTokEVsnational security
So what: A protectionist moat: U.S. robotics names may face little Chinese competition domestically regardless of Chinese tech quality.
“I have a hard time seeing the American government allowing, you know, robots from other nations be the dominant player in America.” ▶ 24:37
Kang references pending bills that would explicitly ban robots from certain countries — first from purchase by federally funded organizations, with the concept expected to expand to everyday life — citing the Biden-era 100% tariff on Chinese EVs as precedent.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumUnited StatesChinaregulationEVs
So what: A concrete regulatory catalyst to watch; passage would directly de-risk U.S. robotics demand.
Kang advocates direct U.S. government investment in robotics companies (as done with Intel), plus favorable loans and trade-education programs, arguing U.S. policy currently lags and should be 'as aggressive as possible.'
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumUnited StatesIntelgovernment policyrobotics
So what: Government capital and reshoring incentives are a potential demand and funding tailwind for domestic robotics equities.
General-purpose vs specialized & training data 4
Kang argues general-purpose humanoids win on unit economics despite per-task inefficiency — mass production at smartphone-like scale can make them ~80% cheaper than application-specific robots — using the GPU-vs-ASIC analogy where both are trillion-dollar markets.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotsGPUASIC
So what: Supports betting on general-purpose humanoid platforms as the larger pool, while specialized robots remain viable niches.
Kang disclosed an investment in Path Robotics (specialized welding robots) as evidence specialized robotics remains a 'humongous' market alongside general-purpose humanoids, not a loser-take-nothing bet.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumPath Roboticsweldingrobotics
So what: Names a portfolio holding and signals the fund spans both general and specialized robotics.
Kang says video-generation 'world models' trained on internet video (e.g. Sora, China's open-source Wan) now serve as backbones for robot foundation models by teaching physics and object interaction, sharply reducing the volume of bespoke robot-collected data previously thought necessary.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumworld modelsSoraWanrobot foundation models
So what: Undercuts the 'robotics has no internet-scale data' bear case and shortens timelines — bullish for model-led robotics labs.
Kang highlights that bespoke training data is still needed for tasks not recorded online (e.g. specific factory processes), referencing data-collection schemes like camera-equipped workers offering free home cleaning in exchange for the data.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumtraining datarobotics
So what: Proprietary task/environment data is a residual moat; watch who controls the best real-world data pipelines.
Robo Strategy: NAV-premium vehicle & portfolio holdings 9
Kang structured Robo Strategy as a publicly traded closed-end fund (permanent capital, management fee only, no carry under U.S. rules) because illiquid private robotics holdings rule out an ETF (capped ~15% private assets) and private raises would top out around $1-2B — too small for the opportunity.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highRobo Strategyclosed-end fundETFventure capital
So what: Explains the vehicle's structural advantages — permanent capital and public-scale fundraising — central to the bull case for the stock.
Kang says Robo Strategy trades at a premium sometimes 300-400% above NAV and uses 'premium crystallization': issuing shares at the premium is accretive — e.g. $100 of equity at 3x NAV issuing 10% new shares raises per-share value ~18% net of dilution, compounding over time.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highRobo StrategyNAV premiumMicroStrategy
So what: The core value-compounding mechanism; understanding it is essential to underwriting the stock's premium rather than fearing it.
“if I have, you know, $100 of robotics equity in my vehicle and it's trading at a 3x nav and I issue 10% new shares ... my per share value actually goes up 18%” ▶ 1:03:16
Kang argues MicroStrategy's NAV-per-share rose from ~$2-4 to ~$150 mainly via premium-crystallized share issuance, not Bitcoin appreciation — noting MSTR's ~$75K average Bitcoin cost is now near or above spot (~$71K), so its BTC is roughly flat-to-underwater.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoinMetaplanet
So what: Reframes MSTR's returns as a capital-markets engine, the template Robo Strategy intends to replicate for robotics; also a notably bearish-leaning BTC price marker (~$71K).
“If you look at their average Bitcoin acquisition cost, it's around 75,000. ... Bitcoin is like what 71,000 today. And so they've actually lost money on their on their Bitcoin holdings.” ▶ 1:04:47
Kang compares the model to public-to-private rollups like Constellation Software, Transdigm and Waste Management, where ~80-90% of growth comes from M&A arbitrage — buying private assets at 3-8x and re-rating them to a 15-40x public multiple.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highConstellation SoftwareTransdigmWaste Management
So what: Grounds the 'public cost of capital, private acquisition prices' arbitrage in proven compounders, validating the strategy's mechanics.
Kang deliberately left all Robo Strategy shares unlocked from day one — rejecting the common 90% lockup — arguing lockups artificially inflate price via constrained supply but guarantee a later sell-off when supply unlocks, killing momentum.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highRobo StrategylockupsIPO
So what: A signal of structural confidence and a differentiator versus locked-up peers; reduces the overhang risk investors usually fear.
≈ “why don't we just do the opposite of that right like just rip off the band-aid on the first day unlock all the shares” paraphrase
Kang predicts the public-venture-fund model will proliferate — several such funds went public earlier in the year and trade at premiums to NAV — but claims most haven't solved how to recycle the premium accretively, his stated differentiator.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumventure capitalclosed-end fundsRobo Strategy
So what: Flags a new asset-class trend (listed VC) and the execution variable — accretive premium recycling — that separates winners from copycats.
Kang names Apptronik as a top fund holding: a 9+ year humanoid 'OG' with proprietary actuator technology that previously built prototypes for leading humanoid companies, recently hired Waymo's ex-CPO and Amazon executives, and partners with Google DeepMind on the robot brain.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highApptronikGoogle DeepMindWaymoAmazon
So what: Identifies a specific large position and its differentiators (actuators, deep-mind partnership) for those tracking the portfolio.
Kang highlights Standard Bots as the premier and only reasonably sized U.S. industrial-arm (cobot) manufacturer, also building a semi-wheeled humanoid, with an AI-native programming approach (natural-language control, torque sensing) versus legacy Fanuc/Yaskawa programming.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highStandard BotsFanucYaskawacobots
So what: Names a reshoring-leveraged holding and the legacy incumbents (Fanuc, Yaskawa) it aims to disrupt.
