Kang flags actuators — roughly 30-50% of a humanoid's bill-of-materials cost and an area that hasn't meaningfully evolved in ~50 years — as a high-potential supply-chain investment niche, citing Dyna Robotics among names building both hardware and robot-brain research.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumactuatorsDyna Roboticssupply chain
So what: Points investors to component-level robotics plays where innovation is overdue and value can accrue outside the humanoid OEMs.
≈ “if you look at actuators, for example, right, they make up around 30 to 50% of the bom cost of of a humanoid. And that space really hasn't significantly evolved for 50 years.” paraphrase
Labor displacement & timeline 4
Kang predicts humanoids reach everyday life in roughly 3-5 years (not 20-50), explicitly rejecting the long-horizon framing and citing recursive self-improvement — robots building robots (Tesla Optimus handling batteries) and AI models like 'opus 4.8' training robot AI — as the accelerant.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotsTesla OptimusAI
So what: An aggressive timeline that, if right, compresses the window to build exposure before the re-rating.
“It's not a 50-year thing. It's not a 20-year thing. This is probably more so like a 3 to 5 year thing” ▶ 1:57
Kang breaks from sugarcoating tech CEOs, arguing AI replaces both cognitive and physical labor simultaneously so there is no higher 'cognitive stack' for displaced workers to climb, making universal basic income a likely necessity.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highAIlabor displacementUBI
So what: Frames mass labor displacement as a base case and UBI as policy response — relevant for macro, fiscal and consumer positioning.
“now with AI basically replacing the complete cognitive ability and physical ability of a of a human there there's no place to kind of expand out to” ▶ 47:21
Kang argues blue-collar / physical-labor jobs likely outnumber white-collar jobs globally, so hardware AI's displacement impact may be as large as or larger than software AI's — a swath he says people in tech bubbles underestimate.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumAIblue-collar laborwhite-collar labor
So what: Suggests the physical-AI / robotics displacement TAM rivals the more-hyped software-AI one.
Kang treats Amazon as a robotics Trojan horse — ~1.5M human employees alongside ~750K robots already — predicting robots eventually outnumber humans there, with non-humanoid application-specific robots adopted earlier and humanoids as the last but biggest market.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highAmazonroboticshumanoid robots
So what: Concrete adoption datapoint and sequencing view: specialized robots first, humanoids as the largest eventual prize.
Why Is Bitcoin CRASHING?!58 min45 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jordi Visser argues Bitcoin's 50% drawdown is a 'rotation' within a bear-market regime (price below 200-day MA), not a structural collapse, and that the bigger 2H story is a rotation out of AI hardware/chips into the application layer — specifically 'human software' (Eli Lilly/peptides) and 'financial guardrails' (crypto/tokenization). He frames Bitcoin as a long-duration durability holding and a direct play on AI agents overtaking humans in commerce, while flagging real bottleneck/revenue risk for the generalist AI model layer.
Bitcoin regime: bear-market technicals & the rotation thesis · 7Positioning, conviction & portfolio construction · 6Volatility regime: BTC vs. single-stock dispersion · 3AI capex, bottlenecks & the model-layer revenue problem · 7LLM commoditization & specialized vs. generalist models · 4Eli Lilly, peptides & 'human software' as the next leg · 9Macro: rates, liquidity, debt & geopolitical bottlenecks · 4Bitcoin & crypto as the financial layer of the AI-agent economy · 5
Bitcoin regime: bear-market technicals & the rotation thesis 7
Bitcoin is down ~50% from its all-time high and remains in a bear-market regime because it failed at the 200-day moving average a few weeks ago and turned lower, so Jordi Visser will not buy aggressively until it bases and reclaims a long-term moving average.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoin200-day moving average
So what: Treat the 200-day (and 200-week) MA reclaim as the buy trigger; until then, expect continued slow bleed rather than a bottom.
“we failed at the 200 day moving average a few weeks ago. We started to go down again all while the stock market was going up nine weeks in a row.” ▶ 1:22
Bitcoin has decoupled from the stock market — it fell ~50% while the S&P rose nine weeks in a row — which Visser views as a necessary precondition for crypto's eventual wealth transfer out of the fiat system, even though near-term it removes equity-beta support.
causal🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoinS&P 500stock market
So what: Don't expect equity strength to rescue BTC near term; the decoupling is structurally bullish for Visser's long thesis but bearish for short-term tape support.
“the good news is Bitcoin's not correlated to the stock market anymore. The bad news is uh it's down 50% off the highs.” ▶ 1:30
Visser sees the market entering a 3-to-6 month 'rotation' where capital moves away from the AI-hardware winners (chips/memory/infrastructure) toward laggard groups, and he is positioning for software — but not traditional SaaS — to catch a bid.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highAI hardwaresoftwareSaaSMicron
So what: Rotate exposure out of crowded chip/memory leaders into the application layer over the next 1-2 quarters.
“I believe we're at a rotation point that's going to last for at least the next 3 to 6 months.” ▶ 2:36
Bitcoin has been 'lumped in' with AI-adjacent equities (crypto miners, cyber, AI-software names) rather than trading as pure software, which Visser cites as a reason its selloff tracked the broader risk complex.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoinBitcoin minerscyber stocksAI software
So what: Watch whether BTC starts trading on its own narrative vs. as an AI-risk proxy; the mischaracterization may unwind as rotation plays out.
Visser dismisses the four-year halving cycle as having no predictive value, noting this is the first 'four-year cycle' where stocks rose while Bitcoin fell, and reframes the relevant long-term level as the 200-week (~4-year) moving average instead.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinfour-year cycle200-week moving average
So what: Stop anchoring entries to the halving calendar; use the 200-week MA as the durable trend reference instead.
“the 200WE moving average is a really important thing... that's kind of a 4-year moving average. So, if the four-year cycle is this, it's fine.” ▶ 5:05
Visser explicitly flags the 60,000 level as a downside zone he is willing to let Bitcoin break, saying it could trade below that without changing his long-term stance.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: 60k is not a defended level in his framework; don't assume it holds as support.
“Let it break through the 60,000 level. Maybe it goes down even below that. I don't really care.” ▶ 3:15
Visser argues prior textbook 'sell-the-news' events — the spot-ETF launch and the US government endorsing crypto plus a presidential memecoin in 2024-25 — were instead bought, and that the real distribution has been OG holders churning out since 2024.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoinspot Bitcoin ETFmemecoinOG holders
So what: The overhang is long-time-holder distribution, not a single catalyst; wait for BTC to rise on bad news as the all-clear signal.
“I do think there's been churn that's been happening now since 2024... until the charts start giving you hope and you get a it going higher on bad news type thing, I'm not going to get too into it.” ▶ 6:22
Positioning, conviction & portfolio construction 6
Visser says he has never sold a single Bitcoin and won't, treating it as a multi-decade durability holding, but he actively trades MicroStrategy in and out repeatedly rather than holding it.
anecdote🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinMicroStrategy
So what: Distinguish core BTC (hold) from the levered equity proxy (trade); MSTR is his tactical vehicle, not a buy-and-hold.
“I've never sold a single one in it, single Bitcoin. I have traded Micro Strategy many many times. Got in, got out, got in, got out.” ▶ 8:08
Visser is accumulating Ethereum throughout the drawdown as a bet on crypto network effects being the dominant story over the next 12 months, treating ETH as a proxy for the broader ecosystem rather than a standalone Bitcoin substitute.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumEthereumBitcoin
So what: He is adding ETH on weakness as an ecosystem/network-effect play — a notable tilt for a Bitcoin-centric audience.
“I've been buying Ethereum during this whole drop. So, I'm happy it's going down because I'm putting bits and pieces in.” ▶ 8:24
Visser recommends a 2-3% Bitcoin allocation as appropriate for essentially any investor regardless of belief, calling both a 100% BTC concentration and a sub-1% allocation 'very stupid'.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A concrete sizing rule: 2-3% BTC as a portfolio resilience sleeve, not a max-conviction bet.
“I believe that it should be at this point in everyone's portfolio at least 2 to 3% regardless of what they believe in.” ▶ 7:52
Visser built an equal-weighted index of 40 crypto-ecosystem names (34 tokens, 6 public stocks) that is highly correlated to Bitcoin even when Bitcoin is excluded, which he reads as confirmation that Bitcoin is 'the S&P 500 of crypto' and the ecosystem is behaving as expected.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoincrypto tokensindex
So what: Crypto remains a single high-beta factor; diversifying across tokens does not meaningfully reduce BTC correlation.
“when I create an equal weight of 40 separate companies, 34 of them being cryptocurrencies or tokens... magically it's extremely correlated to Bitcoin, even when you don't include Bitcoin in it.” ▶ 9:38
Visser frames Bitcoin as a multi-generational 'durability' asset using the Patek Philippe analogy and holds a strategic Bitcoin reserve for his children, prioritizing 50-60 year resilience over short-term return.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinPatek Philippe
So what: His BTC thesis is duration/inheritance-driven, not cycle-timing; explains why drawdowns don't shake him out.
“I don't know of an asset more than Bitcoin that people have confidence in over some very long period of time, 50 years, 60 years, is going to continue to do that.” ▶ 13:00
Visser acknowledges Bitcoin going to zero is a non-trivial part of the outcome distribution, but assigns quantum-computing destroying it a very low probability, and holds BTC small enough that a total loss would not be life-changing.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoinquantum computing
So what: Even the bull sizes BTC as a tail-aware bet; quantum is a known but low-probability kill risk on his radar.
“Do I think Bitcoin might go to zero? Sure. It's part of the distribution of outcomes.” ▶ 7:25
Volatility regime: BTC vs. single-stock dispersion 3
Bitcoin's 50% crash has occurred with collapsing volatility — bleeding ~2% per week rather than 15% single-day drops — which Visser argues makes BTC newly acceptable as an institutional asset by removing its 'too volatile' objection.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Falling BTC realized vol is a structural positive for institutional adoption even amid the price decline.
“this 50% crash has occurred with volatility collapsing... Bitcoin's bleeding like 2% a week.” ▶ 18:14
Leading individual stocks are moving 8-10% per day and single-stock-to-index volatility ratios are at the highest ever recorded, which Visser attributes to retail/zero-DTE option flow and reads as the market training itself for parabolas and speed-crashes.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highzero-DTE optionssingle stocksMicron
So what: Equity single-name vol now exceeds BTC's; expect more parabolic moves and fast crashes driven by 0DTE/retail flow.
“every day we're watching the leading stocks in the stock market move 8 to 10% a day... the highest volatility relative to index vault that we've ever seen.” ▶ 18:31
Visser argues AI is compressing market time so that former four-year crypto cycles may now run as ~9-month cycles, meaning bull and bear markets occur within the same parabolic-and-crash price action.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumBitcoinMicronAI
So what: Shorten cycle-timing assumptions; volatility and round-trips will happen far faster than historical cadence implies.
“maybe these aren't four-year cycles maybe these are now 9mon cycles and we've compress the time and that's what a bubble is. It's price versus time.” ▶ 19:48
AI capex, bottlenecks & the model-layer revenue problem 7
Visser explicitly says this is NOT a stock-market bubble but a sign that AI requires enormous capital now, citing the wave of IPOs, Google's $85B raise, and Berkshire taking a $10B stake in a company up 120% YoY as signals the market is underestimating.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highGoogleBerkshire HathawayIPOsAI
So what: Read the IPO/large-raise wave as capital formation for AI buildout, not late-cycle froth; Berkshire's participation is a tell.
“I think these are a sign of something important. It's not a stock market bubble to me, but it is a sign that people need a lot of capital right now for what is happening with inside the AI world.” ▶ 5:28
The cost of a 1-gigawatt data center has inflated from ~$50B to $60-80B in roughly nine months, with Jensen Huang guiding to $80-100B soon, illustrating severe AI infrastructure bottleneck inflation.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highNvidiaJensen Huangdata centersIBM
So what: Datacenter capex per gigawatt is inflating ~60-100% in under a year — a margin and financing risk for the buildout.
“a data center for 1 gawatt has gone from 50 billion he was saying it's 60 to 80 and Jensen Yuang said in Computex this weekend it'll very quickly be 80 to 100 billion.” ▶ 23:27
Of Jensen Huang's five-layer AI stack (chips, energy, infrastructure, models, applications), only chips/energy/infrastructure are currently monetized, and Visser warns that at projected per-gigawatt costs the revenues may not arrive fast enough to justify the spend.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highNvidiaJensen HuangIBMAI applications
So what: The model and application layers are the unmonetized weak link; revenue timing is the key risk to the whole capex cycle.
“The only thing that's been monetized or the only thing making money are the chips, the energy, and the infrastructure. There's a problem with that eventually.” ▶ 22:40
Visser argues generalist/foundation models face structural margin pressure: DeepSeek competes directly at the model layer, enterprise adoption of generalist models may not materialize, and he doubts a generalist model can sustainably make money.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highDeepSeekgeneralist modelsAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Be skeptical of generalist-model economics; the edge accrues to specialized/proprietary-data models.
“I'm not sure a generalist model anymore you're going to be able to make the money. I don't think the adoption is going to happen on the enterprise side.” ▶ 24:08
Visser cautions that Anthropic's quoted ~$44B figure is ARR (annualized run-rate), not realized revenue, and that parabolic AI growth can break quickly if it tweaks even for a month — citing OpenAI's past year as the cautionary example.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIARR
So what: Discount headline AI ARR numbers; parabolic run-rates are fragile and a single soft month can repriced the whole narrative.
“when we talk about enthropic and we're like it's 44 it's not 44 billion that's the ARR... going like this for 3 months does not ensure you'll be doing that for another 3 years.” ▶ 30:08
Enterprises (Amazon, JP Morgan, Uber) are reportedly blowing through full-year AI token budgets in a single quarter and are now actively engineering token efficiency, which compresses per-customer/per-token revenue for model providers even as aggregate revenue grows.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highAmazonJP MorganUberAnthropictokens
So what: Per-token monetization is eroding as customers optimize; aggregate provider growth masks deteriorating unit economics.
“They're all reporting that they blew through their token budgets, you know, in a single quarter for the entire year.” ▶ 26:57
Visser cites the 'edge' (on-device/edge inference) as the structural bearish argument for all model providers, arguing edge capability is arriving sooner than expected and undermining the centralized data-center borrowing-and-buildout model.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumedge computingmodel providersdata centers
So what: Edge inference is a medium-term threat to centralized model-provider economics; watch the timeline compress.
“the bearish argument for all of the model providers is eventually going to be the edge. So the problem with the edge is the edge is coming sooner than what people think.” ▶ 29:47
LLM commoditization & specialized vs. generalist models 4
Visser argues LLMs are now commoditized: he finds Claude and ChatGPT 'indistinguishable' in capability and has migrated his primary use to GPT-5.5 (via Codex/Cursor) because it is a better brainstorming partner that anticipates his intent, not because of raw model superiority.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highAnthropicClaudeOpenAIChatGPTGPT-5.5CodexCursor
So what: Differentiation among frontier LLMs is shifting from capability to fit/UX; pricing power at the model layer is weakening.
“the LLMs are now commoditized... Claude and Chat GPT they're indistinguishable to me in terms of now being able to use them.” ▶ 35:16
Generalist model providers are effectively conceding the specialized-workflow market by spinning up internal verticals and JVs (biotech teams, PE accounting rollups), an admission that their generalist models can't win narrow domains like finance and healthcare.
causal🗣 hostconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicgeneralist modelsfinancehealthcare
So what: Vertical/specialized AI with proprietary data is the durable moat; generalist providers are pivoting to capture it via JVs.
“they're admitting it because what are they doing? They're creating JVS. They're creating internal teams.” ▶ 28:52
Enterprises are testing replacement of paid frontier models with fine-tuned open-source models (e.g. one firm moving from Claude to DeepSeek V4) to cut costs while claiming ~90%+ performance, a dynamic to watch over the next 30-60 days.
prediction🗣 hostconviction: mediumDeepSeekClaudeopen-source models
So what: Open-source/fine-tuned substitution is an emerging cost-driven threat to paid-model revenue; near-term inflection to monitor.
“they have completely uh eradicated the use of cloud internally and they've gone to Deep Seek V4.” ▶ 32:34
Both speakers name XAI as underrated for its ability to come from nowhere and gain adoption, alongside continued strength for Anthropic and OpenAI, framing AI as a pie-expanding rather than zero-sum market.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumxAIAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Don't treat the frontier-model race as zero-sum; xAI is a sleeper to watch.
Eli Lilly, peptides & 'human software' as the next leg 9
Visser predicts Eli Lilly has a chance to become the largest company in the world and the #1 AI company within 5 years because it is building a specialized model on 150 years of proprietary metabolic-disease data plus a 1,000-GPU/1,000-Blackwell in-house data center.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highEli LillyNvidiaBlackwellGPU
So what: Highest-conviction single-name call in the episode: Eli Lilly as the prototypical proprietary-data 'human software' AI winner.
“Eli Liy... has a chance to be the largest company in the world and the number one AI company in the world within 5 years is because they're building a specialized model.” ▶ 24:21
Eli Lilly's advantage is that it doesn't depend on third-party tokens — it monetizes directly by selling better drugs and making FDA approval more efficient — which Visser contrasts favorably against unmonetized generalist AI bets.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highEli LillyFDAtokens
So what: Favor AI plays with direct revenue and owned compute over those dependent on external token economics.
“if a drug company can make their entire process of getting the FDA approval efficient and at the same time they can benefit by selling better drugs well they're getting revenue directly.” ▶ 25:24
Eli Lilly recently stopped disclosing its phase-one trials — a defensive shift Visser reads as protecting IP from AI-accelerated competitive catch-up rather than serving investors.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumEli Lillyphase one trialsIP
So what: A signal that pharma IP defense is now an AI-competition issue; reduced disclosure is a deliberate moat-protection move.
“Eli Lilly stopped disclosing phase one trials they're working on... because it's much easier to catch up to IP if you have AI.” ▶ 45:44
Visser identifies the obesity TAM at $200-300B, largely untapped due to cost and lack of insurance coverage, with Eli Lilly working on single annual treatments and pill (not shot) formats to expand access.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highEli LillyobesityGLP-1peptides
So what: Massive under-penetrated TAM; cheaper/less-frequent dosing is the unlock that drives the next growth leg.
“The TAM market for obesity in the in the world is between 200 and $300 billion... very little of it right now is being accessed because of the cost.” ▶ 46:11
Visser frames peptides as the 'API key for the human body' — receptors targeted to make the body act differently — and argues GLP-1 'side effects' (reduced addiction, etc.) are actually new monetizable indications Eli Lilly can investigate.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highpeptidesGLP-1Eli Lilly
So what: Each peptide is multi-indication optionality; 'side effects' are pipeline expansion, widening the TAM well beyond weight loss.
“peptides are the API key for the human body” ▶ 40:46
GLP-1 drugs reducing addiction/risk-taking is cited via the anecdote that some hedge funds barred employees from taking GLP-1s because it dampened risk-taking, pointing to a large potential drug/alcohol-addiction treatment market.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: mediumGLP-1hedge fundsaddiction
So what: Addiction treatment is a large second-order GLP-1 market; watch for recovery-center/rehab commercialization.
≈ “some of the hedge funds on Wall Street were requiring people to not take GOP1 because it took away some of the risk-taking genes” paraphrase
Eli Lilly is making strategic acquisitions almost weekly (sometimes multiple per week) tied to diabetes-adjacent areas where GLP-1s showed impact (kidneys, cancers), mirroring how the Mag-7 bought Instagram-style 'API key' assets.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumEli LillyGLP-1Mag 7Instagram
So what: Lilly is rolling up the peptide/metabolic stack; M&A cadence is a leading indicator of its platform ambitions.
“they are buying companies almost every single week and in some weeks they're buying multiple companies” ▶ 46:36
Visser cites Victoria's Secret's stock up 47% on 15% sales growth attributed in large part to GLP-1-driven weight loss as evidence of unexpected positive second-order effects across the consumer economy.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumVictoria's SecretGLP-1consumer staples
So what: GLP-1 ripple effects create both losers (food) and winners (apparel); screen for non-obvious beneficiaries.
“Victoria Secret put their earnings out there... The stock was up 47% earlier this week. They reported 15% sales growth... directly attributable or in a large part to GLP1's people are losing weight.” ▶ 52:20
Visser highlights that healthcare is approaching ~20% of US annual personal expenditures and growing on demographics, framing it as the largest market to focus attention on and the basis for entitlement-reform optionality if peptides/gene-editing let people 'die healthy instead of die sick'.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highhealthcareMedicareMedicaidentitlementspeptides
So what: Healthcare/human-software is the dominant secular TAM; longevity tech also reframes the entitlement/debt outlook.
“we're we're almost at 20% of uh personal expenditures in the country each year are healthcare.” ▶ 49:28
Macro: rates, liquidity, debt & geopolitical bottlenecks 4
Visser anchors his next-up-leg-in-Bitcoin thesis on three-month rates currently sitting below inflation, framing negative real short rates as the macro fuel for the next durable BTC trend.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcointhree-month ratesinflation
So what: Negative real short rates are his core macro tailwind; the next BTC trend higher is the one he considers important.
“we right now have three-month rates that are below inflation. Just wait for the next trend higher in Bitcoin.” ▶ 6:39
Visser is not worried about US sovereign debt because household net worth is roughly five times the size of the national debt, but flags entitlements (Medicare/Medicaid driven by the country's health) as the genuine fiscal problem.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highUS debthousehold net worthentitlementsMedicareMedicaid
So what: Reframes the debt-doom narrative: the binding constraint is entitlement/health spending, not the headline debt level.
“I've never worried about the debt of the country... because the household net worth of the country is five times the size of the debt of the country.” ▶ 51:02
Visser flags that the Strait of Hormuz remains shut and that prediction markets price a full reopening to normal before the midterms at under 50%, urging investors to weight prediction-market odds over confident reopening narratives.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highStrait of Hormuzprediction marketsmidterms
So what: A live energy/supply bottleneck with sub-50% near-term resolution odds — a macro risk that compounds AI buildout shortages.
“the straight of Hormuz is still shut... right now for the straight to completely reopen to normal by right before the midterms is less than 50%.” ▶ 30:57
Visser argues the market needs 'more bubbles and more billionaires' because bubbles are the mechanism that pulls in the capital required to fund AI infrastructure, reframing bubble dynamics as necessary capital formation.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumAI infrastructurebubbles
So what: Counterintuitive macro frame: bubble-like capital raising is a feature funding the buildout, not a bearish signal to fade.
“we need more bubbles and we need more billionaires” ▶ 20:04
Bitcoin & crypto as the financial layer of the AI-agent economy 5
Visser's headline thesis is that Bitcoin is a direct play on AI agents overtaking humans in commerce, citing that there are already more AI agents than humans on HTML sites, so the future of commerce — and the asset that captures it — is agent-driven.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinAI agentsMoonpay
So what: Reframes BTC's bull case around agentic commerce rather than store-of-value; the agent-economy adoption curve is the thing to track.
“I believe Bitcoin is a direct play on agents dominating humans going forward.” ▶ 0:11
Visser argues the two software categories that matter going forward are 'human software' (the body, via peptides/gene-editing) and 'financial guardrails' (crypto/tokenization), because AI agents will operate in both — and these, not traditional SaaS, are where he is focused.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highcryptotokenizationpeptidesAI agentsSaaS
So what: His unifying thesis: position in human-software (Eli Lilly) and financial-guardrail (crypto/tokenization) software, not legacy SaaS.
“the software that people are choosing is for the human body and for the financial guardrails because AI agents are both that part of software.” ▶ 56:22
Visser argues no idea or innovation in history lasts because something better always replaces it, and since stocks and tokens are ideas, AI generating ideas faster means individual names/tokens will turn over more rapidly — reinforcing Bitcoin as the rare durable constant.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinstockstokensAI
So what: Faster innovation turnover argues for owning the durable index-like asset (BTC) over rotating individual names/tokens.
“I don't believe any idea or innovation in the history of mankind has ever lasted... if AI is now going to be creating all of the ideas, that means they're not going to be around that long.” ▶ 10:12
Visser does not believe the S&P 500 is safe past 2030 because AI will disrupt all companies — explicitly including Apple, Google, and Amazon — which underpins his preference for Bitcoin's durability over individual mega-cap equities.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highS&P 500AppleGoogleAmazonBitcoin
So what: A provocative long-horizon call to question passive S&P safety and mega-cap durability beyond 2030.
≈ “I don't think investing in the S&P 500 is safe past 2030. I've said that repeatedly because I think AI will disrupt all companies.” paraphrase
Visser applies a mobile-app user-churn framework to both Bitcoin and AI businesses: each run-up converts a net-new cohort of permanent holders/users even as some churn out, so floors ratchet higher over cycles and capital is durably driven into these assets.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highBitcoinAI businesses
So what: Use the net-user-accretion lens: volatility sheds weak hands but each cycle sets a higher floor — a reason to buy drawdowns.
“you get these big run-ups in price, a bunch of people pile in, you get it kind of bleeds out, some people leave, but you've converted new people. AI is starting to do this now.” ▶ 21:26
The TRUTH About STRC, SATA, Strategy & Strive | Matt Cole52 min37 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Strive CEO Matt Cole explains the mechanics, carry-trade economics, and risk structure of Bitcoin-backed 'digital credit' (Strive's SATA preferred, Strategy's STRC/stretch), argues these perpetual preferreds are the key low-volatility bridge asset for the fiat-to-Bitcoin transition, defends Michael Saylor's first Bitcoin sale as a healthy capital-allocation evolution, and lays out the case for a multi-issuer digital-credit ecosystem scaling into 2027-2029.
Digital credit mechanics & carry-trade economics · 5Dividend reserves & downside survivability · 6Bitcoin return outlook & amplification policy · 3Dividend frequency, tokenization & money-like use · 3Saylor's Bitcoin sale & Strategy narrative risk · 4Institutional adoption & portfolio positioning · 5Ecosystem structure, competition & systemic risk · 5Macro thesis & Bitcoin concentration · 6
Digital credit mechanics & carry-trade economics 5
Strive's SATA digital-credit instrument is a perpetual preferred-equity security backed by Bitcoin, currently paying a variable ~13% rate that the issuer pegs to keep the price near par ($100), versus ~11.5% on Strategy's stretch (STRC) products.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Cole (CEO Strive Asset Management)conviction: highSATAStrive Asset ManagementSTRCStrategyBitcoin
So what: Sets the explicit yield benchmark for the new Bitcoin-backed preferred category; a ~13% perpetual yield reprices what a fixed-income allocator can earn versus HYG's ~6.5%.
“for SATA, it's 13% right now. It's variable rate um because the issuer we are trying to peg it minimize volatility around 100” ▶ 5:40
For the issuer, digital credit is a carry trade: Strive profits if Bitcoin's compounded annual growth rate exceeds the financing cost (~13% for SATA, ~11.5% for Strategy), making Bitcoin's CAGR the hurdle rate for common-equity holders to outperform Bitcoin.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveStrategyBitcoin
So what: Defines the exact condition under which the common equity (ASST/MSTR) beats spot Bitcoin: BTC CAGR must clear the preferred coupon.
“what we are making a investment bet on is that the average compounded annual growth rate of bitcoin into the future will be better than that financing rate” ▶ 5:52
The break-even Bitcoin appreciation for SATA to pay its coupon in perpetuity is only ~6-12% per year, far below the rate needed for common equity to win; at ~6.5% BTC growth the preferred is fully covered while the common equity likely stays flat as all Bitcoin returns filter into the preferred.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveBitcoinASST
So what: Quantifies the safety margin on the preferred and the trade-off: SATA holders win on modest BTC growth, but common equity needs BTC to clear the full coupon to outperform.
“for SATA right now it's in the neighborhood of 6 12% as that break even interest rate where Strive could pay interest literally forever into the future” ▶ 6:23
Digital credit is structured to eliminate two issuer risks: maturity risk (perpetual liability, no refinancing) and negative convexity (no equity conversion when Bitcoin rips higher), with the issuer willing to pay a double-digit coupon to remove both.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveBitcoin
So what: Explains why perpetual preferred beats convertible debt for a BTC treasury: it removes forced-seller maturity walls and caps no upside.
“I was happy to pay a double-digit interest rate to not have the negative convexity to the downside of maturity risk and not have negative convexity to the upside of equity conversion” ▶ 2:11
The single irreducible risk borne by SATA preferred holders is maturity risk, which the issuer deliberately offloaded to investors in exchange for the high coupon; all other listed risks should be probability-weighted by the buyer.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveBitcoin
So what: Frames the real due-diligence question for a SATA buyer as: what is the probability of a Bitcoin scenario severe enough to force a dividend pause.
“there's really one true risk to these instruments... that key risk was maturity risk” ▶ 18:45
Dividend reserves & downside survivability 6
Strive carries no debt and holds ~18 months of cash and marketable securities (over 12 months cash plus ~6 months of stretch/STRC reserves) as a dividend buffer for SATA.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATASTRC
So what: Concrete balance-sheet cushion figure lets analysts model how long SATA dividends survive a BTC drawdown without selling Bitcoin.
“Strive has no debt. uh we have 18 months of cash and marketable securities” ▶ 11:57
If a 2022-2023-style bear market repeated (implying a ~$40,000 Bitcoin bottom with no recovery off the 200-week MA until late 2027), Strive could pay SATA dividends entirely from cash reserves without selling a single Bitcoin.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATABitcoin
So what: Stress-test scenario: at $40k BTC, the reserve alone covers a multi-year bear, so dividend-pause risk only appears in a worse-than-2022 drawdown.
“what that would mean in Bitcoin terms today would be about a $40,000 Bitcoin bottom” ▶ 12:22
Beyond cash reserves, Strive estimates 5-7 additional years of dividend coverage by selling Bitcoin (depending on BTC price), so a dividend pause only becomes plausible in a bear market substantially longer and deeper than 2022-2023.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATABitcoin
So what: Sizes total dividend runway (reserves + BTC sales) at well over a market cycle, pushing the dividend-cut tail risk far out.
“$40,000, 5 to seven years of Bitcoin coverage in of itself” ▶ 13:11
Strive increased its SATA dividend reserve from 12 months to 18 months during the current Bitcoin bear market despite no obligation to, and despite SATA having been IPO'd in November when Bitcoin was above $100,000.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATABitcoin
So what: Actions-over-words signal: the issuer is building, not drawing down, its buffer into weakness, which supports confidence in the par peg.
“what Strive has actually done is we've increased the dividend reserve from 12 months to 18 months” ▶ 14:34
Strive's board has a fiduciary obligation to pause SATA dividends only if paying would risk bankruptcy; the issuer can also lower the variable rate, giving multiple levers before any default.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATA
So what: Defines the legal trigger and flexibility of the instrument: dividends can be cut or rate-reduced, so it is not a hard contractual default like debt.
“we could pause dividends and we actually would have a a fiduciary obligation as a board to pause dividends if paying a dividend would put Strive into bankruptcy risk” ▶ 11:15
The fraction of each SATA dollar raised that goes into Bitcoin versus the dividend reserve is dynamic; Strive sometimes deploys 100% into Bitcoin and is willing to dip into reserves if liquidity conditions dry up, prioritizing confidence in the par peg over a fixed reserve ratio.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumSATAStriveBitcoin
So what: Reserve policy is discretionary, not contractual, so SATA's BTC-backing ratio varies with market conditions and issuance pace.
Bitcoin return outlook & amplification policy 3
Strive's long-term Bitcoin projection is ~30% CAGR over the next couple of decades, and the projected CAGR rises during bear markets to compensate for the drawdown, putting current expectations at the higher end of the range.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highBitcoinStrive
So what: A ~30% BTC CAGR assumption underpins the entire carry-trade thesis; it is the input that makes SATA's ~13% coupon look comfortably covered.
“on average call it a 30% kagger is what I think uh we should expect” ▶ 8:42
For a clean-balance-sheet Bitcoin treasury company, Strive views ~30-70% amplification (leverage) as the manageable range, intending to stop near the upper bound when Bitcoin sits around its 200-week moving average, and advocates pushing leverage aggressively at current depressed prices.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveBitcoin
So what: Quantifies the leverage band BTC treasury firms target and flags that issuers may add the most leverage near cycle lows ('greedy when others are fearful').
“somewhere you know call it in the neighborhood of 30 to 60 70% is kind of for a clean balance sheet company the range of amplification” ▶ 8:00
Cole is bullish on Bitcoin for the back half of the current year, calling a $40,000 bottom a more-bearish-than-base-case scenario rather than his expectation.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: The issuer's own base case is a 2H recovery, not the $40k stress case used for reserve sizing.
“I'm pretty bullish on Bitcoin for the back half of this year” ▶ 12:41
Dividend frequency, tokenization & money-like use 3
Strive's SATA will be the first listed US security to pay a daily dividend (on business days), versus Strategy's stretch moving to twice-monthly and earlier monthly payouts, deliberately to smooth volatility so the instrument behaves more like a money-market fund or savings account.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveSTRCStrategy
So what: Daily dividends are the structural lever to compress price volatility around par and reposition SATA as a cash-substitute, expanding its addressable market.
“it will be the first, literally the first listed security in US capital markets history. So like real innovation here to pay a daily dividend” ▶ 10:19
Monthly-dividend digital credit shows a recurring pattern: price rises toward par with a sharp liquidity/volume spike just before the dividend event, then drops roughly by the dividend amount afterward, making volume (not price) the key tell of underlying behavior.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumSTRCSATA
So what: Actionable trading pattern: the pre-dividend volume spike and post-dividend par drop is exploitable/observable in STRC and SATA on monthly schedules.
“you'll see the price of stretch or SATA move up to par right around the dividend event and you'll see the volume spike” ▶ 9:43
Cole expects digital credit to be tokenized, enabling near-instantaneous dividends and consumer financial products (debit/credit cards, banking features) layered on top, ultimately turning Bitcoin-backed preferreds into a spendable currency alternative.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumSATAStriveBitcointokenization
So what: Points to tokenized securities + card rails as the next product layer, a roadmap for fintech/tokenization plays adjacent to BTC treasuries.
“I think there's no better security to tokenize than digital credit” ▶ 32:44
Saylor's Bitcoin sale & Strategy narrative risk 4
Cole reads Michael Saylor's sale of 32 Bitcoin as a necessary, healthy evolution that ends Strategy's absolutist 'never sell' messaging and signals capacity to manage the balance sheet for total return, including potential tax-loss-harvesting sales with immediate buy-backs.
contrarian🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStrategyMSTRMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Counters the bearish 'tip of the iceberg' narrative: the sale reduces forced-seller tail risk and is bullish for MSTR's risk profile despite consensus reading it as capitulation.
“I think it was a a necessary evolution from strategy” ▶ 19:56
The fact that a mere 32-Bitcoin sale shocked the market proves to Cole that Saylor had to break the 'never sell' precedent now to gain operating flexibility, and he expects Strategy to be a net Bitcoin buyer effectively every month going forward.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStrategyMSTRMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: If Strategy is a net monthly BTC buyer, the 'continual selling' narrative ages poorly; watch monthly net BTC flow as the falsification test.
“I think he's going to be a net buyer of Bitcoin effectively every single month into the future” ▶ 21:51
Strive explicitly told institutional investors during the SATA IPO process that it will sell Bitcoin when advantageous, and institutions appreciated that stance, contrasting with Strategy's prior 'never sell' posture.
anecdote🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATAStrategyBitcoin
So what: Signals that sophisticated institutional buyers prefer flexible BTC-treasury mandates over dogmatic HODL, a differentiator for Strive.
“when we launched SATA in our investor calls the IPO process we explicitly said that if we need to sell Bitcoin we will sell Bitcoin” ▶ 20:03
Cole concedes the shift from 'never sell' to 'intelligent capital allocator, sometimes buyer sometimes seller' makes Strategy's story more nuanced and harder for retail to grasp, creating near-term narrative/perception risk even if it is the correct capital-allocation decision.
causal🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumStrategyMSTRMichael Saylor
So what: Identifies a genuine sentiment overhang on MSTR: a more sophisticated, less sloganized story can weaken the simple retail bull narrative short-term.
Institutional adoption & portfolio positioning 5
Since issuance, both SATA and Strategy's stretch (STRC) have delivered positive total returns even as Bitcoin fell ~50% from its all-time high, because their high yields more than offset modest price drops below par, demonstrating very low correlation to Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATASTRCBitcoin
So what: The proof-point institutions demanded: digital credit held up in a 50% BTC drawdown, validating it as a low-volatility, low-correlation income sleeve.
“the total return for both SATA and Stretch since they've been issued is positive still because they're paying a high yield” ▶ 30:00
Cole pitches digital credit against the HYG high-yield ETF: HYG yields ~6.5% to worst with high volatility, whereas digital credit roughly doubles that yield with lower volatility, making it structurally attractive to institutions accustomed to high-yield risk.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highHYGSATASTRChigh yield
So what: Direct relative-value comparison: ~13% yield with sub-HYG volatility is the institutional sales pitch and a benchmark to watch versus HYG spreads.
“if you just pull up a chart of the HYG high yield ETF and you look at how volatile that thing is and that thing has a yield to worst of 6 12% or so and you double that with digital credit” ▶ 30:52
Cole expects mass institutional adoption of digital credit and Bitcoin ETFs to scale through 2027-2029, framing both ETFs and digital credit (which has existed under a year) as still early in a 3-5 year institutional track-record-building process.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumBitcoinETFsSATAdigital credit
So what: Sets a multi-year adoption timeline; the 2027-2029 window is when investment-policy-statement approvals and track records unlock larger flows.
“you could see mass institutional adoption continue to scale in 2027 2028 2029” ▶ 31:21
Current digital-credit demand is being funded primarily out of fixed-income sleeves (the '40' of the 60/40), with notable flows from real-estate investors selling property to capture more yield with less work.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumSATAdigital creditreal estatefixed income
So what: Identifies the capital sources being cannibalized: bond allocations and hard-asset/real-estate holdings, useful for sizing the displacement opportunity.
“we've seen a lot of examples of real estate investors actually selling some real estate property and buying digital credit” ▶ 32:10
Strive publishes the live risk profile of SATA and its common equity ASST on its website, refreshing every 15 seconds, contrasting with private credit's quarterly opaque marks.
data_point🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveSATAASSTprivate credit
So what: Real-time risk transparency is a differentiator versus private credit and a tool allocators can monitor directly at strive.com.
“you can go to our website and the risk of SATA and the risk of AST, it refreshes every 15 seconds” ▶ 49:37
Ecosystem structure, competition & systemic risk 5
Cole argues the opportunity set for Bitcoin-backed credit is 'close to infinite' and that no single issuer (including Strive) can or should offer all products, citing obvious adjacencies like SATA-equivalents denominated in pounds, yen, and euros for international demand.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highSATAStriveBitcoindigital credit
So what: Flags currency-denominated BTC-backed preferreds as a near-term international product whitespace and a reason to expect many new issuers.
“SATA but paid in the pound. Seda but paid in the yen. Seda but paid in the euro” ▶ 36:08
Cole frames competition between Strive and Strategy as a positive-sum 'friendly' rivalry that drives innovation (cash reserves, willingness to sell BTC, daily dividends) and grows the total pie, while conceding it can shift capital flows from one issuer to the other in the short term.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveStrategySATASTRC
So what: Suggests issuer competition is a feature accelerating product quality, but near-term flow share between STRC and SATA can swing as features leapfrog.
“the great thing about capitalism is that that competition pushes innovation” ▶ 40:09
Strive worked NASDAQ and DTCC to approve the first daily-dividend security, an innovation now effectively open-source for competitors to copy, after Strategy had earlier (incorrectly) assumed NASDAQ rules capped dividend dollar amounts.
anecdote🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highStriveNASDAQDTCCStrategySATA
So what: Regulatory/market-plumbing detail: daily dividends required NASDAQ + DTCC sign-off, a precedent that lowers the bar for all future issuers.
“we found a way within the system to get NASDAQ and DTCC to say yes” ▶ 41:23
Cole contends a diversified ecosystem of 20-50-100 digital-credit issuers reduces systemic risk and single-points-of-failure (including US-government attack surface), and notes a compliant 1940-Act digital-credit ETF would require at least ~30 issuers to be diversified.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highdigital creditStriveStrategyETFs
So what: A digital-credit ETF (a major future demand channel) is gated on having ~30 issuers, so issuer proliferation is a precondition watch-item.
“if you wanted a digital credit ETF, that you need probably at least 30 issuers of digital credit to have a diversified compliant 1940 act product” ▶ 47:56
Cole expects interest-bearing tokenized securities and digital credit to threaten banks by enabling deposit flight, citing the Jamie Dimon vs Brian Armstrong fight over interest-bearing stablecoins as evidence banks see a systemic run risk.
causal🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highbanksJPMorganJamie DimonBrian ArmstrongCoinbasestablecoinsdigital credit
So what: Frames banks as the 'Blockbuster' incumbents at risk from yield-bearing crypto instruments, a long-term structural short/disruption thesis on bank deposits.
“Think about Jamie Diamond, how he's losing his um mind about stable coins paying interest” ▶ 36:43
Macro thesis & Bitcoin concentration 6
Cole projects Michael Saylor's Strategy reaches ~1,000,000 Bitcoin (≈5% of supply) likely this year, and argues 5% is a material but non-controlling stake that does not threaten the network even if doubled.
prediction🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: mediumStrategyMSTRMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Sizes the largest corporate BTC holder's likely 2026 position at ~5% of supply, relevant for concentration-risk and supply-overhang debates.
≈ “when I think about Sailor's stack he probably gets to a million Bitcoin I think probably this year” paraphrase
Cole holds Bitcoin and amplified (leveraged) Bitcoin exposure rather than digital credit himself, stating digital credit is designed for volatility-averse investors who need cash flow, while high-conviction holders should go further out the risk spectrum.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highBitcoinSATAdigital credit
So what: Issuer's own positioning is a candid tell: spot/leveraged BTC for conviction holders, SATA for income/volatility-averse buyers, segmenting the market.
“I do not own digital credit. I own Bitcoin and I own amplified Bitcoin exposure because I'm so confident in the direction that this goes” ▶ 16:38
Cole argues only a tiny self-custodied Bitcoin position is needed for insurance, citing giving family members 0.05 BTC each in 2017 as 'more than the average American could hold' even if 100% of Bitcoin were held in America.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highBitcoinself-custody
So what: Reframes the concentration objection: because BTC is scarce, modest self-custody suffices, so institutional/ETF/treasury custody of most supply is acceptable.
“with 0.05 Bitcoin, even if 100% of the Bitcoin was held in America, you will have more Bitcoin than the average American can hold” ▶ 43:56
Cole's core macro thesis is that the US debt crisis will not improve and the economy is transitioning to a Bitcoin future, with digital credit positioned as potentially the most important low-volatility bridge asset that could itself accelerate hyperbitcoinization by drawing fresh demand.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highBitcoindigital creditUS debtfiat currency
So what: The macro frame underpinning every product claim: digital credit is sold as the smoothed on-ramp for capital fleeing fiat, with a self-reinforcing demand loop into BTC.
“I think digital credit could be the most important asset in this transition period” ▶ 0:05
Cole treats the '60/40 portfolio is dead' as consensus within crypto circles but argues the unresolved question is what replaces the 40% (Bitcoin, prediction markets, trend-following, or digital credit), positioning digital credit as a contender for that allocation.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: high60/40 portfoliodigital creditBitcoinprediction marketstrend following
So what: Identifies the contested fixed-income replacement decision as the battleground digital credit is competing to win, alongside other alt-fixed-income solutions.
“what is completely not consensus is what do you do with the 40” ▶ 2:52
Cole argues regulation cannot ultimately constrain digital credit's growth because 'the problem is the money, the dollar,' and expects securities broadly to become dollar alternatives as consumers exit fiat via tokenized, card-linked instruments.
thesis🗣 guest, Matt Coleconviction: highdigital credittokenizationfiat currencybanks
So what: Long-horizon view that yield-bearing tokenized securities displace dollars regardless of regulatory pushback, a structural tailwind for the category.
“you can't constrain through regulation where this will ultimately go” ▶ 37:24

Risk Reversal · Dan Nathan 3 episode(s) · 109 insights

A VC's Playbook For Investing in the AI Bubble59 min38 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Notable Capital partner Jeff Richards lays out a VC's framework for investing through the AI 'bubble': stay in the 'token path' (businesses that improve as models improve), favor Google/Meta/cybersecurity as durable AI plays, expect a wave of hyper-efficient, fast-growing private companies (Anthropic, Databricks, SpaceX, OpenAI) to IPO and flood the LP ecosystem with liquidity, and treat sharp single-day 20-30% melt-ups in real tech names as a genuine late-cycle warning sign even as he believes the secular AI demand curve still slopes up for a decade.
Token-path framework for AI software winners · 6Cybersecurity as the obvious durable AI trade · 3Google as the best pure-play AI bet · 3Meta undervalued, Microsoft a TBD · 4Enterprise AI adoption: spend, build-vs-buy and talent · 6Semis, robotics and the bubble/supply-constraint debate · 7The coming IPO wave and private-company economics · 5Late-cycle sentiment, Jensen's megaphone and froth signals · 4
Token-path framework for AI software winners 6
Jeff Richards' core stock-picking screen for the AI era is whether a company is 'in the token path'—i.e., its business mechanically improves as underlying models improve and token consumption rises; companies not in the token path won't be raising guidance and should be avoided.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highNotable CapitalAnthropicOpenAIAI software
So what: A reusable screen: track which software names are taking up full-year guidance as model quality jumps—those are 'token path' beneficiaries; laggards on guidance are structurally exposed.
“If you're in the token path, meaning as the models get better, your business gets better. Anybody who's in the token path saw their business get better” ▶ 7:27
Richards argues the quant/ETF market wrongly lumped CrowdStrike, Snowflake, Datadog and Palo Alto Networks in with commoditized 'SaaS,' and that mispricing created the opportunity: those four stocks rallied 70-80% off their Q1 lows after the AI-displacement fear faded.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highCrowdStrikeSnowflakeDatadogPalo Alto NetworksETFsSaaS
So what: Indiscriminate 'SaaS apocalypse' selloffs of differentiated, AI-leveraged software create buyable dislocations; the names that snapped back hardest signal where the durable franchises are.
“If the quant algos and ETFs have convinced you that uh CrowdStrike and Snowflake are the same as SaaS based software, that I don't know what to do for you because they're very different business models” ▶ 5:37
Richards does not expect a near-term collapse in legacy SaaS revenue—Salesforce, ServiceNow and Workday businesses are durable for the next 12-24 months—but the open question is whether they can get into the token path over 2-3 years, and many will have to do it via M&A because they lack the internal teams to build AI-native products.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumSalesforceServiceNowWorkdayAccentureDeloitte
So what: Legacy SaaS is a 'durable but not dead' trade near-term; the multi-year tail risk is real, making M&A optionality (acquiring AI-native teams) the key thing to watch.
“I don't believe you're going to see a huge drop off in the next 12 to 24 months. It's just those businesses are durable, they have great products, they're now starting to harness AI.” ▶ 30:40
Anthropic is now at a ~$47 billion revenue run rate, up from $9 billion at the end of last year—and Richards notes Anthropic added more new run-rate revenue in Q1 alone than the entire IGV software index added in all of 2025 ($60B total, $30B of which was Microsoft Azure).
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAnthropicIGV indexMicrosoftAzure
So what: A historically unprecedented growth rate that reframes how to value private AI labs and explains the positive derivative effects flowing to memory, GPUs and infra.
“Anthropic exceeded that in Q1. So this is something we've really never seen before” ▶ 6:40
Over 50% of Anthropic's AI calls are for coding, while enterprise functions like finance, accounting and marketing are still sub-10% adoption—implying the largest waves of token consumption (and the associated spend scrutiny) are still ahead.
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAnthropiccodingenterprise software
So what: Coding is the proven first vertical; finance/accounting/marketing represent the next legs of demand growth—watch tools that measure non-coding AI output, a coming budget battleground.
“Over 50% of the AI calls for for Anthropic, at least, are are for coding... those other functions... We're still like sub 10%.” ▶ 26:47
Richards flags the recurring 'SanDisk and Micron' memory beneficiaries plus Nvidia and AMD as the second-order trade off Anthropic-style token growth, because surging inference demand makes memory and compute supply the binding constraint.
causal🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumSanDiskMicronNvidiaAMDmemory
So what: Frames memory (Micron, SanDisk) as a derivative play on AI-lab token growth, not just GPUs—useful for building a basket around the constrained supply chain.
Cybersecurity as the obvious durable AI trade 3
Richards calls cybersecurity the most obvious durable AI growth area because agents acting with broad access to corporate systems massively expand the attack surface, and the number one thing holding back enterprise AI adoption is security—he expects CrowdStrike and Palo Alto growth rates to potentially accelerate like Palantir's did.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highCrowdStrikePalo Alto NetworksPalantirOktaAI agents
So what: Positions cybersecurity TAM as underestimated and a gating factor for all enterprise AI; watch for growth-rate acceleration at CRWD/PANW as the bull catalyst.
“the number one thing in my opinion that is holding back large corporations from adopting AI en masse is cybersecurity” ▶ 11:03
Richards highlights CrowdStrike's move from ~$370-400 to ~$760 in roughly two months (orderly ramp from mid-April, then parabolic in the last week) as an example of a fundamentally-driven name where the price action got ahead of itself, but where he won't trade around it because he isn't a trader.
data_point🗣 host (Dan Nathan) and Jeff Richardsconviction: mediumCrowdStrike
So what: A concrete tape: CRWD nearly doubled in ~2 months; the parabolic final week is the kind of melt-up that flags crowding even in a high-quality name.
On current numbers Richards cites the software comp set as DataDog growing 24% at 18x forward revenue, Cloudflare 29% at 28x, CrowdStrike 23% at 30x, and Palantir the outlier growing 63% at 43x—noting anything above ~10x forward revenue is already an aggressive valuation, with the bull case resting on growth-rate acceleration.
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumDatadogCloudflareCrowdStrikePalantir
So what: Gives an explicit valuation map for the AI-software complex; the multiples only work if growth accelerates, so any deceleration is the asymmetric downside trigger.
“you would argue anything above maybe 10 times is an aggressive valuation. But I think what folks are probably thinking is that those growth rates are going to accelerate.” ▶ 10:46
Google as the best pure-play AI bet 3
Richards calls Google probably the best pure-play AI bet ownable because it is vertically integrated across the whole stack (TPUs, GCP infrastructure, AI mode in search, YouTube, Android, advertising, travel) with 12-13 platforms over a billion users each—'if you want QQQ, you want a lot of Google.'
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highGoogleAlphabetTPUGCPYouTubeAndroidQQQVGT
So what: Frames GOOGL as the lowest-controversy way to own the full AI stack; vertical integration (especially TPUs) is the differentiator versus single-layer bets.
“I would argue Google is probably the best pure play bet on AI you can own because of all the different points of differentiation you mentioned.” ▶ 15:55
Richards reads Berkshire Hathaway's $10 billion direct purchase in Google's ~$80 billion equity raise (at roughly double the price Google traded a year ago) as a notably bullish signal—one of the world's most conservative investors making what is largely an AI-infrastructure bet, despite Berkshire's history of missing internet, mobile and social.
causal🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highBerkshire HathawayGoogleAlphabetWarren Buffett
So what: A high-signal endorsement of AI infra from a value investor; the $10B Berkshire stake validates Google's infrastructure thesis at a full valuation.
“one of the arguably most conservative investors in the world just put $10 billion into Google at today's valuation” ▶ 12:49
Richards reframes Google's equity raise as a smart capital-allocation move rather than a red flag: with the stock at the highest valuation it has ever been (~26x forward), issuing equity minimizes dilution, and he argues you'd rather see a company sell stock at a high valuation than buy back stock at a high valuation—several large software firms recently announced big buybacks then fell 30%.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highGoogleAlphabetNvidiaSpaceX
So what: Contrarian capital-allocation lens: equity issuance at peak multiples is a positive, buybacks at peak multiples a negative—useful for judging any AI mega-cap's financing choices, and a possible emerging trend.
“you'd rather have them selling stock at a high valuation than buying stock at a high valuation” ▶ 18:25
Meta undervalued, Microsoft a TBD 4
Meta is the only stock Richards personally bought in the last 30 days because he views it as undervalued—a founder-led company whose VR/infrastructure bet is now poised to pay off in AI, with consumer-business upside (better targeting, algorithms, monetization) that the market hasn't yet connected to its CapEx spend.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highMetaMark ZuckerbergAI
So what: His single highest-conviction recent buy; the catalyst is the market re-rating Meta once CapEx-to-monetization leverage becomes visible, analogous to Google ~18 months ago.
“Meta is the only thing I bought in the last 30 days. I just think it's undervalued.” ▶ 20:41
Richards suggests Meta could convert its AI infrastructure into a third-party compute platform—citing Elon Musk's xAI deal to pay Anthropic ~$1.25 billion a month (with a 90-day out on both sides)—becoming a provider to Anthropic, OpenAI or Mistral, an optionality the market isn't pricing.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumMetaxAIAnthropicOpenAIMistralElon Musk
So what: Hidden optionality: Meta's CapEx could become a revenue-generating compute platform; the xAI/Anthropic $1.25B/mo deal is the template and a data point on compute pricing.
Richards rates Microsoft a TBD outside Azure: Copilot is widely seen as inferior to Claude, the company lacks a consumer-distribution edge like Apple/Google/Meta, and the fate of the Office/PowerPoint/Word franchise is the open question—he wouldn't be shocked to see Satya Nadella pursue a partnership or M&A this year despite regulatory constraints.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumMicrosoftAzureCopilotClaudeSatya NadellaOffice
So what: Frames MSFT as Azure-strong but app-franchise-uncertain; watch for a 2026 partnership or M&A catalyst, and Copilot's competitiveness versus Claude as the swing factor.
“Copilot has been sort of their signature product, which I think most people would acknowledge is not nearly as good as something like Claude.” ▶ 20:00
Richards's safest-AI-bet ranking is Google first, then Amazon and Meta; he flags Amazon and Meta as 'unloved' partly because the market can't yet connect CapEx to future benefit, and points to portfolio company Quince (e-commerce, led by partner Hans) where AI has transformed logistics, inventory, pricing and marketing as evidence the same leverage is coming at Amazon and Meta.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumGoogleAmazonMetaQuincee-commerce
So what: Ranks the mega-cap 'safe' AI plays and uses private-company operating data (Quince) as a leading indicator for Amazon/Meta consumer-AI leverage.
“where are these safest quote-unquote plays in AI? It's Google, it's probably Amazon, it's probably Meta.” ▶ 16:56
Enterprise AI adoption: spend, build-vs-buy and talent 6
Richards pushes back on the 'AI spend will go down' narrative: instead of cutting, enterprises will shift to rigorously measuring output—his own 40-person firm and portfolio companies (one doing 48-hour performance reviews) track productivity output rather than token consumption, and spend stays elevated.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highenterprise AINotable Capital
So what: Counters the bear thesis that consumption-based sticker shock cuts AI budgets; the real second-order play is the wave of tools measuring AI output/productivity.
“Peo- people immediately jumped to, "Oh, the spending's going to go down." And I'm like, "Mm, guys, that's not what's going to happen. They're going to measure it” ▶ 27:57
Richards reports a Fortune 50 company (>$100B revenue) only just signed its first Anthropic deal a month ago, evidence that even the largest enterprises are at 'day one' of AI adoption and that the corporate use case has years of runway.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAnthropicFortune 50
So what: On-the-ground evidence that enterprise AI penetration is far earlier than the stock prices imply—supports a long demand runway for model providers and infra.
“I said, "Hey, where are you guys in terms of deploying AI?" He said, "Oh, we just signed a deal with Anthropic."... So, I think we are still very much at day one” ▶ 24:31
Richards anchors the AI-margin bull case in history: S&P 500 profit margins were flat for 20 years before 1995, then rose 7 points (from 7% to 14%) from 1995-2020 because of the internet—implying AI can drive a similar structural margin expansion, though the bridge for industrials, oil & gas and retail will take years.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highS&P 500AccentureDeloitteinternet
So what: A long-horizon margin-expansion thesis that justifies CapEx; near-term the value accrues to integrators (Accenture, Deloitte) bridging the testing-to-production gap.
≈ “profit margins for the S&P 500 prior to 1995, they were flat for 20 years. And then from '95 to 2020, they went up seven points, from seven to 14. Why? Because of the internet.” paraphrase
Richards distinguishes where AI-native build-vs-buy disruption hits: sales/marketing, design and creative are easy to displace (Canva/Figma ate Adobe share), but back-office ERP, general ledger, payables/receivables are hard categories where large enterprises want a vendor to blame on risk/cybersecurity—so legacy SaaS is safest in the back office.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highCanvaFigmaAdobeERPSalesforceServiceNowWorkday
So what: A category-level disruption map: short/cautious on front-office creative incumbents, more defensive on back-office ERP—useful for picking which SaaS names are exposed.
“large enterprises want somebody to blame if something goes wrong from a cybersecurity or a risk standpoint” ▶ 32:36
Richards says private companies of 50-100 employees are already building internal AI operating systems and peeling off third-party SaaS products, but a Fortune 50 spending $3-4B/year on IT won't rip out and replace its stack anytime soon—so the race is whether incumbents build modern AI-native products before emerging players take them out.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highSaaSFortune 50AI-native software
So what: Build-internally is a real threat at the small-company tier but slow at the enterprise tier; the timing gap determines whether SaaS incumbents survive or get disrupted.
“The idea that a, you know, Fortune 50 company that spends 3 billion or 4 billion dollars a year on IT is going to suddenly rip out all of its existing platforms and replace them with native AI is probably not going to happen anytime soon.” ▶ 34:02
Richards identifies talent as one of Silicon Valley's most underrated dynamics: large enterprise software companies literally cannot recruit top AI engineers—a 30/35-year-old will choose Anthropic, OpenAI, Vercel, Base10 or Fireworks over ServiceNow—and are losing existing staff, which will force acqui-hires (e.g., Zuckerberg's Scale team deal as a template).
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIVercelBase10FireworksServiceNowScale AIMark Zuckerberg
So what: Talent flight is a structural headwind for legacy enterprise software and a driver of forced acqui-hire M&A—watch which incumbents pay up for AI teams.
“if you are a really talented 35-year-old or 30-year-old AI engineer... you have a job an opportunity to go to Anthropic or ServiceNow, which one are you going to choose? ... You're going to go to Anthropic.” ▶ 34:32
Semis, robotics and the bubble/supply-constraint debate 7
Richards notes a VC resurgence into semiconductors—a category VCs avoided for decades—citing Cerebras's IPO and an acquisition by Groq, but cautions that Cerebras took ~14 years to build, so semis are long-gestation bets.
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumCerebrasGroqsemiconductorsventure capital
So what: Signals renewed VC appetite for semis after wins like Cerebras, but the 14-year build time tempers expectations for quick returns.
Richards says robotics is now the category attracting the most capital and attention at Notable and peer firms—not humanoid 'Optimus next to you at a football game,' but specialized use cases in manufacturing, logistics and transportation (Waymo cited as a successful 'robot'), framed as a 5-10 year innovation cycle.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highroboticsTeslaOptimusWaymoNotable Capital
So what: Robotics is the next major VC capital-flow frontier; the near-term value is in specialized industrial/logistics robots (and AV like Waymo), not consumer humanoids.
“the category that is attracting a lot of attention and a lot of capital right now is is robotics” ▶ 36:52
Richards admits the firm and he personally avoid semis beyond owning QQQ, and frames the central semi question as whether the market is still underestimating GPU demand going forward—conceding everyone underestimated it the first time (or they'd have put their net worth in Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom a decade ago).
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumNvidiaAMDBroadcomQQQGPUs
So what: Reframes the semi debate as a demand-estimation question; the bull case persists only if GPU demand is still being underestimated, which is the crux of the bubble call.
“everybody underestimated how much demand there was for GPUs... So, the question you have to ask yourself is are we underestimating it going forward?” ▶ 40:01
Richards' bubble test is whether the entire supply chain—from Anthropic down to GPU providers—is genuinely capacity-constrained, evidenced by users being rate-limited on Anthropic daily; demand exceeds capacity today, but he and Nathan agree the cycle will inevitably asymptote and flip to oversupply (they've 'been through seven cycles').
framework🗣 Jeff Richards and host (Dan Nathan)conviction: mediumAnthropicGPUsAI supply chain
So what: Rate-limiting is the real-time tell that demand still exceeds supply; the inflection to watch is when supply finally exceeds demand—the classic cycle top.
“everyday people are getting rate limited on Anthropic. And so if you believe that that is happening throughout the chain... will we will we will at some point out cuz it would have to asymptote” ▶ 41:05
Nathan argues the market is failing to discount obvious risks—double/triple ordering of chips due to capacity constraints, plus the historical precedent that beloved names like Tesla, Nvidia, Netflix and Meta have each fallen ~70% from highs—and a 2022-style rate-driven correction (inflation not yet tamed) could hit CapEx and the whole complex within ~18 months.
contrarian🗣 host (Dan Nathan)conviction: highTeslaNvidiaNetflixMetainterest rates
So what: The bear scenario: pull-forward demand, double-ordering and a rate shock could trigger a sharp drawdown; nothing being discounted today is the risk-asymmetry the host flags.
“we've seen stocks that have the ability to go down some of your most beloved stocks, whether it was Tesla or Nvidia or Netflix or meta whatever... they went down 70% from the highs” ▶ 28:45
Richards anchors his long-term TAM bull case on the cloud analogy: in 2010 analysts pegged web-services TAM at $5-6B annually when AWS was a $500M business; today AWS, Azure and GCP generate ~$320B/year—so if the AI TAM estimate is even in the same 'zip code' of wrongness, demand is being vastly underestimated.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAWSMicrosoftAzureGCPcloud infrastructure
So what: Quantifies the 'TAM is underestimated' thesis with a 60x cloud miss; the structural bull case for a decade of AI infra demand despite cyclical wobbles.
“back in 2010, analysts estimated the TAM for web services was 5 to 6 billion dollars annually... Today, AWS, Microsoft, and GCP generate 320 billion dollars a year” ▶ 42:36
Richards names data-center and power buildout permitting as the constraint that worries him most—comparing opposition to data centers to the opposition that blocked nuclear—warning that limited power and data-center capacity, alongside interest rates, geopolitics and US fiscal/tax pressure, are the real threats to the AI buildout.
causal🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highdata centersnuclear powerenergyinterest rates
So what: Power and data-center permitting—not demand—is the binding real-world constraint; energy/power infrastructure and policy are the variables that could throttle the whole cycle.
“how about just people constraining the amount of data center capacity we're building? To me, that is the craziest thing I've ever seen.” ▶ 43:15
The coming IPO wave and private-company economics 5
Richards expects SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI to potentially IPO in the next ~6-12 months, and behind them a wave of companies with $500M-$2B+ revenue growing far faster than anything public—where the market won't know how to value growth north of 100% (Palantir's ~43x at 60% growth is the current ceiling) and will be surprised by their margins, cash flow and efficiency.
prediction🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIDatabricksPalantir
So what: A near-term IPO supercycle is coming; the valuation framework for >100% growth is undefined, creating both repricing risk and opportunity—watch how the first deals price.
“there's a whole wave of companies that will hopefully go out behind them that are call it 500 million to a couple billion of revenue with growth rates that are way higher than anything we see in the public markets today” ▶ 50:57
Richards says the new private-company operating model is radically more efficient: the old 'good' benchmark of ~$300K ARR per FTE (circa 2019-2021) is now the low end, with several Notable portfolio companies generating millions of dollars of ARR per FTE—'a very different operating model than your grandpa's software company.'
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highNotable Capitalprivate softwareARR per FTE
So what: ARR-per-FTE has jumped roughly an order of magnitude; this efficiency is the under-appreciated edge public investors will rerate when these companies file.
“a decent metric was sort of $300,000 of ARR per FTE. Now, that's the low end... we have several that are in the millions of dollars per FTE.” ▶ 51:33
Richards argues 'speed is your moat' has replaced durability as the defining competitive trait of AI-era software: companies that don't innovate and ship at high product velocity get run over—not just by Anthropic and OpenAI but by other emerging players—unlike legacy SaaS whose moat was durability and low competition.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIAI software
So what: Reframes the durable-moat investing assumption: in AI-native software, velocity beats durability, so investors should weight product cadence over installed-base inertia.
“the mantra among all these private companies is speed is your moat.” ▶ 52:09
Richards flags a major under-discussed macro consequence: if SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI go public in 6-12 months, the liquidity flowing back to the LP ecosystem (endowments, nonprofits, universities) will be the highest ever after a 2020-2023 liquidity drought, re-fueling startup funding and innovation.
thesis🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAILP ecosystemendowmentsventure capital
So what: The IPO wave's second-order effect is a liquidity flood into LPs that could reignite the entire VC/startup funding cycle—a positive feedback loop few are pricing.
“the amount of liquidity that is going to come back into the LP ecosystem that have invested in venture capital and private equity will be the highest it's ever been” ▶ 54:45
Richards notes public-market investors at last week's investment banking conference have zero exposure to SpaceX, Anthropic or OpenAI because they never participated in private rounds—so demand at IPO will hinge entirely on price, while private rounds (e.g., Databricks per Ali Ghodsi) have consistently been oversubscribed with far more demand than supply at $700-900B valuations.
data_point🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIDatabricksFidelityT. Rowe Price
So what: Pent-up public demand exists but is price-sensitive; persistent oversubscription in private rounds (Databricks) at near-trillion valuations sets the anchor IPO investors must clear.
“I was at an investment banking conference last week, and I was talking with a group of public investors, none of whom had exposure to any of these three” ▶ 50:05
Late-cycle sentiment, Jensen's megaphone and froth signals 4
Nathan warns that Jensen Huang has become a sentiment problem: offhand comments drove a ~20% rally in ServiceNow and a ~30% rally in Marvell (after Nvidia's ~$2B investment and a 'next trillion-dollar semi' remark), and Nvidia's web of investments in Neoclouds, Intel and startups is increasingly circular and self-fulfilling—nothing has dented the company in 3.5-4 years, which itself is a warning.
contrarian🗣 host (Dan Nathan)conviction: highNvidiaJensen HuangServiceNowMarvellIntelNeoclouds
So what: Nvidia's circular investments and Jensen's market-moving commentary are a froth/sentiment risk; the absence of any setback in 4 years is a late-cycle flag to watch.
