≈ “Jensen Huang is becoming a real problem from a sentiment standpoint... he caused a 20% rally in ServiceNow... a 30% rally in Marvell” paraphrase ▶ 44:19
Richards offers two durable late-cycle maxims: 'nobody knows anything'—not Zuckerberg, Huang, Trump or anyone—about what happens tomorrow, and 'everybody talks their book,' so Jensen describing a bright future is simply his job as the CEO at the epicenter; he cites Elon Musk consistently missing 15 years of targets while still doing amazing things as the archetype.
framework🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: highNvidiaJensen HuangMark ZuckerbergElon MuskTesla
So what: A discipline check: discount promotional CEO narratives ('everybody talks their book') when sizing AI-infra bets, especially around Nvidia and Tesla guidance.
“nobody knows anything... And the other thing is everybody talks their book.” ▶ 46:20
Both agree single-day 20-30% gaps in real companies (Intel, Dell, HPE up ~30%, Cisco, plus Snowflake and Datadog) are a genuine worry signal—distinct from meme stocks—forcing the binary read that either institutions are buying undervalued names or a retail rush is pushing stocks up unsustainably.
contrarian🗣 host (Dan Nathan) and Jeff Richardsconviction: highIntelDellHPECiscoSnowflakeDatadog
So what: Violent single-day melt-ups in legitimate large-cap tech are a late-cycle tell; the question of institutional vs. retail-driven moves is the key diagnostic.
“when I see things go up 20 25 30% in a day does it cause me to worry yes for sure.” ▶ 57:51
Richards cites Howard Lindzon's anecdote that Virgin Galactic (SPCE) jumped ~120% in days because retail investors mistakenly thought it might be SpaceX's ticker—calling that kind of mistaken-ticker buying the froth that genuinely worries him, alongside prediction markets and proposed 24/7 stock trading as new volatility sources.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partnerconviction: mediumVirgin GalacticSPCESpaceXprediction marketsHoward Lindzon
So what: Mistaken-ticker manias (SPCE/SpaceX), prediction markets and 24/7 trading are structural volatility risks—classic 1999-style retail froth signals to monitor.
“SPCE, which is Virgin Galactic, had gone up 120%... because people thought that might be the stock symbol for SpaceX.” ▶ 57:08
America's Trillion-Dollar Race to Catch Chinese Miners55 min32 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two-part show: a markets segment on single-stock fragility at index highs, the SpaceX mega-IPO sucking liquidity from telecom/defense, and rising market 'gamification'; followed by an interview with Metals Royalty Company (NASDAQ: TMCR) CEO Brian Paes-Braga arguing the US is in inning one of a US-led critical-minerals commodity super-cycle, with China controlling 19 of 20 energy-critical minerals and refining 30-99% of supply.
Single-stock fragility under a calm VIX · 4Google as the AI-race winner · 2SpaceX IPO as a liquidity vacuum · 4Market 'gamification' and eroding guardrails · 2Oil and the consumer pinch · 2US-led critical-minerals super-cycle · 6Government capital and policy tailwind · 3Metals Royalty Company (TMCR) business model · 5Geopolitics and resource geography · 4
Single-stock fragility under a calm VIX 4
Broadcom fell 16.5% the day after earnings despite solid metrics, because its AI revenue guidance came in below expectations — signaling that mega-cap names that miss on the specific metric the market is watching will be punished immediately.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highBroadcomAVGONvidiaMarvellTaiwan SemiconductorAI semiconductors
So what: At index highs with a sub-16 VIX, position sizing should account for 15%+ single-name gap risk on any guidance miss; the index's calm masks violent dispersion.
“The stock was down 16.5%. This was a 2.3 trillion market cap company.” ▶ 4:10
Broadcom's plunge did not spread — direct competitor Marvell rose 4%, Taiwan Semi rose, and customer Google rose nearly 4% the same day — implying the market read the miss as Broadcom-specific rather than a sector-wide AI demand crack.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumBroadcomMarvellTaiwan SemiconductorGoogleAlphabet
So what: Use the non-contagion as a tell: AI capex demand thesis stays intact; the selloff was an entry, not a regime change, for the broader complex.
Guy Adami reads the Broadcom retrace (a full give-back of its mid-May run from ~415 to ~495) as a possible 'first domino' and warns that large gap-up reactions to earnings (Cisco, Intel, Dell, IBM) are historically a late-cycle, not early-cycle, market characteristic.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumBroadcomCiscoIntelDellIBM
So what: Treat outsized upside earnings gaps on mega-caps as a caution flag on cycle maturity rather than confirmation of a new leg higher.
“That to me is not particularly bullish.” ▶ 7:05
The SEC's repeal of the pattern-day-trading rule, alongside higher retail margin usage, is expected to increase intraday single-stock volatility — especially in the highest-momentum names.
prediction🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSECretail tradersmomentum stocks
So what: Expect fatter intraday tails in momentum leaders; options/vol strategies on those names may be underpriced relative to realized single-stock moves.
Google as the AI-race winner 2
Google is positioned to win the AI race with a still-reasonable valuation, having converted prior weaknesses (lagging Gemini, GCP behind Azure/AWS, no TPU story) into strengths; existential search-disruption fears have receded.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highGoogleAlphabetGeminiGoogle CloudTPUsAzureAWS
So what: Among mega-cap AI plays, GOOGL screens as the rare 'win the race at a reasonable multiple' name to overweight.
Google upsized a capital raise to $85 billion — anchored by Berkshire Hathaway taking $10 billion — signaling a shift among cash-rich AI builders toward selling equity/debt rather than depleting cash flow, which implicitly says management views the AI buildout as a better return than buying back its own stock.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGoogleAlphabetBerkshire HathawayAI capex
So what: Equity issuance by net-cash AI names dilutes future EPS and reduces buyback support — a structural headwind to per-share metrics even as the businesses grow.
“My investments though that I am going to make in this are going to be a better long-term investment than my equity right here, right now.” ▶ 10:27
SpaceX IPO as a liquidity vacuum 4
SpaceX is expected to IPO around June 12th as potentially the largest IPO in history, with Elon Musk personally setting the price, ~30% allocated to retail, 36 banks marketing it, and underwriting fees compressed to ~1% versus a normal 5-6%.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highSpaceXElon MuskJP Morganinvestment banks
So what: A mega-cap, small-float IPO with index-inclusion mechanics forces incremental buying; the aftermarket trade, not the pricing, is the signal to watch for broader market risk appetite.
“He is setting the price as they said in the movie Trading Places.” ▶ 11:41
Telecom (T-Mobile, AT&T, Verizon) and defense primes (Lockheed, Northrop, RTX) are trading poorly near 52-week lows — down 15-30% — as the market pre-emptively prices SpaceX/Starlink as an existential threat rather than waiting to confirm it.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highT-MobileAT&TVerizonLockheed MartinNorthrop GrummanRTXSpaceXStarlink
So what: Starlink-exposed telecom and SpaceX-threatened defense names are being de-rated on narrative; watch for overshoot/mean-reversion or a structural short thesis depending on Starlink execution.
“We're not going to wait around to find out.” ▶ 13:25
Lockheed Martin has fallen from an all-time high near $700 (made in March) to just north of $500, and AT&T has round-tripped from $22 in late January up to ~$29 and back to $22 (its lowest since January 2025), illustrating both the defense de-rating and extreme single-name volatility.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highLockheed MartinAT&T
So what: Concrete levels for the de-rated cohort: LMT ~500 off ~700, T at ~22 — reference points for entry/exit if the SpaceX-threat narrative reverses.
A wave of mega-cap private IPOs — SpaceX, xAI, Anthropic, OpenAI — needs public markets to access new incremental buyers and unlock debt issuance, but the cash to buy them must rotate out of existing positions.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSpaceXxAIAnthropicOpenAIIPO market
So what: Anticipate forced rotation/funding pressure on incumbents as the IPO pipeline drains liquidity; identify likely sources of sold stock.
“Money's got to rotate from somewhere.” ▶ 7:25
Market 'gamification' and eroding guardrails 2
Multiple investor-protection guardrails are being removed at once — pattern-day-trading rule repealed, CFPB abandoned, lightly-regulated prediction markets/event contracts proliferating, elevated margin usage — turning prior market-structure tailwinds into potential headwinds.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highSECCFPBprediction marketsevent contractsretail traders
So what: Deregulation feeds near-term speculative froth but raises the odds of a disorderly downturn; treat the removed guardrails as a building tail risk.
≈ “All of a sudden I think a lot of the kind of guard rails have been pulled.” paraphrase
Adami argues the rule of law in markets will only return after a painful downturn that hurts investors, viewing such a reckoning as inevitable given the current 'casino'/gamification dynamic.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumregulationretail traders
So what: Positions for an eventual volatility/drawdown regime; the speaker sees mean-reversion in market discipline as a when-not-if event.
“I think it's going to take a downturn where people get hurt, which I think there's an inevitability to that to bring those rules back in play.” ▶ 20:29
Oil and the consumer pinch 2
Adami expects a geopolitical risk premium to persist in oil (still a $9-handle, i.e. ~$90) absent a meaningful ceasefire, since US adversaries have a vested interest in keeping prices elevated to weaken the US into the July 4th 250th anniversary.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highcrude oilenergygeopolitics
So what: Stay constructive on energy commodities and equities; expect a politically-driven 'push-pull' keeping oil bid into summer.
“I don't think the energy trade in terms of the commodity is over by any stretch. Nor do I think it's over in the equities.” ▶ 23:28
Elevated oil prices are already weighing on consumer discretionary spending, per commentary from retailers and fast-food chains noting consumers 'feeling the pinch.'
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumconsumer discretionaryretailersrestaurantscrude oil
So what: Watch retail/QSR guidance as a real-time read on how high energy prices are eroding discretionary demand.
US-led critical-minerals super-cycle 6
Paes-Braga argues the US is in 'inning one' of a new commodity super-cycle led by the United States re-industrializing — distinct from the 2001-2010 China/EM-led super-cycle — driven by national-security urgency around mineral independence.
thesis🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highcommodity super-cyclecritical mineralsUnited StatesChina
So what: Frames a multi-year secular bull case for miners and mineral-supply-chain assets; positions early in a cycle the speaker says has years to run.
“We're at the brink of the start of this commodity super cycle and the metals royalty company finds itself right smack in the middle of all of it.” ▶ 49:50
China controls 19 of 20 energy-related critical minerals and refines between 30% and 99% of global supply across metals, giving it security of supply and control of offtakes — the strategic gap the US must close.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highChinacritical mineralsrare earthsrefining capacity
So what: Quantifies the dependency; any US move toward refining/offtake independence is a multi-decade demand driver for Western mining and processing assets.
“19 of 20 critical minerals related to energy are controlled by China.” ▶ 33:08
Paes-Braga draws a direct parallel between today's minerals push and the US shale revolution of ~10-15 years ago, arguing critical minerals are 'where oil was before shale' — a bipartisan, multi-hundred-billion-dollar national effort akin to breaking OPEC's grip.
framework🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highshale revolutionOPECPermian BasinBakkenLNGcritical minerals
So what: Provides a historical analog (shale created huge new US industries and exporters) to size the potential investment opportunity in domestic minerals.
“Where there's more challenges in the mining space is the time it takes to get these operations going.” ▶ 39:05
The structural bottleneck is time, not just capital: average US mine development from discovery to build is 29 years, versus 45-60 days to drill an oil/gas well, leaving the US strategically exposed if China weaponizes supply.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highmining permittingcritical mineralsChina
So what: The 29-year lead time means existing/near-production assets and refining carry scarcity value; supply cannot respond quickly to demand or to a Chinese supply shock.
“From discovery to a build of a mine, the average has been 29 years.” ▶ 32:02
Iron ore independence — not headline rare earths — is the foundational opportunity; the Mesabi iron range in Minnesota that built American steel exemplifies the high-tonnage, jobs-and-taxes assets that matter most, while rare earths are a small slice of the market.
contrarian🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highiron oreMesabi RangeMinnesotasteelrare earths
So what: Redirects investor attention from hyped rare-earth names to large, economically fundamental commodities like iron ore where the real value sits.
“It may not be the headlines of rare earth, but iron ore is fundamental to society.” ▶ 36:05
Copper supply cannot meet demand: AI hyperscaler data centers use 40,000-50,000 tons of copper each, while Chile is not finding meaningfully more copper, Peru's new assets are early-stage, and the resource-rich Congo is hard to ramp due to infrastructure.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highcopperdata centersAIChilePeruCongo
So what: A constrained-supply, AI-driven-demand setup is structurally bullish copper; supply geography (Chile flat, Congo bottlenecked) limits the response.
“These data centers... they're using anywhere from 40 to 50,000 tons of copper.” ▶ 41:14
Government capital and policy tailwind 3
The US government has already committed ~$29 billion across agencies to critical minerals, and Paes-Braga expects total support to reach hundreds of billions — possibly low single-digit trillions — over the next decade given the urgency.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highUS governmentcritical mineralsindustrial policy
So what: A large, sustained public funding stream de-risks domestic mineral projects and is a direct tailwind for assets eligible for government support.
≈ “The last number I saw was they've already committed to $29 billion from different organizations within the government.” paraphrase
Government capital is flowing to the biggest projects because the policy theme is metal-by-metal independence, so scale plus location in tier-one jurisdictions (US, Canada, Australia) is the screen for which assets get backing.
framework🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: hightier-one jurisdictionsUSCanadaAustraliagovernment funding
So what: Favor large-scale projects in tier-one jurisdictions — they command valuation premiums and are the likeliest recipients of government support.
“We're going for the big ones we're going for the best.” ▶ 53:37
Foreign industrial capital is already reshoring the supply chain — e.g., Korea Inc. announced a $7.2 billion refinery project in Tennessee, the first new US refinery of its kind in what may be decades.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highKorea Inc.Tennesseerefiningsupply chain
So what: Concrete evidence the reshoring thesis is funded and underway; refining/processing capacity in the US is a high-conviction build-out area.
Metals Royalty Company (TMCR) business model 5
Metals Royalty Company (NASDAQ: TMCR) provides 'last money in' capital to late-stage mining projects in exchange for 20-50 year royalty/streaming cash flows — e.g., $132.5 million into the Mesabi/Minnesota iron-ore project (up to ~$500 million total) that already had $2.5 billion spent and first production in H2 this year.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highMetals Royalty CompanyTMCRMesabi RangeMinnesotairon oreroyalty streaming
So what: A pure-play, US-listed vehicle to gain diversified critical-minerals exposure via asset-backed royalties rather than single-commodity mining risk.
≈ “We put $132.5 million in. We're going to probably deploy another $132.5 and maybe up to around half a billion.” paraphrase
Paes-Braga benchmarks the model to precious-metal royalty firms (Wheaton, Franco-Nevada) at $40-50 billion market caps trading at 20-40x cash flow with only ~10-250 employees, implying a high-multiple, high-free-cash-flow, low-headcount business he says no one is replicating in base/critical metals.
framework🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highWheaton Precious MetalsFranco-Nevadaroyalty companiesMetals Royalty CompanyTMCR
So what: The royalty model historically re-rates to premium multiples; TMCR is pitched as a first-mover applying it to critical/base metals — a white-space re-rating thesis.
≈ “It's white space. No one's doing this.” paraphrase
TMCR's portfolio already spans nickel, copper, cobalt, manganese and iron ore; management plans 3-5 transactions a year focused on the largest assets, mirroring how Wheaton/Franco-Nevada derive most value from ~20-30 core deals.
data_point🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highMetals Royalty CompanyTMCRnickelcoppercobaltmanganeseiron ore
So what: Defines the deployment cadence and diversification; a concentrated, big-asset strategy where a few deals drive most of the value.
A portfolio royalty structure mutes commodity-price risk: short-term free cash flow is affected by price drops but it is not existential as it is for a single-commodity miner, and falling prices let the firm do its best (distressed) deals.
thesis🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highroyalty modelcommodity pricesMetals Royalty Company
So what: The model is counter-cyclical on deal-making — price downturns are an opportunity, not an existential threat, a key differentiator from pure miners.
“During times where commodity prices roll over, we will probably do our best deals.” ▶ 51:10
Paes-Braga's track record: his prior venture Lithium X bought a project for ~$4-6 million and sold it for ~$200 million in 17-18 months as lithium rose from $5,000 to $22,000 a ton, on a thesis that Tesla's EV demand would drive lithium — a template for the current minerals bet.
anecdote🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highLithium XlithiumTeslaBrian Paes-Braga
So what: Establishes operator credibility and the demand-anticipation playbook now being applied to copper/iron ore/critical minerals.
“Lithium went from 5,000 a ton to 22,000 a ton.” ▶ 51:45
Geopolitics and resource geography 4
Paes-Braga frames three resource superpowers: Russia is self-sufficient (gas plus prolific nickel, aluminum, iron ore, gold), China went outside its borders and 'economically colonized' Africa over the past 25-30 years, and the US must now do the same — leveraging Latin America.
framework🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highRussiaChinaUnited StatesAfricaLatin Americaresource nationalism
So what: Geographic framework for where Western mineral capital must flow; Latin America is the US's analog to China-in-Africa.
“China had to go outside their borders to maintain their economic position in the world and their military strength. They went to Africa.” ▶ 48:54
Paes-Braga calls the US move into Venezuela strategically 'prudent' — unlocking a mineral- and hydrocarbon-rich country with minimal collateral damage — and argues a stronger, stabilized Venezuela strengthens Latin America as a US-aligned resource base.
thesis🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highVenezuelaLatin Americaoil and gascritical minerals
So what: If Venezuela/Latin America stabilizes under US influence, it opens a major new resource frontier; watch regional stability as a catalyst for minerals access.
“I'd say a stronger Venezuela is a stronger Latin America.” ▶ 49:38
The US lacks sufficient domestic mineral endowment (unlike its oil and gas position), so achieving mineral security requires capital formation plus international partnerships across Canada, Australia and Latin America — it cannot be solved within US borders alone.
causal🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: highUnited StatesCanadaAustraliaLatin Americamineral endowment
So what: Domestic-only minerals plays are insufficient; the winning exposure includes allied-nation assets, not just US-soil projects.
“America today does not have enough mineral endowment... they do not have enough mineral endowment within their borders.” ▶ 47:33
Paes-Braga is skeptical that US mining 'exceptionalism' exists today — citing Freeport-McMoRan as the lone large US name but noting most of its operations are abroad and it has not delivered strong shareholder returns — implying the sector is wide open for a new winner.
contrarian🗣 Brian Paes-Braga (CEO, Metals Royalty Company)conviction: mediumFreeport-McMoRanUS mining sector
So what: The absence of a dominant, high-returning US miner is framed as the opportunity gap TMCR aims to fill.
≈ “Where's the exceptionalism in mining? Freeport McMoran. Well, okay.” paraphrase
The Tokenmaxxing Party Is Over and No One Wants to Admit It35 min39 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue the AI 'tokenmaxxing' boom is quietly inflecting: large cloud customers (Microsoft, Amazon, Uber) are slashing AI-token/Claude-code consumption after blowing through annual budgets in a quarter, which threatens the consumption-revenue narrative propping up OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs. They pair this with a broadly bearish macro read — record valuations, a strained lower-income consumer, sticky inflation, and parabolic moves in Dell/software/semis — and warn the S&P/NASDAQ could be cut in half within one to two years.
AI token consumption inflection ('tokenmaxxing' rollover) · 9AI hardware froth — Dell, memory, margin trap · 6Software rotation & enterprise-software rally · 6Macro, rates & Fed under Warsh · 7Strained consumer & retail valuation reset · 5Bubble valuation & crash-risk warnings · 6
AI token consumption inflection ('tokenmaxxing' rollover) 9
Model makers (OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, xAI's Grok) shifted from flat all-you-can-eat subscription pricing to consumption-based token billing, which is inflating their reported revenue ahead of IPOs by making per-query usage monetizable rather than commoditized.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGoogle/GeminixAI/GrokGPTClaude
So what: Consumption pricing is the load-bearing assumption behind AI-lab IPO valuations; if usage falls, the revenue story underneath those listings weakens.
“now these companies are charging by consumption and it's making their this is anthropic and and open. It's making their numbers look a lot better into their IPOs.” ▶ 21:36
Microsoft, Amazon, and Uber have all reported blowing through their entire annual AI-token/cloud budgets within the first months of the year and are now pulling back, signaling enterprise AI consumption may be rolling over rather than compounding.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMicrosoftAmazonUber
So what: Falling enterprise token consumption directly undercuts the usage-growth narrative supporting AI infrastructure and AI-lab valuations.
“they've been blowing through their entire year budgets in a matter of months” ▶ 22:01
Microsoft — which owns 27% of OpenAI, just invested $5 billion in Anthropic, and integrated both into Copilot as its key monetization unlock beyond cloud — is pulling its own engineers back from using Claude Code, a notable tell given Microsoft's deep AI-lab stakes.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMicrosoftOpenAIAnthropicCopilotClaude Code
So what: If the largest AI-lab backer is curbing its own token usage, the demand assumptions baked into Copilot monetization and AI-lab revenue look fragile.
“They own 27% of it. They just invested $5 billion in anthropic... And they are pulling back their engineers from using clawed code.” ▶ 22:12
Uber blew through its entire 2026 cloud-code/token budget within the first quarter and its CEO is explicitly citing a lack of ROI as the reason for pulling back AI spend.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highUber
So what: An explicit CEO 'no ROI' admission is harder evidence than budget overruns that the AI-productivity payoff is not yet materializing for buyers.
“They blew through their entire 2026 budget for cloud code and for tokens within the first quarter of this year. They are pulling back. Their CEO is specifically talking about not getting the ROI.” ▶ 22:35
'Tokenmaxxing' — engineers competing on internal leaderboards to consume the most tokens as a proxy for productivity — is a Goodhart's-law failure: a cost metric was turned into a target and ceased to measure productivity, so the apparent consumption boom may not reflect real value created.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGoodhart's lawJevons paradox
So what: Frames the token-usage surge as partly gamed rather than organic demand, implying downside when companies optimize the metric away.
“When a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure.” ▶ 25:24
The Jevons-paradox bull case for tokens (cheaper compute drives exponentially more usage, fueling the agent inflection) is being undercut in real time as the largest buyers cut consumption, suggesting efficiency gains may not produce the assumed runaway demand for AI labs.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highJevons paradoxAI agentsOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Challenges the core demand thesis underpinning AI-infrastructure and AI-lab valuations as IPOs approach.
“If that is being turned upside down guy, like then you're going to see consumption come down... If that goes away, it might question all of this.” ▶ 23:11
The real inflection/test for the AI bubble will be how OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI trade in the aftermarket after their IPOs, not in the pre-IPO private or public hype — that post-listing price action is where a market top would be signaled.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicxAISpaceX
So what: Tells investors to watch AI-lab post-IPO trading as the tell for the cycle top rather than pre-IPO sentiment.
“The real tell is going to come in the form of how these things trade after the IPOs. That's when you can start hearing people talk about ringing a bell at the top.” ▶ 24:16
Google/Alphabet is positioned to win the AI race by deploying Gemini across 13 platforms with over a billion users each without cannibalizing core Search, while also monetizing via GCP and selling TPUs as a second-source alternative to Nvidia GPUs.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumGoogle/AlphabetGeminiGCPTPUNvidia
So what: Positions Alphabet as the structurally advantaged AI distribution and infrastructure play versus pure-play labs and Nvidia-dependent compute.
Cloud providers that hold equity stakes in AI labs while simultaneously cutting their own customers'/engineers' token usage create a contradiction that will surface more enterprise pullback disclosures like Uber's.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highAmazonMicrosoftUberAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Signals more negative AI-consumption datapoints are likely coming, a forward catalyst for re-rating AI names lower.
“If you're a cloud provider and you have these investments in these companies and you're pulling back on the usage of your own customers, you're sure as going to hear more about like the Ubers and the like.” ▶ 25:48
AI hardware froth — Dell, memory, margin trap 6
Dell jumped 30% in one session (a ~$270B market cap) on a large AI-server number, is up nearly 300% from earlier-year lows, but is printing only an 18% gross margin versus the 23-24% it earned three years ago when AI-server demand was far lower — its margins are compressing, not expanding, even at peak demand.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highDellSuper Micro
So what: Margin compression amid record demand flags that the AI-server boom is commoditized; the parabolic move is unlikely to be a durable three-year trend.
≈ “here's a company that just printed a 18% gross margin... three years ago, okay, when they were not had nearly the demand... they were basically printing 23 24% margins.” paraphrase ▶ 26:53
Server assemblers Dell and Super Micro are caught in a margin vise: capacity-constrained component suppliers (Micron, Nvidia, Intel, AMD, SanDisk, Western Digital) hold pricing power, so server makers' margins likely fall further unless they can raise prices in a commoditized business.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highDellSuper MicroMicronNvidiaIntelAMDSanDiskWestern Digital
So what: Frames the AI server-assembler trade as structurally margin-disadvantaged versus upstream component suppliers — a relative pair-trade signal.
“if Micron and Nvidia and Intel and AMD and SanDisk and Western Digital have all this pricing power that can only weigh on their margins” ▶ 28:03
Micron is expected to swing from a negative gross margin three years ago to a ~77% gross margin, the embodiment of the 'cyclical becomes secular' narrative driving memory stocks up ~1,000% — a framing the hosts treat skeptically as unlikely to persist.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumMicron
So what: A 77% margin assumption in a historically cyclical memory business is a valuation/earnings risk if the cycle mean-reverts.
“Micron had a negative margin just three years ago and now they're going to expect it to have a 77% gross margin. Cyclical becomes secular.” ▶ 27:34
Sell-side analysts are badly behind the AI-hardware moves: the average of 29 analyst price targets on Dell was ~$230 while the stock traded north of $400, showing even specialist coverage failed to anticipate the rally.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumDell
So what: Wide gap between targets and price signals momentum-driven mispricing and limited fundamental anchor on the upside.
“the average price target for Dell, and I round it up, is about $230. And as you just mentioned, we just traded north of 400.” ▶ 28:44
Dell historically trades at a discount to the market and should not command a market multiple; at ~$300 it was ~19.5x forward (reasonable), but at ~$400 valuation becomes a headwind, and this quarter is likely the best result Dell reports for some time.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumDell
So what: Flags Dell as a peak-earnings, peak-multiple short/avoid candidate after the parabolic move.
“my sense is is this one we just heard might be the best thing you hear from Dell in quite some time.” ▶ 29:48
Parabolic 300%-in-two-months moves in hardware names like Dell are a bearish character signal, not a bullish one, and exiting only into strong up-days is the disciplined approach because round-trips down 15%+ feel far worse than profit-taking.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highDell
So what: A risk-management rule: take AI-hardware profits into strength rather than holding for momentum/narrative.
“when you see stocks like this go absolutely parabolic... it it's just it's not bullish.” ▶ 29:55
Software rotation & enterprise-software rally 6
The software ETF IGV bounced from an April low near $73 to its ~99-100 target at the 200-day moving average (its first move back above the 200-day since the first week of the year), making it logical to take profits and look for a re-entry rather than chase.

Oracle is up ~7.5% to a multi-month high and looks to have made a real technical bottom as the capital-raise overhang faded (after heavy debt issuance and a pledge not to raise more), potentially riding a tailwind into an OpenAI IPO.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumOracleOpenAI

Enterprise software is seeing indiscriminate level-buying: Palantir up ~10% (and ~7.5% the prior day for no specific reason as a laggard), ServiceNow up ~13%, and Salesforce up ~9% the second day after a mediocre quarter that initially left it unchanged.

Michael Burry has been publicly 'picking at' enterprise software, adding a notable-investor signal to the software rotation theme.
anecdote🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: lowMichael Burryenterprise software

Software is rallying hard while the semiconductor ETF SMH is still at all-time highs, meaning the software move is NOT funded by rotation out of semis — both are rising together, a sign of broad froth rather than a clean rotation.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumIGVSMHsemiconductorssoftware

“the SMH is still trading at all-time highs... So these software stocks have been able to do that without money really coming out in a meaningful way.” ▶ 18:30
Presidential commentary singling out individual companies (positive remarks on Micron and Palantir) is acting as a tailwind, with investors unwilling to fight stocks the administration appears to favor.
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumMicronPalantirTrump administration
So what: Policy/jawboning risk premium: names mentioned favorably by the president carry a political bid that can distort fundamentals.
“in a world where picking winners and losers and seemingly a track record to support it, people are not going to get in the way of this stuff.” ▶ 19:09
Macro, rates & Fed under Warsh 7
The S&P 500 is near an all-time high around 7,600 with momentum toward 8,000, the dollar near 99, and the 10-year yield having spiked above 4.65% before easing, with markets in a 'see no evil' posture ignoring bad news.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumS&P 500US 10-year TreasuryUS dollar
So what: Sets the late-cycle backdrop: all-time-high equities coexisting with elevated yields and a strong dollar, a tension that historically resolves badly.
≈ “S&P 500 basically at 7600, which is an all-time high... the market's sort of in the see no evil camp right now.” paraphrase
Former Treasury/Bessent adviser Joe Lavorgna argues the Fed should be ~100bp HIGHER — not cutting — because core PCE inflation is back near 4% while Fed funds sits below 4% (upper bound ~3.75%), a non-consensus hawkish view from someone seen as administration-aligned.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumJoe LavorgnaFederal Reservecore PCEScott Bessent
So what: An administration-adjacent strategist calling for hikes (against Trump's rate-cut wish) flags upside rate risk the market is ignoring.
“the Fed fund should be 100 basis points higher... you got inflation that's back near 4% and you have a Fed funds that's below 4%.” ▶ 7:59
Guy Adami's contrarian rates view: a Fed hiking cycle would be championed by the bond market — the front end rises on hikes but the long end (the 10-year everyone watches) could actually fall, re-inverting the curve and getting rates where the administration wants them.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumFederal Reserveyield curveUS 10-year Treasury
So what: Counterintuitive thesis: hikes could lower long-end yields — a setup for long-duration bonds if the Fed turns hawkish.
“I can make an argument that the back end, which is what everybody wants to focus on... would actually go lower” ▶ 9:56
The market has priced the next Fed rate cut all the way out to fall 2027, implying the Fed is largely irrelevant to current all-time-high pricing and is likely to simply stay the course with no pressure to act.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: If equities are at highs without needing cuts, monetary policy is not the marginal driver — rate-cut bets offer little upside catalyst.
“the next rate cut is priced into the fall of 2027... the Fed maybe doesn't matter all that much.” ▶ 10:33
Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh is framed as an independent reformist who won't be pushed around by the president, faces a hard first meeting on June 17th, and wants to reduce Fed communication with markets — adding policy-uncertainty risk.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: A reformist chair who communicates less injects unpredictability into rate expectations around the June 17 meeting.
Crude oil is sticky around $88-90 after the administration's Memorial Day commentary eased war/Iran tensions via a two-month ceasefire 'one-pager' that defers the hardest issues, yet equities are indifferent to the geopolitical outcome.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumcrude oilIran
So what: Oil holding ~$88 keeps an inflation/affordability overhang alive even as markets shrug off geopolitics.
“Crude oil has been a bit sticky up here. It's trading at 88 right now.” ▶ 2:14
The administration treats the stock market as a barometer of presidential popularity and has historically been spooked by the bond market, so a 10-year near 4.65% and a strong dollar are levels policymakers were actively focused on around Memorial Day.
framework🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumTrump administrationUS 10-year Treasurybond market
So what: Implies a policy 'put' tied to yields/equities — useful for anticipating rhetoric-driven reversals near stress points.
“there was no way in the Memorial Day weekend the administration was going to let the market sort of teeter... with crude oil still above $100 a barrel and interest rates zooming higher.” ▶ 2:41
Strained consumer & retail valuation reset 5
The US consumer is weaker than the market believes: auto loan payments now average ~$1,000/month, gas hovers near $4.50/gallon, and 90-day-plus credit-card delinquencies are above 13% — the highest in 14-15 years.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highUS consumerauto loanscredit card delinquencies
So what: Hard delinquency and cost-of-living data argue the consumer-spending leg of the bull case is cracking at the lower end.
“credit card delinquencies, 90-day plus, north of 13%, the highest level we've seen in 14 or 15 years.” ▶ 6:08
Costco fell ~12% in a straight line from a May 19 all-time high (~$1,096 to ~$954, at March-low support) and Walmart fell ~12-13% from its all-time high — both on valuation, not bad quarters, signaling these inflation-beneficiary 'trade-down' names were crowded trades.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highCostcoWalmart
So what: Even quality defensives are being punished purely on valuation — a warning that multiple compression is starting at the safest end of the market.
“These were meant to be the beneficiaries of inflation, the beneficiaries of a trade down by a consumer... were these two crowded trades” ▶ 12:19
The Walmart/Costco selloffs are valuation victims of their own success — solid comps but multiples too high — and the broader tell is the consumer is not as healthy as the market assumes regardless of either company's specifics.
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highWalmartCostcoUS consumer
So what: Reinforces that high-multiple defensives offer poor risk/reward when the consumer narrative turns.
“they're losing to it in the form of valuation. They're sort of a victim of their own success” ▶ 13:32
Dollar Tree (reported this week) and Dollar General (next week) are key reads on the low-end consumer, with Home Depot already disappointing, making the upcoming May jobs report (June 5) a critical near-term catalyst amid retail crosscurrents.
prediction🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumDollar TreeDollar GeneralHome DepotMay jobs report
So what: Dollar-store guidance plus the May payrolls print are the next datapoints to confirm or refute the weak-consumer thesis.
The economy is a 'K' where the upper-income consumer keeps spending on the wealth effect from record equities and a locked, high-value housing market with low legacy mortgage rates, while the lower part is strapped — a bifurcation propping up aggregate spending.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumUS consumerhousing market
So what: The bull case rests on high-end wealth-effect spending, which is itself hostage to equity prices — a reflexive vulnerability if markets fall.
“it's that higher earning consumer that continues to spend... when you think about the wealth effect from the stock market... that's keeping that upper part of the K going.” ▶ 6:35
Bubble valuation & crash-risk warnings 6
Valuation extremes are flashing red: the Buffett indicator (market cap to GDP) is at an all-time high and the CAPE (cyclically adjusted P/E) is at its second-highest reading in history (data back to the late 1800s) — though the hosts stress valuation is not a timing tool.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highBuffett indicatorCAPE ratioS&P 500
So what: Record long-horizon valuation metrics imply poor forward returns even if they can't time the top.
“the cape ratio, the cyclically adjusted price to earnings is the second highest reading in history.” ▶ 5:10
Guy Adami's explicit call: the S&P and NASDAQ will be cut in half at some point in the next one to two years, and this euphoric price action has never historically ended well — 'it is not going to be different this time.'
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQ
So what: A concrete, high-conviction drawdown call (~50%) that argues for active risk management in euphoric conditions.
≈ “The stock market, the S&P and the NASDAQ are going to get cut in half at some point in the next year or two.” paraphrase
The hosts reject the 'irrational exuberance' bull rebuttal — that Greenspan's Dec 1996 warning preceded four more years of ~25%/yr gains and an 85% NASDAQ surge in 1999 — arguing current conditions are unusually extreme and everyone is simply afraid to call the top after being burned in 1998 and 2006.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQAlan Greenspan
So what: Counters the 'bubbles can run for years' complacency with a claim that the current setup is qualitatively more dangerous.
“what's happened here, make no mistake about it, is very, very unusual. It will not end well.” ▶ 32:05
Concentration risk is acute: 30-40% of the biggest names sit in the ETFs/indices held in ordinary retirement accounts, so a mega-cap unwind would hit passive/retirement investors disproportionately.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumETFsindex fundsretirement accounts
So what: Index concentration means passive holders carry hidden single-stock-like risk into any mega-cap drawdown.
“because of the 30 and 40% of these biggest names in the ETFs or the indices that you own in your retirement” ▶ 32:14
Today's exuberance is 'eerily reminiscent' of conditions roughly a year before the 1929 crash, per Andrew Ross Sorkin's book '1929'; while not a definitive full-blown-crash call, the hosts say history is clearly rhyming with the 1920s-30s.
anecdote🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumAndrew Ross Sorkin1929 crash
So what: Adds a historical-analog frame reinforcing late-cycle caution, useful as a sentiment/positioning tell.
≈ “the excitement, the exuberance people feel today are eerily reminiscent of the things people felt... a year or so prior to one of the darkest periods in our history.” paraphrase ▶ 34:16
Dan Loeb said on Patrick O'Shaughnessy's podcast he is reviewing hedge-fund portfolios' semiconductor exposure and 'can't think of a better time to remain long' semis — but the hosts caveat he is also short other names and manages risk in ways retail cannot replicate.
anecdote🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: lowDan Loebsemiconductors
So what: A prominent bullish semi soundbite is being amplified out of its risk-managed, long/short context — a caution against copying headline positioning.
“he's talking about... how exposed they are to semis and he can't think of a better time to remain long them.” ▶ 30:34

Sourcery with Molly O'Shea 3 episode(s) · 98 insights

"We Don't Trust Agents" - What This CTO Knows That You Don't55 min42 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Merge's co-founders (CEO Shensi and CTO Gil) detail how an integrations/infrastructure company repositioned around enterprise AI: agent connectivity (Merge Agent Handler / MCP), model routing and cost control (Merge Gateway), and hard guardrails against agentic data exfiltration. They argue agents can't be trusted once connected to tools, that AI-generated code is creating a supply-chain security crisis, and that the real 2026 inflection is enterprises finally moving from 'ChatGPT in the browser only' to governed, audited agent deployment.
Merge's AI repositioning & product suite · 5Enterprise agent adoption & trust inflection · 7Agentic security & supply-chain risk · 8SaaS disruption & build-vs-buy · 4Salesforce, headless & API-first commerce · 4AI cost economics & model strategy · 3AI valuations & market structure · 2Customer base, reliability & growth metrics · 5Talent, hiring & AI-native culture · 4
Merge's AI repositioning & product suite 5
Merge made a deliberate ~18-month pivot built on three bets — using AI aggressively internally, re-aiming its existing unified-API product at AI companies, and launching net-new AI products — and the founders credit those bets for accelerating the business.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMerge
So what: A template for how an incumbent SaaS-infrastructure vendor stays relevant in the agent era; the pivot working is the bull case for Merge's valuation.
Merge now sells three products: Merge Unified (data sync/normalization to power enterprise search and RAG), Merge Agent Handler (an MCP server giving hundreds of integrations to internal and customer-facing agents), and Merge Gateway (model-routing infrastructure with a security layer plus cost-saving and smart-routing policies).
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeMCPRAG
So what: Maps the three monetization layers — data plumbing, agent connectivity, and model orchestration — where integration vendors are now competing.
Merge Agent Handler functions as a single central node through which all of an employee's AI tools (Claude Code, Codex, ChatGPT, Perplexity) connect to underlying SaaS, letting a CTO see all activity, catch security violations, and revoke or grant access centrally.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge Agent HandlerClaude CodeCodexChatGPTPerplexityMCP
So what: Positions Merge as the governance/control plane for enterprise agent access — a potentially defensible chokepoint as agents proliferate across tools.
Merge builds its own connector/tools even when no public MCP server exists for a target platform, removing integration coverage as a blocker for customers.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeMCP
So what: Breadth of connectors is the moat in integration-aggregation; willingness to hand-build closes the long-tail gap that MCP alone leaves open.
Merge offers granular per-field/per-action permissions on connected systems (e.g., letting a PR intern read Salesforce accounts but be hard-blocked from writing or deleting any data), filling a gap where native tools force an all-or-nothing access choice.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeSalesforce
So what: Fine-grained access control is a concrete differentiator and a buying trigger for regulated enterprises adopting agents.
Enterprise agent adoption & trust inflection 7
Most enterprises in 2025 restricted employees to ChatGPT in the browser with no tool connections, but in 2026 buyers are actively demanding governed agent deployment, marking a real adoption inflection that Merge says it is positioned to monetize now.
prediction🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highChatGPTMergeMerge Agent HandlerMerge Gateway
So what: If the 'agents actually get deployed' inflection is real, 2026 is the year agent-infrastructure revenue ramps — a timing signal for the whole category.
“This year we're now starting to see companies being like, "We need this. We need this."” ▶ 54:21
Enterprise trust in cloud-hosted AI is cyclical: GenAI's arrival triggered no-AI-training and no-cloud contract clauses, those reservations eased as buyers used and trusted models, but strong residual demand persists for AI running in customers' own infrastructure rather than multi-tenant cloud.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Vendors offering self-hosted/single-tenant deployment options win regulated enterprise deals; pure multi-tenant SaaS faces a procurement headwind.
“We don't trust a multi-tenant.” ▶ 14:15
AI-era buyers purchase integration infrastructure without knowing what they need — replacing the old SaaS buyer with seven API experts grilling on rate limits with buyers who ask for a diagram and just want to know if it works with their software.
contrarian🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeMCP
So what: Sales now requires consultative hand-holding and prescriptive best-practices, shifting GTM toward forward-deployed/solutions motions and raising the value of vendor expertise.
Large AI companies buy far faster than financial-services or SaaS buyers because competition is so fierce, and they often arrive having already built Merge's agent into a working proof-of-concept before the first sales call.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMerge
So what: AI-native buyers compress sales cycles dramatically; vendors serving them see faster revenue recognition but must support proactive, self-serve evaluation.
The most common enterprise agent connections are general productivity tools — ticketing, file storage (Google Drive, Box, Dropbox), messaging, email, and code repositories — with function-specific pull for accounting (QuickBooks, NetSuite, Xero) and marketing (HubSpot).
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: mediumGoogle DriveBoxDropboxQuickBooksNetSuiteXeroHubSpotSalesforce
So what: Shows where agent automation is landing first; these connected systems-of-record are both the demand drivers and the data-exfiltration risk surface.
A CTO of a large regulated financial firm told Merge his employees pressure him to allow agent automation ('all my friends are using agents') but compliance blocks connecting internal services to external ones — a demand-vs-governance gap Merge sells into.
anecdote🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Pent-up internal demand colliding with regulatory constraint is the precise wedge for agent-governance products; identifies the buyer and the pain.
Enterprises increasingly require agent-access governance to tightly couple with identity providers so that when an employee leaves, their agent access is immediately revoked.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Identity-provider integration is becoming table stakes for agent-governance vendors; flags IAM/SSO players as adjacent beneficiaries.
Agentic security & supply-chain risk 8
Merge's core security thesis is that agents are inherently untrustworthy and require hard guardrails and blocks — not just rules — because a non-deterministic agent told to move data can leak sensitive fields like social security numbers that a human-reviewed deterministic pipeline never would.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Frames the buy case for data-loss-prevention/guardrail layers around agents; 'we don't trust agents' is the product positioning for the whole category.
≈ “We don't trust agents. We say, "Hey, we can try to set rules, but there need to be hard guard rails and blocks for things like sensitive data being sent across."” paraphrase
Agentic coding is flooding GitHub with pull requests faster than humans can review, so malicious code slips into widely-used open-source packages, and Gil argues this supply-chain vector caused recent attacks and will worsen as AI-generated code grows.
causal🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highGitHubopen source
So what: Supply-chain security tooling and software-composition-analysis vendors face structurally rising demand; a second-order risk to every company depending on OSS.
“Agents are pushing a ton of code. You don't have enough humans to read all that code and so things are slipping by.” ▶ 15:23
An agent is harmless until connected to tools — 'an evil genius locked in a jail cell' — so the act of giving agents tool access, which every company is now racing to do, is precisely where security fails.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: high
So what: A clean mental model for where agent risk concentrates: the tool/integration boundary, which is exactly Merge's product surface.
“The second you connect it to tools, which is what everyone is trying to do right now, that's where everything goes wrong.” ▶ 0:42
AI is making breaches both easier to create and easier to find — Gil cites Whiz reportedly finding (via AI, not manual review) a vulnerability where a single git push of a file could grant access to every repository hosted on GitHub.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: mediumWhizGitHub
So what: Good models will surface long-dormant vulnerabilities in 20-30-year-old packages; both attackers and defenders scale, raising baseline breach frequency.
While on-call, Gil personally intervened against over a thousand bot signups in an hour actively scanning Merge's backend endpoints from IPs worldwide, illustrating that automated attacks are now constant and a single success can mean the end of a company.
anecdote🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Bot-detection and endpoint-protection are now operational necessities, not nice-to-haves; quantifies the rising automated-attack volume.
“All it takes is one and it could mean the end of your company.” ▶ 0:50
Gil argues a company's biggest security threat is internal — phished accounts, the wrong hire, or a planted spy — so Merge prioritizes background checks and reference calls over external port-scanning defenses.
contrarian🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Reframes security spend toward insider-threat and identity controls; relevant for HR-vetting and identity-governance vendors.
Attackers now overwhelmingly hold companies' reputations hostage rather than monetizing stolen data directly — they blackmail the breached firm to prevent a leak, because notification obligations and brand damage make the company value the data far more than the buyer does.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: high
So what: Explains ransomware/extortion economics and why breached firms quietly pay; cyber-insurance and incident-response demand follows this dynamic.
AI removes the labor and language barriers for global cybercrime — attackers who previously lacked English or coding skills now 'speak English perfectly, write code perfectly, and have unlimited manpower,' which Gil expects to drive more attacks.
causal🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: high
So what: Structural increase in the attacker base and capability; durable tailwind for the entire cybersecurity sector.
“They now speak English perfectly, they now write code perfectly, and they now have unlimited manpower, all driven by AI.” ▶ 0:36
SaaS disruption & build-vs-buy 4
Enterprise sales for large SaaS vendors is structurally weakening because the time and cost to rebuild the same product in-house has collapsed, eroding renewal-negotiation leverage as customers credibly threaten 'I could just build this.'
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highSaaS
So what: Bearish read on incumbent SaaS pricing power and renewals; a core driver of the 'SaaS apocalypse' debate and public-market SaaS volatility.
Rising consumer/employee expectations for built-in automation are squeezing legacy SaaS, because buyers no longer want to purchase a platform and build agents on top — they expect agentic automation out of the box.
causal🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highSaaS
So what: SaaS vendors that don't ship native agent automation risk losing to AI-native or buildable alternatives; a feature-gap thesis for shorting laggards.
“The SAS platform cannot keep up with the expected automation. It's just hard to compete.” ▶ 0:27
Merge's counter to SaaS disruption is a forward-deployed model: off-the-shelf software customized cheaply by AI to exact needs still beats rebuilding from scratch because it spares buyers the nuance and detail that fall outside raw coding time.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: mediumMerge
So what: The bull case for surviving SaaS — value migrates to vendors who pair ready-made software with low-cost AI customization, i.e., a forward-deployed engineering motion.
Both founders predict 'English becomes your programming language': as models ask clarifying questions and build robust systems, a plain-English instruction becomes a reusable, deterministic, infinitely-repeatable artifact, making agent-harness and workflow-builder platforms largely unnecessary.
prediction🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: high
So what: Bearish on standalone agent-orchestration/workflow-builder startups; the durable value is auditability and observability, not reliability tooling.
≈ “All that's going to matter is just can you type it in English somewhere, have that run on a periodic cadence for your background agents. Um, and then do you have auditability and observability for security?” paraphrase
Salesforce, headless & API-first commerce 4
The founders are bullish on Salesforce's headless (UI-less, API/MCP-driven) push, arguing its 20-year open-API ecosystem proves the moat was always the data layer, not the UI, so agents doing everything via API only deepens that lock-in.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highSalesforceMarc BenioffMCP
So what: Contrarian to the 'agents kill SaaS UIs' narrative for Salesforce specifically — frames it as a winner if data remains the system of record.
Companies must adopt headless/API-first because agents (and tools like Claude Code picking packages) will increasingly choose vendors automatically, so making signup, account creation, and payment frictionless for agents is now competitive necessity.
prediction🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highClaude Code
So what: Agent-driven vendor selection is a new distribution channel; products not consumable headlessly by agents risk being designed out of the buying decision.
Trust-based categories — infrastructure and security products — remain an exception to agent-driven, no-human purchasing, because buyers still want to meet a vendor and gauge reliability before committing.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: medium
So what: Defines which software categories retain a human, relationship-led sales motion versus those headed toward agent-automated procurement.
Marc Benioff is unlikely to be counted out because Salesforce has stayed dominant for 30 years through multiple tech shifts, which the founders attribute partly to his 'beginner's mind' philosophy of constantly reimagining the company from scratch.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: mediumSalesforceMarc Benioff
So what: A defense of Salesforce's incumbency through the AI transition; relevant to anyone weighing legacy-SaaS survivorship.
AI cost economics & model strategy 3
The 'token-max' productivity push is real but the AI bill hitting the CFO is far worse than expected — companies may double productivity without doubling budget — driving heavy demand for cost-control via routing products like Merge Gateway.
causal🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge Gateway
So what: AI-spend governance is an emerging budget line; FinOps-for-AI and model-routing/gateway vendors have a clear tailwind.
Margin discipline is rising across AI products — Cursor notably expanded its margins, which is very hard to do — and cheap open-source models are often the optimal route for trivial queries, though automating that routing is difficult.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: mediumCursoropen sourceMerge Gateway
So what: Validates intelligent model routing (cheap models for easy tasks) as a margin lever; Cursor cited as proof AI-app margins can improve.
Gil's hot take is that many companies over-engineer ML by building and training custom models while their product lags, when using a generic model and improving the product would serve customers better.
contrarian🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: high
So what: Buy-vs-build signal: custom-model investment often destroys value; favors foundation-model consumers over in-house training efforts.
AI valuations & market structure 2
Merge's founders warn that many AI companies are raising at 3,000x — or routinely hundreds-to-thousands-x — revenue multiples, and based on revenue figures they know firsthand, most will fail or be unable to raise their next round and forced into massive layoffs.
prediction🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: high
So what: An insider's bearish call on a coming AI-startup washout; flags refinancing risk and layoffs as the failure mechanism, not just valuation compression.
≈ “We're looking at thousandx to hundred-to-thousandx multipliers. A lot of these companies are going to fail.” paraphrase
Public and private AI markets tell very different valuation stories, and the founders expect a 'merging' of the two when OpenAI and Anthropic eventually go public.
prediction🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropic
So what: OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs would be the forcing event reconciling private and public AI valuations — a watch item for the whole sector's re-rating.
Customer base, reliability & growth metrics 5
Merge serves over 20,000 (possibly ~25,000) self-served organizations and over 400 enterprise customers, including OpenAI, Perplexity, Netflix, Uber, Mistral, Dropbox, Freshworks, Brex, JP Morgan, US Bank, Ramp, Drata, and Vanta.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeOpenAIPerplexityNetflixUberMistralDropboxFreshworksBrexJP MorganUS BankRamp
So what: A concrete enterprise/AI-lab customer roster signaling Merge's strategic position in the AI integration stack and its blue-chip logo coverage.
Merge grew revenue significantly last year while barely increasing headcount, which the founders attribute to AI-driven leverage on the business.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMerge
So what: Evidence that internal AI adoption can decouple revenue growth from headcount — the efficiency thesis investors look for in AI-native operators.
Merge powers core, mission-critical functions (login flows, AI model data lookups) for major customers at four-to-six-nines uptime, where an outage could lose a customer all of its own customers overnight — and the founders note some companies have recently collapsed this way.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highMerge
So what: Deep operational embedding raises switching costs (a moat) but also concentrates systemic risk; reliability is the product for infrastructure vendors.
Merge runs the business on capital-efficiency metrics — gross margins, cash burn, cash-burn multiple, and runway reviewed weekly — alongside revenue, and treats being the #1 product in its market as non-negotiable.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMerge
So what: Signals a disciplined, burn-multiple-focused operating posture consistent with a company positioning for durability through an AI-startup shakeout.
Merge climbed a 'logo ladder' — using each successive customer's reputation to win the next size tier — and made a concerted post-Series-B push to move up-market with a more mature sales motion and enterprise-ready product.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: mediumMerge
So what: A repeatable enterprise GTM playbook for infrastructure startups; reference-driven up-market motion as the path to blue-chip logos.
Talent, hiring & AI-native culture 4
Merge makes AI usage a cultural mandate tied to performance reviews across all functions — R&D, accounting, recruiting, finance, marketing all work in Claude Code — and screens in interviews for candidates who want to use AI, not necessarily those already cutting-edge.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highMergeClaude Code
So what: A concrete model for enforcing org-wide AI adoption; the 'AI usage in performance reviews' practice is a leading indicator of AI-native operating leverage.
Merge applies Keith Rabois's 'barrels vs ammunition' framework but argues AI shifts hiring toward more barrels, since one person with Codex or Claude Code can now do what previously required a manager plus a team of specialists.
framework🗣 guest, Merge CTO (Gil)conviction: highKeith RaboisCodexClaude Code
So what: AI changes the optimal org shape toward high-agency generalists; a hiring thesis for the AI-native staffing model.
Merge lets engineers expense whatever AI coding tools they prefer (Codex, Claude Code, or others) subject only to security reviews, treating tool choice as individual preference rather than standardizing.
data_point🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: mediumMergeCodexClaude Code
So what: Signals genuine, broad usage of both Codex and Claude Code inside an AI-forward company — a demand data point for both tools.
The founders prize 'missionaries over mercenaries' and weed out candidates chasing hot-company status, top-percentile cash with low burn, or instant senior titles — warning that hot-company hires abandon ship when things get hard.
thesis🗣 guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)conviction: highChristina Cordova
So what: A retention-risk lens for evaluating hot AI startups: teams stacked with status-chasing mercenaries are fragile in downturns.
Why Impulse Raised $500M to Move Things in Space | President & COO Eric Romo48 min28 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Impulse Space President/COO Eric Romo breaks down the unit economics behind Impulse's $500M Series D (>$1.025B total raised): the commercial LEO orbital-transfer-vehicle market is dead-on-arrival (small, ~20% gross margins, brutal working capital), but direct-to-GEO delivery (Helios) and high-delta-V defense maneuvering (Mera) for Space Force are the real, large markets. The defense use-case—rapidly closing on and characterizing unknown Chinese GEO assets—is the unlock, riding a Space Force budget request that quadrupled from ~$17B to ~$71B.
Series D economics & investor signal · 3Why the commercial LEO tug market is dead-on-arrival · 6Helios: direct-to-GEO delivery economics · 5GEO satellite miniaturization & Caravan rideshare · 4Defense unlock: Mera & Space Force · 6Operating model: vertical integration & cycle time · 4
Series D economics & investor signal 3
Impulse Space raised a $500M Series D led by 137 Ventures and Banner VC, bringing total funding to over $1.025B, and the round was massively oversubscribed by insiders alone before outside allocation.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse Space137 VenturesBanner VCspace economy
So what: Confirms private capital is crowding aggressively into deep-tech space; an oversubscribed-by-insiders round signals strong informed-money conviction and a likely high private valuation mark for a pre-revenue-scale hardware company.
“we're just massively overs subscribed just from insiders” ▶ 4:13
137 Ventures, lead investor in Impulse's round, is a large SpaceX shareholder owning more than 1% of SpaceX and is about to deliver an outsized return to its LPs, and recently raised ~$700M in new funds.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: high137 VenturesSpaceXBrex
So what: Identifies 137 Ventures as a concentrated SpaceX-exposure vehicle nearing a marquee exit; their backing of Impulse is a credibility signal and a tell on where SpaceX-adjacent capital is rotating next.
“somebody who owns more than 1% of SpaceX” ▶ 3:32
Impulse raised the $500M round with substantial cash already on the balance sheet and no forcing function, choosing to be opportunistic because investor demand for space was unusually high.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse Space
So what: Raising from strength rather than need signals the company sees a once-cheap capital window in space and is choosing to extend runway aggressively—watch for accelerated hiring and capex as a forward indicator.
“We had quite a lot of money on the balance sheet even before we raised this round” ▶ 5:03
Why the commercial LEO tug market is dead-on-arrival 6
The commercial LEO orbital-transfer-vehicle market is not investable: Rocket Lab does ~30 launches/year and an OTV play is essentially trying to steal share from that small base, leaving a market too small to build a large business on.
thesis🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highRocket LabElectronSpaceXTransporterorbital transfer vehicles
So what: A direct negative read on pure-play LEO 'space tug' startups; the addressable market is far smaller than the SPAC-era pitch implied, so revenue projections built on LEO repositioning should be heavily discounted.
“Rocket Lab does something like 30 launches a year and you're basically pitching that you're going to steal share from them” ▶ 12:08
The LEO arbitrage between Rocket Lab Electron (~$8M for 200kg) and a SpaceX Transporter slot (~$2M) only lets an OTV save a customer roughly $1M while earning ~20% gross margins—thin economics for hardware.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highRocket LabElectronSpaceXTransporter
So what: Concrete unit economics showing the LEO tug value proposition is structurally low-margin; investors can use the ~$8M vs ~$2M spread and ~20% gross margin as a benchmark to stress-test any OTV business model.
“like a 20% gross margin, something like that, it's not great in in hardware” ▶ 12:21
An at-scale LEO orbital-transfer business has crippling working capital dynamics—needing ~40 launches and roughly $50–70M tied up in pre-bought SpaceX launches for a company that had only raised $30M at the time.
causal🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highSpaceXImpulse Spaceorbital transfer vehicles
So what: The capital intensity of pre-buying launch inventory makes the LEO-tug model nearly unfinanceable at small scale—a key reason to favor space companies whose customers buy the launch directly.
“I need 40 launches, which means I need I don't know like 50 $70 million tied up in launch costs for a company that had only raised $30 million at the time” ▶ 12:37
Most SPAC-era space-mobility theses—LEO-to-LEO tugboating, satellite repositioning, and life-extension in LEO—have failed to produce real transactions and largely do not represent functioning markets.
contrarian🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highspace tugssatellite life extensionSPACs
So what: An insider rejecting the entire 'move things around LEO' category; useful for re-rating legacy SPAC space names and dismissing pitches built on repositioning/tug demand that has never materialized.
“just generally that market has not manifested at all” ▶ 27:35
Orbital debris remediation is characterized as largely a scam for raising capital: cleaning up all space debris would be worth only tens of millions of dollars today, with congestion graphics deliberately misleading.
contrarian🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highorbital debris remediationNASA
So what: A blunt negative on debris-cleanup startups raised on ESG/social-good framing; the economic value today is trivial, so such ventures should be valued on near-zero current demand, not headline congestion fears.
“it's a scam I think largely” ▶ 28:00
Satellite life-extension is economically useful only in GEO, where satellites are extremely expensive, and never in LEO where replacement is cheap and easy.
framework🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highsatellite life extensionGEOLEO
So what: A clean rule for evaluating on-orbit servicing plays: orbit determines whether the value proposition exists—GEO yes, LEO no.
“Useful in geo where uh satellites are really expensive... Never going to be useful in LEO. It's not a LEO. Just replace it.” ▶ 29:52
Helios: direct-to-GEO delivery economics 5
Helios delivers satellites direct to GEO the same day (~8 hours after launch) versus the 6–10 months a standard GEO transfer orbit takes, collapsing months of lost, non-revenue-producing time for operators.
framework🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosImpulse SpaceGEOFalcon HeavyFalcon 9
So what: The core value driver is pulling forward 6–10 months of satellite revenue; this time-value framing is how the entire Helios business case is underwritten.
“Helios gets them to their same same place they want to go the same day of the launch uh eight hours later” ▶ 19:51
The time value of going direct-to-GEO is in the middle-tens of millions of dollars per single Helios flight (pulled-forward revenue, contract-penalty avoidance), plus ~$5–10M in avoided hard costs like fuel and radiation shielding.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosGEO
So what: Quantifies Helios's value-based pricing ceiling at roughly $50M+ time value plus ~$7M hard cost per flight—the per-mission revenue envelope for modeling Impulse's commercial line.
“the middle of that distribution is in the you know is in the tens maybe middle tens of millions of dollars of value of that time per single flight of Helios” ▶ 24:54
Helios is priced between a Falcon 9 and a Falcon Heavy, undercutting the historical ~$70–80M Falcon 9-to-Falcon Heavy price gap while still going direct-to-GEO.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosFalcon 9Falcon HeavySpaceX
So what: The ~$70–80M Falcon Heavy premium defines Helios's pricing headroom; Impulse can capture most of that spread because its all-in price sits below Falcon Heavy.
“the difference between the Falcon 9 and a Falcon Heavy it's like 70 80 million something like that” ▶ 25:18
SpaceX is structurally reluctant to fly Falcon Heavy commercially because each launch shuts down the pad longer than a Falcon 9, making it more economical for SpaceX to fly Falcon 9s—leaving the commercial direct-to-GEO lane underserved.
causal🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highSpaceXFalcon HeavyFalcon 9Space ForceNASA
So what: SpaceX's own launch-cadence economics create the market gap Helios fills; commercial direct-to-GEO is scarce because Falcon Heavy flies mostly for government, not commercial customers.
“it's actually economically better for them to launch Falcon 9” ▶ 19:15
Helios sells as a service that contractually resembles a launch contract, with launch procurement flexible—Impulse buys the Falcon 9 in some deals (e.g. Caravan) while the customer buys it in others.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumHeliosCaravanFalcon 9SpaceX
So what: Letting the customer buy the launch in many deals sidesteps the working-capital trap that killed the LEO model—an important structural advantage over pure orbital-transfer businesses.
GEO satellite miniaturization & Caravan rideshare 4
GEO is undergoing the same miniaturization LEO already saw: monolithic ~4-ton, hundreds-of-millions-of-dollars satellites are giving way to much smaller (~2,200 lb) spacecraft, with Astranis a bellwether of this 'consumer-electronicization' of GEO.
thesis🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highAstranisGEOPlanet LabsStarlink
So what: A structural shift opening a new class of small-GEO operators and demand for small-satellite delivery; track Astranis and similar small-GEO players as the growth edge of the comms market.
“you're now finally seeing this almost uh uh you know consumer electronicization of geo spacecraft” ▶ 21:03
Caravan is a GEO rideshare ('SpaceX Transporter but for GEO') aggregating four-to-seven small spacecraft per flight; demand has exceeded expectations with flights already booked for 2028.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highCaravanImpulse SpaceSpaceXTransporterGEO
So what: Stronger-than-expected Caravan demand and a 2028 backlog validate the small-GEO thesis and give Impulse a second commercial revenue line beyond Helios.
“this as it turns out has been a really big hit in the market. More demand than we expected” ▶ 22:08
Availability of Helios/Caravan lets satellite makers redesign cheaper spacecraft—shrinking electric propulsion, radiation shielding and tanks—where the design savings alone can nearly pay for the Helios ride.
causal🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumHeliosCaravanelectric propulsion
So what: A second-order demand flywheel: direct-to-GEO delivery changes upstream satellite design economics, deepening customer lock-in and expanding the addressable market beyond a simple delivery fee.
“that by itself actually could come close to paying for the ride on Helios, just the changes that they made in the spacecraft” ▶ 22:46
The GEO comsat market has been roughly flat for the last 10 years, and Impulse is betting that improving operator economics via cheaper/faster delivery can finally reignite demand growth.
thesis🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumGEO comsat marketHeliosCaravan
So what: The bull case hinges on supply-side cost reduction expanding a stagnant market—an elastic-demand bet investors should watch via GEO order volumes rather than assume.
“the the geo comat market, you just look at the data, it's it's kind of flatted down in the last 10 years” ▶ 23:21
Defense unlock: Mera & Space Force 6
Mera's true product-market fit is defense, not commercial: a high-thrust, high-delta-V maneuvering spacecraft 'closer to a fighter jet than a tugboat' that lets Space Force rapidly close on and characterize unknown foreign assets in GEO.
thesis🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highMeraSpace ForceImpulse SpaceChina
So what: Reframes Impulse from a commercial logistics play to a defense-prime supplier; the defense use-case is the larger, better-funded market and the real driver of company value.
“the product market fit for Mera for a high thrust I delta V space vehicle is it's closer to a fighter jet than a tugboat” ▶ 30:11
Flying Mera once shifted Space Force conversations from open-ended exploration to a laser-focused, defined defense program, with Space Force effectively guiding Impulse through a v1-to-v2 redesign to fit the mission.
anecdote🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highMeraSpace ForceImpulse Space
So what: Demonstrated on-orbit capability is the gate to defense contracts; the customer co-defining the product signals deep, sticky government demand and a defensible position.
“all of a sudden our conversations with Space Force kind of shifted in tenant really quickly” ▶ 13:36
Space Force's budget request has roughly quadrupled from ~$17B a few years ago to ~$71B, driven by the new need to defend space assets against Chinese and Russian threats.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highSpace ForceChinaRussiaStarlinkStar Shield
So what: A ~4x budget expansion defines the funding tailwind behind defense-space companies; this is the single biggest macro driver for the Mera/defense thesis and the sector broadly.
“Space Force's budget going from $17 billion a few years ago to now the request is for $71 billion” ▶ 33:13
