Defending LEO assets relies on proliferation (thousands of cheap targets), but that strategy is impractical in the volume between LEO and GEO, which is literally a million times larger—so defense there requires fast-maneuvering spacecraft instead.
framework🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highSpace ForceLEOGEOMera
So what: The million-times-volume argument is the structural reason high-delta-V maneuvering (Impulse's niche) is irreplaceable for GEO defense; proliferation cannot solve it, creating a durable demand moat.
“Neo and Gio are a million times bigger literally a million times bigger than than Leo” ▶ 33:54
Ground-based tracking cannot resolve what a foreign GEO satellite actually is (a GEO object appears as roughly one pixel), so physically maneuvering close to it for characterization is the only way to assess capabilities and threats.
causal🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highChinaSpace ForceGEOMera
So what: The physics of GEO observation mandates proximity inspection, which only fast in-space maneuvering enables—an enduring, non-substitutable demand source as China launches more GEO assets.
“the picture of the thing in geo is literally like one pixel is a dot you just don't I have no idea what the heck that thing is” ▶ 35:03
China is funding roughly five separate companies attempting to replicate SpaceX's launch capacity, largely for defense rather than purely commercial purposes, intensifying the great-power space race.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highChinaSpaceXSpace Force
So what: China's multi-company SpaceX-replication push is the adversary backdrop driving US defense-space spend; a key geopolitical signal for the sustained Space Force budget trajectory.
“They've got, you know, five different companies trying to be SpaceX over there” ▶ 32:53
Operating model: vertical integration & cycle time 4
Impulse's core operating edge, inherited from early SpaceX, is reducing development cycle time by insourcing component manufacturing—building in-house rather than waiting ~2 weeks per iteration on outside vendors.
framework🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceSpaceX
So what: Vertical integration as a deliberate speed lever distinguishes Impulse from traditional primes; iteration velocity is the durable competitive advantage investors should weigh in deep-tech hardware.
“if you want to move quickly you absolutely must insource a lot of the the development” ▶ 43:09
Legacy prime contractors' boast of suppliers across 50 states masks supply-chain risk and slow development cycles, the opposite of the vertically integrated, fast-iterating model Impulse runs.
contrarian🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highdefense primesImpulse Space
So what: A direct critique of incumbent primes' distributed-supplier model; frames new-space vertically integrated entrants as structurally faster and lower-risk in development.
“they very proudly proclaim like hey we have suppliers in 50 different states... those 50 different suppliers in 50 different states on their spacecraft create a ton of risk” ▶ 43:34
Impulse is ~500 people, scaling Mera production to one unit per month, building Helios as a third stage roughly the size of a Falcon 1 second stage, and developing additional engines (e.g. the announced RALE engine); spend is ~85–90% technical headcount, not capex.
data_point🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceMeraHeliosRALE engineFalcon 1
So what: Counters the assumption that deep-tech space is capex-heavy—people are the dominant cost—so the $500M raise translates mainly into ~200+ technical hires and parallel program execution to watch as a progress metric.
“it's typically you know 85 to 90% is those people and the rest is kind of overhead like me” ▶ 44:46
Founder Tom Mueller's propulsion pedigree (SpaceX employee #2, building engines in his garage post-retirement) let Impulse capitalize quickly and recruit best-in-class technical talent, since engines are the hardest critical-path component.
anecdote🗣 Eric Romo (President & COO, Impulse Space)conviction: highTom MuellerImpulse SpaceSpaceX
So what: Founder quality and propulsion know-how are the talent/funding moat in a sector where few can recruit across the needed technical breadth—an intangible but decisive due-diligence factor.
“when you're when you're Tom Mueller and you start a company you can just kind of raise your hand and people have been sending checks your way” ▶ 9:09
Tom Mueller: SpaceX's #1 Employee Built the Merlin Engine. Now He's Building Impulse Space44 min28 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Tom Mueller, SpaceX founding propulsion lead and Merlin-engine designer, tours his startup Impulse Space, which is building in-space transportation: the Mirror orbital maneuvering vehicle, the Helios kick-stage, and a high-performance methalox engine (Denib). He frames launch as 'mostly solved' and in-space mobility plus cislunar/lunar logistics as the next investable frontier, and argues compute and resource extraction will inevitably move off Earth.
In-space transportation as the next frontier post-launch · 5Propulsion edge: Denib engine and performance records · 5Vertical integration and advanced manufacturing as moat · 5Propulsion roadmap: electric, solar-electric, and nuclear · 4Compute and resources moving off Earth · 4Lunar priority, SpaceX read-throughs, and ecosystem · 5
In-space transportation as the next frontier post-launch 5
Tom Mueller's thesis is that Earth-to-orbit launch is 'mostly solved' by SpaceX, so the next big opportunity is moving payload and cargo around once it is already in space, which is the entire reason he founded Impulse Space.
thesis🗣 guest, Tom Mueller (founder/CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceSpaceXin-space transportationorbital transfer vehicles
So what: Positions in-space mobility / orbital transfer as the next distinct space-economy layer to back, separate from launch incumbents; Impulse is a pure-play on it.
≈ “the next big opportunity is to move all that payload, all that cargo around in space” paraphrase
Impulse Space's product stack is a two-part architecture: Mirror, a highly propulsive orbital maneuvering vehicle that can stay on orbit up to five years, and Helios, a kick-stage that adds an effective third stage to a two-stage rocket to deliver payloads to high-energy orbits.
framework🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse SpaceMirrorHeliosorbital maneuvering vehicle
So what: Clarifies Impulse's dual product lines for anyone tracking the orbital-transfer-vehicle (OTV) competitive set vs. rivals like Momentus, Launcher/Vast, or Atomos.
Helios can take a 4-ton satellite from low Earth orbit to geostationary orbit, deliver up to 5 tons of payload to the Moon on a single Falcon 9, and improve payload to Mars by up to a factor of five.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highHeliosFalcon 9geostationary orbitMoonMars
So what: Quantifies the performance leverage of a dedicated kick-stage riding on cheap Falcon 9 capacity, the economic basis for the OTV business case.
≈ “It can take, you know, all five tons of payload to the moon on a Falcon 9. It can improve your payload to Mars by up to a factor of five.” paraphrase
Impulse already has three Mirror spacecraft on orbit, performed an autonomous rendezvous bringing two of them within 1,200 meters of each other, and had to execute a collision-avoidance maneuver within the first three to four months of operations.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse SpaceMirrorrendezvouscollision avoidance
So what: Demonstrated on-orbit rendezvous and maneuvering is a hard technical milestone that de-risks Impulse vs. paper-stage competitors and signals readiness for servicing/inspection missions.
≈ “We did a rendezvous last year between two of them. Got within 1,200 meters of each other, which is really damn close in space.” paraphrase
Impulse Space is also bidding on lunar lander work, including human landers on the Moon, which Mueller envisions powered by a scaled-up multi-engine version of its Denib engine.
thesis🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse Spacelunar landerDenib engineMoon
So what: Signals Impulse intends to compete for NASA/commercial lunar-lander contracts, expanding its addressable market beyond OTVs into cislunar logistics.
Propulsion edge: Denib engine and performance records 5
Impulse's Denib is an oxygen-rich staged-combustion methalox engine producing ~15,000 lbf of thrust, which Mueller claims will be the highest-performing hydrocarbon engine ever flown thanks to very high mixing efficiency and a large high-area-ratio carbon nozzle.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highDenib enginestaged combustionmethaloxrocket propulsion
So what: A record-class in-house engine is the core IP moat for Impulse and the basis for its claim to be the 'prime mover' across its product line and future landers.
≈ “this engine will probably be the highest performing hydrocarbon engine ever flown because it has a very high area ratio” paraphrase
Mueller built the Merlin engine at SpaceX, which he states is the most reliable rocket engine ever developed and has the highest thrust-to-weight of any rocket engine ever developed, and he led propulsion for Falcon, Dragon, and the origins of Starship.
anecdote🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highMerlin engineFalcon 9DragonStarshipSpaceX
So what: Founder pedigree is a key diligence signal: the person who built SpaceX's flagship engine is the technical core of Impulse, lowering execution risk on the engine roadmap.
≈ “the Merlin engine which is currently flying on Falcon 9, the most reliable rocket engine ever developed and also the highest thrust to weight of any rocket engine ever developed” paraphrase
The Denib engine targets an ISP of roughly 385-400 seconds versus ~290 seconds for Impulse's small Saif thruster, and its carbon-ceramic nozzle skirt is expected to run over 3,000 degrees F, which Mueller claims would be the hottest nozzle ever flown in space.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highDenib engineSaif thrusterISPspecific impulse
So what: Specific-impulse and thermal records are the quantitative performance edge that justifies premium pricing and competitiveness for high-energy and lunar missions.
≈ “This this engine has about a 290 second ISP. Whereas the DNA engine will have almost 400 second ISP.” paraphrase
Oxygen-rich staged combustion is, in Mueller's words, a 'very difficult development,' and Impulse is only now finishing turbopump development and beginning engine testing, signaling Denib is still pre-flight maturity.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: mediumDenib enginestaged combustionturbopumpengine testing
So what: Flags the key technical risk and timeline gap on Impulse's flagship engine for anyone underwriting the company's near-term lunar/GEO ambitions.
≈ “oxid stage combustion is a difficult very difficult development” paraphrase
Mueller states it takes three iterations to reach a 'really tight' rocket-engine product, citing that both Merlin and Raptor required three versions to mature, a reusable framework for judging where an engine program sits on its development curve.
framework🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highMerlin engineRaptorSpaceXengine iteration
So what: A heuristic investors can apply to any new-space engine program to gauge maturity and remaining iteration risk before flight readiness.
“it takes really three three iterations to get to a really really tight product” ▶ 25:17
Vertical integration and advanced manufacturing as moat 5
Impulse Space is extremely vertically integrated, running its own machine shop, assembly, propulsion, and test areas on-site to build, assemble, test, and iterate rapidly, a model Mueller explicitly carried over from SpaceX ('vertically integrated, hire the best, move fast').
framework🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse Spacevertical integrationSpaceXrapid iteration
So what: Vertical integration plus fast iteration is the operational playbook that produced SpaceX's cost advantage; its replication is a key bull signal for Impulse's margins and speed.
“Vertically integrated, hire the best and move fast. That that's what we learned at SpaceX.” ▶ 42:00
Mueller calls metal 3D printing 'almost like a cheat code' for rocket engines because complex injector and cooling passages can be drawn and printed directly, dramatically easing what is otherwise difficult machining, welding, and brazing.
causal🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: high3D printingadditive manufacturingrocket engines
So what: Reinforces additive manufacturing as a structural cost/speed advantage in new-space propulsion, relevant to suppliers like Velo3D and EOS and to the broader make-vs-buy thesis.
≈ “For rocket engines, it's almost like a cheat code. It's so easy to make a high performance rocket engine with 3D printing” paraphrase
Impulse bought its metal 3D printers within the first year of founding and runs three in-house, including Velo3D machines printing a proprietary burn-resistant alloy called Novaloy for hot-oxygen parts like the turbopump and main injector.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse SpaceVelo3DNovaloyInconel3D printing
So what: Names Velo3D as a printer supplier to Impulse and flags a proprietary alloy as IP, useful for mapping the additive-manufacturing supply chain in new space.
Impulse brought composite overwrapped pressure vessel (COPV) tank production in-house early because tanks are a major portion of spacecraft mass and a properly designed COPV weighs about half of a state-of-the-art all-metallic titanium tank.
causal🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highCOPVcomposite tankscarbon fiberImpulse Space
So what: Mass reduction directly improves payload economics; controlling COPV production internally is both a performance and a supply-chain-resilience advantage.
≈ “a properly designed COPV will be about half the mass of a state-of-the-art all metallic tank like a titanium spacecraft sphere” paraphrase
COPVs are hard to source from industry, with quoted lead times of one to two years and prices of hundreds of thousands of dollars apiece, which is why Impulse insourced their production to control quality, quantity, and cost.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highCOPVsupply chainspace components
So what: Quantifies a real space-supply-chain bottleneck (long lead, high cost on pressure vessels) that creates both insourcing pressure and an opportunity for merchant suppliers.
“if you wanted to buy one of these, it might take, you know, after you order it might take a year or two to get them and be hundreds of thousands of dollars a piece” ▶ 16:44
Propulsion roadmap: electric, solar-electric, and nuclear 4
Impulse uses Krypton-fueled Hall-effect electric thrusters (the same type used by Starlink) for north-south station-keeping in geostationary orbit, preserving chemical propellant for fast customer maneuvers, blending electric and chemical propulsion in one OTV.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highHall thrusterelectric propulsionKryptonStarlinkgeostationary orbit
So what: A hybrid electric+chemical architecture differentiates Impulse from all-electric GEO satellites; krypton/hall-thruster supply chain is a watch-item.
≈ “That's a hall thruster just like Starlink has. It runs on Krypton.” paraphrase
Mueller predicts solar-electric propulsion will remain the dominant form for cislunar and Earth-Mars transport on cost and performance, especially as space solar panels get very light, while nuclear-electric propulsion becomes important only for the outer planets.
prediction🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highsolar electric propulsionnuclear electric propulsioncislunarMars
So what: Sets a hierarchy for where to expect propulsion R&D dollars and contracts; tempers near-term nuclear-propulsion hype for Earth-Mars routes.
Mueller is a strong proponent of nuclear-electric propulsion for the outer planets but expects big nuclear stages are likely a few decades away absent more funding, noting NASA's SPACE Reactor 1 (SR1) Mars mission is starting the timeline now.
prediction🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: mediumnuclear electric propulsionNASASR1outer planets
So what: Frames nuclear propulsion as a long-dated, funding-dependent theme rather than a near-term investable wave; SR1 is the early demonstrator to track.
“I think it's pretty far in the future... maybe a few decades away before we're really doing big nuclear stages” ▶ 36:49
Mueller argues a nuclear-electric stage would transform deep-space science economics by letting probes slow down and enter orbit around distant bodies like Pluto rather than flying past, and by powering instruments and radios for decades, unlike fading RTG-powered Voyager.
causal🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highnuclear electric propulsionNew HorizonsPlutoVoyager
So what: Articulates the long-term value case for nuclear propulsion that could justify future NASA/government funding flows into the sector.
Compute and resources moving off Earth 4
Mueller's hot take is that compute must move to space because it only needs power as an input and produces data as an output, making it the ideal megastructure to build off-planet, with data beamed down via terawatt laser links.
thesis🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highdata centers in spacecomputespace solarlaser communications
So what: A founder bull case for orbital data centers; if it materializes it expands demand for in-space transport, power, and laser-comms infrastructure that OTVs like Impulse serve.
“compute needs to move to space. It's it makes the most sense of anything to move to space to build mega structure because all you need as an input is power and all you have as an output is data.” ▶ 39:30
Mueller cites that compute power demand is growing more than 15% per year and that some project it could equal all of Earth's current base power use within 20-30 years, framing terrestrial power scarcity as the forcing function pushing compute to orbit.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: mediumcompute power demanddata centerselectricity
So what: Quantifies the power-constraint thesis behind both terrestrial-power scarcity trades and the space-data-center narrative.
“compute power for compute is growing at greater than 15% per year. Some people say that it'll be equal to all the earth's base power right now in about 20 or 30 years.” ▶ 39:59
Mueller says the real bottleneck on data centers is how fast power can be procured, citing up to seven years to get power for a new data center today, which is the gap orbital data centers could close.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: mediumdata centerspower procurementgrid interconnection
So what: Frames grid-interconnection delay (up to 7 years) as the binding constraint on AI buildout, a key signal for power, grid, and alternative-siting investments.
≈ “it can take, you know, up to seven years right now to get power for a new data center” paraphrase
Mueller predicts copper scarcity (driven heavily by data-center demand) will force space resource extraction from the Moon and asteroids, which he says hold a near-infinite, thousands-of-years supply, and could also drive a shift toward photonic chips.
prediction🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: mediumcopperspace miningasteroidsMoonphotonic chipsdata centers
So what: Links AI/data-center copper demand to both a commodity-scarcity thesis and long-dated space-mining and photonics optionality.
≈ “people are predicting we're going to run out of copper here in the next few years. Just data centers require so much copper. We need to start getting copper from the moon, from the asteroids.” paraphrase
Lunar priority, SpaceX read-throughs, and ecosystem 5
Mueller believes the Moon is more important than Mars in the near term, aligning with Jared Isaacman's stated NASA goal of building and staying at a base on the Moon, which shapes where he is steering Impulse's lander ambitions.
contrarian🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highMoonMarsJared IsaacmanNASAlunar base
So what: A Moon-first stance from a top space technologist signals where near-term commercial and NASA contract dollars (and Impulse's strategy) are likely to concentrate.
“I think the moon is more important than Mars in the near term.” ▶ 42:50
Mueller recounts that SpaceX's internal Starlink spreadsheets showed compelling ROI from the start, and insiders pushed to build and launch satellites as fast as possible while public naysayers doubted it, foreshadowing the business that became SpaceX's value driver.
anecdote🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highStarlinkSpaceXsatellite constellations
So what: Insider confirmation that Starlink's economics were obvious internally early reinforces the pattern that vertically integrated mass-production constellations can hockey-stick valuations.
“This is going to, you know, this is going to hockey stick this the stock which it exactly did.” ▶ 41:03
Impulse Space supplied the complete propulsion system, including thrusters, for Vast's space station, indicating Impulse is already a propulsion vendor to commercial space-station developers.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highImpulse SpaceVastspace stationpropulsion
So what: Confirms a B2B revenue channel for Impulse beyond its own OTVs: selling propulsion subsystems to the emerging commercial-station market (Vast, and by extension Axiom/Orbital Reef peers).
“We provided the whole propulsion system for vast including the thrusters.” ▶ 16:17
Space-situational-awareness warnings to operators come from both the U.S. Air Force and commercial providers such as LeoLabs, which Impulse relied on for its early collision-avoidance maneuver.
data_point🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highLeoLabsU.S. Air Forcespace situational awarenesscollision avoidance
So what: Names LeoLabs as a commercial SSA provider in operational use, a data point for the growing space-traffic-management and tracking market.
Mueller credits Elon Musk's edge to urgency and a talent obsession, stating the key to success is a great team and that recruiting top talent is a core practice he replicates at Impulse.
framework🗣 guest, Tom Muellerconviction: highElon MuskSpaceXtalentImpulse Space
So what: Reinforces that talent density and urgency are the soft moats behind new-space outperformance, a qualitative diligence lens for the sector.
“the key to success is having a great team” ▶ 41:48

Natalie Brunell 2 episode(s) · 77 insights

I Was a Bitcoin Skeptic — Until This67 min37 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ben Honeywell, CFO of Prevalon Energy (first corporate treasury to hold Strategy's STRC/'Stretch' preferred), explains the investment case for Bitcoin-collateralized perpetual preferreds yielding ~11.5-13%, why a self-healing price floor makes them low-risk, and why Strive's daily-dividend SATA could be the first credible challenger to Strategy. He pivots to the AI/data-center energy buildout, arguing US power shortages, off-grid gas/solar generation, battery storage, and China's manufacturing/rare-earth edge are the underpriced second-order story.
STRC / Bitcoin-backed preferreds as a treasury asset · 10Strive SATA & the daily-dividend innovation · 4Strategy's credit-rating roadmap & capital-markets strategy · 4AI / data-center energy bottleneck · 5Battery storage economics & grid stabilization · 5China, rare earths & manufacturing edge · 4Macro: inflation, affordability & the physical ledger · 5
STRC / Bitcoin-backed preferreds as a treasury asset 10
Prevalon Energy (a Mitsubishi Power JV) was the first corporation to hold STRC ('Stretch'), Strategy's Bitcoin-collateralized perpetual preferred, on its corporate treasury — signaling these instruments are migrating from retail into operating-company balance sheets.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategyMSTRPrevalon EnergyMitsubishi Power Systems AmericasBitcoin
So what: Corporate-treasury adoption of STRC is a new structural demand source for Strategy's preferreds; watch other operating companies (not just BTC treasury firms) for similar disclosures.
“the first corporation to hold STRC, the digital credit instrument issued by Strategy” ▶ 0:44
STRC's prospectus structure caps the buy-side price near par (~100.01) regardless of size, so a buyer can deploy $1-2 billion without pushing the preferred price up — the liquidity risk is entirely on the exit, not the entry.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategy
So what: Large allocators can size into STRC without slippage; the binding constraint to model is exit liquidity versus average daily volume, not entry impact.
“you can bring a billion dollars or $2 billion to STRC and your price is not going to get above 100.01” ▶ 16:51
STRC has a 'self-healing floor' around 98-99: a sharp price drop doubles the yield, drawing in yield-chasers and cost-basis improvers who buy the dip, while Strategy's redemption mechanics set the ceiling near par — making a sustained de-peg unlikely.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategyBitcoin
So what: Frames STRC as a bounded, mean-reverting yield instrument rather than a directional Bitcoin bet; the floor is the key risk parameter to monitor in a stress event.
“it is it's a self-healing floor. The market heals the floor and strategy sets the ceiling” ▶ 19:28
Honeywell dismisses the de-peg-in-a-stress-event thesis: he argues the 80s/low-90s dips seen since the July launch reflected early misunderstanding, and that even a hypothetical 50% drop would double the yield and be quickly bought, so he is 'not concerned' about depegging.
contrarian🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategyBitcoin
So what: Counters the most common bear case on Bitcoin-backed preferreds; useful as a stress-test framework but note it is the view of a large holder talking his book.
“if you were to have a, you know, let's call it a crazy drop, say 50% drop. If you had something like that, the yield would double. And there's too many people chasing that yield” ▶ 18:57
STRC behaves predictably around its dividend: liquidity peaks the day before ex-dividend, the price drops roughly 94 cents right after (versus a ~95 cent dividend), and capital rotates out post-dividend, creating a brief illiquidity window that forces treasury holders to hold ~45-60 days or keep emergency cash buffers.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategy
So what: Treasury users must match STRC holdings to cash not needed for ~60 days and keep money-market buffers; the post-ex-div drop creates a recurring entry point for higher effective yield.
“you normally get about a 94 cent drop which just ties in and the math there is pretty simple. You make 95 cents if you get the dividend” ▶ 14:43
STRC holders are ~80% retail, not institutional — surprising given the high yield — because publicly held corporations face governance limits (no single-asset overexposure, debt/AAA-only mandates) and Strategy's polarizing reputation blocks institutional treasury adoption until a credit rating exists.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumSTRCStrategyMichael Saylor
So what: Institutional STRC demand is a future catalyst gated on credit rating and governance acceptance; the 80% retail base is the current marginal price-setter.
“I was actually surprised when Fong revealed that it was 80% retail holders” ▶ 19:53
Honeywell argues publicly held corporations would benefit most from STRC because they hold retained earnings in cash yielding only 3.5-4% taxed as ordinary income (a 'melting ice cube'), whereas STRC offers tax-deferred income — making a large STRC holding accretive for shareholders.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategy
So what: Positions STRC as a cash-management upgrade for corporate treasuries sitting on idle cash; the tax-deferral angle is the differentiated pitch versus money-market funds.
“a large holding in on a corporate balance sheet uh would be, you know, very very accreative for shareholders” ▶ 22:27
STRC is best suited to 'lumpy' project-based businesses that hold large cash balances idle for 2-4 months between deployments, rather than to small businesses where the cash is the owner's own — a targeting framework for which treasuries the product fits.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumSTRC
So what: Helps identify the addressable corporate market for Bitcoin-backed preferreds: capital-intensive, project-cash-flow businesses, not owner-operator small firms.
“if you have a lumpy projectsbased business where you you may have a large amount of cash that comes in that will sit for three months or two months or four months um putting that to work while it's waiting to be deployed that's the key” ▶ 22:57
Honeywell reframes the 'too good to be true' objection to STRC by analogizing it to an insurance company: premiums function like capital raises, claims like the dividend, and the underlying balance sheet (stocks/bonds for insurers, Bitcoin for STRC) backs it — i.e. it is a known structure, not a Ponzi.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCStrategyBitcoinJeff Walton
So what: The insurance-float analogy is the cleanest mental model for valuing Bitcoin-backed preferreds and for rebutting Ponzi accusations.
“is an insurance company a Ponzi? I was like that is that is the best way to explain it” ▶ 8:40
STRC liquidity has matured: Prevalon at times made up a significant share of Stretch's daily trading volume early on, but the rule of thumb is that exiting more than ~7% of average daily volume moves the price down — the binding scale constraint for treasury-size positions.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSTRCPrevalon Energy
So what: The 7%-of-ADV exit rule is a concrete sizing constraint for any large STRC/SATA position; rising volume is the gating metric for institutional-scale entry.
“if you're more than around 7% of the average daily volume of an individual uh stock, you tend to move the price when you get out” ▶ 17:19
Strive SATA & the daily-dividend innovation 4
Strive's move to daily dividends on its SATA preferred is described as a potential game-changer that could let Strive raise immense capital and become the first credible challenger to Strategy's preferred-issuance lead.
prediction🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumStriveSATAStrategyJeff Walton
So what: Strive/SATA is the name to watch as the #2 in Bitcoin-backed preferreds; rising SATA volume would be the signal that it can absorb treasury-scale capital.
“I think you've heard people say that there's no one that will ever catch strategy ... this is going to be a real gamecher for Strive ... So if anybody can catch them might be Strive” ▶ 12:03
Honeywell bought his first SATA shares and Strive common equity (in non-small size) the same day Strive announced daily dividends, specifically to start tracking volume improvement before considering it for treasury use.
anecdote🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumStriveSATA
So what: A sophisticated treasury operator personally front-running the SATA trade signals conviction; the gating factor for institutional flows is liquidity, not the thesis.
“I can honestly say I bought my first share of SATA on Thursday and I bought my first uh share of their common equity on Thursday and uh they were not small positions” ▶ 11:43
Daily dividends remove the incentive to rotate capital out of a preferred: holders previously moved between STRC and SATA around ex-dividend dates (sacrificing tax efficiency), but a daily-paying product makes holders stay put, structurally improving liquidity and stickiness.
causal🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highSATASTRCStriveStrategy
So what: Daily-dividend mechanics should reduce volume volatility and de-peg risk; expect Strategy to copy it, which Honeywell predicts they will do quickly.
“But SATA paying daily, there's no incentive to get out. There's no incentive to rotate your money out” ▶ 15:39
Strategy is reportedly considering moving STRC to semi-monthly (every two weeks) dividends, which would halve dividend-cycle volatility and cut the required cash-holding window from ~45-60 days to a couple of weeks — and Honeywell expects Strategy to ultimately follow Strive to daily payments.
prediction🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumStrategySTRCStriveSATA
So what: Shorter dividend cycles make STRC more usable as working-capital-adjacent treasury; a Strategy move to daily dividends would be a competitive response to Strive worth front-running.
“By going to two weeks it would cut the volatility in half” ▶ 15:29
Strategy's credit-rating roadmap & capital-markets strategy 4
Strategy's stated willingness to sell a token amount of Bitcoin is, in Honeywell's read, a deliberate move to prove to S&P that Bitcoin is genuinely spendable 'digital capital' (S&P declined to count never-to-be-sold Bitcoin as capital) — part of a checklist to earn an investment-grade rating.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highStrategyS&PBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Reframes Saylor's surprising 'willing to sell Bitcoin' statement as a rating-agency play, not a bearish signal; an upgrade is the catalyst to watch.
“the only way you can prove that is by spending a little bit of it” ▶ 24:17
An investment-grade (toward AAA) credit rating for Strategy would unlock more than $20 trillion of capital currently restricted to high-rated instruments, since many institutions cannot hold STRC unless it is AAA-rated.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumStrategySTRCS&P
So what: Quantifies the institutional TAM gated on a rating upgrade; a Strategy credit-rating upgrade would be a major structural demand catalyst for its preferreds.
“It opens up like more than$20 trillion dollars worth of capital” ▶ 26:24
Strategy is shifting messaging toward protecting common shareholders: retiring convertible debt, relying more on STRC versus common-stock ATM issuance, and building a cash reserve — a response to disgruntled common holders who felt burned.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highStrategyMSTRSTRC
So what: Signals a capital-structure pivot at Strategy that affects MSTR common holders directly; convert retirement and reduced ATM dilution are concrete shareholder-friendly moves to track.
“the announcement to retire the convertible debt um more reliance on STRC versus common stock uh ATM” ▶ 25:41
Honeywell endorses Strategy using common-stock ATM issuance aggressively as long as mNAV is above 1, but says he is comfortable even at 1-for-1 ('it's a wash') — a view on when dilution is accretive versus destructive.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumStrategyMSTR
So what: mNAV >1 as the threshold for accretive ATM issuance is the key valuation gate for MSTR common holders evaluating dilution.
“the higher than than the MNAV the better but I'm perfectly fine at one for one. It's a wash” ▶ 25:54
AI / data-center energy bottleneck 5
Data centers are increasingly forced to bring their own power plants because the grid buildout cannot keep pace with AI demand — and gas turbines take years to manufacture, unlike mass-produced goods, making behind-the-meter generation the binding constraint on the AI buildout.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highAI data centersgas turbinesnatural gaselectric grid
So what: The investable bottleneck in AI is generation hardware and behind-the-meter power, not just chips; gas-turbine and power-equipment supply chains are the choke point to watch.
“most of these data centers are having to bring their own power plants with them because the speed of the buildout is outpacing the speed that the grid can be built out” ▶ 27:54
Fast-built simple-cycle gas turbines convert only ~40-50% of fuel to power with the rest as waste heat, while combined-cycle plants (adding a heat-recovery steam generator) capture that heat for much higher efficiency and far less thermal waste — so anti-data-center 'pollution' claims often target the cheaper simple-cycle builds.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highgas turbinescombined cyclesimple cycleAI data centers
So what: Efficiency of the turbine choice materially changes a data center's economics and PR exposure; combined-cycle adoption is a differentiator when evaluating power-build projects.
“with a combined cycle gas turbine, you have a heat recovery steam generator, which is just a fancy way of saying a boiler” ▶ 34:24
Behind-the-meter (off-grid) generation creates a new technical problem: AI compute loads can oscillate between 10% and 95% several times a second, far faster than any generator's ramp rate (~20 MW/minute), which damages generation equipment and destabilizes power.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highAI data centersgeneratorsbattery storage
So what: Load-volatility mismatch is an underappreciated engineering risk in off-grid AI power; it creates demand for power-stabilizing hardware (batteries, shock-absorber systems).
“some of these data center loads will oscillate between you know 10% and 95% four or five times a second” ▶ 47:17
Prevalon sells a 'hybrid power stabilizer' (HPS) that acts as a shock absorber for off-grid power plants, letting generators run at stable output while the system absorbs the rapid ups and downs of AI compute loads — protecting equipment and smoothing power delivery.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumPrevalon Energybattery storageAI data centersHPS
So what: Identifies a specific product category (power-stabilization/battery buffering for off-grid AI) as a beneficiary of the data-center buildout.
“our HPS system, what it does, it it allows the the generation equipment to run at a stable speed and power output and then our system captures the ups and downs” ▶ 47:34
A large share of data-center power waste comes from cooling and from AC-to-DC conversion; since solar, wind, and battery power are natively DC, feeding DC directly into servers could cut the thermal/conversion load — a future efficiency lever for data-center design.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: lowAI data centerssolarwindbattery storage
So what: DC-native data-center architecture is a longer-dated efficiency theme that could favor renewable/battery integration; speculative but worth tracking.
“if you have solar power or uh wind power or even battery power that's going in, all of that is DC. So you could actually go directly into the PCs” ▶ 46:04
Battery storage economics & grid stabilization 5
Battery energy storage monetizes via energy arbitrage (charge cheap when daytime solar floods supply in California, discharge into expensive evening peaks), peak shaving (avoid demand charges), and deferring transmission-line buildouts (~$1M+/mile) by placing batteries at congested line ends.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highbattery storagePrevalon Energyelectric grid
So what: Multiple stacked revenue streams (arbitrage, peak-shaving, transmission deferral) underpin the BESS investment case; transmission deferral economics scale with line-build costs.
“putting a battery system at the end of that line will address that load, which means you don't have to build that transmission line” ▶ 43:38
Battery costs are rising globally — Prevalon confirmed with Chinese executives that China's own AI data-center buildout is driving battery and input demand (lithium, lithium bicarbonate, electrolyte, graphite) higher, a supply-side squeeze affecting US buyers.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highbatterieslithiumgraphiteChinaAI data centers
So what: Rising battery and lithium/graphite input costs are a margin and inflation signal driven by global (not just US) AI demand; relevant to BESS and EV cost forecasts.
“battery costs are are going up ... It's because of their AI data center buildout” ▶ 49:30
Bitcoin mining and battery storage both want the cheapest power, so there is little direct synergy between them; Bitcoin's value is as a flexible, interruptible load that stabilizes the grid (can be shut off when strained), which is why Texas has courted miners.
framework🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highBitcoin miningbattery storageTexaselectric grid
So what: Positions Bitcoin mining as grid infrastructure (flexible load) rather than just an energy drain; supports the bull case for miner-friendly jurisdictions.
“Bitcoin mining is amazing grid stabilization” ▶ 42:08
Bitcoin mining can monetize stranded power — flare gas from US oil production (natural gas is an oil byproduct currently suppressed in price and often flared when storage/pipeline infrastructure is lacking) and waste heat from inefficient thermal plants — because miners need only cheap power and a basic data connection.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highBitcoin miningnatural gasflare gasoil production
So what: Stranded-energy monetization is a real, growing use case tying Bitcoin mining economics to US oil/gas expansion and suppressed gas prices.
“Bitcoin is really good at coming in and grabbing that kind of u stranded power and using it for Bitcoin mining because you don't require a big pipe” ▶ 36:38
Honeywell argues against a wholesale renewable transition: solar/wind work best on the load/demand side (rooftops, parking garages, streetlights) but are inefficient for bulk generation, and a real grid needs all sources (nuclear, thermal, wind, hydro, wave) deployed where each makes sense.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highsolarwindnuclearhydrorenewables
So what: An all-of-the-above energy view favors diversified generation exposure over pure-play renewables; relevant to energy-infrastructure allocation.
“the green transition should not be a in my humble opinion a wholesale transition to renewable power” ▶ 39:56
China, rare earths & manufacturing edge 4
China powers its AI data-center buildout largely off-grid with massive solar/wind farms in the Gobi Desert and Mongolian step, openly because it lacks the fuel resources and turbine-manufacturing capability the US has — not primarily for green/carbon reasons.
data_point🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highChinasolarwindGobi DesertAI data centers
So what: China's solar/wind data-center strategy is fuel-driven necessity, and overbuilding (5-6x nameplate plus storage) drives outsized battery/input demand globally.
“we don't have the fuel resources that you have in the United States ... mostly it's the fuel” ▶ 50:14
The rare-earth 'monopoly' narrative is overstated: the global rare-earth market is under $10 billion (tiny), rare earths are abundant ('everywhere you have dirt'), and the US has processing capacity in Texas and California — China dominates only because it overproduces ~99x its need and dumps subsidized output, undercutting US producers by ~20x on cost.
contrarian🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highrare earthsChinaUnited States
So what: Reframes the rare-earth geopolitical risk as a price/subsidy problem solvable with tariffs or price floors, not a true scarcity — relevant to mispriced rare-earth and critical-minerals narratives.
“Their system uses our capitalist economy against us successfully because we won't operate ... and process rare earth and make these magnets ... at a loss” ▶ 53:43
Rare earths and silver appear in everything (phones, EVs, batteries, hard-drive magnets) but only in tiny per-unit dollar amounts (~$20 of silver per car), so the financial market size is small even though physical reach is broad — a caution against rare-earth/silver scarcity price theses.
contrarian🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumsilverrare earthsEVs
So what: Tempers bull cases that extrapolate ubiquity into large market size; per-unit dollar content, not prevalence, drives the actual market.
“the silver that's in a car may be $20 worth of silver ... It's the financial size of the market is very small” ▶ 54:43
China has decisively surpassed the US in manufacturing capability — heavily automated factories now produce high-quality, highly engineered goods — repeating the post-war quality arc of Japan and Korea; the US is 'definitely not far ahead' on manufacturing.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highChinaUnited Statesmanufacturing
So what: A firsthand supply-chain view that China's manufacturing lead is structural and quality-competitive, with implications for reshoring feasibility and cost.
“manufacturing wise, China has they have vastly um, bypassed us from a manufacturing capability. I've seen it firsthand” ▶ 55:24
Macro: inflation, affordability & the physical ledger 5
Honeywell's macro thesis traces back to 2020: anticipating the Fed's COVID money printing is what pushed him from Bitcoin skeptic to maximalist, and he frames ongoing inflation/affordability (cars, housing, food) as the central, persistent challenge for ordinary Americans.
thesis🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: highBitcoinFederal Reserveinflation
So what: Restates the debasement/monetary-inflation thesis as the core driver of Bitcoin adoption; affordability erosion is the macro backdrop he expects to persist.
“when I saw what was going to happen with the Fed about to issue that much money and I knew we knew what was coming” ▶ 1:51
The key macro frame is a bifurcation between the financial ledger (numbers on a screen, unbacked) and the physical/material ledger (energy, data centers, fuel) — and 'you can't print energy,' so whoever controls physical inputs at lowest cost ultimately wins.
framework🗣 Natalie Brunell (host), citing guest Craig Tinddaleconviction: highenergyBitcoinCraig Tinddale
So what: A 'physical ledger over financial ledger' lens argues for owning real-asset/energy bottlenecks rather than purely financial claims — a portfolio-tilt thesis.
“you can't print energy, you can't print data centers, you can't print the things that actually matter the most” ▶ 1:03:38
Honeywell characterizes the AI capex cycle as a genuine, well-funded, government-backed boom rather than a confirmed bubble like dot-com — but flags two bubble-like signals: investors throwing money at anything 'that smells like AI' (e.g. a shoe company's stock up 700-800% on an AI pivot) and earnings being inflated by pulling demand forward in the accounting.
contrarian🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumAIdot-com bubbleAI capex
So what: A nuanced 'boom not yet bubble' call with specific froth indicators (AI-pivot speculation, demand pull-forward in earnings) to monitor for a turn.
“Is it a boom? It It's definitely a boom. I don't I don't know if I would call it a bubble yet” ▶ 48:20
Honeywell flags pull-forward in AI earnings accounting as a concern: government backing means demand is being pulled forward in how earnings are calculated, which differs from the dot-com era when the government wasn't financing the buildout.
causal🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumAIAI capex
So what: Earnings-quality risk: AI-sector reported earnings may overstate durable demand; a reason to scrutinize the accounting behind AI capex beneficiaries.
“they all are pulling a lot of demand forward in terms of the accounting of how how the earnings are really being calculated right now which is a little concerning” ▶ 49:10
US natural gas prices are currently suppressed because rising US oil production (partly a response to Strait of Hormuz disruption) generates byproduct natural gas that gets flared for lack of storage/pipeline infrastructure — cheap gas that benefits both thermal power and Bitcoin mining.
causal🗣 Ben Honeywell, CFO Prevalon Energyconviction: mediumnatural gasoil productionStrait of HormuzBitcoin mining
So what: Links suppressed US gas prices to oil-production dynamics and geopolitics; cheap flared gas supports both data-center power and mining economics.
≈ “natural gas prices are fairly suppressed because of what's going on in the straits, you know, straits hormuz” paraphrase
The $40K Bitcoin Bottom Coming?55 min40 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Luke Groman argues Bitcoin is the 'last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity' and is being drained by AI, oil and commodities sucking liquidity out of the system. His intermediate/long-term base case: stocks up in dollars but down in gold and Bitcoin terms, much higher gold and Bitcoin, crushed long bonds, with the Iran/Hormuz crisis and US 'Suez moment' driving secular inflation, a weaker dollar, and a global shift to gold-settled, no-trust ('no ticky, no washi') trade.
Bitcoin as liquidity smoke alarm · 4AI sucking liquidity out of everything · 9Gold/Bitcoin price suppression & the dollar · 6Rare earths & China's processing monopoly · 3Iran, Hormuz closure & the US 'Suez moment' · 8Gold settlement & the no-trust monetary order · 7Sovereign-debt endgame & realist framing · 3
Bitcoin as liquidity smoke alarm 4
Luke Groman has sold most of his Bitcoin and as of this recording has not bought back in any real way, only nibbling a little, because Bitcoin's weak price action (vs all-time-high stocks) is signaling deteriorating liquidity.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A prominent macro analyst is positioned out of BTC and waiting for lower prices; his re-entry would be a liquidity all-clear signal.
“I didn't sell it all but I sold most of it. Um so the answer is no I haven't.” ▶ 1:41
Groman frames Bitcoin as 'one of, if not the last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity,' so its recent weakness is read as a warning that system-wide liquidity is being drained, not as a Bitcoin-specific problem.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highBitcoinliquidity
So what: Treat BTC price action as a real-time liquidity gauge; weakness while equities make highs is a system-wide tell, not idiosyncratic.
“Bitcoin's one of, if not the last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity, and it's telling us not good things.” ▶ 4:00
Groman cites Northstar & Bad Charts (analysts he likes on technicals) projecting a Bitcoin cycle bottom in Q3/Q4 somewhere in the $40Ks, and concedes 'I might get my number' rather than a $60K bottom, indicating he sees meaningful further downside as plausible.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumBitcoinNorthstarBad Charts
So what: A $40K-range bottom in late 2026 would be a far better entry than current levels; sets a watch zone for accumulation.
≈ “they're predicting like Q3 Q4 bottom somewhere in the 40ks. Like I really think that we could see that. I might get my number.” paraphrase
Bitcoin was trading around $68,000 at the time of recording per Groman's offhand reference, a level he uses as a baseline against the ~$168K he'd expect if Iran were settling oil in size via Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumBitcoinIran
So what: Anchors the episode's price context (~$68K) and implies large sovereign BTC demand is NOT currently present.
AI sucking liquidity out of everything 9
Groman's working hypothesis is that AI, oil, and commodities are each draining liquidity out of the system, so everything not tied to those three (including Bitcoin) is flat to down despite headline indices at all-time highs.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highAIoilcommoditiesBitcoinS&P 500
So what: Explains the BTC/equity disconnect; favors owning the liquidity magnets (AI, energy, commodities) over the drained 'everything else' until liquidity broadens.
“AI is sucking all the oxygen out of the room, all the liquidity out of the room, and it's all in one area. And I think that's happening to Bitcoin as well.” ▶ 3:44
Market breadth is very poor: per a chart Groman cites, ex-AI stocks the S&P 500 is flat to down slightly versus the day before the Iran war started, meaning the index gains are concentrated in roughly seven stocks.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highS&P 500AI
So what: Headline index strength masks a weak underlying market; a stumble in the AI complex could expose the index with little cushion.
≈ “X things related to AI, the S&P 500 is flat to down slightly uh from the day before the Iran war started.” paraphrase
Emerging-market outperformance is an illusion driven by a few AI/memory names: stripping out TSMC, Samsung, and another large AI/memory name, MSCI EM is actually getting crushed relative to MSCI US.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highMSCI EMMSCI USTSMCSamsung
So what: Don't mistake EM index strength for a broad EM bull case; the move is a narrow AI-supply-chain bet.
“if you take those three or four AI related names out, AI's or EM is actually getting crushed.” ▶ 3:29
AI buildout accounting front-loads reported earnings: revenues are booked up front while capex is amortized over a longer period, so the faster and more cash-flow-negative the buildout, the higher and faster reported earnings rise even as companies become cash-poor and shift from cash funding to borrowing more.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highAIdata centers
So what: High reported AI earnings can mask deteriorating cash flow and rising debt; watch the cash-flow statement and incremental borrowing, not just EPS.
“the faster you build and the more cash flow negative you are the higher and faster your reported earnings will go” ▶ 6:58
When the AI buildout slows for any reason (commodity shortages, chip holdups, data-center permitting delays), the accounting flips: earnings slow sharply or decline but become largely non-cash while cash flow actually rises meaningfully.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumAIdata centerssemiconductors
So what: A buildout slowdown is the key inflection; the earnings-down/cash-up signature is the tell to watch for the regime change in AI names.
“the second it slows, essentially your revenue growth starts to slow and the lagged effect of the uh amortization... starts to catch up and then it flips.” ▶ 8:00
Groman expects AI stocks to underperform once their buildout-driven earnings slow, because capital that chased the momentum leaves the space for whatever else had its oxygen sucked out, even though the stocks screen relatively cheap on valuation.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumAI
So what: Cheap-looking AI valuations may not protect against a momentum-unwind rotation; the trigger is the earnings-growth slowdown.
“if the earnings slow why do I own this versus... anything else that has had the oxygen sucked out of and so does capital start to leave the space” ▶ 9:00
Unlike the 1999 dot-com bubble that burst under its own weight in free markets, today's AI boom is unlikely to be left to deflate because AI has been identified as a key battlefield of great-power competition, so the US government will likely support keeping the buildout going.
contrarian🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highAIUS governmentChina
So what: Government backstop means an AI top may be later and more managed than a 2000-style crash; betting on a clean self-inflicted bust may be premature.
“AI has been identified as a key... battlefield of the great power competition and so... the government is likely going to support this” ▶ 0:20
For AI valuations (as a percent of total US equity market cap) to be justified, AI must eviscerate white-collar employment; if instead big-tech leaders are right that AI won't disrupt employment, then it isn't the biggest thing since the internet and doesn't deserve those valuations.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highAIwhite-collar employmentprivate credit
So what: Either outcome is bearish: no job destruction breaks the valuation/private-credit case; mass job destruction breaks US tax receipts. Frames AI as a fiscal/credit risk either way.
“for the business case of AI to make any sense relative to the valuation... they have to kill employment. They have to kill white collar employment. That's the only way the math works.” ▶ 49:52
Groman flags US labor-market weakness as evidence AI may already be biting: a recent month showed only 95,000 jobs in a country of 350 million, versus the 200,000-300,000 'good month' of 20-30 years ago when the population was ~20% smaller.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumUS labor marketAI
So what: Weak job growth pressures tax receipts (employment is ~half of US tax receipts) and could force political intervention in AI economics.
“We had 95,000 jobs... in a month in a country of 350 million people. When I grew up, a good month was 200 300,000 jobs in a month.” ▶ 50:59
Gold/Bitcoin price suppression & the dollar 6
Groman's intermediate-to-long-term base case is that this resolves with much higher gold and much higher Bitcoin, higher equities in dollar terms but lower equities in gold and Bitcoin terms, and a stable bond market with 10-year yields ranging roughly 3.75%-4.5%.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highgoldBitcoinequities10-year Treasury
So what: Core actionable framework: measure equity performance in gold/BTC terms, not dollars; own gold and BTC as the unit of account that wins.
“the way this ends is with stocks up big in dollars and stocks down in gold and stocks down in Bitcoin.” ▶ 0:26
Even with gold's selloff and an equity bounce, the average US stock is probably still down ~40% in gold terms since the Fed started hiking in 2022 (headline indices down ~20-30% in gold terms), illustrating the regime that has quietly been in place since 2022.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highS&P 500goldFederal Reserve
So what: Dollar-denominated equity highs hide a major loss of purchasing power; gold has been the better store of value through the hiking cycle.
“the average stock certainly is probably still down 40% in gold terms over the last 5 years.” ▶ 15:50
Groman suggests authorities could suppress gold and Bitcoin in the short run via expansion of derivatives (the way gold has historically been managed), keeping Bitcoin in a '58 to 72K' range, though he believes this cannot work for Bitcoin in the long run.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumgoldBitcoinderivatives
So what: Derivative-satisfied demand (e.g., buying calls instead of spot BTC) can cap price short-term; long-term Groman expects suppression to fail, favoring spot accumulation.
“to the extent that you can expand derivatives... Somebody wants to own Bitcoin, but they're not buying Bitcoin. They're buying a call on Bitcoin.” ▶ 13:22
The US reshoring and trade-rebalancing agenda mathematically requires a much weaker dollar against the Chinese yuan (and possibly yen and euro), which is structurally bullish for gold and Bitcoin even if politically resisted.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUS dollarChinese yuanJapanese yeneurogoldBitcoin
So what: Trade policy is a structural tailwind for gold/BTC via dollar debasement; positions against the dollar align with stated US objectives.
“all of those things cannot happen unless the dollar is much weaker uh against the Chinese yuan” ▶ 16:07
Some US elements resist a weaker dollar and rising gold/BTC because those moves signal to the world 'you're just inflating,' creating Treasury-market financing problems, especially given the 50-60bp rise in the 10-year yield since the Iran war started.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumUS Treasury10-year TreasurygoldBitcoin
So what: Explains the policy tension behind optics management of gold/BTC; rising yields constrain how much inflation signaling officials will tolerate.
“those things will be... communicating to the world, hey, you're just inflating. Uh and that creates some issues on the financing side with the Treasury market” ▶ 0:36
US long-term bond futures are down ~90% against gold over the last 10 years while GDP rose over that period, which Groman argues shows 90% wasn't enough and bonds remain structurally vulnerable versus hard assets.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUS Treasurylong-term bondsgold
So what: Long-duration Treasuries have been a wealth-destroyer in gold terms; Groman sees more downside ahead relative to gold/BTC.
“long-term bond futures in the United States are down 90% 90% against gold over the last 10 years.” ▶ 17:08
Rare earths & China's processing monopoly 3
Rare earths are a tiny market by monetary cap and tonnage but underpin tens of trillions of US tech equity market cap, an 'inverted pyramid' where enormous valuations rest on a thin sliver of materials China largely controls.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highrare earthsChinaUS tech equities
So what: A small, China-controlled input is a systemic chokepoint for huge swaths of tech equity value; a supply squeeze has outsized downstream impact.
“you've got tens of trillions of market cap in US equities... resting on this little sliver of of of in volume terms of rare earth.” ▶ 18:38
China dominates rare-earth refining not just via reserves but through 30 years of work, more engineers, fewer environmental regulations, and likely under-discussed innovation in the actual refining machinery and processes, which the US would struggle to replicate.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highChinarare earthsUnited States
So what: Closing the gap requires mine, refine, engineering and educational base plus machines that may come from China and that China may refuse to sell, making 'reshoring' rare earths slow and expensive.
“no one ever talks about whether they've innovated in the machinery the actual refining machinery and the the refining processes and my understanding is they have.” ▶ 19:48
Groman notes the Trump administration's recent rule-breaking pattern of taking equity stakes in companies (citing Intel and 'MP') that have done well, suggesting government-backed US rare-earth names could benefit from the domestic buildout despite the old rule that government involvement makes for poor stocks.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumrare earthsIntelMPTrump administration
So what: US rare-earth companies with government backing are a thematic long on supply-chain reshoring; Groman points investors to 'rare earth US companies' without naming tickers.
“if you... Google rare earth US companies and you start sort of sniffing around those things, th those would be the types of companies that should stand to benefit” ▶ 21:14
Iran, Hormuz closure & the US 'Suez moment' 8
Groman correctly predicted (in early-mid March) the Strait of Hormuz would stay closed through July 4th, with major brokers now saying it could remain closed through Labor Day, while admitting he got the market reaction 'dead wrong' so far.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highStrait of HormuzIranoil
So what: A prolonged Hormuz closure that markets have not priced is the central unpriced macro risk; complacency creates asymmetric downside.
“I said there's going to be I would start preparing for it to be closed still on July 4th and people thought it was crazy” ▶ 24:37
WTI crude was around $95 at recording and oil is up ~50% since the Iran war started, yet Groman argues that a known multi-month Hormuz closure would normally imply far higher oil, much lower equities, and higher yields, so the market has not yet priced the disruption.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highWTI crudeoilIran
So what: If pricing eventually catches up to a sustained closure, expect a sharp oil spike and equity/bond repricing; energy is the asymmetric long.
≈ “that would be wow. Like that would be oil way higher than $95 or wherever we are today on on WTI and markets would be way lower.” paraphrase
Groman likens the current calm to a man falling past the 40th floor saying 'this feels just like flying'; the 'sudden stop' that hurts comes when global oil inventories hit tank bottoms, which Exxon and Chevron have warned could come within two to three weeks.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highoilExxonChevronglobal inventories
So what: The tank-bottom moment is the catalyst that forces demand rationing via price; near-term (2-3 week) window flagged by majors makes energy the key trade to monitor.
“It's not the... fall that kills you. It's a sudden stop. And the sudden stop in this case is when you start hitting tank bottoms.” ▶ 26:02
The Hormuz standoff is a 'pain contest': Iran is sustained by morphine-hit supplies (Russia via the Caspian, China via rail) while the oil-short world is buffered by SPR and global inventory draws, and whoever hits their limit first loses.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highIranRussiaChinaSPRoil
So what: Track SPR and global inventory levels versus Iran's backdoor supply; inventory exhaustion in the West is the trigger for a stagflationary oil shock.
“It's a pain contest. It's unquestionably a pain contest.” ▶ 32:49
Groman argues Iran retains more fire control over the Gulf than the US admits, evidenced by leaked reports that US Middle East bases were heavily hit, air defenses underperformed, and aircraft losses exceeded official figures, which is why insurers won't write coverage and the strait stays closed.
contrarian🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzUS military
So what: If Iranian Gulf control is real and durable, the closure is structural not transient; the unpriced risk persists longer than markets assume.
“the Iranians have more fire control of the Gulf than people want to admit.” ▶ 28:14
Groman calls this a US 'Suez moment'; when Britain had its 1956 Suez moment, median UK inflation ran almost 7% per year for the next 20 years, implying a comparable US loss of status could be structurally inflationary.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUnited StatesUnited KingdomSuezinflation
So what: A US Suez analog implies a multi-decade inflationary regime, reinforcing the long-gold/long-Bitcoin, short-long-bond thesis.
“When the Brits had their Suez moment in 56, the... median rate of inflation over the next 20 years was almost 7% per year for 20 years.” ▶ 30:51
Groman expects the US loss in Iran/the Gulf to accelerate multicurrency energy pricing away from the dollar, meaning less global need to hold dollar reserves, more demand to hold gold, and ultimately the Fed (or banking-system proxies) printing money to absorb Treasuries because yields above ~4.6-4.8% on the 10-year are unaffordable.
prediction🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUS dollargoldFederal Reserve10-year TreasuryIran
So what: Sets a hard yield ceiling (~4.6-4.8%) that forces eventual QE/monetization, a secularly inflationary outcome bullish for gold and BTC.
≈ “We can't really afford above 4.8 4.6 4.8% on the 10-year. So... at some point that somebody is going to be the Fed with printed money” paraphrase
Groman doubts reports that Iran is settling oil payments in Bitcoin at scale, reasoning that if it were, BTC would be near $168,000 rather than ~$68,000; he sees the bigger real signal in China's CIPS interbank payment volumes, which have exploded higher since March, implying yuan settlement that de facto means gold.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumIranBitcoinCIPSChinese yuangold
So what: Watch CIPS volumes, not Bitcoin headlines, for evidence of de-dollarized energy settlement; the flow is going through yuan into gold.
“I don't think Bitcoin would be at 68,000... it would be probably 168,000.” ▶ 36:31
Gold settlement & the no-trust monetary order 7
For five of the last six months, non-monetary gold has been the single largest US export, bigger than aircraft and pharmaceutical preparations, with flows running from the US to Switzerland/London and onward to China and Hong Kong, de facto settling the US-China trade deficit in gold.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highgoldUnited StatesChinaSwitzerlandHong Kong
So what: Gold is already functioning as trade-settlement money between the US and China; the largest marginal driver of the shrinking trade deficit is gold exports.
“five of the last six months non-monetary gold has been the single largest export of the United States... bigger than aircraft, bigger than pharmaceutical preparations.” ▶ 38:00
Groman argues gold trade-settlement 'needs to happen at a much higher gold price, otherwise we're going to run out of gold,' implying the structural flow itself is a mechanism for a higher gold price over time.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highgoldUnited StatesChina
So what: Physical gold outflows create a built-in upward repricing pressure; supports a structurally higher gold price target.
“It needs to happen at a much higher gold price because otherwise we're going to run out of gold.” ▶ 40:03
Groman speculates Bessent may have struck a deal where ~$4,500 gold buys ~$6,000 of Chinese rare earths, a good trade for the US that nonetheless implies gold prices move structurally higher over time.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: lowgoldrare earthsScott BessentChina
So what: If gold is being bartered for strategic materials at favorable ratios, it cements gold's monetary role and a rising price path.
“If... the world will sell him gold at 4,500 and he has struck some sort of deal with the Chinese that 4500 in gold will buy $6,000 of rare earths, that's a good deal.” ▶ 40:12
Because IMF rules require reporting non-monetary gold but not monetary gold, central banks (or the US) can ship gold and reclassify it so it never appears in statistics, meaning the true sovereign gold flows to China may be larger and unrecorded.
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumgoldIMFcentral banksChina
So what: Official gold-flow data understates the real shift; positioning should assume more gold is moving east than headlines show.
≈ “if you buy non-monetary gold and then you reclassify it as monetary it never has to show up.” paraphrase
Groman argues the world is moving to a 'no ticky, no washi' (no trust) settlement system where paper is accepted in payment but final settlement is in gold or commodities, evidenced by China placing offshore yuan clearing banks in every major gold hub (London, Switzerland, Dubai, Singapore, Hong Kong, Shanghai).
framework🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highgoldChinese yuanChinacommodities
So what: A gold-anchored, trust-minimized settlement architecture is being built now; gold (and potentially BTC) is the structural beneficiary of declining counterparty trust.
“I will take your paper in payment, but in settlement, I am going to exchange your paper for something that's going to hold value.” ▶ 45:29
Groman cites China's biggest courier opening a 2,000-ton gold vault at Hong Kong airport as physical proof-of-work infrastructure for gold settlement, asking why a courier would need a vault unless China is building a physical-gold (not paper-gold) clearing system.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumgoldChinaHong Kong
So what: Concrete buildout of physical gold logistics signals institutional commitment to gold-based settlement, a long-term structural bid for gold.
“China's biggest courier... is opening a 2,000 ton gold vault at the Hong Kong airport.” ▶ 43:57
Groman believes 70-something-year-old heads of state (Putin, Xi, Polish leadership) will trust physical gold next to their tanks over Bitcoin due to concerns about exchange back doors, so gold wins the sovereign settlement role for now even though he views Bitcoin as technologically superior digital gold.
thesis🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: mediumgoldBitcoinPutinXi Jinping
So what: Sovereign demand favors gold near-term; Bitcoin's sovereign settlement role is a longer-dated, not-yet-arrived thesis.
“Are they going to go send me the gold and I'm going to put it right here next to all my tanks, all my missiles... They're making the latter choice.” ▶ 45:07
Sovereign-debt endgame & realist framing 3
Of 58 countries in 150 years that hit 130% debt-to-GDP, 57 defaulted, mostly via significant inflation; the lone exception was Japan, which is now itself inflating with a shrinking bond market, rising rates, and a stock market down ~20% in gold terms over five years.
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highsovereign debtJapaninflationgold
So what: Historical base rate (now effectively 58/58) argues the US debt path resolves through inflation; favors hard assets over long-duration bonds.
“There have been 58 countries in 150 years of history that have had debt to GDP hit 130%. And as of three years ago, 57 of them defaulted on that debt mostly via... significant inflation.” ▶ 47:04
The US crossed 130% debt-to-GDP in summer 2020; since then long-term US Treasuries have fallen ~50-60% against gold, which Groman frames as the inflationary resolution already underway ('it's not doom, it's just math').
data_point🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUS TreasurygoldUS debt
So what: The bond-vs-gold debasement has already begun and Groman expects it to continue; reinforces underweighting long bonds versus gold/BTC.
“long-term US Treasury bonds have fallen, I think, 60% against gold, 50% against gold” ▶ 48:07
US entitlements plus interest are already at or near 100% of federal revenues, so a stagflationary recession (rationing oil demand via price) would simultaneously drop revenues and raise interest expense, making a Hormuz-driven oil shock fiscally catastrophic for the West.
causal🗣 Luke Gromanconviction: highUS fiscalentitlementsinterest expenseoil
So what: Ties the oil/Hormuz risk directly to a fiscal doom-loop; an oil-driven recession forces monetization, the bullish-gold/bearish-bond endgame.
“in the US at least... you're already at 100% of revenues or close to it in terms of your entitlements plus interest.” ▶ 34:57

Animal Spirits · Carlson & Batnick 2 episode(s) · 76 insights

The Recession Signals Are All Wrong | TCAF 24570 min36 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
RenMac's Neil Dutta and Employ America's Skanda Amarnath argue the traditional recession playbook is broken because the cycle is now driven by an AI capex boom (~7% of GDP, past the 2005 housing peak) rather than housing or labor. The live debate: inflation is re-accelerating on supply shocks while the labor market has stabilized, pushing the market to price a Fed hike, and the spending/wealth-effect story rests on equity gains and RSUs rather than wage growth.
AI capex as the new business cycle · 6Everything trades like a semiconductor · 3Wealth effect, RSUs and the spending engine · 5Inflation, supply shocks and the Fed hike debate · 6Profit-vs-labor divide and productivity · 4Labor market: white-collar vs blue-collar split · 4Consumer signals: noise vs real squeeze · 4What to watch for the cycle turn · 4
AI capex as the new business cycle 6
AI-related investment (tech equipment, software, industrial equipment for data centers) has reached roughly 7% of US GDP at end-2025, surpassing the 2005 housing-investment peak and well past the 1990s dot-com/telecom peak, making the tech cycle and business cycle effectively the same thing.
framework🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: highAIdata centersGDPhousingsemiconductors
So what: If AI capex is now the cyclical swing factor (replacing housing), the next recession will be triggered by a capex slowdown, not by housing starts or labor-market leading indicators investors traditionally watch.
“we've ripped past the the peak for housing investment in the 2000s” ▶ 17:56
What is unusual this cycle is that non-residential business fixed investment / equipment capex is leading the economy, even though capex normally LAGS growth (the accelerator effect) and growth expectations are not actually inflecting higher.
contrarian🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highcapexequipment investmentAI
So what: Because capex is leading rather than lagging, the usual sequencing of business-cycle indicators is inverted; investors relying on capex as a confirming signal will be late.
“what's unusual about this cycle is that it's not like growth expectations are really taking off in any material extent. Um but you have this sort of spectacular capital spending boom nonetheless” ▶ 30:29
There is no clean leading indicator for the tech/AI capex boom that now drives GDP volatility; the only readable signals are specific names' order books and orders within the tech supply chain.
framework🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumAItech sectorGDP
So what: Investors should monitor hyperscaler capex guidance and key supply-chain order books (e.g., Nvidia's per-customer revenue) rather than macro leading indicators to time the cycle.
Hyperscalers and others are spending roughly $700 billion of capex, which is large enough that a modest pullback in consumer spending would not derail the economy because investment, not consumption, is the volatile driver of the business cycle.
data_point🗣 host (Michael Batnick) / Skanda Amarnathconviction: highhyperscalerscapexconsumer spending
So what: The cycle's fate hinges on whether AI capex commitments continue, not on incremental consumer softness, so capex guidance dominates the macro signal.
“In a world where these hyperscalers and others joining the party are spending $700 billion of capex, doesn't matter if the consumer pulls back a little bit.” ▶ 29:14
Anthropic's revenue reportedly went from roughly $9B to $45B while compute capacity remains tight and end-user adoption of agentic AI is still low, which the host reads as evidence the AI buildout is early-stage, not late-cycle.
thesis🗣 host (Michael Batnick)conviction: highAnthropicAIcompute
So what: If demand is real and supply-constrained while adoption is still nascent, the buildout (and the trades levered to it) could run far longer than bubble-callers expect.
“nobody's even using these things and all we're hearing about is a shortage of compute” ▶ 32:36
The bullish 'it's not a bubble because earnings are strong' argument is flawed because AI earnings are partly tied to a temporary data-center buildout; when the buildout stops, those earnings disappear, so a non-extreme P/E multiple tells you nothing about risk.
contrarian🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highAIdata centersP/Eearnings
So what: Valuation comfort from low forward P/Es is misleading if the E is buildout-driven; investors should stress-test earnings for a capex stop, not just multiples.
“the earnings are tied to some kind of temporary phenomenon... at some point the data center buildout will stop and the earnings won't be there” ▶ 33:40
Everything trades like a semiconductor 3
RenMac found 15 non-tech S&P 500 companies worth ~$2 trillion in market cap that now move with the SMH semiconductor ETF at daily-return correlations of 0.5 or higher; 12 of the 15 are industrials whose order books have effectively become AI-capex order books.
data_point🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highSMHS&P 500industrialssemiconductorsCaterpillarVertivEatonCumminsGE Vernova
So what: GICS sector labels understate true AI exposure; investors can get levered AI-capex beta through mispriced 'industrial' names like Caterpillar (gensets), Vertiv (cooling/power), and GE Vernova (gas turbines) that screen as non-tech.
“These are not tech stocks. They trade like semis because their order books have become AI capex order books.” ▶ 37:43
Year-to-date the equal-weight S&P 500 is up ~12.6% versus ~7.3% for the Mag 7, an unusually large spread driven not by classic market 'broadening' but by AI-capex spillover into industrials, freight, and power names within the S&P 493.
data_point🗣 host (Michael Batnick) / Neil Duttaconviction: highequal-weight S&P 500Mag 7industrialsGeneracCaterpillar
So what: The equal-weight outperformance is concentrated AI exposure in disguise, not genuine diversification, so it offers less recession protection than a breadth signal implies.
“the Mag 7 are up... 7.3% year to date... But the [equal weight] are up 12.6%” ▶ 38:36
