In a risk-on environment correlations go to one in both directions, so AI optimism lets unrelated sectors (e.g., Ford pivoting to supply batteries for the power buildout) participate beyond any rational mechanical linkage.
framework🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumFordbatteriespowerAI
So what: Broad participation is partly a risk-appetite artifact; when sentiment reverses, the same names will sell off together, so the apparent diversification is fragile.
Wealth effect, RSUs and the spending engine 5
Consumer spending is being funded by equity gains and a collapsing personal saving rate rather than paychecks, since spending growth (~2%) is outrunning sluggish wage and salary income growth.
causal🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highconsumer spendingsaving ratewages
So what: A spending engine running on portfolio gains rather than income is more vulnerable to an equity drawdown than to a labor wobble; watch the stock market as the consumer's real funding source.
“where are you funding this consumer spending? If you look at the personal saving rate right now, it's kind of collapsed” ▶ 13:55
The wealth effect runs mainly through investment accounts, not home equity, because investors can liquidate brokerage gains in days and 'the bucket fills again,' whereas home-value gains aren't on a screen and don't translate into spending.
framework🗣 host (Michael Batnick)conviction: highwealth effectequitieshousing
So what: This means the marginal spender is the stock-owning upper-income household; equity volatility, not housing, is the key transmission channel to consumption.
≈ “your investment account, it leads to overconfidence... You can take money out of your investment account, pay for a home renovation or a toy... and in 10 days, the bucket is full again” paraphrase ▶ 27:13
Over half the growth in California income-tax withholding is driven by vesting RSUs at big tech companies, showing AI-driven equity comp is materially funding consumer spending and state government finances in ways simple GDP accounting misses.
data_point🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highRSUsCaliforniabig techincome tax
So what: Tech equity comp is a hidden macro stabilizer; a tech selloff would hit RSU values, withholding, state budgets, and high-end consumption simultaneously.
“over half the growth in income tax withholding is a function of all the RSUs that are vesting” ▶ 21:01
The 2022 bear market (Amazon/Meta halved, Nvidia down ~two-thirds) did not stop consumer spending because income growth and pandemic savings were still present, unlike today where income growth is weak and savings have been drawn down.
contrarian🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: high2022 bear marketAmazonMetaNvidiaconsumer spending
So what: A 2022-style drawdown would bite harder now because the income/savings cushion that absorbed the last one is gone, raising the consumer's sensitivity to an equity correction.
Sky-high NBA Finals ticket prices (Knicks get-in price ~$7,000, double Super Bowl get-in) are read as evidence of a powerful upper-income discretionary spending boom driven by stock-market wealth, even if not backed by the broad job market.
anecdote🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumNBA Finalsdiscretionary spendingwealth effect
So what: The 'K-shaped' top end is spending aggressively; luxury/experiential and travel exposure benefits while broad consumer plays may not.
Inflation, supply shocks and the Fed hike debate 6
The macro risk distribution has flipped versus 12 months ago: the labor market has moved off the left tail to 'solid' while inflation now has right-tail properties, driven by oil (Strait of Hormuz closure), tariffs, and AI-driven hardware/memory price increases.
framework🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: highinflationlabor marketoilStrait of Hormuztariffs
So what: With recession risk receding and inflation risk rising, the policy debate has shifted from cuts to hikes, repricing rate-sensitive assets.
“the labor market's not at the left tail and the inflation stuff's at closer to the right tail” ▶ 11:04
The AI boom is itself inflationary: information-technology commodity prices (laptops, software, memory) are RISING due to shortages, the opposite of the 1990s/2000s tech booms where IT prices deflated steadily.
contrarian🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highAImemorylaptopssoftwareinflation
So what: Rising tech-commodity prices undercut the 'AI productivity disinflation' thesis and add a structural source of inflation that complicates Fed easing.
“the prices for information technology commodities... it's actually going up like... in the 90s or 2000s it was deflating month after month” ▶ 34:23
The futures market is now pricing a Fed rate hike (implied fed funds path through Sept 2027), with three dissents at the last meeting on the hawkish side and an easing-to-tightening bias shift seen as the next battle.
data_point🗣 host (Michael Batnick) / Skanda Amarnathconviction: highFederal Reservefed fundsrate hike
So what: A market already leaning toward hikes means rate-sensitive sectors (housing, credit, long duration) carry asymmetric downside if the Fed confirms a tightening bias.
“the market is pricing at a rate hike” ▶ 56:31
Dutta argues the Fed should look through these supply shocks and stay dovish because labor and housing are soft, while Amarnath argues the Fed WILL hike (and that if they hike it will be 75-100bp / three hikes, not one) because they keep underbaking inflation and will overreact to its breadth and persistence.
prediction🗣 Neil Dutta & Skanda Amarnathconviction: highFederal Reserverate hikeinflationlabor market
So what: The dovish-vs-hawkish split frames the key 2026 risk: a supply-driven hiking cycle that tightens into weak demand would be 'very bad' for equities and credit, unlike a demand-driven hike.
“They're not going to go once. They're going to go 75 to 100.” ▶ 57:23
Hiking rates because of supply shocks (oil, cattle/beef prices, El Nino) the Fed cannot control would exacerbate stresses in credit-sensitive areas like housing and credit rather than cooling genuine demand, because the labor market is not overheating as it was in 1999-2000.
causal🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highFederal Reserveoilcattle priceshousingcredit
So what: A 'wrong-reason' hike is a bearish setup for risk assets; investors should distinguish demand-driven hikes (benign) from supply-driven hikes (corrosive) when positioning.
“if they're hiking for the reasons that Scandanda is talking about, like that is not a good outcome” ▶ 58:20
Kevin Warsh, now seen as Fed chair/boss, is more likely to be captured by the Fed staff and committee than to capture it; pushing too hard for dovish cuts will provoke hawkish dissents (a 'Chinese finger trap'), and his shift to a 'data guy' coincided suspiciously with campaigning for the job.
thesis🗣 Neil Dutta & Skanda Amarnathconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reservemonetary policy
So what: Don't assume a new dovish chair guarantees cuts; institutional dynamics and a hawkish committee could neutralize him, keeping rate-cut bets at risk.
“it's as likely that the Fed captures him than he captures the Fed” ▶ 51:45
Profit-vs-labor divide and productivity 4
Labor's share of gross domestic income fell to ~51%, the lowest since records began in 1947, while corporate profit share climbed to ~12.1%, the highest since 1950, with Q1 worker compensation up only 0.8% versus corporate profits up 2.7%.
data_point🗣 host (Michael Batnick) citing Greg Ipconviction: highlabor sharecorporate profitsGDIwages
So what: Record-high profit share / falling unit labor costs is a structural tailwind for the stock market but a political/social headwind; it supports margins now while sowing instability.
“labor share of gross domestic income sank to 51%, the lowest since records began in 1947, and profit share climbed to 12.1%, the highest since 1950” ▶ 45:58
Nominal GDP is growing ~6% while nominal wage-and-salary compensation runs below ~4%, an unusually wide and historically rare disconnect.
data_point🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highnominal GDPcompensationwages
So what: How this gap closes is the master variable: convergence of wages up to GDP means a hawkish Fed; GDP down to wages means the Fed can wait, so the spread dictates the rate trajectory.
“nominal GDP is growing about 6%... and nominal compensation like wages and salaries... is running below 4%” ▶ 48:48
This is a strange 'productivity boom' because real compensation growth has been negative this year, so all the gains are flowing to margins rather than raising household living standards, unlike the genuinely productive 1990s.
contrarian🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highproductivityreal compensationmargins
So what: If AI productivity gains never reach households as lower prices/higher real wages, the boom is margin-driven and politically/economically less durable than bulls assume.
“We just had negative real compensation growth this year. So right now all the growth is flowing to... margins I guess” ▶ 34:55
Measured productivity is not as rosy now as in 2023-24 because current supply shocks (higher gasoline, energy, airfares, cattle/beef prices) will feed through and weigh on productivity, so much of the touted AI productivity reflects gains from one-to-three years ago.
causal🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumproductivityenergy pricesairfaresAI
So what: The 'productivity boom' narrative supporting a dovish Fed and high multiples may not show up in the hard data, undermining a key bull pillar.
Labor market: white-collar vs blue-collar split 4
White-collar / professional and business services hiring (including temp help) is picking up, defying predictions that AI would replace white-collar jobs, while blue-collar and manufacturing job growth (ex data-center construction and Boeing ramp) is weak.
data_point🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumwhite-collar jobsprofessional and business servicesBoeingmanufacturingAI
So what: The AI-eats-white-collar-jobs thesis is contradicted by current hiring data; the actual soft spot is blue-collar/manufacturing, which reshapes consumer-exposure and policy bets.
Job openings data (e.g., a reported 668,000 jump in professional/business services openings to a 2-year high of 7.6M) is dismissed as the 'fakest' labor indicator inflated by fake postings; hires and quits are far more informative, and Indeed postings are softening.
framework🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: highjob openingsJOLTSIndeedlabor market
So what: Investors should weight hires/quits and Indeed data over headline JOLTS openings when judging labor strength to avoid being misled by a noisy print.
“openings are like the fakest thing” ▶ 1:01:00
The sharp 2025 slowdown in job growth stabilized on its own (initial jobless claims peaked and normalized, defying 'object in motion' fears), with stabilization visible around the December jobs report post-shutdown.
data_point🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: mediumjobless claimsjob growthlabor market
So what: The labor market self-stabilizing removes a near-term recession catalyst, supporting the 'recession signals are wrong' thesis and the case against cuts.
Layoffs are a lagging, last-to-go signal, so by the time mass layoffs appear the downturn is already over; companies also hesitate to cut because they risk losing the knowledge workers needed to actually leverage AI.
framework🗣 Neil Dutta & Skanda Amarnathconviction: highlayoffslabor marketAI
So what: Waiting for layoff headlines to de-risk is a losing strategy; investors need forward signals (credit spreads, capex willingness) rather than employment confirmation.
≈ “by the time layoffs are showing you something in terms of a signal, like it's over, buddy” paraphrase
Consumer signals: noise vs real squeeze 4
Weak consumer-discretionary and restaurant stocks (Cava, Shake Shack, Chipotle, Domino's) are largely idiosyncratic (post-IPO overvaluation, changing preferences, GLP-1 effects) and NOT reliable evidence the broad consumer is slowing, given Hyatt and Delta sit at all-time highs.
contrarian🗣 host (Michael Batnick)conviction: highCavaShake ShackChipotleDomino'sHyattDeltaGLP-1
So what: Don't read restaurant/discretionary weakness as a macro slowdown signal; the real shift is goods-to-services and GLP-1-driven rotation, favoring travel and experiences over food/staples.
≈ “if you look at a stock like Hyatt, which is at an all-time high today, and Delta, which is at an all-time high today, I don't care what Domino's Pizza stock is doing” paraphrase
The real consumer squeeze is coming through staples passing inflation through: petrochemical-based household cleaning and paper products plus food prices are being marked up to preserve margins, forcing a real consumption squeeze even as nominal spending holds.
causal🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: highconsumer staplespetrochemicalsfood pricesinflation
So what: Watch staples pricing/volume: rising prices with contracting real volumes (e.g., apparel volumes shrinking) signal the squeeze, which caps real demand and pressures consumer-facing earnings.
“someone's going to hit the squeeze. It might be the consumer in that case and that's really like a real consumption squeeze” ▶ 43:36
GLP-1 drugs are having a materially underestimated impact on the economy, driving a rotation away from grocery and food-services spending toward recreation, accommodations, and resort spending.
thesis🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: highGLP-1groceriesfood servicestravelresorts
So what: GLP-1 is a structural demand-mix shift; underweight food/snack staples and overweight experiential/travel names exposed to the redirected spend.
≈ “I completely underestimated the GLP1 story and it is having a material impact on areas of the economy” paraphrase
Credit-card, mortgage, and agricultural delinquencies are ticking up at the margin (noted in the latest Beige Book), and if wage growth keeps slowing the household sector's stresses will worsen, suggesting banks should provision more for loan losses over the next year.
causal🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: mediumdelinquenciescredit cardsmortgagesbanksBeige Book
So what: Rising delinquencies plus sluggish wages argue for caution on consumer-credit-exposed lenders; a 'body in motion' dynamic could compound if labor doesn't accelerate.
What to watch for the cycle turn 4
Credit spreads are the best (if episodic and often late) proxy for the cycle turn; the credit market not freaking out during Liberation Day kept the hosts comfortable being on the right side of the positive trend.
framework🗣 Skanda Amarnath / hostconviction: highcredit spreadscorporate credit
So what: Monitor corporate credit spreads as the primary early-warning gauge; widening spreads, not equity headlines, would flag the regime change.
“business cycles are risk cycles” ▶ 21:57
The key fragility to watch is the willingness of NON-tech companies to keep spending on AI, because for them it is an expense requiring ROI, unlike hyperscalers for whom capex is a moat; a 2000-style frontloading-then-fatigue in AI/token expenditure is the likely turn mechanism.
thesis🗣 Skanda Amarnath, Employ Americaconviction: highAInon-tech companieshyperscalerstokenscapex
So what: Track ROI commentary and AI/token spend from non-tech enterprises (the marginal, discretionary buyer) as the most likely first crack in the boom.
“for them it's an expense and they need to see payoff on that expense at some point” ▶ 1:07:28
Conditions turn less favorable into next year as the 'one big beautiful bill' fiscal tailwind fades into a headwind by year-end, and a Fed on hold while nominal compensation stays weak amounts to a passive tightening for the labor market.
prediction🗣 Neil Dutta, RenMacconviction: mediumfiscal policyFederal Reservecompensation
So what: A fading fiscal impulse plus passive tightening sets up a tougher 2026 macro backdrop even absent an outright Fed hike; position for deteriorating conditions late in the year.
“the one big beautiful bill tailwind is going away by the end of the year, that... transitions to more of a headwind” ▶ 1:05:53
Nvidia now breaking out its revenue by hyperscaler versus everybody else gives investors a cleaner read on whether AI demand is broadening beyond the megacap behemoths.
framework🗣 host (Michael Batnick)conviction: mediumNvidiahyperscalersAI revenue
So what: Nvidia's per-customer revenue disclosure is now a key diffusion gauge; broadening non-hyperscaler demand would validate durability, concentration would flag fragility.
The Biggest Short Squeeze of All Time | Animal Spirits 46767 min40 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Carlson and Batnick survey a fearless, fundamentally-driven AI/semiconductor melt-up where 11 of the S&P 500's top names now top $1T, argue mega-cap IPOs like SpaceX create a structural 'short squeeze' on index funds via constrained float, and flag the AI bear case (circular capex), Google's unprecedented equity raise, and a savings-rate plunge as the late-cycle signals to watch.
AI/semiconductor melt-up & fundamentals · 9SpaceX IPO & the index 'short squeeze' mechanic · 7AI bear case & capex sustainability · 6Global AI trade & emerging markets · 4Capital supply: Google equity raise & concentration · 6Macro: savings, money markets, labor · 4Crypto, real estate & other markets · 4
AI/semiconductor melt-up & fundamentals 9
Chip stocks powered the S&P 500 up 16% across April and May 2026, a two-month surge matched only four other times since 1950, and in each prior instance the index was higher six months later by a median of 17%.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highS&P 500semiconductorsWall Street Journal
So what: Historical base rate argues against fading the rally near-term; momentum has tended to persist 6 months out.
≈ “Chip stocks powered the S&P 500 up 16% across April and May. A two-month surge matched only four other times since 1950. The index was higher 6 months later each time by a median of 17%.” paraphrase
From an all-time high, the S&P 500 has historically made another all-time high within the next year 99% of the time, undercutting the instinct to sell into record highs.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highS&P 500
So what: New highs are not a reliable sell signal; supports staying invested rather than de-risking on record prints alone.
≈ “99% of the time, within the next year, the S&P 500 made another all-time high after making one.” paraphrase
Every stock in the top 10 of the S&P 500 now carries a trillion-dollar-plus market cap, with Micron becoming the 11th member of the trillion-dollar club, while Walmart and JPMorgan are now smaller than Micron.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highMicronS&P 500WalmartJPMorganEli Lilly
So what: Extreme index concentration in AI/semis means breadth and benchmark returns hinge on a tiny cohort of names.
Micron is the fastest company in history to go from a $500B to a $1T market cap, with the next two fastest (Samsung and SK Hynix) happening concurrently, and its trailing-12-month EPS is projected to rise roughly fivefold-to-tenfold over the next three quarters.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highMicronSamsungSK HynixBloombergDRAM
So what: The memory super-cycle re-rating is earnings-backed, not purely speculative, but the speed signals crowding risk.
The memory/semiconductor boom emerged with almost no advance warning; even a year ago virtually no one was pounding the table that memory chipmakers like Micron would re-rate this dramatically.
causal🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: mediumMicronsemiconductorsmemory chips
So what: The unforecastable speed of the move argues for humility on both timing the top and predicting the next leg.
Dell raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to $165-169B from a prior $138-140B after sales climbed 88%, sending the stock up 38%, evidence the AI infrastructure boom is lifting decades-old hardware names.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highDell
So what: Legacy hardware vendors are direct, fundamentally-driven beneficiaries of AI capex, not just the obvious chip designers.
Jensen Huang said Marvell could become the next trillion-dollar company, and Marvell stock rose ~16% premarket on the comment.
data_point🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: mediumMarvellJensen HuangNvidia
So what: CEO endorsements from Nvidia's orbit are moving second-tier semi names sharply; watch custom-silicon/ASIC suppliers.
Roundhill's Memory ETF (ticker DRAM), launched in early April 2026, surpassed $10B in AUM in only 35 days (fastest ETF in history) and reached ~$14B, and is up 127% since launch.
data_point🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highRoundhillDRAM ETFMicronSanDiskSK Hynix
So what: Record-pace thematic ETF inflows are a flow signal of euphoria concentrated in the memory trade.
≈ “The Roundhill Memory ETF ticker DRAM surpassed $10 billion in assets under management in only 35 days, faster than any ETF in history.” paraphrase
The current AI rally is a real, fundamentals-driven move distinct from the 2020-2021 GameStop/AMC era, which the hosts characterize as 'stupid' meme-stock speculation rather than genuine earnings growth.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highGameStopAMCAIMAG7
So what: Frames the melt-up as justified by earnings, lowering the bar for the hosts to stay bullish despite valuation discomfort.
SpaceX IPO & the index 'short squeeze' mechanic 7
A mega-cap private company can engineer a structural short squeeze on index funds by selling minimal float to price-sensitive buyers, locking up the rest, and then forcing passive funds (which must own ~24% of an average S&P member) to chase a supply they cannot fully buy.
framework🗣 guest, Matt Levine (quoted)conviction: highSpaceXS&P 500Vanguardindex fundsMatt Levine
So what: Constrained-float IPOs structurally inflate the post-inclusion price; a model for how SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic could pressure passive flows.
“you have effectively created a short squeeze for the index funds. They have to buy stock at any price and there isn't enough stock for them to buy.” ▶ 31:56
Despite a ~$1.8 trillion valuation, SpaceX would enter the S&P 500 only around the 195th position (near Amgen, Gilead, Shopify, Honeywell) under float-adjusted rules, not as an automatic top-10 weight.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumSpaceXS&P 500AmgenGileadShopifyHoneywell
So what: Counters panic that index holders get massively exposed at IPO; float-adjustment caps initial passive demand.
With a $75B raise, passive demand for SpaceX from index-tracking funds would be near $20B (about a quarter of the offering) per Bloomberg Intelligence estimates.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumSpaceXBloomberg Intelligenceindex funds
So what: Quantifies the forced passive bid that supports the stock post-inclusion.
Including an IPO of this size in major indexes within 15 days is dangerous because there is insufficient time for price discovery, even though the companies legitimately belong in cap-weighted indexes.
contrarian🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highSpaceXAnthropicNASDAQindex providers
So what: Index-rule changes accommodating fast inclusion create exit-liquidity risk for passive investors; watch for further rule tweaks.
“I think to include an IPL of this size in the index after 15 days is kind of nuts.” ▶ 29:59
Goldman forecasts US IPO gross proceeds will hit a record $225B in 2026 (raised from $160B), with Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX alone set to raise more combined than all 1999-2000 dot-com IPOs put together.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highGoldman SachsAnthropicOpenAISpaceXIPO market
So what: A ~$200-250B wall of new equity supply must be funded by selling existing holdings, a potential drag on the broader market.
Anthropic raised a $65B Series H at a $965B post-money valuation and filed its S-1 four days later, signaling an imminent IPO.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highAnthropic
So what: Confirms the largest AI labs are racing to public markets at near-trillion-dollar private valuations.
The hosts float a hypothetical rule barring companies above a certain market-cap threshold from staying private or IPO'ing with only a tiny float, to prevent megacaps from coming public at multi-trillion valuations releasing just ~4% of shares.
framework🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: exploratorySpaceXindex fundsIPO market
So what: Signals regulatory/structural pressure may build against constrained-float mega IPOs; a policy risk to monitor.
AI bear case & capex sustainability 6
TS Lombard's Dario Perkins estimates roughly 80% of current AI revenue is 'circular' (capex cycling between firms like Google, Oracle, and Nvidia) rather than flowing to the broader economy, and a failure to broaden out is the key bear-case risk.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dario Perkins (cited)conviction: mediumTS LombardDario PerkinsGoogleOracleNvidia
So what: If AI spend stays self-referential, the boom lacks external demand validation; watch hyperscaler vs non-hyperscaler revenue splits.
“80% of the revenue right now is circular meaning it's going from Google to Oracle to like it's it's the capex from one company going to these other companies.” ▶ 22:38
Nvidia has begun breaking out quarterly revenue by hyperscaler versus non-hyperscaler customers, likely in response to 'circular financing' criticism, giving investors a new metric to judge whether AI demand is broadening.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: mediumNvidiaBen Thompsonhyperscalers
So what: The non-hyperscaler revenue line is the mile-marker to validate or invalidate the circular-capex bear thesis.
No one can reliably time a bubble; Stanley Druckenmiller, who fought the late-1990s bubble while underinvested, went all-in on tech stocks within hours of the 2000 top and lost roughly $3B in 24 hours, concluding he learned 'nothing'.
anecdote🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highStanley Druckenmillerdot-com bubble
So what: Even elite managers capitulate at tops; argues against confident bubble-timing and for rules-based discipline.
“He lost $3 billion in 24 hours, something like that. And as he reflected on what he learned, his answer was nothing.” ▶ 26:49
Since the pandemic, S&P 500 new highs have occurred at higher VIX prints than the low-volatility, complacent new highs of the 2013-2020 era, indicating investors keep paying up for protection even during rallies.
framework🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: mediumS&P 500VIXDuality Research
So what: This is a hedged, more cautious bull market; fear can return fast even amid record highs, unlike the 2010s.
This market exhibits 'no fear' on a given day but sentiment now whipsaws extremely fast, as shown by a recent 2% mid-day pullback that forced large inflows into cash and money-market ETFs.
thesis🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highVIXmoney market ETFsWarren Pies
So what: Positive price momentum paired with defensive flows is atypical of a classic top; complacency is shallow and reversible.
The hosts urge writing down your rationale before every buy or sell because, after the fact, both up and down moves appear obvious in hindsight, which erodes honest assessment of one's forecasting ability.
framework🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: high
So what: A practical discipline to counter hindsight bias and overconfidence in a euphoric tape.
Global AI trade & emerging markets 4
A basket of Chinese AI stocks and a basket of US AI stocks have tracked each other beat-for-beat since the start of 2024, with Chinese names slightly outperforming, showing the AI trade is now a global phenomenon.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highChinaUS AI stocksemerging marketsNASDAQ 100
So what: AI exposure can be diversified globally; EM and Chinese tech may move with, not against, US AI leaders.
Carlson argues AI, unlike the internet, may actually narrow the global gap and elevate the rest of the world toward US-level productivity rather than entrench US dominance.
thesis🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: lowAIemerging marketsUS
So what: Contrarian framing that supports allocating to international/EM equities as AI beneficiaries.
South Korea's stock market now has a larger total market cap than India and has surpassed the UK and Canada, with the iShares South Korea ETF (EWY) outperforming the S&P 500 over ten years, all of that outperformance coming in the past year.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highSouth KoreaEWYIndiaS&P 500SK Hynix
So what: Korea's memory-chip exposure has driven a violent re-rating; a concentrated, momentum-driven EM trade.
A 2x SK Hynix ETF in Hong Kong has grown AUM 10x in 2026 to become the third-biggest ETF in that market at 8.5% of all assets, trading over $1B/day, levels with no US equivalent.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highSK HynixHong Kongleveraged ETFsEric Balchunas
So what: Extreme single-name leveraged-ETF concentration in Asia is a speculative-froth warning sign in the memory trade.
Capital supply: Google equity raise & concentration 6
Alphabet is doing an ~$80B equity offering, its first since 2005, including a $10B private placement to Berkshire Hathaway at only a ~4% discount, an almost unprecedented move for a company this large and mature.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highAlphabetGoogleBerkshire Hathaway
So what: A cash-rich megacap raising equity signals AI capex needs may be outgrowing operating cash flow, a possible business-model shift.
Alphabet guided 2026 capex to $180-190B with a significant further increase expected in 2027, despite generating $174B of operating cash flow and issuing $85B of debt over the trailing 12 months.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highAlphabetGoogleNvidia
So what: Insatiable AI infrastructure demand even from the most cash-generative firms; Batnick reads it as a reason to buy more Nvidia.
Cumulative US equity flows into tech since the March 2026 low are about +$30B while cumulative flows ex-tech are about -$4B, confirming investors are crowding almost exclusively into technology.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: hightechnology sectorTodd Sohn
So what: Positioning is one-sided; exiting tech means shedding roughly half the market, making it a crowded, hard-to-fade bet.
Dan Loeb stated on Patrick O'Shaughnessy's podcast that tech is by far Third Point's biggest exposure, illustrating that even discretionary hedge funds are heavily concentrated in the AI trade.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: mediumDan LoebThird Pointtechnology sector
So what: Smart-money concentration in tech reinforces the crowding theme and the difficulty of being underweight.
The S&P 500 is increasingly stretched above its 200-day moving average while the percentage of members above their own 200-day moving average struggles to rise, confirming narrowing breadth driven by megacaps.
data_point🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highS&P 500Kevin GordonSMH
So what: Deteriorating breadth beneath record highs is a classic late-cycle fragility signal.
The SMH semiconductor ETF's distance above its 200-day moving average is unlike anything in the data back to 2001, prompting Batnick to warn new buyers to have a predefined exit plan.
contrarian🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highSMHsemiconductors
So what: Extreme overextension argues for risk management/profit-protection on fresh semiconductor entries.
Macro: savings, money markets, labor 4
The US personal savings rate fell from 5.5% in April 2025 to 2.6% in April 2026, which the hosts attribute counterintuitively to a wealth effect (booming asset prices reducing the felt need to save) rather than purely to consumer stress.
causal🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: highUS consumerpersonal savings rateHeather LongBank of America
So what: Savings rate is negatively correlated with household wealth; the plunge reflects a boom, but also less buffer if asset prices reverse.
There is now $8.28 trillion in money market funds and this cash is not leaving for equities, because rates have held near 3.5-4% rather than the Fed cutting as expected, so it should not be treated as fuel for the stock market.
thesis🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: highmoney market fundsFederal Reserveinterest rates
So what: Debunks the 'cash on the sidelines' bull narrative; sticky money-market yields keep that capital parked.
“This money's not leaving.” ▶ 42:32
Torsten Slok highlights US ADP weekly employment rising as possible Jevons' paradox in real time, where cheaper AI technology creates more demand and more jobs, though the hosts caution it is far too early to draw conclusions.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: lowTorsten SlokADPAIJevons paradox
So what: An early, tentative data point that AI could be net job-creating; a key debate for the economic-impact bull case.
The hosts argue AI will likely create more work rather than save time, because users will offload existing tasks but then take on new tasks they never did before.
framework🗣 Ben Carlsonconviction: mediumAIGeminiproductivity
So what: Reframes the productivity debate: AI adoption may expand task volume, supporting the labor-demand (not labor-destruction) view.
“People think that it's going to save them time. It's just going to be doing more tasks that you weren't doing before.” ▶ 49:02
Crypto, real estate & other markets 4
Michael Saylor's Strategy sold 32 Bitcoin (~$2.5M) to make payment obligations, its first sale since the 2022 crypto winter, with Bitcoin around $68,000 and the stock in a 70%+ drawdown.

The housing market is so slow that real estate agents are quitting; 71% of NAR-surveyed agents in 2025 said real estate was their only profession, the lowest share on record, and a typical agent with 2 years' experience and 3 transactions earned just $8,100 gross in 2024.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highhousing marketNARreal estate agentsmortgage rates

Movie theater attendance is rebounding, with AMC posting its highest May attendance since 2019, while cheap, original horror films from young filmmakers are outperforming franchise IP.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumAMCIMAXmovie theaters
