This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis 2 episode(s) · 68 insights

This Startup Fused Human Brain Cells with Silicon Chips | E229566 min33 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two deep-tech founder interviews: Cortical Labs (Hon Weng Chong) on commercializing 'biological computing' — silicon chips fused with lab-grown human neurons, now in a 120-unit 'biological data center' and raising $30M — and Pyka (Michael Norcia) on large autonomous electric/hybrid cargo and crop-spray drones deployed at scale in Brazil, with a defense-oriented hybrid-diesel 'Dropship' variant. Both surface the same investor thesis: hardware-software-fused systems gated by real-world deployment and regulation, not by code.
Cortical Labs commercial traction & business model · 5Biocomputing tech edge: sample efficiency & energy · 5Biocompute infrastructure, sovereignty & consciousness ethics · 5Developer ecosystem & accidental-platform thesis · 3Pyka: large autonomous drones & deployment moat · 5Regulation as the binding constraint on autonomous flight · 3Pyka Dropship: dual-use defense logistics · 3Vertical integration, supply chain & VC dynamics · 4
Cortical Labs commercial traction & business model 5
Cortical Labs sold out its entire capped run of 30 CL1 biological-computing units at ~$35,000 each (roughly $1M revenue), with units now deployed at five US institutions including Johns Hopkins, Mass General, UCSF, and Dartmouth.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO, 'Han')conviction: highCortical LabsJohns HopkinsMass GeneralUCSFDartmouthCL1
So what: Validated early demand and brand-name research customers de-risk a category most investors would dismiss as science fiction; the $35K hardware price anchors unit economics for the category.
“We've kind of exhausted our entire stock of 30 units that we had capped.” ▶ 1:40
Cortical Labs is raising a $30M round, framed around scaling its 'Cortical Cloud' rather than hardware sales, to lower the accessibility barrier to biological computing the way free CUDA did for GPUs.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsCortical CloudNvidiaCUDA
So what: A live raise plus an explicit 'be the CUDA of wetware' platform thesis is the core investable narrative; watch whether cloud access compounds a developer ecosystem.
“we're raising 30” ▶ 33:38
90% of Cortical Labs' purchases come from academic R&D institutions, with stated primary use cases in drug discovery, disease modeling (Alzheimer's/dementia at Mass General), toxicology and animal-testing alternatives (Hopkins), and movement disorders (UCSF).
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsMass GeneralJohns HopkinsUCSFdrug discoverytoxicology
So what: Near-term TAM is biomedical research / animal-testing replacement, not AI compute; the 'net good' framing also serves as regulatory/ethical cover for the more speculative compute use case.
Cortical Labs has deployed 120 CL1 units in its Melbourne 'biological data center' but only ~20 cloud users are live (with ~10 more pending), citing 'teething' issues growing neurons and a ~2-month lead time from cell-culture decision to usable compute.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsMelbourneCortical CloudCL1
So what: Biological supply-chain latency is the real bottleneck on revenue ramp; capacity utilization (~20/120) is the key operational metric to track before the next raise.
“while we do have 120, only about 20 or 30 users are on the cloud at the moment. Actually, no, 20 users.” ▶ 29:40
Each CL1 currently serves one customer/workload at a time (no chaining yet), but Cortical Labs is developing PDMS microfluidic chiplet partitioning to virtualize a single chip into many isolated neuron wells — a VMware-style multi-tenancy play to raise yield per unit.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsPDMSmicrofluidicsVMwareCL1
So what: Multi-tenant virtualization would step-change cloud gross margins and is the path from boutique hardware to scalable SaaS-like economics; it is still R&D, not shipping.
“maybe you can get a hyper like a virtual machine with multiple people running the same thing. So that way we can increase yield” ▶ 31:09
Biocomputing tech edge: sample efficiency & energy 5
In unpublished reinforcement-learning path-finding benchmarks with a research partner, Cortical Labs' neurons were ~5,000x more sample-efficient than the partner's GPU-based RL systems, meaning a biological unit learns in one step what a GPU needs ~5,000 steps to learn.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical Labsreinforcement learningGPUNeurIPS
So what: Sample efficiency is the exact pain point in modern RL/robotics; a verified 5,000x edge (pending NeurIPS-style publication later this year) would be a genuine non-obvious threat to GPU-bound RL workloads.
“the neurons we had were 5,000 times more sample efficient than their GPU based systems.” ▶ 11:05
GPUs can offset their RL sample-inefficiency by accelerating simulated time ~5,000x, but that advantage collapses for embodied/robotic agents that must operate at real-world speed — pointing biological compute at robotics RL specifically.
causal🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumGPUreinforcement learningrobotics
So what: Identifies the wedge where biocompute could beat silicon: real-time embodied learning where GPUs can't fast-forward the clock; a watch-item for robotics-RL investors.
“you can't accelerate time if you're a robot. You you're operating at the same speed like everyone else in the physical world.” ▶ 11:29
A single CL1 unit draws only ~30 watts, letting data-center partners add biological compute without touching their power and cooling budgets — a structural advantage as AI data centers hit energy caps.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsCL1data centerDay OneSingapore
So what: In a power-constrained AI buildout, ~30W compute that bolts onto existing racks under fixed megawatt caps is a real commercial hook for data-center operators.
“one unit only uses about 30 watts of energy” ▶ 12:48
Chong frames biology — even a fly or cockroach — as possessing 'generalized intelligence' (e.g. real-time evasion/prediction) that today's 'super-intelligent but not generally intelligent' LLMs like GPT-5.5 lack, positioning biocompute as a path to AGI-adjacent generalization without human-level neuron counts.
contrarian🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumLLMsGPT-5.5AGICortical Labs
So what: A contrarian framing that generalization, not raw scale, is the missing ingredient — if it holds, it reframes where AGI capex should flow away from pure transformer scaling.
“even very simple organisms like you know a fly has generalized intelligence um something that none of our machines have” ▶ 8:16
Chong argues the current limit on biological computing is algorithmic (how to encode digital/internet information into analog neural systems), not hardware — the same write-to-neuron problem that brain-computer-interface firms Neuralink and Synchron are solving.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsNeuralinkSynchronbrain-computer interface
So what: Casts Cortical Labs as adjacent to/benefiting from BCI R&D (Neuralink, Synchron); algorithmic breakthroughs there could be read-throughs for biocompute, and vice versa.
Biocompute infrastructure, sovereignty & consciousness ethics 5
Cortical Labs' next biological data center is in Singapore via data-center partner Day One, sited under a 200MW government energy cap, scaling to ~1,000 CL1 units (~200,000 neurons each), with an on-site lab to grow neurons locally.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsDay OneSingaporeCL1
So what: A named data-center partner and a 10x capacity jump (120 to ~1,000 units) is the concrete scaling milestone; Day One's motivation (compute outside the power budget) is a reusable go-to-market for the category.
“they've built not just the space to house a thousand CR1 units, but next to it a laboratory for us to grow the cells for the compute on site.” ▶ 13:09
Growing neurons on-site decouples each biological data center from a single overseas chip vendor, making each facility self-sufficient and removing supply-chain constraints — a 'sovereign compute' angle distinct from GPU-vendor concentration.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical Labssupply chaindata center
So what: Pitches biocompute as a vendor-independent, geopolitically resilient alternative to Nvidia-dependent AI infrastructure — a differentiated narrative for sovereign-AI and supply-chain-anxious buyers.
“every data center technically is not reliant on one vendor from one country.” ▶ 14:10
CL1 neurons can live a long time if maintained; the recurring consumable is the filtration ('kidney') tube-set cartridges, swapped every 4–6 months — defining the razor-and-blade aftermarket of the platform.
data_point🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsCL1
So what: Consumable cartridge replacement is the recurring-revenue / maintenance-cost line item that determines true cloud margins and customer lock-in.
Cortical Labs draws a hard 'red line' against creating conscious systems because a conscious system could suffer, using stem-cell-derived neurons (not harvested brains) and engaging bioethicists and even the Vatican proactively to pre-empt ethical backlash.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highCortical LabsVaticanbioethicsstem cells
So what: Proactive ethics engagement is a deliberate regulatory/PR moat for a category one scandal could kill; the consciousness 'red line' is both genuine constraint and de-risking narrative for institutional buyers and regulators.
“You do not want to create conscious systems because um ethically uh a conscious system has the ability to suffer” ▶ 22:24
Chong says the industry's first priority is agreeing on shared nomenclature across the biocomputing and consciousness research fields, because without common definitions there's 'a lot of fear with not much understanding' that stalls progress and adoption.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical Labsbiocomputingconsciousness research
So what: Standards-setting is an early-mover advantage; whoever frames the nomenclature shapes regulation and category perception — a soft moat for the incumbent.
Developer ecosystem & accidental-platform thesis 3
A non-biologist student developer built a working 'Doom on neurons' demo at a Stanford hackathon using only Cortical Labs' API/SDK (pip-installable, Python/Jupyter), and was subsequently accepted into a top incubator — evidence the platform is low-barrier enough for outside developers.
anecdote🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsDoomStanfordPythonJupyter
So what: External-developer adoption with no biology background is the leading indicator of a real platform; the cloud + SDK is the flywheel the $30M raise is meant to fund.
“the uh Doom was actually a student developer um who um uh participated in a hackathon at Stanford. no biology background, used our APN SDK” ▶ 25:32
Chong argues today's AI boom was a 'fluke' — it took ~7 years from CUDA's creation to AlexNet — and Nvidia's real masterstroke was making CUDA free on even the cheapest gaming GPUs, an accessibility playbook Cortical Labs is explicitly copying with its cloud.
framework🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: highNvidiaCUDAAlexNetCortical Labs
So what: Reframes Nvidia's moat as distribution/accessibility rather than serendipity, and signals Cortical Labs' deliberate strategy to seed an ecosystem before the 'killer algorithm' arrives.
“what Jensen did you know I think brilliantly at the start was he made CUDA free.” ▶ 24:43
Chong is explicitly 'team human,' arguing LLMs will not solve biocomputing applications and human developers remain essential to invent use cases — flagging upcoming hackathons at MIT to recruit talent.
thesis🗣 Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)conviction: mediumCortical LabsLLMsMIT
So what: Talent/developer recruiting is the gating input for ecosystem growth; hackathon pipeline at MIT is a concrete signal of where the next applications (and hires) come from.
“I'm on team human here, so we still need developers to think of really cool applications. The LMS are not going to solve this for us.” ▶ 35:35
Pyka: large autonomous drones & deployment moat 5
Pyka claims to be the only company on Earth deploying Group 4 UAS (drones over 1,320 lbs) to commercial customers at scale, operating exclusively in rural Brazil where customers run 7-day-a-week, 12–13-hour shifts.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaGroup 4 UASBrazilagricultural drones
So what: A defensible 'only at scale' position in heavy autonomous cargo/ag drones; real-world operating hours are the proprietary data moat competitors lack.
“we're actually the only company on Earth that I'm aware of that have deployed a group for UAS to commercial customers at scale.” ▶ 41:00
Pyka's Pelican ag drone starts at ~$550,000 with a roughly 2–3 year payback, but its core value is spray quality and a cheaper labor pool — operators trainable in 2–4 weeks versus ~18 months for a manned aerial-application pilot.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanagricultural drones
So what: Concrete unit economics and a labor-arbitrage moat (weeks vs. 18 months of pilot training) define the adoption case; payback period is the metric for ag-tech ROI underwriting.
“you can train someone to operate a Pelican in 2 to 4 weeks versus a aerial application pilot is you know 18 months” ▶ 49:59
The chemical sprayed costs ~4x the cost of application, so Pyka's edge is even, precise deposition that reduces waste of an expensive input — amplified now that a blocked Strait of Hormuz is spiking global fertilizer prices.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanfertilizerStrait of Hormuz
So what: Ties drone ROI to volatile agrochemical input costs; rising fertilizer/chemical prices (Hormuz disruption) strengthen the precision-spray value prop and demand timing.
“the cost of the chemical that is being sprayed is typically about four times the cost of the application.” ▶ 50:18
Pyka's electric Pelican burns ~2 gallons/hour even when charged off a dirty diesel generator, versus the competing manned Air Tractor's ~55 gallons of jet fuel per hour — a ~25x fuel-efficiency advantage.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelicanAir Tractor
So what: Order-of-magnitude operating-cost advantage versus the incumbent crop-duster aircraft is the hard quantitative case for switching, fuel-price-resilient.
“our uh the Pelican in the worst case if you're charging it off of a diesel generator is about 2 gall per hour.” ▶ 47:11
US adoption requires ~20,000-acre farms to fully utilize a Pelican (very large by US standards, midsize by Brazil's), pointing to a 'spray-as-a-service' contractor model in the US where one operator services many smaller farms in a radius.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumPykaPelicanspray-as-a-serviceUnited States
So what: Signals the US business model will be service/utility, not unit sales — relevant for sizing US TAM and the eventual recurring-revenue structure.
Regulation as the binding constraint on autonomous flight 3
Pyka holds the largest drone approved for US commercial use by the FAA, but is capped at ~4km from takeoff (vs. 5–15km in Brazil), which Norcia calls 'not commercially viable today'; beyond-visual-line-of-sight rules are the gating constraint and Pyka is working with the FAA to expand the limit.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaFAABVLOSBrazil
So what: US regulatory timeline — not technology — is the swing factor for Pyka's domestic revenue; FAA BVLOS rulemaking is the catalyst to watch for the whole large-drone sector.
“We can only fly the drone about 4 kilometers away from where it took off and landed from, which is not commercially viable today.” ▶ 44:51
Brazil deregulated agricultural drones ~2 years ago, which is the primary reason Pyka deploys there; Norcia argues the US is 'shooting itself in the foot' by failing to enable scaled BVLOS operations on a relevant timeline.
causal🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaBrazilFAABVLOS
So what: Regulatory arbitrage (Brazil, Rwanda for Zipline, Ukraine for combat drones) is where real-world deployment data is being captured — a recurring pattern investors can use to find deployable hardware companies.
“Brazil actually deregulated agricultural drones about 2 years ago, which is very convenient for us.” ▶ 44:34
Norcia generalizes that hardware-software-blended products' value is '100% determined by their exposure to the real world,' citing SpaceX (blew up rockets to learn), Zipline (Rwanda), and Ukraine combat-drone makers as the few firms that found a path to collect deployment data.
framework🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highSpaceXZiplinePykaUkraine
So what: A reusable diligence lens: deep-tech hardware is only as valuable as its real-world flight/operating data — screen for companies that have unlocked a deployment geography.
“their value is 100% determined by their exposure to the real world.” ▶ 58:52
Pyka Dropship: dual-use defense logistics 3
Pyka built its hybrid turbo-diesel 'Dropship' from CAD to first flight in 180 days as a dual-use logistics aircraft, hitting Air Force/SOCOM/Army-derived requirements: ~1,000-mile range with 500-lb payload, fits a 20-ft container, removable tail, heavy-fuel (JP5/diesel) operation, and air-droppable payloads.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaDropshipUS Air ForceSOCOMUS Army
So what: A defense-spec product hitting unwritten contested-logistics requirements opens a far larger DoD TAM than ag; 180-day CAD-to-flight is the speed signal that attracts defense-tech capital.
“we went from uh like initial CAD renderings to first flight in 180 days.” ▶ 53:22
Pyka sent three electric Pelican Cargo aircraft to the Air Force and one to the Army, received positive feedback, a follow-on contract interest from the Air Force, and invitations to major demos in ~2.5 months — concrete early defense traction.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaPelican CargoUS Air ForceUS Army
So what: Tangible government pilot deployments and follow-on contract signals are leading indicators of a defense revenue ramp; the upcoming demos are a near-term catalyst.
Pyka deliberately did not optimize Dropship as a low-cost kinetic bomber — its vehicles are 'too nice,' reliable and long-lived — choosing the reusable contested-logistics niche over expendable munitions, a different defense positioning than attritable attack drones.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumPykaDropshipdefense
So what: Clarifies Pyka's defense segment as reusable logistics, not the crowded expendable-FPV-strike market — distinct competitive set and procurement logic.
“our vehicles last too long. Basically, they're too nice.” ▶ 52:50
Vertical integration, supply chain & VC dynamics 4
Pyka vertically integrated nearly every critical component (motors, batteries, motor controllers, avionics, airframes) because off-the-shelf parts didn't exist when it started, letting it now ship NDAA-compliant versions and manufacture battery management systems in the US for defense.
thesis🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaNDAAsupply chainChina
So what: China-free, NDAA-compliant vertical integration is a defense procurement moat and de-risks supply chains — a key diligence checkbox for defense-hardware investors.
“we actually vertically integrated essentially every critical component on our product.” ▶ 1:00:06
Norcia quantifies the cost of supply-chain sovereignty: if you own the design, US vs. China manufacturing is ~2x; if you don't own the design and buy from an OEM, US vs. China is ~4x — so design ownership roughly halves the reshoring cost penalty.
data_point🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highPykaChinaUnited StatesOEM
So what: A concrete reshoring cost framework: owning IP cuts the US-manufacturing premium from ~4x to ~2x — useful for evaluating any hardware firm's China-decoupling economics.
“If you don't own the design and you're buying from an OEM... it's probably gonna be like 4x difference.” ▶ 1:01:28
Norcia says current VC capital is flowing heavily to 'enterprise agent orchestration / MCP server' and AI-wrapper SaaS startups (which he urges VCs to stop backing), implying capital is mispriced away from deployable deep-tech hardware like drones.
contrarian🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: mediumventure capitalMCPAI SaaSPyka
So what: A founder-side read on capital rotation: crowded AI-agent/SaaS funding vs. relatively under-capitalized deep-tech hardware — a potential contrarian allocation signal.
“Stop backing SAS companies that are slapping on an AI rapper. Back something cool. We're building drones, y'all.” ▶ 1:05:06
Norcia argues the most successful hardware companies (Apple, Tesla, DJI) win by owning both hardware and software to deliver a 'magical' simple experience, and warns that integrating disparate third-party systems produces a visibly worse product — implicitly framing Boeing's outsource-and-integrate model as the cautionary failure.
framework🗣 Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)conviction: highAppleTeslaDJIBoeingPyka
So what: A reusable thesis that full-stack hardware-software ownership is the durable moat in hardware (Apple/Tesla/DJI) and outsourced integration is the path to Boeing-style decline — a screen for hardware investments.
“you have to own the hardware and the software in my opinion. like that's how you make that really magical experience.” ▶ 1:03:51
The project SpaceX's first employee raised $500M for61 min35 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two-part TWiST deep-tech interview: Tom Mueller (SpaceX's first employee, CEO/CTO of Impulse Space) details a $500M Series D taking total raised past $1B for in-space mobility (Mera tug, Helios kick-stage), and the thesis that lunar resources will feed orbital AI data centers; then Tessa Lau (CEO, Dusty Robotics) lays out a construction-layout robot rented as a subscription, riding the data-center buildout, positioning as the 'orchestration layer' for all future jobsite robots.
Space-capital cycle: deep-tech rounds rivaling AI · 4In-space mobility economics (Mera & Helios) · 9Lunar resources feeding orbital data centers · 4Vertical integration & space talent market · 3Orbital congestion & GEO market structure · 3Dusty Robotics: construction layout as a real-revenue robot · 4Data-center buildout as construction-tech tailwind · 3Physical AI vs. humanoid-hype capital allocation · 5
Space-capital cycle: deep-tech rounds rivaling AI 4
Impulse Space closed a $500M Series D that pushes total capital raised past $1B, and was oversubscribed on every round since the Series C, with insiders pushing to deploy and the company upsizing the round to admit new investors.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceTom MuellerSeries D
So what: Signals that late-stage capital is now flowing to in-space infrastructure at AI-adjacent scale; watch for follow-on rounds and a private secondary/IPO path for Impulse.
“We were over subscribed on all of our rounds uh even since the C round.” ▶ 1:23
Impulse Space describes itself as 'absolutely not capital constrained,' implying it can fund multi-year hardware development (Helios, landers) without near-term dilution pressure.
thesis🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse Space
So what: A well-capitalized private space prime can out-wait competitors on hard milestones; reduces financing risk for backers but raises the bar for new entrants.
The host frames the moment as space companies finally raising 'enormous amounts of money' after a period where it seemed only three AI companies could, marking a broadening of mega-round capital beyond pure AI.
thesis🗣 host (Alex, TWiST)conviction: mediumAIspace sectorventure capital
So what: A rotation of risk capital into deep-tech/space could re-rate listed space names and pull more LP money toward hard-tech funds.
“It seemed for a long time like it was going to be three AI companies and literally no one else.” ▶ 1:04
SpaceX has filed to go public, an event Mueller expects to trigger a 'post-PayPal-mafia' style wave of talent and founders breaking off to start the next generation of space companies.
prediction🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumSpaceXIPOPayPal mafia
So what: A SpaceX IPO could both create a liquid public benchmark for space valuations and seed a fresh cohort of fundable startups; watch the El Segundo space cluster for new formations.
“I'm hoping you fingers crossed that we end up with a like like a post PayPal IPO exploding explosion of talent and founders” ▶ 26:30
In-space mobility economics (Mera & Helios) 9
Impulse's Mera tug provides ~900 m/s of delta-V (fine for moving within a LEO band) but is over 4x short of the ~4 km/s needed for a LEO-to-GEO transfer, so commercial demand was weak; Space Force became the anchor customer with over half a dozen units in production, mostly government.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highMeraImpulse SpaceSpace ForceLEOGEO
So what: Government, not commercial, is the near-term revenue base for small in-space tugs; investors should weight defense procurement exposure over commercial ride-share for this product line.
“Turns out Space Force really liked it. So we've got you know over half a dozen of them in in production right now mostly for government” ▶ 5:00
Helios is a Falcon-9 third stage holding 12 tons of LOX/methane with a ~15,000-lbf pump-fed engine that takes a 4-ton satellite directly LEO-to-GEO in ~8 hours, versus the ~10 months electric-propulsion comsats currently need to reach final orbit.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosFalcon 9GEOelectric propulsion
So what: Compressing 10 months of orbit-raising to hours is a step-change in satellite time-to-revenue; could shift comsat operators away from all-electric platforms and pressure incumbent in-space propulsion vendors.
“most of the commercial geos are going on a Falcon 9 to a transfer orbit and then they use uh onboard uh electric propulsion and take up to you know up to 10 months” ▶ 8:55
Helios is priced at $25M and on a Falcon 9 does roughly what a $70-100M Falcon Heavy does, so a customer adds ~$25M to a ~quarter-the-cost launch and gets up to ~5x the payload to Mars (and ~4x to the moon).
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosFalcon 9Falcon HeavyMarsmoon
So what: A cheaper kick-stage that substitutes for Falcon Heavy reframes high-energy launch economics; the host calls it a 'Falcon 9 moment for post-launch in-space movement.'
≈ “our our price for this thing is 25 million and a launch vehicle is like 70 to 100 million. So, you're getting five times the payload for only $25 million more” paraphrase
Helios is already selling well before its first flight, with a customer backlog into 2028 and the first ride-share 'caravan' mission sold out and the second filling, anchored by SES (a top global comsat operator) and Astranis.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highHeliosSESAstraniscaravan ride-share
So what: Pre-revenue backlog from named blue-chip operators de-risks Helios commercially; SES/Astranis adoption is a leading indicator for GEO smallsat demand.
Helios first flight is targeted for 2027 ('next year' per the conversation) and is positioned as a premium product where Mueller is deliberately slowing to ensure it works first time given the unforgiving nature of in-space ignition.
prediction🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumHeliosImpulse Space
So what: Execution/first-flight risk is the key catalyst to watch; a successful debut would validate the whole kick-stage thesis, a failure would reset timelines.
There is a structural backlog of small commercial and government payloads wanting GEO because GEO ride-shares are rare (only when giant comsats like Viasat's 7-ton payloads leave spare capacity), and Mueller estimates ~20 things go to GEO per year, of which Impulse thinks it can capture roughly half.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumGEOViasatHelioscaravan ride-share
So what: Quantifies the GEO ride-share TAM (~20/yr, target ~10) and the cadence ceiling (potentially approaching monthly), useful for sizing Impulse's near-term GEO revenue.
“there's probably 20 about um things going to geo um a year right now. Um we could maybe capture half of that” ▶ 28:40
Helios is expendable because its cryogenic LOX/methane won't store long on-stage; reusability would require LEO and GEO propellant depots, which Mueller puts ~5-10 years out and contingent on SpaceX building tankers/depots for its own moon ambitions.
prediction🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumHeliospropellant depotsSpaceXLEOGEO
So what: In-orbit refueling/propellant-depot startups have a 5-10 year demand runway from kick-stage operators; depot availability is a gating dependency for in-space reusability economics.
“I think we're a ways away from having propellant depots.” ▶ 14:14
Impulse's business works on the current Falcon 9 generation and does not depend on Starship, but flying on Starship (three Helios at once) could cut Impulse's launch cost by about a third even at Falcon-9-equivalent Starship pricing.
thesis🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceFalcon 9StarshipHelios
So what: Lower platform risk than data-center-in-space bets that hinge on launch-cost collapse; Starship success is upside, not a survival requirement, for Impulse.
“if even if Starship was selling at Falcon 9 cost, it would cut our costs in by a third” ▶ 23:09
A speculative 'mega Helios' third stage on Starship (~50 tons propellant, six Deneb engines) could deliver ~30 tons to the moon, enough to start moving cargo like water/ice, though Mueller stresses Impulse is not working with SpaceX on it.
anecdote🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: lowmega HeliosStarshipDeneb enginemoon
So what: Illustrates the long-run TAM (cislunar cargo logistics) and Impulse's engine roadmap optionality, even if speculative today.
Lunar resources feeding orbital data centers 4
Mueller's core thesis is that compute must move to space because power demand for compute grows at least 15% per year, an exponent that becomes 'crushing' over decades, making space's unlimited power the eventual home for AI data servers.
thesis🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highAI data centersspace-based computeElon Musk
So what: Frames in-space logistics as the picks-and-shovels layer for an eventual orbital compute build-out; a long-dated but large demand driver for launch and in-space mobility.
“Power for computes grown at least 15% per year. That's such a such such a steep exponent that it's just becomes crushing after a few decades.” ▶ 21:16
It takes roughly 20x less energy to move material from the lunar surface to LEO than from Earth's surface to LEO, so building orbital mega-structures will economically favor lunar-sourced material plus additive manufacturing and mass drivers (which Musk is already discussing).
causal🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highmoonLEOadditive manufacturingmass driversElon Musk
So what: Energy math makes the moon, not Earth, the supply base for in-space construction; bullish long-term for lunar mining, in-space manufacturing, and cislunar transport.
“it's literally about 20 times less energy to bring material from the surface of the moon to low earth orbit than it is from the surface of the earth” ▶ 21:39
Helios increases lunar payload on a Falcon 9 from ~200 kg to ~1,000 kg and gets ~3+ tons on a Falcon Heavy, enabling delivery of rovers and cargo to a moon base, with Impulse targeting NASA's Eclipse lunar lander program and having started a Mars lander with Relativity.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumHeliosmoonNASAEclipse programRelativity SpaceMars lander
So what: Concrete lunar-cargo capacity gains plus NASA program pursuit point to a government-backed cislunar logistics revenue line; Relativity tie-in worth tracking.
StarCloud's space-data-center thesis (relayed by the host) is explicitly a bet that launch costs decline massively, without which orbiting that much hardware is uneconomic on current rockets.
thesis🗣 host (Alex, TWiST) citing Philip of StarCloudconviction: mediumStarCloudspace data centerslaunch costsStarshipNew Glenn
So what: Space-data-center startups are levered bets on launch-cost deflation; their viability is a derivative of Starship/New Glenn cost curves, a key dependency to monitor.
“our company, if you want to back it, it's a bet that we're going to see launch costs decline massively.” ▶ 22:22
Vertical integration & space talent market 3
Impulse is 'extremely vertically integrated,' which is where much of the $1B raised goes; Mueller argues vertical integration is capital-intensive but essential in space because space vendors are 'notoriously late and expensive' due to cost-plus contracting and the primes not caring.
framework🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse Spacevertical integrationspace primescost-plus contracts
So what: Explains why space deep-tech burns capital and why vertical integration is the moat; a lens for evaluating capital efficiency across space startups vs. legacy primes.
“space vendors are notoriously late and expensive” ▶ 24:11
Impulse more than doubled headcount in the past year, just passed 500 employees with ~200 open reqs, even as space talent gets harder to recruit because experienced SpaceX/industry engineers increasingly leave to found their own companies and recruit their peers.
data_point🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highImpulse SpaceSpaceXspace talent market
So what: Talent scarcity is becoming the binding constraint and cost driver in space hardware; founder spin-out dynamics both fragment talent and seed new investable companies.
“We just passed 500 employees. We've got open recrecks for about 200 more.” ▶ 25:40
The El Segundo / Los Angeles 'SpaceX mafia' cluster has already spun out numerous space and robotics startups, a formation effect Mueller expects to be supercharged by the coming SpaceX IPO.
thesis🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumSpaceX mafiaEl Segundospace startupsrobotics startups
So what: Geographic founder cluster is a sourcing signal for early-stage space/robotics deal flow; IPO liquidity could accelerate spin-out velocity.
Orbital congestion & GEO market structure 3
GEO needs far fewer satellites than LEO (three can cover the whole Earth) and has limited orbital slots (~one per degree, ~360 around the equator), structurally capping the number of GEO births versus mega-constellation LEO traffic.
framework🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highGEOLEOorbital slots
So what: Explains why GEO is a high-value but low-volume market, framing the ceiling on Helios GEO cadence and the relative scale of LEO vs GEO opportunity.
“you can have you can cover the whole Earth with three satellites from GEO.” ▶ 29:09
Mueller expects orbital debris to require active management in under 100 years, noting responsible constellation operators already maneuver and deorbit their satellites while some players leave spent stages in orbit and 'pollute space.'
prediction🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: mediumorbital debrisspace sustainabilityconstellations
So what: Frames space-debris remediation as a real but not imminent market; differentiates operators by deorbit behavior, relevant to ESG and future regulation.
Government launch access runs through the highly contested NSSL (National Security Space Launch) lanes that SpaceX once sued to enter, a politically charged onboarding hurdle every new launch/space provider including Impulse must clear to fly US government payloads.
causal🗣 Tom Mueller (CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)conviction: highNSSLSpaceXUS governmentSpace Force
So what: NSSL certification is a regulatory gating factor and political risk for accessing the largest space customer (US government); a milestone to track for any space-provider thesis.
“This was the thing that SpaceX sued to get into all those years ago” ▶ 18:49
Dusty Robotics: construction layout as a real-revenue robot 4
Dusty Robotics sells an 'inkjet on wheels' that prints consolidated multi-trade floor plans (walls, plumbing, electrical, framing, plus machine-readable QR codes) directly onto construction floors, automating the manual tape-measure-and-string layout process.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsconstruction techField PrinterAutodesk RevitAutoCAD
So what: A deployed, revenue-generating physical-AI product (contrast with pre-revenue humanoids) attacking a universal construction workflow; defensible via the Revit/AutoCAD integration and data layer.
“we've built essentially a uh inkjet on wheels that uh goes around takes that digital version of that blueprint and it prints it on the floor” ▶ 32:43
Dusty runs at roughly 10-17x the speed of a manual layout crew, doing in about a day what a 2-4 person crew would take several weeks (or months on a large data center) to complete by hand.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsconstruction labordata centers
So what: Quantifies a 10-17x labor-productivity gain that converts directly to time/cost savings, the core ROI pitch driving adoption.
“we are tracking at roughly 10 to 17x the speed of a manual layout crew” ▶ 34:41
Dusty monetizes via robot subscriptions (like leasing a car: hardware plus a software license usable on unlimited projects) rather than selling devices or per-outcome, plus paid implementation/training via 'Dusty Academy.'
framework🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsrobot subscriptionDusty Academy
So what: Recurring subscription + training revenue with self-serve onboarding lowers adoption friction and improves revenue quality vs. one-time hardware sales; a model template for fielded service robots.
“we sell robot subscriptions and when you purchase a robot subscription you it's like leasing a car.” ▶ 40:49
Dusty has completed over 330 million square feet of layout lifetime (up from a cited 91M sq ft for the prior generation) and is accelerating, with ~150 robots deployed across the US and Canada (up over 100% year-over-year), targeting ~200 by end of next year.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty RoboticsField Printer 2
So what: Concrete fleet-growth and usage traction (>100% YoY robot growth) evidences real demand and a scaling path, useful for sizing the company's trajectory.
“we are we're now about 150 deployed robots all across the US and Canada.” ▶ 55:27
Data-center buildout as construction-tech tailwind 3
The data-center construction craze is driving major investment into Dusty, with some of the biggest data-center names starting to mandate Dusty and pushing the requirement down onto their general contractors.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsdata centersgeneral contractors
So what: Owner-mandated adoption (top-down demand pull) is a powerful distribution flywheel and a strong leading indicator of revenue durability tied to the AI capex cycle.
“some of the biggest names in data centers um are starting to mandate Dusty.” ▶ 54:17
Lau cites a stat that 60% of data centers earmarked for 2027 aren't yet under construction, with the industry worried timelines won't be met, making any time-removing tool highly valuable.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: mediumdata centersconstruction timelines
So what: Quantifies a looming data-center construction bottleneck (60% of 2027 capacity not started), bullish for schedule-compression tools and a risk flag for AI-infra capacity timelines.
“60% of data centers earmarked for 2027 aren't under construction yet and people are worried that the timelines just aren't going to work out.” ▶ 40:24
Lau argues all data-center construction value reduces to time and money: saving time saves money, and eliminating rework saves time, so speed and accuracy are the same selling point.
framework🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsdata centersconstruction
So what: Reframes the ROI case as a single time-equals-money lever, the clearest articulation of why schedule-critical projects (data centers, hospitals) are the sweet spot.
“It all boils down to time and money.” ▶ 54:45
Physical AI vs. humanoid-hype capital allocation 5
Lau contrasts Dusty's deployed, paying-customer product with humanoid-robotics companies getting hundreds of millions despite not having built something people need, arguing AI is currently sucking up venture dollars while 'physical AI' with real products is comparatively underfunded.
contrarian🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highhumanoid robotsphysical AIventure capitalAI
So what: Flags a potential mispricing/rotation opportunity: revenue-generating physical-AI/construction robots may be undercapitalized relative to pre-revenue humanoids, a contrarian allocation angle.
“I think a lot of people are are are chasing AI companies and AI returns.” ▶ 52:43
Dusty has publicly announced ~$70M raised but Lau implies undisclosed additional capital ('that's what we've publicly announced'... 'I can neither confirm nor deny'), and confirmed a CFO is in place with an IPO as her stated long-term dream.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: mediumDusty RoboticsfundingIPOCFO
So what: Suggests a larger-than-reported balance sheet and IPO-track maturation (CFO hired), worth watching for a future raise or public listing in construction tech.
Dusty positions layout as the construction 'lynchpin/orchestration layer': everything upstream (design) funnels through it and everything downstream (every future jobsite robot) builds on Dusty's printed layout and machine-readable QR codes, giving the company a translation layer it owns.
thesis🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: highDusty Roboticsconstruction roboticsorchestration layerQR codes
So what: If layout becomes the data substrate for all jobsite robots, Dusty captures a durable platform position and expansion path both upstream (design tools) and downstream (robot orchestration).
“every single person or robot you know in the future that's ever going to build anything on top of a job site is going to be building on a dusty layout” ▶ 47:34
Dusty's service-partner network bundles scanning, field coordination, and layout into a higher-priced value-add package, which Lau says lets partners charge significantly more, making partner distribution a net positive for both reach and Dusty's economics.
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: mediumDusty Roboticsservice partnersfield coordination
So what: Bundled-partner channel expands TAM (solving the full field-coordination problem) without sacrificing margin, an under-appreciated unit-economics positive.
Dusty mitigates hardware supply-chain risk learned during COVID by stockpiling long-lead-time parts so builds aren't blocked, saying supply chains 'haven't been impacting us at all.'
data_point🗣 Tessa Lau (CEO/founder, Dusty Robotics)conviction: mediumDusty Roboticssupply chainCOVID
So what: Inventory strategy reduces a key hardware-startup execution risk; relevant when comparing hardware robotics companies' resilience.

The Prof G Show · Scott Galloway 2 episode(s) · 62 insights

Can GLP-1s Save America's Healthcare System?49 min30 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Eli Lilly CEO David Ricks lays out the bull case for GLP-1s as the structural lever on US healthcare costs and the deficit: Lilly is the closest thing to a pure-play GLP-1 index, has crossed a $1T market cap by betting on obesity biology in 2018 before it was a market, is cutting prices via a direct-to-consumer model that exploits elastic demand, and sees applications expanding far beyond weight loss into inflammation/pain, addiction, and cancer. He argues AI in drug discovery is currently overhyped (a 10-year arc, not 2) and dismisses the online 'peptide' craze as unstudied medicine.
GLP-1 as the lever on US healthcare costs & the deficit · 6GLP-1 indication expansion beyond weight loss · 6Pricing, access & the direct-to-consumer model · 6Lilly's competitive moat & capital allocation · 6AI in drug discovery — currently overhyped · 4Online 'peptides', distrust of healthcare & the gray market · 2
GLP-1 as the lever on US healthcare costs & the deficit 6
Eli Lilly CEO argues the US federal deficit cannot be addressed without cutting Medicare/Medicaid costs, which in turn cannot fall without reducing obesity, making GLP-1 obesity drugs a structural macro-fiscal lever, not just a healthcare product.
thesis🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyMedicareMedicaidGLP-1US deficit
So what: Frames GLP-1 demand as politically durable and entitlement-linked; supports a long runway for Lilly/Novo volumes if payers accept the cost-offset case.
“Changing the costs of Medicare and Medicaid um can't happen without changing the obesity rates.” ▶ 6:23
About one-third of all US healthcare spending is obesity-related, roughly $1.4 trillion per year — comparable in size to the entire proposed US Defense Department budget.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highUS healthcareobesityDefense Department
So what: Sizes the total addressable cost pool GLP-1s could attack; anchors the long-term volume opportunity for obesity therapeutics.
“about a third of all health care is obesity related. So $1.4 trillion dollars a year.” ▶ 6:37
Downstream chronic diseases now account for ~80% of all US healthcare costs, and obesity is a 'nodal' condition upstream of more than 200 chronic diseases.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highchronic diseaseobesityUS healthcare
So what: The breadth of obesity-linked comorbidities underpins a multi-indication expansion thesis for GLP-1 franchises.
“the downstream chronic diseases which are now 80% of all healthcare costs” ▶ 6:31
US per-capita healthcare cost (~$13,500) is roughly 2.4x Japan's (~$5,500), with the US carrying a ~40% obesity rate versus Japan's ~4%, implying obesity is a primary driver of America's cost gap.
data_point🗣 host (Scott Galloway)conviction: highUnited StatesJapanobesityhealthcare costs
So what: Quantifies the international cost-vs-obesity correlation investors can use to gauge the savings ceiling from mass GLP-1 adoption.
“healthcare costs in Japan at 5,500 bucks per person versus the US at 135” ▶ 5:14
Roughly 70% of Americans are overweight or obese, framing the obesity-drug market as a mass-market rather than niche opportunity.
data_point🗣 host (Scott Galloway)conviction: highUnited StatesobesityGLP-1
So what: Establishes the size of the eligible patient base that elastic GLP-1 pricing could convert into paying users.
“70% of America is overweight or obese” ▶ 5:09
GLP-1 obesity medicines are claimed to be cheaper than treating the downstream disease they prevent, supporting a payer cost-offset business case.
causal🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highGLP-1payersMedicaid
So what: If payers accept this, formulary coverage expands — the key swing factor for GLP-1 volume and Lilly/Novo revenue.
“the medicines themselves are quite a bit cheaper than treating the disease. I think that's been demonstrated.” ▶ 6:56
GLP-1 indication expansion beyond weight loss 6
Lilly's triple-acting candidate retatrutide (targeted for launch ~next year) was, in trials for knee osteoarthritis pain, described as the most powerful pain-reducing agent ever tested pharmacologically, opening a large pain/arthritis indication overlapping with the overweight population.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyretatrutideosteoarthritisinflammationGLP-1
So what: A blockbuster pain indication would materially expand retatrutide's TAM beyond obesity; a near-term catalyst to watch into launch.
“it was the most powerful pain reducing agent ever tested pharmacologically” ▶ 8:40
GLP-1/incretin drugs reduce hedonic and addictive behaviors — alcohol, smoking, gambling, online shopping — an effect observed spontaneously in Lilly and Novo Nordisk trials and now being studied prospectively.
causal🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highGLP-1incretinsaddictionalcoholNovo Nordisk
So what: Addiction/behavioral indications represent optionality that markets aren't yet pricing into GLP-1 franchises.
“addiction and kind of the consumption of like, um, hedonic activity and substances, um, smoking, uh, gambling, online shopping are mitigated” ▶ 9:10
Lilly has a GLP-class medicine, bimagrumab/'binepetide' (per transcript), in Phase 3 seeking an indication for alcohol use disorder, and a Phase 2 pilot in several hundred people already showed reduced daily alcohol consumption.
prediction🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: mediumEli Lillyalcohol use disorderGLP-1Phase 3
So what: An AUD approval would be a first-in-class addiction indication and a fresh revenue vertical; pipeline catalyst to track.
“we're pursuing a medicine called binepetide in phase three right now which will seek to get indicated for alcohol use disorder” ▶ 12:45
Ricks pushed back on the claim that alcohol-use-disorder is a bigger market than obesity, saying he doubts it, tempering speculation that addiction indications eclipse the core weight-loss franchise.
contrarian🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: mediumalcohol use disorderobesityGLP-1
So what: CEO signals obesity remains the dominant value driver; useful to calibrate hype around addiction TAM estimates.
“Is that a bigger marker than obesity? I doubt that.” ▶ 12:50
Observational data show patients on GLP-1 drugs for 5-6 years (for diabetes) appear to have lower rates of obesity-linked cancers such as colorectal and breast cancer, a potential long-term spillover benefit.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: mediumGLP-1cancercolorectal cancerbreast cancer
So what: Cancer-risk reduction, if confirmed, strengthens the long-term value narrative and payer ROI case for chronic GLP-1 use.
“people who've been using GOP1 drugs for treating their diabetes for five or six years seem to have lower rates of cancer” ▶ 10:12
Ricks frames incretins as a whole family of gut-signaling hormones Lilly intends to exploit with double-, triple-, and quadruple-acting agonists, signaling a multi-generation product pipeline rather than a single drug class.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highincretinsGLP-1Eli Lillyretatrutide
So what: Successive multi-agonist generations give Lilly a path to keep raising the efficacy bar and defend share as early drugs face genericization.
“We're going to exploit more of them in including double, triple, and quadruple acting ones.” ▶ 7:39
Pricing, access & the direct-to-consumer model 6
Unlike most pharma, GLP-1 obesity demand is price-elastic: Lilly finds that lowering price grows users more than proportionally, so its strategy is to maximize return via volume, not price.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyGLP-1Zepboundpricing
So what: Elastic demand inverts the usual pharma pricing-power thesis — investors should model GLP-1 economics on volume/share, not list price.
“the more we lower the price the more users we get and it's not one to one. We tend to have done a little bit better by lowering the price.” ▶ 17:31
Via 'Lilly Direct', cash-pay Zepbound has fallen ~60% since launch to roughly $399/month max, and the new oral Fonduo/orforglipron product starts at $159/month (up to $349), versus a ~$1,086 list price almost nobody pays.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyLilly DirectZepboundorforglipronPBMs
So what: Direct-to-consumer pricing bypasses PBMs and resets the GLP-1 price curve downward — pressure on rivals and on the PBM/retail-pharmacy margin chain.
“now it's 3.99 kind of maximum for Zepbound. It's come down 60% since we launched. and Fondeo, the oral one we just launched, starts at 159” ▶ 16:20
From July 1 (2026), every Medicare-eligible senior can buy Lilly's GLP-1 medicine for $50/month out of pocket, a change Ricks calls a seminal accessibility event for ~70 million people.
prediction🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyMedicareGLP-1seniors
So what: A hard near-term catalyst (July 1) that could step-change Lilly's volume from the Medicare population; watch script/volume data after.
“starting July 1st for $50 out of pocket a month every senior in America can buy our medicine for $50 a month” ▶ 18:53
Lilly cut a deal to supply GLP-1s through Mississippi's Medicaid program — the state with the highest obesity (~78-79% of adults) and among the highest Medicaid enrollment and lowest income — on a multi-year hospital-cost-offset ROI case.
anecdote🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyMississippiMedicaidGLP-1
So what: A template for state-Medicaid GLP-1 access deals; each state contract incrementally expands the covered, low-income patient pool.
“we just managed to cut a deal with them to provide um our products in their Medicaid program” ▶ 18:08
Lilly is investing ~$50 billion in new US factories to eliminate the GLP-1 supply shortages caused by demand outstripping supply, with capacity coming online over the next couple of years enabling further price cuts.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillymanufacturingGLP-1US factories
So what: Capacity ramp removes the supply constraint and enables the lower-price/higher-volume flywheel; a structural margin and share consideration.
“about $50 billion in new factories in the US some of which are online some which will continue to come online” ▶ 16:51
The US drug-pricing system is regressive: the largest buyers get the lowest prices while uninsured individuals (often with the worst or no insurance) pay the most — the gap Lilly Direct is designed to close.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highPBMsLilly Directdrug pricingretail pharmacy
So what: Disintermediating PBMs/retail pharmacy is a deliberate strategic threat to those intermediaries' economics.
“the biggest actor s get the lowest price and the individuals pay the most. It's a totally regressive pricing model” ▶ 15:42
Lilly's competitive moat & capital allocation 6
Eli Lilly is the first healthcare company to reach a $1 trillion market cap and is up roughly fivefold over the past five years, worth more than Johnson & Johnson, Pfizer, Merck and AbbVie combined per the host.
data_point🗣 host (Scott Galloway)conviction: highEli LillyJohnson & JohnsonPfizerMerckAbbVie
So what: Quantifies how much GLP-1 success is already priced in; sets the bar for the 'more running room' bull case versus large-cap pharma peers.
“You're worth more than all these companies combined.” ▶ 27:45
Lilly's capital-allocation philosophy is to invest before a market is obviously big — it bet on obesity biology in 2018 when it was considered a non-market — because by the time a drug market is large it is too late to invest.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli Lillyobesitycapital allocationR&D
So what: Signals where Lilly's next bets sit (not the now-crowded GLP-1 market) — a tell for investors hunting Lilly's future growth vectors.
“by the time a a medicine market is big, it's basically too late to invest” ▶ 29:40
Lilly cut R&D cycle time from invention-to-market by five years (from ~11 years), while the rest of the industry stayed flat, letting it start later and still beat rivals or start level and win decisively within the 20-year US patent life.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyR&Dpatents
So what: Speed is a durable moat that compounds patent-protected revenue years; a key differentiator versus the GLP-1 fast-followers.
“we've now chopped five years out of our cycle time and the rest of the industry is about the same” ▶ 31:30
Lilly's GLP-1 lead is a deliberate strategy to outrun both its own and competitors' patents: when Mounjaro/Zepbound go generic (Ozempic's US patent expires ~2031-2032, dropping price from ~$300 to ~$60/month), fast-followers will struggle to differentiate against the much cheaper generic.
thesis🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyMounjaroZepboundOzempicNovo Nordiskpatents
So what: Genericization timeline (~2031-32) is the key long-horizon risk; Lilly's defense is continuous next-gen launches, framing it as a treadmill investors must underwrite.
“when a breakthrough like Mjaro or Zepbound goes generic, all these followers are going to find it hard to differentiate their drug against something much much cheaper” ▶ 32:19
Ricks attributes Lilly's execution edge to being the only scaled drug company that has never done a major merger, giving it a singular Indiana-based, low-turnover culture with <50,000 employees where leaders solve problems without committee meetings.
thesis🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyIndianapoliscorporate culture
So what: A no-M&A, organic-growth culture distinguishes Lilly's operating model from acquisition-driven big pharma peers — relevant to durability of execution.
“Lily is, I think, the only scaled drug company that's really never had a major merger.” ▶ 32:39
Roughly two-thirds of Eli Lilly's total sales now come from GLP-1-related products, making the stock effectively the closest thing to a pure-play GLP-1 index fund.
data_point🗣 host (Scott Galloway)conviction: highEli LillyGLP-1
So what: High GLP-1 revenue concentration means Lilly is the cleanest equity expression of the GLP-1 thesis — but also the most exposed to GLP-1-specific risk.
“about twothirds of your total sales come from a GLP-1 related uh product” ▶ 3:03
AI in drug discovery — currently overhyped 4
Lilly's CEO says AI/LLMs in drug discovery are currently overhyped — useful for synthesizing existing knowledge but poor at the core task of creating new knowledge, because only ~10-20% of human-body biology is understood, so models trained on it predict the rest badly.
contrarian🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highAILLMsdrug discoveryEli LillyOpenAIAnthropic
So what: A direct counter to AI-driven biotech hype (e.g., Nvidia/Jensen Huang's pitch); tempers near-term expectations for AI-accelerated pharma pipelines.
“the fundamental problem in drug discovery is the word discovery is that we we're not synthesizing existing knowledge. We have to create new knowledge.” ▶ 35:12
Ricks frames AI's impact on drug discovery as a ~10-year arc, not a 2-year one — maybe 5-7 years before Lilly can skip many lab experiments, and human clinical trials and FDA review won't be meaningfully sped up soon.
prediction🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highAIdrug discoveryFDAclinical trials
So what: Sets realistic timing for AI-driven productivity gains in pharma — relevant for valuing 'AI biotech' acceleration narratives.
“I think it's more of a 10-year arc, not a two-year arc.” ▶ 38:35
Lilly is working with Nvidia to build biology-specific datasets that teach AI the rules of biological systems, and runs a free 'Lilly TuneLab' workbench that gives biotechs model access in exchange for their data to train the models.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: mediumEli LillyNvidiaLilly TuneLabAIbiotech
So what: A data-network strategy: Lilly accumulates proprietary biological training data others lack — a potential long-term AI-in-pharma moat.
“We have a thing called Lily Tune Lab, which I think is the leading kind of workbench for drug developers in the world because it's free to biotechs.” ▶ 37:05
Only ~20 of roughly 1,000 pre-clinical drug-development steps have so far been automated with AI/ML at Lilly, illustrating how early AI penetration of the discovery pipeline really is.
data_point🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highEli LillyAImachine learningdrug development
So what: A concrete metric to gauge AI adoption depth in pharma R&D and to discount aggressive AI-productivity claims.
“maybe like 20 of those steps we've automated with AI or machine learning” ▶ 37:36
Online 'peptides', distrust of healthcare & the gray market 2
Lilly's CEO dismisses the booming online 'peptide' market — including Chinese peptides and human-growth-hormone mimetics — as 'unstudied medicines' sold without clinical trials, safety data, or FDA approval, warning HGH mimetics are pro-tumor.
contrarian🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highpeptidesFDAhuman growth hormoneEli Lilly
So what: Flags regulatory/safety risk in the unregulated peptide gray market and positions Lilly's FDA-validated products as the quality differentiator.
“most of mostly I call these unstudied medicines. That's sort of the best way to put it.” ▶ 23:58
Ricks argues the COVID-era distrust of institutions and the online-peptide/self-medication boom are two sides of the same coin: consumers reject a healthcare system that is opaque on price and treats them poorly, and seek agency online despite lacking the tools to validate claims.
framework🗣 David Ricks, CEO Eli Lillyconviction: highhealthcareconsumer behaviorpeptidesCOVID
So what: The trust gap and opacity in healthcare are a structural demand driver for direct-to-consumer and gray-market channels — a force reshaping how drugs reach patients.
“healthcare is pretty much one of the worst consumer experiences we all have” ▶ 0:00
Can China REPLACE Nvidia? | China Decode45 min32 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
China Decode hosts dissect three China themes: Huawei's new 'Tao' scaling law as a workaround to Nvidia/Moore's-law limits and a fast-growing China AI-chip market; a brewing EU-China trade war where Chinese FDI and onshoring may blunt European tariffs; and Hong Kong overtaking Switzerland as the world's top offshore wealth hub on mainland capital flight.
China AI chips vs Nvidia: the Huawei 'Tao' scaling law · 10China chip-equity trade and capital markets · 4EU-China trade war and auto/chemicals displacement · 10RMB and currency outlook · 2Hong Kong overtakes Switzerland as wealth hub · 6
China AI chips vs Nvidia: the Huawei 'Tao' scaling law 10
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has stated his company has 'largely conceded' China's AI chip market to Huawei, which has captured roughly 50% of the Chinese AI chip market in 2026 so far.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highNvidiaJensen HuangHuaweiChina
So what: Direct admission of share loss caps Nvidia's addressable China revenue and validates Huawei as the domestic default; watch Nvidia China-segment guidance.
“Jensen Hang, the CEO of Nvidia, has said that his company has largely conceded, those are his words, largely conceded China's AI chip market to Huawei.” ▶ 0:03
China's AI chip market is estimated at about $21 billion in 2026 and, per Morgan Stanley, could reach about $67 billion by 2030.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumChinaMorgan StanleyAI chips
So what: A ~3x market expansion frames the prize Huawei is capturing and the revenue Nvidia is forgoing under export controls.
“the size of the AI chip market in China is supposed to be about 21 billion US this year, but by 2030, according to Morgan Stanley, it could be worth about 67 billion US.” ▶ 3:47
Huawei's chip chief He Tingbo ('the chip queen') has announced a 'Tao' scaling law that shifts chip performance gains away from transistor miniaturization toward data movement speed, chip stacking/clustering, wiring and memory architecture.
framework🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumHuaweiHe TingbosemiconductorsMoore's law
So what: If architecture-led scaling gains traction it lets China route around lithography sanctions, reducing dependence on EUV/leading-edge nodes and ASML-class tooling.
“chip companies in the future might stop obsessing so much as they do right now about how small the transistors in a semiconductor should be and they might focus more instead on how fast the data moves through a semiconductor.” ▶ 4:26
TSMC is reportedly hitting the upper limit of Moore's law and moving into 3D stacking, an approach conceptually similar to Huawei's 'Tao' efficiency philosophy.
causal🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumTSMCHuaweiMoore's law3D stacking
So what: Industry-wide pivot to advanced packaging/3D stacking elevates packaging and memory suppliers (HBM, CoWoS) over raw node shrink as the next compute lever.
“TSMC is understanding that it's hitting the upper limit of Mo's law and is now going into 3D stacking which is not too dissimilar I think from this towel philosophy” ▶ 7:22
Per Council on Foreign Relations research, the best US AI chips remain about 5x more powerful in compute than Huawei's, the gap is widening, and in the most bullish scenario Huawei can produce only ~4% of the aggregate AI compute Nvidia can.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumHuaweiNvidiaCouncil on Foreign RelationsAI chips
So what: Bear case on China self-sufficiency: a 5x per-chip gap plus tiny aggregate output means China likely can't meet surging domestic AI demand, leaving a structural supply hole.
“the best US AI chips are still five times more powerful and compute than what Huawei can currently do and apparently according to them that gap is widening” ▶ 8:06
Nvidia's Blackwell chips are considerably more powerful chip-for-chip than anything Huawei is close to producing, but China can plausibly stack and scale indigenous Huawei chips into a 'useful workaround.'
thesis🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumNvidiaBlackwellHuaweiChina
So what: Scaling cheaper, weaker indigenous chips in volume erodes the moat that per-chip superiority gives Nvidia/Blackwell in the China market.
“even if China doesn't get its hands on these Blackwell, it can stack these chips and scale these chips, the Huawei indigenous chips in such a way that it can probably have a useful workaround.” ▶ 10:45
China's cheap electricity advantage, including solar-powered data centers near the Gobi Desert, offsets the higher power draw of stacking and scaling weaker chips to run large language models.
causal🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumChinadata centerssolar power
So what: Energy cost as a competitive lever favors Chinese AI inference economics and undercuts a key US/EU data-center cost assumption.
“you can run data centers close to the Gobi Desert using solar power that provide incredibly uh cheap electricity and thereby overcome the fact that you're having to spend use more power” ▶ 11:11
Huawei is positioned as the symbol of China's resistance to the US export-control regime, having prevailed as the primary target of American sanctions to still develop competitive semiconductors.
thesis🗣 James Kingconviction: highHuaweiUS export controlssemiconductors
So what: Sanctions have hardened rather than killed Huawei, implying export controls accelerate Chinese self-sufficiency and a bifurcated chip world.
“Huawei is that it's the symbol of China's resistance to American pressure under the export control regime” ▶ 13:09
Jensen Huang was named to the board of Tsinghua University's School of Economics and Management, the same body on which Apple CEO Tim Cook sits, a possible signal that US-China tech floodgates may be opening softly.
anecdote🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: lowJensen HuangNvidiaTim CookAppleTsinghua University
So what: Out-of-consensus bullish read on Nvidia-in-China access; a thaw could reopen some China revenue currently written off.
“Jensen Huang just got announced that he is uh on the board to Chinua University's School of Economics and Management um on which uh the Apple CEO Tim Cook is also a member.” ▶ 9:15
Huawei is becoming 'the Samsung of China' — a vertically integrated technology leader spanning telecom, handsets and chip production with a resilient full supply chain across the stack.
framework🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumHuaweiSamsungChina
So what: A vertically integrated Huawei competes across multiple tech layers, broadening the set of Western firms (chips, handsets, telecom) facing Chinese substitution.
“Huawei is increasingly becoming the Samsung of China which is this global technological leader in the broader ecosystem” ▶ 12:46
China chip-equity trade and capital markets 4
A China semiconductor ETF is up at least 40% year-to-date, outpacing AI stocks and the Shanghai and Hong Kong composite indices, as investors treat domestic chips as a pick-and-shovel AI play.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: highsemiconductor ETFShanghai CompositeHang SengAI stocks
So what: Confirms a momentum trade in China chip names as the domestic AI-hardware proxy; the host explicitly flagged this rally as a prediction that played out.
“I was just looking at year-to- date performance on one of the ETFs and it's up at least 40% year to date. It's done outpaced the AI stocks” ▶ 14:00
CXMT, a large memory/DRAM supplier, is expected to come online this year, adding domestic Chinese memory capacity to the AI-hardware stack.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumCXMTDRAMmemory
So what: New domestic DRAM supply pressures global memory pricing and reduces China's reliance on Samsung/SK Hynix/Micron over time.
“we'll have CXMT which is a big memory DRAM supplier coming online probably this year” ▶ 14:30
MiniMax, a major Chinese AI model company, is planning a mainland Shanghai STAR Market listing in addition to Hong Kong, signaling momentum in the AI-hardware-and-models capital-raising cycle.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumMiniMaxShanghai STAR MarketHong KongAI models
So what: Dual onshore listings deepen domestic AI funding and create new China-listed AI exposure for investors locked out of US-listed names.
“Miniax which is a big AI model company is looking to list additionally in addition to Hong Kong also in in mainland China on the Shanghai store starboard” ▶ 14:37
Chinese chip foundries Hua Hong and SMIC face an open question of whether they can reach the aggregate output needed to meet rising domestic AI compute demand even as efficiency improves.
thesis🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumSMICHua HongfoundriesChina
So what: Capacity, not just design, is the binding constraint on China chip self-sufficiency; foundry capex and yield are the key metrics to track.
“can they reach the aggregate amount needed to meet Chinese demand?” ▶ 7:52
EU-China trade war and auto/chemicals displacement 10
China's trade surplus with Europe hit over $400 billion last year, the highest on record, concentrated in cars and machinery, with China making growing inroads into chemicals.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaEuropean Unionautoschemicalsmachinery
So what: A record and widening surplus is the trigger for EU tariff escalation; auto and chemicals are the most exposed European sectors.
“It just apparently hit about uh over $400 billion last year, the highest level on history and probably will increase again in 2026.” ▶ 16:35
A French-led coalition of five EU countries — France, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands and Lithuania — is urging Brussels to deploy stronger cross-sector tariffs and defensive trade tools against subsidized Chinese imports, with a G7 summit in France on June 15 expected to focus on reducing reliance on China.
prediction🗣 James Kingconviction: highFranceItalySpainNetherlandsLithuaniaEuropean CommissionG7
So what: Concrete near-term policy catalyst (June 15 G7) for new EU trade-defense measures; watch sectors named for tariffs.
“We've got a French-led coalition of five EU countries, that is France, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands, and Lithuania.” ▶ 19:29
Emmanuel Macron has called on the EU to copy US-style trade protection of strategic industries, saying 'China is literally killing a large part of European industry.'
anecdote🗣 James Kingconviction: highEmmanuel MacronEuropean UnionChina
So what: Top-level political cover for protectionism marks a regime shift in EU-China trade posture and raises tariff risk for Chinese exporters.
“China is literally killing a large part of European industry.” ▶ 18:05
EU trade defenses are likely to fail because Germany's biggest companies have China as their largest market and are lobbying to keep EU-China friction mild for fear of Chinese retaliation against their interests.
contrarian🗣 James Kingconviction: highGermanyChinaEuropean Union
So what: German corporate lobbying likely caps the severity of EU tariffs, limiting downside for Chinese exporters and upside for protected EU firms.
“many of Germany's biggest companies have China as their biggest market in the world. And those big German companies are lobbying the German government to ensure that a trade war or trade friction between the EU and China doesn't get too nasty” ▶ 21:41
Chinese FDI into Europe grew 57% year-on-year last year (per the Rhodium report), driven by M&A acquisitions of European firms and greenfield investment concentrated in automotive and battery/green-tech, in places like Hungary, France and Germany.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: highRhodium GroupChinaHungaryFranceGermanyautomotivebatteries
So what: Chinese onshoring via factories in the EU can neutralize tariffs (1980s-Japan playbook), reshaping who captures European auto/battery production.
≈ “FDI grew 57% yearonear. Um and a lot of that is actually driven by both M&A activity, so buying up European companies and green field investment” paraphrase
Despite over a year of EU tariffs on Chinese EVs, China continues to set record EV exports into Europe, and firms like BYD, Chery and CATL setting up European factories may render tariff barriers a 'nothing burger.'
contrarian🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumBYDCheryCATLEuropean UnionEVs
So what: Tariffs that fail to stop Chinese EV penetration imply continued share loss for BMW/Audi/VW and a structural threat to European autos.
“over a year ago, they did put restrictions on EVs with tariffs, tariff measures, but we still continue to see record Chinese exports of EVs into Europe.” ▶ 22:39
BMW and Audi are in 'dire straits' on their China outlook, and their shrinking domestic Chinese market share weakens their lobbying power against EU trade policy.
anecdote🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumBMWAudiChina
So what: Deteriorating China share for German premium automakers is both an earnings risk and a political signal that German auto lobbying may wane.
“it's d it's just dire straits and Audi too in terms of just their outlook in in China.” ▶ 24:35
China's likely counterpunch in a trade war would target European agriculture — French cognac, Spanish pork — plus weaponizing critical-minerals and rare-earth exports that Europe depends on.
prediction🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaFranceSpainrare earthscritical mineralsagriculture
So what: Identifies the specific EU export sectors and rare-earth supply chains exposed to Chinese retaliation; Europe lacks a near-term rare-earth onshoring response.
“China has other tools that it can use as well, such as exports of critical minerals and rare earths, which many European countries need.” ▶ 26:33
Per an OECD report, 60% of Chinese market gains are driven by subsidies, framing the EU's grievance over state-subsidized Chinese competition.
data_point🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumOECDChinasubsidies

“60% of Chinese um market gains are driven by subsidies. Uh this is based on their own calculation.” ▶ 43:54
The host predicts Europe will NOT have a full-blown trade war with China — only skirmishes — because German multinationals' interests will prevail in Brussels via lobbying.
prediction🗣 James Kingconviction: highEuropean UnionChinaGermany
So what: Base case of contained escalation argues against pricing in a severe EU-China trade shock for exporters and German autos.
“Europe will not have a full-blown trade war with China. There may be some skirmishes, but ultimately the interests of Germany's big multinationals that are in China and doing business will prevail.” ▶ 42:48
RMB and currency outlook 2
The host expects the CNY — up 3.4% year-to-date versus the dollar and against most trade-weighted peers — to give back appreciation gains and drift from ~6.7 toward the 7 region as China resists an export-unfriendly stronger currency.
prediction🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumCNYRMBUS dollarChina exports

“we're currently at uh 6.7. I think it might dip a little bit more before it starts to rebound uh in the second half of the year and move towards closer towards the seven uh region.” ▶ 44:39
A potential oil supply shock from events in Iran could drive global demand destruction and a real shock to China's export engine, undermining CNY strength.

Hong Kong overtakes Switzerland as wealth hub 6
Hong Kong overtook Switzerland as the world's top offshore wealth hub, ending 2025 with $2.95 trillion in cross-border AUM (up 10.7% year-on-year) per Boston Consulting Group, mostly mainland Chinese money.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highHong KongSwitzerlandBoston Consulting Groupcross-border AUM
So what: Structural inflow into Hong Kong supports its banks (HSBC, Standard Chartered), asset managers and exchange listings as the Asia wealth gateway.
“Hong Kong ended 2025 with $2.95 trillion US in crossborder assets under management” ▶ 33:22
BCG projects the Hong Kong–Switzerland gap will widen to nearly $600 billion by 2030, a durable long-run trend of mainland wealth flowing through Hong Kong.
prediction🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumHong KongSwitzerlandBoston Consulting Group
So what: A multi-year secular tailwind for Hong Kong financial infrastructure and a headwind for Swiss private banking AUM growth.
“by 2030 the gap between Hong Kong and Switzerland will widen to nearly 600 billion US.” ▶ 34:09
An estimated $2–5 trillion of mainland 5-year fixed-term deposits yielding ~5% are expiring this year while current term-deposit yields sit near 2%, pushing mainland savers to seek higher returns in Hong Kong asset managers, mutual and pension funds.
causal🗣 Alice (Ellis Hen)conviction: mediumChinaHong Kongfixed-term depositsasset managers
So what: A large reinvestment wave from low-yielding deposits is a structural inflow into Hong Kong wealth products and risk assets as real estate and onshore equities disappoint.
“fixedterm deposits ... yielding around 5%. Have about to expire this year. Current yields for a term deposit is 2% which is extremely low.” ▶ 32:03
Hong Kong's draw rests on the freely convertible Hong Kong dollar versus the capital-account-restricted RMB, letting mainland wealth convert and invest globally — driving accelerating mainland flows even after the 2020 National Security Law.
framework🗣 James Kingconviction: highHong Kong dollarRMBHong KongChina
So what: Currency convertibility is the structural moat behind Hong Kong's wealth-hub ascent; capital-flight motive persists regardless of political tightening.
“the Hong Kong dollar, unlike the rem is a freely convertible currency. You can convert it to any other currency in the world.” ▶ 34:48
Hong Kong now has the second-most billionaires of any city (71, behind only New York), with many being mainland Chinese tech tycoons — and CATL chairman Robin Zeng is the city's richest person.
