data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumHong KongNew YorkCATLRobin Zeng
So what: Concentration of mainland tech wealth in Hong Kong reinforces its listing pipeline and wealth-management demand; CATL's chairman as top tycoon ties the battery boom to HK capital.
“There are 71 billionaires in Hong Kong and that is second only to New York.” ▶ 36:35
The thesis holds that Beijing safeguards Hong Kong's economic freedoms even while circumscribing political ones, because China will not 'kill the golden goose' that hosts a thriving tech-listing market.
thesis🗣 James Kingconviction: highHong KongBeijingChina
So what: Reassures investors that political tightening hasn't impaired Hong Kong's capital-markets function — supportive for HK-listed equities and IPOs.
“your political freedoms are being circumscribed, but your economic freedoms are clearly being safeguarded because China doesn't want to kill the golden goose.” ▶ 41:24

Decypher Podcast 2 episode(s) · 61 insights

The Most Important Crypto Tech Nobody Understands ft. Rand Hindi from Zama46 min32 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rand Hindi (founder, Zama) argues that as finance moves on-chain, privacy via FHE on public chains—not private chains—is the inevitable encryption layer, the 'HTTPS for on-chain finance.' He frames real-world-asset tokenization (T-REX/Apex), B2B payments, and institutional trading as the demand drivers, positions FHE as already post-quantum, and makes a contrarian case that instant settlement should give way to latency as a security feature.
Public chains + FHE privacy vs. private chains · 4RWA tokenization as the demand driver · 4Institutional privacy use cases · 3Moats: integrations, network effects, AI-era defensibility · 5Programmable compliance via FHE · 2Quantum threat and post-quantum migration · 6Latency vs. instant settlement as security · 4Crypto maturing into on-chain finance · 4
Public chains + FHE privacy vs. private chains 4
Zama positions itself as a confidentiality/encryption layer added on top of public chains (Ethereum, Solana) rather than a private chain, on the thesis that if finance moves on-chain it must be private—analogous to HTTPS being added to the public internet.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamaFHEEthereumSolanaHTTPS
So what: Frames privacy as a non-optional layer of on-chain finance; watch FHE-on-public-chain as the structural winner over private-chain approaches.
“If we seriously think that finance is moving on chain... there is no world in which this cannot be private.” ▶ 4:42
Hindi argues private institutional chains (e.g. Canton) are the 'intranets' of crypto—useful for internal transactions but destined to be eclipsed by public chains with an encryption layer, just as intranets were eclipsed by the encrypted public internet.
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highCantonZamaEthereumprivate chains
So what: Bearish framing for permissioned/private-chain plays (Canton et al.) relative to public-chain privacy infrastructure; a directional bet for infra investors.
“if you're comparing Kanton, an internet with Zama on Ethereum, a public network with HTTPS” ▶ 8:44
Hindi claims Zama has never seen a single institutional customer evaluating any privacy technology other than Zama for public chains—the only competing approach institutions consider is private networks like Canton.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamaCantoninstitutions
So what: Implies a near-monopoly competitive moat in public-chain confidentiality if true; key claim to verify against ZK-privacy competitors.
“we have never seen a single customer that we spoke to considering another privacy technology for public chains than Zama” ▶ 6:34
Zama intends to be a horizontal, cross-chain privacy layer (live on Ethereum mainnet, expanding to multiple chains in 2026 including Solana and non-EVM ecosystems), aiming to make adding a new chain only a few days of work by next year.
prediction🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamaEthereumSolanaEVML1L2
So what: Cross-chain composability of shielded assets is the expansion vector; multi-chain rollout cadence in 2026 is a milestone to track.
RWA tokenization as the demand driver 4
Zama is the confidentiality layer for the T-REX protocol, launched by Apex (a ~$3 trillion asset management group), which Hindi says is targeting tokenizing $100 billion of assets within 12 months and going to mainnet with $100B TVL on day one.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highT-REXApexZamaRWAEthereum
So what: A single named pipeline ($100B RWA via T-REX/Apex) dwarfs current on-chain RWA; concrete catalyst to monitor for Zama adoption and RWA-infra theses.
“T-Rex is tokenizing a hundred billion dollar of assets in the next 12 months. So, T-Rex is going to production on mainet with a hundred billion dollar of TVL on day one.” ▶ 11:19
Hindi estimates Ethereum currently holds only ~$20 billion of RWA TVL, so capturing just 5% of T-REX's initial $100B and shielding it via Zama would make Zama the largest privacy protocol by dollars encrypted in crypto history—larger than Zcash.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highEthereumRWAZamaZcashT-REX
So what: Quantifies how small a capture rate flips Zama to category leadership; a low-threshold bull case and a yardstick (dollars-encrypted) for the privacy sector.
“if only 5% of that initial TL just 5% gets shielded with Zama it would make Zama a bigger privacy protocol by dollar encrypted than any privacy protocol in the history of crypto including Dcash” ▶ 11:52
Hindi argues the core value of RWA tokenization is composability—mixing tokenized assets, using one as collateral for another, recreating the entire derivatives market on-chain—which requires a shared public ledger with atomic settlement, something impossible if each RWA sits on a separate private chain.
causal🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highRWADeFiderivativespublic chainsatomic settlement
So what: Composability requirement is the structural reason RWAs migrate to public chains over private ones—central to the public-chain infra investment case.
“the whole point of RWAS is not just to trade the RWA is to actually compose them together to create derivative products” ▶ 10:23
Hindi contends RWA tokenization opens a pie 'orders of magnitude larger' than native DeFi TVL, reframing crypto from fighting over a fixed DeFi pie to capturing trillions of traditional-finance assets moving on-chain over the next four years.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highRWADeFiTVLtokenization
So what: Reframes the addressable market; supports overweighting RWA-rails/infra over zero-sum DeFi-native competition.
“trillions are moving on chain... like we had billions before” ▶ 12:19
Institutional privacy use cases 3
Zama identifies three high-demand privacy segments: (1) real-world assets (RWAs), (2) B2B payments, and (3) crypto-institutional finance (professional traders, liquid funds, VCs, exchanges).
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamaRWAB2B paymentsVCsexchanges
So what: A taxonomy for where on-chain privacy demand concentrates; useful map for sizing the privacy-infra TAM.
Hindi argues B2B payments—not retail payments—are the lucrative privacy use case: low-frequency but high-value (supplier payments in thousands, cross-border subsidiary balancing in millions), and companies do not want payroll or supplier payments leaked publicly.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highB2B paymentsstablecoinsZama
So what: Points investors toward B2B/enterprise stablecoin payment rails over high-volume retail payments as the value-capture layer.
“B2B payments, on the other hand, less frequent transactions, huge value” ▶ 13:25
Public on-chain wallets create signaling/front-running risk for large holders: a VC holding 1–3% of a token's supply gets front-run when moving to an exchange, forcing sales at lower prices—confidential tokens remove this signaling by hiding wallet identity and amounts.
causal🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highVCsconfidential tokensexchangesZama
So what: Confidential-token adoption could materially change large-holder execution and on-chain transparency analytics; relevant to trading-flow and surveillance-based strategies.
“The second the second you're moving those tokens to an exchange... the market has panicked, they frontr run you, the token price goes down” ▶ 14:44
Moats: integrations, network effects, AI-era defensibility 5
Zama's core focus for 2026 is integrations—custody solutions, wallets, on/off-ramps, stablecoins, DeFi protocols, and exchanges—because shielded assets have no retention unless holders can swap, earn yield, trade, and move them.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamacustodywalletsDeFiexchangesstablecoins
So what: Integration breadth (not the core tech) is the stated 2026 execution KPI and stickiness driver to track for Zama.
“in order to have retention of shielded dollars, you need to provide as much utility and integrations as possible” ▶ 16:47
Zama announced a partnership with Fireblocks (MPC custody-grade self-custody, used as an API by banks, market makers, and fintechs), letting all Fireblocks customers integrate confidential token flows—confidential payments, swaps, and earnings—as easily as standard ERC20 tokens.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highFireblocksZamaMPC custodyERC20market makers
So what: Distribution via Fireblocks' API reach is a named go-to-market lever; an integration milestone for the adoption thesis.
Hindi frames Zama's durable moats as (1) network effects of shielded value (encrypted dollars don't port to another privacy protocol), (2) integrations that are hard to replicate, and (3) 28 filed patents—arguing the marginal cost of building software is trending to zero so code itself is no moat.
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamanetwork effectspatentsAI coding
So what: In an AI-commoditized-software world, value-locked network effects + IP + integrations are the defensible moats—a lens for evaluating any infra protocol.
“If someone is encrypting a dollar in Zama, it doesn't work with another privacy protocol... that creates a network effect of money” ▶ 41:24
Hindi says he won't hire another engineer until his whole team has integrated AI coding agents (to find the gaps), but continues hiring aggressively in two areas: security (no compromise) and business development.
anecdote🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamaAI coding agentshiringsecurityBD
So what: A concrete signal of where human headcount value concentrates as AI commoditizes engineering—security and BD over generic dev roles.
“we're not hiring a single engineer until everybody has integrated AI so that we know where the gaps are on the engineering side” ▶ 43:02
Hindi argues that as AI drives the marginal cost of software toward zero, the winners in software and blockchain will be whoever has the most network effects and brand recognition, plus the human relationships behind enterprise deals that AI cannot replace.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highAInetwork effectsbrandenterprise sales
So what: Reorients competitive analysis of AI-era infra toward distribution, brand, and relationships rather than technical novelty.
“whoever has the most network effect and the most brand recognition is going to win” ▶ 42:18
Programmable compliance via FHE 2
Hindi argues FHE enables 'programmable compliance' superior to ZK: ZK forces a binary see-everything-or-nothing model, whereas with Zama a token issuer can define in the smart contract who can decrypt which encrypted data, tailoring compliance per asset and service.
contrarian🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highFHEZKZamacompliancesmart contracts
So what: A specific technical differentiation (FHE vs ZK) that Hindi claims is unique to Zama; a competitive edge claim to weigh in the privacy-tech stack.
“contrary to ZK that kind of forces you into this very binary you see everything or you see nothing kind of compliance world. FHE and Zama allows the service provider the token issuer to decide what rules and compliance they need” ▶ 22:16
Hindi positions Zama as a pragmatic, compliance-friendly privacy protocol explicitly for legitimate financial use cases—differentiating from earlier cypherpunk permissionless privacy projects that enabled bad actors—and treats configurable compliance as part of the product.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highZamacomplianceprivacy protocols
So what: Regulatory pragmatism is the wedge for institutional adoption and distinguishes Zama from sanctioned-risk privacy tools; relevant to regulatory-risk assessment.
“the Zama protocol is for legitimate financial use cases and we've made it clear that we'll take every step that we can to prevent people from... abusing the protocol” ▶ 21:29
Quantum threat and post-quantum migration 6
Hindi asserts FHE (and therefore Zama) is already quantum-resistant because it uses the same lattice-type cryptography standardized by the US government for post-quantum communications—so data encrypted on a public chain via Zama today cannot be decrypted even by future quantum computers.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highFHEZamapost-quantum cryptographyquantum computing
So what: Post-quantum readiness is positioned as a structural edge over ZK (most ZK is not post-quantum); a differentiator for long-horizon security buyers.
“fortunately FHE is already quantum resistant... the same type of cryptography that has been standardized by the American government for postquantum secure communications” ▶ 24:19
Hindi predicts that any L1 not offering post-quantum signatures within 18 months is 'dead'—not because quantum will break it that fast, but because institutions will price in the failure to react to threats and move away.
prediction🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highL1post-quantum signaturesinstitutions
So what: A concrete 18-month deadline by which to judge L1s on post-quantum roadmaps; a screening criterion for chain-level allocation.
“my conviction is if an L1 does not offer postquantum signatures in the next 18 months, they're dead” ▶ 25:45
Hindi argues the post-quantum migration is a governance problem, not a technical one: the technical solutions for post-quantum Bitcoin and Ethereum exist, but the hard question is what to do with un-migrated coins (burn, recycle as mining rewards, redistribute pro-rata) and whether Satoshi's coins move.
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highBitcoinEthereumpost-quantumSatoshigovernance
So what: Reframes quantum risk as a governance/coordination overhang for Bitcoin and Ethereum—watch governance decisions, not just tech, as the catalyst.
“The bottleneck is honestly it's a governance decision on what to do, what cut off date for losing your coins” ▶ 28:41
Hindi cites the estimate that ~one-third of all bitcoins (~6 million, ~$600 billion at $100k each) are lost to forgotten keys, making the post-quantum cut-off decision an existential governance threat for Bitcoin specifically.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: mediumBitcoinlost coinspost-quantum
So what: Sizes the lost-coin overhang that any Bitcoin post-quantum migration must resolve; a tail-risk variable for BTC holders.
“the estimate is about a third of bitcoins are lost because people lost their keys” ▶ 28:51
Hindi notes ERC20 tokens are often upgradable, so issuers can take action to migrate to post-quantum, contrasting with Bitcoin where lost coins cannot be recovered—making quantum less of an existential threat for upgradable token ecosystems than for Bitcoin.
causal🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: mediumERC20EthereumBitcoinpost-quantum
So what: Differentiates quantum risk across asset types; upgradable-token ecosystems have a migration escape hatch Bitcoin lacks.
Hindi says post-quantum readiness is now 'table stakes' in institutional conversations—if you don't check the box you're not even in the discussion—whereas many crypto-native OG founders still don't take quantum seriously.
data_point🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highinstitutionspost-quantumZama
So what: Signals an institutional procurement filter forming around post-quantum; a gating requirement for vendors selling to TradFi.
≈ “like table stakes you know if you don't take the postquantum readiness box you're not even in the conversation” paraphrase
Latency vs. instant settlement as security 4
Hindi makes the contrarian case that crypto's instant settlement is a security liability and the industry will trade instant settlement for latency: most hacks could have been mitigated if large transactions took hours to settle, giving humans time to react and revert.
contrarian🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highinstant settlementlatencyDeFi securityhacks
So what: If latency-as-security takes hold, it reshapes DeFi UX and risk design; a directional thesis against the instant-finality dogma.
“we've been kind of redpilled into thinking that instant settlement was necessary for crypto to survive... most of the hacks that happened could have been prevented with some kind of latency” ▶ 33:53
Hindi argues prevention of hacks is delusional (especially with AI) and the right security goal is mitigating impact—everybody gets hacked eventually, so the real question is how bad it is when it happens.
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highcybersecurityAIhacks
So what: A mitigation-over-prevention security philosophy that favors latency/reversibility designs; lens for evaluating protocol risk posture.
“Preventing a hack is delusional especially with AI today.” ▶ 33:41
Hindi proposes a market-based fix for latency: instead of instant settlement at the protocol level, let third parties run an instant-settlement market on top of delayed settlement (as bridges already do for optimistic rollups' 7–14 day withdrawal delays), charging a 1–5% fee to take the latency risk.
framework🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highoptimistic rollupsbridgessettlementDeFi
So what: Identifies a fee-bearing instant-settlement-as-a-service market layered on delayed settlement—an emerging design pattern and potential business model.
“let people actually create a market for instant settlement on top of delayed settlements” ▶ 37:51
Hindi argues encrypting all wallets via Zama would improve security by removing attacker targeting information (nobody can see which wallets hold more), though it cannot protect a single signing wallet from social-engineering hacks like the six-month Drift compromise.
causal🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: mediumZamaFHEDriftoperational security
So what: Privacy reduces attacker target-selection but not operational/social-engineering risk—useful nuance on what FHE does and doesn't secure.
Crypto maturing into on-chain finance 4
Hindi frames the current disconnect between institutional adoption and negative crypto-social sentiment as the industry maturing: now that the US is 'open for business,' crypto companies are being measured on the same KPIs as any company—utility, revenue, adoption, growth—with multiples of ~10–20x recurring revenue.
thesis🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highcrypto industryUS regulationrevenue multiples
So what: Signals a fundamentals-driven repricing of crypto equities/tokens on revenue multiples rather than narrative; relevant to valuation frameworks.
“it's uh 10 to 20x uh recurring revenue uh 100x if you're Elon Musk and you're promising data centers in space” ▶ 2:45
Hindi divides crypto businesses into three categories: (1) successful token businesses (e.g. Hyperliquid), (2) crypto-rails businesses that IPO'd or sit adjacent to TradFi (e.g. Coinbase, Circle), and (3) token-based businesses struggling with product-market fit or a token that never made sense.
framework🗣 host (Jack)conviction: mediumHyperliquidCoinbaseCircletokens
So what: A taxonomy for sorting crypto businesses by viability; useful screen for separating durable token models from broken ones.
Hindi predicts trillions of dollars will move on-chain within four years and that many beloved incumbent crypto protocols will become 'the Yahoos of crypto'—huge and loved today but irrelevant once the on-chain-finance era's equivalent of Google arrives.
prediction🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highcrypto protocolson-chain financeYahooGoogle
So what: A warning that current protocol brands may not survive the TradFi transition—argues against assuming incumbency durability in crypto allocations.
“a lot of the brands that we love, they're going to be the Yahoos of crypto” ▶ 45:19
Hindi argues crypto-native risk management is structurally weaker than TradFi's: in TradFi a 0.01% chance of losing funds equals trillions and is unacceptable, while crypto treats million-dollar tail risks casually—so institutions weight low-probability events like quantum far more heavily.
causal🗣 Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)conviction: highTradFicryptorisk managementquantum
So what: Explains why institutional and crypto-native priorities diverge on tail risk—predicts institutional demand for security/post-quantum features the crypto base undervalues.
“In TRFI, a 0.01% chance of losing your funds is trillions. Okay? In crypto, it's millions. The scale is just not the same.” ▶ 31:53
Upgrading the Perp Dex with Dango32 min29 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Larry (founder of Dango, a six-week-old perp DEX ranked ~25-30 by DefiLlama volume) breaks down why Hyperliquid won the perp-DEX wars (owned infrastructure + owned HLP liquidity), why prior-generation perp DEXs (dYdX, GMX, Perpetual Protocol, Synthetix) each failed, and Dango's differentiation bet: a unified cross-collateralized trading account that boosts capital efficiency for basis trades. He argues perps are a zero/negative-sum game and frames a multi-instrument 'house of finance' endgame.
Why Hyperliquid won the perp-DEX wars · 4Why prior-gen perp DEXs failed · 4Dango's differentiation bet · 6Market structure & competitive framing · 4DeFi cycle maturation thesis · 5Lean-team operating model · 3Founder background & survivor lessons · 3
Why Hyperliquid won the perp-DEX wars 4
Hyperliquid's edge over prior perp DEXs came from owning its own L1 infrastructure rather than building on Ethereum/Solana, enabling gasless trading, no per-transaction wallet pop-ups, and sub-second deposits from Arbitrum versus ~15-minute bridge waits elsewhere.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidEthereumSolanaArbitrum
So what: App-specific owned infrastructure is now the table-stakes architecture for a competitive perp DEX; protocols building on shared general-purpose chains carry a structural UX disadvantage.
“Hyperlid owns their own infrastructure. They didn't build their app on top of Ethereum or Salana or any other existing chain.” ▶ 7:31
Hyperliquid's HLP house liquidity pool let it list new pairs instantly while rivals needed an 8-10 week market-maker onboarding cycle, so it captured the Solana meme-coin boom by being the only venue to trade a new meme coin for ~2 months after launch.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidHLPSolanameme coinsmarket makers
So what: Owning protocol liquidity collapses time-to-list and is a durable moat during fast-rotating speculative manias; listing speed, not just UX, drove Hyperliquid's volume capture.
“a new meme coin spawns on Solana and for the next two months, Hyperlrid is the only place where you can trade it.” ▶ 9:26
Hyperliquid's explosive growth coincided with and was driven by the Solana meme-coin season, tying perp-DEX volume capture to the underlying spot speculation cycle.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidSolanameme coins
So what: Perp-DEX volume leadership is correlated to where new speculative assets first appear; watch listing latency as a leading indicator of which venue captures the next mania.
Today's leading perp exchanges have all converged on the Hyperliquid playbook of running their own (or off-) chain plus their own LP pools, meaning future competition will differentiate on product rather than on the liquidity/infrastructure layer.
thesis🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidperp DEXs
So what: The infrastructure-and-liquidity moat is being commoditized; the next round of perp-DEX alpha will be won on product features and UX, so handicap entrants on differentiation not plumbing.
Why prior-gen perp DEXs failed 4
dYdX faded because its founder retired and the team disengaged, failing to list a single new asset for over a year.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highdYdX
So what: Listing velocity is an operational health signal for a perp DEX; a stalled new-asset cadence flags a declining venue regardless of legacy brand.
“for DYDX that that's simple. The founder retired... they didn't list a single new asset for over a year.” ▶ 9:56
GMX could not scale because its maximum open interest is capped by the TVL of its LP pool, and a zero-slippage trading design was exploited in a hack that eroded trust.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highGMX
So what: LP-pool-bounded open interest is a hard ceiling on a perp DEX's addressable volume; pool-backed designs structurally cannot out-scale orderbook/house-liquidity models.
“the maximum amount of open interest they can support is the TVL of their LP pool. So they cannot scale faster than that.” ▶ 10:14
Perpetual Protocol's virtual AMM (vAMM) is a structurally less efficient marketplace because traders trade against a single synthetic maker rather than against each other.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: mediumPerpetual ProtocolvAMM
So what: vAMM-based perp designs are deprecated relative to orderbook/peer-to-peer matching; treat them as a losing architecture when assessing perp protocols.
Top-20 perp exchanges are largely interchangeable commodities at the interface level, with most indistinguishable once the logo is hidden.
framework🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highperp DEXs
So what: Commoditization of the trading product means brand identity and a distinct UX are the scarce differentiators; undifferentiated entrants have no moat.
“you cover the logo, I think there's a good chance you're not going to able to tell which one you're on.” ▶ 3:36
Dango's differentiation bet 6
Dango's core planned differentiator is a unified cross-collateralized trading account that nets spot, perp, prediction-market, and third-party on-chain positions under a single margin pool.
thesis🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangoprediction markets
So what: Cross-margin unification is Dango's product wedge against commoditized rivals; its traction depends on shipping this feature, which is not yet fully live.
“it unifies your spot trading and your per trading and your... prediction markets trading... they will collateralize all those positions under one one unified margin.” ▶ 5:46
Dango claims its unified account delivers ~2x capital efficiency for basis trades (long spot, short perp) because the system recognizes offsetting long/short exposure in the same asset and grants higher effective leverage at lower real risk.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangobasis trading
So what: Capital-efficiency gains target sophisticated basis/delta-neutral traders, a high-volume cohort; if real, it could pull professional flow away from siloed-account venues.
“if you're doing basis trading meaning your long spot and your short... per you can basically do 2x the capital efficiency compared to what you are able to do on pretty much every other dexes.” ▶ 6:07
Dango launched roughly six weeks before the interview and already ranks ~25-30 among perp DEXs by DefiLlama volume with only four trading pairs and no US-equities perps yet.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangoDefiLlama
So what: Early ranking traction with minimal product surface signals product-market resonance; expansion to more pairs and equity perps is the stated growth lever to watch.
“we are currently around 25 to 30 like ranking on D5 lama among other all other all the perexes.” ▶ 19:36
Dango's distinct visual identity and brand (named after a Japanese snack, echoing the DeFi-summer food-token meme of Sushi/Pickle/Yam) is treated as a deliberate top-of-funnel acquisition lever via Twitter profile clicks.
framework🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: mediumDangoTwitterSushiSwapPickle FinanceYam Finance
So what: In a commoditized space, brand/visual distinctiveness is a measurable user-acquisition channel; an undifferentiated brand is a competitive liability.
“first you need a very distinct visual identity” ▶ 20:23
Dango originated as a new smart-contract VM project (a competitor to EVM/SVM/Move) before pivoting in April 2024 to build an app, choosing perps for proven product-market fit with a beatable incumbent.
anecdote🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangoEVMSVMMove
So what: Dango brings custom-VM infrastructure expertise to its perp build, aligning with the owned-infrastructure thesis that beat prior-gen DEXs.
Dango initially planned a spot DEX with moderate-leverage margin (cheaper than perps) but pivoted to perps after research showed crypto users are risk-seeking and want 10-20x+ leverage, not 3x.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangoperps
So what: Demand skews to high-leverage perps over capital-efficient margin; product strategy should follow the risk-seeking majority, validating the perp focus.
“most of the users in this space are very risk-seeking like nobody wants to trade 3x like everybody wants to go 10x 20x.” ▶ 16:17
Market structure & competitive framing 4
Larry argues it is too early to declare a single perp-DEX winner even if Hyperliquid prevails, analogizing to CEXs where Binance dominates yet differentiated players survive by product or jurisdiction.
contrarian🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidBinanceDango
So what: Pushes back on the 'Hyperliquid is the single winner' consensus; implies room for multiple differentiated perp-DEX equity/token bets, not a winner-take-all market.
“even even if hyperlid it really is going to be the eventual winner, that's not going to mean there is no space for anybody else.” ▶ 1:14
Crypto historically overestimates first-mover advantage, illustrated by EtherFi succeeding in liquid restaking despite consensus that Lido had already won the staking category.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highEtherFiLidoliquid staking
So what: Incumbency is a weaker signal than assumed in fast-moving crypto verticals; late, differentiated entrants can still capture share, informing how to price 'winner' narratives.
“crypto historically over estimates kind of first mover advantage” ▶ 2:17
Differentiation in CEXs historically came from finance-side features, not UX: FTX and Bybit offered portfolio margin before Binance, and Bitget was first to launch RWA perps.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highFTXBybitBinanceBitgetRWA perpsportfolio margin
So what: Finance-side features (portfolio margin, RWA perps) are proven share-takers; perp DEXs that lead on margin mechanics and new instrument types can carve niches against larger incumbents.
≈ “FTX and uh by bit they they offer portfolio margin when when Binance didn't... Bitgat... were the very first in launching RWA perp” paraphrase ▶ 4:45
Hyperliquid is currently doing roughly 100x the volume of Dango, so Larry frames it as a model to emulate rather than an immediate competitive target, focusing instead on rivals ranked 20-30.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highHyperliquidDango
So what: Quantifies the volume gap (~100x) between the leader and a fast-rising entrant; the real competitive battleground for new entrants is the rank 20-30 cohort, not the leader.
“Hyperlid currently is doing like 100x more volume than us.” ▶ 2:47
DeFi cycle maturation thesis 5
Larry argues the experimental 2020-2021 DeFi era is not coming back because the industry has matured and ~99% of that era's experiments (GameFi, self-paying loans, SocialFi, DePIN) have been proven not to work.
contrarian🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highGameFiDePINSocialFiDeFi
So what: Frames most 2021-era narrative categories as failed; capital should concentrate on the proven use cases rather than chasing a return of the experimentation phase.
“99% of those stuff have been proven to not work. Game fi like... we know it doesn't work.” ▶ 22:24
Larry identifies exactly four proven crypto use cases that work: payments, exchange/trading, passive yield (savings), and stablecoins, with institutional capital now entering to capture them.
framework🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highpaymentsstablecoinsexchangesyield
So what: A tight investable map of durable crypto sectors; institutional inflows into these four areas are the structural tailwind, and ventures outside them face skepticism.
“There's payment, there's exchange, there's uh passive yield savings... there's a stable coin. So those are basically the four things we have proven to work.” ▶ 22:36
Barriers to entry in crypto have risen sharply since 2021: launching a credible project now requires raising eight figures, contracting market makers, and pursuing CEX listings and institutional (e.g. BlackRock) relationships, versus an anonymous solo Uniswap-pool launch before.
causal🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highUniswapBlackRockmarket makersCEXs
So what: Rising barriers favor well-capitalized, institutionally connected teams; the permissionless solo-builder edge has eroded, reshaping who can win new crypto markets.
“today, well, you have to raise eight figures. You have to contract market makers... You're better off having friends in in Black Rock” ▶ 18:18
Larry's long-term vision is for Dango to become 'the one place that houses all finance' (echoing Hyperliquid), expanding from perps into spot, prediction markets, and other instruments.
prediction🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: mediumDangoHyperliquidprediction markets
So what: Signals a multi-instrument super-app endgame for perp DEXs; the competitive frontier is breadth of tradable instruments under one margin account.
“to to become the h the one place that the house is all financed.” ▶ 25:24
Larry contends perp trading is a zero-sum (negative-sum after tax and fees) activity that produces no intrinsic value, contrasting it with finance that funds real production and risk-hedging.
thesis🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highperpsfinance
So what: A founder candidly framing his own category as negative-sum sets expectations that long-run value must come from enabling real-economy financing, not speculation volume.
“per exchange is is a zero sum game... actually a negative sum if you consider tax and fees and it it fundamentally does not contribute any value.” ▶ 25:47
Lean-team operating model 3
Dango operates with nine people and intends to cap headcount around ten, running a flat non-hierarchical structure where devs participate in product, design, and business.
data_point🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDango
So what: Demonstrates a top-25 perp DEX can be built by a sub-10-person team, signaling extreme capital efficiency and a counterpoint to the eight-figure-raise norm.
“our company has nine people and I I want to keep that not exceeding 10.” ▶ 24:44
Larry personally handles every tagged Discord support ticket and directs rapid shipping of small fixes, treating direct founder-user communication as the primary growth and retention engine.
framework🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDangoDiscord
So what: Founder-led, high-touch support plus aggressive iteration is the early-traction playbook for a resource-constrained DEX; a watchable proxy for product velocity.
“Right now I I am personally going into every discord ticket” ▶ 20:42
Larry warns founders that most advisors take significant token allocations and deliver nothing, and that most market makers will try to rip founders off, urging extreme care in deal negotiation.
anecdote🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highadvisorsmarket makers
So what: Flags token-allocation leakage to advisors and predatory market-maker deals as recurring value destroyers in crypto cap tables; a due-diligence red flag for token investors.
“most advisors are not worth it. They're going to take a lot of your tokens and do nothing. Uh most of the market makers are going to try to... rip you off.” ▶ 28:19
Founder background & survivor lessons 3
Larry quit a biochemistry PhD less than a year before completion to enter DeFi, taught himself to code, and was hired by VC firm Delphi after they noticed his open-source tooling for projects he was investing in.
anecdote🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highDelphiDeFi
So what: Context on founder pedigree and Delphi network ties; relevant for assessing execution credibility behind Dango's infrastructure-heavy approach.
Larry's Delphi-incubated projects on the Terra blockchain reached over $2 billion TVL at the Terra peak before being wiped out as collateral damage when Terra collapsed, and never regained momentum on other Cosmos chains.
anecdote🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: highTerraCosmosDelphi
So what: Firsthand survivor of the Terra collapse; the $2B-to-zero experience underpins his emphasis on owned infrastructure and user trust over fragile dependencies.
“We had more than $2 billion of TDL... at the at the peak of the Terara Hap. And then... Terara crashed and we became the collateral damage.” ▶ 13:18
Larry says he stays in blockchain because it has the lowest barrier to entry of any field (versus needing a business-school network for finance or a Stanford PhD and SF connections for AI), and because it is where he ranks in the top tier of ability.
framework🗣 Larry (guest, Dango founder)conviction: mediumblockchainAIfinance
So what: Articulates crypto's enduring meritocratic, low-barrier talent draw as a structural advantage for sourcing builder talent the space retains.
“blockchain still has the lowest barrier to entry for any new developer” ▶ 30:18

The Lawfare Podcast 5 episode(s) · 58 insights

Rational Security: The “Mosquitos and Heat and Sweaty and Eww” Edition74 min16 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Lawfare national-security roundtable on three fronts: Ukraine's drone/long-range-strike campaign degrading Russian logistics and oil infrastructure (with a 5-year-horizon read on whether sanctions plus attrition break Russia), an unresolved US-Iran conflict keeping oil ~$95 and threatening a near-term spike, and the politicization of US intelligence under a new acting DNI. Market-relevant signal is in defense-tech, energy, and sanctions/geopolitical-risk channels.
Ukraine drone warfare as a defense-tech disruptor · 4Strikes on Russian oil and logistics · 2Russia sanctions and the long-horizon attrition trade · 4Iran conflict and the oil-price spike risk · 4Politicization of US intelligence · 2
Ukraine drone warfare as a defense-tech disruptor 4
Ukraine has industrialized cheap interceptor drones and medium-range strike drones that are battle-tested and iterate far faster than expensive US/Western platforms, and Gulf Arab states are now turning to Ukraine rather than the US to learn counter-drone defense.
thesis🗣 Ari Tabatabai (Lawfare contributing editor, former government)conviction: highUkrainedrone warfarecounter-droneGulf statesdefense technologyUnited States
So what: Signals a structural shift in defense procurement toward cheap attritable drones and counter-UAS over legacy big-ticket platforms; watch drone/counter-UAS suppliers and the relative positioning of legacy prime contractors.
“partners have tended to kind of look to the United States as you know wanting these big sophisticated expensive platforms that take years and years to produce and deliver and expensive in maintenance. And by the way then they're using those same very expensive very sophisticated capabilities to shoot down these really cheap drones.” ▶ 19:40
Roughly 80% of casualties on the Ukraine front line are now caused by drones, making drones the decisive weapon system of the conflict rather than a supporting one.
data_point🗣 Nastia (Lawfare Ukraine fellow)conviction: mediumUkraineRussiadrones
So what: Quantifies the dominance of unmanned systems in modern attritional war, underpinning demand growth for both strike and counter-drone capability.
Iran originally supplied Shahed drones to Russia for use against Ukraine, and Russia is now sending modified Shaheds back to Iran for use in the Middle East conflict, making Ukrainian counter-drone know-how directly relevant to Gulf air defense.
causal🗣 Ari Tabatabaiconviction: mediumIranRussiaUkraineShahed dronesGulf statesBahrain
So what: Drone-tech proliferation links the Ukraine and Middle East theaters into one market for counter-UAS systems across the Gulf.
A Peter Thiel-linked think tank (Defense Analyses and Research Corporation) is funding a 'Dark Fellowship' sending young Americans to Ukraine to learn drone and counter-drone warfare, signaling private tech capital flowing into battlefield-derived defense R&D.
data_point🗣 guest (panelist)conviction: lowPeter ThielDefense Analyses and Research CorporationUkrainedrone warfare
So what: Tracks the venture/defense-tech crossover thesis; private capital is treating Ukraine as the proving ground for next-gen defense, relevant to defense-tech startup flows.
Strikes on Russian oil and logistics 2
Ukraine has sharply intensified long-range strikes on Russian oil facilities over recent months, and a new 'logistics lockdown' program is funneling more money into medium-range strike drones targeting Russian supply routes including the Crimea land bridge.
thesis🗣 Nastiaconviction: highUkraineRussiaoil refineriesCrimealogistics
So what: A sustained campaign against Russian refining/export capacity is a slow-burn upward pressure on global oil supply risk; watch Russian throughput and crude export disruptions.
Russia's Starlink frontline communications were cut off starting February 1 after SpaceX honored a Ukrainian request to deregister terminals, and Russia has no comparable analog, creating a hard-to-remedy battlefield comms gap.
data_point🗣 Nastiaconviction: highStarlinkSpaceXRussiaUkraine
So what: Illustrates Starlink's strategic chokehold value and the absence of a Russian/Chinese LEO-comms substitute at scale, relevant to the satellite-comms competitive landscape.
Russia sanctions and the long-horizon attrition trade 4
Western export controls have decimated Russia's incoming supply chain for defense-tech components, forcing heavy reliance on black-market technology transfers, but the cumulative economic damage operates on a 5-to-10-year horizon rather than forcing a near-term capitulation.
framework🗣 Scott R. Anderson (host)conviction: highRussiasanctionsexport controlsdefense industry
So what: Reframes sanctions as setting an outer time horizon in a war of attrition, not an immediate off-switch; tempers any near-term 'Russia collapse' positioning.
The Trump administration has eased pressure on Russia, including granting licenses for Russian oil exports over the past two months because of the Iran conflict, while Europe has continued tightening, opening a divergence in the sanctions regime.
data_point🗣 Scott R. Andersonconviction: mediumTrump administrationRussiaoil exportsEuropesanctions
So what: US-EU sanctions divergence and US oil-export licensing to Russia reduce near-term supply-side disruption from sanctions and complicate the Russian-crude discount picture.
Russia's budget is sustained almost entirely by energy exports and has enjoyed a windfall from the Iran-war-driven oil price spike, so a resolution of the Iran conflict that lowers oil prices would itself be a major source of economic pressure on Russia.
causal🗣 Scott R. Andersonconviction: highRussiaoil pricesIranenergy exports
So what: Links the Iran-conflict oil premium directly to Russian fiscal resilience; a de-escalation that drops oil would compound Russian budget stress, a cross-asset linkage worth watching.
Russia has not declared general mobilization even five years into the war, so it still has manpower options, and Putin's decision-making is not constrained by domestic economic pain in the way a rational Western actor's would be.
contrarian🗣 Nastiaconviction: highRussiaPutinmobilization
So what: Cautions against pricing a quick economically-driven Russian exit; geopolitical-risk premium on Russia/Ukraine exposure likely persists longer than economic models imply.
“even this latest reporting by Bloomberg right that the Russian central bank and sort of the top Russian economists are openly articulating to Putin that this is becoming unsustainable. I don't know how much this will actually have an effect on him.” ▶ 30:47
Iran conflict and the oil-price spike risk 4
US-Iran negotiations have collapsed and hostilities continue, with oil around $95/barrel and panelists citing talk of a possible ~50% further spike within weeks as global oil reserves approach being fully tapped.
prediction🗣 Scott R. Andersonconviction: mediumIranUnited Statesoil pricesStrait of Hormuz
So what: Flags a concrete near-term tail risk of a sharp oil spike from depleted reserves plus an unresolved Iran conflict; directly relevant to energy and inflation-sensitive positioning.
“Oil prices are supposed to climb dramatically beyond where they've been now. I think they're like 95ish uh dollars a barrel... people are talking about in the next few weeks oil prices going significantly higher than that like 50% higher than that.” ▶ 53:31
Iran's strongest leverage is maintaining the status quo squeeze on the Strait of Hormuz and an experimental 'tolling system' on transit, while widening attacks on Gulf states (e.g., the Kuwait airport strike) may backfire by legitimizing a military reopening of the strait.
causal🗣 Scott R. Anderson / Ari Tabatabaiconviction: mediumIranStrait of HormuzKuwaitGulf states
So what: The Strait of Hormuz remains the central oil-shipping chokepoint risk; escalation that spreads to Gulf targets raises the odds of a military intervention scenario that markets would price as either a relief or a further-spike catalyst.
Domestic and political pressure (unpopular war, primaries/midterm season, oil-price-driven affordability concerns) pushes Trump toward needing an Iran deal, but any achievable deal would offer Iran more sanctions relief for less than the JCPOA and leave much of Iran's capability intact.
thesis🗣 Ari Tabatabaiconviction: mediumTrump administrationIranJCPOAsanctionsmidterm elections
So what: If a 'more-for-less' deal lands, expect meaningful Iranian sanctions relief and added Iranian crude returning to market, a bearish oil catalyst, but political resistance makes timing highly uncertain.
Israel's continued aggressive campaign against Hezbollah, against US ceasefire pressure, is the key wild card capable of derailing any Iran de-escalation, with Israel preferring managed regional chaos while the US bears the global economic cost.
causal🗣 Scott R. Andersonconviction: mediumIsraelHezbollahIranNetanyahuTrump administration
So what: Divergent US-Israel strategic interests keep Middle East escalation risk live; an Israel-driven flare-up is the main scenario that sustains the oil-risk premium even if US-Iran talks resume.
Politicization of US intelligence 2
Trump's appointment of Bill Pulte (FHFA director, no national-security background) as acting DNI reflects a loyalty/spoils pattern and a downgraded ODNI, with the CIA reportedly elevated over the DNI in intelligence briefings.
thesis🗣 Tyler McBryan (Lawfare managing editor)conviction: mediumBill PulteDNIODNICIATrump administrationFHFA
So what: Signals continued institutional weakening of US intelligence coordination; lower-confidence policy intelligence raises the chance of geopolitical-risk mispricing and surprise.
Hyper-politicization and talent exodus at the intelligence community mean US-disclosed intelligence may no longer be trusted by allies the way it was in 2022, when public Russia intelligence helped build the sanctions coalition.
causal🗣 Ari Tabatabaiconviction: highintelligence communityDNIalliesRussia
So what: Erosion of allied trust in US intelligence weakens the West's ability to coordinate rapid coalition responses (e.g., sanctions), a structural drag on future geopolitical-crisis response.
“Now we're back to the Iraq war on crack where uh we have an intelligence community that is being hyperpoliticized and so next time we need our allies and partners and the international community to pay attention to what we're telling them. They may not believe us in the way that they did just four years ago.” ▶ 1:05:47
Lawfare Daily: Pope Leo XIV Takes on Silicon Valley with Christopher Hale and Renée DiResta52 min8 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Discussion of Pope Leo XIV's first AI-focused encyclical and its collision with Silicon Valley politics. Mostly ethics/theology, but contains real market signal: a Trump AI executive order on model auditing was killed at the last minute after a David Sacks phone call, Silicon Valley's public favorability has fallen ~25% (likened to Wall Street's 2003-2008 arc), and threats against data centers are rising — all relevant to the AI-regulation and big-tech backlash trade.
AI Regulation & the Killed Executive Order · 3Silicon Valley Backlash & Sentiment · 2Labor Displacement & UBI · 1Anthropic, the Vatican & AI Politics · 2
AI Regulation & the Killed Executive Order 3
A Trump administration executive order that would have imposed AI model auditing/disclosure requirements (a 'positive step toward AI regulation') was nixed at the last minute after AI czar David Sacks made a phone call, per Christopher Hale.
data_point🗣 Christopher Haleconviction: highDavid SacksTrump administrationAI regulationexecutive order
So what: Signals the federal regulatory environment for frontier AI labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) remains light-touch and that incumbent-aligned VCs retain veto power — bullish for unconstrained AI capex/deployment, bearish for near-term compliance-cost catalysts.
“David Saxs made a phone call um and this was going to be a very a very positive step towards AI regulation and it got it got nixed um at the last minute.” ▶ 31:16
The AI safety debate in the US was successfully reframed as a 'censorship' fight roughly two years ago, a framing that stuck and effectively shut down one side of the model-oversight conversation, according to Renée DiResta.
causal🗣 Renée DiResta (host)conviction: highAI safetyMarc Andreessencensorship
So what: Explains why US model-auditing/disclosure mandates keep failing — the political coalition for them has been neutralized, reducing regulatory risk to AI deployment in the near term.
Guardrails on AI systems can privilege the largest incumbents at the expense of smaller players, a 'semi-legitimate' critique that also serves the book of VCs like David Sacks and Marc Andreessen who oppose regulation that would hurt their portfolios.
framework🗣 Renée DiResta (host)conviction: mediumMarc AndreessenDavid Sacksventure capitalAI incumbents
So what: Watch for regulation framed as 'safety' that in practice raises moats for the largest AI labs — a regulatory-capture dynamic that would favor mega-cap incumbents over startups.
Silicon Valley Backlash & Sentiment 2
US public favorability of the term 'Silicon Valley' (as a concept, not specific products) has fallen ~25% over the past five years, putting it 'deeply underwater,' with the only comparable analog being Wall Street's reputation from 2003 to 2008, per polling Hale was shown.
data_point🗣 Christopher Haleconviction: highSilicon ValleyWall Streetpublic opinion
So what: A 2003-2008 Wall Street analogy implies rising political/regulatory backlash risk against big tech over a multi-year horizon — a headwind to watch for the sector's social license and future policy treatment.
≈ “the word Silicon Valley popular versus unpopular like versus dislike in the past five years that has gone down 25%” paraphrase
The volume of threats against physical AI infrastructure such as data centers is rising, with a DHS-linked report this week flagging an alarming increase in anti-tech extremist rhetoric, per DiResta.
data_point🗣 Renée DiResta (host)conviction: mediumdata centersDHSAI infrastructure
So what: Physical-security and siting risk for data center buildouts is a tail concern for AI-infrastructure and power/REIT plays as anti-tech sentiment escalates.
“volume of threats against things like data centers is is rising like that rhetoric is actually heating up” ▶ 42:24
Labor Displacement & UBI 1
A growing share of the public expects AI to be a serious near-term job killer with no government or Silicon Valley safety net, while VCs counter that AI will create abundance and new jobs — a divide with no concrete transition roadmap from the optimists.
thesis🗣 Renée DiResta (host)conviction: mediumAIlabor displacementUBIElon Musk
So what: The absence of any credible UBI/transition policy mechanism (DiResta: nobody can answer 'who does it, when, with what political capital') means displacement risk is likely to surface as political backlash rather than orderly policy — a slow-building overhang for AI-exposed labor sectors.
“I have no confidence whatsoever that that will actually happen” ▶ 41:55
Anthropic, the Vatican & AI Politics 2
Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah spoke at the Vatican encyclical event, and Pope Leo XIV reportedly found Anthropic's stand against the Pentagon over autonomous AI weapons 'courageous,' a recent history that drove the lab's elevated access to the Vatican.
anecdote🗣 Christopher Haleconviction: mediumAnthropicChris OlahPope Leo XIVPentagonautonomous weapons
So what: Illustrates Anthropic positioning itself as the 'ethical' AI lab and willing to publicly clash with the Pentagon on AI weapons — a brand/regulatory-posture differentiator versus competitors that could matter for enterprise and government trust.
“Pope Leo the 14th based on my reporting and my conversations found that anthropic stand up against the against the um Pentagon was quote courageous” ▶ 22:18
The Vatican structurally favors binding global/international regulatory bodies (with 'teeth') over national AI regulation, putting Pope Leo XIV's framework directly at odds with JD Vance's pro-innovation, anti-guardrail stance articulated at the Feb 2025 Paris AI summit.
framework🗣 Christopher Haleconviction: highVaticanJD VanceParis AI summitAI regulationUnited Nations
So what: Frames the durable fault line in AI governance: globalist binding rules vs. national innovation-first deregulation — the latter currently winning in the US, keeping near-term regulatory drag low for AI labs.
Lawfare Daily: Drone Wars in Ukraine45 min15 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
An American veteran serving as senior Western military advisor to Ukraine's 3rd Army Corps lays out a five-stage framework for drone warfare's evolution, arguing the front line has progressed from cheap ISR quadcopters to AI-autonomous targeting and that the next leap ('stage five') requires marrying Ukrainian drone ingenuity with a first-rate, well-resourced military-industrial base. The cost-curve asymmetry (a $70k Shahed vs. a $4M Patriot; a $500 FPV killing a $1M tank) is presented as the defining economic logic of modern war.
Drone cost-curve asymmetry · 3Five-stage drone-warfare framework · 3Commoditized supply chain & proliferation · 2Autonomy, AI targeting & named vendors · 3Ground robotics & logistics edge · 1Stage five & the military-industrial gap · 2Battlefield state & Russian attrition · 1
Drone cost-curve asymmetry 3
Modern drone warfare is defined by extreme munition-to-target cost asymmetry: a $500 FPV race drone destroys a $1M tank, and a ~$20,000 maritime drone can destroy a $20M ship.
data_point🗣 guest, 'Jackie' (US veteran, senior Western military advisor to Ukraine's 3rd Army Corps)conviction: highFPV dronesnaval dronesdefensetanks
So what: The collapsing cost of precision lethality is structurally bearish for legacy high-cost platforms (tanks, surface ships) and bullish for low-cost attritable drone makers; watch procurement budgets shift toward mass-cheap systems.
“The famous being the $500 race drone is going to destroy uh a $1 million tank” ▶ 24:44
Air-defense economics are inverted against drones: intercepting a ~$70,000 Iranian-designed Shahed with a ~$4M Patriot missile is a losing trade, making cheap interceptors a structural need.
causal🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highShahedPatriot missileair defenseinterceptor drones
So what: Persistent demand signal for low-cost counter-UAS / interceptor-drone systems; legacy expensive interceptors (Patriot-class) are economically unsustainable against drone swarms.
“It's 70 grand it's 70 grand and the Patriot's $4 million.” ▶ 38:29
A viable next-generation ('stage five') drone platform must cost roughly $150,000-$300,000 per shot, not $20M, because exceeding the cost curve defeats mass deployability.
framework🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highdefense procurementdronesmilitary-industrial base
So what: Sets an explicit price-point target for defense primes/startups building attritable autonomous systems; programs priced like exquisite legacy weapons will be uncompetitive at scale.
“You cannot make a stage five platform $20 million. It's not going to work.” ▶ 39:01
Five-stage drone-warfare framework 3
Drone warfare can be mapped as five stages: stage 0 (large Reaper/Predator platforms), 1 (democratized ISR via many small quadcopters), 2 (strapping lethal payloads onto drones for high-value targets), 3 (industrialized, unlimited cheap drones replacing artillery), and 4 (AI/software-driven autonomy and swarming).
framework🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highReaperPredatorFPV dronesartilleryAIautonomous weapons
So what: A reusable lens for assessing where any defense vendor or nation's drone capability sits; value is migrating from hardware (stage 3) to software/AI (stage 4+), redirecting investment toward autonomy software.
“stage three is about the hardware, stage four is about the software, about the AI models, about autonomous weapons and semi-autonomous weapons.” ▶ 27:59
The Ukraine front line is currently at roughly 'stage 4.4', with perhaps one to two years until stage four is exhausted, meaning self-guiding, self-targeting and swarming weapons are already in field use.
prediction🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: mediumautonomous dronesswarmingAI targetingUkraine
So what: Establishes a near-term (1-2 year) timeline for autonomous-weapons maturation, framing the investment window for AI-targeting and swarm-control software.
“where we're fighting in Ukraine today is actually we're something like stage 4.4, maybe. So, we've got maybe a year or two until we get to the end of stage four.” ▶ 28:07
The hard barrier in modern drone warfare is not hardware but command-and-control processing: an edge is won by software able to ingest ~70 simultaneous drone feeds rather than two Predator feeds, a capability Western militaries underestimate.
contrarian🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highcommand and controlC2 softwareISRdata processing
So what: Points investors toward C2 / battlefield-data-processing software (the real bottleneck) over commoditized drone airframes; flags a capability gap in Western militaries that vendors can fill.
“That's one of the secrets of to the Ukrainians that the Western militaries under underestimate is how well they're doing this command and control.” ▶ 23:53
Commoditized supply chain & proliferation 2
Stage-three drone warfare runs almost entirely on commercially available hardware sourced from consumer marketplaces like Temu and Amazon, so a non-state actor (e.g. a cartel) could assemble a full capability minus only explosives and know-how.
causal🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highTemuAmazonDJIconsumer electronicsnon-state actors
So what: Commoditized drone hardware means low barriers to entry and proliferation risk; the durable value/moat sits in energetics, autonomy software and integration, not the off-the-shelf airframe. Also flags coming counter-UAS / homeland-security demand.
“It's on Temu and Amazon. So, if you are, you know, a a cartel lord over in South America, you can actually buy a full stage three component commercially now.” ▶ 27:15
Chinese maker DJI's consumer 'wedding drones' (e.g. the Phantom and Mavic) became the foundational tactical ISR platform in Ukraine starting in 2014, multiplying ISR by dividing one ~$20M Predator into 70-100 cheap units.
data_point🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highDJIMavicDJI PhantomOrlan-10ChinaISR
So what: Underscores DJI's entrenched battlefield role and the strategic dependency/risk of Chinese-made drones in Western supply chains, a tailwind for domestic-drone reshoring policy and NDAA-compliant alternatives.
“you divide that into, you know, 70 or 100 uh Mavic DJI Mavics. So you you democratize or multiply your ISR by a lot of small platforms.” ▶ 20:47
Autonomy, AI targeting & named vendors 3
The most autonomous battlefield 'system' is targeting — rapidly identifying and actioning targets across many sensors and drones — the domain associated with Palantir and the disputed Anthropic defense use case, and the decisive capability in a potential Indo-Pacific/Taiwan conflict.
thesis🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highPalantirAnthropicAI targetingIndo-PacificTaiwansensor fusion
So what: Names targeting/sensor-fusion software (Palantir-style) as the high-value autonomy layer and frames a Taiwan scenario as the demand driver; a watch item for defense-AI exposure.
“the most autonomous system is targeting. That's what they're talking about with like Anthropic or that's what that's what Palantir does” ▶ 31:39
AI final-guidance interceptor drones such as the 'MiRops' are valuable specifically because air-interception decisions exceed human reaction time, and such systems are aiding Gulf-state defense and Ukraine.
data_point🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: mediumMiRopsinterceptor dronesAI guidanceGulf statescounter-UAS
So what: Concrete example of where AI autonomy is already mission-critical (terminal interception); counter-drone systems with onboard AI guidance are an active procurement category across multiple theaters.
“the MiRops interceptor drone, which has been pretty important uh to help defend the Gulf states” ▶ 28:45
Ukraine faces no internal debate over autonomous-lethality limits — unlike US development circles — because its high-intensity, civilian-sparse front line serves as an unconstrained test bed for fully autonomous targeting.
contrarian🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highautonomous weaponsUkraineAI ethicsdefense policy
So what: Ukraine's permissive operating environment accelerates autonomous-weapons fielding ahead of Western regulatory comfort, meaning capability and combat-proven data will emerge there first.
“There's no debate in Ukraine. It's like we need to leverage as much capability as much lethal capability as we possibly can.” ▶ 33:23
Ground robotics & logistics edge 1
The 3rd Army Corps pioneered ground robotic vehicles primarily for logistics and medevac, cutting casualties sharply because most losses occurred during exposed resupply and relief-in-place movements rather than static defense.
causal🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highunmanned ground vehiclesUGVslogisticsmedevac3rd Army Corps
So what: Ground-robotics logistics/medevac is an emerging, under-hyped category (vs. aerial drones) with demonstrated casualty-reduction ROI; a niche for UGV makers.
≈ “ground drones our ground drones were the obvious answer” paraphrase
Stage five & the military-industrial gap 2
The strategic opportunity ('stage five') is to combine Ukraine's combat-proven drone ingenuity with a first-rate, well-resourced industrial base, producing a ~$150k-$300k platform able to defeat 100-200 cheaper stage-three drones.
thesis🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highmilitary-industrial baseUnited Statesdefense primesdronesUkraine
So what: Lays out a concrete product spec and addressable opportunity for Western defense primes/startups: cheap, mass-producible, AI-enabled, force-multiplying platforms — the likely direction of future drone procurement.
“What if you applied the resourcing from the Americans phase zero to the capabilities that we've now built in Ukraine. That is what stage five looks like.” ▶ 37:40
Stage-five viability rests on three pillars — favorable cost curve, mass/deployability, and C2 processing bandwidth — all of which must hold simultaneously at scale.
framework🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highdefense procurementdronescommand and controlmanufacturing scale
So what: A three-pillar screen (cost, mass, C2) for evaluating which defense-drone vendors and programs are actually competitive versus headline-grabbing but unaffordable systems.
“number one cost curve... two is going to be mass and mass deployability... And third is going to be your processing bandwidth or or command and control C2.” ▶ 38:19
Battlefield state & Russian attrition 1
Russia exhausted its strategic defensive reserve over winter 2025-26 in a failed gamble to seize Sloviansk and Kramatorsk via force plus negotiation, leaving Russian forces in a notably weak position by spring 2026.
data_point🗣 guest, 'Jackie'conviction: highRussiaUkraineDonbasSlovianskKramatorskPokrovsk
So what: A shift toward Ukrainian battlefield optimism and Russian manpower depletion is a macro/geopolitical input for risk assets exposed to the war's trajectory (European defense, energy, Ukraine reconstruction).
“the Russians actually dumped the whole reserve over the winter. They just sent everybody.” ▶ 41:55
Scaling Laws: Governing the Frontier with Owen Larter of Google DeepMind43 min12 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Owen Larter, a Google DeepMind policy lead, argues frontier-AI governance is converging on a flexible, standards-based model anchored by industry self-regulation (Frontier Safety Frameworks, the Frontier Model Forum) rather than rigid statute. He discloses concrete adoption traction (900M Gemini users; a 5-country government-partnership program) and frames AI's commercial unlock as scientific breakthroughs plus mundane public-sector workflow automation.
Gemini commercial traction · 2Government-partnership go-to-market · 2AI for scientific breakthroughs · 2AI for cyber/bio defense · 1Self-regulatory governance regime · 3Adoption gap & emerging markets · 2
Gemini commercial traction 2
Google DeepMind reports 900 million Gemini users for a product that effectively did not exist roughly 18 months ago, evidence of rapid consumer scaling of Google's flagship AI assistant.
data_point🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindGeminiAlphabetGOOGL
So what: A 900M-user base supports the bull case that Alphabet is converting search/Android distribution into AI engagement, a counter to the narrative that OpenAI owns consumer AI; watch whether this monetizes.
“We do have 900 million Gemini users now for a product again that didn't exist sort of 18 months ago.” ▶ 18:22
Larter points to Gemini's multimodal reasoning (parsing charts, diagrams and graphics from documents) as the differentiating capability behind real enterprise/government deployments rather than chat alone.
thesis🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: mediumGeminiGoogle DeepMindAlphabet
So what: Multimodal document reasoning is the wedge into high-value enterprise/public-sector workflows; a feature axis to track when comparing Gemini vs. OpenAI/Anthropic for B2B share.
Government-partnership go-to-market 2
Google DeepMind launched a National Partnerships Program at the end of 2025 and is already working with five countries (US, UK, Singapore, India, South Korea) to put Frontier AI into public services.
data_point🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindAlphabetUnited StatesUnited KingdomSingaporeIndiaSouth Korea
So what: Sovereign/government AI is becoming a distinct revenue channel and a competitive moat via incumbency; a five-country head start could lock in public-sector lock-in ahead of rivals.
“we have five different countries around the world that we're partnering with now. US, UK, Singapore, um India and South Korea.” ▶ 21:15
A Gemini-based UK planning-application tool compresses approvals that 'often take months' down to hours by combining multimodal document reasoning with local planning law, illustrating the concrete public-sector productivity unlock DeepMind is selling.
anecdote🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: mediumGeminiGoogle DeepMindUnited Kingdom
So what: Quantified time-compression (months to hours) is the ROI story that drives government procurement budgets; a template DeepMind can replicate across its partner countries.
AI for scientific breakthroughs 2
Larter frames the largest economic value of AI as driving scientific breakthroughs, citing DeepMind's AlphaFold (open-sourced, millions of researchers) and newer tools 'co-scientist' and 'AlphaEvolve' that help generate and test hypotheses.
thesis🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindAlphaFoldco-scientistAlphaEvolveAlphabet
So what: Signals where DeepMind concentrates differentiation vs. pure LLM rivals; the science-acceleration narrative underpins long-horizon Alphabet AI optionality (drug discovery, materials).
Larter says AI systems including Google DeepMind's were used 'in the last few days' to solve decades-long mathematical challenges, evidence frontier capability is still improving rather than plateauing.
data_point🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindAlphabet
So what: Counters the 'scaling has stalled' bear thesis; continued capability gains support sustained capex and frontier-lab valuations.
AI for cyber/bio defense 1
Google DeepMind has an AI system ('CodeMender'/'Codeman') that identifies and patches software vulnerabilities, positioning AI as a defensive cybersecurity tool, not just a risk vector.
data_point🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindCodeMenderAlphabetcybersecurity
So what: AI-driven automated vulnerability patching is an emerging product category; a competitive and disruptive signal for the security-tooling sector and incumbents.
“we have an AI system called codemen for example which you know identifies and patches vulnerabilities” ▶ 41:47
Self-regulatory governance regime 3
The frontier-AI governance model is consolidating around industry self-regulation: every major lab now runs a Frontier Safety Framework (DeepMind on v3.1) and all coordinate through the Frontier Model Forum.
framework🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindFrontier Model ForumFrontier Safety FrameworkOpenAIAnthropicMicrosoftAmazonMeta
So what: If voluntary industry frameworks crowd out hard statute, regulatory-cost risk to the labs stays low and incumbents who write the standards gain an advantage; a tailwind for frontier-lab margins.
The Frontier Model Forum is a six-member club (Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta) led by Chris Meserole that publishes 20+ technical/issue briefs to formalize safety best practice into eventual standards.
data_point🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highFrontier Model ForumGoogle DeepMindOpenAIAnthropicMicrosoftAmazonMetaChris Meserole
So what: Maps the inner circle of frontier-AI incumbents; standards set by these six become barriers to entry for smaller labs and shape future compliance costs.
“Google Deep Mind, OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta led by Chris Mezerol” ▶ 28:58
Larter argues globally divergent AI regulation is in nobody's interest because AI products are built and exported across borders, signaling industry will push for harmonized, internationally coherent (and lighter) rules.
thesis🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: highISOFrontier Model ForumAI regulation
So what: Fragmented regimes (EU AI Act vs. US vs. China) are a cost/compliance headwind; watch ISO 42001 standardization as the convergence vehicle the labs prefer.
“It does not make sense and is not in anyone's interests to have wildly divergent regulatory frameworks.” ▶ 26:52
Adoption gap & emerging markets 2
Larter contends real AI adoption is far lower than headline reports because much usage is trivial (e.g., checking a game time), implying a large gap between claimed and meaningful enterprise penetration.
contrarian🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: mediumGeminiAI adoption
So what: If US adoption is overstated, the monetization runway is longer but also less proven; tempers near-term revenue expectations for AI-exposed names.
Larter reports AI optimism and eagerness to adopt are markedly higher in much of the rest of the world than in the US, with emphasis on 'meaningful access' in local languages via the India AI Impact Summit.
thesis🗣 guest, Owen Larter (Google DeepMind policy)conviction: mediumIndiaGeminiGoogle DeepMindglobal south
So what: Emerging markets, especially India, may be the faster-growth AI demand pool; localization (language/context) is the moat to watch for whoever captures it.
Lawfare Daily: Inside the Upheaval of the Second Trump Administration with Emily Bazelon39 min7 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
NYT journalist Emily Bazelon recaps her oral-history reporting on DOJ, FBI, and DHS under the second Trump administration, arguing the rule-of-law guardrails that held in Trump 1 have been removed in Trump 2 — including a ~$8B taxpayer-funded settlement fund created via the dropped Trump-vs-IRS suit, the weaponization of DOJ/FBI against perceived enemies, and a mass exodus of career staff. Investor density is low; the episode is qualitative institutional analysis, but it sketches a regime where federal legal/enforcement institutions are politicized and the federal district courts are the last reliable check.
DOJ politicization & a politically-controlled settlement fund · 2Federal district courts as the last institutional check · 1Immigration enforcement reallocating federal manpower · 2Institutional capacity destruction & brain drain · 2
DOJ politicization & a politically-controlled settlement fund 2
The second Trump DOJ, under Pam Bondi and acting AG Todd Blanche, agreed to drop a Trump lawsuit against the IRS in exchange for creating a roughly $8 billion fund to pay 'victims' of Biden-era prosecutions, plus an effective civil pardon shielding Trump, his family, and business from future tax investigation, per Bazelon.
data_point🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: highDepartment of JusticeIRSPam BondiTodd BlancheDonald Trump
So what: A ~$8B discretionary federal fund steered by political loyalty signals that DOJ enforcement and federal litigation outcomes can now be transactional — a tail risk for any company or individual exposed to politically-charged federal investigation or settlement.
“the you know, the the 1. almost $8 billion slush fund is like the epitome of all of this” ▶ 31:05
Bazelon frames Trump 2 DOJ as a tool the president can use to inflict damage on enemies or do favors for allies, a qualitative shift from Trump 1 where AG Bill Barr and career staff refused to weaponize the department and forced a back-off.
thesis🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: highDepartment of JusticeBill BarrDonald Trump
So what: A predictably politicized DOJ raises regulatory and litigation uncertainty for firms; outcomes correlate less with legal merits and more with political alignment, a factor to price into sectors with heavy federal-enforcement exposure.
Federal district courts as the last institutional check 1
Bazelon argues federal district courts have been the single strongest functioning check on the second Trump administration, with judges across appointing presidents largely insisting the government follow the law, but warns this protection erodes as the judiciary turns over and is reshaped by appointments.
framework🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: highfederal district courtsjudiciary
So what: Courts, not agencies, are currently the binding constraint on executive action — so litigation/injunction outcomes (not policy announcements) are the better real-time gauge of where federal policy actually lands; that buffer is described as durable short-term but not long-term.
“that is something that like we can rely on in the short, but not the long term” ▶ 37:18
Immigration enforcement reallocating federal manpower 2
Roughly a third of FBI resources have been redirected to immigration enforcement, with DOJ lawyers, Border Patrol, and even USCIS benefits staff pulled into enforcement and fraud work — a large-scale reallocation away from their normal functions, per Bazelon's sources.
data_point🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: mediumFBIICEBorder PatrolUSCISDepartment of Homeland Security
So what: A federal enforcement apparatus reoriented toward immigration means reduced bandwidth for corruption, fraud, and other investigations — relevant to the regulatory-enforcement backdrop for sectors that normally face FBI/DOJ scrutiny.
“Now, you know, a third of the resources of the FBI are going into enforcement” ▶ 16:43
Bazelon notes interior immigration enforcement has become deeply unpopular and a political liability for the administration, even as border security itself has been a political success.
causal🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: mediumICEimmigration enforcementTrump administration
So what: A wedge between popular border policy and unpopular interior enforcement is a political-risk variable for the administration's standing — a soft input for those tracking midterm and policy-durability scenarios.
Institutional capacity destruction & brain drain 2
About 70% of career attorneys in DOJ's Civil Rights Division have left, the FBI's elite public-corruption unit CR-15 was disbanded, and senior intelligence/analyst leadership was pushed out — a hollowing-out of institutional expertise that Bazelon says will be a giant task to repair.
data_point🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: highDOJ Civil Rights DivisionFBICR-15Kash Patel
So what: Loss of institutional capacity in federal legal/enforcement bodies implies slower, less predictable, more politically-directed enforcement for years — a structural shift in the regulatory environment rather than a short-term swing.
“70% of the lawyers had left, the career attorneys. It's probably higher at this point” ▶ 25:34
Bazelon argues the durable fix would require statutory (not norm-based) independence for DOJ criminal investigations from the White House, since norms collapse the moment an actor decides to ignore them.
framework🗣 Emily Bazelon (NYT journalist, guest)conviction: mediumDepartment of JusticeWhite Houserule of law
So what: Until any such statute exists, DOJ independence is contingent on the incumbent's restraint — a reminder that institutional guardrails investors assume are fixed are in fact discretionary.
“norms are really strong until like someone decides they don't care about them anymore” ▶ 29:35

Wealthion 2 episode(s) · 57 insights

Michael Howell: AI Will Fuel Inflation, And Markets Aren’t Ready43 min33 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
