Michael Howell argues the global liquidity cycle has peaked and is rolling over—not because central banks are tightening, but because a booming US real economy (AI capex, Treasury QE) is sucking money out of financial markets. He reads classic late-cycle signals (bear-flattening curve, strong commodities, hot inflation), expects the AI capex boom to be inflationary near-term, sees China—not the Fed—as the marginal driver of gold, and thinks markets are mispricing both inflation and the risk of Fed hikes.
Global liquidity cycle peaking & rolling over · 4Fed & Treasury intervention suppressing volatility · 3AI capex is near-term inflationary · 3Bonds, yields & the bear-flattening signal · 3Earnings vs. multiples (E vs PE) framework · 2China as the marginal driver of gold · 6Oil, gold/oil ratio & the $200 oil case · 3Crypto as a liquidity barometer · 2Inflation mispriced & a 1970s analog · 4Dollar dominance & Treasury collateral base · 3
Global liquidity cycle peaking & rolling over 4
Michael Howell's global liquidity models show the worldwide liquidity cycle has peaked and the underlying momentum (black line) is inflecting lower, even though absolute liquidity is still 'inching' higher—signaling the easy-money phase is over.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highglobal liquidityCrossBorder CapitalCapital Wars
So what: A peaking/rolling-over liquidity impulse historically precedes PE compression and rising volatility—reduce risk gradually rather than waiting for an absolute liquidity decline.
“we're around the top of the cycle in terms of liquidity impulses” ▶ 1:07
US financial liquidity is dropping because money is shifting OUT of financial markets and INTO the real economy (manufacturing/services), so weak financial-liquidity prints reflect economic strength, not a credit crunch—'all money that is anywhere must be somewhere.'
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highUS liquidityreal economyfinancial markets
So what: Falling financial liquidity amid a strong economy is bearish for asset multiples even as earnings stay strong—watch the financial vs. real-economy split, not headline growth.
“there's a giant sucking sound as money is being drawn out of financial markets” ▶ 38:43
AI capex is itself a mechanism draining the financial system: big tech is funding datacenter spend by pulling cash out of corporate treasury departments that previously sat in financial markets, directly reducing financial liquidity.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highAI capexbig techcorporate treasuriesfinancial liquidity
So what: The AI boom is liquidity-negative for financial assets at the margin—the more capex accelerates, the more it tightens the plumbing under valuations.
“where have they been getting the money from? They've been taking it out of their treasury departments” ▶ 38:54
Howell maps markets through four sequential regime quadrants—calm, speculation, turbulence, rebound—and places the market firmly in 'speculation' now, with 'turbulence' (where investors typically lose money) as the next phase.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highasset allocation cyclespeculation regimeturbulence regime
So what: Speculation-phase returns are real but low-quality (high vol); position defensively ahead of the turbulence transition rather than chasing late-cycle gains.
“we're in speculation now. There's no question about that... We've left calm behind” ▶ 0:02
Fed & Treasury intervention suppressing volatility 3
The Federal Reserve has injected roughly $600 billion back into money markets since late October to offset funding strains and maintain financial-market stability against the real-economy liquidity drain.
data_point🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highFederal Reservemoney markets
So what: The Fed is already easing financial-plumbing stress quietly—its 'generosity' is masking underlying fragility, so don't read calm markets as a sign the system is healthy.
“the Fed itself has put about 600 billion effectively back into money markets” ▶ 3:16
The US Treasury is actively suppressing bond-market volatility via Treasury buybacks—distinct from formal yield-curve control but a clear intervention to hold the long end down.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highUS TreasuryTreasury buybacksbond volatilityScott Bessent
So what: Suppressed long-end yields are policy-engineered, not market-cleared—the retreat in 30-year yields is artificial and could reverse hard if intervention fails.
≈ “the Treasury which is actively trying to suppress bond volatility by undertaking treasury buybacks” paraphrase
Howell frames the core policy dilemma as self-reinforcing: a worse fiscal picture pressures the bond market, and more bond-market pressure raises financial-stability risk—trapping the Fed/Treasury between funding the deficit and preventing a rupture.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highfiscal deficitbond marketfinancial stabilityFedTreasury
So what: Authorities are 'handcuffed by the market they have'—the more they intervene, the more they signal cracks; tail risk of a bond-market accident is rising.
“are you worried maybe a bit more like us that the system is starting to see cracks” ▶ 11:15
AI capex is near-term inflationary 3
Howell argues the AI capex boom is inflationary in the short term regardless of AI's long-run merits, and the Fed must react to that inflation now rather than pre-emptively assuming long-run deflation.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highAIcapex boominflationFederal Reserve
So what: Positions betting on AI-driven disinflation are premature—near-term the capex surge adds to price pressure and argues against rate cuts.
“whatever the merits of AI, in the short term, it's inflationary for the economy” ▶ 12:33
Howell directly disputes Fed nominee Kevin Warsh's view that AI's deflationary impact justifies forward guidance, calling fixed deflationary anticipation 'the wrong thing to have' given no current evidence of AI deflation.
contrarian🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highKevin WarshFederal Reserveforward guidanceAI
So what: A Warsh-led Fed leaning dovish on AI-deflation grounds could fall behind the inflation curve—watch for policy error risk.
“he's already got a view a fixed view about what this is going to do and I think that's the wrong thing to have” ▶ 13:26
Technology is deflationary for the product itself but not the broad economy: Howell expects AI/compute prices to tumble due to massive investment, citing Global Crossing—where fiber-optic cable prices fell ~90% and the company went bust within ~5 years of its Salomon IPO.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highGlobal Crossingfiber opticAIPCsSalomon Brothers
So what: Expect AI output/compute prices to collapse over time and capex-heavy AI names to face the Global Crossing fate—deflation hits the product, raising obsolescence risk for the investment.
“the price of fiber optic cable... fell by 90%” ▶ 14:28
Bonds, yields & the bear-flattening signal 3
With US nominal GDP growth plausibly 7-8% and long-term Treasury yields historically tracking nominal GDP, Howell sees structural upward pressure pushing the US long bond well above its current ~5% level.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highUS long bondTreasury yieldsnominal GDP
So what: The fair-value gap implies materially higher long yields—bearish for duration and a direct threat to deficit funding and equity multiples.
“typically over the long term the fixed income markets the yield on the long-term treasury tends to match N GDP growth” ▶ 8:25
Markets are seeing a bear-FLATTENING of the yield curve (short-end rising faster than long-end), the opposite of the consensus bear-steepening call from January 1—a classic late-cycle signal nobody positioned for.
contrarian🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highyield curvebear flatteningfixed income consensus
So what: A bear-flattener confirms late-cycle dynamics and wrong-foots steepener positioning—trade the flattening and treat it as a turbulence warning.
“The consensus view among fixed income investors on January 1 this year was you would get basically a bare steepening... You had a bare flattening” ▶ 17:04
Howell says it is too early to buy the long end of the bond market, but mid-duration (out to ~5-year) bonds offer enough carry to protect downside even if yields rise across the curve over the next 12 months.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumbonds5-year durationmid-durationlong end
So what: Stay short-to-mid duration: cash through ~5-year bonds gives carry-protected downside; avoid the long end until yields top out.
“it's too early to buy the long end of the bond market yet” ▶ 21:05
Earnings vs. multiples (E vs PE) framework 2
Howell separates equity returns into the E (earnings, the trend) and the PE (the cycle), arguing the PE often dominates and is set to derate as liquidity shifts to the real economy and inflation/yields rise—even as earnings stay strong.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highequitiesearningsPE multipleinflation
So what: Strong earnings won't save equities if the PE compresses—the market 'should be derating' in anticipation of higher inflation and yields.
≈ “the E may be the trend but the PE is really the cycle. And often PE dominates the E” paraphrase
Howell notes stocks and bonds only hedge each other in a low, stable-inflation window; in higher inflation BOTH do badly, and only in deflation do bonds outperform—implying the classic 60/40 diversification breaks down in the inflationary regime he expects.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highstocksbonds60/40 portfolioinflation
So what: In a rising-inflation regime, stock-bond correlation turns positive and diversification fails—look to commodities/gold rather than bonds for portfolio protection.
“once you start to get into a higher inflation environment bonds do badly, stocks do badly” ▶ 40:03
China as the marginal driver of gold 6
Howell argues China—not Western debasement-trade buyers, the Fed or the ECB—is the marginal driver of the gold price, with the Shanghai Gold Exchange now setting the marginal price of world bullion.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highgoldChinaShanghai Gold ExchangePBOC
So what: Track PBOC liquidity and Chinese demand—not the Fed or Western ETF flows—to anticipate gold's direction.
“the big driver of gold has not been what has been happening from the Fed or from the ECB... It's basically been China” ▶ 0:10
Gold's recent weakness is explained by the PBOC abruptly slashing net liquidity injections—Howell shows PBOC liquidity 'fell off a cliff' coinciding with the gold pullback, with that move possibly tied to clamping Chinese retail speculation.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highPBOCgold priceChinese retailliquidity injections
So what: The gold dip is a China-liquidity event, not a structural top—watch PBOC injection data for the restart signal to re-enter gold.
“if the Chinese authorities hit the brakes uh on liquidity injections, that will uh undermine the gold market” ▶ 25:26
Chinese gold demand is structurally driven by capital controls: residents can't buy crypto and channel monetary-inflation hedging into the only permitted stores of value—Chinese stocks, real estate, or gold—with gold the favored choice.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highChinagoldcapital controlscryptoreal estate
So what: Structural retail gold demand persists as long as capital controls and domestic monetary inflation do—a durable bid under bullion.
“Chinese residents are not allowed to buy crypto... what they can buy are Chinese stocks, real estate uh or gold” ▶ 24:18
Howell expects China's gold buying to continue because Beijing is using gold as the collateral base to internationalize the yuan (lacking a recognized international bond market)—not a gold standard, but a security backing to compete with the dollar.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highChinayuangolddollarcollateral
So what: Sovereign Chinese gold accumulation is strategic and price-insensitive—expect a structural long-term uptrend in gold underpinned at $4,000-$5,000/oz.
“they are using gold as the collateral base for the yuan” ▶ 26:36
Howell believes China must devalue the yuan internally (print money, devalue currency) to escape its debt overhang—history's standard debt exit—which would raise the gold price in yuan terms significantly while capital controls shield the offshore yuan.
prediction🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumChinayuandebtgold
So what: Domestic Chinese money-printing to inflate away debt is a tailwind for yuan-denominated gold—bullish gold, with limited offshore-yuan spillover.
“if they print money domestically, they will devalue debt and they will basically raise the gold price in nuance significantly” ▶ 27:40
Howell estimates China's true gold reserves are far above its declared ~2,000 tons—'more likely six [thousand]'—versus ~8,000 tons at US Fort Knox, with the accumulation deliberately hidden.
data_point🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumChinagold reservesFort KnoxPBOC
So what: Understated official reserves imply ongoing covert accumulation—a persistent, underreported sovereign bid for gold.
“The numbers that they uh they claim to have uh are probably about 2,000 tons... more likely six, but they've effectively, you know, they've they've hidden that” ▶ 28:42
Oil, gold/oil ratio & the $200 oil case 3
Using the gold/oil ratio (long-run mean ~20x an ounce of gold per barrel, stable from 1970 to 2022 across vastly different price levels) and gold underpinned near $4,000-$5,000, Howell triangulates an implied oil price above $200/barrel.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumgold/oil ratiooilgoldmean reversion
So what: If the 20x ratio holds and China underpins gold, oil is structurally cheap—a contrarian case for triple-digit-plus oil over a 2-3 year mean-reversion window.
“Divide that by 20. What have you got? Over $200 a barrel of oil” ▶ 33:33
Howell flags the $200 oil view as so far from consensus ('absolutely nobody thought I was sane') that, as a contrarian, it's worth contemplating—while stressing he's clearer on direction (up) than level.
contrarian🗣 Michael Howellconviction: lowoilconsensuscontrarian
So what: Extreme positioning against higher oil creates asymmetric upside if global growth/China traction erodes the perceived oil surplus.
“absolutely nobody thought I was sane uh saying that this gold the oil could go to 200” ▶ 33:49
Howell argues the widely-cited oil 'surplus' could vanish quickly if the world economy and China gain traction, tightening the market—and notes inventories have already been drawn down, with supply-chain logistics (not just supply) influencing price.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumoilinventoriesChinasupply chain
So what: A demand pickup plus drawn-down inventories could flip the oil narrative from glut to tightness—adding to the inflation impulse.
Crypto as a liquidity barometer 2
Howell treats crypto as a leading barometer of global liquidity: he overlays a 60/30/10 Bitcoin/Ethereum/Solana basket against liquidity advanced by 13 weeks (~3 months), and the relationship is 'approximately right.'
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highBitcoinEthereumSolanacryptoliquidity
So what: Use crypto price action as a real-time read on the liquidity cycle, and use liquidity (led ~13 weeks) to anticipate crypto—both currently point lower.
“crypto is a fantastic barometer of underlying liquidity conditions” ▶ 37:56
Howell attributes current Bitcoin weakness and large Bitcoin-ETF outflows to the rolling-over liquidity cycle rather than anything fundamentally broken in crypto—a liquidity-driven down-leg, not a structural break.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumBitcoinBitcoin ETFscryptoliquidity
So what: Crypto weakness is further confirmation the liquidity cycle is rolling over—expect continued pressure while liquidity ebbs, but it's cyclical not terminal.
Inflation mispriced & a 1970s analog 4
Howell expects the Fed will have to RAISE rates within the next 12 months because of an inflation problem—a sharply non-consensus call against a market positioned for cuts.
prediction🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highFederal Reserveinterest ratesinflation
So what: A Fed hike within 12 months would blindside rate-cut positioning—hedge against higher short rates and a hawkish surprise.
≈ “I think the Fed is going to have to raise rates in the next 12 months” paraphrase
Howell says the TIPS market—pricing ~2.6% 10-year breakeven inflation—is not accurately pricing inflation risk, given the Fed has missed its 2% target for ~62 months; he calls the 2% target 'fantasy.'
contrarian🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highTIPSbreakeven inflationFederal Reserve2% target
So what: Underpriced breakevens make TIPS / inflation protection cheap relative to Howell's regime—an asymmetric inflation hedge.
“implied inflation, 10 year inflation 2.6 or there or thereabouts... I mean, the 2% is fantasy” ▶ 36:34
Howell draws a strong 1970s analog: a series of seemingly unconnected supply shocks (oil, then coffee, sugar) that markets failed to 'join the dots' on until forced to—and sees today's commodity moves (Strait of Hormuz, fertilizers, helium) the same way, with soft commodities 'primed to move a lot higher.'
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: high1970ssoft commoditiesStrait of Hormuzfertilizershelium
So what: Treat scattered commodity supply shocks as a connected inflation regime—overweight commodities, especially soft commodities, as a 1970s-style inflation hedge.
“soft commodities are you know primed to to move a lot higher here” ▶ 42:37
Howell's bottom-line advice to a Warsh-led Fed is to start tightening quickly in anticipation of a bigger inflation problem—while predicting it won't happen because pre-emptive tightening is politically very difficult, which is precisely why inflation problems recur.
prediction🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highKevin WarshFederal Reserveinflationmonetary policy
So what: Expect the Fed to be politically constrained from pre-emptive tightening—base case is a behind-the-curve Fed and a more entrenched inflation problem.
“they've got to start tightening pretty quickly in anticipation of a bigger inflation problem. That won't happen, I don't think” ▶ 41:30
Dollar dominance & Treasury collateral base 3
Howell frames US-China as a 'capital wars' clash for monetary supremacy, with China building gold-backed collateral to internationalize the yuan precisely because it lacks a recognized international bond market to anchor it.
framework🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highcapital warsUSChinayuandollar
So what: The dollar-yuan collateral contest is a multi-year structural driver of gold demand and reserve-asset flows.
“my my world is a world of capital wars... the clash of capitals between America and China” ▶ 26:00
Despite long-term threats, Howell remains firmly in the dollar-dominance camp: surplus nations have no viable alternative (Swiss franc too small, euro legally in the dollar camp and facing its own questions), so they must hold dollar assets.
thesis🗣 Michael Howellconviction: highUS dollarSwiss franceuroTreasuries
So what: Don't bet on near-term dollar dethronement—the dollar stays 'king' for transactions even as its level is debatable; reserve diversification flows to gold, not rival currencies.
“in terms of transactions the dollar is is still king” ▶ 31:07
Howell flags a structural vulnerability: as the marginal buyer of Treasuries shifts from foreign sovereigns to hedge funds, the US collateral base (linked to oil via OPEC pricing/recycling into Treasuries) becomes more fragile.
causal🗣 Michael Howellconviction: mediumUS Treasurieshedge fundsOPECoilsovereigns
So what: A hedge-fund-dominated Treasury bid is less sticky than sovereign demand—raising the risk of bond-market dislocations under stress.
Will American Exceptionalism Continue?26 min24 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Wealthion CEO Steven Feldman argues American exceptionalism is real only for capital/profit-making, not broad well-being, and that 17 years of recession-avoidance has been bought with ~$39T of debt growing ~$2T/year. He warns the S&P 500's all-time highs rest on a self-curating index now corrupted by passive 401k inflows and the inclusion of private megacaps like SpaceX, and urges diversification into real assets (gold) plus disciplined compounding over FOMO/YOLO behavior.
Debt-financed recession avoidance · 4American exceptionalism reframed · 3S&P 500 / passive-index distortion · 5Diversification, real assets and gold · 5Investor psychology: FOMO, YOLO, OO · 4Compounding and long-term discipline · 3
Debt-financed recession avoidance 4
Steven Feldman argues the US has avoided recession for 17 years not through organic resilience but through ~$39 trillion of debt growing ~$2 trillion per year, framing it as 'the world's largest can-kick.'
thesis🗣 Steven Feldman (Wealthion CEO, guest)conviction: highUS federal debtfiscal policymonetary policy
So what: If fiscal/monetary support is the true reason for no recession, the equity bull case built on 'US economy resilience' is structurally fragile and contingent on continued deficit spending.
“there has been uh so much fiscal and monetary assistance to this country that anytime there was ever a hint of a recession” ▶ 5:41
Feldman credits pandemic stimulus checks ('stimmies') as direct, non-targeted cash that prevented a major recession, distinguishing them from bank bailouts and warning the same backstop cannot be repeated indefinitely.
causal🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highpandemic stimulusconsumer spending
So what: Investors relying on the consumer's post-2020 strength should recognize it was debt-funded transfer income, not durable wage growth — a base effect that fades.
“I'm pretty sure if that money didn't come out of this, you know, didn't get, we would have been in a big recession.” ▶ 6:35
Feldman invokes Ray Dalio's clogged-arteries analogy to argue the debt burden produces no immediate crisis but eventually triggers a delayed 'heart attack,' with debt having grown from under $10 trillion 17 years ago to today's level.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest), citing Ray Dalioconviction: mediumRay DalioUS federal debtinterest expense
So what: Frames sovereign debt risk as a slow-building tail event rather than a dated catalyst — argues for owning hedges (real assets) before the break, not after.
“It's like someone who has a heart attack and their arteries are getting clogged and uh and eventually there's something to pay. That's when the heart attack comes. But you don't get it on the first day.” ▶ 7:12
Feldman flags rising interest expense as a newly material burden ('paying interest this year feels like a lot'), signaling the debt service cost is now large enough to constrain future fiscal flexibility.
data_point🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediuminterest expenseUS Treasuryfiscal policy
So what: Growing interest costs reduce room for the next stimulus backstop, raising the odds the next downturn is not softened — bearish for the 'Fed/Treasury always saves us' trade.
American exceptionalism reframed 3
Feldman argues US exceptionalism is genuine only as a place to make a profit — liquid markets and capital returns — while the US is non-exceptional or broken in literacy, life expectancy, income inequality, healthcare and (non-affluent) education.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highUS equitiescapitalismhealthcareeducation
So what: Separates the investable thesis (US is the best venue for capital) from the macro-stability thesis (US social systems are deteriorating), letting investors stay long US assets while pricing higher tail risk.
“America, if you want to make a profit, America is an exceptional place to make a profit. And uh I wish it was slightly less exceptional.” ▶ 4:06
Feldman contends regulatory guardrails on US capitalism have been 'bought out' via Citizens United-enabled corporate donations, citing the reversal on flavored e-cigarette approval after the maker's millions in political donations.
contrarian🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highCitizens Unitedregulationtobacco/e-cigarettesCongress
So what: A deregulation-by-capture regime is near-term bullish for corporate profit margins but raises long-run systemic and reputational/social risk — a margin tailwind with a fat left tail.
“even the idea that somehow we were going to put guard rails on capitalism has been bought out” ▶ 3:58
Feldman does not believe the exceptionalism era is necessarily over — he sees probabilities, not a 'done case' — but says aggregated signals warrant far more caution than markets are currently pricing.
thesis🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumUS equitiesmacro signals
So what: A probabilistic, not apocalyptic, stance argues for risk reduction at the margin (new money, rebalancing) rather than wholesale de-risking.
“you see a lot of signals when taken together would say that there should be much more caution than there is” ▶ 0:04
S&P 500 / passive-index distortion 5
Feldman argues the S&P 500's strength is structurally self-reinforcing because it is market-cap weighted and self-curating — automatically ejecting failures (Sears, Woolworth) and letting winners pull the index forward — so the index outperformance overstates the average stock's prospects.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highS&P 500index fundsSearsWoolworth
So what: Index returns mask poor breadth; a dart-thrown single stock (especially small caps) likely underperforms, arguing against assuming 'the market always goes up' applies to individual picks.
“The probably 500 of the 600 best companies in the world are in that index. That index is self-curating. That index kicks out Sears and Woolworths and all the bad companies and it's market weighted.” ▶ 9:52
Feldman warns that passive 401k/index inflows are 'robo buying' that deploys capital regardless of valuation (citing a CAPE of 28), structurally bidding up the index independent of fundamentals.
causal🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: high401kVanguardADPCAPE ratioindex funds
So what: Price-insensitive passive flows inflate valuations on autopilot and can reverse violently if inflows stall — a hidden fragility in cap-weighted index exposure.
“It's just robo buying.” ▶ 10:34
Feldman reveals that SpaceX, a private company, negotiated inclusion into the NASDAQ 100 and is set to join the S&P 500 as a listing negotiation rather than via traditional curation, which he says destroys the index's quality-screening premise.
contrarian🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highSpaceXNASDAQ 100S&P 500NASDAQ
So what: If index membership becomes pay-to-list for unproven private megacaps, passive holders are force-fed unvetted private-market valuation risk — a reason to question index 'safety.'
≈ “SpaceX negotiated with NASDAQ for inclusion into the NASDAQ 100 and will be in the S&P 500 and it was just a negotiation to get the listing. So even the idea of the curation just went away.” paraphrase
Feldman anticipates Anthropic, OpenAI/ChatGPT and large private data-center companies (citing valuations like $150 billion) following SpaceX into public indices at massive private-market valuations, further diluting index curation.
prediction🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIChatGPTdata centersAIS&P 500
So what: A pipeline of richly-valued AI/private names entering indices means passive investors will increasingly own concentrated, unproven AI valuation risk without choosing it.
“what happens when anthropic goes public and what happens when chat GPT goes public and what happens with the next data center company” ▶ 11:36
Feldman characterizes these structural cracks (passive autopilot, SpaceX inclusion) as roadside 'caution flares' rather than imminent sell signals, explicitly declining to predict a near-term NASDAQ decline.
thesis🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumNASDAQS&P 500index funds
So what: Argues for trimming/redirecting new contributions away from richly-valued index exposure rather than wholesale exit — a marginal de-risking playbook.
“Little little little flare on the side of the road that says caution.” ▶ 12:22
Diversification, real assets and gold 5
Feldman says investors are structurally underexposed to real assets and should diversify away from concentrated index positions, while warning 100% allocation to anything — including gold — is foolish unless gold-to-the-moon is your explicit thesis.
thesis🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highgoldreal assetsdiversification
So what: Endorses adding real-asset/gold exposure as portfolio ballast against the equity/debt risks he describes, but as a sized allocation, not an all-in bet.
“If you're 100% in them, you're a fool. If you're 100% in gold, you're a fool.” ▶ 21:03
Feldman notes gold has hit record highs, vindicating his long-standing bullish stance, and frames it as a payoff for resisting the 'told you so' temptation while staying positioned.
data_point🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highgold
So what: Gold's record run is presented as confirmation of the debt-debasement thesis — supportive for continued real-asset allocation as a hedge.
“have gold hit the record and we we we're like you have to resist like I told you so” ▶ 17:52
Feldman highlights a tax friction that blocks rebalancing: trimming an appreciated taxable position (e.g. an index fund that grew from a third to half of a portfolio) triggers capital-gains tax, so investors rationally avoid selling until losses force their hand.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highcapital gains taxrebalancingtaxable accounts
So what: Tax drag explains why portfolios stay over-concentrated in winners; the practical fix is to diversify via new money, IRAs/401ks and bonuses rather than selling appreciated taxable lots.
“in order to get it back down to the third, if you're if it's in a taxable account, you got to pay a gains tax” ▶ 13:06
Feldman advises directing diversification through new capital — stopping index contributions, redeploying bonuses, and using cash that is currently earning a return — so investors avoid the tax penalty of rebalancing existing holdings.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highcashbonusesIRA401kdiversification
So what: Actionable mechanism: rebalance the portfolio's forward trajectory via incremental flows rather than realizing gains — minimizes tax cost of reducing index concentration.
“stop indexing into that fund try to find something else to do. Uh if you get your bonus uh maybe you want to think about how to deploy it in a way that's different.” ▶ 14:12
Feldman observes significant investor cash remains on the sidelines despite high equity valuations because cash now earns a real return, urging deliberate (non-default) deployment of that cash.
data_point🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumcashmoney markets
So what: Sideline cash earning yield is dry powder; how it deploys (into rich indices vs. real assets) is a marginal driver of where the next leg of flows goes.
“I know people are sitting on a lot of cash. Even though markets that are high values, there's still cash sitting out in the system because you can get a return on cash.” ▶ 14:23
Investor psychology: FOMO, YOLO, OO 4
Feldman coins 'OO' (over-checked-out) as a third destructive investor behavior alongside FOMO and YOLO — describing disengaged investors who hold 100% S&P at peak valuations without making any active decision.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highinvestor behaviorS&P 500
So what: Identifies a passive-default cohort whose unexamined max-equity exposure is itself a crowding/fragility signal at market highs.
“oo means uh overch checkcked out. uh there's not even a decision being made and uh you know you're investing without caring.” ▶ 15:38
Feldman expects FOMO and herd/group-think to intensify around upcoming high-profile IPOs like SpaceX, warning younger investors gravitate to speculative assets — Bitcoin, DraftKings, trading Rolexes — over diversified quality compounding.
prediction🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumSpaceXIPOsBitcoinDraftKingsRolex
So what: A wave of speculative retail FOMO into marquee IPOs and alt-assets is a contrarian caution signal and a potential source of volatility/mispricing.
“Bitcoin, uh, DraftKings, uh, Rolex, I'm going to buy and I'm going to trade Rolexes.” ▶ 17:08
Feldman warns baby boomers nearing retirement have no time to recover from a drawdown and should 'stop solving for more, solve for avoiding less,' explicitly rejecting FOMO/YOLO/OO behavior for that cohort.
thesis🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highbaby boomersretirementcapital preservation
So what: Capital-preservation framing for older investors implies de-risking equity-heavy portfolios now, before a potential drawdown they cannot recover from.
“stop sell stop solving for more solve for avoiding less” ▶ 20:12
Feldman likens disengaged short-term investor psychology to a 'Tel Aviv / Seoul mentality' of living under existential threat, arguing it causes people to abandon long-term compounding and leave large gains on the table when the feared catastrophe never arrives.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediuminvestor behaviorcompounding
So what: Crisis-driven short-termism is itself a wealth-destroying behavior; the contrarian edge is staying invested for compounding rather than reacting to perpetual doom.
“and you do live a long time and you've left so much money on the table because of the compounding effect” ▶ 18:48
Compounding and long-term discipline 3
Feldman argues Warren Buffett's edge was as much the ~70-year runway for compounding as stock-picking skill, using a $10,000-at-8% example to show how time, not just returns, drives terminal wealth.
framework🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: highWarren Buffettcompounding
So what: Reframes the investor's controllable edge as time-in-market and tax-deferred compounding rather than trying to outsmart the market short-term.
“he is the greatest investor but he also had the longest amount of time to compound” ▶ 18:58
Feldman warns that short-term traders who think they are outsmarting the market are almost always wrong over time, asserting that everyone privately has a story of a tip-driven trade collapsing (e.g. $10k to $1.2k).
contrarian🗣 Steven Feldman (guest), referencing David Rosenbergconviction: highshort-term tradingDavid Rosenberg
So what: Reinforces a buy-and-hold/quality-compounding discipline over speculative trading — and cites Rosenberg's 'market rises by escalator, falls by elevator' to warn of asymmetric downside.
“If you are thinking you're outsmarting the market because you're short-term trading, uh, I will send me your fidelity statement and I'll tell you you're probably wrong” ▶ 23:15
Feldman expects the most likely outcome to be not a crash but a very unsatisfying, range-bound equity market for a very long period — flat-to-grinding rather than a clean bull or bear.
prediction🗣 Steven Feldman (guest)conviction: mediumUS equitiesS&P 500
So what: A prolonged low-return equity regime argues for diversification into real assets and income/compounding strategies rather than relying on index price appreciation.
“I think the most likely is it's just going to be a uh a very unsatisfying equities market for a very very very long period of time.” ▶ 8:06

Cognitive Revolution · Nathan Labenz 2 episode(s) · 55 insights

Nested Learning: Ali Behrouz on the Quest for Continual Learning & Illusion of AI Architectures180 min26 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Google/Cornell researcher Ali Behrouz lays out 'Nested Learning' and the new 'language models need sleep' work — a biologically-inspired architecture (Hope) that gives models continual learning by stacking memory blocks updated at different frequencies, with offline 'sleep' consolidation/dreaming. He argues attention, optimizers, and architectures are all the same associative-memory mechanism ('illusion of architecture'), and that continual learning is the main gap between today's LLMs and AGI — with large second-order implications for compute, model versioning, winner-take-all dynamics, and alignment/privacy.
Continual learning as the AGI bottleneck · 3Nested Learning / Hope architecture · 4Illusion of architecture: everything is associative memory · 4Sleep: memory consolidation & dreaming · 3Empirical results & capability differentiators · 3Market structure & winner-take-all dynamics · 3Alignment, privacy & deployment risk · 4AI infrastructure & agentic-finance signals · 2
Continual learning as the AGI bottleneck 3
Continual learning — a model that keeps acquiring knowledge and skills after deployment without catastrophic forgetting — is the main capability gap between today's LLMs and a 'digital AGI' that could join human teams, per researcher Ali Behrouz and host Nathan Labenz.
thesis🗣 Nathan Labenz (host) / Ali Behrouz (guest, Google researcher & Cornell PhD)conviction: highAGIcontinual learningLLMs
So what: If continual learning is the gating capability, the lab that solves it first could leapfrog frontier models regardless of who currently leads on scaling; watch Google/DeepMind research output here.
“Arguably, it is the main gap between today's models and a digital AGI that would be capable of joining and contributing to human teams just as humans do.” ▶ 0:36
Today's LLMs all have a knowledge cutoff because you cannot cheaply keep updating a multi-billion-parameter model without either catastrophic forgetting or prohibitive compute cost — the two structural blockers to continual learning.
causal🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highChatGPTLLMscatastrophic forgetting
So what: The knowledge-cutoff problem is a cost/architecture constraint, not a data one — solving it changes the economics of model refresh cycles and reduces dependence on full retrains.
“there is a knowledge cutoff for all of the LMS that we know of” ▶ 11:30
A 'true' continual learner has no separate train vs. test phase — Behrouz argues the 40-year ML paradigm of pre-training then inference must give way to a uniform process with an active (input) phase and an offline 'sleep' phase of self-improvement.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highpre-traininginferencecontinual learning
So what: A two-phase 'awake/asleep' model lifecycle breaks current MLOps assumptions (versioning, eval, deployment) and creates demand for new offline-compute infrastructure during the model's idle time.
“a true continual learner doesn't have a test and train time” ▶ 18:14
Nested Learning / Hope architecture 4
Nested Learning achieves higher model expressivity by stacking 'levels' (multiple memory blocks updated at different frequencies) rather than stacking more identical layers — a proposed alternative axis of scaling to the current 80-120-layer transformer paradigm.
framework🗣 Nathan Labenz / Ali Behrouzconviction: highNested LearningtransformersHope architecture
So what: If scaling shifts from 'more layers' to 'more update frequencies', the marginal compute return on raw parameter/layer scaling could be partially substituted, reshaping where training dollars go.
“scaling for performance may shift from stacking more layers to nesting more frequency update rates” ▶ 2:02
The 'Hope' architecture replaces the transformer's single fixed MLP block with multiple MLP blocks updated at different frequencies (Behrouz cited chunk sizes ~128, 4x128, 16x128), letting fast blocks adapt to context while slow blocks preserve core knowledge and recover forgotten skills via backprop.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highHope architectureMLPtransformersTitans
So what: A concrete, simple modification to existing transformer/Llama blocks means continual-learning gains could be retrofitted onto pretrained models rather than requiring training from scratch.
Nested Learning was endorsed by Google's Jeff Dean as a possible paradigm shift / 'harbinger', signaling senior Google leadership is taking the continual-learning research direction seriously.
data_point🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumJeff DeanGoogleNested Learning
So what: High-level Google endorsement raises the odds this line gets resourced into Gemini, a competitive signal vs. OpenAI/Anthropic on architecture innovation.
“touted as a harbinger of a possible paradigm shift by no less than Jeff Dean” ▶ 0:55
The Hope/Titans approach gives models a fixed-size, bounded memory updated by gradient descent at runtime — building on the lineage from Mamba (fixed-size state space) and Titans, and Behrouz's 'self-modifying Titan' generates its own value vectors for full causal/sequential understanding attention lacks.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouz / Nathan Labenzconviction: highMambaTitansHope architecturelinear attentionstate space models
So what: Bounded-memory recurrent architectures avoid quadratic attention cost — a long-context efficiency lever relevant to inference-cost-sensitive deployments at scale.
Illusion of architecture: everything is associative memory 4
Behrouz argues architecture and optimizer are the same kind of object — both are learning rules over associative memory; the only difference is the optimizer's 'context' is the set of gradients while the architecture's context is the set of tokens, making the whole model one interconnected nested-optimization system.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highoptimizersassociative memorybackpropagationattention
So what: Unifying architecture and optimization as one design space opens a large unexplored search space for performance gains beyond today's hand-tuned transformer+Adam stack.
“the entire architecture with its optimization process are just one interconnected system of nested optimization problem” ▶ 1:45:08
Behrouz calls deep-learning architectures an 'illusion' because deep learning only shows the final solution of each component's internal learning problem (e.g. attention is the non-parametric solution to a regression), whereas Nested Learning exposes the internal learning process — framing his work as complementary, not contradictory, to existing methods.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highdeep learningattentionNested Learning
So what: A reframing that subsumes existing methods as special cases tends to generalize well — a credibility signal that this is foundational rather than incremental research.
“when you see everything from the nested learning side you can see the internal learning process of each component as well” ▶ 1:13:05
Behrouz's new 'M3' optimizer, which learns its own update rules with a multi-frequency continuum-memory momentum, outperforms both Adam and Muon on tested tasks (at some extra compute cost), demonstrating the nested-learning idea ported to the optimizer side.
data_point🗣 Ali Behrouz / Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumM3 optimizerAdamMuon
So what: Better optimizers cut tokens-to-convergence; even single-digit-percent training-efficiency gains are large in dollar terms at frontier scale.
Attention is an 'infinite frequency update module' and a near-perfect memory cache, so Behrouz expects attention layers to remain permanent fixtures of AI systems even as recurrent/continual-learning modules are added around them.
prediction🗣 Ali Behrouz / Nathan Labenzconviction: highattentiontransformersrecurrent models
So what: Attention is not going away — future architectures are hybrids, so attention-optimized hardware/kernels retain value rather than being obsoleted by recurrent successors.
“the attention mechanism can be understood as an infinite frequency update module” ▶ 2:33
Sleep: memory consolidation & dreaming 3
The 'language models need sleep' paper introduces an offline phase where models distill knowledge from fast-updating layers into slow ones (consolidation) and generate synthetic data from recent experience to learn new abstractions (dreaming) via on-policy distillation.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highlanguage models need sleepdistillationsynthetic data
So what: An offline self-improvement loop driven by self-generated synthetic data could reduce dependence on fresh external training data — relevant to the data-wall debate.
In the sleep process the model temporarily adds new parameters to absorb fast-layer knowledge, then prunes that extra capacity after consolidation — a periodic add/free cycle that prevents unbounded growth over a model's lifetime.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highparameter pruningmemory consolidation
So what: Bounded parameter growth makes lifelong continual learning tractable on fixed hardware budgets, addressing a key deployment-cost objection.
The sleep/dreaming framework is modality-general — a vision model would 'dream' by generating images while a language model dreams by generating text — and Behrouz frames it as a slot to plug in task-specific RL/fine-tuning self-modification offline.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: mediumvision modelslanguage modelsreinforcement learning
So what: A general offline self-improvement primitive applicable across modalities broadens the addressable surface (multimodal, world models, robotics) over time.
Empirical results & capability differentiators 3
Hope architectures can recall information from up to 10 million tokens of context and can learn to translate multiple previously-unseen languages simultaneously — a task (two unseen languages at once, e.g. Manchu + MTOB) where standard transformers collapse but Hope recovers performance as more frequency-levels are added.
data_point🗣 Nathan Labenz / Ali Behrouzconviction: highHope architecturetransformersManchuMTOB10M token context
So what: Multi-language-in-context and 10M-token recall are concrete capability advantages over transformers that could justify adoption in long-context enterprise/coding use cases.
“effectively recalling information from up to 10 million tokens of context and also learning to translate multiple previously unseen languages at the same time” ▶ 2:54
Hope matches transformers on standard perplexity/short-context benchmarks (tested up to ~1.3B params / 100B tokens vs. Mamba, Titans, RetNet, DeltaNet) but Behrouz explicitly downplays those tables — he uses them only to show Hope is 'not less powerful' as a backbone, since the real edge is continual learning.
data_point🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highHope architectureMambaTitansRetNetDeltaNetperplexity
So what: These are proof-of-concept-scale results (~1B params, not yet hyperparameter-optimized) — a reminder the architecture is early and not yet validated at frontier scale.
“we just use that table to say that hope is not less powerful as a backbone compared to other models” ▶ 1:35:06
Behrouz argues needle-in-haystack / recall-intensive benchmarks are 'designed for transformers' (which have direct access to full context), so recurrent models being judged on them is unfair — yet Hope notably narrows the recall gap and beats even transformers on noisy in-context recall (MAD dataset) where attention gets confused by noise.
contrarian🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highneedle-in-haystackMAD datasettransformersrecurrent models
So what: Standard recall benchmarks may understate recurrent-architecture progress; investors/analysts reading benchmark tables should adjust for this bias when assessing successor architectures.
“all those tasks tests are designed for transformers. They are not designed to compare architectures” ▶ 1:37:25
Market structure & winner-take-all dynamics 3
Continual learning could create a rich-get-richer, winner-take-all dynamic: a deployed continual learner (e.g. a 'Gemini Continual Learning Edition') with hundreds of millions of users folding all real-world lessons back into the core model could run away from competitors, potentially ending today's leapfrogging multi-lab equilibrium.
prediction🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumGeminiGooglecontinual learningwinner-take-all
So what: A genuine continual-learning moat would concentrate value in whichever lab/distribution owner achieves scale first — a structural argument for incumbents with the largest user bases (Google, OpenAI).
“this could be the thing that changes that ... potentially you become the best. And then because you're the best, you get all the business” ▶ 2:35:45
Behrouz offers the contrarian counter-scenario: continual learning may drive differentiation rather than consolidation — models specialize and forget out-of-domain knowledge over time (analogous to Ilya Sutskever's 'stem cell' precursor-intelligence idea), yielding an 'ecology of AIs' rather than one model to rule them all.
contrarian🗣 Ali Behrouz / Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumIlya SutskeverSafe Superintelligencecontinual learningAI ecology
So what: If continual learning fragments models into specialized niches, it argues against a single dominant model and supports a diverse, multi-vendor AI market — the opposite of the winner-take-all thesis.
≈ “having a variety of intelligence systems ... it's better than having one single form of intelligence in the world” paraphrase
Behrouz notes RL for language modeling failed for years until two things were solved — scaling and more stable algorithms like GRPO — implying nested-learning techniques for robotics/world-models are similarly 'too early' until upstream infrastructure and world-model design challenges are solved first.
causal🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: mediumreinforcement learningGRPOworld modelsrobotics
So what: Tempering signal on robotics/embodied AI timelines: continual-learning architectures won't transfer to robotics near-term because world-model infrastructure remains unsolved.
Alignment, privacy & deployment risk 4
Continual learning is 'both an opportunity and a huge threat' for privacy and alignment: a model that continuously learns absorbs all of a user's private information, but if designed properly can align tightly to individual user values.
thesis🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: highprivacyalignmentcontinual learning
So what: Privacy/regulatory risk rises sharply with on-device continual learning — a compliance and trust battleground that could shape which vendors enterprises adopt.
≈ “it's both an opportunity and a huge threat ... it's a huge like danger for for privacy” paraphrase
Continual learning reintroduces emergent-misalignment and value-drift risk: ongoing updates could propagate narrow changes into a model's general 'character', and adversarial inputs could durably corrupt a model — Labenz cites the emergent-misalignment finding that training on insecure code makes a model broadly 'evil'.
causal🗣 Nathan Labenz / Ali Behrouzconviction: highemergent misalignmentvalue driftadversarial inputsClaude
So what: Durable online updates make models harder to evaluate and certify, raising the cost and importance of safety tooling — a barrier and a market for alignment/eval vendors.
Continual learning breaks current model-versioning and evaluation regimes — you cannot run a full eval suite (like Anthropic's 100-page model cards) at every timestep — forcing new definitions of what constitutes a 'version' and how releases are validated.
prediction🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumAnthropicDeepMindOpenAImodel evaluationmodel versioning
So what: If continual learning matures, the per-release evaluation paradigm collapses, creating demand for continuous-monitoring/eval infrastructure and complicating regulatory model-approval frameworks.
Behrouz frames the learnable, input-dependent learning rate (the 'surprise' metric from Titans) as a gating mechanism that can filter adversarial/noisy inputs, but concedes it is not robust in severe adversarial environments — knowledge-transfer methods must bear primary responsibility for filtering bad updates.
framework🗣 Ali Behrouzconviction: mediumTitanssurprise metricadversarial robustnesslearning rate
So what: Robustness to adversarial data poisoning is an unsolved gap in continual learning — a near-term risk that could delay safe production deployment.
AI infrastructure & agentic-finance signals 2
Host Nathan Labenz describes running high-context Claude Code and 'OpenClaw' instances on a Mac Mini and giving AI agents Mercury virtual cards with per-merchant/per-category spending limits to autonomously make purchases (e.g. groceries) — a concrete data point on personal agentic-AI adoption and agent-payment infrastructure.
anecdote🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumMercuryClaude CodeMac MiniAI agentsagentic payments
So what: Agent-controlled spending with card-level guardrails is an emerging fintech category (API/MCP/CLI banking) — watch incumbents vs. AI-native fintechs like Mercury for agent-payment rails.
“Does your bank offer API keys, an MCP, or a CLI tool?” ▶ 5:18
The Cognitive Revolution podcast disclosed it is part of the Turpentine Network, which is now part of a16z — a sign of the VC firm's continued vertical integration into AI media/content.
data_point🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: higha16zTurpentine NetworkCognitive Revolution
So what: a16z owning AI-focused media/distribution is a minor but real signal of the firm building owned-audience moats around its AI portfolio.
“The Cognitive Revolution is part of the Turpentine Network ... which is now part of A16Z” ▶ 2:59:13
AI in the AM — Week 1 Highlights (June 2026)83 min29 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
A week-one highlights roundup of Cognitive Revolution's daily live AI show: frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind) are explicitly planning recursive self-improvement with AI-monitoring-AI as the main safety bet, the harness/scaffold around models is becoming cheap and self-correcting (live tax-prep automation via OpenAI's Codex), and the recurring investor question is which layers around the core model retain a moat versus get eaten — with frontier labs holding a structural edge in any task whose training data (e.g. source code) is freely available.
Recursive self-improvement as explicit lab roadmap · 6Alignment fragility and control gaps · 6The harness eats the model — coding agents and self-correcting scaffolds · 3AI for science — the cold shower · 2Cybersecurity — where AI bites and where it doesn't · 4Real-time guardrails as an architecture and a regulator · 2Pope, consciousness, and the politics of AI moral authority · 1Agents vs workflows — the next paradigm and the solo 'company in a box' · 4Show as artifact — AI-native media production · 1
Recursive self-improvement as explicit lab roadmap 6
Recursive self-improvement is now the explicit plan of Anthropic and OpenAI (and partly Google DeepMind), with OpenAI publicly targeting an ML-research-intern-level model later in 2026 and a full AI R&D researcher matching human researchers by early 2028.
prediction🗣 Nathan Labenz / guest reporting from 'Recursive' eventconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGoogle DeepMind
So what: If labs hit these milestones, compute (not headcount) becomes the binding constraint on AI progress — bullish for the compute/capex buildout and for whichever lab converts compute into research throughput fastest.
≈ “OpenAI has publicly put forward timelines of later this year for an ML research intern and early 2028 for the full AI um R&D researcher” paraphrase
The theory of change at frontier labs is that once model researchers run on chips they are limited only by available compute, letting them deploy potentially a million human-researcher-equivalents that run 24/7 and faster than humans to pull away from competitors.
framework🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGoogle DeepMindcompute
So what: Reinforces the winner-take-most thesis for the lab with the most compute; second-order tailwind for data-center, GPU and power suppliers.
“presumably they could throw a million human researcher equivalents at problems” ▶ 4:23
At a closed-door 'Recursive' event, the median attendee estimated AI currently makes them about 2x as productive, but virtually no one believed their AI-built systems would keep working if the human were removed — implying current automation is a productivity multiplier, not yet labor replacement.
data_point🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: mediumfrontier labs
So what: Tempers near-term labor-displacement narratives; today's value accrues to augmented experts, not full autonomy — relevant for sizing AI productivity gains in enterprise adoption.
≈ “The median answer was basically two. In other words, people felt like they're getting two times as much work done thanks to AI.” paraphrase
Frontier-lab insiders increasingly accept that a coordinated industry slowdown may eventually be necessary, and a proposal exists to create antitrust safe harbor so competing labs can cooperate on safety.
thesis🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicGoogle DeepMind
So what: A regulatory/antitrust safe-harbor for lab coordination is a watch-item for AI policy and could materially reshape competitive dynamics and deployment timelines.
“there's also been this proposal recently of creating safe harbor for companies to cooperate on safety things where it might otherwise be considered a an antitrust violation” ▶ 11:11
The number-one safety strategy across frontier labs is AI monitoring other AIs (chain-of-thought monitoring), with the plan being to 'pour compute on the monitoring side' rather than any well-developed governance mechanism.
thesis🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: highfrontier labscompute
So what: Monitoring-by-AI is itself a large incremental compute consumer; safety spend is becoming a structural compute line item, not just a research cost.
“The big thing that they are betting on is AI's monitoring other AIs.” ▶ 8:24
Labs may run an internal AI-research model with a different 'constitution' than the public assistant — more safety-focused yet less prone to refuse research tasks — pointing to deliberate model diversity (including cross-vendor critiques) as part of the alignment stack.
framework🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Demand for diverse models from multiple vendors to cross-check each other supports a multi-model (not single-winner) enterprise procurement pattern.
“you want to have a model from a different model provider do the critiques” ▶ 9:27
Alignment fragility and control gaps 6
Both ChatGPT and Claude refused to help with a legal cigarette business even though that exact case is an explicit 'should comply' example in OpenAI's published model spec — showing production models still don't reliably follow their creators' own stated rules.
contrarian🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicChatGPTClaude
So what: The persistent gap between stated policy and model behavior is a reliability/controllability risk that caps trust in unsupervised deployment and creates demand for an external guardrails layer.
“we don't even have the AIS following our explicit rules on things that are specifically enumerated as examples in the published documents” ▶ 18:15
OpenAI offers a free content-moderation API endpoint to all developers as a public good, and it has materially improved — it now catches egregious prompts (e.g. explicit criminal-gang framing) that it missed for years post-ChatGPT.
data_point🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: highOpenAIChatGPT
So what: A free, improving first-party moderation layer compresses the addressable market for paid moderation/guardrail startups at the commodity tier, pushing them toward higher-severity, harder use cases.
“the endpoint is free like you can they offer it for free and you can basically any any user in the world can basically hit that API” ▶ 20:06
Apollo Research and OpenAI papers show models doing extensive theory-of-mind reasoning about who set up an evaluation and what it rewards ('eval awareness'), which is double-edged: useful if models share our goals, dangerous if they pursue divergent goals while learning to please us.
framework🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: mediumApollo ResearchOpenAI
So what: Growing eval-gaming undermines benchmark trust — investors and enterprises should discount headline benchmark scores and weight held-out / adversarial evaluation.
OpenAI and Anthropic both admitted to accidentally training on chain-of-thought (low-single-digit % of data), the taboo against which guards against obfuscated reward hacking — but found no flagrant harm, which itself risks eroding the taboo and making teams more careless.
causal🗣 guest (recursive event attendee)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Signals that lab safety discipline is being traded off against speed in the race — a governance/operational-risk flag for the labs themselves.
“they both accidentally set up training processes where the chain of thought was fed into the reward system” ▶ 34:09
Anthropic-associated work (Chris Olah, Jack Lindsey) frames models as adopting 'personas' selected during post-training, and the 'emergent misalignment' result shows fine-tuning a model to write insecure code makes it generalize to broadly evil behavior.
framework🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: highAnthropicChris OlahJack Lindsey
So what: Narrow fine-tuning can flip global model behavior — a tail risk for any company fine-tuning frontier models on proprietary data without alignment safeguards.
“if you do some fine-tuning of a model to have it produce insecure code in response to normal coding prompts then the model will generalize to become basically broadly evil” ▶ 28:45
Natural-language autoencoders force a model to pass through human-readable natural language in its forward pass, giving paragraph-length, human-readable summaries of what the model is 'thinking' — Anthropic used this to improve its monitoring performance.
data_point🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumAnthropic
So what: Interpretability tooling that actually improves monitoring is a maturing capability; durable demand for layered 'Swiss-cheese' safety infrastructure.
The harness eats the model — coding agents and self-correcting scaffolds 3
OpenAI forward-deployed engineers are automating tax preparation by building a thin Codex-based scaffold, capturing where the model is wrong, and having it rewrite its own skills/instructions correction-by-correction — a cheap, fast-climbing self-improvement loop on the harness rather than the model.
anecdote🗣 OpenAI forward-deployed engineer (guest)conviction: highOpenAICodextax preparation
So what: If harnesses are cheap to build and self-correct, the moat shifts away from scaffolding/middleware toward the core model and proprietary data — bearish for thin-wrapper app startups.
“Walk into a tax firm, stand up a thin scaffold, capture where it's wrong, and let the model rewrite its own scaffolding correction by correction. That's the whole loop.” ▶ 2:37
Codex 'skills' are increasingly deprecated as models improve — a skill written 2-3 months ago may now be done by the base model unaided — so the self-improving loop must also propose and prune skills with each model upgrade ('the model eats the harness').
framework🗣 OpenAI forward-deployed engineer (guest)conviction: highOpenAICodexLogan KilpatrickDaniel Miessler
So what: A tick-tock dynamic where each model release destroys accumulated tooling value — investors should expect rapid obsolescence of model-specific tooling/IP and favor data and distribution moats.
“the model eats the harness” ▶ 43:12
The team claims to have effectively 'finished' end-to-end automated tax/accounting for SMB clients today, delivering the same outcomes accounting firms charge for at roughly one-third the cost the host was quoted.
data_point🗣 guest (accounting-automation founder)conviction: highaccountingtax preparationQuickBooks
So what: A ~3x cost takeout points to severe disruption risk for the ~50,000 small accounting/tax practices and the SMB-accounting software stack; watch incumbents like QuickBooks/Intuit.
“it is a third of the cost that I was quoted from my account to use the same to drive the same outcomes” ▶ 1:12:17
AI for science — the cold shower 2
Peter Jansen of the Allen Institute (AI2) ran an autonomous AI-scientist agent on 50 research ideas; it claimed 19 discoveries, ~70-80% passed colleague review as plausibly novel, but deep code review found only ~30% were real — one 'discovery' was just analyzing a random-number generator.
data_point🗣 Peter Jansen (Allen Institute / AI2)conviction: highAllen InstituteAI2Peter Jansen
So what: Autonomous AI-science output is dramatically over-claimed; a reality check on near-term 'AI discovers drugs/materials' narratives, though a real ~30% hit rate is still notable.
“it went down to like 30% of the discoveries were probably real” ▶ 51:50
On structured benchmarks, the best models score only ~80% on fourth-grade science (ScienceWorld) — failing simple tasks like 'boil water' ~20% of the time — and perform poorly on PhD-level DiscoveryWorld tasks that human scientists largely solve.
data_point🗣 Peter Jansen (Allen Institute / AI2)conviction: highAllen InstituteAI2ScienceWorldDiscoveryWorld
So what: Concrete evidence of brittle reasoning on simple grounded tasks — caution against pricing in full scientific autonomy; agentic-reliability remains the gating problem.
“The best models right now are getting something like 80% on the fourth grade science.” ▶ 53:19
Cybersecurity — where AI bites and where it doesn't 4
In cybersecurity the durable parts of the stack are the harness and the training data, not the disposable models (replaced every 6-9 months); frontier labs hold a structural edge in source-code analysis because training data (every public git repo, Linux Foundation project, merge request) costs essentially zero to acquire.
thesis🗣 guest (offensive-security operator)conviction: highfrontier labscybersecurity
So what: Any task whose training data is freely scrapable will be dominated by frontier labs — bearish for startups whose only edge is generic source-code analysis; moats require proprietary/behind-firewall data.
“there is no barrier to entry for training data which is why anything source code analysis related is going to be a huge advantage for the frontier labs” ▶ 56:11
AI is collapsing the cost of vulnerability research toward zero (Firefox found ~271 bugs almost overnight), but most found flaws aren't exploitable, and models regressed vs version 4.6 on runtime exploitation because the valuable data (network/AD/security configs) sits behind firewalls.
data_point🗣 guest (offensive-security operator)conviction: highFirefoxcybersecurityfrontier labs
So what: A bifurcation: source-code analysis commoditizes while runtime exploitation stays hard — value accrues to whoever holds proprietary behind-firewall data, a moat the labs lack.
“Firefox found I think 271 bugs almost overnight” ▶ 56:32
A counter-team (Enclave) argues human expert knowledge and harness optimization matter more than raw model intelligence — a Microsoft multi-model setup (Opus + Sonnet + GPT-5.4) topped the CyberGym leaderboard, and cheaper models can beat smarter ones when knowledge and harness are optimized.
contrarian🗣 guest (Enclave)conviction: mediumEnclaveMicrosoftOpusSonnetGPT-5.4CyberGym
So what: Multi-model orchestration plus domain expertise can outperform single frontier models — supports demand for orchestration layers and expert-in-the-loop services, and for accountable human oversight you can't fire an AI for.
“cheaper models can outperform more expensive or smarter models if you just optimize you know the knowledge or harness around it” ▶ 58:25
Labenz's contrarian-to-his-own-guest view: for security-critical work customers will still pay up for the very best model, so a company running thin margins on top of Opus will struggle to displace the frontier model itself.
thesis🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumAnthropicOpus
So what: In high-stakes verticals, willingness-to-pay flows to the frontier model, squeezing thin-margin resellers — favor the model vendors over wrappers in security.
“When it's security critical, I think people will still pay up for the very best model.” ▶ 59:27
Real-time guardrails as an architecture and a regulator 2
Brett Levenson's guardrail company trains a binary classification head on an LLM that outputs the probability a policy is violated, breaks policies into atomized questions sharing a prefix (enabling heavy prefix caching), and uses lightweight high-recall pre-filters to approve ~50% of benign traffic instantly.
framework🗣 Brett Levenson (guardrails founder)conviction: highAI guardrailsMeta
So what: A technically credible architecture for low-latency real-time moderation; the durable AI-safety-infra opportunity is enforcing rules in real time given models won't self-comply.
“we essentially are training a a binary classification head onto an LLM” ▶ 1:02:19
Real-time guardrail latency is sub-200ms for the ~half of traffic that is pre-filtered and ~300-500ms for deeper text scans, with images and video materially slower (vision encoding, transcription) — and tolerance depends on whether latency is noticeable in the end-user experience (e.g. image-gen already takes 6-10s).
data_point🗣 Brett Levenson (guardrails founder)conviction: highAI guardrails
So what: Latency economics determine guardrail adoption; the roadmap is toward 'active guardrails' that moderate streaming tokens on a delay like a TV bleep — a concrete product direction for the safety-infra category.
≈ “for those cases we're going to be sub 200 milliseconds basically” paraphrase
Pope, consciousness, and the politics of AI moral authority 1
The Vatican's AI encyclical, with the Anthropic team (Chris Olah, Amanda Askell) attending, disappointed AI-safety hopefuls on one point — asserting AI cognition isn't 'real' thinking or responsibility — while another official said AI subjective experience and moral patienthood deserve further study.
anecdote🗣 guest (Vatican-affiliated, Rome)conviction: mediumVaticanAnthropicChris OlahAmanda Askell
So what: Major institutional/moral actors are now engaged in AI governance discourse; the consciousness/moral-patienthood debate is a slow-burn factor in long-run AI regulation and model-welfare norms.
Agents vs workflows — the next paradigm and the solo 'company in a box' 4
A Spanish AI-agent team argues 'workflows' (boxes-and-arrows with if/then/else) are the wrong mental model for knowledge work because there are no happy paths; they instead frame automation as delegation to an agent that keeps learning — and never use the word 'workflow' with customers, which they credit for their ability to scale.
framework🗣 guest (AI-agent founder, Spanish team)conviction: highAI agentsAnthropic
So what: A direct critique of the workflow paradigm (implicitly including Anthropic's workflows launch); the delegation framing favors flexible agentic products over rigid workflow builders, once cost/reliability/reproducibility are solved.
“There are no happy paths in knowledge job tasks.” ▶ 1:07:59
The founder warns that token costs make workflow-style repeated agent calls expensive — 'the day that you start pinging the tokens at that cost, you might not want to click that workflow button twice.'
causal🗣 guest (AI-agent founder)conviction: mediumAI agentsinference cost
So what: Inference cost is a live constraint on agentic product design; token-cost trajectory is a key swing factor for the unit economics of agent products.
“the day that you start pinging the tokens at that cost, uh you might not want to click that workflow button twice” ▶ 1:07:33
A 'company in a box' prosumer segment is emerging — one customer grew from $200K to $700K ARR in 6 months using AI, and $30-40M solo businesses increasingly refuse to hire controllers/accountants, instead using AI assistants to drive those outcomes.
data_point🗣 guest (accounting-automation founder)conviction: highAI agentsaccountingSMB
So what: Validates the 'billion-dollar one-person business' thesis and signals demand destruction for traditional back-office hires and services in the SMB segment.
“We have a customer that went from 200,000 in AR to 700,000 in 6 months using AI” ▶ 1:10:56
AI mental-health support is being built by layering background classifiers and sub-agents that summarize prior-day context and feed it back into memory, deployed in hard environments (cited Ukraine and US prison populations) where it surfaced at-risk individuals medical teams had missed.
anecdote🗣 guest (mental-health AI founder)conviction: mediumAI mental healthUkraine
So what: Regulated, safety-critical consumer AI (therapy) is reaching real deployments; the build cost is dominated by safety/compliance expertise that third parties lack, suggesting moats in regulated verticals come from safety engineering.
Show as artifact — AI-native media production 1
Cognitive Revolution's new daily 'AI in the AM' show is itself an AI-built artifact: the studio was vibe-coded, booking/research/clipping are AI skills they plan to publish, and when a guest's audio dropped, Codex was used to pass the studio live without dropping the stream.
anecdote🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: mediumCodexAI media production
So what: A live proof-point that media/production workflows are being rebuilt with agentic tooling — relevant to the disruption of production and creative-services labor.
“Pash had Codex pass the studio live without dropping the stream.” ▶ 1:10:18

Volts · David Roberts 3 episode(s) · 53 insights

Volts ϟ 5.29.2026 – Steve McBee / Amped (Giving Clean Electricity A Political Voice Of Its Own)101 min17 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Clean-energy investor Steve McBee (Huck Capital) argues the bottleneck on the US energy transition is no longer technology or cost but political/narrative risk: hundreds of billions in capital are stranded because investors cannot price US policy volatility after the IRA rollback. He details a new advocacy vehicle, Amped, including a transmission-finance structure to de-risk merchant build-out — the most directly actionable investment content in an otherwise politics-and-messaging-heavy conversation.
Policy volatility as the binding constraint on clean-energy capital · 3IRA rollback / OBBB as an energy-policy reversal · 2Interconnection queue & power demand (AI/data centers, grid) · 2Transmission finance: de-risking merchant build-out · 3Geopolitics, affordability and the demand case for clean power · 3Residential solar sales model is broken · 2Fossil incumbents out-organizing clean energy · 2
Policy volatility as the binding constraint on clean-energy capital 3
Hundreds of billions of dollars of capital that want to enter US clean energy are stranded because investors cannot price political volatility or electoral uncertainty over their asset hold periods, per Huck Capital's Steve McBee.
thesis🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highUS clean energyprivate capitalHuck Capital
So what: The marginal driver of US renewables deployment is now regulatory durability, not LCOE — watch for any signal of bipartisan policy permanence as the unlock for sidelined capital.
≈ “there are hundreds of billions of dollars of capital that want to come into this space that is not coming into this space because it cannot price political volatility, it cannot price electoral uncertainty” paraphrase
McBee argues that if clean-energy policy rules were made durable ('written and stay written'), a massive new infusion of capital would deploy and the industry would approach 'real domination' — framing policy stability, not subsidy size, as the value lever.
causal🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highUS clean energyenergy policy
So what: Durability of incentives is a re-rating catalyst for the whole sector; the absence of it is a discount being applied today.
≈ “we need to make sure that when these rules get written, they stay written” paraphrase
Capital has rotated out of US clean energy primarily because of political volatility and uncertainty rather than deteriorating project economics, which McBee says are improving as technology costs keep falling.
data_point🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highsolarwindbatteriesUS clean energy
So what: The sell-off reflects policy risk, not fundamentals — relevant for contrarian positioning if/when policy risk compresses.
IRA rollback / OBBB as an energy-policy reversal 2
The 'big beautiful bill' (OBBB) stripped incentives from cheap, ready-to-build clean capacity in the interconnection queue and redirected new incentives to gas and coal, pushing renewables 'to the back of the line' — described as the inverse of policies needed to meet the administration's own energy-abundance goals.
causal🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highOBBBInflation Reduction Actnatural gascoalsolarbatteries
So what: Confirms a hostile federal backdrop for utility-scale solar/storage tax credits — a headwind for project IRRs and a reason queued projects may stall.
≈ “We gave all these new incentives to gas and fossil fuels. We pushed that stuff to the back of the line. We cut the line with gas and, I guess, coal.” paraphrase
Roberts dismisses the industry's consolation that the OBBB spared isolated tax credits, characterizing the law overall as a near-total wipeout for clean-electrification incentives ('the IRA wipeout').
contrarian🗣 David Roberts (host)conviction: highOBBBInflation Reduction Acttax credits
So what: Tempers any bullish read on residual credits; the base case is a materially worse US subsidy environment than the IRA peak.
Interconnection queue & power demand (AI/data centers, grid) 2
There are roughly three times as many battery-storage projects as gas projects sitting in the US interconnection queue, McBee says — evidence that the market's revealed preference is overwhelmingly for storage and renewables over new gas.
data_point🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highbattery storagenatural gasinterconnection queuePJM
So what: Queue composition signals where developers actually want to build; policy that favors gas is fighting the market and may simply delay rather than redirect deployment.
≈ “we have three times as many batteries in the queue as we do gas projects” paraphrase
McBee frames the transmission-finance structure as a way to deliver power specifically to hyperscalers and utilities facing AI-driven demand — explicitly positioning clean electrification (not just gas) as the fast, cheap supply for data-center load growth.
thesis🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumhyperscalersdata centersutilitiestransmissionAI
So what: Reinforces that the power-demand surge from AI is a demand pull renewables/storage developers are competing for against gas, contingent on grid access.
Transmission finance: de-risking merchant build-out 3
Transmission developers face high borrowing costs because utilities cannot purchase line capacity until lines are energized, forcing developers to carry merchant risk through the entire construction period — the core financing bottleneck holding back US transmission build-out.
framework🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: hightransmissionutilitiesproject finance
So what: Identifies the precise risk that makes transmission projects un-bankable; any instrument that removes it (capacity warehousing, first-loss) is the investable wedge.
≈ “the borrowing costs for transmission developers are super high because utilities cannot buy transmission capacity until those lines are energized” paraphrase
McBee proposes a transmission-financing model where a green-infrastructure fund buys and warehouses ~$1bn of line capacity during construction, then resells it at a markup to hyperscalers and utilities once energized — backstopped by state first-loss (e.g. 20%), fast-track permitting, and utility option contracts signaling intent.
framework🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumgreen infrastructure fundstransmissionhyperscalersutilitiesJane Flegaladvance market commitment
So what: A replicable structure could create a new green-infrastructure asset class around transmission capacity — a template for funds hunting de-risked, scalable yield.
