≈ “This capital provider is going to buy a billion dollars of capacity and it's going to hold that capacity while the lines are being built and then it will sell that capacity at a markup to the hyperscalers and the utilities” paraphrase
McBee says the clean-energy industry has ~$1 trillion of capacity either installed or sitting in the queue, an under-leveraged scale ('throw weight') he wants to organize to solve market bottlenecks like transmission.
data_point🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumUS clean energytransmissioninterconnection queue
So what: Frames the installed+queued base as collateral/bargaining power; aggregation plays (à la NFL/AARP commercial arms he cites) could become a new industry monetization model.
Geopolitics, affordability and the demand case for clean power 3
McBee and Roberts argue a (2026) Iran war and an associated ~$1.5tn defense budget plus ~$200bn supplemental — framed as protecting Middle East energy interests — make the affordability and energy-security case for domestically sourced clean power that the industry is failing to exploit.
thesis🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumIrandefense budgetMiddle Eastenergy securityChina
So what: Oil-price/geopolitical shocks strengthen the cost-and-security narrative for renewables; watch for affordability becoming the dominant pro-clean-energy political frame.
Lawmakers who repeat that clean energy is cheaper have shallow conviction and default to gimmicks (gas-tax freezes, rate freezes) at the first political backlash, McBee says — meaning policy support is brittle and easily reversed.
causal🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumUS Congresselectricity ratesgas tax
So what: Political support for clean energy is thinner than headlines suggest, raising the odds of abrupt policy reversals that capital must price.
McBee cites China building '500 gigawatts' of clean capacity as the competitive benchmark US policy is ignoring, framing the transition as a China-competition race rather than a climate issue.
data_point🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumChinaUS clean energyDoug Burgum
So what: The China-competition frame is the emerging bipartisan justification for clean-energy support — a watch item for any policy thaw.
Residential solar sales model is broken 2
McBee, a former NRG Home CEO, says he has spent ~10 years trying to 'blow up' the residential solar model — door-to-door selling — calling it a disaster for consumers, trust and the industry, with consumers arriving biased against the product.
contrarian🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highresidential solarNRG Homedoor-to-door sales
So what: A structural negative for resi-solar origination economics and customer-acquisition cost; reinforces the sector's well-known distress.
≈ “I have spent 10 years trying to blow up the residential clean energy model, which solar proxies for, which I think has been a disaster for consumers and trust and the industry” paraphrase
McBee notes that at NRG Home twelve years ago the addressable resi clean-energy market was limited to climate-forward homeowners willing to pay a premium because products were not yet cost-competitive and lacked VPP/grid-services revenue; he says that VPP/shared-savings opportunity now exists.
framework🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: mediumVPPvirtual power plantsgrid servicesresidential solarNRG Home
So what: VPP/grid-services monetization is the emerging path to make resi distributed energy economic without a green premium — a structural shift worth tracking for DER aggregators.
Fossil incumbents out-organizing clean energy 2
McBee says the fossil-fuel industry is running at least three disinformation 'air wars' high in the consumer funnel (panels as 'Chinese spyware', chemicals, unreliability), out-competing clean energy in digital/social media despite renewables being the lower-cost product.
causal🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highfossil fuel industrysolarsocial mediaGood Power
So what: Narrative/permitting opposition is a real cost driver for project siting and community approval — a soft risk that delays renewables build-out at the local level.
McBee flags that the main clean-power trade association (ACP) is roughly half fossil-gas membership, structurally preventing it from positioning clean electrification as a substitute for gas — and that SEIA elected Tim Pawlenty as president.
data_point🗣 Steve McBee (Huck Capital, guest)conviction: highAmerican Clean Power AssociationSEIATim Pawlentynatural gas
So what: The industry's own lobbying apparatus is conflicted, weakening its policy advocacy — a reason the durable-policy unlock the thesis depends on may remain elusive.
Volts ϟ 5.27.2026 – Betony Jones (A Limited Defense of Biden's Everything-Bagel Industrial Policy)68 min14 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Betony Jones, who ran labor/jobs policy at Biden's DOE, argues from interviews with 12+ federally funded clean-energy firms that IRA/BIL labor and community-benefit standards were not the bottleneck on deployment — permitting, contracting timelines, and political volatility were. Investor-relevant takeaways: the prevailing-wage/apprenticeship tax-credit bonus survived OBBB while base solar credits were repealed; clean-energy projects with hundreds of billions committed are mostly proceeding; and battery/manufacturing labor turnover is a real execution risk for the US supply-chain buildout.
What survived OBBB · 2Where projects actually stalled · 3Manufacturing labor as execution risk · 3Durability of the buildout · 3Implementation lessons for capital allocators · 3
What survived OBBB 2
Under the OBBB rollback, the IRA prevailing-wage and apprenticeship tax-credit standards were retained with bipartisan support while the underlying solar/clean-energy base credits were repealed or phased out.
data_point🗣 Betony Jones (guest, ex-DOE labor/jobs policy lead)conviction: highIRAOBBBsolar tax creditprevailing wageapprenticeship
So what: Signals where federal political support is durable — labor/wage standards persist even as headline clean-energy subsidies are cut; models pricing IRA repeal risk should separate the wage-standard layer (sticky) from the base ITC/PTC (politically fragile).
≈ “with the IRA and the OBBB, the thing that stuck with bipartisan support was the prevailing wage and apprenticeship standards. The underlying credits, the solar tax credits, changed, went away. But the prevailing wage and apprenticeship credits were not repealed.” paraphrase
The IRA prevailing-wage-and-apprenticeship structure raised the clean-energy investment tax credit five-fold, from 6% to 30%, making the labor conditions a de facto requirement rather than an optional add-on because no developer would forgo that magnitude of subsidy.
data_point🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highIRAinvestment tax creditprevailing wageapprenticeship
So what: Quantifies the actual economics driving developer behavior on IRA projects — the 6%-to-30% step-up dwarfs prevailing-wage cost, so compliance was near-universal; useful for sizing the labor-cost delta in clean-energy project models.
≈ “with the tax credits you got five times more. Your tax credit went up from 6% to 30% if you paid prevailing wage and you registered.” paraphrase
Where projects actually stalled 3
Per interviews with 12+ federally funded clean-energy firms, the binding constraints on deployment were permitting delays, contracting timelines, and post-2024 political volatility — not the labor or community-benefit standards, which firms generally found workable or even helpful.
thesis🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highDOEpermittingclean energyIRABIL
So what: Reframes the deployment-risk thesis for clean-energy investors: permitting/contracting and policy reversal — not compliance overhead — are the levers that move project timelines and returns.
≈ “The real bottlenecks were in implementation — permitting delays and contracting timelines.” paraphrase
Firms told Jones that the single biggest pain point was the cost and uncertainty of volatility under the second Trump administration, including stalled projects awaiting renegotiation of grant/loan terms.
data_point🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highTrump administrationDOE grantsDOE loans
So what: Policy-reversal risk, not regulatory burden, is the dominant value-at-risk for clean-energy projects — watch for grant/loan renegotiations as a catalyst for project delays or cancellations.
≈ “A lot of what I heard was how damaging the volatility is with the second Trump administration and how costly that is.” paraphrase
Domestic-content requirements created a chicken-and-egg problem because aggressive compliance timelines were imposed before the US supply chains existed, and many firms could not even identify the origin of all their product inputs, forcing reliance on a waiver process.
causal🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: mediumdomestic contentsupply chainIRA
So what: Flags supply-chain traceability and domestic-content waivers as a recurring friction point and potential opportunity for compliance-tooling / supply-chain-mapping vendors serving clean-energy manufacturers.
≈ “the timelines were so aggressive in meeting these domestic content standards and not every product even knows what its inputs are.” paraphrase
Manufacturing labor as execution risk 3
US battery and clean-energy manufacturing plants frequently pay below fast-food wages and run 30-40% annual turnover, which Jones argues undermines the ability to build a globally competitive, durable manufacturing workforce.
data_point🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highbattery manufacturingStarbuckslabor turnoverclean energy manufacturing
So what: Labor turnover and wage levels are an under-appreciated operational risk for US battery/EV-supply-chain plants — a margin and ramp-speed drag investors should probe in factory buildouts.
≈ “a lot of these manufacturing companies are paying less than Starbucks in a lot of cases. Then they're saying, 'We have 30, 40% turnover, but we'll just go back to the fast food industry and see who else we can find.'” paraphrase
Davis-Bacon prevailing-wage rules apply only to construction workers building federally funded clean-energy facilities, not to the factory workers who later operate them, creating a wage cliff where construction labor is paid well but ongoing manufacturing jobs are not.
framework🗣 host (David Roberts) / Betony Jonesconviction: mediumDavis-BaconWalsh-Healeymanufacturingconstruction
So what: Helps investors distinguish which clean-energy job categories carry mandated wage floors — construction is covered, ongoing operations are not, affecting opex assumptions and political-durability of the workforce.
Analysis cited by Jones found the IRA battery production tax credit is generous enough to absorb upper-quintile union-pattern wages, meaning manufacturers receiving the credit have room to raise wages without eroding the subsidy's value.
data_point🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: mediumbattery production tax creditIRA45X
So what: Implies battery-cell makers claiming the production credit have margin headroom that could be redirected to wages or pressured by future labor standards — relevant to cost-curve and unionization risk for domestic cell producers.
≈ “take the battery production tax credit — are so generous that more union pattern upper quintile wages could have been accommodated” paraphrase
Durability of the buildout 3
Jones asserts the clean-energy industrial policy succeeded in getting firms to make hundreds of billions of dollars of irreversible investments in US clean-energy supply chains, and the vast majority of those projects are still moving forward despite the OBBB rollback.
thesis🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highclean energy supply chainIRADOE
So what: Suggests the physical buildout has momentum independent of the subsidy regime — sunk capital and supply-chain commitments may make the clean-energy manufacturing base more resilient to policy reversal than headlines imply.
≈ “We got firms to make irreversible investments in building clean energy supply chains in the US. They did that with hundreds of billions of dollars. Those projects, the vast majority, are moving forward.” paraphrase
Firms that adopted DOE community-benefit and labor-partnership practices report keeping them voluntarily after the Trump administration removed the requirement, and states have adopted similar conditions, indicating practice persistence beyond the federal mandate.
anecdote🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: mediumDOEcommunity benefits plansstateslabor agreements
So what: Community-engagement and labor-partnership norms are diffusing into private practice and state policy — a structural, durable cost/process input for project developers regardless of federal rules.
≈ “The Trump administration has told us we don't have to do this stuff anymore, but it's baked into our project. We're going to keep doing it.” paraphrase
Jones reframes the political case for clean energy as primarily an economic-equity and national-security argument with climate benefits as ancillary, arguing the energy system is shifting from extracted-molecule combustion to a highly manufactured base that the US must domestically produce and secure.
framework🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: highenergy securitynational securityindustrial policysupply chain
So what: The national-security/reshoring framing is the more politically durable rationale for continued support of domestic clean-energy manufacturing — the angle most likely to survive partisan turnover and attract bipartisan funding.
≈ “The energy system is changing from subsurface molecule combustion to one that is highly manufactured. We need to manufacture those things and get the resources that go into them in the US” paraphrase
Implementation lessons for capital allocators 3
The regulatory burden of a requirement like Davis-Bacon is not fixed but depends on administrative capacity: DOE built a centralized electronic payroll-compliance system that cut the compliance burden, whereas the CHIPS program at Commerce, lacking that foresight, found Davis-Bacon a genuine drag on chip-fab construction.
causal🗣 host (David Roberts) / Betony Jonesconviction: highDavis-BaconDOECHIPS ActDepartment of CommerceMike Schmidt
So what: Agency execution capacity is a real differentiator in how fast federal money converts to built projects — a variable worth tracking program-by-program when assessing deployment speed of federal industrial-policy dollars.
≈ “how much burden a particular requirement... will place on the government and the private sector is not a fixed quantity.” paraphrase
DOE deployed clean-energy capital during a period of historically low unemployment and a tight construction labor market, making workforce availability — secured via project labor and collective bargaining agreements — a genuine supply-chain risk to building projects on time and budget.
causal🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: mediumlabor marketproject labor agreementscollective bargainingDOE
So what: Workforce availability, not just capital, gates clean-energy project timelines in a tight labor market — labor-secured projects (PLAs/CBAs) may carry lower schedule risk than the market assumes.
≈ “Collective bargaining agreements, project labor agreements help provide that assurance — they reduce that particular risk... It's also a critical supply chain issue to build your project.” paraphrase
Jones notes German-style commercialization-of-technology industrial policy was cited by multinational firms as more bureaucratic but politically durable and insulated from electoral swings, contrasting with the US model's whipsaw between administrations.
contrarian🗣 Betony Jones (guest)conviction: lowGermanyindustrial policytechnology commercialization
So what: Policy durability is itself a country-risk factor for industrial-policy-dependent investments — US clean-energy exposure carries higher political-reversal risk than comparable European programs.
≈ “how Germany invests in the commercialization of technology and how it is bureaucratic and politically durable. It is not subject to these political winds.” paraphrase
Volts ϟ 5.22.2026 – Kristin George Bagdanov + Panama Bartholomy (How To Phase Out Residential Gas)68 min22 insights2026-05-26
▶ Watch on YouTube
Building Decarbonization Coalition leaders argue the US residential gas system is in a self-reinforcing cost spiral — ~$49B/yr utility capex (two-thirds of the gas bill now infrastructure, not commodity) on a flat-to-declining demand base — and lay out the regulatory levers (line-extension allowances, fuel-neutral obligation-to-serve, future-of-gas proceedings, neighborhood-scale electrification and thermal energy networks) that will reroute that spend over the 2020s-2040s. Investor relevance: heat-pump demand is structurally strong (US now #1 globally at 63% market share), gas-distribution capex faces growing stranding/political risk, and shareholder-funded retirement-fund proposals could shift end-of-life liabilities from ratepayers to utility equity.
Gas death spiral & utility capex unwind · 5Heat-pump market leadership · 3Regulatory levers: line-extension allowances & obligation to serve · 5Neighborhood-scale electrification & thermal energy networks · 5Stranded-asset & shareholder-liability risk · 4
Gas death spiral & utility capex unwind 5
US gas utilities now spend ~$49B/year on their systems, ~$28B of it on distribution (pipes, services, meters); this capex has tripled since ~2010 while per-customer gas demand declines, meaning customers pay more for a product they use less.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanov (Building Decarbonization Coalition)conviction: highnatural gas utilitiesAmerican Gas Associationgas distribution infrastructure
So what: Gas LDC distribution capex is on an unsustainable trajectory against falling throughput; investors in gas-only LDCs face rising regulatory scrutiny of capex prudence and eventual stranding risk.
≈ “We're at about $49 billion a year that gas utilities are spending on their systems, and about $28 billion of that is just on the distribution side system” paraphrase
Roughly two-thirds of the average US residential gas bill now goes to infrastructure rather than the gas commodity — the reverse of the 1980s ratio — because the regulated return model rewards capital spending regardless of utilization.
framework🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highgas utilitiesresidential ratepayers
So what: Bill composition has flipped to fixed/infrastructure costs, making gas bills rise even as usage falls — a setup for political backlash and rate-case losses for LDCs.
≈ “in the 80s and it's more like the reverse, where about two-thirds is going to the commodity cost and one-third is going to the infrastructure” paraphrase
Each $1 of gas-utility capex yields $2-$3 in customer costs over time because of shareholder returns and financing, so the rate-of-return model structurally incentivizes overbuilding ('gold plating') a shrinking system.
causal🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highinvestor-owned utilitiesratepayers
So what: The 2-3x bill multiplier on capex is the engine of the spiral; reforms that cap or claw back this capex directly threaten LDC earnings growth.
≈ “Every dollar they spend in capital expenditures yields $2 to $3 in customer costs because of those shareholder returns and financing costs.” paraphrase
A defection rate as low as ~2% of gas customers can drive remaining-customer gas prices up ~50% over a decade, so the cost spiral does not require mass exit to become acute.
data_point🗣 host (David Roberts, citing a webinar model)conviction: mediumgas utilitiesratepayers
So what: The tipping point for gas-rate escalation is low — affluent-customer electrification in a few percent of homes can trigger steep rate increases and accelerate further defection.
≈ “about a 2% defection rate causes prices to rise by 50% over a decade” paraphrase
US gas bills rose ~60% faster than electric bills in the 2025 CPI, most of it infrastructure-driven, so the affordability crisis often framed as an electricity problem is already more acute on the gas side.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highConsumer Price Indexgas utilities
So what: Gas affordability is a present, not future, political pressure point — supports regulatory momentum to curb gas capex.
≈ “we see that gas bills outpaced electric bills by 60%” paraphrase
Heat-pump market leadership 3
The US has moved from #3 to the #1 heat-pump market globally in five years and now holds ~63% market share, with heat pumps outselling gas furnaces for four straight years and heat-pump water heaters accelerating.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomy (Building Decarbonization Coalition)conviction: highheat pumpsheat pump water heatersgas furnacesHVAC manufacturers
So what: Heat-pump demand is a rare US clean-energy growth leadership story (vs lagging EVs/batteries/renewables) — structurally bullish for heat-pump and HPWH manufacturers and installers.
≈ “we're talking 63% market share, where we can proudly stand up and say we were third place five years ago, we're now number one” paraphrase
Building decarbonization legislation held steady at the state level in 2025 despite expectations of a post-2024-election drop-off, with the same volume of bills on standards, incentives and education as 2024.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: mediumstate legislaturesbuilding decarbonization policy
So what: Policy tailwind for electrification appliances is more durable than the federal political narrative suggests — de-risks state-incentive-dependent demand.
Gas-appliance manufacturers themselves now universally describe the electrification transition as inevitable, signaling the supply chain at the end of the gas pipe is repositioning.
thesis🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: highgas appliance manufacturersinduction stovesheat pumps
So what: When incumbent gas-appliance OEMs concede inevitability, it confirms a durable product-mix shift toward electric/induction — a read-through for appliance-maker capex and product roadmaps.
≈ “When you talk to the manufacturers of gas appliances, universally, they say this transition is inevitable.” paraphrase
Regulatory levers: line-extension allowances & obligation to serve 5
Eliminating gas line-extension allowances (free new hookups subsidized by existing customers) nationally would save ratepayers an estimated $3-$7 billion/year, making it the highest-leverage near-term reform.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: mediumline extension allowancesgas utilitiesratepayers
So what: Killing hookup subsidies removes a large recurring transfer that props up gas-customer growth — a multi-billion-dollar headwind to LDC connection growth as more states adopt it.
“The range was from $3 to $7 billion a year.” ▶ 47:05
California removed electric line-extension allowances for mixed-fuel buildings, granting the subsidy only to all-electric developments — a two-sided incentive design that markedly shifted new builds toward electrification.
causal🗣 Panama Bartholomy / Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highCaliforniareal estate developersnew construction
So what: Pairing gas-disincentive with electric-only carrots is a proven lever to flip new-build economics — watch other states copy it; bearish for gas in new construction.
≈ “California has gone one step further where they have removed electric line extension allowances ... if it is to a mixed-fuel building” paraphrase
Six states plus DC are actively deliberating line-extension-allowance reform (incl. Maryland, Minnesota, Illinois) and ~8 states are pursuing utility-led thermal energy networks, indicating a broad multi-state regulatory wave rather than a California outlier.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: mediumMarylandMinnesotaIllinoisthermal energy networks
So what: The reform set is diffusing across states, raising the systemic regulatory risk to gas-distribution growth nationwide.
Many state 'obligation to serve' statutes do not mention gas and may already be interpretable as fuel-neutral (obligation to serve end-uses, all of which electricity can now meet), so the main barrier to swapping gas for electric is fear of litigation, not law.
framework🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: mediumobligation to servepublic utilities commissionsgas utilities
So what: Reinterpreting obligation-to-serve as fuel-neutral could legally unlock managed gas wind-downs; litigation risk and legislative clarification (e.g. Massachusetts dispute) are the gating factors to watch.
≈ “there's nothing legally stopping this other than the fear of litigation” paraphrase
New York scrapped its arbitrary 100-foot free-hookup rule but suspended implementation through end-2027 after industry opposition and a lawsuit, showing reforms can pass yet stall under legal and election-cycle pressure.
anecdote🗣 host / Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highNew YorkAmerican Gas Association
So what: Implementation lag and litigation mean the gas-capex unwind will be uneven and reversible state-to-state — timing risk for theses that price in rapid policy change.
Neighborhood-scale electrification & thermal energy networks 5
PG&E launched a Zonal Electrification Equity Pilot — the first public utility RFP for service-territory-wide neighborhood-scale electrification — with the first homes converting this month after winning neighborhood agreement.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: highPacific Gas and ElectricPG&Eneighborhood-scale electrification
So what: Zonal electrification creates a new procurement channel (consultants, installers, analysis vendors) and a template for converting gas neighborhoods wholesale — watch PG&E pilot outcomes as a scaling proof point.
≈ “Pacific Gas and Electric has the Zonal Electrification Equity Pilot Program. It's the first public RFP that's been released by a utility ... neighborhood-scale electrification.” paraphrase
California reduced the neighborhood-conversion approval threshold from 100% to two-thirds of building owners, removing the single-holdout veto that blocks zonal gas decommissioning.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: highCalifornianeighborhood-scale electrification
So what: Lowering the consent threshold materially improves the feasibility/economics of decommissioning whole gas segments — a structural enabler for at-scale conversion.
≈ “reduced the threshold for approval for a project like this from 100% of building owners down to only two-thirds” paraphrase
Early zonal-electrification outreach offers homeowners ~$35,000 checks to electrify in lieu of a coming pipeline replacement, framing whole-neighborhood gas shutoff as cheaper than reinvesting in the pipe.
anecdote🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: mediumzonal electrificationgas utilitiesheat pumps
So what: Avoided-pipeline-replacement economics can fund large per-home electrification incentives — a demand pull for heat-pump/appliance installers funded by deferred gas capex.
≈ “instead give you a $35,000 check to make upgrades to your house” paraphrase
California identified 150+ priority zones for neighborhood-scale pilots and New York has ~10 utility-led thermal energy network pilots nearing construction greenlight, with one-in-four states holding TEN-related legislation.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highCaliforniaNew Yorkthermal energy networksUtility Thermal Energy Network and Jobs Act
So what: A visible pipeline of pilots gives geothermal/thermal-network developers and engineering firms a near-term addressable market; ~few-hundred successful projects over five years is the stated scaling threshold.
Gas-only utilities may become advocates for thermal energy networks because TENs preserve a pipe-based business and workforce, whereas all-electric conversion eliminates them entirely.
thesis🗣 host / Panama Bartholomyconviction: mediumgas-only utilitiesthermal energy networksgeothermal
So what: Survival incentives could turn gas LDCs from blockers into champions of thermal networks — a possible pivot path (gas-to-thermal utility) for otherwise-stranded gas-only operators.
Stranded-asset & shareholder-liability risk 4
Utilities are installing pipes designed to last decades — described as a seven-year investment in a system meant to be retired in ~20 years — in states with earlier decarbonization deadlines, building assets destined to be stranded or to blow through carbon targets.
causal🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: highgas distribution pipelinesstranded assets
So what: Ongoing gas-pipe replacement in decarbonizing states is accumulating stranding risk now; the question of who absorbs the write-off (ratepayers vs shareholders) is becoming a live policy fight.
≈ “we're also gold-plating the gas system in a seven-year investment in a system we want to be off of in 20 years” paraphrase
California's Senator Stern introduced legislation for a shareholder-funded utility retirement fund, aiming to shift gas-system end-of-life costs from ratepayers onto utility shareholders.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: mediumCaliforniaSenator Sternutility shareholdersinvestor-owned utilities
So what: If shareholder-funded decommissioning becomes a model, it materially raises end-of-life liability for gas-LDC equity holders — a direct valuation risk to watch in IOU exposure.
≈ “a retirement fund paid for by shareholders of the utilities in order to force this conversation forward” paraphrase
Massachusetts now requires utilities to evaluate non-pipeline alternatives (NPAs) before approving new gas pipeline projects, removing the default to status-quo expansion.
data_point🗣 Kristin George Bagdanovconviction: highMassachusettsnon-pipeline alternativesgas utilities
So what: Mandatory NPA evaluation inserts a cost-effectiveness gate that can systematically divert capex away from gas pipes toward electrification/thermal alternatives.
14 states containing ~47% of US residential gas customers have opened future-of-gas proceedings, making the managed-transition question a near-majority-of-market regulatory reality rather than a fringe debate.
data_point🗣 Panama Bartholomyconviction: highfuture of gas proceedingspublic utilities commissionsresidential gas customers
So what: Nearly half the residential gas market is under active future-of-gas review — broad regulatory exposure for gas-LDC business models across the country.
≈ “Those 14 states — you have just about 47%. Just under half of all residential gas customers in the country are in those 14 states.” paraphrase

Peter H. Diamandis 1 episode(s) · 48 insights

Anthropic Files $965B IPO, Trump Signs AI Executive Order, and ChatGPT Crosses 1B Users | EP #262124 min48 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
WTF Just Happened in Tech panel (Diamandis, Dave Blundin, Alex Wissner-Gross, Imad Mostaque) reacts to Anthropic's confidential trillion-dollar-plus IPO filing, Trump's deregulatory AI executive order, ChatGPT hitting 1B MAU, Microsoft's in-house model push, Nvidia's move into PC CPUs, Bernie Sanders' AI sovereign-wealth-fund bill, and a wave of multi-billion-dollar longevity/gene-therapy news. Core investor thesis: a self-funding 'AI economy' with winner-take-all distribution moats, record capital flows, and trillion-dollar valuations reached in ~5 years.
Anthropic IPO & AI-lab valuations · 8AI policy: Trump EO, sovereign wealth fund, bio-screening · 7Adoption, distribution moats & second-order market structure · 8Microsoft, Nvidia & compute stack repositioning · 10Data-center energy, water & social-license risk · 3AGI generality shrinking & specialized models · 3Longevity & gene-therapy investment wave · 5How retail can access AI value & media/trust collapse · 4

Anthropic has confidentially filed IPO paperwork with the SEC and could be the first major frontier AI lab to go public, beating OpenAI to the public markets.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAISEC
So what: First pure-play frontier-lab listing creates a tradable public proxy for AI-model economics and a valuation benchmark for OpenAI/xAI; watch for the S-1 and lockups.

Polymarket prices a 60% chance Anthropic surpasses $1.8 trillion market cap on its first trading day, the same figure cited for SpaceX's anticipated IPO.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: mediumAnthropicPolymarketSpaceX

“Poly Market gives it a 60% chance that Anthropic surpasses 1.8 trillion in market cap on its first day.” ▶ 0:07
Anthropic is run by only ~5,000 employees, implying ~$9.4M revenue per employee versus ~$2.5M at Apple and ~$2.1M at Google, roughly 4x the best legacy tech firms.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis / Dave Blundinconviction: highAnthropicAppleGoogle

Imad Mostaque estimates Anthropic revenue around $50-60B implying roughly a 20x revenue multiple, which he calls high but not extreme, and says $100-150B revenue next year would not surprise anyone.

These newly-public AI labs will hold unprecedented dry powder and could each do thousands of billion-dollar acquisitions, spawning a whole ecosystem of supplier and target companies.
thesis🗣 Dave Blundin (guest, investor)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Post-IPO acquisition firepower is a tailwind for adjacent AI-tooling, infra and data startups as acquisition targets; position in the supplier/target ecosystem, not just the labs.
“This group of companies can do thousands of billion dollar acquisitions.” ▶ 0:17
Time-to-trillion-dollar valuation has collapsed: Apple took 42 years, Google 21, SpaceX 24, OpenAI ~10, and Anthropic roughly 5 years.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highAppleGoogleSpaceXOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Radically compressed value-creation timelines mean equity windows open and close fast; investors must access pre-IPO/early secondary or AI ETFs sooner.
Panel expects the AI/IPO wave to be oversubscribed with roughly a trillion dollars of capital seeking exposure, so SpaceX and Anthropic listings will not face demand shortfalls.
prediction🗣 Dave Blundin (guest)conviction: highAnthropicSpaceX
So what: If demand is as deep as claimed, allocation scarcity (not price) is the constraint; aftermarket pops are likely and crowding into AI names continues.
“No, this will be over subscribed. SpaceX will be oversubscribed.” ▶ 40:14
Blundin frames the AI capital wave as the biggest flow of money in world history by an order of magnitude or more and urges investors not to become numb to trillion-dollar IPOs.
thesis🗣 Dave Blundin (guest)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAISpaceX
So what: Treats AI capital formation as a once-in-history reallocation; argues against complacency and for active participation across the stack.
“this flow of money is the biggest in the history of the world by an order of magnitude or more” ▶ 36:26
AI policy: Trump EO, sovereign wealth fund, bio-screening 7
Trump signed an AI executive order rejecting heavy regulation and asking labs to voluntarily give government access to new models 30 days before public release, with Sam Altman and Anthropic publicly endorsing it.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highTrumpOpenAIAnthropicSam AltmanWhite House
So what: A deregulatory, voluntary-disclosure regime removes a near-term overhang on US lab deployment speed and favors US labs over EU competitors; bullish for US AI capex and release cadence.
“President Trump just signed an executive order that basically says America is not going to slow down on AI.” ▶ 0:25
The panel reads the EO as regulatory capture in motion: Altman and Dario lobbied the White House and got the proposed regulation watered down to voluntary self-regulation, analogous to Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan reversing his position after a White House meeting.
causal🗣 Dave Blundin (guest)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicSam AltmanDario AmodeiIntelLip-Bu TanDavid SacksElon Musk
So what: Incumbent labs shaping policy entrenches their position; political access becomes a competitive moat investors should weight when assessing lab durability.
“They met with the White House and then you have a complete watering down of the regulation” ▶ 8:44
Bernie Sanders is introducing the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act proposing a one-time 50% tax on the stock (not profits) of OpenAI, Anthropic and other major AI firms to give the public an ownership stake.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highBernie SandersOpenAIAnthropicUS Congress
So what: Even if it fails, it shifts the Overton window toward equity/stake mandates as a condition of operating; a tail policy risk for AI-lab cap tables and domicile choices.
“require OpenAI, Anthropic, and other major AI companies to contribute 50% of their stock to a public wealth fund” ▶ 1:06:56
Blundin argues a stock-based sovereign wealth fund is structurally unworkable because the government would have to dump trillions in illiquid AI stock to fund any public benefit, likely within one election cycle, crashing the market.
contrarian🗣 Dave Blundin (guest)conviction: highBernie SandersAI sovereign wealth fund
So what: Implies forced-selling/overhang risk if such a fund were ever created; income-tax structures are cleaner, so the equity-stake version is unlikely to survive in current form.
“you have to sell the stock to turn that into any public good. You can't dump $5 trillion of stock on the market.” ▶ 1:10:02
Wissner-Gross argues a US sovereign wealth fund is likely to happen and would naturally hold large stakes in OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX via a broad market index, pointing to the administration's existing 10% stake in Intel and golden-share equivalents as precedent.
prediction🗣 Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)conviction: mediumIntelOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXUS Treasury
So what: Signals growing state equity participation in strategic tech; investors should track government stake-taking (Intel precedent) as a structural feature of the AI cycle.
“this administration taking 10% stakes in Intel and Golden Share equivalents in other companies as the basis potentially for a sovereign wealth fund” ▶ 1:12:17

Adoption, distribution moats & second-order market structure 8
ChatGPT passed 1 billion monthly active users roughly three years after its Nov 2022 launch, faster than YouTube (a decade), Instagram (8 yrs) and TikTok (5 yrs), with OpenAI year-over-year growth at 62%.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highOpenAIChatGPTYouTubeInstagramTikTok

The panel argues distribution, not model quality, is the dominant moat between frontier labs, citing Altman's reported preference for a billion users over the strongest model, with OpenAI's GPT-5.5 now claimed to lead on both.
thesis🗣 Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)conviction: highOpenAISam AltmanGPT-5.5

The panel predicts the most valuable AI product will be the personal coordinating 'Jarvis' agent next to the user that orchestrates all other agents, making it the focal point of customer-acquisition spend.

Mostaque argues the AI economy is now funded to live within itself: post-IPO labs can spend $100B directly into an agent economy (e.g., on diseases) generating internal cash flow without touching JPMorgan, Main Street or legacy business.
thesis🗣 Imad Mostaque (guest)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAI

“this conduit of capital through these IPOs back into the pure AI world, it could entirely build an economy of its own” ▶ 43:57
Mostaque says a new lab can still catch OpenAI/Anthropic on algorithms but it is very hard because the incumbents' data and distribution advantages, which they will spend hundreds of billions to lock down, make it a winner-take-all go-to-market race.
thesis🗣 Imad Mostaque (guest)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Frames frontier AI as a winner-take-most market gated by distribution/capital, not just model quality; argues against betting on unknown challenger labs absent a novel distribution channel.
“distribution effects count for so much... this is typically how we see markets in terms of winner takes all” ▶ 1:51:58

Wissner-Gross argues the generality of AGI is starting to shrink as bio and cyber capabilities (e.g., OpenAI's Rosalind biodefense and GPT-cyber/Mythos series) get carved out of general models into restricted, post-trained models available only to governments and trusted researchers.

Verve 102, a one-time CRISPR base-editing gene therapy delivered via mRNA/LNP that permanently switches off the liver PCSK9 gene, reduced LDL cholesterol by 62% (sustained up to 18 months) and cut PCSK9 protein levels 88% in a NEJM-published Phase 1 trial.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandis (host)conviction: highVerve 102PCSK9RepathaNew England Journal of Medicine
So what: A single-shot, durable LDL-lowering gene therapy could disrupt chronic statin/PCSK9-antibody (e.g., Repatha) markets and validate in-vivo base editing; a clinical catalyst with broad cardiometabolic TAM.
“In a phase 1 trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine, the highest dose reduced LDL cholesterol by 62%.” ▶ 1:45:33
Wissner-Gross frames Verve 102's mechanism (derived from rare humans with naturally low-LDL mutations) as a template for many single-injection mRNA/LNP base-editing therapies targeting protective human gene variants for diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer.
framework🗣 Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)conviction: mediumVerve 102base editingmRNA
So what: Mining protective natural human variants for one-shot base-editing therapies defines a broad pipeline opportunity across major disease categories; a screening framework for gene-therapy investment.
“how many other human in varants in in human natural human biodiversity are there for people who never get Alzheimer's never get cancer” ▶ 1:47:37

Jordi Visser 1 episode(s) · 42 insights

“We Will Have a Crash”: Why AI Brings Out the Fear in People60 min42 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
Visser argues the AI trade is entering a new two-sided regime where supply-chain bottlenecks (memory, optical, gas turbines, semi test equipment, power) are the real second-half risk, not a 'bubble.' He stays long the 'physical-world upgrade' thematic basket (chemicals, industrials, power, optical) over the hyperscalers, dismisses crash-callers as psychologically motivated, and lays out a technical (20/50/200-day MA) discipline for riding bubbles. He flags oil/energy as the key macro swing factor, sees pharma (esp. Eli Lilly) as the next AI-native winner, and stays structurally bullish Bitcoin/tokenization despite calling crypto a current bear market.
New AI regime: breadth breakdown & bottleneck risk · 5Bubble psychology & technical-discipline framework · 5Physical-world AI buildout & thematic basket · 9Macro: oil, inflation & power as the swing risk · 4No-recession / compute-energy economy thesis · 5Software/SaaS not dead under agents · 4Pharma as the next AI-native winner · 5Crypto bear market but structural conviction · 5
New AI regime: breadth breakdown & bottleneck risk 5
Visser says the S&P is up ~5% on the month but only 3 of 11 sectors are higher, with gains concentrated almost entirely in AI semis, hardware and power — a narrowing-breadth signal he reads as the AI trade entering a two-sided regime rather than continued one-way melt-up.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highS&P 500AIsemiconductorsconsumer discretionaryTeslaAmazon
So what: Watch sector breadth and the AI-vs-rest spread; a regime where 'crappy beta' outperforms high-earnings AI names is a top-building, not crash, signal to trim into.
“only three of them were up” ▶ 1:22
Visser warns AI supply-chain bottlenecks (memory, optical, copper, silver, gas turbines, NAND, semiconductor test equipment) create real production risk for H2 2026 earnings that the market is not pricing, citing Fuji Kura's sharp post-earnings drop and Modine flagging supply constraints despite strong numbers.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highFuji KuraModine Manufacturingoptical networkinggas turbinessemiconductor test equipmentAI supply chain
So what: The H2 risk is delivery/buildout delay, not demand; monitor optical/memory names' guidance for bottleneck language as the catalyst for the next AI-trade drawdown.
“I think the market is not building in the production risk for the second half of the year, which I think is going to be greater than what people think.” ▶ 14:20
Visser cites Morgan Stanley data that semis, hardware and power have accounted for 97% of long/short fund long returns year-to-date, with the remaining 75% of long books up less than 1% — evidence of extreme crowding in the AI complex.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highMorgan Stanleysemiconductorshardwarepowerlong/short funds
So what: Returns this concentrated mean any bottleneck-driven stall in AI hardware hits the entire hedge-fund complex at once — elevated correlated drawdown risk.
“semis hardware and power have accounted for 97% of long short fund long returns year to date with the remaining 75% of long portfolio up less than 1% year-to date” ▶ 6:17
Visser flags Korea retail-mania signals — record stock-trading accounts, record margin, teenagers and family gatherings discussing surging shares — as classic peak behavior that has shifted the risk/reward against AI/memory longs even if the trend continues.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumKoreaKOSPImemoryretail investors
So what: Frothy retail participation in Korea is a sentiment caution flag for the global AI/memory trade; treat it as a reason to manage size, not to short outright.
“margin is at the highest level record ever. Those types of sayings are absolutely consistent with peaks in markets” ▶ 7:37
Visser's AI-specific 'turbulence model' fired seven turbulent signals in May — the most of any month — while his broad-market turbulence model still reads completely healthy, signaling stress is concentrated inside the AI complex rather than market-wide.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumAI stocksturbulence model
So what: Localized AI turbulence with a calm broad market argues for trimming AI beta specifically rather than de-risking the whole portfolio.
“I had seven turbulent signals in the month of May. We hadn't had any month that big.” ▶ 28:33
Bubble psychology & technical-discipline framework 5
Visser's core framework: never try to pick a bubble top — instead wait for the 20-day MA to cross below the 50-day after all-time highs and exit/stay-out once price breaks a down-turning 200-day MA, which historically (1929, 1987, 2000, 2008) lets you avoid the bulk of a crash while giving up only a small portion of gains.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: high20-day moving average50-day moving average200-day moving averageElliott Wave
So what: A rules-based MA-cross exit lets investors stay long parabolic AI names while capping downside — actionable risk management vs. binary bubble bets.
“I'm not going to bail out of anything or worry about a bubble until the 20-day moving average crosses the 50-day moving average.” ▶ 17:38
Visser argues crashes don't wipe you out in a day — bubbles build tops over time, so the danger is being bearish and out the entire way up; the asymmetry of bubble-calling (look brilliant if it crashes, claim 'bubbles go further' if it doesn't) makes it a socially low-risk but financially costly stance.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highbubblessentimentAndrew Ross Sorkin
So what: Discount pundit crash calls; the opportunity cost of sitting out a parabolic AI advance is larger than the avoided drawdown if you use MA stops.
“Bubble calling is asymmetrical. If prices crash, they look brilliant. If prices keep rising, they can say bubbles always go further than what people expect.” ▶ 22:58
Visser dismisses the Buffett indicator and GDP-based valuation metrics as obsolete because GDP was built for the industrial revolution and cannot measure intangibles or AI output, arguing expanding profit margins and rising earnings justify current prices.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBuffett indicatorGDPprofit marginsintangibles
So what: If you believe traditional valuation gauges are broken, market-cap/GDP 'bubble' warnings lose force — lean on price/earnings momentum instead.
“It does not handle intangibles. It does not handle what is going on in AI. It is a stupid thing to pay attention to as the only metric.” ▶ 20:48
Visser notes markets have already had three 20%+ corrections (two of 30%+) in the past three years with confidence notably low, arguing this is the opposite of classic bubble conditions where confidence is euphoric.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumcorrectionsmarket sentiment
So what: Recent repeated corrections plus low confidence undercut the 'unsustainable euphoria' bubble narrative; expect another ~20% correction but not a structural top.
“We've had three 20 plus% corrections. Two of them 30% or higher over the course of the last three years.” ▶ 21:19
Visser sees no systemic leverage to fuel a crash — credit spreads are benign and the main IG issuance is from cash-flush AI hyperscalers whose equity dwarfs their debt, so he is unworried about an AI-driven credit event near-term.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumcredit spreadsinvestment gradehyperscalersTorsten Slok
So what: Absent leverage and widening spreads, a 1929/2008-style cascade is unlikely; the risk is an equity correction, not a credit blowup — for now.
“right now they're just flush with cash and more importantly their equity is worth far more than the debt that they have” ▶ 19:32
Physical-world AI buildout & thematic basket 9
Visser's central trade is the 'benchmark arbitrage': underweight the hyperscalers/spenders and overweight the suppliers they pay — chemicals, industrials, power, optical and select semis — because capex-heavy spenders with rising receivables and debt are vulnerable to a buildout slowdown while the receivers benefit.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highhyperscalerschemicalsindustrialspoweropticalInstitutional Investor
So what: Long AI-supplier basket / short hyperscalers is positioned as both an outperformance trade and a hedge against a buildout stall.
“I don't see the spenders outperforming the receivers in any way, shape, or form except for consolidations and rotations.” ▶ 27:05
Visser cites Jensen Huang's $90 trillion AI buildout figure over the next 10-15 years — nearly 85% of today's global economy — spread across energy, chips and infrastructure, as the structural demand backstop for his physical-upgrade thesis.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highJensen HuangNvidiaAI buildoutenergychipsinfrastructure
So what: A multi-decade $90T buildout frames AI capex as secular, supporting 'first or second inning' positioning in suppliers despite near-term bottlenecks.
“It's a $90 trillion buildout. $90 trillion, which is almost 85% of the global economy today.” ▶ 11:58
Visser argues chemicals are replacing oil as the key intensity-versus-GDP input over the next five years and are critical to AI infrastructure, making them a core overweight in his thematic portfolio.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highchemicalsoilAI infrastructure
So what: Positions chemicals as a non-obvious AI-infrastructure play distinct from semis — a way to get AI exposure outside crowded chip names.
“chemicals, in my opinion, are replacing oil in terms of intensity versus GDP over the next 5 years” ▶ 27:26
Visser holds Dell as validation of the physical-upgrade thesis after it raised FY2027 EPS guidance to $18 from $12, lifted revenue guidance to $165-195B from $138-142B, beat by $8B and grew 88% y/y — arguing the parabolic chart is justified by fundamentals, not a bubble.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highDell
So what: Dell's guidance raise is concrete proof the AI hardware buildout is real-revenue driven; cited as a counter to chart-based bubble calls.
“Dell raised its fullear fiscal 2027 earnings guidance to $18 per share from 12.” ▶ 10:05
Visser cites Cisco's G2 Patel that AI raised projected enterprise WAN/network traffic growth from ~2.5x to ~9x over the next decade — a 4x repricing of the entire networking opportunity — which he calls the single statement explaining why optical names like Corning have gone parabolic.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highCiscoCorningoptical networkingWANagentic AIVera Rubin
So what: A 4x demand repricing of network traffic is the fundamental engine behind optical/fiber names — supports staying long optical despite Fuji Kura's bottleneck wobble.
“without AI was uh agentic AI was projected to grow about two and a half times over the next decade but now it's jumped to nine times” ▶ 40:46
Visser notes Goldman Sachs expects only 50-60% of data-center capacity scheduled for the next one-to-two years to actually come online, reinforcing the bottleneck/shortage thesis driving supplier earnings.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumGoldman Sachsdata centers
So what: Sub-60% capacity delivery means chronic shortage pricing power for suppliers but execution risk for hyperscaler ROI timelines.
“Only about 50 to 60% of data center capacity scheduled for the next one to two years is expected to come online.” ▶ 55:09
Visser flags a Goldman chart showing trailing-12-month capex dollar growth has hit 60% — the highest since the dot-com bubble — while growth-stock cash levels sit near 25-year lows, a combination he views as buildout intensity rather than a red flag.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumGoldman Sachscapexgrowth stocks
So what: Record capex growth plus low cash buffers raises fragility if revenue lags spend — the key thing to monitor for the buildout thesis to break.
“trailing 12-month capital spending dollar growth has reached 60% the highest reading since right before the do or right during the dotcom bubble” ▶ 40:09
Visser exited Micron around an average of ~$600-650 (after riding it from ~$100) and rotated into silver and Bitcoin, arguing that even though Micron rose another ~50% the risk/reward shifted because all three memory makers hit $1T market caps and the move (~1,000% in 15 months) is unlikely to repeat.
anecdote🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highMicronmemorysilverBitcoin
So what: A named CIO-style rotation out of crowded memory into silver/Bitcoin signals where he sees better forward risk/reward despite near-term underperformance.
“I do believe from here the riskreward has changed significantly.” ▶ 8:15
Visser is wary that memory's cyclicality may not be permanently 'fixed' — algorithmic efficiency gains, Chinese competition, or simply more supply could reassert the cycle even as bulls assume this time is different.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediummemoryMicronNvidiaChina
So what: Bet against the 'memory is now secular' consensus; watch for algorithmic/Chinese supply catalysts that could re-introduce the cycle and cap memory multiples.
“you got to bet that the memory situation is not going to be cyclical this time and that we're not going to be able to make more of it. I'm a little bit more wary about that situation.” ▶ 8:54
Macro: oil, inflation & power as the swing risk 4
Visser names rising oil as the most likely trigger for a 20-30% correction: he'd buy oil stocks into weakness as a hedge, arguing if oil pushes higher from here the broad market falls, and oil names stay cheap and under-owned around $80-85.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highoilenergy stocksExxonChevron
So what: Accumulate energy on dips as a cheap correction hedge; oil breaking higher is the macro tripwire to watch for the next equity drawdown.
“if oil goes higher, then the market's going to go down. I would be putting on oil into weakness here.” ▶ 53:57
Visser cites Exxon's and Chevron's super-majors warning that oil could hit $160 within weeks as global inventories approach record lows, with Chevron's Mike Wirth saying we're 'weeks away from oil shortages' — a tail risk amplified if Iran disrupts Strait of Hormuz flows to influence the midterms.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumExxonChevronMike WirthoilIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: A geopolitically-driven oil spike to $160 would simultaneously stoke inflation and pressure equities — the cleanest macro hedge case in the episode.
“We had Exxon and Chevron both out this week saying oil prices could hit 160 within weeks.” ▶ 53:15
Visser sees inflation re-accelerating as a genuine correction risk now that temporary buffers (tariff front-running, etc.) have been exhausted, with commodity prices rising and a new Fed chair entering — a setup that could force the Fed's hand.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediuminflationCPIFederal ReservecommoditiesMichael Cembalest
So what: Inflation reasserting after buffers run out is the macro fundamental that could justify a 20-30% pullback — watch CPI and commodity prints.
“We've gone through the temporary buffers” ▶ 52:57
Visser warns grid/power blackouts are a real risk — summer peak load passed 135 GW with reserves at only 4% — and an East Coast blackout would be bearish for stocks because it could force a pullback in AI usage.
causal🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumpower griddata centersAIelectricity
So what: Grid fragility is an under-appreciated AI-trade risk; a blackout headline could be the catalyst that forces AI-demand and equity de-rating.
“Summer heat drove peak load past 135 gigawatts with reserves at 4%.” ▶ 54:34
No-recession / compute-energy economy thesis 5
Visser maintains there are no recessions or business cycles in the modern economy because the world no longer depends on credit and GDP no longer measures real activity — a view he says he seeded with Raoul Pal.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highRaoul Palrecessionsbusiness cycleGDPcredit
So what: If business cycles are obsolete, traditional recession-hedging and cyclical timing frameworks misfire — stay structurally long secular AI growth.
“I think I was the first person to say there's no such thing as recessions” ▶ 35:12
Visser endorses Raoul Pal/Julien Bittel's framing that the economy is shifting from capital-and-labor dependency to compute-and-energy dependency — the structural reason he says business cycles no longer exist.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highRaoul PalJulien Bittelcomputeenergycapitallabor
So what: A compute/energy-driven economy reorients where alpha lives — toward compute, energy and infrastructure rather than traditional cyclicals.
“We're leaving a world that's been dependent on capital and labor and we're moving into a world that's entirely dependent on comput and energy.” ▶ 37:44
Visser endorses Torsten Slok's reframing of asset allocation as 'the new 60/40 is AI vs non-AI,' agreeing that single-factor over-exposure is the real risk and that most portfolios are dangerously underweight AI.
framework🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highTorsten Slok60/40AIfactor investing
So what: Reframe diversification around AI exposure; the index Visser built with Morgan Stanley exists to close advisors' AI underweight gap.
“The new 6040 is AI verse non AI.” ▶ 39:27
Visser argues AI will disrupt every traditional wealth-management asset class over the next five years — he sees VC, private credit, private equity and real estate all failing to come back — pushing advisors toward AI infrastructure, pharma and crypto for alpha.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highventure capitalprivate creditprivate equityreal estatewealth management
So what: A blunt call that legacy private-asset allocations underperform structurally — reposition toward AI infra, pharma and crypto/tokenization.
“I don't see VC coming back. I don't see PC coming back. I don't see PE coming back. I don't see real estate coming back.” ▶ 59:28
Visser highlights Mark Rowan's point that the AI buildout breaks the VC model because the VC ecosystem was never capital-intensive, whereas today's fast-growing AI labs need enormous capital and must tap debt markets and go public.
causal🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumMark Rowanventure capitalAnthropicdebt markets
So what: Capital-intensive AI shifts financing from VC to debt/public markets — bullish for credit issuance and IPO pipelines tied to AI infrastructure.
“you have an entire VC ecosystem that has never been capital intensive” ▶ 38:50
Software/SaaS not dead under agents 4
Visser relays Dan Shipper's (Lenny's Podcast) view that agents do not eliminate SaaS but change its surface — tools like Codex or 'Corowick' become the new operating layer where users work inside an AI environment that sees the browser instead of opening 10 separate SaaS apps.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumDan ShipperSaaSAI agentsCodex
So what: The agentic-AI-kills-SaaS short thesis is overstated; SaaS persists in a reconfigured form — nuance for software-sector positioning.
“agents do not eliminate SAS” ▶ 36:49
Visser cautions that despite heavy spend on Claude/AI licenses, companies are questioning whether soaring AI spending is delivering returns, and adoption-driven productivity will arrive in 'fits and starts' rather than smoothly — a reason to watch S&P margins ex-hardware.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumClaudeAnthropicenterprise AIprofit marginsS&P 500
So what: Lagging AI ROI means software/enterprise margin gains disappoint near-term; strip out hardware to see the true (weaker) margin picture.
“companies are starting to question whether soaring AI spending is delivering returns” ▶ 41:52
Visser notes DeepSeek made its 75% price discount permanent and that cheap, improving Chinese models are a structural pricing-power threat to hyperscalers even if Western firms hesitate to adopt them.
causal🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumDeepSeekChinahyperscalersAI pricing
So what: Chinese model price deflation pressures hyperscaler AI economics — a margin headwind reinforcing the underweight-spenders stance.
“Deepseek makes it 75% discount permanent.” ▶ 41:40
Visser cites Anthropic raising $65B this week at a ~$965B valuation with Brad Gerstner and Sequoia involved, arguing sophisticated late-stage investors at that level signal the AI revenue trajectory is real, not retail-driven.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumAnthropicBrad GerstnerSequoia
So what: A ~$965B Anthropic round backed by top funds is offered as evidence the AI buildout has institutional conviction — supports the secular thesis.
“Anthropic also raised 65 billion this week at a 965 billion.” ▶ 42:48
Pharma as the next AI-native winner 5
Visser argues AI is rebuilding drug discovery around foundational models and revaluing pharma's 'buried' failed-trial data and IP — letting companies take stalled-at-the-20-yard-line drugs to the goal line — making pharma a distinct human-software beneficiary rather than enterprise-productivity software.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highpharmaceuticalsAI drug discoveryfoundational modelsDeepMindGenesis Mission
So what: AI re-rates pharma's dormant IP; positions drug discovery as an under-owned AI vertical with M&A and revaluation upside.
“Failed data is still a great idea that now is useful.” ▶ 46:58
Visser holds Eli Lilly as the only pharma name in his thematic portfolio, citing 55% y/y revenue growth, a PEG below 1, new all-time highs, ~2x the performance of any Mag Seven name since end-2017, plus its 1,000-Blackwell 'Lily Pod' supercomputer and Nvidia partnership.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highEli LillyNvidiaBlackwellMounjaroOzempicMag Seven
So what: Eli Lilly is framed as an accelerating AI-native pharma compounder at a sub-1 PEG — a specific long combining GLP-1 growth and AI optionality.
“They have a PEG ratio below one.” ▶ 51:43
Visser highlights Eli Lilly's aggressive AI/gene-editing M&A — the $22M Engaged deal, a Bezos-backed Proffluent gene-editing partnership, ramped Insilico ties, a $1B Nvidia drug-discovery lab, and the Verve Therapeutics acquisition yielding early data on a one-time gene-editing cholesterol therapy.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumEli LillyProffluentInsilicoVerve Therapeuticsgene editingNvidia
So what: Lilly's gene-editing dealmaking targets the world's #1 killer (heart disease) with one-time cures — a concrete pipeline catalyst to track.
“They acquired Verve Therapeutics to advance one-time treatments for people with cardiovascular risk.” ▶ 49:10
Visser frames AI-driven drug discovery as a societal necessity because U.S. health spending is heading to ~20% of nominal GDP (~$6 trillion/year) and entitlement costs will exceed revenues unless AI makes people healthier — a structural tailwind for pharma AI adopters.
causal🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumhealthcare spendingGDPentitlementspharmaceuticals
So what: A ~$6T health market plus fiscal pressure forces AI adoption in pharma — sizes the addressable opportunity for AI-native drug companies.
“it's very quickly becoming 20% of nominal GDP in terms of health spending which means it is about $6 trillion a year” ▶ 50:43
Visser cites Gavin Baker (All-In) that AI's most powerful positive story is ordinary motivated people using LLMs to attack disease discovery directly — turning individuals into research teams and expanding (not eliminating) biotech, especially for rare diseases.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumGavin BakerbiotechLLMsrare diseases
So what: AI broadens biotech participation rather than commoditizing it — bullish breadth signal for the drug-discovery vertical.
“AI turns motivated humans into research teams.” ▶ 44:57
Crypto bear market but structural conviction 5
Visser declares Bitcoin and crypto are in a clear bear market — trading below a down-turning 200-day MA that rejects every rally, with nearly four years of unchanged price at the lows — and advises waiting to buy until price reclaims the 200-day rather than catching the falling knife.
prediction🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoincrypto200-day moving average
So what: Discipline over conviction: don't add to Bitcoin until it reclaims the 200-day; that reclaim is the trigger to deploy aggressively.
“That is the absolute definition of a bare market.” ▶ 32:51
Visser remains structurally bullish, calling Bitcoin 'the S&P 500 of the future of the capital system' and expecting it materially higher next year, driven by parabolic growth in stablecoins and tokenization that he says is just beginning.
thesis🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: highBitcoinstablecoinstokenization
So what: Frames Bitcoin as the benchmark asset of a tokenized future — a long-horizon accumulation thesis once the technical signal turns.
≈ “Bitcoin is basically the S&P 500 of the future of the capital system.” paraphrase
Visser flags bearish flow/sentiment in crypto — IBIT gave up all recent gains, Bitcoin ETFs posted the second-worst month ever for net flows, Ethereum ETH shares outstanding keep falling, and Polymarket odds on the Clarity Act passing fell from 75% back to 50% — explaining current non-participation.
data_point🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumIBITBitcoin ETFsEthereumClarity Act
So what: Record-bad ETF outflows plus stalled crypto legislation are concrete reasons crypto lags now — watch flows and Clarity Act odds for a turn.
“we posted the second worst month ever in terms of inflows versus outflows in the ETF” ▶ 58:15
Visser reads bearish macro commentators (Odd Lots-style) as a contrarian crypto-positive signal, noting their crypto 'capitulation' tone and willingness to call a possible low even as everything they cited — social instability, inflation, market manipulation — he interprets as pro-crypto.
contrarian🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: lowcryptoBitcoinmacro commentators
So what: Bearish-pundit capitulation is a soft contrarian buy signal for crypto — monitor sentiment extremes alongside the 200-day technical trigger.
“it felt more like a capitulation type thing” ▶ 56:10
Visser has built a tokenized 40-name (34 tokens + 6 public stocks) thematic crypto basket mirroring his AI approach and will launch dedicated crypto YouTube content this year, with the agentic-crypto sleeve up ~40% YTD.
anecdote🗣 Jordi Visserconviction: mediumtokenizationcrypto basketagentic AI
So what: Signals where Visser sees intra-crypto alpha (agentic/tokenization sleeves) and an upcoming product/content push around tokenization.
