The Ezra Klein Show 2 episode(s) · 38 insights

The Right’s Pre-Modern ‘Masculinist’ Fantasy | The Ezra Klein Show101 min9 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ezra Klein and Atlantic writer Helen Lewis dissect the 'masculinist' new right (Bronze Age Pervert, Doug Wilson, Scott Yenor, Helen Andrews) as the one unifying ideology of a fracturing MAGA coalition. Mostly cultural, but it carries a concrete policy agenda — pro-natalist tax/family policy, rollback of no-fault divorce, reversal of DEI into white-male protected-class claims — advanced via incremental administrative 'smuggling' rather than headline legislation. Useful second-order reads on fertility decline, aging-society political economy, and the funneling of young men into crypto/gambling/day-trading.
Pro-natalist family policy as fiscal agenda · 2Fertility decline & aging-society political economy · 2Young men funneled into speculative finance · 1Policy via administrative 'smuggling' · 2Markets, AVs, and culture-war fatigue · 2
Pro-natalist family policy as fiscal agenda 2
The Heritage Foundation published a January report calling for a 'Manhattan Project to support families,' favoring tax breaks for married single-earner households while opposing dating apps, daycare, and single-parent benefits — signaling a concrete pro-natalist tax/transfer agenda on the right.
data_point🗣 Helen Lewisconviction: highHeritage Foundationfiscal policytax policychildcare
So what: A real, institutionally backed push to regear the US tax code toward single-earner families would shift subsidies away from daycare/childcare providers and toward household tax credits — watch for child-tax-credit expansion, marriage-penalty changes, and reduced support for the paid-childcare sector.
“The Heritage Foundation put out a report in January that said they wanted a kind of Manhattan project to support families. They are against dating apps, um, daycare, you know, single parent benefits.” ▶ 1:23:31
Scott Yenor's 'family wage' proposal would legalize preferential hiring, promotion, and higher pay for married men — an explicit call to reintroduce gender discrimination into labor markets as policy.
thesis🗣 Helen Lewisconviction: mediumScott Yenorlabor marketwagesemployment law
So what: Even if unlegislated, normalizing 'family wage' rhetoric foreshadows looser enforcement of sex-discrimination law and a policy tilt in employment regulation worth tracking for labor-cost and litigation exposure.
Fertility decline & aging-society political economy 2
Few if any liberal democracies remain at or above replacement fertility (Israel cited as a possible exception), and a measurable multi-decade decline in male testosterone and sperm count across many countries is real — framed here as a demographic problem the left under-engages.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highfertility ratedemographicsIsrael
So what: Sustained sub-replacement fertility is a structural headwind for long-run labor supply, consumption, and entitlement solvency — a durable bull case for automation/productivity tech and a bear case for pay-as-you-go pension and healthcare systems.
“relatively uh few liberal democracies are now at a at replacement rate or above if any of them are. I think Israel is” ▶ 13:17
Developed economies are now governed around the social preferences of the baby-boomer cohort because they are the dominant demographic bloc, shaping policy in ways only beginning to be reckoned with.
framework🗣 Helen Lewisconviction: mediumdemographicsaging societybaby boomers
So what: An aging electorate biases policy toward asset-price protection, low-risk fiscal stances, and entitlement preservation over pro-growth/pro-young investment — a lens for anticipating housing, healthcare, and rates policy.
“at the moment, we live by the baby boomer's social like their social conditions that they find most amanable to them” ▶ 39:10
Young men funneled into speculative finance 1
Young men are being disproportionately funneled toward crypto, gambling, and day trading, and the majority of problem gamblers are men — framed as a gender-skewed monetization pattern driven by social-media platforms.
causal🗣 Helen Lewisconviction: highcryptocurrencyonline gamblingday tradingsocial media
So what: Confirms a structurally male, algorithm-fed demand pipeline for crypto, sports-betting, and retail trading apps — a tailwind for those platforms but also a regulatory and problem-gambling backlash risk to monitor.
“you get boys who are funneled towards crypto, gambling, day trading. You know, those things are are more heavily pedled to to men.” ▶ 42:25
Policy via administrative 'smuggling' 2
The US system is unusually friendly to minoritarian agendas: unpopular policies get advanced not by referendum or headline legislation but through incremental administrative tweaks — the model being the pre-Dobbs erosion of abortion access via state-house regulation and Project 2025 restrictions on abortion-pill distribution.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highProject 2025regulationDobbsadministrative state
So what: Policy risk in the US increasingly arrives through regulatory and state-level rule changes rather than visible federal laws — sectors (reproductive health, pharma, family/labor) should monitor agency rulemaking and state houses, not just Congress.
“it is very friendly to minoritarian ideas. It is easy to capture and for people who have got things that wouldn't pass a referendum to nonetheless smuggle them through” ▶ 1:29:17
The EEOC chair has solicited discrimination claims from white men ('Are you a white male who's experienced discrimination at work based on your race or sex'), signaling federal civil-rights enforcement is being repointed against DEI programs.
data_point🗣 Helen Lewisconviction: highEEOCDEIcivil rights lawemployment law
So what: A reversal of DEI enforcement into reverse-discrimination claims raises corporate legal exposure around diversity hiring programs and points to rising employment-litigation and compliance costs.
“Are you a white male who's experienced discrimination at work based on your race or sex? you may have a claim to recover money under federal civil rights laws. Contact the EOC as soon as possible.” ▶ 1:26:14
Markets, AVs, and culture-war fatigue 2
Klein argues the strongest opponents of autonomous vehicles and driverless trains are the incumbent labor groups they displace (taxi and train-driver unions), framing AV adoption as a labor-vs-automation political fight.
causal🗣 hostconviction: mediumWaymoautonomous vehicleslabor unionsautomation
So what: Names organized labor (taxi/transit unions) as the key political obstacle to AV rollout — a guide to where regulatory friction for Waymo and driverless transit will concentrate.
“Who hates Whimos, which I think are awesome having been to San Francisco recently? ... Who hates them more than taxi drivers unions? You know, who who hates driverless trains more than train drivers unions?” ▶ 1:18:36
Klein predicts running a culture-war playbook during an inflation shock with rising gas prices is a political loser — voters punish a focus on identity issues over cost-of-living, as Democrats learned in 2024.
prediction🗣 hostconviction: mediuminflationgas priceselectionsRepublicans
So what: Implies cost-of-living (inflation, energy prices) will dominate the next electoral cycle's economic policy debate over culture-war issues — a steer on which policy levers candidates will actually pull.
“Donald Trump in the middle of a huge inflation shock oncoming, gas price rising, going, I actually don't care about any of that.” ▶ 1:37:19
Ian Bremmer on the Risks America Poses to the World | The Ezra Klein Show90 min29 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ian Bremmer argues the US itself is now the principal driver of global geopolitical risk: Trump's Iran war has shut the Strait of Hormuz and could shave ~1.5% off global GDP, his April tariff push against China failed when Beijing weaponized rare-earth/critical-mineral chokeholds, and longer-term the US is ceding the 'electrostate' energy-infrastructure and clean-tech race to China while allies quietly redirect defense and trade spending away from the US.
Iran war: Strait of Hormuz shock and global GDP hit · 6US-China: tariff climbdown and rare-earth leverage · 5Energy: petrostate vs electrostate and the end of OPEC · 5US as the principal source of global geopolitical risk · 4Allies quietly diversify away from the US · 5Domestic stratification, AI divide and the closed-system advantage · 4
Iran war: Strait of Hormuz shock and global GDP hit 6
Iran has shut/tolled the Strait of Hormuz in response to the US-Israeli strikes, and a UN food-distribution estimate puts the resulting hit to global GDP next year at as much as 1.5%, concentrated outside the US.
data_point🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highIranStrait of Hormuzglobal GDPUnited Nations
So what: A sustained Hormuz closure is a live tail risk for global growth, energy, fertilizer and food prices; watch the strait's status as the key macro toggle.
“the impact on the global GDP uh next year uh could be as much as 1 and a.5%” ▶ 1:12:53
Because the US is a major oil producer and exporter, it is far less exposed to the Hormuz shock than oil-importing economies; Asian petrochemical/plastics producers, the Philippines, and sub-Saharan African importers face the most acute distress.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highUnited StatesAsiapetrochemicalsplasticsPhilippinesSub-Saharan Africaoil
So what: Trade the divergence: US energy producers relatively insulated, while EM oil importers and Asian chemical/plastics names bear the price spike and rationing.
A fertilizer supply crunch from the Iran war has already missed the planting season, so a food-price shock is coming with a lag once this growing season's harvests fail to materialize, with the global south most at risk of being priced out.
prediction🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highfertilizerfoodagricultureglobal south
So what: Lagged upside risk to agricultural commodity and fertilizer prices; the food leg of this shock has not yet been priced.
The deal currently on the table would unfreeze Iranian assets via Qatar in a lump sum in exchange for reopening the strait and lifting the US blockade, with the nuclear file negotiated afterward, leaving Iran arguably geopolitically stronger than before the war.
thesis🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highIranQatarTrumpStrait of Hormuz
So what: Any settlement likely requires the US to undo its own war goals; an off-ramp that releases frozen Iranian cash is the base case for de-escalation and lower oil.
Trump is desperately seeking a credible off-ramp from the Iran war he cannot exit cleanly, which Bremmer calls the biggest US foreign-policy mistake since the Iraq war.
contrarian🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highTrumpIranIraq war
So what: Signals the war is a policy liability the administration wants ended, biasing toward eventual de-escalation despite tough rhetoric.
“It is by far the biggest foreign policy mistake of the Trump administration. I frankly of any administration since the Iraq war.” ▶ 1:07:24
Within the Gulf, Saudi Arabia is relatively insulated because it moves ~7 million barrels/day via its east-west pipeline through the Red Sea, bypassing Hormuz, while Kuwait and Qatar are stranded if the strait stays closed.
data_point🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highSaudi ArabiaKuwaitQatarRed SeaStrait of Hormuzoil
So what: Differentiates Gulf producers' exposure and explains divergent Saudi vs UAE/Israel war stances; pipeline routing is the key variable.
US-China: tariff climbdown and rare-earth leverage 5
Trump's April 2nd 'Liberation Day' tariff strategy to force China to capitulate failed when Beijing retaliated with reciprocal tariffs and then required export licenses for critical minerals and rare earths, threatening to shut down US industrial production.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highChinaTrumptariffsrare earthscritical minerals
So what: China's mineral chokehold is its decisive counter-leverage; supply-chain dependent US manufacturers and defense are the vulnerable link.
US CEOs went to Mar-a-Lago warning that factory floors would shut down without rare-earth access, forcing the administration into a complete trade climbdown and a constructive-dialogue summit Beijing views as a historic win.
anecdote🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highChinaTrumprare earthsmanufacturing
So what: Confirms US negotiating leverage over China has shifted toward Beijing; the summit cements a G2 dynamic, not a containment win.
Having seen China's rare-earth/critical-mineral leverage, the US (via Pentagon investment in producers in the US, Chile and Brazil) will likely neutralize that chokehold within 5 to 10 years, after which the one-time leverage cannot be used again.
prediction🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: mediumUnited StatesPentagonChileBrazilcritical mineralsrare earths
So what: Multi-year tailwind for ex-China critical-mineral and rare-earth miners/processors backed by US strategic investment.
Bremmer argues Nvidia's push to sell H200 chips to China serves only Nvidia and not US policy, since once the Biden export controls signaled a 'cold war on semiconductors,' China is already racing to build domestic chips and selling them just lets China catch up.
contrarian🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highNvidiaH200ChinaHuaweisemiconductorsBiden
So what: Frames Nvidia-China sales as misaligned with national strategy; policy risk that export access gets re-tightened, and a tell that China chip self-sufficiency is the base case.
“Nvidia is pressing an issue that is of sole interest to Nvidia. It is of no interest whatsoever to the country.” ▶ 1:03:13
Chinese AI firms say compute is their binding constraint, not talent or energy, and Deep Seek surprised Silicon Valley on how small the US-China model gap is, implying the US lead in AI is roughly 6 months.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumChinaDeep SeekcomputeAINvidiaSilicon Valley
So what: A narrow, shrinking US AI lead raises the strategic value of compute/chip access controls and the risk premium on assuming durable US AI dominance.
Energy: petrostate vs electrostate and the end of OPEC 5
The US is now the world's largest oil producer (more than Saudi Arabia) while China is becoming the dominant 'electrostate,' with China's exports of green-energy technology now larger than US oil-and-gas exports.
data_point🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highUnited StatesChinaSaudi Arabiaoilsolargreen energy
So what: Frames the core energy-competition divergence: US doubling down on 20th-century fuel exports, China owning the 21st-century clean-tech build-out and its export infrastructure.
China controls the solar-cell and clean-energy-infrastructure supply chain that any country needs to become an electrostate, giving it the same kind of growth-gating leverage over others' economies that it holds via critical minerals.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highChinasolar cellsclean energyAIcompute
So what: Solar/clean-tech supply chain is a strategic chokepoint like rare earths; reshoring it is a multi-year policy and investment theme.
The Iran war is effectively ending OPEC as a cartel: the UAE left OPEC during the war, reflecting Gulf producers' rush to monetize 'stranded' oil reserves fast before demand erodes.
thesis🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highOPECUAEIranoil
So what: Cartel breakdown plus stranded-asset selling pressure is structurally bearish for long-run oil prices once the acute supply shock passes.
“OPEC is over. Uh, the UAE left OPEC in the middle of the Iran war” ▶ 55:30
Bremmer contends the Iran war and OPEC's demise will accelerate the global shift to post-carbon energy (EVs, solar, wind, nuclear), making Trump inadvertently the leader who did the most after China to speed that transition.
prediction🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: mediumelectric vehiclessolarwindnuclearTrumpChinaoil
So what: Geopolitical fragility of oil chokepoints is a structural tailwind for clean-energy and EV adoption regardless of US policy hostility to them.
Texas is simultaneously the top US producer of both renewable energy and petroleum, modeling the 'do both' energy posture Bremmer says the US should pursue rather than Trump's hostility to wind, solar and EVs.
data_point🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highTexasrenewable energyoilsolarwind
So what: State-level renewable build-out continues despite federal hostility; Texas is the bellwether for US clean-energy capacity additions.
US as the principal source of global geopolitical risk 4
Bremmer's central thesis is that the United States, not China, is now the principal driver of geopolitical uncertainty worldwide via tariffs, industrial policy, the Iran war, unpredictability toward Europe, and rewriting the rules of the world's largest market.
thesis🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highUnited StatesChinaTrumptariffsEurope
So what: Reframes the geopolitical risk premium as US-originated and policy-driven, meaning US political decisions are the key swing factor for global markets.
“The United States is the principal driver of geopolitical uncertainty in the world today.” ▶ 1:26:07
Bremmer characterizes Trump as having 'a big punch but also a glass jaw'—able to escalate but unable to absorb retaliation—citing both the China tariff climbdown and the Iran Hormuz reversal.
framework🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highTrumpChinaIran
So what: A model for trading Trump-driven escalations: initial shock followed by climbdown when counterparties retaliate hard, favoring fading the most aggressive threats.
“He has a big punch but also a glass jaw. Can't take one hard from the other from another side.” ▶ 49:38
Bremmer rejects the Thucydides Trap framing and instead invokes a 'Gracchi Trap'—internal political dysfunction and norm-breaking that makes allies see the US as undependable and erodes the system from within, as happened to the Roman Republic.
framework🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highUnited StatesChinaRome
So what: Positions US internal political instability—not US-China war—as the dominant long-run risk to the US-led order and its asset-price implications.
Bremmer told Latin American CEOs and Dominican Republic President Abinader (approval down from ~70 to ~55 on inflation) to blame Trump's Iran war, not their own governments, for the inflation spike across import-dependent economies.
anecdote🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highDominican RepublicAbinaderLatin AmericaTrumpinflation
So what: Illustrates the political and inflation contagion of the oil shock into EM, raising sovereign and approval-rating instability in import-dependent states.
Allies quietly diversify away from the US 5
European, Polish, German and Canadian defense spending is rising sharply but is being directed into domestic and European military-industrial build-out rather than US contractors, removing money that historically flowed into US defense jobs.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highEuropePolandGermanyCanadaNATOdefense
So what: Structural headwind for US defense primes and tailwind for European defense champions as allied procurement reshores away from America.
India is building out its military and shifting procurement away from Russia toward the US and increasingly Europe, with legacy-system parts/service/training contracts that lock in spending away from the US for decades.
prediction🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: mediumIndiaRussiaEuropeUnited Statesdefense
So what: India's diversification is a multi-decade reallocation of defense procurement that European suppliers increasingly capture.
The newly concluded EU-Mercosur trade agreement (which Bremmer credits Trump's unilateral tariffs with catalyzing) will lower tariffs and divert Latin America-Europe trade flows away from the United States.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highEUMercosurLatin AmericaTrumptrade
So what: Trade is rerouting around the US; cumulatively these deals erode US indispensability and trade-flow share over time.
China's one booming sector is its export of manufactured goods—a dumping strategy that is genuinely hollowing out European industries—even as European mistrust of the US does not translate into trust of China.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highChinaEuropemanufacturingtrade
So what: Chinese export dumping is a live margin/volume threat to European industrials and a driver of EU trade-defense and tariff action.
Mistrust of the US is not pushing allies into China's arms in a bloc sense; Japan is in an active dispute with China over Taiwan (China cutting Japanese tourism and seafood imports), keeping it close to a US it nonetheless finds 'extortionate.'
contrarian🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highJapanChinaTaiwanUnited States
So what: The world is fragmenting into complexity, not two clean cold-war blocs; pair-wise frictions (Japan-China) matter more than a single alignment axis.
Domestic stratification, AI divide and the closed-system advantage 4
Bremmer argues his 2006 'J-curve' is now a 'U': top-down, surveillance and algorithmic technology (including AI) now structurally advantages closed societies like China and undermines open ones, reversing the 2006 internet dynamic.
framework🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highChinaUnited StatesAItechnology
So what: If closed systems gain a durable stability/tech edge, it reprices long-run political-stability assumptions embedded in DM vs authoritarian-state assets.
Bremmer and Klein warn of an AI divide where the wealthy get powerful curated AI agents while everyone else gets manipulative, ad-supported, hallucinating models, arguing this makes data-center moratoria counterproductive because they raise already-scarce compute costs.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumAIdata centerscompute
So what: Compute scarcity is a binding constraint and a bull case for data-center/compute buildout; moratoria are a policy risk that would tighten supply further.
China is deliberately steering AI toward defense, industrial and strategic-sector integration rather than consumer LLMs, distrusting the TikTok-style attention model and letting Trump take TikTok rather than make other concessions.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highChinaAITikTokTrumpdefense
So what: China's AI bet is on industrial/military productivity, implying a different (and potentially more economically productive) AI deployment path than US consumer-LLM focus.
Bremmer ties US grievance politics to collapsed class mobility: the US had top-of-OECD mobility in the 1970s-80s and is now less mobile than Europe or Canada, driven by hollowed-out 'places' plus 'gated' superstar cities that block housing supply.
causal🗣 Ian Bremmerconviction: highUnited StatesEuropeCanadaOECDhousing
So what: Structural mobility collapse underpins durable populist/grievance politics, sustaining the policy unpredictability that drives the US-originated risk premium.

Yardeni Research · Ed Yardeni 1 episode(s) · 38 insights

Fed Turning Hawkish35 min38 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ed Yardeni argues the Fed will drop its easing bias at the June FOMC and may jump straight to a tightening bias, with an outlier call for an eventual rate hike, driven by firming labor data and an oil shock; yet he holds an 8,250 S&P 500 year-end target on 'fabulous earnings momentum,' advises an energy overweight as a geopolitical hedge, and flags the SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI IPO wave as potentially 'rigged for success' via rule changes forcing index and retail buying.
Fed pivot to hawkish · 7Inflation, oil shock and the dual mandate · 5FEMO earnings-led melt-up · 8Positioning: energy overweight, stay-home, caution · 6SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI IPO wave · 4Rates, credit and gold cross-asset · 6Structural economy: labor share and boomer wealth · 2
Fed pivot to hawkish 7
Yardeni expects the Fed to drop its easing bias at the June 17 FOMC statement, citing April FOMC minutes that read as hawkish (three dissenters wanted the easing bias dropped completely) despite Powell's dovish framing of them.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highFederal ReserveFOMCJerome Powell
So what: A confirmed removal of the easing bias would reprice front-end rates and pressure rate-cut bets; watch the June 17 statement and presser.
“I think it's pretty clear that the easing bias will be dropped at the June, uh, in the June statement.” ▶ 8:16
Yardeni thinks the Fed could skip a neutral bias and jump straight to a tightening bias at the June meeting, with the question being whether they sit on it into July or act on it.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumFederal ReserveFOMC
So what: A direct move to a tightening bias is more hawkish than markets price; a hawkish surprise risk for risk assets into late June.
“I I think, uh, they could jump, uh, straight to a tightening, uh, bias.” ▶ 9:05
Yardeni's outlier view is a likely Fed rate hike, probably a quarter point, that markets are not currently expecting, motivated partly by the Fed's wounded credibility from being late to tighten in 2021-early 2022.
contrarian🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumFederal ReserveFOMC
So what: An out-of-consensus hike call; if it materializes it could trigger a buying-opportunity dip rather than a sustained selloff per Yardeni.
“rate cuts are definitely off the table. Uh a tightening bias is very likely at the June meeting coming up. And then um uh a likely rate hike uh uh that that's our outlier view” ▶ 23:12
Yardeni expects a July hike only if the Fed first moves incrementally, telegraphing the possibility of a hike through speeches before acting.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumFederal ReserveFOMC
So what: Watch Fed speakers this week and after June for hike-signaling language as the tell on July timing.
“I think they would want to more incrementally uh to to to move more incrementally towards hiking. If that's their plan, which would includes starting to discuss the possibility of a hike in various speeches.” ▶ 30:57
Yardeni expects Kevin Warsh, who has signaled he may be less communicative than Powell, to nonetheless hold a press conference at the June meeting laying out his view.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: lowKevin WarshFederal ReserveJerome Powell
So what: A Warsh-led FOMC implies a more hawkish reaction function; his communication style is a new variable for rate volatility.
Yardeni argues a Fed rate hike cannot resolve an Iran-driven oil/inflation shock or a bond selloff, but the Fed may tighten anyway out of concern about financial stability and speculative excesses if the market keeps melting up and SpaceX 'goes to the moon.'
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumFederal ReserveSpaceXIran
So what: Frames a hike as a lean-against-bubbles move rather than an inflation cure; speculative froth itself becomes a tightening trigger.
“I think the the the Fed has to be concerned about the potential of uh them being uh not restrictive enough when it comes to financial stability and speculative excesses.” ▶ 27:42
Yardeni concedes the Fed may matter less than it thinks, noting that hindsight suggests hiking 3-6 months earlier in 2021-2022 would have made little difference given the pandemic-driven nature of that inflation.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: lowFederal Reserve
So what: A reminder to weight supply-side/geopolitical drivers over Fed policy when judging the inflation path.
“maybe the Fed doesn't matter as much as they think they matter.” ▶ 28:03
Inflation, oil shock and the dual mandate 5
Yardeni sees the dual-mandate risk balance shifting: employment-side risks are diminished while inflation-side risks are increased, leading him to conclude the Fed could issue a tightening bias in June.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highFederal Reserveinflationlabor market
So what: A firming labor market removes the dovish offset, clearing the path for a hawkish Fed; jobs data surprises now cut hawkish.
“the the risks are diminished on the employment side. The risks are increased on the on the inflation side.” ▶ 12:24
Oil prices are up roughly 6-7% on the day as Iran refuses to exchange notes with the US on extending the Gaza-area ceasefire and threatens to shut the Strait of Hormuz, making the Fed more likely to treat the oil shock as persistent rather than transitory by FOMC time.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highcrude oilIranStrait of HormuzIsraelHezbollahLebanon
So what: A persistent oil shock is the swing factor pushing the Fed hawkish; track Hormuz/ceasefire headlines into June 17.
“the oil prices are up about 6 7%.” ▶ 9:26
Exxon and Chevron have both warned that global oil inventories are running 'bone dry,' which Yardeni says could drive an oil price spike to $150.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumExxonChevroncrude oil
So what: A tight-inventory backdrop amplifies geopolitical oil upside; supports energy exposure and a higher-inflation tail.
“Exxon and Chevron have been both warning that the inventories are running bone dry Uh around the world and that could lead to a spike in oil prices up to $150.” ▶ 11:27
Yardeni warns the Fed could conclude it faces not just an energy problem but an energy shock with potential to spread into a broader list of prices, ahead of the CPI, PPI and employment reports that precede the June FOMC.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediuminflationCPIPPIFederal Reserve
So what: Watch whether the pre-FOMC CPI/PPI show oil bleeding into core; broadening price pressure is what converts a bias to a hike.
“they've got not just an energy problem but an energy problem that has a potential to spread into a broader list of prices.” ▶ 11:46
Yardeni expects May labor-market data to surprise to the upside, noting March and April were stronger than expected and all major labor indicators point to continued strength.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highlabor marketemployment
So what: A hot May jobs report would reinforce the hawkish-Fed thesis; position for upside-surprise risk in payrolls.
“labor market's kind of been going our way. It's we've been saying that it could surprise people to the upside” ▶ 12:06
FEMO earnings-led melt-up 8
Yardeni maintains an 8,250 S&P 500 year-end target, framing the rally as an earnings-led 'melt-up' (his 'FEMO' / fabulous earnings momentum) rather than a valuation-driven 'FOMO' bubble.
thesis🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500
So what: An above-market year-end target anchored to earnings, not multiples; the bull case survives a hawkish Fed but breaks on recession.
≈ “Still though hanging on to 8,250 S&P 500 by by year end.” paraphrase
Q1 earnings season is finishing around 19% growth, with double-digit growth rates being raised across the board all the way through Q4, driven heavily by AI and semiconductor companies beating expectations.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500AIsemiconductors
So what: Concrete earnings-momentum evidence underpinning the melt-up; rising forward estimates, not multiple expansion, are doing the work.
“19% is the way we're uh Now, ending up uh the Q1 earning season.” ▶ 17:11
S&P 500 forward earnings keep hitting record highs and will converge by year-end toward analysts' rising 2027 estimates, with information-technology 2026 and 2027 estimates both climbing.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500information technology
So what: The time-weighted roll to 2027 numbers mechanically lifts forward EPS, supporting the index even if multiples hold.
Tech-sector forward earnings have been so strong that the tech forward P/E is now only modestly above the overall market, versus a prior tech forward P/E near 40 against a much lower market multiple.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highinformation technologyS&P 500
So what: Earnings catch-up has de-risked tech valuations relative to history, a key plank of the not-irrational-exuberance case.
“the forward PE was was 40 for the technology sector and high but much lower for the rest of the market.” ▶ 19:52
Yardeni says the recession scenario is the key risk that would 'clobber' the bull case on both P/E and E, collapsing the FEMO thesis and bringing the ~21x S&P 500 (23-24x tech) multiple down.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500information technology
So what: Defines the binary: no recession = melt-up continues; recession = bear market via simultaneous P/E and E hits. Monitor recession signals above all.
“but bear markets is what you get when you have a recession. Because you get clobbered on both PE and E.” ▶ 2:58
Yardeni notes breadth is improving, with small-cap and mid-cap forward earnings breaking out to record highs after a prolonged 'coma,' and the equal-weighted S&P 500 also making a new record high.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumsmall capsmid capsS&P 500 equal weight
So what: Broadening earnings breadth supports rally durability beyond mega-cap tech; constructive for small/mid-cap exposure.
Yardeni reads bull-bear sentiment as neutral, which he interprets as leaving more upside in the bull run rather than signaling a near-term top.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumsentiment
So what: Lack of euphoria is a contrarian positive; a stretched bull-bear ratio at highs would be the warning he's not yet seeing.
“not not too many bulls, not too many bears. That suggests that there's more upside from a sentiment perspective” ▶ 20:06
The S&P 500 sits about 11% above its 200-day moving average, with technology, materials, energy, services and some industrials the most extended areas.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumS&P 500technologymaterialsenergyindustrials
So what: Flags near-term stretch and pullback vulnerability in the most-extended sectors despite the bullish secular call.
≈ “the market is a bit extended here at 11% for the S&P 500 above its 200-day.” paraphrase
Positioning: energy overweight, stay-home, caution 6
Yardeni is sticking with an energy-sector overweight as a hedge against current geopolitical risks, viewing it as a reasonable place to be given the Iran/oil backdrop.
thesis🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highenergy sectorIrancrude oil

“we're we've kind of stuck with our energy overweight and kind of thinking about that's not a bad place to be kind of on a hedging basis for the geopolitical issues” ▶ 13:28
Yardeni advises near-term caution, expecting June to get choppy as markets juggle oil prices, the FOMC, and the SpaceX IPO.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumcrude oilFOMCSpaceX

“I'm inclined to think things could get a little choppy here.” ▶ 13:23
Yardeni favors 'stay-home' US assets over 'go-global' for now, arguing global/emerging-markets outperformance won't work until the war is over and oil falls, and that the ex-US trade is really just a South Korea and Taiwan play.
thesis🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highUnited StatesSouth KoreaTaiwanemerging marketsEMXC

“until the war's over at the a stay home is going to outperform for a while for a while longer.” ▶ 30:41
Yardeni argues emerging markets will not appreciate a Fed moving toward a tightening bias or an actual hike, adding a second-order headwind to ex-US equities.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumemerging marketsFederal Reserve
So what: A hawkish-Fed scenario compounds the case against EM; links his rate call to his stay-home positioning.
“Emerging markets are not going to uh enjoy seeing the Fed move towards a tightening bias, and they certainly aren't going to appreciate a Fed rate hike.” ▶ 30:22
Yardeni believes the market can absorb a Fed hike, expecting any dip to be another buying opportunity as investors conclude a quarter or 50 bps makes little difference against a resilient economy.
thesis🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500Federal Reserve
So what: Reconciles his hawkish-Fed and 8,250 bull calls: treat a hike-driven selloff as a dip to buy, not a regime change.
“it could present another one of these uh buying opportunities uh because people will say what difference does a quarter or 50 basis points make and the economy will continue to to show its resilience.” ▶ 31:28
Yardeni flags Middle East data centers and Persian Gulf undersea telecom cables as physically vulnerable to the conflict, reinforcing a US-asset premium while the war continues.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: lowdata centersMiddle EasttelecomUnited States
So what: Geopolitical infrastructure risk argues for a US-located premium in AI/data-center exposure.
“Data centers in the Middle East look somewhat vulnerable. So do the uh telecom cables under the strait running in the Persian Gulf look vulnerable.” ▶ 29:24
SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI IPO wave 4
Yardeni estimates SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI could carry a combined public market cap of $4-5 trillion once trading, while actually raising only about $250-300 billion across the three.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Sizes a massive new supply of mega-cap AI/space equity; the gap between market cap and cash raised shapes the liquidity question.
“the market cap could be somewhere between $4 and $5 trillion.” ▶ 4:25
Yardeni suggests the IPOs may be 'rigged for success': exchanges changed rules to ease public trading and index inclusion, forcing index funds to buy, while brokers cold-call retail (including Yardeni himself) to participate amid a small SpaceX float.
contrarian🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAISECindex funds
So what: Forced index buying plus a small float can drive sharp initial upside; flags structural demand but also rigged-pricing risk for late retail buyers.
“are these things being rigged for success? I mean, certainly look looks that way.” ▶ 5:51
The SpaceX IPO is scheduled for June 12, just five days before the June 17 FOMC statement, clustering two major catalysts in mid-June.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highSpaceXFOMC
So what: Concrete event calendar: position for a high-event-density mid-June window combining the IPO and a hawkish Fed.
“SpaceX is scheduled for June 12th.” ▶ 6:56
Yardeni warns the small SpaceX float and retail offer could see early gains met by daily retail selling, making post-IPO price action unpredictable.
prediction🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: lowSpaceXretail investors
So what: Caution on chasing the IPO pop; retail distribution could cap or reverse initial upside.
“Question is whether that that'll may be met by a daily selling by selling by by retail.” ▶ 6:49
Rates, credit and gold cross-asset 6
Yardeni views a 10-year Treasury yield range of roughly 4.25-4.75% (broadly 4-5%) as a normal, range-bound bond market consistent with a healthy economy, not the 'crashing' bond market headlines feared.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: high10-year Treasurybond market

“we kind of view 4 to 5% really 4 and 1/4 to 4 and 3/4% is kind of the the range-bound bond market that is consistent with with normal.” ▶ 20:58
Yardeni notes the US is running roughly $2 trillion deficits (stated as '200 billion'), citing fiscal stimulus as a reason the economy has stayed resilient and bond yields are elevated.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumUS fiscal deficitbond marketTreasury
So what: Fiscal support is part of why the economy passes stress tests; also a structural floor under yields.
“We are running 200 billion dollar deficits.” ▶ 21:34
Yardeni's long-run framework sees a rotation into gold and bonds via rebalancing as the S&P 500 climbs toward 10,000 by the end of the decade and investors grow nervous and de-risk.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumgoldbondsS&P 500
So what: Rebalancing flows are a structural long-term bid for gold and bonds even in a bull-equity scenario.
“they'll rebalance into bonds and they'll rebalance into gold.” ▶ 34:27
Yardeni notes gold's recent driver has shifted: prior central-bank buying has given way to some central banks selling dollar and gold reserves to defend their currencies amid the war, and a re-escalation would likely push gold down rather than up.
causal🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: lowgoldcentral banks

“the war has caused some central banks to have to scramble to uh support their currency and they've been doing it by selling dollars uh reserves and and gold reserves.” ▶ 33:50
Margin buying is at a record high, which Yardeni says does not itself cause a bear market but would make any future bear market more severe.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediummargin debt
So what: Record leverage is an amplifier, not a trigger; raises the downside tail if a recession or shock hits.
“It's at a record high margin buying. The margin buying only tells you that the bear market could be more severe” ▶ 34:35
Yardeni flags consensus long-term earnings growth expectations (LTEG) for the S&P 500 at about 22% per year over the next 3-5 years, a record ex-pandemic, which he calls unrealistic versus a more realistic 10-15%.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highS&P 500LTEG

“long-term earnings growth expectations by analysts on a consensus basis is up to like 22% per year over the next 3 to 5 years” ▶ 32:28
Structural economy: labor share and boomer wealth 2
Yardeni confirms labor's share of national income has fallen to a record-low 53.8%, down from 65% in 1978, the flip side of strong corporate margins driving FEMO.
data_point🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: highlabor sharenational incomecorporate margins
So what: Margin expansion at labor's expense is structurally bullish for earnings but a political/economic fault line to watch.
“the labor mark labor share in uh national income is down. Uh in 1978 was 65% of GDP.” ▶ 24:05
Yardeni argues the consumer economy's resilience is driven by baby-boomer demographics: ~$89 trillion of boomer net worth, retirees spending down while they still can, plus ~30% of unmarried young adults living at home and ~80% receiving parental financial support.
framework🗣 Ed Yardeniconviction: mediumbaby boomersconsumer spendingdemographics
So what: Boomer wealth transfer and spending explains the unusually resilient consumer; a structurally bullish demand backdrop independent of wages.
“what you're not mentioning is the 89 trillion dollars of net worth of the baby boomers” ▶ 26:20

The Defiant 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

DeFi Hacks Happening Every Day; Institutions Are Still Coming57 min37 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Defiant panel with Kraken (John Settler), Veda (Sun Raghupathi) and Centrifuge/Centura (Anthony D. Martino) argues the recent ~$1.1B/12mo of DeFi losses are mostly key-management/supply-chain failures rather than smart-contract bugs, so risk is migrating from the contract layer to the operational layer. They make the case that pooled lending (Aave) survives because of balance-sheet backstops, that vault 'risk curation' needs disclosure and insurance, and that despite gloomy crypto price action, enterprise/fintech adoption of yield-bearing DeFi is accelerating, not slowing.
Hack post-mortem: supply-chain vs smart-contract risk · 5Risk migrates to the operational layer · 6Composability: from cure-all to managed liability · 3Future of on-chain lending: pooled vs isolated · 3Vault business model, curation and incentives · 6Kraken Bitcoin Vault product mechanics · 5Institutional/enterprise adoption accelerating · 5Macro/regulatory tailwinds and the fundamentals-vs-price divergence · 4
Hack post-mortem: supply-chain vs smart-contract risk 5
The recent wave of DeFi losses (~$630M in April, ~$1.1B over the trailing 12 months) is dominated by supply-chain/key-compromise attacks (e.g., Bybit) rather than smart-contract vulnerabilities, where an individual insider is phished via a malicious PDF rather than the contract code being broken.
framework🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highBybitDeFiKrakenNorth Korea/Lazarus
So what: Distinguishing operational from contract risk reframes where due diligence and capital should go — audit-clean protocols can still be drained via human/key failure, so investors should price operational controls (multisig, time-locks) not just audit status.
“a number of the attacks, the bybit one... a lot of these have been supply chain attacks versus actually smart contract vulnerabilities. I think that's a really important distinction.” ▶ 1:59
OpenZeppelin co-founder Manuel Aroz publicly recommended exiting DeFi entirely, but the firm OpenZeppelin distanced itself from its former founder's claim — a notable signal because OpenZeppelin audited nearly all of DeFi's code.
data_point🗣 Cam Russo (host) / John Settlerconviction: mediumOpenZeppelinManuel ArozDeFi
So what: A capitulation call from a top auditor's co-founder is a sentiment marker near a cycle low; the firm's pushback suggests the bearish take is contested, not consensus.
“the co-founder of Open Zeppelin, Manuel Ara, said that he recommends getting out of DeFi entirely. This is the guy who co-founded the auditing firm that's reviewed almost all of DeFi code.” ▶ 0:41
The LayerZero/Kelp DAO incident — where a counterfeit rsETH was created via a LayerZero DVN setup, posted as collateral, and real ETH was drained from Aave — is the clearest recent example of an actual smart-contract-layer exploit, but DeFi participants coordinated to make users whole.
anecdote🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highLayerZeroKelp DAOrsETHAaveETH
So what: Cross-protocol DVN/oracle trust assumptions are a live attack surface; the fact that recovery hinged on Aave's backstop (not code) is the key tell for where systemic safety actually resides.
“this kind of counterfeit RSE was created, posted as collateral, and then real we was stolen from the A protocol” ▶ 2:56
Frontier AI models (referencing an 'Mythos'/Anthropic-class model said to be finding vulnerabilities) are likely being used offensively for exploit discovery, and North Korea/Lazarus is suspected of deploying a similar advanced model to find code vulnerabilities, since crypto theft is among North Korea's largest economic imports.
thesis🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: mediumAIAnthropicMythosNorth Korea/Lazarus
So what: AI-accelerated offense raises the baseline exploit rate and shortens the time-to-attack on weak protocols — a structural tax on under-audited, under-secured DeFi that favors scale incumbents.
“Lazarus North Korea is probably using something similar to a mythos model... stealing money from crypto investors is seems to be one of the biggest imports of the North Korean economy” ▶ 9:52
Host's contrarian pushback: framing an exploit as 'only a supply-chain or private-key issue, not a smart-contract hack' does not reduce investor risk, because a safe contract surrounded by unsafe off-chain configuration still loses user funds.
contrarian🗣 Cam Russo (host)conviction: highDeFi
So what: Investors should treat the 'it wasn't the contract' defense as a red flag, not reassurance; total system risk = contract risk + operational/key risk, and the latter is now the bigger driver.
“it's like, okay, great, your smart contract is safe, but all the rest isn't. So... I'm not very reassured when I hear those types of arguments” ▶ 8:09
Risk migrates to the operational layer 6
DeFi risk is structurally moving from the smart-contract layer to the operational layer, so the industry's next priority must be establishing standards (akin to SOC 2 in cybersecurity) for which roles and keys control user funds, with full transparency to depositors.
thesis🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highVedaDeFiSOC 2
So what: Protocols that publish role/key configurations and operational-security disclosures will earn a trust premium; this becomes a differentiator and a screen for allocators.
“Risk is now moving from the smart contract layer into the operational layer. And that is not trivial” ▶ 15:45
Many of the past year's 'exploits' stem from a highly-permissioned role being held by an externally-owned account (EOA) rather than a multisig gated by a time-lock — a basic, solvable design failure rather than a code break.
causal🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highEOAmultisigtime-lockVeda
So what: A concrete, checkable due-diligence test: verify no fund-bearing role is controlled by a bare EOA; presence of EOA-controlled admin keys is a direct disqualifier.
“a highly permissioned role being granted to a very low security holder like an EOA as opposed to something like a multisig gated by a time lock” ▶ 15:25
Vaults have a smaller attack surface than lending protocols or DEXes because their only public-facing functions are deposit and withdraw; the complexity lives in internal, permissioned roles (adding markets, setting withdrawal cooldowns), making role configuration the primary security variable.
framework🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highVedavaultsDEX
So what: When underwriting a vault, scrutinize internal role permissions and guardrails rather than public function complexity — the risk is concentrated in admin design, not user-facing surface.
“the only public facing functions are deposit and withdraw because that's all users need to do” ▶ 14:27
Risk management must be continuous, not a point-in-time audit; Centura compares a one-time audit to putting a single lock on a house versus deploying cameras, sensors and a dog, arguing dynamic markets make audit-only security inadequate.
framework🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaDeFi
So what: Allocators should favor protocols/curators with live monitoring over those relying on a stale audit; 'audited' is necessary but not sufficient.
“an audit is done as a moment in time... relying on a point in time is yeah obviously not the right solution” ▶ 7:17
Centura runs a proprietary 'Risk Radar' that flags real-time anomalies (bad debt approaching a threshold on a money market, large inflows/outflows, oracle price divergences) and embeds on-chain risk triggers in deployment contracts that auto-unwind positions — e.g., if a stablecoin drops below 0.9997 the contract returns funds to the user's wallet.
data_point🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaRisk Radarstablecoinsoracles
So what: Automated depeg-triggered unwinds are a concrete mitigation investors can demand; the specific 0.9997 threshold shows how tight the guardrails can be set for stable-collateral strategies.
“if a stable coin drops below 9997, the smart contract has authorization to unwind the position and put it back in the user's wallet” ▶ 18:49
Centura screened out rsETH before the Kelp exploit — it was never approved or included in any Kraken or Centura vault — illustrating that deep per-asset due diligence (Centura claims a ~6-month process) can pre-empt key-management/asset-level failures.
anecdote🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaKrakenrsETHKelp DAO
So what: Asset-selection discipline is a measurable edge; a curator that rejected the asset that later blew up is a track-record signal worth weighting.
“RS was never approved and was never part of any any Kraken vault or Centur vault” ▶ 18:30
Composability: from cure-all to managed liability 3
Contagion and composability are the newly-salient risks: the Kelp incident woke participants to the fact that an exposure in one protocol can damage an unrelated protocol, so 'money Legos' must be treated as a risk channel, not just a feature.
framework🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highcomposabilityKelp DAODeFi
So what: Map second-order exposure: a vault's risk includes every protocol its collateral touches, so 'I'm not using protocol X' is no protection if your collateral is connected to X.
“the concept of contagion where something over here can actually impact something over there” ▶ 7:04
Reflexive composability is now seen as a mistake for complex assets: making a vault receipt token usable as collateral elsewhere is 'almost never worth it from a risk perspective'; composability should be reserved for simple assets like staking tokens and stablecoins.
contrarian🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highVedavaultsstablecoinsstaking tokens
So what: A maturation thesis that contradicts the early-DeFi 'infinite composability' dogma; vault tokens deliberately kept non-composable are lower-risk, and the long-tail-lending-everything thesis is fading.
“not every asset needs to be collateral in a lending protocol not every asset needs to have liquidity on dexes” ▶ 22:58
Risk isolation is the key structural defense, delivered two ways: protocol-level isolated markets (pioneered by Morpho, extended by Aave's v4 instance) and the vault itself acting as a curated isolation layer where a risk manager chooses which collateral types are included.
framework🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highMorphoAaveAave v4vaults
So what: Favor isolated-market designs (Morpho/Aave v4) over shared-pool exposure for raw collateral plays; isolation limits blast radius when a single asset fails.
“Morpho pioneered this with their isolated markets and A has extended it with their A v4 instance” ▶ 20:21
Future of on-chain lending: pooled vs isolated 3
Isolated lending (Morpho, Euler) has been vindicated as the venue for self-directed, raw-exposure positions, but it is unproven whether isolated lending can charge meaningful fees at scale — the economics of the primitive are still untested.
thesis🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: mediumMorphoEulerisolated lending
So what: Isolated-lending tokens/protocols carry monetization uncertainty; the primitive is valuable but its fee-capture (hence token value accrual) is the open question to watch.
“we don't know whether isolated lending can charge fees. We just haven't seen it at scale” ▶ 26:24
Aave is the only protocol that could absorb the Kelp-style exploit without users taking losses because its strong balance sheet/treasury let it bring parties together and backstop deposits — making pooled lending a 'scale business' that an under-capitalized protocol cannot replicate.
thesis🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highAaveKelp DAOpooled lending
So what: Balance-sheet strength is a moat: Aave's ability to make users whole gives it pricing power and a healthy treasury, a flywheel that favors the incumbent and is a reason to weight AAVE over smaller pooled lenders.
“there is only one protocol on the planet that could have come back from this without users taking losses and that's a” ▶ 25:20
Pooled lending is currently 'the only thing that makes money' in on-chain lending because users pay for trusted DAO-brand risk management, giving Aave pricing power; both pooled and isolated models will coexist rather than one replacing the other.
prediction🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highAavepooled lendingisolated lending
So what: Revenue/fee durability currently sits with brand-backed pooled lenders; a barbell of pooled-incumbent (fees + backstop) plus isolated-primitive (flexibility) is the likely market structure.
“poolled lending is the only thing that makes money right now” ▶ 25:54
Vault business model, curation and incentives 6
Vault curators on Morpho face a perverse incentive: whoever first adds the highest-yielding asset attracts the most stablecoin deposits, gets the most looping and the most fees — pushing curators to onboard unworthy assets to chase TVL.
causal🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highMorphovault curatorsTVL
So what: Yield-chasing curator incentives are a systemic vault risk; treat unusually high vault yields as a signal of riskier collateral admission, not curator skill.
“the p the first person that adds a 10% asset sees all the stable coins come to their vault. It gets looped. They make the most amount of fees” ▶ 30:26
Centura deliberately stayed out of the vault market until October last year and rejects assets like Usual, Stream and Resolve that launched within a week, because its own asset due-diligence takes roughly six months — explicitly trading yield/TVL growth for risk discipline.
data_point🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaMorphoUsualStreamResolve
So what: Names Usual/Stream/Resolve as assets a disciplined curator rejected — a watchlist of higher-risk yield sources; the 6-month vs 1-week onboarding gap quantifies the diligence spectrum.
“things like usual or stream or resolve more recently. There's this demand to bring assets that are just not worthy to the market” ▶ 30:43
There is a meaningful distinction between a 'vault curator' (a name on a Morpho vault) and 'risk curation' — the Kraken/Veda/Centura partnership frames itself as the latter, a more complex risk-managed approach than simply spinning up a Bitcoin-vs-stablecoin vault.
framework🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: mediumMorphoKrakenVedaCentura
So what: Branding/positioning move that signals a tiering of vault providers; allocators should distinguish thin 'curators' from full risk-curation stacks when comparing vault TVL and yields.
“the name sitting on a morpho vault is a curator. What the three of us do together is a risk curation. It's a much different level of risk management” ▶ 21:18
Centura's stated mandate as a Morpho vault curator is to protect stablecoin depositors, not leveraged speculators — calibrating asset-price risk against LTVs and publishing prospectus-like disclosures (e.g., for its vault using Figure's prime product) rather than claiming zero risk.
thesis🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaMorphoFigurestablecoinsLTV
So what: Know whom a vault is designed to protect: stablecoin-depositor-protective vaults are structurally safer for conservative capital than yield-max vaults that implicitly favor leveraged users.
“we are there to protect the stable coin depositors in those falls” ▶ 31:30
The core structural advantage over TradFi managers is that vaults are non-custodial with cryptographic guardrails: a curator like Centura cannot swap depositor Bitcoin to Doge or take hidden leverage, and depositors can see live positions and withdraw at will — unlike opaque centralized earn products (BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager) that blew up.
thesis🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highVedaCenturaBlockFiCelsiusVoyagerBitcoin
So what: The verifiability + withdrawal-rights feature set is the genuine edge of on-chain vaults over the last cycle's CeFi yield products; it is the underwriting basis for trusting these structures with retail capital.
“that vault cannot take your Bitcoin and swap it to Doge and just do crazy trading with it” ▶ 34:39
Independent risk ratings for vaults are coming, potentially from credible traditional risk raters interested in coming on-chain to assess vaults — an anticipated layer of institutional trust infrastructure.
prediction🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: mediumvaultsrisk ratersDeFi
So what: Watch for TradFi rating agencies entering on-chain vault assessment; their arrival would be a catalyst for larger institutional allocations and a screen investors can use.
“There will be independent risk raers including potentially some of the most credible traditional institutions” ▶ 36:35
Kraken Bitcoin Vault product mechanics 5
Kraken's new Bitcoin Vault routes user BTC through a three-party stack — Kraken (front-end/distribution, ~30M users) issues a KBTC wrapper (redeemable 1:1 for Bitcoin at Kraken), Veda issues a receipt token and routes capital, and Centura deploys into protocols (Morpho, Aave) and strategies.
data_point🗣 John Settler (Kraken) / Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highKrakenKBTCVedaCenturaMorphoAaveBitcoin
So what: Defines the distribution-to-yield pipeline (CEX front-end → wrapper → vault → lending) that channels mainstream BTC into DeFi yield — a template likely to be replicated across exchanges.
≈ “Kraken is the the front end... 30 million users... Those funds flow into VETA and VETA gives them a receipt token for their share of the pool and then those funds come to the Centur side” paraphrase
The Bitcoin yield mechanism is a borrow/lend carry: KBTC is posted as collateral in a borrow-lend protocol, stablecoins are borrowed against it and deployed to farm yield, and the spread between farm yield and borrow cost (converted back to BTC) is the return to depositors.
framework🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highKBTCKrakenBitcoinstablecoins
So what: Understand the return source: this is a levered basis/carry trade whose yield compresses if borrow costs rise or farm yields fall — not native Bitcoin yield, and exposed to depeg and rate risk.
“the Bitcoin or the KBDC is being deposited as collateral into a borrow lend protocol. Stable coins are being borrowed against it. Those stable coins are then being deployed to farm yield and then that spread... is the return” ▶ 42:03
The Kraken Bitcoin Vault reached ~$60M TVL within one day of its launch and grew faster than Kraken's prior USDC vault launch, with institutional inbound (custody/Prime access requests) trailing retail demand.
data_point🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highKrakenBitcoinTVLUSDCKraken Prime
So what: Strong day-one BTC-yield demand quantifies the appetite for Bitcoin yield ('the white whale'); faster ramp than the USDC vault suggests BTC-yield is the higher-demand product category.
“we launched this yesterday and we're already at like $60 million in TVL which is pretty crazy. Like it's really exciting and grew a lot faster even than our prior USDC vaults launch” ▶ 42:32
Earlier Bitcoin-yield attempts failed because they used opaque TradFi-style lending desks (BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager) that took excessive risk and gave users no way to underwrite it; the on-chain, transparent, KBTC-wrapped approach is positioned as the corrected version.
causal🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highBlockFiCelsiusVoyagerKBTCBitcoin
So what: Frames the prior CeFi-yield blowups as a solvable transparency problem, supporting the thesis that on-chain BTC yield can attract the capital that fled after 2022.
“they pursued it in a very wrong way. They went with these like, you know, opaque Tradfy style BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager type of lending desks that were taking on way too much risk and blew up” ▶ 41:23
Centura is building 'Firelight,' a DeFi insurance product that converts hard-to-understand DeFi risk into over-collateralization risk that users do understand — depositors accept slightly lower yield in exchange for a collateral buffer that fully covers or caps losses, which the panel expects to become table stakes for the next institutional wave.
thesis🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturaFirelightDeFi insurance
So what: DeFi has lacked an FDIC analog; an over-collateralized insurance layer that becomes 'table stakes' could be the unlock for the next large institutional wave — a product category to watch.
“we're building a product called Firelight, which is DeFi insurance... convert the DeFi risk that people don't understand into overcolateralization risk” ▶ 38:31
Institutional/enterprise adoption accelerating 5
The next ~$150B into DeFi will not be DeFi-native — it will arrive via enterprise channels (remittance companies, neobanks, payments firms) that move the traditional model of earning a fraction of T-bills on fiat toward stablecoins-in-a-wallet earning DeFi yield.
prediction🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highDeFistablecoinsneobankspayments companies
So what: The marginal DeFi buyer is now enterprises abstracting DeFi for retail; infrastructure that handles abstraction, compliance and distribution captures this $150B, not consumer dApps.
“the next 150 billion that's coming into DeFi is not DeFi native. It is coming through the channels that John opened up” ▶ 37:31
Despite the April hacks, Centura closed three enterprise-payments earn deals in the following five weeks (all but one proceeded), and contagion fears did not produce the expected pullback — bank and asset-manager conversations heated up rather than slowed.
data_point🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highCenturapayments companiesbanksasset managers
So what: Hard evidence that enterprise demand is rate-insensitive to DeFi exploit headlines — a counter-signal to the bearish 'institutions will flee' narrative.
“since April... we actually landed three deals in the last five weeks... we've actually seen all but one of those deals actually go through” ▶ 44:31
Banks view vaults as a way to move lending off balance sheet (a blockchain version of an SPV) with capital and client benefits, while asset managers want leverage/looping and are bringing new real-world-asset-backed products (RBAs) to be deployed into vault structures.
framework🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highbanksasset managersvaultsRWASPV
So what: Identifies two distinct institutional demand drivers — off-balance-sheet lending (banks) and levered RWA distribution (asset managers) — that define which vault/RWA infrastructure benefits.
“banks see vaults as a way to basically move their lending business off balance sheet... it's kind of like a electronic or a blockchain version of an SPV” ▶ 44:56
Enterprises proceed despite DeFi risk because the competitive stakes are existential: payments providers retain very little of their high volume on-platform, but offering on-chain yield lets them capture, retain and monetize far more assets — so being even six months behind a competitor is a substantial disadvantage.
causal🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highpayments companiesfintechDeFi
So what: Explains the durability of enterprise demand through a unit-economics lens — yield is a customer-retention weapon, so adoption is driven by competitive necessity, not risk appetite.
“if you don't do it, and even if you're six months behind and your competitor does it, you're at a substantial disadvantage” ▶ 47:30
Institutional vault demand differs from retail on three axes: institutions are slower and do deeper diligence, require compliance/KYC restrictions on vault participants, and expect thinner institutional-scale pricing versus richer retail pricing — but otherwise want the same yield products.
framework🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highKrakeninstitutionsKYCvaults
So what: Productizing for institutions means adding KYC-gated, compliance-aware vaults at thinner spreads; the yield design itself need not change, lowering the cost to serve institutional capital.
“they sometimes have slightly different prompt requirements such as compliance, KYC... institutional scale pricing is usually a bit thinner” ▶ 43:18
Macro/regulatory tailwinds and the fundamentals-vs-price divergence 4
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's prediction of ~$3 trillion in stablecoins by 2030 — framed as stabilizing foreign Treasury ownership and exporting dollar hegemony through dollar-dominated trade — was a marquee catalyst that gave institutions like JP Morgan cover to treat DeFi/stablecoins as inevitable.
data_point🗣 Anthony D. Martino (Centura)conviction: highScott BessentUS TreasurystablecoinsJP Morgan
So what: A top-down policy endorsement reframes stablecoins as a strategic dollar tool, de-risking institutional participation — a structural tailwind for stablecoin issuers and dollar-denominated DeFi yield.
“Scott best the treasury secretary of the United States basically put out a prediction of three trillion of stable coins by 2030” ▶ 48:45
Four fundamental forces are converging in DeFi's favor — surging stablecoin adoption/market cap, a friendlier US regulatory climate (Genius Act passed, Clarity Act expected to pass), expanding borrow-lend/vault financing activity, and real-world assets coming on-chain — each described as 'custom-built' for institutions.
thesis🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highstablecoinsGenius ActClarity ActRWADeFi
So what: A consolidated bull framework for allocators: legislative clarity plus stablecoin and RWA growth plus vault financing are mutually reinforcing institutional on-ramps — the multi-year DeFi-adoption thesis.
“I'm talking about Genius Act. I'm talking about Clarity Act which looks like it's going to pass... UA's real world assets in all forms” ▶ 52:36
There is now a near-complete decoupling between DeFi price action (described as 'garbage,' echoing a 2022-style four-year-cycle decline) and fundamentals (stablecoin usage, regulation, financing, RWA all improving) — and institutions are entering precisely because they focus on fundamentals, not token prices.
contrarian🗣 John Settler (Kraken)conviction: highDeFistablecoinsBitcoinETHRWA
So what: The price-fundamentals gap is the central investable tension: bearish charts may mask the strongest fundamental backdrop ever, arguing for accumulation by fundamentals-driven capital despite weak price.
“there is just this complete separation now between price action and fundamentals. And on one hand, price action just looks garbage... but in the fundamentals world, things have never been better” ▶ 51:49
The DeFi customer base has structurally shifted from speculative 'degens' (memecoins, NFTs, game-theory yield schemes) to enterprises — capital, funding and developer focus have moved away from servicing the marginal degen, making it hard to launch a new project that can't sell to enterprises or prove code at billion-dollar scale.
thesis🗣 Sun Raghupathi (Veda)conviction: highDeFienterprisesmemecoinsNFTs
So what: A 'feast or famine' regime favoring battle-tested, enterprise-ready protocols over experimental new entrants — relevant for token selection (incumbents win) and for the muted on-chain speculative activity.
“The natural customer of DeFi is no longer dens... nobody cares about memecoins anymore” ▶ 50:10

The Market Huddle · Kevin Muir 1 episode(s) · 34 insights

THE WILE E. COYOTE MOMENT (Guest: Matt Zeigler)105 min34 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
Allocator Matt Zeigler argues every modern portfolio has become a leveraged bet on AI, with the S&P 500's top 10 names at ~40% and roughly half of this year's earnings growth coming from a handful of chip/AI names; he favors quality, equal-weight and scrubbed international/EM exposure to keep equity-risk-premium on while cutting AI concentration, and frames the fiscal/private-credit/private-equity excesses as a Wile E. Coyote 'already off the cliff' situation that needs two or three things to break simultaneously before gravity hits.
AI concentration as hidden portfolio leverage · 6Equity risk premium and the flow out of stocks · 4Valuation, treading water, and quality tilt · 6International and European equity opportunity · 3Fiscal excess and the Wile E. Coyote thesis · 4Private credit risks · 4Private equity as the bigger forward risk · 3The bull case and sentiment divergence · 4
AI concentration as hidden portfolio leverage 6
Most diversified stock-and-bond portfolios are now a single leveraged bet on AI: the S&P top 10 names are ~40% of the index, the Qs hold the megacaps, and private credit financing data centers adds further AI exposure on the bond side.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highAIS&P 500QQQprivate creditdata centersSMH
So what: Investors should map total AI exposure across portfolio AND career/balance sheet, not just equity sleeve, because the same risk is embedded in multiple supposedly-diversified holdings.
“no matter what you do, everything has just become a leveraged play on AI” ▶ 3:41
Roughly 50% of this year's S&P earnings growth came from just Micron and one other chip/AI name, making index-level earnings growth fragile and narrow despite the headline 20x multiple.
data_point🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumMicronS&P 500Cameron DawsonAI semiconductors
So what: Index investors are already overexposed to a single sector's earnings; a stumble in chips would hit reported index growth disproportionately.
AI disruption is hitting non-obvious sectors like retail (online selling, supply chains, vendor/order flows), creating an intra-sector dispersion trade between AI-disrupted and AI-immune industries.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumAIretail sectorKai WuSparkline Capital
So what: A long-AI-immune / short-AI-disrupted dispersion pairing can isolate the disruption signal rather than betting on the index direction.
Per Kai Wu's research, the value factor still works once you split the universe into AI-disrupted industries (where value fails) versus industries AI hasn't figured out how to disrupt (where value still works).
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumKai WuSparkline Capitalvalue factorintangiblesAI
So what: Conditioning value screens on AI-disruption risk, and re-pricing intangibles, can revive a factor that looked dead at the aggregate level.
The practical AI-derisking move is simply to buy non-tech stock exposure and to move fixed income away from private credit that is levered to the tech/data-center lending complex.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highAInon-tech equitiesprivate creditdata centers
So what: Concrete rotation: pair down megacap tech and tech-adjacent credit, add inflationary real-economy assets as counterweight.
“If you have to have stock exposure, get non- tech exposure. If you have to have fixed income exposure, get away from private credit” ▶ 6:49
Growth/AI-heavy investors are typically underweight inflationary real assets — rail cars, energy, utilities — that are historically uncorrelated with the big tech investment over market cycles and serve as a counterweight.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumrail carsenergyutilitiesinflationary assetsAI
So what: Specific counterweight basket for AI-concentrated books: real-asset / inflation-sensitive sectors that don't move with the tech complex.
Equity risk premium and the flow out of stocks 4
The perceived equity risk premium has gone negative: the S&P earnings yield (~3.5-4%) now sits below the ~4.5% 10-year yield, inverting the ~4-5% premium that existed in 2020 when the 10-year was ~1% and the earnings yield 5-6%.
data_point🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: highequity risk premiumS&P 50010-year Treasuryearnings yield
So what: With bonds now out-yielding stocks, the long-run case for paying up for equities is weaker than at any point since pre-2020.
The equity risk premium has near-zero predictive power for next-year returns but becomes a clear signal over 5-10 year horizons, which is why it drives large institutions doing asset-liability matching rather than tactical traders.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highequity risk premiumendowmentspension fundsasset-liability matching
So what: Don't trade ERP for the next 12 months; watch it as a slow multi-year flow driver as institutions lock in bond rates.
With high stock valuations, mid-range bond valuations, and a neutral inflation view, endowments and pensions can now asset-liability match 3-5-10 year liabilities by locking in bond rates, creating a structural multi-year flow OUT of stocks.
prediction🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highendowmentspension fundsbondsS&P 500asset-liability matching
So what: This is a slow, persistent equity-outflow tailwind for bonds that allocators should expect to play out over quarters and years.
“I have a way to solve for three, five, and 10 years out right now, if I just lock in some rates” ▶ 17:48
Asset classes behave like non-mixing religions: equity and bond investors almost never arbitrage relative value between them except in rare dislocations, so the negative ERP won't self-correct quickly — only nimble allocators exploit it.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highequity risk premiumstocksbondsallocators
So what: Don't expect a fast mean-reversion of the stock/bond valuation gap; the edge is for allocators willing to cross 'religions' when stuff gets weird.
Valuation, treading water, and quality tilt 6
Lofty post-COVID S&P valuations (~20x) are tolerable because they've been accompanied by genuine earnings, revenue and cash-flow recovery — the market is 'treading water' at 20x rather than re-rating on hope.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumS&P 500valuationsearningscash flow
So what: Bears citing the Buffett indicator/GDP overvaluation should weigh that earnings have actually grown into the multiple, lowering crash odds absent an earnings break.
“It's hard to hate treading water if we're actually getting the growth that's delivered” ▶ 19:58
Equal-weight S&P should outperform cap-weight over the long run; the only reason large institutions avoid it is liquidity constraints that force cap-weighting, not poor returns.
contrarian🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: highequal-weight S&PRSPcap-weight indexliquidity
So what: Retail and smaller allocators not bound by liquidity can capture the equal-weight premium as a concentration hedge.
Mike Green's 'enshittification of the index' — fast-track inclusion rules letting in junkier names (e.g., the SpaceX/AI IPO inclusion debate) — degrades the quality of cap-weighted benchmarks over time.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumMike GreenS&P 500SpaceXindex inclusionIPOs
So what: Cap-weight index quality is silently eroding via inclusion rules; favoring profitability/quality screens guards against the incoming lower-quality constituents.
“the inshitification of the index” ▶ 23:10
Cap-weight and equal-weight S&P leadership rotates in roughly 3-5 year cycles, so a relative-strength rule that piles into the leading version until it stops working (e.g., Dorsey Wright style) is a defensible way to manage the tilt.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumequal-weight S&Pcap-weight S&PRSPDorsey Wrightrelative strength
So what: Treat cap-weight vs equal-weight as a rotational relative-strength trade rather than a permanent allocation.
Blending an equal-weight index with a quality or momentum factor is highly complementary — it strips out the trash at both ends and smooths the ride while keeping equity-risk-premium exposure on.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highequal-weight indexquality factormomentum factor
So what: A simple equal-weight + quality/momentum overlay is a buildable way to stay in equities while reducing concentration and junk exposure.
Equal-weight carries its own concentration risk when a sector (e.g., financials) swells to 20-30% of the index, so the tilt needs monitoring for sector clumping.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumequal-weight indexfinancials sector
So what: Equal-weight is not automatically diversified; watch for fat sector weights that recreate concentration.
International and European equity opportunity 3
Europe becomes a genuinely investable index if you simply exclude the financials, which a Fidelity contact argued 'are there to lose money' — strip them out and the remaining businesses are sound.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumEuropeEuropean financialsFidelityinternational equities
So what: Ex-financials European exposure can deliver decent businesses at reasonable prices for investors trying to cut US-tech weight.
“if you just exclude all the financials in Europe, it's not a half bad index” ▶ 28:07
In 2026, scrubbed exposure to emerging, European, Japanese and developed-international equities offers good businesses at decent prices and good growth — a viable way to reduce US-tech concentration via country/factor selection rather than top-level indices.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumemerging marketsEuropeJapandeveloped internationalUS tech
So what: Use selective (scrubbed) international exposure as a de-AI-concentration tool, not broad EM/EAFE beta which doesn't deliver the targeted exposure.
Follow government money: Japan's energy buildout and continental Europe / Germany / UK deficit spending on defense and energy will create businesses that profit from state-directed capital flows.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumJapanGermanyUKEuropedefenseenergy
So what: Position in defense/energy beneficiaries of European and Japanese fiscal stimulus; depoliticize the view and just track where state money flows.
“if governments are throwing money into situations, there's going to be some businesses who figure out how to not just turn their lights on the next day, but maybe make an extra buck on the side” ▶ 30:26
Fiscal excess and the Wile E. Coyote thesis 4
The reason inflation hasn't returned to 2% is straightforwardly that the government is spending too much — running a ~7% deficit-to-GDP in good times while the Fed chair argues for lower rates.
causal🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: highfiscal deficitinflationFederal Reservedeficit-to-GDP
So what: Sticky ~3% inflation is a spending story, not transitory; structurally argues against the bond rally that rate-cut hopes imply.
