Government deficits are never financially constrained, only real-resource constrained; 2020 proved spending CAN reflate, but crossing into inflation in 2021 proved that capacity was overshot.
framework🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: highMMTfiscal deficitinflationreal resources
So what: MMT mechanics explain the plumbing, but the binding limit is real resources/inflation — once breached, more deficit just feeds inflation.
“the government is never financially constrained. It's always real resource constrained” ▶ 39:14
The fiscal danger is generational: depression-raised boomers feared spending principal, but the next cohort of politicians 'won on the first hand' with pandemic stimulus and mistook luck for skill, so deficit spending has not yet overshot in the reckless direction.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highfiscal policydeficit spendinggenerational behaviorpandemic stimulus
So what: Expect fiscal excess to keep expanding before it breaks — the political learning curve points to more, not less, deficit spending.
The economy is 'Wile E. Coyote already off the cliff' — fiscal/valuation excess can run much further than appreciated because no single shock causes a crisis; you need two or three things to break at once and unrelenting bad news, which hasn't happened since the GFC.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highWile E. Coyotefiscal deficitbear marketrecessionGFC
So what: Don't short the excess on valuation alone; wait for multiple simultaneous breaks and a steady drumbeat of bad news before positioning for the fall.
“We have wy coyoted off the cliff. Nobody's held a sign up yet.” ▶ 40:40
Private credit risks 4
Private credit is an unregulated banking system born from post-GFC bank-lending constraints; it fills a multi-trillion-dollar middle-market lending need with no alternative source, so it won't simply disappear and isn't categorically bad.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highprivate creditmiddle marketbanksGFCMark RubensteinNet Interest
So what: Removing private credit would gut a multi-trillion part of the banking system — the risk is manager quality and leverage, not the asset class existing.
“we have an unregulated banking entity basically functioning inside of the United States and abroad” ▶ 49:37
Private credit's appeal came from 'volatility laundering' — levering 5-7% floating-rate loans to 10-12% returns that go 'up and to the right' with little drawdown, and notably holding up (~8-10%, up) in 2022 when both stocks and bonds fell.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highprivate creditCliff Asnessvolatility launderingleveragefloating rate notes
So what: The smooth return stream is an artifact of not marking distressed loans; treat reported low volatility as understated risk.
Loan standards in private credit visibly dropped in 2023-24 as too much capital flooded in — diligence meetings surfaced absurd exposures like subprime motorcycle financing near the South Korean border, prompting Zeigler's firm to systematically fire managers.
anecdote🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highprivate creditloan standardssubprime lending
So what: Declining manager quality is a real-time tell that underwriting has loosened; deep manager diligence is the only defense.
“they're kind of subprime motorcycle loans in uh South Korea, you know, near the border” ▶ 47:57
A private-credit blowup becomes systemic only if it stacks with other failures — e.g., a lender exposed to Medicare-funded nursing homes hit simultaneously by Medicare funding cuts (already enacted) and a labor/unemployment shock could ripple outward.
prediction🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumprivate creditMedicarenursing homesFirst Brandsunemployment
So what: Watch for clustering of stressed end-markets (healthcare funding cuts + labor) as the trigger that turns isolated defaults into contagion.
Private equity as the bigger forward risk 3
Zeigler is more worried about private equity than private credit because PE now overpays — buying companies above public-market valuations via volatility laundering — versus the historic model of buying private firms at a discount/liquidity premium.
contrarian🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: highprivate equityprivate creditvolatility launderingliquidity premium
So what: The disappearance of the entry discount removes PE's core return engine; check evergreen structures and realistic exit paths before allocating.
Private equity and private credit are both just leverage on the equity/credit risk premium; PE should beat public equity on paper because it adds leverage plus a liquidity premium — but only if the entry discount and exit still exist.
framework🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumprivate equityprivate creditequity risk premiumleverage
So what: Demystifies PE returns as leveraged beta; once the discount erodes, the levered structure cuts both ways.
“You're just le levering up the equity or the credit risk premium” ▶ 52:02
Private equity poses a 5-10 year systemic and societal risk as it rolls up Main Street businesses (HVAC, audiology practices) that have no succession buyers, with a new generation of PE operators lacking the easy ladder Mitt Romney's cohort had to hit promised returns.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumprivate equityMain StreetHVACMitt Romneyroll-ups
So what: Forward PE returns are at risk as cheap entry points vanish and exit-to-public/strategic buyers dries up; the model needs the public market to keep absorbing flips.
“one day you wake up and you realize you're the last private equity guy rolling up the last HVAC company” ▶ 53:55
The bull case and sentiment divergence 4
The clearest bull case is that fiscal deficits create private-sector credit/earnings: Trump has pushed the rest of the world (Europe, Japan) to spend, so monstrous aggregate global deficits keep feeding corporate earnings until capital actually runs out.
thesis🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: mediumfiscal deficitTrumpglobal stimulusS&P 500 earningsprivate sector credit
So what: As long as global deficits run large, the earnings tailwind persists — fade the deficit-driven rally only when capital constraints actually bind.
“fiscal deficits create private sector credits” ▶ 1:00:12
Financial repression (Russell Napier-style yield curve control) would force capital into equities by removing alternatives, providing a structural bid even at stretched valuations.
prediction🗣 Kevin Muirconviction: mediumfinancial repressionRussell Napieryield curve controlequities
So what: If YCC arrives, the scarcity of acceptable destinations for capital pushes money into stocks regardless of valuation — a tail-bull scenario.
Corporations have learned to manage not just earnings beats (~80% beat rate via guided bars) but the entire investor narrative, letting markets 'defy gravity' and stay elevated far longer than bears expect outside an actual economic contraction.
thesis🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumcorporate earningsearnings guidanceBen Huntnarrative
So what: Narrative management argues against going all-cash; keep some equity exposure because the 'don't look down' dynamic can persist.
“the narrative of not looking down could make us defy gravity for longer than people expect” ▶ 59:49
There is a striking sentiment divergence: allocators' actual clients ('civilians') are optimistic while macro-podcast audiences are jarringly bearish, suggesting bearishness is concentrated in a self-selected commentariat rather than real money.
data_point🗣 Matt Zeiglerconviction: mediumsentimentretail investorspodcast audiences
So what: Contrarian read: extreme online bearishness may not reflect positioning of actual clients, weakening the 'everyone is bearish so we crash' setup.
“my clients are optimistic. Yeah. It's the podcast listeners who are not. And it's jarring how wide that spread is right now.” ▶ 58:28

80,000 Hours Podcast 1 episode(s) · 34 insights

I lead AGI safety at Google DeepMind – here's the view from the inside | Rohin Shah168 min34 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rohin Shah, head of AGI alignment & safety at Google DeepMind, lays out an unusually concrete insider view: AI progress is continuous/forecastable (no abrupt capability jumps), an intelligence explosion is coming but is years not months away and his own AGI timeline is ~a decade, chain-of-thought monitoring stays useful ~4-5 years for structural GPU/transformer reasons, and prosaic alignment plus third-party auditing (not binding commitments or pre-deployment eval mandates) is the right safety path. Rich with second-order signal on compute/inference scaling, DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning, and what becomes the safety bottleneck (implementation/software engineering, not ideas).
AGI timelines & intelligence-explosion mechanics · 5AI progress is continuous & measurable (no abrupt jumps) · 4Compute, inference scaling & the cost curve · 3DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning · 4Safety → regulation: auditing over commitments & eval mandates · 6Misalignment risk: lower than headlines, but real · 3Chain-of-thought monitoring & technical safety bets · 4Governance gap & second-order societal effects · 3Labor, hiring & the AI-application deluge · 2
AGI timelines & intelligence-explosion mechanics 5
Rohin Shah's median timeline to AI systems that can automate most AI R&D is roughly a decade, which he notes would once have been called 'extremely short' but is now framed as 'medium to long' — a longer view than the current AGI-2027/2030 consensus among AI-safety insiders.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shah, head of AGI alignment & safety at Google DeepMindconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAGI
So what: A senior DeepMind insider pushing timelines out to ~10 years is a counterweight to the most aggressive AGI bets priced into AI-exposed equities and compute names; useful as a dampener on near-term 'AGI by 2027' narratives.
≈ “most of my timelines delay is just thinking that even getting to that point will take quite a while — quite a while, like a decade maybe, which I feel like in past years would have been called “extremely short timelines” and nowadays gets called “medium to long timelines.”” paraphrase
Shah expects an intelligence explosion to be a multi-year process (5-10 years from the crossover point), not months, because the first AI systems that can automate AI R&D will likely be more expensive than human researchers and only become cost-competitive gradually.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIAI R&D
So what: Implies the transformative-AI payoff curve is more gradual than 'fast takeoff' scenarios — supports a longer runway for incumbents and compute buildout rather than a sudden winner-take-all discontinuity.
≈ “the first automated systems that can really automate AI R&D will probably just be very expensive, to the point of potentially being more expensive than humans would be” paraphrase
Shah argues the popular 'superhuman coder triggers the intelligence explosion' thesis is overstated because a coding-specialized AI is just a better tool (like inventing the microscope) rather than a population of idea-generating researchers, and tool improvements historically produce straight-line, not hyperbolic, progress.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI R&Dsuperhuman coderMoore's law
So what: Reframes what milestone actually matters for a takeoff (AIs generating novel ideas across all of ML R&D, not just coding), giving investors a sharper signal to watch than coding-benchmark hype.
≈ “the superhuman coder is not something that can have ideas across the spectrum, across everything that happens in ML R&D. It has ideas within the realm of coding in particular” paraphrase
Shah frames the intelligence-explosion question through endogenous-growth economics (Kremer 1993): sustained progress depends on whether AI becomes a freely-copyable 'population' of researchers driving hyperbolic growth, versus merely a tool — and population growth (more idea-generators) is the crucial variable.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highKremer 1993endogenous growthGDP growth
So what: Gives a concrete macro lens for distinguishing real takeoff from incremental tooling gains — watch whether AI starts generating cross-domain novel ideas, which is the trigger for non-linear economic growth.
Shah is confident an intelligence explosion will eventually occur but only under heavy preconditions — most critically that AI becomes cheaper than humans for nearly all economically valuable tasks, which he stresses is 'not a given.'
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIlabor automation
So what: Cost-per-task parity (inference economics), not raw capability, is the gating variable for mass labor displacement — a cleaner metric for investors than capability benchmarks alone.
≈ “which, among other things implies that the AI is cheaper than the human, which I don’t think is a given” paraphrase
AI progress is continuous & measurable (no abrupt jumps) 4
Shah holds an 'approximate continuity assumption': AI capability progress is smooth and forecastable with respect to compute and labor inputs (even if calendar-time speed varies), so a model becoming 'unexpectedly capable' between releases is very unlikely.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindFrontier Safety Framework
So what: Underpins the view that capability roadmaps are predictable — reduces tail risk of a surprise capability shock that could violently re-rate AI markets, and supports the idea progress can be tracked quantitatively.
≈ “AI development progresses fairly smoothly and continuously” paraphrase
DeepMind worked with Epoch to build 'A Rosetta Stone for AI benchmarks,' stitching disparate benchmark scores into a single general-capability score from 2020 to today; plotted against release date it forms a near-straight line, indicating AI progress has been roughly linear, not accelerating.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highEpoch AIGoogle DeepMindRosetta Stone for AI benchmarks
So what: A concrete, watchable instrument: if this composite line bends upward, that is the empirical early-warning of an intelligence explosion — a quantitative signal investors can monitor rather than relying on vibes.
≈ “when you start plotting this against release time, it’s just a nice line. It’s great. And you see that AI progress mostly looks pretty linear on this particular graph.” paraphrase
Shah dismisses the widespread 'AI progress is slowing down' perception, arguing the Epoch composite-benchmark line is still linear and that an actual slowdown or acceleration would be visually striking on that graph and not masked by benchmark gaming.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumEpoch AIAI benchmarks
So what: Counters the bearish 'scaling has plateaued' thesis circulating in markets; suggests steady underlying capability gains continue even if individual releases feel incremental.
Shah was not surprised by the o1/o3 reasoning-model wave because he had expected reinforcement learning to be applied to LLMs since ~2019; his timelines barely moved because RL did NOT generalize beautifully across all tasks the way instruction-following did.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highOpenAIo1o3reinforcement learningreasoning models
So what: Generalization (not benchmark wins on RL-targeted tasks) is the real signal for an AGI re-rate; investors should discount narrow-benchmark breakthroughs that don't transfer.
≈ “it could have been the case that once we do reinforcement learning it just generalises beautifully to everything — the same way that instruction following really does generalise beautifully to everything — and actually that was not the case” paraphrase
Compute, inference scaling & the cost curve 3
Shah says investment has shifted heavily toward inference-time compute / test-time scaling over the past year (citing Google's Deep Think algorithm) and expects this trend to continue, with the first automated AI researchers being compute-hungry systems that explore many dead ends.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogleDeep Thinkinference computetest-time scaling
So what: Reinforces sustained demand for inference compute (chips, datacenters, energy) even past pretraining-scaling debates — supportive for the inference-serving and accelerator supply chain rather than just training clusters.
≈ “there’s just been way more investment in inference time compute, inference time scaling. Google has a Deep Think algorithm which applies even more inference scaling to get even better results.” paraphrase
Shah predicts the cost of automated AI research will fall quickly once achieved ('as is usually the case in AI'), so the acceleration begins when AI crosses human cost-parity and then becomes progressively cheaper — making cost-per-research-unit the key acceleration trigger.
causal🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI R&Dinference compute
So what: Falling inference cost is the mechanism that converts capability into economic disruption; the inflection to watch is AI research cost dropping below human researcher cost.
Pretraining remains by far the most powerful method for instilling capability, and its heavy reliance on parallel GPU/TPU computation structurally forces low 'opaque serial depth' — a hardware-economic constraint, not a temporary artifact.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGPUsTPUspretrainingtransformers
So what: Ties model interpretability/safety durability directly to GPU/TPU architecture economics; as long as parallel-compute hardware dominates, models stay structurally legible — relevant to both safety and chip-demand narratives.
≈ “GPUs and TPUs are very powerful computationally, but the way that they get this power is by doing a tonne of computations in parallel” paraphrase
DeepMind vs OpenAI/Anthropic positioning 4
Shah says he trusts Google/DeepMind's public safety statements more than Anthropic's or OpenAI's because Google is 'paranoid' about commitments and only states what it will actually do, citing that DeepMind's first Frontier Safety Framework deliberately avoided the word 'commit.'
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAnthropicOpenAIFrontier Safety Framework
So what: Insider signal on relative corporate credibility/governance culture across the three frontier labs — relevant for assessing reputational/regulatory risk exposure in Alphabet vs private peers.
≈ “This is one place where I do actually trust Google more than Anthropic — or OpenAI, for that matter.” paraphrase
DeepMind is hiring 'quite a bit less' than Anthropic and OpenAI right now, with only a couple of safety-relevant roles open, contrasting with peers hiring 'hand over fist.'
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Hiring intensity is a proxy for capital deployment and expansion posture; DeepMind's restraint vs aggressive peer hiring signals differing burn/scaling strategies among the labs.
≈ “No, I think we are hiring quite a bit less than Anthropic and OpenAI at the moment.” paraphrase
Anthropic's models are the least overfit to public benchmarks, so the Epoch composite score tends to UNDER-estimate Claude relative to peers — i.e. Claude is better than its benchmark scores suggest while open-source models are likely more benchmark-overfit.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumAnthropicClaudeopen-source modelsbenchmarks
So what: Benchmark leaderboards systematically misrank labs; investors comparing model quality by public benchmarks should discount benchmark-optimized players and credit Anthropic's real-world capability.
≈ “it does tend to underestimate Claude or Anthropic’s models relative to models from other providers — because generally Claude models, since they are not overfit to the benchmarks, will tend to underperform on benchmarks relative to how good the model actually is” paraphrase
DeepMind's safety/alignment research is based in London and tends to 'fly under the radar' versus the Bay Area where word-of-mouth dominates; Shah names underappreciated DeepMind papers (MONA on reward hacking, the AGI safety position paper, Rosetta Stone benchmarks).
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindLondonBay Area
So what: Geographic distance from the Bay Area information network means DeepMind's technical edge is systematically underweighted in industry discourse — a possible perception gap vs reality on Alphabet's AI capabilities.
Safety → regulation: auditing over commitments & eval mandates 6
Shah argues binding public safety 'commitments' from AI companies don't work because firms can and do walk them back (citing Anthropic dropping 'commitment' language across RSP versions), and rules written in advance are too rigid for fast-changing research.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAnthropicResponsible Scaling PolicyAI regulation
So what: Insider skepticism that voluntary commitments constrain labs — suggests regulatory risk to AI firms is lower than commitment rhetoric implies, but also that accountability will have to come from elsewhere (auditors).
≈ “Intelligence and optimisation pressure applied against a rule will always get around the rule.” paraphrase
Shah favors third-party auditors/evaluators with deep technical access (modeled on Federal Reserve / Bank of England continuous bank supervision) as the right oversight mechanism, naming the AI Lab Watch scorecard as his favorite emerging accountability tool.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI Lab WatchZach Stein-PerlmanFederal ReserveBank of EnglandUS AISIUK AISI
So what: Points to the likely shape of future AI oversight — expert technical auditors and government safety institutes (AISIs) rather than blunt rules; the Fed-style model implies a heavily-resourced, embedded-supervisor regulatory regime is plausible.
≈ “I think both the Bank of England and the Federal Reserve in the US are really heavily involved with all of the banks: monitoring their books, basically in a constant conversation with them” paraphrase
Shah opposes mandatory pre-deployment evaluations as the dominant safety strategy, arguing they impose large costs on launch schedules while continuous progress plus a 'safety buffer' lets you rely on evals of the prior model — the approach DeepMind already uses in its Frontier Safety Framework.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindFrontier Safety Frameworkpre-deployment evals
So what: A DeepMind safety lead arguing against the policy approach much of the safety/regulatory community favors — signals labs will resist eval-gating that slows product launches, lowering the odds of launch-delaying regulation.
≈ “launch schedules are just really quite important at a company, and you try to keep them as short as possible” paraphrase
For misalignment/loss-of-control specifically, Shah says the real threat model is INTERNAL deployment (where a model gets permissions and access), not external product release, so pre-external-deployment evals miss the more dangerous scenario.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindAI control
So what: Redirects where catastrophic risk actually lives — internal agentic deployments inside labs — which is where future controls/regulation will need to focus, not consumer-facing model cards.
Shah rejects safety asks that merely 'signal allegiance to the x-risk community' and warns that pressure to look good (e.g. logging hours spent on a model) creates perverse incentives toward measuring inputs over outcomes — he will simply refuse such requests.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI safety communityGoogle DeepMind
So what: Reveals genuine friction between frontier labs and the safety-advocacy community; tempers expectations that external pressure campaigns reliably change lab behavior.
≈ “there’s things I would categorise as pay costs to signal allegiance to the x-risk safety community. And I’m not about those. I don’t want to do them. If you ask for them, I’m just going to say no.” paraphrase
Shah pushes back on Zvi Mowshowitz's critique of Gemini 3's Frontier Safety Report, clarifying the cyber 'shortcut' was counted as a success (toward an 11/12 score) not waved away, and that the report's purpose is a formal safety determination, not a detailed reproducible audit document.
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGemini 3Zvi MowshowitzFrontier Safety ReportCBRN
So what: Direct insider rebuttal to a prominent safety critic on Gemini 3 — clarifies that model cards are determinations, not transparency instruments, shaping how to read any lab's safety disclosures.
Misalignment risk: lower than headlines, but real 3
Despite being among the earliest AGI-safety researchers (since 2017), Shah believes catastrophic misalignment is plausible but unlikely-by-default, because no current argument compellingly shows it happens by default and RL is typically run over week-to-month horizons, not the long horizons needed for world-takeover goals.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAGIreinforcement learningmisalignment
So what: A credentialed insider giving a relatively optimistic base rate on catastrophic AI risk — a counterweight to doom narratives that could otherwise weigh on AI-sector sentiment and invite heavy-handed regulation.
≈ “we’re not going to do reinforcement learning over the course of one-year trajectories. Maybe we’re going to do reinforcement learning over a week or a month at most.” paraphrase
Shah says today's steerable, helpful models provide little reassurance about future safety because current systems haven't yet hit the regime that worried him (superhuman models reasoning in ways humans can't follow), so success on current alignment isn't strong evidence for future success.
contrarian🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI alignmentfrontier models
So what: Cautions against extrapolating current model docility into confidence about superhuman systems — the hard safety problems are still ahead, tempering 'alignment is basically solved' optimism.
Shah expects 'evilness'/propensity (unlike capability) CAN shift sharply between models because it's a contingent property of post-training, so DeepMind does run pre-deployment propensity evals (e.g. will the model help write suicide notes or incite violence) and will block launches if numbers are bad.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindpost-trainingmodel propensity
So what: Distinguishes predictable capability from volatile behavioral safety — the latter is where a model could fail a launch gate, a real (if rare) product-release risk for labs.
Chain-of-thought monitoring & technical safety bets 4
Shah expects chain-of-thought monitoring (reading models' reasoning) to remain useful for ~4-5 years (his median), longer than most peers, because transformers must externalize hard reasoning into legible English tokens due to low 'opaque serial depth' forced by parallel GPU/TPU pretraining.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highchain-of-thought monitoringtransformersGPUsTPUs
So what: If CoT monitoring stays viable for years, a major safety lever survives longer than feared — reducing one near-term catastrophic-risk vector and the regulatory urgency that would follow its loss.
≈ “my median is probably like four years, five years, I don’t know” paraphrase
DeepMind plans for AI control (treating internally-deployed AIs as 'untrusted insiders' with separate identities, per-permission justifications, and CoT monitoring) to be the medium-term basis for arguing Gemini is safe against loss-of-control, building on Redwood Research's control framework.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindGeminiRedwood ResearchAI controlBuck ShlegerisRyan Greenblatt
So what: Signals the actual safety architecture Alphabet will lean on for AGI-era deployment — infrastructure/security engineering, identifying AI-control tooling and security hiring as a growth area.
≈ “control is, in the medium term, probably how we are going to argue that Gemini is safe, at least for misalignment and internal deployments” paraphrase
DeepMind's MONA technique (myopic optimization with non-myopic approval) can prevent multi-step reward hacking even when an overseer couldn't detect it, and Shah speculates it could actually IMPROVE capabilities/sample-efficiency at current levels by giving granular credit assignment — though at the cost of more human input.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindMONAreward hackingreinforcement learning
So what: A safety method that may double as a capability/efficiency gain is exactly the kind of 'free' alignment that labs will adopt — watch for safety techniques that don't trade off against performance, as those diffuse fastest.
Shah suggests an internal or industry-wide governance standard could cap a model's 'opaque serial depth' to preserve monitorability, and says DeepMind is publishing a paper (with JAX code) showing how to compute opaque serial depth for any architecture.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumGoogle DeepMindJAXopaque serial depthAI governance
So what: Hints at a concrete, measurable industry safety standard that could become a soft regulatory norm — a watchable governance development that could shape model-architecture choices across labs.
Governance gap & second-order societal effects 3
Shah's top concern is that AI will dramatically accelerate capabilities and technical alignment research but NOT governance, because governance requires different skills and 'governance people' (unlike capabilities researchers) are unlikely to want to use AI to accelerate their own work — leaving oversight lagging.
thesis🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI governanceForethought ResearchCoefficient GivingAjeya Cotra
So what: Flags a structural lag: technology races ahead while institutional oversight stalls — the macro risk vector most likely to produce disorderly, surprise regulation or instability that markets aren't pricing.
≈ “capabilities researchers want to actually accelerate themselves with AIs, and I don’t get the sense that governance people will want to do this” paraphrase
Shah argues the binding bottleneck to any global AI pause is that people don't yet agree AI will take over the world, so the highest-leverage move is generating decisive scientific evidence (which he expects will mostly show scaling is safe) rather than advocacy to slow down.
framework🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highAI pauseScott AlexanderAI safety
So what: Implies a near-term global pause/slowdown of AI development is unlikely absent dramatic new evidence — supportive of continued unconstrained capability/compute buildout in the medium term.
Shah takes seriously that the world could see 'a century's worth of progress in a decade,' implying a flood of unanticipated problems requiring flexible government intervention that today's rule-bound institutions are poorly equipped to handle.
prediction🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: mediumAI governancenational governments
So what: Compressed-progress scenario means rapid, hard-to-anticipate disruptions across sectors — a regime of elevated policy and macro uncertainty that favors adaptability over fixed long-horizon bets.
≈ “people talk about a century’s worth of progress in a decade” paraphrase
Labor, hiring & the AI-application deluge 2
Shah says the binding constraint at DeepMind's safety team is now implementation, not ideas — shifting hiring demand toward software engineers who 'do the obvious thing and land it' rather than researchers writing novel papers.
data_point🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindsoftware engineeringML research
So what: A leading-indicator on AI-lab labor demand: execution/SWE talent over pure research — signals where AI-sector hiring value is migrating as the field industrializes.
≈ “more of a focus on things like getting things done, software engineering, implementation now, relative to even just a year ago” paraphrase
DeepMind has been hit by a 'deluge of AI-assisted applications' that has degraded resume screening to the point that it is moving away from open hiring calls; even an LLM-captcha defense produces ~5% human false positives.
anecdote🗣 Rohin Shahconviction: highGoogle DeepMindLLMhiringrecruiting
So what: Concrete early evidence AI is breaking the hiring/credentialing market — a second-order effect that opens demand for AI-application-detection and verification tooling and may force application fees industry-wide.
≈ “we are a little bit less likely in the future to make these open calls for hiring, because it really is just such a pain to go and do the resume screening for all of them” paraphrase

0xResearch 1 episode(s) · 32 insights

Crypto’s Flow-Driven Market Has Arrived | Shaundadevens & Kunal Doshi50 min32 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
0xResearch argues crypto has entered a flow-driven, dispersion regime where TradFi/DAT/ETF flows — not fundamentals or cheap multiples — dictate which tokens (HYPE, Zcash, Morpho) outperform while BTC and majors bleed; the panel favors leaning into proven-strength names over contrarian value bets, and is bearish Ethena as basis-trade yields collapse and it devolves into a low-margin stablecoin wrapper.
BTC structural buyer base flipping from inflows to outflows · 5Flow-driven dispersion regime: lean into strength, not cheap multiples · 5Hyperliquid (HYPE): flow story dominates valuation · 7Lending sector: Morpho premium, fixed-rate shift, looping risk repricing · 7Ethena bear case: basis trade dead, devolving into stablecoin wrapper · 6Yield-bearing stablecoin landscape: Sky and Maple outpace Ethena · 2
BTC structural buyer base flipping from inflows to outflows 5
Bitcoin's two main 2024 price drivers — ETF inflows and MicroStrategy/Saylor buying — have reversed in 2026 to ETFs net selling while Saylor remains the only structural buyer.
thesis🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highBitcoinBTCMicroStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoin ETFs
So what: If the marginal BTC buyer is now a single concentrated actor, BTC's downside support is structurally thinner than in 2024 — watch ETF flow prints as the key tell.
“if you look in the last like 28 days, the ETFs have sold more than Saylor bought, even though he's had like record strike selling and buying BTC” ▶ 2:26
Over the trailing 28 days Bitcoin ETFs sold more BTC than Saylor bought, despite Saylor running record at-the-money/strike issuance to fund purchases.
data_point🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highBitcoin ETFsBTCMicroStrategySaylor
So what: Net ETF supply is now negative even against record corporate buying — a quantitative bear signal for BTC flow balance.
BTC is down roughly 17% year-to-date and posted its worst day of ETF outflows of the year while Nasdaq, S&P 500 and AI equities rip, marking a decoupling of BTC from risk-on equities.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highBTCNasdaqS&P 500AI equitiesBitcoin ETFs
So what: BTC underperforming on both up and down equity days suggests it has lost its risk-asset beta and become a flow-starved laggard.
Retail capital is being absorbed by semiconductor and AI equity plays, draining the marginal capital pool that would otherwise rotate into crypto markets.
causal🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumsemiconductorsAI equitiescryptoretail flows
So what: Crypto's bid is competing directly with the AI equity trade for retail dollars — a sustained AI rally is a headwind for broad crypto.
The S&P 500 is at overbought levels with put-to-call ratios heavily skewed toward calls, creating downside risk that would drag BTC lower if major indexes pull back.
prediction🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumS&P 500BTCput-to-call ratio
So what: An equity-led risk-off event is the main near-term tail risk for BTC even though ETF outflows may already be front-running it.
Flow-driven dispersion regime: lean into strength, not cheap multiples 5
The 'don't hide in BTC' thesis (Austin Barrack) has been directionally correct as names like HYPE, Zcash, cards and Morpho decouple from majors on fundamentals and idiosyncratic catalysts.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumBTCHYPEHyperliquidZcashMorphoAustin Barrack
So what: In a dispersion tape, asset selection beats beta — holding BTC as a 'safe' crypto proxy has been the losing trade.
“this is not the market to be hiding in BTC” ▶ 4:16
BTC's historical edge as the only structural funnel for TradFi flows has eroded as ETH, SOL and now HYPE get their own ETFs and DATs, removing access scarcity as a reason to own BTC.
thesis🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highBTCETHSOLHYPEETFsDATs
So what: With access democratized, allocation now hinges on fundamental growth/narrative rather than which asset TradFi can technically buy — reduces BTC's relative bid.
“the argument that previously existed, which was BTC was the only one positioned to capture these tradfi flows, doesn't exist anymore” ▶ 6:36
Shaundadevens advocates a momentum strategy: lean into proven-strength names that have already captured flows because institutions will bid those same winners with size, rather than buying optically cheap tokens whose multiples only look good because price bled for years.
framework🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highcryptoTradFivalue investingmomentum
So what: A reusable allocation rule: distinguish 'cheap because revenue grew' from 'cheap because price collapsed' and only buy the former; default to winners.
“it's probably way better to lean into strength, lean into assets that have already proven like inside this crypto niche, they've gotten flows” ▶ 0:28
Crypto valuations are decoupling from fundamentals just as TradFi equities trade at 100x+ price-to-sales, implying the market now pays premiums purely for credible 10-100x future revenue growth rather than current cheapness.
contrarian🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highcryptoTradFi equitiesprice-to-sales
So what: Mean-reversion value screens are broken in this regime; underwrite forward revenue trajectory and flow access instead of trailing multiples.
“It it just proves again that the flows are the only thing that matters” ▶ 28:05
A value approach can still work in select crypto names (Meta DAO, Pendle, Aave) but only with a 1-3 year horizon, and better entry points are likely available than current prices.
thesis🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumMeta DAOPendleAaveAave V4
So what: Long-horizon value buyers should wait for better entries; short-term capital is better deployed in momentum/flow names.
Hyperliquid (HYPE): flow story dominates valuation 7
Hyperliquid's spot ETFs (21Shares, Bitwise) had the strongest debut of any crypto spot ETF, accumulating ~1% of circulating supply in the first 10 trading days versus ~0.6% for BTC's launch.
data_point🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHYPEHyperliquid21SharesBitwiseBTC ETF
So what: HYPE is absorbing institutional demand faster than BTC did at launch — a quantifiable structural bid underpinning the token.
“the ETFs accumulated 1% of the circulating supply compared to BTC that did like uh 0.6%” ▶ 9:03
Hyperliquid Strategies (the HYPE DAT) bought $82M of HYPE in a single week — roughly 40 days of assistance-fund buying (~$2M/day) — far exceeding even the successful ETF inflows.
data_point🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHyperliquid StrategiesHYPEassistance fundDATs
So what: DAT demand is the dominant marginal buyer for HYPE, ~40x the protocol's own assistance-fund buyback rate.
“They bought like 40 days of assistance fund flows in a week” ▶ 9:31
Hyperliquid Strategies trades at ~1.2x NAV and, in the same week it bought $82M of HYPE, grew its cash by $43.4M to $126M, signaling demand strong enough to fund buying without depleting cash.
data_point🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHyperliquid StrategiesHYPENAV premium
So what: A sustained premium-to-NAV plus rising cash is a self-reinforcing accumulation flywheel and a likely TradFi access vehicle for HYPE.
“they actually increased in the same week their cash by 43.4 million, and they now hold 126 million” ▶ 11:18
FDV is the wrong valuation metric for HYPE because the ~38% earmarked for future emissions is unlikely to be released, or would be released positive-EV to holders; outstanding-supply market cap is ~$32B.
framework🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHYPEHyperliquidFDVmarket cap
So what: Bears citing ~$50B FDV vs CME's ~$100B cap overstate HYPE's richness; circulating-supply structural demand/supply imbalance is the right lens.
“FDV is obviously not the right metric to use to value hyperliquid supply” ▶ 11:43
HYPE's DAT-driven rally differs from ETH's 2025 DAT pump because the DATs are neither the only buyer nor the only narrative — HYPE was already growing in revenue, had an assistance fund, and was trending up before DATs arrived.
contrarian🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHYPEETHDATs
So what: Don't expect an obvious 'DAT runs out of money' top in HYPE like ETH printed; more likely a range-then-range structure with less violent reversal.
“I definitely don't think it's going to be such an obvious telegraph top as we saw on ETH” ▶ 15:21
HYPE is struggling to break $60 because BTC weakness is capping alt upside; in a more risk-on tape HYPE could move materially higher.
causal🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: mediumHYPEBTC
So what: HYPE's near-term ceiling is a function of BTC beta; a BTC bounce is the unlock for a HYPE breakout above all-time highs.
TradFi's bull case for Hyperliquid is its simplicity: a blockchain powering 24/7 trading of any priced instrument (equities, pre-IPOs) that is extremely profitable and enables markets that don't exist in TradFi.
thesis🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highHyperliquidHYPEHIP-3
So what: The pitch resonates with institutions because it maps to a familiar, scalable exchange-revenue model — supports durable allocator demand.
“It's a blockchain that's powering 24/7 trading of any equity asset, any instrument with a price feed, and it's just extremely profitable” ▶ 9:54
Lending sector: Morpho premium, fixed-rate shift, looping risk repricing 7
Morpho's Midnight white paper introduces non-custodial fixed-rate, fixed-term credit markets using isolated permissionless markets with zero-coupon-like credit/debt units, pushing DeFi toward the structure needed to attract sophisticated lenders.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumMorphoMorpho MidnightDeFi lendingMapleSky
So what: Fixed-term DeFi credit is the maturation path; Midnight positions Morpho alongside Maple and Sky in the institutional credit design space.
Morpho trades at over 100x price-to-sales (assuming a 10% take rate it does not currently charge) versus ~17-18x for Aave/Kamino/Euler and ~10x for Sky/Maple/Spark, an unjustified ~10x premium.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highMorphoAaveKaminoEulerSkyMapleSpark
So what: Morpho is the lending sector's most valuation-stretched name; a fee-switch activation would force a painful re-rating once revenue is visible.
“I did some napkin numbers recently on Morpho and it was trading at over 100 X price to sales” ▶ 24:41
Morpho's premium is partly justified by its Coinbase integration funneling retail onboarding, but a 10x premium over peers makes no sense and token unlocks ahead will add supply pressure.
thesis🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumMorphoCoinbaseAave
So what: The narrative can carry Morpho higher until the fee switch flips; watch unlock schedule and any fee activation as the catalyst that ends the premium.
The Coinbase + Morpho Midnight integration's clearest near-term use case is retail BTC-collateral loans or fixed-rate stablecoin deposits (e.g. deposit BTC, borrow USDC at ~5% fixed for 6 months), not sophisticated looping.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumCoinbaseMorpho MidnightBTCUSDC
So what: Retail fixed-rate products via Coinbase are the realistic flow driver; no launch date for Midnight yet is the key thing to watch.
After the Aave drama where USDC suppliers effectively subsidized sUSD/Syrup looping, the market will demand higher rates for looping, shrinking the set of profitable looping assets and raising the cost to loop across DeFi.
causal🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumAaveUSDCsUSDSyrupEthena
So what: Looping-driven yield strategies (a core Ethena/sUSD demand source) get less profitable — a second-order drag on synthetic-dollar yields.
Euler stopped charging protocol fees (announced ~last week) to stay competitive with fee-free Morpho, illustrating that lending protocols can scale usage but struggle to capture economics without hurting competitiveness during growth mode.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highEulerMorpho
So what: Fee monetization is the structural weak point of DeFi lending; turning on fees risks deposit churn, capping realizable revenue and supporting the bear case on stretched multiples.
Aave saw large outflows post-drama that still haven't recovered, with capital migrating to Spark, Maple and Morpho or sitting idle in protocol treasuries waiting for more certain collateral and rate terms.
data_point🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumAaveSparkMapleMorpho
So what: Sidelined treasury capital is latent demand for fixed-rate/fixed-term products — first protocol to capture it (likely Morpho via Coinbase) wins material flows.
Ethena bear case: basis trade dead, devolving into stablecoin wrapper 6
Ethena's stablecoin supply has collapsed from a ~$17B peak (September last year) to just above $5B as basis-trade yields compressed.
data_point🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: highEthenasUSDeUSDe
So what: A ~70% supply contraction signals the core product's value proposition is broken — directly bearish for ENA fundamentals.
“at the peak in September last day, it was 17B, and now it's just slightly above 5 billion” ▶ 35:26
Ethena's yields have fallen from nearly 10% at the 2025 peak to ~3-4% as the cyclical market kills sustained basis-trade profitability and the strategy's replicability compresses returns as more capital enters.
causal🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: highEthenasUSDebasis trade
So what: At 3-4%, sUSDe yields are barely above Treasuries while carrying more risk — weak incentive to hold absent token incentives.
Ethena increasingly allocates away from the basis trade into liquid cash, USDtb (Treasury yields) and incentive-driven lending deposits (MegaETH, Plasma, Jupiter, Kamino, USDG campaign), making it function like an on-chain asset manager rather than a synthetic-dollar issuer.
thesis🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: highEthenaUSDtbMegaETHPlasmaJupiterKaminoUSDG
So what: Its current 3-4% yield leans on transient lending incentives; as those decay, yields likely fall further — reframe ENA as a low-margin allocator, not a yield engine.
With the basis trade no longer lucrative, Ethena has 'no choice' but to become a stablecoin wrapper, which the panel views as bearish given little value-add over safer yield-bearing stables.
contrarian🗣 Shaundadevensconviction: highEthenasUSDeUSDe
So what: Loss of the differentiated funding-yield product undercuts Ethena's investment case; a clear sector short/avoid signal.
“their old strategy is not lucrative anymore and they've essentially become a stablecoin wrapper and I don't really see much value in that” ▶ 43:21
RWA and equity basis trades cannot scale for a $5B protocol like Ethena: gold and even SOL basis trades lack the order-book depth (checked on Binance and Hyperliquid) to reverse during outflow events like the ~$1B redemption on 10/10.
data_point🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: highEthenagoldSOLRWABinanceHyperliquid
So what: Diversification can't rescue Ethena's yields at scale; SOL never exceeded ~0.5% of reserve backing for the same liquidity reason.
“it's just not possible to do it in size” ▶ 46:18
Even Aave's proposed yield diversification (gold RWA basis trade via Centrifuge CLOs, institutional lending, USDC/Maple lending) backtests to only ~4-5% — more stable but not meaningfully higher than current basis-trade yields.
data_point🗣 Kunal Doshiconviction: mediumAaveCentrifugeCLOsgoldUSDCMaple
So what: Gold/CLO trades offer the best diversification from the BTC/ETH basis trade but won't restore double-digit DeFi yields — the high-yield-stablecoin era is structurally over.
Yield-bearing stablecoin landscape: Sky and Maple outpace Ethena 2
Maple's Syrup USDC and Sky's USDS have outperformed Ethena's sUSDe with simpler, safer strategies — Syrup lends to institutions against over-collateralized (mostly BTC) loans at roughly a 1% premium to lending markets.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highMapleSyrup USDCSkyUSDSEthenasUSDeBTC
So what: Safer, transparent yield products are winning share from basis-trade dollars — favor Maple/Sky over Ethena in the synthetic-dollar trade.
Sky abstracts rate volatility via a governance-set Sky Savings Rate (~3.65%, above sUSDe), pushing the burden of generating excess spread onto capital allocators like Spark, Obex and Grove — a bank-deposit-like model.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highSkySky Savings RateSparkGroveObex
So what: Sky's fixed-rate UX plus allocator-borne spread risk is a more durable retail product; allocators (Spark) are where the real yield-generation alpha and risk sit.

Hidden Forces · Demetri Kofinas 1 episode(s) · 32 insights

Why History Can't Prepare Us for What’s Coming | Alap Shah54 min32 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Hedge fund manager Alap Shah (ex-Viking, ex-Citadel) argues AI is the first technology that substitutes rather than enables cognitive labor, closing the white-collar 'escape valve' that absorbed prior automation waves. The core trade: a synthetic short on the US consumer economy is embedded in the long-AI/semiconductor trade, because the same capex that lifts chips destroys the white-collar wages that drive consumer spending. He sees agentic disintermediation gutting internet and financial-services moats, and forecasts a political realignment toward redistribution (AI dividend fund, corporate AI taxes) becoming necessary as labor share of income breaks below 50%.
Substitution vs enablement — why this cycle breaks the playbook · 5Synthetic short on the US consumer — the core trade · 6Agentic disintermediation of internet & financial services · 4AI capex supercycle & evidence on the ground · 5Labor share, entry-level collapse & distribution · 4Policy interventions & political realignment · 6The doom loop & market reflexivity · 2
Substitution vs enablement — why this cycle breaks the playbook 5
Alap Shah argues AI is fundamentally different from prior tech waves because past booming technologies (e.g. the telephone) were enablers of human labor that created new white-collar jobs, whereas AI is a truly substituting technology that can do the work humans previously did, so it is unclear where replacement jobs come from.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shah (hedge fund manager, ex-Viking/Citadel)conviction: highAIlarge language modelslabor market
So what: If correct, the historical 'productivity boom creates new jobs' transmission mechanism fails, undermining the bull case that AI capex automatically lifts employment and broad demand.
“The problem this time is we don't have an enabling technology. We have a truly substituting technology.” ▶ 13:56
Shah frames AI as a massive rightward/outward shift of the supply curve for intelligence that drives the price of a unit of human intelligence down, with large downstream consequences once that price falls significantly.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highAIhuman intelligencelabor pricing
So what: Reframes AI not as a demand boom but as a deflationary shock to white-collar wages — the analytic lens for shorting wage-dependent consumption.
“it's just a huge rightward shift, outward shift of the supply curve for intelligence” ▶ 12:49
Shah uses a 2x2 matrix (manual/cognitive × routine/non-routine) to argue routine manual work was automated since the Industrial Revolution and routine cognitive work since the 1980s IT revolution, with displaced workers absorbed into non-routine cognitive work — the 'escape valve' he believes will no longer hold in an AI-first world.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highautomationwhite-collar laborIT revolution
So what: Identifies non-routine cognitive work as the specific quadrant now at risk, with no fourth quadrant left to absorb displaced workers.
“that escape valve, our belief is, will no longer hold in this AI-first world” ▶ 25:49
Shah contends AI need not exceed human intelligence to displace large amounts of labor — being almost as good is enough, because humans are expensive, need benefits and time off, and require a large 'coordination tax' that agents avoid by sharing identical context and updating in real time.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highAI agentsMicrosoft Officelabor costs
So what: Lowers the bar for displacement: investors should not wait for AGI before pricing labor substitution; near-parity plus cost advantage is the trigger.
“you can think about the entire Microsoft Office Suite as just a large coordination suite for humans” ▶ 18:56
Shah estimates the cost of an agent doing a unit of work is typically less than 1% of what it costs a human, and agents work 24/7 with no benefits.
data_point🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumAI agentslabor costs
So what: A ~100x unit-cost edge implies the economic incentive to substitute is overwhelming wherever quality is near-parity.
“The cost of doing a unit of work is a tiny fraction of what it costs a human to do something. You know, I would typically if the numbers will represent less than 1%.” ▶ 19:16
Synthetic short on the US consumer — the core trade 6
Shah's central market call is that a large synthetic short on the US consumer economy is embedded in the AI trade: semiconductors and Anthropic rise precisely because they substitute for human labor, so the long-AI position is structurally short the wages that drive consumer spending.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highsemiconductorsAnthropicUS consumerAI trade
So what: The actionable framing: pair long AI infrastructure against short wage-dependent consumption rather than treating AI as broadly bullish.
“I believe there is a large synthetic short on the consumer economy that is inherent in the AI trade today.” ▶ 46:41
Shah recommends a barbell: stay long semiconductors and the AI complex as clear winners, and short sectors tied to human wages and consumer spending — most of retail, consumer, the leisure economy, and serving sectors like trucking.
prediction🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highsemiconductorsretailconsumer discretionaryleisuretrucking
So what: Concrete sector map for expressing the thesis: long AI capex beneficiaries, short wage-sensitive consumer and downstream logistics.
“semis keep going up, but the consumer economy, which is the bulk of the US economy, will suffer” ▶ 47:21
Shah argues a few white-collar job losses go a long way because even employees who keep their jobs will sharply raise savings rates and retrench spending after seeing colleagues in accounting, operations or customer success displaced — a behavioral demand shock layered on top of the direct job losses.
causal🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highUS consumersavings ratewhite-collar employment
So what: Implies consumer spending can crack before unemployment data confirms it — a leading-indicator risk for consumer cyclicals.
“you still have your job, but you're massively going to retrench on your spending and take your savings rate up very significantly” ▶ 15:19
Shah warns displaced white-collar workers will move into the blue-collar economy seeking work, depressing blue-collar supply-demand balance and wages there too, broadening the demand hit beyond the directly displaced.
causal🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumblue-collar laborwageslabor market
So what: Second-order effect: wage pressure spreads from white-collar to blue-collar, widening the consumer-spending drag.
“they're going to depress the blue-collar sort of supply-demand balance as well” ▶ 15:37
Shah argues traditional countercyclical heuristics will fail: even if the Fed cuts rates aggressively into falling inflation, demand from workers fearful of ever-more-powerful AI won't rebalance the way it historically has, so standard recession-fighting tools lose potency.
contrarian🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumFederal Reservemonetary policyinflation
So what: Challenges the 'buy the dip, Fed will rescue demand' reflex; a structural-fear-driven slowdown may not respond to rate cuts.
“all of this sort of traditional heuristics around how economic cycles work will stop applying in the same way because AI is just this completely new technology” ▶ 16:19
Shah notes the synthetic short is unlikely to resolve cleanly: a market where semiconductors keep rising while most of the consumer economy slows risks recession-like outcomes and ultimately the risk that AI capex and AI funding dry up.
prediction🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumsemiconductorsAI capexconsumer economy
So what: Tail risk for the AI longs themselves — a consumer-driven downturn could starve the capex cycle that the bull case depends on.
“it's unlikely we're going to have a market that keeps going up when, you know, semiconductors keep doing really well and most of the consumer economy continues slowing down” ▶ 48:21
Agentic disintermediation of internet & financial services 4
Shah argues trillions of dollars of enterprise value were built on monetizing human limitations — search costs, laziness and imperfect information — and that agents flip this by being infinitely patient, reading every listing and review, collapsing the intermediation tax much of the market cap rests on.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highinternet platformsintermediationAI agents
So what: Targets the moats of intermediary platforms as structurally vulnerable; surplus shifts from platforms to consumers.
“a huge amount of our economy, and especially huge amount of our market cap, is just a tax on, you know, human time, human laziness, and human imperfect information” ▶ 29:01
Shah predicts consumer behavior shifts from opening trusted apps (DoorDash, Uber, booking apps) to issuing voice/text intent to an AI assistant (Gemini, ChatGPT, Android) that executes the task, aggregating demand into the intent layer and stripping value from app-level intermediaries.
prediction🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highDoorDashUberGeminiChatGPTAndroidGoogle
So what: Value accrues to whoever owns the agent/intent layer (Google, OpenAI) at the expense of named consumer apps — a re-rating risk for DoorDash/Uber/booking platforms.
“you just speak into your your AI or into your phone, you know, "Order me a pizza from Joe's. Book me a four-star hotel in Boston for me and my family."” ▶ 30:23
Shah identifies financial services as the other large intermediation layer at risk: agents will read insurance contracts, find the best product, and iteratively negotiate on the consumer's behalf, eroding banks' and insurance brokers' margins built on consumer inertia and information asymmetry.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highfinancial servicesbanksinsurance brokersAI agents
So what: Flags banks and insurance intermediaries as disintermediation targets; surplus flows to consumers, compressing intermediary fee pools.
“the agent's going to be able to find A, it's going to be able to find the best contract, but it also is then going to be able to iteratively negotiate on your behalf” ▶ 31:53
Shah frames disintermediation as a partial cushion: most of the surpluses stripped from internet and financial-services intermediaries flow back to individual consumers, which could partly offset the blow from AI-driven job losses.
causal🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumconsumersintermediationconsumer surplus
So what: A counterweight to the consumer short — falling intermediation costs boost real consumer purchasing power even as wages fall.
“most of those surpluses are going to flow back to individual consumers and that will hopefully cushion some of the blow” ▶ 32:23
AI capex supercycle & evidence on the ground 5
Shah projects AI capex will exceed 1% of global GDP by 2028 and calls that a floor, citing the recent run in semiconductor stocks and a clear ROI on AI compute.
data_point🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highAI capexglobal GDPsemiconductors
So what: Quantifies the scale and durability of the capex cycle underpinning the long-AI side of the barbell.
“By 2028, AI CapEx will be more than 1% of global GDP. I think that is a floor and it will continue to go up.” ▶ 26:47
Shah cites cloud run-rate adding the equivalent of entire SaaS-complex ARR in a single month as evidence of the AI virtuous cycle accelerating to a new level.
data_point🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumcloud computingSaaSARRAI
So what: Datapoint supporting the durability of AI demand and the capex flywheel, reinforcing the long-infrastructure leg.
“it's adding the equivalent of like, you know, entire SAS complexes in ARR in a single month” ▶ 27:07
Shah reports his own software-development process changed fundamentally since last fall: using Claude and agentic coding, a new feature goes from idea to a clean spec in ~20 minutes and to a working prototype in under an hour, versus weeks with a product manager, designer and engineering team previously.
anecdote🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highClaudesoftware developmentAI coding agents
So what: Concrete proof point that software headcount needs are collapsing (he cites needing 30-40% of prior staff); software is the leading edge of broader displacement.
“Now, my process, if I want to create that new feature, is I talk to Claude, and in the course of 20 minutes, often less, I have a clean spec” ▶ 10:39
Shah says he increasingly goes directly to Claude and ChatGPT for investment-fund financial analysis instead of hiring analysts, because the AI answers in ~30 seconds with the internet's knowledge built in versus 20 minutes for a human analyst, letting him iterate through several decision branches before consulting anyone.
anecdote🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highClaudeChatGPTinvestment researchequity analysts
So what: Buy-side research roles are an early displacement target; AI compresses the analyst function inside funds themselves.
“I increasingly find myself just going directly to Claude and ChatGPT and not going to analysts much at all for that analysis” ▶ 22:01
Shah calls software development 'the tip of the spear' — the domain with the most training data and focus, hence the fastest to accelerate — implying the same automation curve generalizes downstream to other cognitive work over time.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumsoftware developmentAIcognitive labor
So what: Use software-job displacement as the leading indicator for where other white-collar functions head next.
“software development is just the tip of the spear, the thing that we've got the most training data on” ▶ 11:09
Labor share, entry-level collapse & distribution 4
Shah cites labor share of national income falling from 60%+ in the 1980s-90s to around 54% today, and proposes that a definitive break below 50% should trigger new redistributive policy interventions.
data_point🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highlabor share of incomenational incomeAI policy
So what: Provides a concrete, trackable macro trigger (labor share < 50%) for when redistribution politics and AI taxes become live.
“labor share of income, you know, started out in the 60 plus percent range, you know, in the '80s and '90s and it's been trending down to around 54% today” ▶ 43:22
Shah notes that recent college graduates — historically enjoying lower unemployment than older workers — have seen that relationship definitively flip since 2023 and worsen monthly, which he attributes substantially to AI doing entry-level work better and far cheaper.
data_point🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highrecent graduatesyouth unemploymententry-level jobsAI
So what: Entry-level white-collar hiring is the first observable casualty; a real-time gauge of AI labor displacement to watch.
“young people coming out of college historically had much better unemployment rates than older folks and that has very definitively flipped since 2023” ▶ 38:22
Shah argues the gains from AI are real and large but the distribution is the problem — spoils concentrate to a few highly productive leaders/owners while the bottom half of the skill distribution is hit first, because AI matches the least experienced workers soonest.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highincome inequalityK-shaped economyAI
So what: Distribution, not aggregate productivity, drives the political and consumption risk — the gap is what threatens demand and stability.
“It's the distribution that's the problem.” ▶ 37:43
Shah says the most advantaged people will be the 'agentic by nature' — those with initiative and entrepreneurial vision who can direct AI/agents — because startup capital requirements have collapsed (a company can now be started essentially with a Claude subscription) while more capital chases such founders.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highentrepreneurshipstartupsAI agentsClaude
So what: Favors early-stage/startup formation and lean, AI-leveraged operators; bearish for incumbents reliant on headcount moats.
“the company that you previously could not have started now you can start you know with essentially a cloud subscription” ▶ 33:28
Policy interventions & political realignment 6
Shah argues the current tax code subsidizes AI over labor because human work carries ~7-8% FICA plus Medicare taxes while equivalent agent work is untaxed, and proposes measuring and taxing agent work comparably to remove the incentive to substitute automation for humans.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highFICAMedicare taxAI agentscorporate tax
So what: An 'agent tax' would raise the relative cost of automation; a policy risk for capex-heavy automators if labor share keeps falling.
≈ “we tax labor today more than we tax AI. We have, you know, social security and Medicare taxes on labor that doesn't exist at all when you instead tax corporations.” paraphrase
Shah proposes targeting higher corporate taxes specifically at firms where margins are exploding due to aggressive headcount cuts (leaving steady-margin, still-hiring firms untouched), using the proceeds via an EITC-style framework to support displaced workers conditioned on reskilling or community work.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highcorporate taxprofit marginsEarned Income Tax Creditreskilling
So what: Margin-expansion-via-layoffs becomes a tax target — relevant to the highest-margin AI-leveraged firms; watch for policy that taxes exactly the winners of the synthetic short.
“let's go directly to the source of where the jobs are disappearing, the places where corporations are aggressively cutting humans, and let's go and tax right there” ▶ 44:49
Shah advocates an 'AI dividend fund' modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund — using AI-era taxation to give every American a usable stake — as the more aggressive intervention if displacement deepens, funded by surging GDP and corporate profits.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumAlaska Permanent FundAI dividend fundredistributionuniversal capital
So what: Signals the eventual end-state of AI redistribution policy; a structural transfer mechanism investors should expect as labor share falls.
“what we we call kind of an AI dividend fund, which is you know, like the Alaska Permanent Fund” ▶ 45:59
Shah notes Dario Amodei and others have floated a token tax on AI, which Shah sees as a plausible end-state, but he favors corporate taxes near-term because Americans dislike income, wealth and rich-targeted taxes far more than corporate taxes.
framework🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumDario Amodeitoken taxcorporate taxwealth tax
So what: Maps the realistic policy path (corporate taxes first, token tax later) for pricing future AI-sector tax risk.
“Dario and others have talked about a token tax and, you know, I think there's a world in which that makes sense, especially kind of the end state.” ▶ 44:00
Shah forecasts a political realignment in which the cultural left-right divide stays intact but both parties move significantly left on economic redistribution — the right moving partway as non-wealthy voters who once prioritized social issues are forced to prioritize economic security as AI erodes jobs.
prediction🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highpolitical realignmentredistributionAI politics
So what: Anticipates bipartisan momentum for redistribution/AI taxes — a regime shift in the policy backdrop for AI-winner equities.
“both parties will move significantly to the left because that's the only logical replacement to AI” ▶ 51:11
Shah predicts AI will rise from roughly the 10th-ranked voter issue to a top-three issue by the midterms and the single dominant issue by 2028, because the displacement is moving fast and visibly enough to override other concerns.
prediction🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumAI policyelectionsmidterms2028
So what: A timeline for when AI/labor policy becomes politically actionable — sets the horizon for redistribution-driven regime change.
“by the midterms, it's going to be, you know, top two, top three issue. And by 2028, I think it's going to be the only issue that matters” ▶ 51:46
The doom loop & market reflexivity 2
Shah describes a self-fulfilling 'doom loop': because the consumer economy is the US economy, a depressed consumer leads companies to cut further, which depresses demand more, creating a reflexive downward spiral rather than the usual self-correcting cycle.
causal🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: highUS consumer economyrecessionreflexivity
So what: Argues the AI labor shock could be reflexive and non-mean-reverting — a tail-risk scenario for broad equity beta, not just consumer names.
“once that happens, it could be pretty self-fulfilling because those companies will cut the consumer economy is ultimately the US economy” ▶ 15:48
Shah's resolution thesis: if society passes the right AI taxation and redistribution, it would 'close' the synthetic short on the consumer — good for society and markets — because a market cannot keep rising indefinitely with semis up and the consumer economy slowing.
thesis🗣 guest, Alap Shahconviction: mediumredistribution policyconsumer economyequity marketssemiconductors
So what: Frames redistribution policy as the catalyst that could unwind the consumer short and re-broaden the market rally — a policy-dependent inflection to watch.
“kind of close that synthetic short, which I think will be good for society, will be good for the markets” ▶ 48:16

Top Traders Unplugged 1 episode(s) · 31 insights

Why Macro Investing Is Becoming More Systematic | Allocator | Ep.3562 min31 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions) argues macro investing has shifted from concentrated discretionary bets (Soros/Druckenmiller era) to nimble, data-rich systematic strategies; he favors mostly-fundamental multi-model regime frameworks, sees a still-pro-risk but inflation-flagged environment with oil as the dominant risk factor, and views LLM/NLP on text as the current frontier of alpha and risk extraction.
Macro is going systematic · 3Regime modeling & multi-model discipline · 4Inflation as the dominant risk factor · 460/40, privates & allocation problems · 3Downside protection & options · 2LLMs & machine learning for alpha · 4Overfitting, adaptiveness & faster regimes · 4Market structure, retail & quant edge · 2Current positioning: opportunities & risks · 4Career & skills in quant · 1
Macro is going systematic 3
The big concentrated macro opportunities of the 1990s (Soros, Druckenmiller, Robertson making large directional bets) have largely disappeared; the explosion of alternative data (GDPNow, web-scraped prices, geospatial, behavioral tracking) has forced macro investing to become far more systematic and nimble.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highGeorge SorosStanley DruckenmillerJulian RobertsonPGIM Quantitative Solutionssystematic macro
So what: Edge in macro now accrues to firms with broad real-time data and disciplined process rather than to star discretionary callers; allocators should weight systematic macro and CTA-style managers accordingly.
“the big opportunities that the the macro concentrated traders have had in the '9s really just don't exist today. You have to be much more nimble” ▶ 7:31
The underlying drivers macro managers care about (inflation, growth) are unchanged since the 1990s, but the data used to proxy and measure them has exploded — replacing once-a-month/quarter government data with NLP monitoring of global news feeds and foreign-language sources in near real time.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highinflationgrowthnatural language processingalternative data
So what: Faster, higher-frequency proxies mean macro positioning can shift before official prints, compressing the window in which slower investors can react to data.
PGIM Quantitative Solutions runs ~$110bn, split roughly 50% systematic multi-asset and the remainder quant equity (benchmark-relative, typically 2-4% tracking error), with track records going back 25-40 years.
data_point🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highPGIM Quantitative Solutionsquant equitymulti-asset
So what: Sizes one of the longest-running systematic multi-asset shops; useful context for who is institutionalizing the systematic-macro shift.
Regime modeling & multi-model discipline 4
PGIM Quant classifies the macro regime with a Gaussian mixture model that treats the economy as an unobservable state inferred from many data points, deliberately excluding market-based data because markets move faster than the real economy; this medium-term view currently reads moderately pro-risk.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highGaussian mixture modelregime detectionrisk assets
So what: Shows a concrete, replicable regime-detection method and signals current positioning bias (pro-risk) from a major systematic allocator.
“a a Gaussian mixture model that basically views the world, views the economy as some sort of unobservable state” ▶ 19:47
No single model should drive macro positioning; winning approaches combine multiple models (traditional inflation-high/rising quadrant views plus statistical methods), echoing how Kaggle competition winners are almost always multi-model ensembles.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highKaggleensemble modelsquad framework
So what: Argues against betting on one regime classifier; allocators evaluating quant managers should probe for ensemble/multi-model robustness rather than a single signature model.
≈ “If you look at the the the winners of those projects... they're all multimodel” paraphrase
Roughly 80% of PGIM Quant's macro models are derived from fundamental data rather than pure price/statistical signals, specifically because fundamental grounding lets managers keep faith in a model during drawdowns instead of unplugging an unexplained black box.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highfundamental modelsstatistical modelsmacro
So what: Implies a fundamental tilt is the behavioral antidote to capitulation; a screen for whether a systematic manager will survive a bad stretch.
“If you have a purely statistical model and it tells you to go long, you know, go long Europe and short the US... there's going to be a period of time when that doesn't work” ▶ 25:45
The macro modeling problem has very few degrees of freedom, so models must be built with economic priors and insight-checking at construction time rather than by running regressions and dropping coefficients into a new model.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highdegrees of freedomoverfittingmacro modeling
So what: Explains why brute-force ML is risky in macro (few independent observations) and why prior-driven design matters when assessing a manager's process.
“this is really true with the macro side because the degrees of freedom is very very very few” ▶ 26:41
Inflation as the dominant risk factor 4
Since the start of Middle East military actions, markets have collapsed to essentially one principal component — oil up / inflation up / equities down (or vice versa) — so a single asset's morning move now telegraphs the rest of the market.
causal🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highoilinflationequitiesMiddle Eastprincipal component
So what: When cross-asset correlation collapses to one factor, diversification quietly fails; watch oil as the lead indicator and treat equity-bond hedges as compromised.
≈ “there's been you know like there's been one principal component right it's been you know oil's up equity down or oil up inflation up equities down” paraphrase
Patterson uses a ~4% inflation rule of thumb: below 4% equities can still perform (with commodities gaining importance), above 4% is where material damage to both markets and fiscal policy starts; he does not expect the US to breach 4% in the near term.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediuminflationequitiescommoditiesGDP
So what: Gives a concrete tripwire (4% CPI) for rotating defensiveness and adding commodity exposure; a level to monitor for the equity bull case breaking.
“typically the rule of thumb is around 4%. If you get you know if inflation's under 4% you can still do okay with equities” ▶ 14:52
Commodities are the 'forgotten stepchild of multi-asset' and the best liquid inflation hedge, performing especially in inflation shocks; PGIM Quant always carries some commodity exposure, with energy delivering huge YTD returns on geopolitical risk.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highcommoditiesenergyinflation hedgereal estateinfrastructure
So what: Argues for a structural commodity sleeve in multi-asset portfolios specifically to absorb inflation shocks that bonds and equities cannot.
“Commodities if you ask me are I I kind of call them the the forgotten stepchild of multi-asset” ▶ 18:28
Inflation risk has caused PGIM Quant to shrink active exposures via a short-term tactical overlay while remaining pro-risk overall; their base case is conditions persist 3-6 months and then stabilize rather than worsen ('not going back to where they were, but probably not going to get worse').
prediction🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediuminflationtactical overlayactive risk
So what: Signals a measured de-risking (smaller active bets) rather than outright defensiveness — a template for trimming rather than exiting into elevated inflation.
60/40, privates & allocation problems 3
Reports of 60/40's death are overstated; it still works most of the time and survives, but diversification can now be augmented in ways unavailable 30 years ago — institutional overlays adding convexity/downside protection, return stacking, and democratized derivative-income/derivative-protection products once exclusive to private banks.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: high60/40return stackingderivative incomeconvexity overlays
So what: Frames the practical menu (overlays, return stacking, defined-outcome products) for upgrading a 60/40 rather than abandoning it.
Institutions have over-loaded into private markets and now most commonly ask PGIM Quant to get them back to strategic allocation using liquid, disciplined, systematic exposure with some alpha — because privates can't be sold without accepting a material discount.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highprivate marketsliquiditystrategic asset allocation
So what: Identifies a real allocator pain point — illiquidity overhang from privates — and a growing demand for liquid completion/overlay mandates as the trade.
“once you've got invested in them you largely can't trade them unless you're willing to accept a material discount” ▶ 12:50
