Most PGIM Quant multi-asset mandates are customized solutions to a specific client problem (e.g. realigning a publics/privates blend), not generic '60/40 plus overlay' recommendations; clients rarely arrive with a blank sheet asking 'what do you recommend?'
anecdote🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediummulti-asset solutionscustomized mandatesendowmentsfoundations
So what: Signals the business has shifted toward bespoke completion/solution mandates — relevant to how institutional allocation demand is being met.
Downside protection & options 2
Simply buying puts (or calls) for downside protection is expensive; PGIM Quant instead replaces some equity with longer-dated call exposure and harvests protection through rebalancing, supplemented by defensive-equity and sector-rotation strategies that add convexity as markets fall.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highputscallsconvexitysector rotationdefensive equity
So what: Concrete cost-efficient hedging recipe — the protection comes from rebalancing dynamics, not from paying outright option premium.
≈ “it's very expensive to just go out and buy a call or go out and buy a put” paraphrase
Long-volatility hedges are regime-dependent: they paid off spectacularly in COVID but offered little convexity in 2022, when equities fell while volatility stayed only moderately elevated and never spiked — a caution against assuming long-vol always protects.
causal🗣 host (Alan Dunn)conviction: mediumlong volatility2022COVIDVIX
So what: Grind-down bear markets (like 2022) defeat long-vol hedges; tail-protection design must account for the path of volatility, not just direction.
LLMs & machine learning for alpha 4
PGIM Quant's current ML focus is language models on text — evolving from bag-of-words to BERT/FinBERT sentiment to LLMs that ascertain market themes — which they describe as a rich source of both alpha and, more importantly, risk extraction across quant equity and multi-asset.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highLLMsBERTFinBERTbag of wordsNLPsentiment
So what: Pinpoints text/NLP as the live frontier of quant edge; FinBERT remains 'surprisingly good' for sentiment, so edge is in application not just model size.
“so much of human knowledge is encoded in te in text” ▶ 34:03
LLMs are effective for evaluative tasks like judging the tone of a Fed speaker or scoring text sentiment because they pattern-match speech against the past, but they remain next-token statistical predictors only as good as their training data — not a substitute for understanding.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediumLLMsFederal Reservesentiment analysisnext-token prediction
So what: Calibrates where LLMs add value (sentiment/tone extraction) versus where over-reliance is dangerous, guiding due diligence on 'AI-driven' quant claims.
“all of these LLMs are basically trying to predict the next token that's all they're doing” ▶ 35:48
PGIM Quant keeps a 'human in the loop' — portfolio managers review every trade to ensure consistency with investment philosophy — and prioritizes tracing positioning back to raw underlying data for transparency, rejecting unconstrained robo-trading.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highhuman in the looptransparencyrobo tradingmodel risk
So what: A governance template for systematic AI strategies; transparency/traceability is sold as a feature, useful when vetting black-box managers.
“There's still what we call a human in the loop” ▶ 37:42
Patterson is skeptical AI is yet generating profits: building/calibrating these models costs hundreds of thousands to millions in time, and he doubts anyone is really making money in the space yet despite high commercialization hopes.
contrarian🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediumAImodel calibration costscommercialization
So what: A cautionary read on AI monetization from a practitioner — relevant to the debate over whether the AI capex cycle gets rewarded.
“I I don't know if anyone's really making money in this space yet” ▶ 39:10
Overfitting, adaptiveness & faster regimes 4
The worst thing in model building is optimizing in-sample performance; the real problem is out-of-sample ('what happens next'), so models need just enough complexity to represent the world's richness without becoming a 'loaded weapon.'
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highoverfittingin-sampleout-of-samplebacktestingmodel complexity
So what: On the simplicity-vs-complexity quant debate, Patterson lands on parsimony-plus-judgment; a lens for evaluating whether a strategy's backtest is real edge or curve-fit.
“the absolute worst thing you can do is is optimize in sample performance” ▶ 45:09
PGIM Quant guards against overfitting by forcing researchers to argue what would kill their own research ('give me the scenario that this fails') and runs a humbling exercise freezing development-period parameters to compare them against true out-of-sample live performance — which is usually positive but less positive than the backtest suggested.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highresearch processout-of-sample decayinformation ratiomodel validation
So what: A concrete, transferable research-governance practice (pre-mortem plus frozen-parameter live audit) that allocators can ask managers to demonstrate.
“What I keep my question to them is always what do you have to do to kill this research?” ▶ 46:31
Because policy and market responses are now far faster (SVB's overnight bank run, COVID's record-speed crash and rebound) than slower episodes like the GFC, signals must be designed up front with shorter parameters and kill-switches rather than waiting for failure.
causal🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highSilicon Valley BankCOVIDGlobal Financial Crisispolicy responseliquidity
So what: Compressed reaction times mean panicking at the COVID bottom forfeited the rebound; tactical models need faster decay and pre-built shutoffs to avoid being whipsawed.
“If you if you got if you panicked at the bottom of COVID you really missed out on the rebound” ▶ 50:16
Forward curves are systematically poor predictors of future rates — historically they forecast hikes that never materialize or cuts that never happen — reinforcing a data-dependent, nimble stance over multi-month rate forecasts.
data_point🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highforward curvesinterest ratesrate forecasts
So what: Don't trust market-implied rate paths as forecasts; position for revision rather than anchoring to the curve's projected hikes/cuts.
“rarely is it accurate. I mean it often times they're forecasting hikes which then never materialize” ▶ 41:15
Market structure, retail & quant edge 2
Quant models aim for only a small edge — being right slightly more than 50% of the time (a Moneyball/Billy Beane 'get on first' analogy) — which compounds over time and produces a smoother ride by avoiding big bets; PGIM Quant rarely makes large 'grossly over/undervalued' calls.
framework🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highBilly BeaneMoneyballinformation ratioedge
So what: Sets realistic expectations for systematic returns (many small wins, low single-bet risk) — useful when benchmarking a systematic manager's return profile.
“we want to be right a little bit more than 50% of the time” ▶ 43:04
Rising retail participation and US meme-stock 'pockets of hopes and dreams' create persistent short-run dislocations where market-neutral/long-short equity can be right long-term yet unable to hold the position short-term — but this irrationality is a feature creating opportunity, unlike the institution-dominated bond market.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highretail investorsmeme stockslong-short equitymarket neutralbonds
So what: Retail-driven equity inefficiency is the source of quant-equity alpha; the risk is the short-term 'steamroller,' so position sizing/timing matters more than the thesis being right.
“in the US you know you get these little pockets of hopes and dreams” ▶ 51:36
Current positioning: opportunities & risks 4
Today's mega-cap leaders are unlike the dot-com bubble because they have real cash flows and are profitable businesses (vs. 1999 valuations on clicks and eyeballs), so the open question is whether the AI capex cycle gets rewarded — something Patterson says investors will simply have to wait and see.
contrarian🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediummega-cap techdot-com bubbleAI capexcash flows
So what: Pushes back on the AI-bubble comparison while flagging capex-payback as the real swing factor to monitor for the mega-cap trade.
“the tech bubble was all about names that were not profitable... The mega cap names that are doing well today all have cash flows” ▶ 53:49
Emerging markets are the most common incoming institutional question and PGIM Quant's flagged long-term strategic growth area — long ignored and out of favor, but now showing genuine change with attractive long-term GDP growth and profitability.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediumemerging marketsGDP growthstrategic allocation
So what: A contrarian, under-owned strategic allocation idea backed by rising institutional interest — a candidate for long-term overweight.
“most of the incoming questions we get are about emerging markets” ▶ 54:49
The biggest longer-term unmodelable risk is the US fiscal situation (debt-to-GDP, unaddressed long-term policy) heading toward a 'tipping point' that neither party is tackling; the term premium swinging from significant to negative and now growing again encapsulates these deficit concerns, but the timing of the breaking point is unknowable.
prediction🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediumUS fiscal deficitdebt to GDPterm premiumTreasuries
So what: A slow-burning tail risk for long-duration Treasuries; watch a rising/expanding term premium as the market's signal that fiscal concerns are repricing.
“at some point they are going to become painful enough. We are going to you know hit the so-called tipping point” ▶ 42:02
There is a single dominant macro 'oil/inflation' factor right now but the broad economic data (forward rates, corporate profitability, employment) keeps PGIM Quant in a generally benign, pro-risk environment despite the US economy's surprising resilience and acknowledged risks on the horizon.
thesis🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: mediumrisk assetsUS economyforward ratescorporate profitability
So what: Net read from a major systematic allocator: stay pro-risk but trimmed, with oil/inflation as the factor that would flip the stance.
Career & skills in quant 1
Patterson's top career advice for technical/quant professionals is to invest in communication (writing and speaking), build a network before you need it ('by the time you need it, it's too late'), and commit to continual learning — none of his roles came from blind applications, all from connections.
anecdote🗣 George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)conviction: highcareer advicenetworkingcommunication
So what: Practical guidance for building a career in quant/systematic investing; signals that soft skills increasingly differentiate technical talent.
“you need to build it when you don't need it. And because by the time you need it, it's too late to build it” ▶ 58:39

New Era Finance Podcast 1 episode(s) · 31 insights

Charles Hoskinson: AI Agents Will Run Crypto37 min31 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
Cardano founder Charles Hoskinson argues mainstream crypto adoption will come not from migrating users to new layer-1s but from AI agents transacting on users' behalf across abstracted chains, with privacy (zero-knowledge) as the control layer. He positions Cardano's Midnight as the cross-chain coordinator/middleman that earns fees facilitating intents-based settlement, Bitcoin DeFi, and compliant 'Web 2.5' enterprise flows.
AI agents as the crypto adoption vector · 8Chain abstraction and the middleman thesis · 5Bitcoin DeFi and Cardano's Pogen play · 3Privacy, compliance and zero-knowledge settlement · 4Web 2.5 enterprise on-ramp and Midnight strategy · 5Crypto fundamentals and contagion mechanics · 3Intents and protocol-revenue signals · 3
AI agents as the crypto adoption vector 8
Hoskinson argues that long-term, mainstream users will only interact with cryptocurrencies through AI agents that hold delegated authority, not through wallets and seed phrases directly, making agent infrastructure the real adoption driver.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentscryptocurrencyCardanoMidnight
So what: Bet on agent-payment rails and abstraction layers rather than on getting retail users to learn self-custody; agent-native protocols are the adoption play.
“The only way long-term people are going to use cryptocurrencies is through agents. They're going to delegate authority to AI to do this on their behalf.” ▶ 22:30
Agents are a near-perfect complement to crypto because agents are non-deterministic and creative while blockchains are deterministic and rule-based, and combining them yields a neuro-symbolic system with both provable rules and creative ambiguity-handling.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentsblockchainneuro-symbolic AI
So what: Frames the agent-plus-blockchain stack as the defensible architecture; watch projects fusing LLM agents with on-chain proof systems.
“Blockchains are deterministic, they're rule-based systems... So, if you could put these two things together, you basically create a perfect neuro-symbolic AI system where you get the best of both worlds.” ▶ 26:15
Agents reduce crypto consumer risk because an AI never tires, distinguishes a zero from an 'O' in an address, resists spoofed websites, and cannot be socially engineered when paired with proof systems, so consumer protection actually increases under agent delegation.
causal🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentsconsumer protectionzero-knowledge proofs
So what: Security/UX is the wedge for agent adoption; protocols that harden agents against phishing and address errors gain a safety narrative regulators like.
“The AI never gets tired... It knows the difference between an a zero and an O in terms of an address. It doesn't get confused by spoofed websites. You can't social engineer it if you have proof systems.” ▶ 22:40
Hoskinson says Cardano/Midnight will support the OWS open agent standard in June/July and the X402 agent-payment standard, giving each agent its own wallet and the ability to spend autonomously.
prediction🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highCardanoMidnightOWSX402agent wallets
So what: Concrete near-term catalyst/timeline for ADA/Midnight; X402 and OWS are the agent-commerce standards to track for adoption across chains.
“There's already open standards like OWS for this, and we're just going to support it as of June July here in Cardano Midnight... For example, the X402 standard.” ▶ 23:05
Agent security is a 'public attack surface' problem identical to smart-contract security, so the same techniques (Docker/HSM sandboxing against prompt injection, public-code hardening) transfer one-to-one between the two domains.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentssmart contractsprompt injectionHSMDocker
So what: Crypto-security teams have transferable edge in agent safety; prompt-injection containment is a fundable problem at the agent/chain intersection.
“Agents have to be built for a public attack surface. So, the exact same security problems we have to resolve and the techniques we use are one-to-one applicable in the agentics world.” ▶ 27:32
Hoskinson identifies the core agent paradox: agents must know you to be effective, but the more they know the more dangerous they are, which he says only privacy-preserving (zero-knowledge) sharing resolves for enterprise-scale agent use.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentsprivacyzero-knowledge proofsenterprise
So what: Enterprises with fiduciary duty over IP and customer data are the natural buyers of privacy-controlled agent frameworks; a B2B TAM thesis for ZK plus agents.
“The paradox of agents is that to make them effective, they have to know you. But, the more they know about you, the more dangerous they are to you.” ▶ 28:19
Midnight's privacy stack converges trusted execution environments (agent sandboxes), zero-knowledge proofs (proving behavior matches intent), and multi-party computation (letting agents coordinate without revealing state) as the toolkit for safe agent coordination.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highMidnightTEEzero-knowledge proofsmulti-party computationAI agents
So what: Defines the specific cryptographic primitives underpinning the Midnight pitch; TEE/MPC/ZK convergence is the technical moat to evaluate.
“We have to put trusted execution environments, multi-party computation, zero-knowledge proofs together in just the right flavor that you can build an infinitely scalable privacy system.” ▶ 27:48
Hoskinson built 'Midnight City,' an agentic civilization where users deploy agents that autonomously form a society (marrying, divorcing, making child-support payments), used internally to stress-test X402, OWS and delegated-authority standards while serving as a consumer-facing front end for the privacy network.
anecdote🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: mediumMidnight CityAI agentsX402OWS
So what: A novel go-to-market/testbed for agent standards plus a Tamagotchi-style consumer hook; watch engagement metrics ('watchability', 'empathy') as adoption proxies.
“Literally, you can get an agent, you can put it in or bring your own agent, and then they just start running around doing things inside Midnight City.” ▶ 23:58
Chain abstraction and the middleman thesis 5
Hoskinson argues the biggest fallacy in crypto is launching a new layer-1 expecting users to migrate; with 11 million tokens already issued, 550 million existing users will not move chains, so abstraction (not migration) is the only adoption path.
contrarian🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highlayer-1 blockchainschain abstractionaccount abstraction
So what: Bearish on new L1 token launches as adoption plays; bullish on abstraction/coordination infrastructure that captures users where they already are.
“Everybody's launching a new layer one. There's 11 million tokens... 550 million users aren't going to magically migrate cuz you want them. That's the land of wishful thinking.” ▶ 20:10
Hoskinson positions the largest value capture in crypto as controlling abstraction and cross-chain transactions (the 'middleman'), not running settlement, citing IBM ceding the OS to Microsoft as the analogy for why the connecting layer beats the underlying chain.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highchain abstractionMicrosoftIBMMidnightcross-chain
So what: Reframes investment value away from base-layer tokens toward abstraction/orchestration layers; the 'OS layer' of crypto is where outsized returns sit.
“IBM thought hardware was like where all the money was, so they didn't mind letting Microsoft own the operating system segment. Biggest blunder in the history of business... The real value is who controls abstraction.” ▶ 31:21
Hoskinson predicts agents (not humans) will control the solver and settlement layers of the application-permission-solver-settlement stack and will route liquidity to wherever value is, breaking the liquidity dominance of Bitcoin and ETH.
prediction🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highAI agentsBitcoinEthereumliquidityintentssolvers
So what: Intents-based, agent-routed settlement could redistribute liquidity premia away from incumbents; underlying-chain choice becomes 'immaterial,' compressing L1 moats.
“The solvers and settlement are not going to be controlled by humans. They'll be controlled by agents and agents are going to go where the value is.” ▶ 30:43
Hoskinson illustrates abstracted execution by example: buying one Bitcoin could be silently sourced as 0.2 on Binance, 0.1 as wrapped BTC on Sui, plus portions on Ethereum and Solana, with the consumer seeing none of the multi-chain routing.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highBitcoinBinanceSuiEthereumSolanawrapped Bitcoin
So what: Multi-chain settlement makes per-venue liquidity fungible; venue/chain branding loses pricing power as agents arbitrage across all of them.
“I want to buy a Bitcoin. Okay, well, we're going to get 0.2 of that on Binance and 0.1 of that on the Sui network as a wrapped Bitcoin and some of that on Ethereum and some of that on Solana. You as a consumer see none of that.” ▶ 30:54
Hoskinson invokes tech-monopoly mortality (BlackBerry, Internet Explorer, AOL, MySpace, Yahoo all once had 100M+ users and died) to argue Bitcoin/ETH liquidity dominance is ephemeral and will be displaced when the technology paradigm shifts to agent-routed intents.
contrarian🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highBitcoinEthereumBlackBerryInternet ExplorerAOLMySpace
So what: A contrarian case against assuming permanent BTC/ETH dominance; technology shifts, not user loyalty, reset winners.
“Everybody thinks they're hot and they own the entire world, but technology has a way of breaking monopolies relentlessly.” ▶ 29:43
Bitcoin DeFi and Cardano's Pogen play 3
Hoskinson calls Bitcoin DeFi one of the biggest opportunities in the industry, sizing it at roughly a trillion dollars, and is launching Cardano's 'Pogen' as Cardano's Bitcoin DeFi product to capture it this year.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highBitcoin DeFiPogenCardano
So what: Names a specific TAM and a 2026 Cardano catalyst; Bitcoin-yield products are a sector to watch as idle BTC seeks return.
“We have technology called Pogen. It's our Bitcoin DeFi play and it's the one of the biggest opportunities in our industry, trillion dollars of Bitcoin DeFi.” ▶ 31:45
Hoskinson describes a concrete Bitcoin-yield flow going live on Cardano/Midnight this year: 50 BTC via Taproot bridged through BitVM to Cardano, lent against a stablecoin (USDC/USDM), deployed into a real-world asset for yield, then converted via DEX back to wrapped BTC on the Bitcoin network.
prediction🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highBitcoinPogenBitVMTaprootCardanoUSDMreal-world assetswrapped Bitcoin
So what: A specific, dated product mechanic for evaluating Cardano's Bitcoin DeFi execution; BitVM/Taproot bridging and RWA yield are the components to verify on delivery.
≈ “I'm going to take 50 Bitcoin of my own Bitcoin... put it into Pogen... Through that bridge, BitVM, go to Cardano. And then I'm going to lend it to a stable coin... place it in a real-world asset and it's going to generate a yield.” paraphrase ▶ 31:54
Hoskinson frames the Bitcoin DeFi UX as a single 'big red button that says make me money,' where the user experiences only Bitcoin while Cardano/Midnight handle private orchestration underneath, and argues the same model scales to custodians like Xapo, BitGo and Blockchain.com holding millions of BTC.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highBitcoin DeFiMidnightXapoBitGoBlockchain.comcustodians
So what: Custodian distribution (hundreds of billions in BTC) is the scale-up path; partnerships with large BTC custodians would validate the thesis.
≈ “If it works there, it'll work with Zappo or BitGo or blockchain.com when you talk about millions of Bitcoin, collectively hundreds of billions of dollars of value.” paraphrase
Privacy, compliance and zero-knowledge settlement 4
Hoskinson predicts settlement will evolve to embed zero-knowledge proofs about the actors (jurisdiction, sanctions, collateral) so that 'settlement becomes compliance,' replacing today's value-only transactions with a built-in compliance game of 21 questions.
prediction🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highzero-knowledge proofscompliancesettlementMidnightselective disclosure
So what: ZK-native compliance could let institutions transact on-chain without doxxing counterparties; a regulatory unlock thesis for privacy-with-compliance protocols.
“Settlement is going to start adding proofs to the layer... there's going to be some proof about the actors involved and you play a game of 21 questions and settlement becomes compliance.” ▶ 7:35
Hoskinson notes Zcash's rising valuation is re-elevating the privacy debate and argues privacy-by-default is a 'social lubricant' and the only basis for trust, reframing privacy as enterprise/parental/IP-licensing necessity rather than a tool for bad actors.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highZcashprivacyMidnightzero-knowledge
So what: Cites Zcash's price action as a market signal that privacy is back in favor; supports a rotation thesis into privacy-tech tokens and ZK infrastructure.
“We see Zcash going up in terms of valuations and therefore raising the entire debate surrounding privacy.” ▶ 9:42
Hoskinson argues the market has proven it cannot self-govern (citing FTX, Luna, meme-coin mania, hacks and scams) and that rules are necessary, but warns the US conflates legitimate compliance with geopolitical sanction agendas, eroding moral authority.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highFTXLunameme coinsregulationsanctions
So what: Signals support for rules-based crypto but flags US sanction overreach as a risk that drives demand for jurisdiction-neutral, selective-disclosure rails.
“The market has proven that it cannot self-govern. It just has. You see what happened with FTX and Luna, the meme coin mania, you see the hacks and scams.” ▶ 0:30
Hoskinson promotes 'selective disclosure' over global disclosure, arguing today's transparency has a temporal risk (information disclosed now can be weaponized decades later, e.g. Iraq's Ba'ath Party de-platforming) so users should reveal the minimum viable info per transaction.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highselective disclosureprivacyzero-knowledge proofs
So what: A durable design principle favoring minimal-disclosure credentialing; tailwind for ZK identity/credential systems over full-KYC data collection.
≈ “You don't want a global disclosure regime, you want a selective disclosure regime that you're able to disclose at the time of asking and you disclose the minimum viable amount of information to get the deal done.” paraphrase
Web 2.5 enterprise on-ramp and Midnight strategy 5
Hoskinson defines 'Web 2.5' as legacy Web2 companies entering crypto while keeping boards, shareholders and regulation, and observes the richest people in the industry (CZ now richer than Bill Gates, plus Tether and Circle principals) are Web 2.5 operators, not pure Web3 builders.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highWeb 2.5BinanceCZTetherCircleBill Gates
So what: Directs investor attention to compliant enterprise-crypto hybrids as the wealth-creation cohort; Midnight is positioned as the open home for that segment.
“The richest people in the industry are the Web 2.5 guys, not the Web 3 guys.” ▶ 18:44
Hoskinson says Cardano was architected in the 2016 white paper as a settlement layer (CSL) with a network of control layers / 'partner chains,' and Midnight is one such control layer extending privacy as a modular upgrade to the whole space, not just Cardano.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highCardanoMidnightpartner chainssettlement layer
So what: Frames Midnight as cross-ecosystem infrastructure rather than a Cardano-only feature, broadening its addressable surface and fee opportunity.
“When I wrote the white paper for Cardano back in 2016, I had this idea of Cardano CL or Cardano SL, so a settlement layer and a control layer.” ▶ 16:52
Hoskinson reports Cardano's enterprise deals (Dish, New Balance, Ethiopia) repeatedly stalled along three axes—too complex, not safe/private enough, or compliance too hard—which motivated building Midnight as a reusable privacy+compliance network instead of bespoke one-off solutions.
anecdote🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highCardanoMidnightDishNew BalanceEthiopiaenterprise adoption
So what: Identifies the three concrete blockers (complexity, privacy, compliance) gating enterprise crypto adoption—the checklist to assess any enterprise-chain thesis.
“They'd either stall out because it's too complicated. They'd stall out because it wasn't safe enough... Or the rules were too hard to set, i.e., the compliance side was too difficult.” ▶ 17:28
Hoskinson frames the permissionless-vs-permissioned fight (echoing Hyperledger Fabric and R3 Corda) as recurring in the compliance era, arguing open permissionless compliance networks will beat invitation-only 'closed gardens' controlled by a few actors.
thesis🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highMidnightHyperledger FabricR3 Cordapermissionless blockchains
So what: A competitive framing pitting open compliance rails against permissioned enterprise-consortium chains; a thesis on which model wins regulated flows.
≈ “Are we going to have closed gardens that a handful of invitation only actors control... Or do you want to have an open permissionless system for people to go in and build their web 2.5 business?” paraphrase ▶ 19:24
Hoskinson describes 'Midnight Passport,' a phone-native account-abstraction wallet that needs no 24-word seed backup, uses the phone's secure enclave/ledger and biometrics, recovers at account creation, and via multi-chain signatures can spin up Bitcoin, Ethereum or Solana wallets with fees payable in any token.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highMidnight Passportaccount abstractionBitcoinEthereumSolanamulti-chain signatures
So what: Seedless, cross-chain phone wallets are the UX unlock; multi-chain signature wallets paying fees in any token is a feature set to benchmark competitors against.
“You don't have to back up 24 keywords anymore... when you have multi-chain signatures, you can create a Bitcoin wallet with it or Ethereum wallet with it or Solana wallet.” ▶ 22:03
Crypto fundamentals and contagion mechanics 3
Hoskinson lays out four immutable crypto fundamentals—money wants to be free (liquidity/velocity is king), consumers demand simplicity, stability is king (driving stablecoins), and from stability comes credit then tradable risk—arguing crypto is replaying the Baronial Age of banking but compressed from decades into years.
framework🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highliquiditystablecoinsTetherCirclecreditDeFi
So what: A reusable lens for evaluating protocols: those that maximize liquidity velocity, simplicity and stability win; the banking-history analogy maps where the sector heads next.
≈ “In real time, we're kind of going through what people learned during the Baronial Age of Banking... we're reconstructing Wall Street, but instead of decades, we're doing it in years.” paraphrase
Hoskinson cites MetaMask data that ~35% of wallets are not backed up and most users (even those who shouldn't) leave funds on exchanges, as evidence that simplicity—not decentralization purity—governs real user behavior.
data_point🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: mediumMetaMaskself-custodyexchangesUX
So what: Quantifies the self-custody UX failure; products that remove backup/key burden (account abstraction) address a measured pain point.
“MetaMask, I think, 35% of their wallets don't are backed up, and most people leave their money on exchanges, including people who really really shouldn't.” ▶ 2:11
Hoskinson dissects the Kelp DAO hack as a contagion case study: Ethereum staking lockups drove liquid-staking (Lido) and yield-looping across chains, creating hidden bridge risk; attackers turned a ~$290M problem into a multi-billion-dollar one in 42 minutes by lending stolen assets to create bad debt rather than selling, spiking Ethereum unstaking ~26,000%.
data_point🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highKelp DAOEthereumLidoliquid stakingbridge riskcontagion
So what: A live example of how looped liquid-staking and bridges concentrate hidden systemic risk; flags leverage-looping and bridge exposure as the 2008-style fault lines to monitor.
“In 42 minutes, you go from like a $290 million problem to a multi-billion dollar problem. And now there's an enormous amount of unstaking. Unstaking on Ethereum is up 26,000% or something like that.” ▶ 4:34
Intents and protocol-revenue signals 3
Hoskinson cites NEAR intents as proof of the intents model, noting it went from nothing to billions of dollars of transactions and $71 million in protocol revenue, positioning Midnight to add privacy and take a fee on cross-chain intent settlement.
data_point🗣 Charles Hoskinsonconviction: highNEARintentsprotocol revenuecross-chainMidnight
So what: A concrete revenue proof point for intents ($71M); supports watching NEAR and other intent-settlement layers as monetizable infrastructure.
“Near intents are a great example of that where they went from nothing to, you know, billions of dollars of transactions, $71 million in protocol revenue.” ▶ 21:14
The interviewer discloses he holds NEAR despite not being a 'fanboy,' specifically because of the AI-agent revenue being generated on it, and asks how liquidity can spread beyond Bitcoin/ETH—an investor signal that agent-revenue is driving token selection.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: mediumNEARAI agentsBitcoinEthereumSolanaliquidity
So what: Reflects real allocator behavior: rotating into agent-revenue-generating tokens regardless of brand preference; revenue from agent activity is becoming a buy thesis.
“NEAR is also one of the protocols that I'm not really a fanboy of, but I'm actually holding that token given the fact that there's so much revenue being generated within the AI agents.” ▶ 29:17
The episode discloses an OKX sponsorship around 'X Perps,' a fully regulated crypto-futures platform relaunched in Europe after ~1.5 years, letting users post crypto as collateral, match against OKX's global liquidity engine, and hedge positions while earning funding rates.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: lowOKXX Perpscrypto futuresEuroperegulation
So what: Sponsor read, but signals regulated crypto derivatives returning to Europe—a market-structure data point for EU-regulated perps availability.

Decouple · Chris Keefer 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

Limits to Growth of LLMs w/ David Helmer92 min30 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
AI consultant David Helmer argues the LLM boom is a bubble: scaling laws are hitting diminishing returns, hallucinations/reliability are architecturally unfixable for high-consequence use cases (only code is a partial exception), and trillion-dollar IPO valuations for OpenAI/Anthropic/SpaceX rest on an AGI story with no profitability behind it. The downstream risk is severe market concentration in the Mag 7 plus circular vendor financing — but he thinks data-center-driven nuclear/power demand may persist on sovereign-power and energy-security grounds even if the AI bubble bursts.
Scaling-law breakdown & where capability gains now come from · 4Reliability & hallucinations as an architectural ceiling · 6Compute economics & the unit-cost / monetization problem · 5Bubble, trillion-dollar IPOs & market-concentration risk · 7Datacenter power demand & the nuclear thesis · 4Misalignment, agent risk & where the real harms are · 4
Scaling-law breakdown & where capability gains now come from 4
The LLM 'scaling law' is not a true law and is hitting diminishing returns — throwing more data and compute at transformer models is no longer the main source of capability gains.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMstransformersscaling lawscompute
So what: Undercuts the core investment thesis that more GPUs/datacenters reliably buy better models; watch capex-justification narratives at OpenAI/Nvidia for cracks.
“It's not clear that that scaling law is going to continue to hold. It's not a law in the way of other things that scale where you know every time it gets bigger you get this much better.” ▶ 27:26
The most interesting recent AI advances come from neuro-symbolic methods — adding deterministic rules and symbolic reasoning to LLMs — not from raw scale, which Helmer expects to define future direction.
prediction🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highneuro-symbolic AIsymbolic reasoningLLMs
So what: Capital concentrated purely in scale (mega-clusters, frontier-model training) may be misallocated vs. cheaper hybrid approaches; favors efficiency plays over brute-force compute.
≈ “most of the really interesting recent advances haven't come from scale... putting more symbolic reasoning and and hard deterministic rules into this and marrying them” paraphrase ▶ 27:42
Successive frontier releases (e.g. GPT-5 vs GPT-4) are no longer dramatically better than predecessors, signaling a capability plateau rather than a linear rise toward AGI.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: mediumGPT-5GPT-4OpenAIAGI
So what: A visible plateau removes the 'next model changes everything' option value that underpins valuations.
'AGI' and 'super intelligence' have no agreed definition and function mainly as marketing terms; AI is already superhuman at many narrow tasks without needing a city's worth of power.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAGIsuper intelligence
So what: Frames AGI hype as a fundraising device, not a roadmap — a tell for spotting promotional vs. substantive AI claims.
≈ “There is no such thing first as as AGI or super intelligence... first ask somebody to define it because you'll find that it doesn't really mean anything” paraphrase
Reliability & hallucinations as an architectural ceiling 6
Hallucinations and reliability failures are embedded in ML/LLM architecture and cannot be closed where outputs can't be deterministically verified — every transcription/summarizer tool's terms of use put error liability on the user precisely because vendors have no path to eliminate it.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMshallucinationsmachine learning
So what: Caps the addressable enterprise market to use cases tolerant of error; high-consequence verticals (medical, legal, finance) carry liability that erodes the value prop.
“why do the terms of use always say that errors aren't their fault?” ▶ 46:33
Agent reliability compounds catastrophically: a per-task error rate applied across a 40-step workflow makes end-to-end success collapse, so agentic deployments fail exactly where they'd replace workers.
framework🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highAI agentsagentic workflows
So what: Multi-step agent automation — the basis for labor-replacement ROI claims — is mathematically fragile; discount revenue projections built on agent autonomy.
≈ “if you have a agent that's got a 95% reliability... they have to do a workflow of 40 tasks, if you kind of multiply that out, that becomes a real problem” paraphrase
Updating to a newer model does not monotonically reduce hallucinations — sometimes hallucination rates go up — so 'the next version fixes it' is unreliable.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMsmodel updateshallucinations
So what: Removes the assumption that error rates trend monotonically to zero, weakening forward reliability projections.
“sometimes when they update the model, hallucination rates, as you think of them, go up” ▶ 41:29
Guardrails are inference-based and therefore inherently breakable — jailbreaks via iambic-pentameter poetry, 'it's just a dream' framing, or Morse-code prompts repeatedly defeat safety, making it a permanent whack-a-mole rather than a solvable problem.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLM guardrailsprompt injectionAI safety
So what: Unfixable guardrails create open-ended legal/liability tail risk for any agent touching money or regulated decisions.
“anytime you have an inference-based thing... you can't guarantee the safeguard. That's why the suicide things fail.” ▶ 43:21
Prompt-injection of agents has already caused real losses — one agent was tricked into giving away hundreds of thousands of dollars via hidden Morse code, and at least three production databases have been deleted by rogue agents.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAI agentsprompt injectioncybersecurity
So what: Because agents operate at scale, a single vulnerability propagates fast — a structural risk for fintech/enterprise agent deployments and their insurers.
≈ “one person tricked an agent into giving away hundreds of thousands of dollars by putting Morse code in the prompt” paraphrase
Code is the one near-exception to the reliability problem because outputs can be deterministically tested (CI/CD, vulnerability scans, test suites) and wrapped in neuro-symbolic guardrails — but even there, large production codebases, maintenance, and rerun costs make the value prop ambiguous.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highClaude Codecoding copilotsCI/CDproduction code
So what: If code is the only robust frontier-model use case, the TAM justifying trillion-dollar valuations is far narrower than priced.
“code being sort of a a weird case... the positive use cases are much harder to find than the negative ones” ▶ 53:16
Compute economics & the unit-cost / monetization problem 5
Per-user economics are negative: every added user currently costs the labs money, and $20/month consumer subscriptions don't cover capex on GPUs and datacenters — Helmer frames a roughly 10-order-of-magnitude gap between revenue and full infrastructure cost.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: highOpenAIAnthropichyperscalersGPUsdatacenters
So what: Negative marginal economics with no obvious price escalation path is the bear core; pricing power is the key variable to watch.
“every user that they add is currently costing them money” ▶ 1:18:07
There is little willingness to pay frontier-model prices for low-value tasks — nobody pays hundreds of dollars to write emails faster — so raising subscription prices to reach profitability risks demand collapse.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLM pricingenterprise SaaS
So what: The monetization ceiling, not the tech ceiling, is the binding constraint; pricing experiments (e.g. Anthropic token-count changes that 'almost' caused a revolt) are the canary.
“would you be willing to pay $250 an email to write better” ▶ 1:21:12
GPUs in datacenters have brutal economics — high first-year infant-mortality failure, and effectively none lasting to three years — so datacenters are a continuous capex sink, not a one-time fiber-optic-style buildout.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highGPUsdatacentersNvidia
So what: Recurring chip-replacement capex makes the railroad/fiber 'durable surplus infrastructure eventually gets used' analogy break down for AI compute.
“no you wish you got 5 years out of a chip in a data center... None of them are lasting to three years.” ▶ 1:17:29
OpenAI's Sora video-generation product was effectively pulled back because at scale it 'stopped being a loss leader and became a loss' — a tell that generative video economics don't work.
anecdote🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: mediumSoraOpenAIgenerative video
So what: Concrete evidence that even flagship generative features get cut on cost — a template for how unprofitable AI features unwind.
“your cat video stopped being a loss leader. It just became a loss” ▶ 39:39
Beyond infrastructure and compute, AI labs face large unbooked future costs: paying for training data, labor (e.g. Kenyan content moderators), and mounting legal liability — Anthropic's >$1B settlement for pirated books was reportedly rejected by the judge as too small.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicMetadata licensingcopyright litigation
So what: Today's cost base understates true unit economics; copyright settlements, injunctions, and data-payment obligations are a latent cost wave on top of compute.
≈ “Anthropic had a settlement for pirated books that to my understanding got kicked back by the judge because it wasn't enough at over a billion dollars” paraphrase
Bubble, trillion-dollar IPOs & market-concentration risk 7
Helmer's base case is that AI is a bubble: '95% of LLM investments failed to return value' (MIT study), and the fundamentals can't justify the valuations being floated.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highMIT studyLLMsAI bubble
So what: An explicit bubble call from a technical diligence advisor; the 95% failure stat is a citable bear data point.
≈ “MIT did a study last year where they said 95% of LLM investments failed to return value” paraphrase
OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX are being lined up for ~trillion-dollar IPOs and possible accelerated S&P 500 inclusion without demonstrated profitability — Helmer likens these names to meme stocks trading at 10x–100x any defensible worth.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXS&P 500IPO
So what: Accelerated index inclusion of unprofitable mega-caps would force passive flows into stretched valuations — a structural risk for index investors.
≈ “they're much closer to a meme stock than than they are to that at at this time” paraphrase
Anthropic's reported profit is suspect: it's non-GAAP, excludes GPU capital cost, and appears to lean on a one-quarter discounted-compute deal with SpaceX (~$1B+/month of compute) that reverts to full price afterward.
data_point🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicSpaceXGAAP accounting
So what: Headline 'profitability' may be an accounting artifact tied to a temporary discount — scrutinize whether the next quarter reverses it.
“they don't really tell you how they calculated it... they're not using GAP methods” ▶ 1:16:03
Circular vendor financing props up the boom — labs receive investment from Oracle/Amazon/Nvidia and spend it buying those same vendors' chips and cloud, echoing the dotcom bust; much of Nvidia's operating cash flow flows to customers who then buy Nvidia chips.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highNvidiaOracleAmazonAWSOpenAIdotcom bubble
So what: Circular financing inflates apparent demand; the vendor-customer feedback loop is the classic bubble fragility to monitor.
“you're paying Amazon back with their own money” ▶ 1:19:09
Market concentration is unprecedented: the Magnificent 7 plus prospective trillion-dollar AI IPOs dominate the index and account for much of recent GDP growth via the datacenter buildout, so a re-rating of the AI story would be 'brutal' for the broad market.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highMagnificent 7GoogleAmazonMicrosoftTeslaS&P 500
So what: Single-narrative concentration means an AI-thesis repricing is a whole-index event, not an isolated sector drawdown.
≈ “the degree of market concentration, I don't think we've ever really had that with this kind of exposure” paraphrase
A burst would not bankrupt all players — Google (search), Amazon (AWS), and Microsoft have real non-AI bases — but post-bust the glut of cloud compute everyone built would intensify competition and compress hyperscaler margins.
prediction🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: mediumGoogleAmazonAWSMicrosofthyperscalers
So what: Even survivors face margin compression from compute oversupply — a second-order short on hyperscaler cloud margins distinct from the equity drawdown.
“everybody has now invested in all of this compute... So, they suddenly have more competition than they used to. It's going to put pressure on their margins” ▶ 1:26:20
AI compute is unlike the dotcom fiber-optic overbuild because fiber was durable and got cheaply reused (enabling Amazon-style low-marginal-cost scaling), whereas GPUs depreciate in under three years and each AI user adds heavy marginal cost.
framework🗣 host (Chris Keefer) / guestconviction: highdotcom bubblefiber opticsGPUsAmazon
So what: The 'surplus infrastructure eventually pays off' bull comfort doesn't transfer — depreciating GPUs offer no durable residual value.
Datacenter power demand & the nuclear thesis 4
Keefer believes the sector is 'very solidly in a nuclear bubble,' evidenced by soaring, bumpy valuations of advanced-reactor names like Oklo, with overlap from AI-hype figures (Sam Altman took Oklo public via SPAC).
thesis🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highOkloSam AltmanSPACadvanced reactorsnuclear
So what: Nuclear equities are riding the same AI-demand narrative; if AI power demand evaporates, speculative reactor SPACs are most exposed.
≈ “I think we're very solidly in a nuclear bubble um as evidenced by some of the soaring valuations” paraphrase
AI-driven baseload power demand has catalyzed concrete nuclear restarts — Three Mile Island Unit 1, Palisades, and Duane Arnold — replacing climate as the dominant narrative driver under the Trump administration.
data_point🗣 host (Chris Keefer)conviction: highThree Mile IslandPalisadesDuane Arnoldnuclear restartshyperscalers
So what: These restarts are leveraged to AI demand assumptions; a compute-demand reset would directly threaten their economics and PPAs.
≈ “the restart of 3M island unit one palisades Dwayne Arnold a huge surge in interest in nuclear as a result of of the AI demand” paraphrase
If the AI bubble bursts and projected datacenter power demand evaporates, it undermines the case for new nuclear — but Helmer argues nuclear/clean-power demand may persist anyway on sovereign-power, energy-security, and re-industrialization grounds because governments pivot slowly.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: mediumnuclearenergy securitysovereign powerChinarenewables
So what: Decouples the longer-term nuclear/power thesis from the AI bubble — the durable bull case for power is geopolitical, not compute-driven.
“I don't know that an AI collapse necessarily means a collapse on that part of the energy market” ▶ 1:30:11
'Datacenters in space' is not happening — half the chips would die within a year with no maintenance possible — making it an unrealistic basis for valuations (e.g. SpaceX) that price in space datacenters and lunar colonization.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highSpaceXspace datacentersGPUs
So what: Flags speculative 'optionality' (space compute) baked into SpaceX's trillion-dollar valuation as non-credible.
“in a year half your chips are dead and you can't do maintenance in space” ▶ 1:17:41
Misalignment, agent risk & where the real harms are 4
The genuine near-term danger is not sentient super-AI but misaligned automated systems given real-world permissions at scale — social-media ad-revenue optimization is the canonical example of an objective-function gone wrong, not a sci-fi paperclip maximizer.
framework🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAI alignmentsocial mediaAI agentsNick Bostrom
So what: Reframes 'AI risk' as a permissions/governance failure mode — relevant for assessing operational and regulatory risk in agent deployments.
“this isn't theoretical. This is what social media is.” ▶ 1:05:58
AI doom and 'too dangerous to release' messaging functions to pump valuations and dodge liability — an 'effective altruism' framing of infinite future benefit lets companies discount present-day harms (e.g. the suicide cases) to near zero.
contrarian🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: higheffective altruismOpenAISam AltmanAI safetyEd Zitron
So what: Treats existential-risk PR as a marketing/liability instrument — a lens for discounting 'capabilities so dangerous' announcements.
≈ “The idea that AGI is coming and we must be there first is really helpful if you're trying to justify hundreds of billions of dollars of investment.” paraphrase
Anthropic's framing of Claude as a sentient 'entity' would be commercially convenient — it could shift legal accountability onto the AI — yet their own constitution lets the model register objections while still being forced to comply, undercutting the safety-leader positioning.
thesis🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highAnthropicClaudeAI sentienceliability
So what: Anthropic's safety/ethics branding is, in Helmer's read, inconsistent with its conduct (DoD deployment, waived safety commitments, shipped known-defective models) — relevant to reputational/regulatory risk pricing.
“if some of the companies actually believed what they were saying about these models being... entities, you would have to have pretty significant questions about what they're doing” ▶ 56:23
LLMs are excellent for fraud and low-trust scale attacks — a criminal sending a thousand scam emails needs only one to land — so the clearest reliable use cases skew negative (hacking, fraud) rather than positive enterprise value.
causal🗣 David Helmer (guest, AI consultant)conviction: highLLMsfraudcybersecurity
So what: If the most error-tolerant, scalable use cases are malicious, the legitimate monetizable surface shrinks further — and cyber-defense becomes a growth adjacency.
≈ “if you are a criminal organization trying to defraud people, LBS are actually great” paraphrase

All-In Podcast 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

The IPO Comeback: Why Tech Giants Are Finally Going Public | All-In Liquidity IPO Panel32 min30 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
An All-In liquidity panel with Brad Gerstner (Altimeter), Andrew Feldman (Cerebras) and Will Marshall (Planet Labs) argues the IPO window has reopened: Cerebras priced its AI-chip IPO at $185 to a ~$50-60B cap, Planet 10x'd in the public markets, and Gerstner contends the 'stay-private-forever' pendulum is swinging back, meaning more sub-$5B companies should IPO so public investors—not just private holders—capture the multi-trillion AI/space upside.
IPO window reopening & the stay-private pendulum · 6Cerebras IPO mechanics & valuation · 3Where the IPO value accrues: private vs public capture · 6Space economy: imaging, launch costs & data centers in orbit · 7AI silicon: domain-specific architecture vs the GPU · 5Public-market discipline & the AI/space convergence · 3
IPO window reopening & the stay-private pendulum 6
2026 could be an all-time record year for IPOs, with the AI/tech listing window having swung from shut to wide open.
prediction🗣 host / panel introconviction: highIPO marketCerebrasAI
So what: Signals a re-opening primary-issuance window; watch for a wave of AI and tech listings as supply of new public equity rises.
“Hey, 2026 could be an all-time record for IPOs.” ▶ 0:00
Brad Gerstner argues the decade-long 'stay private forever' thesis pushed by Andreessen Horowitz is reversing, with portfolio companies now planning to IPO at $1B, $3B or $5B valuations.
thesis🗣 Brad Gerstner, founder/CEO Altimeter Capitalconviction: highAltimeter CapitalAndreessen HorowitzIPO marketventure capital
So what: If small-cap tech IPOs resume, public investors regain access to early-stage compounding; bullish for IPO underwriters and growth-equity secondary liquidity.
“We had this period of a decade where Andrees was really pushing stay private forever and I see the pendulum swinging back” ▶ 29:52
Gerstner rejects the view that Anthropic, OpenAI and SpaceX staying private at multi-trillion valuations is the 'new normal,' predicting public markets are shifting back toward earlier listings.
contrarian🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highAnthropicOpenAISpaceXIPO market
So what: A contrarian call against mega-private-rounds; implies the locus of AI value creation may migrate back to listed equities investors can actually buy.
“I hear a lot of people thinking that anthropic open AI and SpaceX are the new normal. I actually think the public markets may be shifting back in this direction” ▶ 29:33
Cerebras's path to IPO was blocked for years because the UAE was an investor, triggering CFIUS scrutiny under the Biden administration before conditions turned favorable.
causal🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highCerebrasUAECFIUSBiden administration
So what: Foreign (esp. Gulf) cap-table exposure is a gating risk for AI-hardware IPOs; regulatory regime change can flip a name from un-listable to hot.
“One of their investors was the UAE. So there was questions about CPHAS” ▶ 5:05
Gerstner frames IPO timing as binary—nine and a half years of 'really hard' followed by twelve months of 'really easy' where everybody wanted in—rather than a smoothly improving market.
framework🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highIPO marketCerebras
So what: IPO windows are step-functions, not gradients; investors should position before the 'easy' 12-month window rather than chase it.
“everything was really hard until it got really easy like n 9 and a half years of really hard and then 12 months you know of of of really easy where everybody wanted to get in” ▶ 5:18
Feldman says Cerebras got IPO timing right only by getting it wrong for a decade, implying durable execution—not market timing—is what ultimately opens the window.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldman, founder/CEO Cerebrasconviction: highCerebras
So what: Reinforces that survivable fundamentals, not tactical timing, gate a successful listing; a filter for evaluating IPO candidates.
“a lot of people asked us about how we got the timing right ... the answer is by getting it wrong for a decade” ▶ 6:17
Cerebras IPO mechanics & valuation 3
Cerebras priced its IPO at $185 with the range raised two times, opened at ~$320, and trades around $230, implying roughly a $50-60B market cap.
data_point🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highCerebrasIPO market
So what: A concrete demand signal—2x upsized range and a $320 open—shows aggressive appetite for AI-silicon equity; the pullback to $230 frames a post-IPO entry band.
“They priced the IPO at 185 which was up the range was taken up two times ... The stock opened at $320 a share I think. Today it's at 230 bucks a share, 5060 billion dollars in market cap” ▶ 5:33
Feldman argues that going public changes nothing operational—no extra sales, no engineering progress, same vendor relationships—beyond putting more cash in the bank.
thesis🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highCerebrasIPO market
So what: Tempers the narrative that an IPO is a business catalyst; the value is capital and validation, so post-IPO theses should rest on fundamentals not the listing event.
“not a damn thing changes in the important parts of your business” ▶ 4:11
Cerebras and its bankers structured an innovative 'dribble lockup' that releases shares over six months subject to performance hurdles, a structure Gerstner expects SpaceX to copy.
data_point🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highCerebrasSpaceXAltimeter CapitalIPO lockups
So what: Performance-gated, staggered lockup releases reduce post-lock supply shocks; a structural template to watch in upcoming marquee IPOs including SpaceX.
“you guys have innovated with the banks on what I call a dribble lockup. So over 6 months the shares can be dribbled out according to a bunch of performance hurdles” ▶ 26:51
Where the IPO value accrues: private vs public capture 6
Gerstner contends more money is made after the IPO than before—true in both percentage and absolute terms—because venture rounds can only absorb modest capital while post-IPO markets can absorb far more.
framework🗣 Brad Gerstner / Andrew Feldmanconviction: highIPO marketventure capitalCerebras
So what: Argues holders should not reflexively distribute at IPO; deployable capital and absolute-dollar upside favor staying long winners post-listing.
“historically more money's made after IPO than before ... there is more money to be made both in percentage and in ... absolute” ▶ 27:39
Altimeter invested in a company pre-IPO at a $1B valuation, distributed shares at $3-4B under LP pressure, then watched it run to $50B over the next 24 months—a cautionary example of distributing too early.
anecdote🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highAltimeter CapitalIPO marketLPs
So what: LP-driven early distribution can forfeit the bulk of returns; a live argument for funds and LPs to hold listed winners through the lockup and beyond.
“We invested preipo at a billion dollars. We distributed the shares I think at three or four billion and then it went to 50 billion over ... the course of the next 24 months” ▶ 26:19
Planet Labs went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$2B valuation; ~90% of the value was created only in years three and four, and most early VCs (DST, Founders Fund, Capricorn) and Google held rather than sold.
data_point🗣 Will Marshall, founder/CEO Planet Labsconviction: highPlanet LabsGoogleDST GlobalFounders FundCapricornSPAC
So what: A proof point that public-market patience captured the 10x; SPAC stigma masked a winner whose value compounded years after listing.
“Really, it wasn't until year three or four that 90% of the value was created” ▶ 25:24
Planet Labs' stock rose from $5 to $50 (a 10x) in the public markets over roughly 12 months, which Gerstner cites as venture-scale returns occurring on listed equity.
data_point🗣 Brad Gerstner / Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet Labs
So what: Concrete evidence that a small-cap space name can deliver VC-like multiples publicly—supports the thesis that IPO-ing earlier hands upside to public investors.
“over the last 12 months your stock has gone from five bucks a share to 50 bucks a share, some 10x move in the public markets” ▶ 8:08
Gerstner argues the three multi-trillion mega-IPOs being discussed concentrate value with private-market investors, whereas earlier listings (Cerebras at ~$10B vs ~$50B, per his estimate) would have handed a ~5x to public investors.
thesis🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highIPO marketCerebrasSpaceXOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Quantifies the cost of staying private—public investors are structurally excluded from early AI compounding; a policy/market argument for earlier IPOs.
“Had Andrew had his way, he would have been public 18 months ago, probably at $10 billion rather than $50 billion. And that 5x ... would have gone to public market investors” ▶ 29:14
Marshall notes most big tech companies went public at a few billion, not a few trillion, so for SpaceX to replicate that liftoff from its current valuation it would need to target 'quadrillion' valuations—an implausibly high bar.
causal🗣 Will Marshallconviction: mediumSpaceXIPO market
So what: Flags that buying mega-cap private names near peak private valuations leaves little public upside; the math caps the multiple SpaceX investors can expect.
“for the equivalent liftoff, SpaceX would have to be aiming at quadrillion valuations” ▶ 28:39
Space economy: imaging, launch costs & data centers in orbit 7
Planet Labs operates the largest earth-imaging fleet (~200 satellites) imaging the entire earth daily, producing a time-series of the planet usable by farmers, energy firms, civil governments and defense.
data_point🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet Labsearth observationsatellitesdefense
So what: A unique daily-refresh geospatial dataset is the moat; recurring multi-sector demand underpins the revenue base.
“We have the largest earth imaging fleet about 200 satellites. They image the entire earth every day.” ▶ 10:38
Marshall estimates a $75-100B addressable market in earth observation alone, expanded by applying large language models to earth imagery.
data_point🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet Labsearth observationAILLMs
So what: Sizes the near-term TAM and links it to the AI wave; an AI-data-layer angle on a listed space name.
“I I estimate there's a 75 to$100 billion market just on Earth observation” ▶ 14:55
Two structural cost drivers reshaped space economics: launch costs fell ~4-5x over 10 years, and satellite miniaturization cut craft that once cost ~$1B and weighed 20 tons to a few-to-hundreds of kg—analogous to the mainframe-to-desktop transition.
framework🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highSpaceXsatellitesStarlinkspace economy
So what: The cost collapse plus miniaturization unlocks a long tail of space applications; a secular tailwind for space-data and launch-dependent businesses.
“the same satellite that used to cost a billion dollars and weigh 20 tons now cost a few kg or a few tens or hundreds of kg” ▶ 12:46
Planet's study with Google found orbital data centers become cheaper than terrestrial ones once launch costs fall to ~$200-300/kg; costs are ~$1,000/kg today, down ~10x in a decade, and likely to reach the threshold in 2-3 years via Starship.
data_point🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet LabsGoogleSpaceXStarshipdata centers
So what: A concrete cost-curve trigger (~$200-300/kg) for the space-data-center thesis; track Starship launch pricing as the catalyst gating a trillion-dollar market.
“when launch costs come down to about $200 to $300 a kilogram ... it would be cheaper, just simply cheaper to put the data centers in space. Now we're about $1,000 a kilogram” ▶ 15:40
Orbital data centers can run on solar alone because a sun-synchronous dawn-dusk orbit gathers ~5x more energy per panel than ground and needs no batteries, gas or nuclear—simplifying the power problem to panels, chips and RF links.
framework🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highdata centerssolar powerspace economy
So what: Reframes data centers as a power game that space solves elegantly; an argument that could redirect energy-constrained AI compute investment off-planet.
“you can put a solar panel in a suns synchronous ... dawn dusk orbit where you're 24/7 looking at the sun. So you can have a solar panel that collects ... five times more energy per solar panel than on the ground” ▶ 16:42
Marshall predicts that within 10 years most compute will be placed in space—a market worth trillions and larger than any other space business including earth imaging—and Planet is launching Google TPUs (after already launching Nvidia GPUs) on early tests.
prediction🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet LabsGoogleNvidiaTPUsGPUsdata centers
So what: A trillion-dollar 10-year TAM call with live hardware tests; positions Planet as infrastructure for orbital compute and ties Google/Nvidia silicon to space.
“no question within 10 years most compute will be putting in space, which to give you a sense is a lot of money, like trillions” ▶ 17:37
Feldman is skeptical of near-term orbital data centers, noting the industry can't yet build the clusters and inter-chip communication needed even on the ground, and warns it may be a 'last 10% is 80% of the time' problem like self-driving.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: mediumdata centersPlanet Labsself-driving
So what: A credible technical pushback on the space-data-center timeline; tempers Planet's 10-year claim and flags clustering/interconnect as the binding constraint.
“We're not good at doing it on the ground. We're really not good at doing it in space.” ▶ 18:34
AI silicon: domain-specific architecture vs the GPU 5
Feldman argues AI's key economic unlock since ~2015-16 was letting computers attack images and language—previously foreclosed domains—which simultaneously expanded the addressable workload and the data being generated.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highAICerebrascompute
So what: Frames AI demand as an aperture-widening of compute, not just faster chips; supports a durable, broad-based compute-consumption growth thesis.
“what AI did starting in about 2015 16 is it opened the door the aperture” ▶ 20:18
Feldman's framework: new workloads are when compute market share changes hands—graphics birthed Nvidia, mobile moved share to ARM from Intel/AMD, networking created Cisco/Juniper/Arista—and AI is the next such shift.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highNvidiaARMIntelAMDCiscoJuniperArista
So what: A reusable lens: workload transitions, not incumbency, redistribute silicon share—justifies betting on AI-native challengers over incumbents.
“Share changed when the rise of graphics emerged and you got the dedicated GPU. That's how Nvidia was born.” ▶ 21:57
Feldman argues that to be 20x better than Nvidia an architecture cannot look like a GPU, because a GPU-shaped design has no path to beat the incumbent who already harvested the low-hanging fruit.
thesis🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highCerebrasNvidiaGPU
So what: A differentiation test for evaluating AI-chip challengers—me-too GPU designs are dismissible; only radically different architectures can take share.
“if you build a GPU, the odds that you're better than Nvidia in our view are approximately zero” ▶ 23:08
Cerebras's bet was a dinner-plate-sized wafer chip with memory placed next to compute, solving the core memory-to-compute data-movement bottleneck and enabling faster memory than postage-stamp-sized GPUs allow.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highCerebrasNvidiaGPUAI silicon
So what: Names the architectural moat (wafer-scale + co-located memory) behind Cerebras's speed claims; the technical basis for its differentiation story.
≈ “The hard part here ... is moving data from memory to compute. This is the fundamental problem in AI. And we solved it ... to build a very big chip and to put memory right next to compute.” paraphrase ▶ 23:16
Feldman claims Cerebras runs 15-18x faster than a GPU when OpenAI uses it, arguing inference latency is existential because users won't wait for AI any more than they tolerate dial-up or slow search.
data_point🗣 Andrew Feldmanconviction: highCerebrasOpenAIGPUinference
So what: Positions latency as the competitive axis in inference and names OpenAI as a customer—an edge that could drive share gains in real-time AI workloads.
“when OpenAI uses us, we're 15 or 18 times faster than a GPU” ▶ 23:57
Public-market discipline & the AI/space convergence 3
Gerstner argues the scrutiny of public markets sharpens focus and accelerates innovation—'steel sharpens steel'—so going public sooner is where great companies are forged, not just where liquidity is found.
thesis🗣 Brad Gerstnerconviction: highIPO marketCerebrasPlanet Labs
So what: Reframes the IPO as an operational catalyst via accountability; supports a structural bullish case for the renewed early-IPO trend.
“getting public sooner having the scrutiny of public markets ... sharpens the focus it steel sharpens steel iron sharpens iron” ▶ 30:19
Marshall predicts AI plus space converge into 'large earth models'/'planetary intelligence'—feeding real-world satellite data into LLMs that are otherwise 'blind' to the physical world—unlocking a huge new application economy.
prediction🗣 Will Marshallconviction: highPlanet LabsAILLMsearth observation
So what: Positions proprietary earth data as the scarce input that grounds LLMs in reality; a differentiated AI-data thesis distinct from text-only models.
“all the cool stuff that we're doing on the with LLMs now is really based on just the text of the internet ... they don't know about the real world. I call them blind” ▶ 31:20
Marshall reframes Planet from a passive map/data source toward a tool for accomplishing tasks—including security/defense use cases that now form a larger revenue fraction than originally expected—by giving customers advance warning to prevent conflict.
thesis🗣 Will Marshallconviction: mediumPlanet Labsdefenseintelligenceearth observation
So what: A defense-revenue mix shift raises both growth and geopolitical/regulatory exposure; relevant to valuing Planet as part dual-use defense-tech.
“security ... is part of the initial thing that we said we would do ... but it's true there's a bigger fraction today than perhaps we would have guessed” ▶ 11:34

What Bitcoin Did · Peter McCormack 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

The State Has Become a Monster | Julian Jessop66 min28 insights2026-06-06
▶ Watch on YouTube
Classical-liberal economist Julian Jessop argues the UK's post-2008 stagnation is self-inflicted policy failure from an oversized state (40% of GDP, near-record tax burden, 90+ taxes), and lays out an investor-relevant map of UK fiscal/political risk: Reeves's tax-led fiscal headroom, bond-market sensitivity to a Starmer/Reeves exit, supply-side energy and planning failures, and a base case that the UK can outperform Europe on AI and light-touch regulation IF it avoids an EU regulatory 'reset.'
UK oversized-state thesis & supply-side stagnation · 5Labour-market & minimum-wage policy risk · 4Price controls & the war on markets · 3UK politics, gilts & market sensitivity · 5NHS, public-sector productivity & state IT failure · 4Comparative models: Milei, China, Singapore, Poland · 3UK base case & AI/Brexit optionality · 4
UK oversized-state thesis & supply-side stagnation 5
Julian Jessop argues UK productivity has been structurally weak since the 2008 global financial crisis and that this — not inequality or the fading IT revolution — is the core driver of stagnant living standards, framing the slowdown as a fixable policy failure rather than an inevitable structural decline.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK economyproductivity2008 financial crisis
So what: Bull case for UK assets is contingent on a supply-side policy reversal; without it, expect continued low-growth, low-productivity drift that caps UK equity and currency upside.
“since then, pretty much everything has has gone horribly wrong” ▶ 0:01
Jessop says the UK state has grown to roughly 40% of national income and argues the appropriate size is closer to 20% — implying the entire current fiscal and regulatory footprint is roughly double what he considers efficient.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK government spendingGDPpublic goods
So what: Frames the magnitude of any credible 'small-state' reform program; investors should treat a halving of state share as the radical-bull scenario, not the base case.
“I state it's about 40% of of of national income taxing or or spending. Um, I think it could be maybe half that, maybe 20%” ▶ 48:30
Jessop identifies UK energy and housing supply as the root macro problem — the UK imports oil and gas (including North Sea gas drilled by Norway on the opposite side) and under-builds housing and infrastructure due to restrictive planning, rather than addressing demand-side symptoms.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highNorth Sea oil and gasNorwayUK housingplanning reformenergy market
So what: Domestic energy production and planning liberalisation are the key catalysts to watch for UK homebuilders, energy producers and infrastructure plays; continued under-supply keeps cost-of-living and inflation pressure structurally elevated.
“we're importing um oil and gas including you know oil and gas that Norway has dug out from the opposite side of the North Sea from us which makes no sense at all” ▶ 0:13
Jessop contends the UK has at least 90 separate taxes — including at least one levied on something that no longer exists — creating a large 'dead weight' that disproportionately burdens small businesses while entrenching large incumbents who can afford compliance teams and lobbyists.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK tax systemsmall businesseslarge corporatescompliance
So what: Regulatory and tax complexity is a structural moat for large-cap UK incumbents versus SMEs; favours scaled players and is a headwind for UK small-cap/AIM exposure.
“we have at least you 90 separate taxes now in the UK” ▶ 21:47
Jessop frames a 'ratchet' mechanism in UK fiscal policy whereby spending commitments like the winter fuel payment and the state-pension triple lock are nearly impossible to remove once introduced, locking in structurally rising entitlement spending.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highwinter fuel paymentstate pension triple lockGordon Brownentitlement spending
So what: Entitlement ratchet implies the UK fiscal trajectory bends toward higher spending and taxes regardless of governing party — a structural negative for gilts and the long-run tax burden.
“anytime something is introduced it's then almost impossible to to take it away” ▶ 13:30
Labour-market & minimum-wage policy risk 4
Jessop attributes the recent spike in UK youth unemployment — rising at a pace last seen during COVID and the 2008 crisis — to government interventions, specifically the national minimum wage and day-one employment rights that make employers reluctant to hire inexperienced young workers.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highUK youth unemploymentnational minimum wageemployment rightslabour market
So what: Rising youth unemployment is a leading indicator of UK labour-market weakness; watch for further hiring deterioration in low-margin, labour-intensive sectors (hospitality, retail).
“unemployment amongst young people is has spiked again. Uh the last time it rose this quickly was was during COVID and before then it was during the global financial crisis” ▶ 9:00
Jessop describes the proposed £15/hour minimum wage and France's relatively generous minimum wage (with even higher youth unemployment than the UK) as cases where wage floors raise unemployment and hurt the intended beneficiaries.
contrarian🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highminimum wageFranceyouth unemployment
So what: A £15 minimum wage would pressure margins in UK hospitality/retail toward unviability per McCormack's own coffee-shop example; a watch-item for UK consumer-facing labour-intensive businesses.
“every historical example has shown that if you if you cap rents or massively increase minimum wages, it ends up hurting the very people you're supposed to help” ▶ 40:04
Peter McCormack states that a £15 minimum wage would push his coffee-shop business from viable to unviable, forcing layoffs — a first-hand operator data point on how wage-floor increases convert thin-margin UK hospitality businesses into loss-makers.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host, business owner)conviction: highminimum wageUK hospitalitysmall business
So what: Ground-truth on UK SME hospitality fragility under rising labour costs; reinforces the structural margin squeeze thesis for the sector.
“it would take the business from viable to unviable” ▶ 7:45
Jessop says Labour's manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, VAT or corporation tax forced it to raise employer National Insurance contributions instead — a 'tax on jobs' (Reeves's own prior phrase) that is now contributing to labour-market weakness.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highemployer National InsuranceRachel Reevesincome taxVATcorporation taxLabour
So what: Employer NIC increases are a direct margin and hiring headwind for UK labour-intensive employers; the manifesto tax-lock means further revenue will likely come through stealth/marginal taxes, raising policy unpredictability.
“As Rachel Ree herself said when she was shadow chancellor, it's a tax on jobs” ▶ 22:44
Price controls & the war on markets 3
Jessop warns of an escalating UK 'war on prices' — rent caps returning to favour, minimum-wage hikes, voluntary supermarket price caps, and courts deciding 'fair' comparative pay between shop and warehouse workers — arguing administered prices distort the signalling function markets rely on.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highrent controlsprice capssupermarketsminimum wageequal pay litigation
So what: Spreading price administration raises regulatory risk for UK landlords, supermarkets and retail employers; rent caps specifically would reduce housing supply and quality, a negative for residential rental and homebuilder economics.
“there seems to be this war on prices” ▶ 27:51
Jessop argues Reform UK's proposal to exempt overtime above 40 hours/week from income tax — modelled on a US idea — would fail as it did in France, where well-off people with accountants reclassified hours as overtime, lower-paid workers gained nothing, and tax revenue fell.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highReform UKovertime tax exemptionFrancetax avoidance
