So what: Signals that headline 'pro-grafter' tax giveaways are gameable and revenue-negative; investors should discount populist tax-cut pledges from both Reform and Greens as fiscally unfunded.
“all that happened is that relatively well-off people with clever accountants fiddled their arrangements to make sure that more of their hours counted as overtime” ▶ 19:30
Jessop frames regulatory complexity with the maxim that 'the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it,' arguing dense rules entrench large businesses (who can staff HR, legal and compliance) at the expense of small competitors.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highregulationlarge corporatesSMEscompliance
So what: Reinforces that rising UK regulation is a competitive moat for incumbents — structurally favours large-cap defensives over UK small-caps and new entrants.
“the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it” ▶ 20:23
UK politics, gilts & market sensitivity 5
Jessop says UK financial markets have reacted negatively every time Keir Starmer or Rachel Reeves looked at risk of leaving, because the market views the alternatives (e.g. Andy Burnham) as worse for fiscal stability — making Reeves's perceived commitment to fiscal rules a stabiliser for gilts and sterling.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highKeir StarmerRachel ReevesAndy BurnhamgiltsUK bond market
So what: A leadership change away from Reeves is a tail risk for gilts and sterling; the bond market is effectively pricing Reeves as the fiscal-credibility anchor — watch Labour leadership signals as a gilt catalyst.
“every time there have been concerns that either Starmmer or Rachel Reeves might might go, the markets have reacted negatively” ▶ 1:00:19
Jessop notes Reeves built extra fiscal headroom into the budget but did so by raising taxes rather than controlling spending, which markets disliked even as they credit her commitment to the fiscal rules.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumRachel Reevesfiscal headroomUK budgetfiscal rules
So what: Tax-led rather than spending-led consolidation keeps the UK tax burden rising and growth suppressed; the quality of any future headroom-building (taxes vs cuts) is a gilt and growth signal.
“she's done it by raising taxes, not by controlling spending” ▶ 1:00:46
Jessop argues the 2022 Truss/Kwarteng 'mini budget' bond-market blowup was already calming and was driven partly by unlucky timing — the Bank of England starting to sell gilts (QT) into a fragile market amid globally rising rates — rather than purely by the policy itself.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumLiz TrussKwasi KwartengBank of Englandquantitative tighteninggiltsmortgage market
So what: Reframes UK gilt fragility as partly a QT/liquidity timing problem; relevant for assessing how future unfunded fiscal moves could interact with BoE balance-sheet operations to trigger disorderly gilt selloffs.
“the bank of England was starting to sell government bonds back onto the market so the bond market was fragile interest rates were rising around the rest of the world” ▶ 24:21
Jessop says the Greens and Reform UK are between them polling over 50% by promising popular but unfunded policies, with Reform paradoxically favouring nationalisation and keeping the pension triple lock — i.e. expanding rather than shrinking the state.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highGreen PartyReform UKnationalisationtriple lock
So what: Rising vote share for fiscally expansionary insurgent parties raises medium-term UK policy and gilt risk; nationalisation rhetoric is a specific overhang for UK utilities and rail/water-adjacent equities.
“between them, they're probably polling more than 50% in the poll” ▶ 14:40
Jessop names Gary Stevenson ('Gary's Economics') as a credible-seeming public advocate lending intellectual cover to redistributionist/rent-control policies (alongside Zack Polanski/Greens), illustrating how city-trader backstories are used to legitimise interventionist economics.
anecdote🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumGary StevensonZack PolanskiGreen Partyrent controls
So what: Maps the influencers shaping UK left-wing economic narrative; their rising platform is a leading indicator of redistributionist policy momentum and associated UK asset-tax/rent-control risk.
“Gary Stevenson is one um v various others I can mention as well who who who have a sort of veneer of intellectual respons respectability” ▶ 44:53
NHS, public-sector productivity & state IT failure 4
Jessop argues the NHS delivers one of the worst standards of care in the developed world and identifies three root causes — unreliable Treasury-dependent funding, lack of competition stifling innovation, and heavy unionisation resisting change — while pointing to mixed public-private models in Europe and Asia (not the US) as the reform template.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highNHShealthcarepublic-private partnershipunions
So what: A shifting NHS reform debate toward European-style mixed models is a long-horizon tailwind for UK private healthcare providers and insurers; watch polling shifts as the catalyst.
“the hugely bloated National Health Service um which provides one of the worst standards of care anywhere in the in the developed world” ▶ 49:30
Both speakers point to a thriving private GP market (e.g. a £50 walk-in 'medic centre' in Bedford) as a free-market response to NHS waiting lists, suggesting growing consumer willingness to pay out-of-pocket for faster UK primary care.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: mediumprivate GP servicesNHS waiting listsprimary care
So what: Out-of-pocket private primary-care demand is rising structurally — a growth signal for UK private clinics, diagnostics and health-services businesses.
“£50 for an appointment” ▶ 51:27
Jessop argues the UK government is systematically bad at large IT and procurement — citing the Post Office Horizon scandal and warning a state digital ID scheme will be late, broken at launch and leak data — because a monopoly buyer over-pays for a single system with no competitive selection.
causal🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highdigital IDPost Office Horizon scandalgovernment procurementHS2
So what: Flags execution risk in UK state digital programmes; relevant for assessing government-IT contractors and the political durability of any digital-ID rollout.
“the the NHS of course would only want to have one system as well for which it would massively overpay and we saw the problems with that with the the post office scandal” ▶ 54:02
Jessop credits the 2010-era Cameron government with materially improving public-sector productivity (without large real spending cuts — spending rose but was controlled), and says public-sector productivity is now going backwards because the current government is throwing money at services without structural reform.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumDavid Cameronpublic sector productivitygovernment spending
So what: Declining public-sector productivity under unreformed higher spending implies poor fiscal multipliers and worsening value-for-money — a structural drag on UK growth and the public finances.
“it's now going backwards because all the government is doing is throwing more money at it rather than structurally reforming it” ▶ 38:08
Comparative models: Milei, China, Singapore, Poland 3
Jessop cites Javier Milei's Argentina as a positive real-world test of shock free-market reform — where ~100+ economists predicted surging poverty and collapse but roughly the opposite occurred — and invokes the Laffer curve and anti-nationalisation arguments Milei popularised.
data_point🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumJavier MileiArgentinaLaffer curvenationalisation
So what: Milei's apparent success is the template free-market reformers will cite for the UK; useful as a base-rate reference for how crisis-then-reform can re-rate a distressed economy and its assets.
“pretty much the opposite has has happened” ▶ 46:33
Jessop argues China's and Singapore's edge is institutional — the absence of frequent elections lets China plan long-term and build fast (contrasting with HS2 delays), while Singapore recruits genuinely impressive officials from military/business/academia, implying a big state only works with high-calibre administrators.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumChinaSingaporeHS2long-term planning
So what: Frames why UK state capacity is weak (short electoral cycles, low-calibre administrators); relevant to judging whether large UK infrastructure programmes can ever deliver on time/budget.
“I honestly think we will colonize Mars more quickly than we will complete HS2” ▶ 34:22
Jessop says Poland is on track to overtake the UK on living standards within 5-10 years, crediting its simple tax system, straightforward planning rules and well-run economy — a concrete benchmark of relative UK decline.
prediction🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumPolandUKliving standardstax systemplanning
So what: Relative-decline benchmark: Poland overtaking the UK is a tangible marker of Central European convergence — supports a structural preference for reform-track CEE exposure over stagnant Western Europe.
“a lot of people are horrified by the prospect that Poland will overtake the UK at some point in the next 5 to 10 years on living standards” ▶ 30:17
UK base case & AI/Brexit optionality 4
Jessop's constructive base case is that the UK is well-placed to benefit from the AI revolution — better than the rest of Europe — and could outperform Europe by moving toward a US-style light-touch, business-friendly model rather than re-aligning with the EU.
thesis🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumAIUK economyUnited StatesEuropean Union
So what: The UK bull case is explicitly conditioned on AI adoption plus regulatory divergence from the EU; investors should treat 'UK as European AI/light-touch outperformer' as the upside scenario to monitor.
“We're well well placed to benefit from the AI revolution. Um certainly compared to the rest of Europe” ▶ 1:04:59
Jessop, a self-described Brexit optimist, flags the EU-UK 'reset' as his key downside risk — warning that being dragged back toward the overregulated EU model would undermine the UK's potential to be a light-touch, high-business-culture outperformer.
prediction🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumBrexitEU-UK resetregulation
So what: The EU-UK regulatory 'reset' is the specific policy swing factor between the UK's bull and bear paths; its direction is a watch-item for the UK growth/regulation re-rating thesis.
“I'm very concerned about the EU UK reset and the direction of travel there” ▶ 1:05:22
Jessop argues equity and financial markets take genuinely long-term views — evidenced by highly valued companies that are years from profitability — directly contradicting the common claim that 'the City' is short-termist, and contrasting this with politicians' 5-year (or 24-hour news) horizons.
contrarian🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: highfinancial marketsequity valuationunprofitable companies
So what: Defends long-duration/growth equity valuation logic against the short-termism critique; supports patience with pre-profit growth names where markets price long-run potential.
“there are lots of companies that haven't yet made profits and probably won't make profits for a very long time then nonetheless very highly valued by the markets because they can see the the potential” ▶ 32:34
Jessop compares the UK to Japan — a wealthy country with near-zero growth for decades that tolerates stagnation because it started rich — invoking the 'boiling frog' analogy and suggesting a genuine crisis might be necessary to force reform.
framework🗣 Julian Jessop (economist, guest)conviction: mediumJapanUKeconomic stagnation
So what: The Japan parallel implies the UK can drift in low-growth stagnation for a long time without forcing change — a base case of muddle-through rather than either crisis or reform, capping UK asset re-rating potential.
“It's had next to no growth for decades now. But because it starts from a reasonably comfortable position, people can sort of put up with things” ▶ 42:16

Perun 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

The New U.S. Navy Shipbuilding Plan - Nuclear Battleships, FF(X) & America's Naval Buildup58 min28 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
Analysis of the US Navy's FY2027 30-year shipbuilding plan: a record-breaking, sub-budget being consumed by the new $14-17B nuclear-powered Trump-class battleship (USS Defiant) at the expense of frigates and other firepower, while submarines remain the dominant spend and DDG(X) is quietly cancelled. Net effect over most of the plan is FEWER battle-force ships than the prior FY2025 plan despite far more money, with heavy execution risk around industrial base and Congressional pushback.
Shipbuilding budget topline & structure · 4Trump-class nuclear battleship (BBGN / USS Defiant) · 6Nuclear submarine force — the budget core · 5Frigates: Constellation cancelled, FF(X) downscaling · 4Carriers, destroyers & the DDG(X) cancellation · 3Unmanned systems underweight & supply-chain bottlenecks · 3Execution risk & strategic trade-off · 3
Shipbuilding budget topline & structure 4
The US Navy's FY2027 30-year shipbuilding plan raises battle-force shipbuilding investment from sub-$40B (FY2025) to north of $60B in FY2027, 2028, 2030 and 2031 (with FY2029 dipping to ~$50B), a record-breaking shipbuilding budget.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUS NavyUS shipbuildingdefense budget
So what: A durable double-digit-percent uplift in naval procurement spend is a multi-decade tailwind for US naval primes and the shipbuilding supply chain; watch for the annual Congressional shipbuilding markup that historically adds even more.
Despite spending far more money, the FY2027 plan would have the US Navy operating FEWER battle-force ships through most of the 2030s and all of the 2040s than the FY2025 plan, with both plans converging on ~400 ships in 2056 (up from 291 today).
contrarian🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUS Navybattle force
So what: More dollars buying fewer hulls means spend concentrates in a few high-unit-cost programs (battleship, subs); investors should track per-unit cost inflation rather than fleet headcount as the growth signal.
“how do you plan to spend a shit ton more money and end up with fewer boats?” ▶ 10:22
The shipbuilding plan is a statement of intent, not an approved budget; the US Congress controls funding and has consistently voted up MORE shipbuilding money than the White House/Navy requested, and has legislated a minimum carrier count.
framework🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUS CongressUS NavyDepartment of the Navy
So what: Congressional adds are a recurring upside catalyst for shipbuilders; the plan sets a floor, not a ceiling, for procurement-exposed names.
Over the 5-year horizon the plan spends more on nuclear submarines than on carriers, battleships, destroyers and frigates COMBINED, confirming the submarine force as the structural priority of the US Navy budget.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highnuclear submarinesUS NavyVirginia-classColumbia-class
So what: Submarine-exposed primes and their suppliers (General Dynamics Electric Boat, HII Newport News) capture the largest and most durable share of naval procurement dollars.
Trump-class nuclear battleship (BBGN / USS Defiant) 6
The Trump-class guided-missile battleship (USS Defiant) is now expected to cost ~$17B per hull (vs an earlier ~$9B estimate), with $43.5B requested through FY2031 for 3 ships (~$14.5B average) — as or more expensive than a nuclear aircraft carrier nearly three times its displacement.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUSS DefiantTrump-classBBGNbattleship
So what: A single ~$17B program line is the marginal budget swing factor for the whole plan; any builder winning the battleship contract gets an enormous backlog, but the program is the most likely cancellation/de-scope candidate.
“they don't actually expect Defiant to cost $9 billion, they expect it to cost closer to $17 billion” ▶ 19:28
The battleship has been switched from conventional CODAG to NUCLEAR propulsion using the existing A1B reactor from the Ford-class carrier (steam generator, pressuriser, loop and coolant-pump components), reusing an existing reactor design and supply chain.
data_point🗣 Chief of Naval Operations (admiral, quoted)conviction: highA1B reactorFord-classUSS DefiantBBGNnuclear propulsion
So what: Reusing the A1B reactor concentrates demand on the existing naval-reactor supply chain (BWX Technologies for naval nuclear components) and limits build to nuclear-capable yards (HII Newport News, GD Electric Boat).
“We are going to use the Ford reactor, the A1B reactor, and the components that support that from the vessel” ▶ 22:23
Going nuclear narrows the battleship build to the small set of US shipyards and workforces with nuclear capability, worsening already-acute schedule and capacity risk for the program.
causal🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumnuclear shipyardsHII Newport Newsshipbuilding industrial base
So what: Nuclear-capable yard capacity is the binding constraint; the same yards already strained on carriers and submarines now face battleship demand, a bottleneck (and pricing-power) signal for incumbents.
The plan procures the first battleship in FY2028 and builds toward a peak of 11 in service by 2055, putting the Navy on a roughly 1:1 battleship-to-carrier ratio in the 2050s; over 5 years battleships absorb more funding than destroyers and frigates combined and nearly twice the carrier allocation.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highbattleshipBBGNaircraft carriersdestroyersfrigates
So what: The battleship cannibalizes the rest of the surface-combatant budget; if cancelled, freed funds redirect to Burkes, frigates, drones and munitions — making it a binary swing factor across multiple supplier segments.
On a cost-for-cost and ton-for-ton basis the battleship is a poor way to add missile firepower: Defiant is outgunned roughly 2:1 to 3:1 in VLS cells by a trio of Arleigh Burkes or Chinese Type 055 cruisers at similar or lower displacement and price.
contrarian🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUSS DefiantArleigh BurkeType 055ZumwaltVLS
So what: The capability-per-dollar critique underpins the cancellation thesis; the strategic logic favors more Burkes/frigates/drones over the battleship, where the bulk of efficient firepower spend would otherwise flow.
Congress has reportedly moved to block the battleship program until all its weapon systems (railgun, hypersonics, directed-energy weapons) reach sufficient technical readiness — which, given those systems are largely experimental, could effectively kill it.
prediction🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumUS Congressbattleshiprailgundirected-energy weaponshypersonics
So what: A concrete near-term legislative risk to the largest line item; battleship-exposed contractors face binary program risk tied to immature-tech gating and the 2028 administration change.
Nuclear submarine force — the budget core 5
The plan orders 10 Virginia-class attack submarines over 5 years with per-unit cost falling from ~$7B (FY2027) to ~$5.6B (FY2031), ~$6.3B average; at 2 boats/year ($62.9B over 5 years) Virginia is the single most expensive line item in the entire plan.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highVirginia-classattack submarinesGeneral Dynamics Electric BoatHII Newport News
So what: Virginia is the largest and most stable revenue stream in naval procurement; the GD Electric Boat / HII Newport News teaming and their suppliers are the prime beneficiaries.
The Columbia-class ballistic-missile submarine remains the top procurement priority at one per year, ~$12.4B per unit and ~$62B over 5 years, replacing the Ohio SSBN fleet over the plan; cost is in line with the UK Dreadnought and France's next SSBN.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highColumbia-classOhio-classSSBNDreadnoughtGeneral Dynamics
So what: Columbia is a protected, must-fund nuclear-deterrence program — the most schedule-critical and politically insulated backlog for GD Electric Boat and its supply chain.
After retiring all SSGN Tomahawk-cruise-missile submarines (154 cells each) in FY2027-2029, the plan restarts SSGN procurement in FY2038 at one every 3 years — timed 2-3 years after the last Columbia, hinting the Columbia production line/workforce would be kept warm with a large-payload follow-on boat.
thesis🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumSSGNColumbia-classOhio SSGNTomahawklarge payload submarine
So what: Signals a continuous, decades-long large-hull submarine workload for the Columbia yard beyond 2035 — extending the duration of GD Electric Boat's submarine backlog.
The next-gen SSN(X) attack sub (entering service mid-2040s) is being designed from the ground up to host robotic and autonomous undersea systems, positioning each boat as a control node for cheaper unmanned underwater vehicles.
thesis🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumSSN(X)unmanned underwater vehiclesautonomous systemsNGAD
So what: Long-dated optionality for undersea-autonomy suppliers and the UUV ecosystem; analogous to NGAD's manned-node-plus-drones concept for the Air Force.
The binding risk on the entire submarine plan is industrial capacity, not design quality: 2 attack boats per year has been a stated goal for years and delays are the norm rather than the exception.
causal🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highsubmarine industrial baseVirginia-classGD Electric BoatHII
So what: Submarine-yard throughput and workforce/supplier scaling are the key execution variables; investments in submarine-industrial-base expansion (suppliers, casting, workforce) are a watch-list theme.
Frigates: Constellation cancelled, FF(X) downscaling 4
The Constellation-class (FFG-X) frigate program was cancelled after just two ships (CBO estimated ~$1.4B/hull), replaced by FF(X), a navalized Coast Guard Legend-class cutter that drops the 32-cell VLS, Aegis combat system, high-end sensors and sonar.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highConstellation-classFFG(X)FF(X)Legend-class cutterAegisFincantieri Marinette Marine
So what: Constellation's cancellation removes a major Fincantieri Marinette Marine backlog and shifts frigate work to whichever yard builds the cutter-derived FF(X); a clear loser/winner reshuffle in small-combatant builders.
FF(X) is described as cheap but actually costs ~$1.75B per hull ($7B+ for 4 over 5 years, peaking at $2.76B in FY2031) — more than the cancelled Constellation it replaces, while delivering less capability and arriving later.
contrarian🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highFF(X)Constellation-classfrigates
So what: The 'cost-effective' frigate is neither cheaper nor faster, undermining the savings rationale; expect program churn and possible re-scoping (VLS/ASW added back), which is execution and margin risk for the frigate builder.
“a small surface combatant that can't really do surface combat, a cheap ship that is actually expensive, and a fast to deliver option that actually arrives later than the slow to deliver one it replaced” ▶ 41:00
Swapping Constellation (32-cell VLS) for FF(X) (no VLS) strips ~2,600 VLS cells from the future fleet; total fleet missile cells barely stay net-positive only by counting the battleship's ~1,900 added cells and containerized/hypersonic add-ons.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highVLSFF(X)Constellation-classbattleshipConventional Prompt Strike
So what: Despite record spend, fleet vertical-launch capacity barely grows — a structural argument for sustained munitions/VLS demand and for missile-magazine alternatives (unmanned magazine ships).
Allied frigate designs (UK Type 26/Type 31, French FDI/FREMM, Japanese Mogami) deliver VLS and ASW at similar or lower cost than FF(X); adopting one (e.g., Type 26) would create industrial commonality with the UK, Canada and Australia.
thesis🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumType 26Type 31FDIFREMMMogamiBAE SystemsNaval Group
So what: An off-the-shelf allied design pivot would benefit BAE/Naval Group/MHI ecosystems and AUKUS-aligned supply chains; a latent optionality if FF(X) falters.
Carriers, destroyers & the DDG(X) cancellation 3
The Ford-class carrier is now treated as a 'battle-proven design' (10,500 fixed-wing launch/recoveries on its June-2025 deployment) and the plan keeps procuring them, while the carrier force stays roughly static in numbers over 30 years.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highFord-classaircraft carriersHII Newport News
So what: Steady, de-risked carrier procurement underpins HII Newport News's long-cycle backlog without large fleet-count growth.
The next-generation destroyer DDG(X) has been entirely deleted from the plan — absent from designs, budget and build list — with the battleship explicitly stated NOT to be a destroyer replacement, leaving the 1980s-era Arleigh Burke (procured 1-2/year through the 2050s) as the only destroyer.
thesis🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highDDG(X)Arleigh BurkedestroyersHII IngallsGeneral Dynamics Bath Iron Works
So what: DDG(X) cancellation extends the Arleigh Burke production line (good for HII Ingalls/GD Bath Iron Works) but leaves a long-term destroyer-modernization and hypersonic-cell gap that may require a future program restart.
“in this shipbuilding plan DDG(X) is nowhere to be seen” ▶ 47:43
Killing DDG(X) removes the planned hypersonic-capable destroyer fleet, likely leaving far fewer Conventional Prompt Strike hypersonic cells in the future force than previously planned despite hypersonics being a stated priority.
causal🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumDDG(X)Conventional Prompt StrikehypersonicsZumwalt
So what: Fewer surface hypersonic platforms concentrates that capability on Zumwalts and submarines; a watch item for hypersonic-weapon program demand allocation.
Unmanned systems underweight & supply-chain bottlenecks 3
Unmanned systems are funded but dwarfed by the battleship: $1.1B for 16 XLUUVs (Orca) over 5 years (less than one FF(X)) and $3.1B for 47 medium unmanned surface vessels, with no German-style armed 'magazine-extender' uncrewed missile vessel in the plan.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highXLUUVOrcaMUSVunmanned surface vesselsLarge Remote Missile Vessel
So what: This is not an unmanned-centric budget; the gap versus Ukraine/Iran-era lessons suggests upside for low-cost unmanned and missile-magazine concepts if priorities shift, and underweight current exposure for USV/UUV suppliers.
“This is not a revolution in naval shipbuilding so much as an incremental step in it.” ▶ 51:04
US military shipbuilding is the bedrock of the domestic yards because the US now builds only a fraction of a percent of global civilian ships (bulk carriers/LNG tankers ceded to Japan, South Korea and China), so Navy/Coast Guard plans are effectively the entire demand signal for large-ship yards.
framework🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highUS shipbuildingChinaSouth KoreaJapanUS NavyCoast Guard
So what: US yard revenue is almost entirely government-driven, making these primes pure-play defense-budget exposures with little commercial offset; allied (Korean/Japanese) yards are the natural partners for any capacity expansion.
The combat-logistics and support-vessel category (oilers, etc.) sees a front-loaded surge between FY2027 and 2037, one of the few fast-growing fleet segments early in the plan.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumcombat logisticsoilerssupport vesselsauxiliary ships
So what: Near-term growth in auxiliary/support shipbuilding offers earlier revenue than the slow-ramping combatant programs — a less-watched beneficiary segment.
Execution risk & strategic trade-off 3
The plan's central trade-off: cutting the battleship at constant funding could instead buy ~7 additional Arleigh Burkes plus an extra carrier with cash to spare, or refill magazines and fund a large ASW frigate fleet plus unmanned systems — the Navy cannot fund both the battleship and a broad force refresh.
framework🗣 Perun (host)conviction: highbattleshipArleigh Burkeaircraft carriersmunitionsF/A-XX
So what: Frames the battleship as a binary capital-allocation switch across the whole supplier base; cancellation would broadly benefit Burke builders, munitions makers, frigate and unmanned suppliers, and naval air (F/A-XX).
“the battleship is the trade, and a lot of other things get the off” ▶ 52:11
Perun's base case is that the battleship is most likely eventually cancelled or seriously revised, with risk that an administration change at end-2028 wastes billions and years of DDG(X) opportunity cost, leaving the Defiants a tiny Zumwalt/Constellation-like orphan class.
prediction🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumbattleshipUSS DefiantZumwaltConstellation-classDDG(X)
So what: Treat battleship-linked revenue as high-risk/optional and the DDG(X) gap as a likely future restart; position for redirection of funds rather than full battleship execution.
Funding remains unsettled for key US naval-air programs including the F/A-XX 6th-generation fighter and various Navy unmanned aircraft, which compete against the shipbuilding battleship spend.
data_point🗣 Perun (host)conviction: mediumF/A-XX6th generation fighternaval aviationunmanned aircraft
So what: Battleship cost growth pressures the broader Navy aviation budget; F/A-XX program timing/funding is a contested catalyst for naval-air primes.

Joe Lonsdale 1 episode(s) · 27 insights

SpaceX’s Secret, Nuclear's Rebirth, and the AI Power Crisis40 min27 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Scott Nolan (Founders Fund partner, ex-SpaceX, founder/CEO of General Matter) lays out the US nuclear-fuel supply-chain gap: America stopped enriching uranium at scale in 2013 and now imports HALEU from Russia, exactly the fuel that the coming wave of advanced/SMR reactors needs. He argues the AI/reindustrialization power crisis (China now at ~3x US grid capacity) makes domestic enrichment a national-security bottleneck, and walks through General Matter's $900M DOE milestone contract, EXIM-backed export financing to Japan/Korea, and a faster NRC licensing regime as the catalysts that move the constraint from regulation onto execution speed.
US enrichment gap & General Matter's wedge · 4AI power crisis & US vs China grid · 5Advanced reactors, SMRs & HALEU fuel economics · 5Policy tailwinds, financing & national security · 6SpaceX execution playbook applied to hard-tech · 4Energy abundance, space nuclear & macro optimism · 3
US enrichment gap & General Matter's wedge 4
Enrichment is the single missing link in the US nuclear fuel chain — mining, conversion, deconversion and fuel fabrication all have multiple domestic providers, but the US enriches no uranium at scale, forcing reactor builders to import fuel from Russia.
thesis🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highGeneral Matteruranium enrichmentRussianuclear fuel cycle
So what: Pure-play domestic enrichment is a structurally protected niche with near-zero US competition and a captive national-security buyer base — a rare monopoly-shaped opportunity in energy.
“The reason we're doing enrichment is because that's the one step the US doesn't do at any scale.” ▶ 17:07
The US held over 80% of global enrichment in the 1980s using WWII-era gaseous diffusion, then deliberately exited — blending down Russian warheads (Megatons to Megawatts) and outsourcing to Europe — shutting its last enrichment site (western Kentucky, on DOE land) in 2013.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highgaseous diffusionMegatons to MegawattsDOEKentuckyRussia
So what: Frames domestic enrichment as a reindustrialization/onshoring play with policy tailwinds — rebuilding a capability the US voluntarily abandoned, now seen as a strategic error.
≈ “We shut down the last enrichment site in 2013. And that site was actually in western Kentucky on DOE land that our company is now building on.” paraphrase
General Matter is deliberately staying a narrow single-step enrichment company (not a vertically integrated utility), applying Thiel's 'Zero to One' logic of dominating one underserved bottleneck rather than chasing a slice of the trillion-dollar energy market.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highGeneral MatterFounders FundPeter ThielZero to One
So what: Signals capital discipline and a defensible moat thesis; investors should expect focus on enrichment economics, not utility-scale capex sprawl, at least until that niche is won.
“Don't think of the energy market as a trillion dollar market and oh if only I can get 1% of that trillion dollar market.” ▶ 35:49
General Matter's facility is essentially a large warehouse/data-center-sized building that takes in one cylinder of uranium gas and outputs two cylinders (one enriched, one depleted) — a scalable manufacturing problem framed like Starlink user-terminal mass production.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumGeneral MatterStarlinkmanufacturing
So what: The bet is on manufacturing-scale execution (cost-per-unit learning curve), so the company should be judged like a factory-scaling story, not a one-off plant.
AI power crisis & US vs China grid 5
The US and China were neck-and-neck on grid capacity in 2010 (~4,000–5,000 TWh/yr each); since then China has tripled and now runs roughly 3x US grid capacity, adding the equivalent of a Texas-sized grid every year.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highChinaUS gridelectricity capacity
So what: Quantifies the structural power deficit driving the entire bull case for US generation buildout — a multi-year tailwind for nuclear, gas, and grid infrastructure plays.
“Since then, in the 15 years since 2010, they've tripled. I think this year they're actually at 3x the US grid capacity.” ▶ 21:55
The old consensus that GDP decouples from energy use as economies shift to services has flipped: data centers, EVs and reshored advanced manufacturing now make power demand re-correlate with growth, so a flat US grid is a binding constraint on reindustrialization.
contrarian🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highdata centersEVsadvanced manufacturingGDPenergy demand
So what: Validates the secular 'power is the new bottleneck' thesis underpinning data-center, generation and grid investment over the next decade.
“I think now that's completely flipped with data centers consuming so much power and needing them to scale the next few years.” ▶ 22:43
The binding constraint for US competitiveness is not just the quantity of power but its cost — cheap enough nuclear could reshore electricity-intensive industries like aluminum and steel where electricity is the dominant input cost.
thesis🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highnuclearaluminumsteelreindustrialization
So what: Points to downstream beneficiaries: if cheap nuclear materializes, energy-intensive domestic manufacturing (aluminum, steel) becomes investable on a reshoring thesis.
“It's not even the amount of power, it's the cost of power.” ▶ 23:18
Nuclear already supplies ~18.7% of the US grid (a share most people underestimate) and is the focus of hyperscalers planning large 2030s data centers, with near-term growth from uprating existing plants with different fuel and restarting shuttered plants.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highnuclearhyperscalersdata centersUS grid
So what: Plant uprates and restarts are nearer-term, lower-risk nuclear exposure than greenfield SMRs; hyperscaler demand is the demand anchor to track.
“You talk to any of the hyperscalers who want to build big data centers in the, you know, 2030s and I think for all of them it's about nuclear.” ▶ 24:32
Historic nuclear production cost was about 3 cents/kWh in today's dollars in the 1960s–70s, versus ~30 cents/kWh retail in California today — implying large headroom, and Nolan argues factory-built reactors could beat 3 cents.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumnuclearelectricity costCalifornia
So what: Sets the cost target investors should benchmark SMR/reactor economics against; sub-3-cent factory nuclear would be a step-change for power-cost-sensitive industries.
“Back in the what was it 60s or 70s I think in today's dollars uh nuclear was costing about 3 cents per kilowatt hour.” ▶ 23:51
Advanced reactors, SMRs & HALEU fuel economics 5
Advanced/small reactors converge on 19.75% enrichment (HALEU) — just below weapons grade — versus 3–5% for gigawatt-scale plants, which dramatically increases upstream uranium and enrichment throughput per reactor.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highHALEUSMRsuranium enrichmentU-235
So what: The SMR wave is structurally more enrichment-intensive per MW, so enrichment demand scales faster than reactor count — directly bullish for General Matter's volume.
“Halo stands for high assay lowenrich uranium. So that's the 10 to 20% uh enrichment level which everyone converges on 19.75.” ▶ 26:38
Smaller, more-enriched reactors invert nuclear's cost structure: fuel can flip from single-digit-percent of energy cost (low-enrich, gigawatt plants) to more than half the cost in small reactors, even as the reactor hardware gets cheaper via factory production.
causal🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highSMRsnuclear fuel costfactory production
So what: Makes the fuel supply chain (enrichment) the dominant value pool in the SMR era — a reason to prefer fuel-cycle exposure over reactor OEMs as reactors commoditize.
“Now where the fuel is often more than half the cost and the smaller the reactor the higher the cost proportion.” ▶ 16:21
Advanced-reactor safety is converging on TRISO-style coated fuel — poppy-seed-sized uranium kernels in a silicon-carbide/zirconium shell that withstands >1,000°C and self-moderates by absorbing neutrons as it heats, making meltdown effectively impossible.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highTRISO fuelsilicon carbidezirconiumadvanced reactors
So what: Passive safety de-risks public/regulatory acceptance, supporting the SMR deployment timeline that drives fuel demand; coated-fuel fabricators are an adjacent niche.
Nuclear is the safest base-load energy source by deaths per terawatt-hour (lower than any other base load and effectively tied with solar) and carbon-free — its only historical disadvantage is cost, which factory-scale build aims to solve.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highnuclearsolarbase load energy
So what: Reframes the nuclear risk narrative for capital allocators; the investable variable is cost reduction via scale, not safety.
“Fewer human deaths per terowatt hour than any other base load, and even any other energy source except solar.” ▶ 12:56
Nolan argues the US should scale all three reactor classes simultaneously — micros (e.g. stranded-demand sites like off-grid Alaskan villages), SMRs (data centers/industrial), and gigawatt-scale built identically to climb the cost-learning curve — expecting winners in each category.
thesis🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highmicro reactorsSMRsgigawatt reactorsRadiant
So what: Suggests diversified reactor exposure rather than betting on one form factor; standardized repeat-build gigawatt plants are a distinct cost-curve thesis from SMRs.
“I think there's going to be winners in every single category.” ▶ 26:16
Policy tailwinds, financing & national security 6
NRC licensing, historically the timeline bottleneck, has accelerated sharply — the NRC committed to a 12-month fast-track on a newly filed fuel-cycle license application, shifting the critical path from regulation back onto company execution speed.
causal🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highNRCnuclear licensingfuel cycle facilities
So what: Faster licensing materially de-risks the entire advanced-nuclear buildout timeline; execution capability now differentiates winners more than regulatory navigation.
“The NRC announced that a new fuel cycle license application went in and they're committing to put it on fast track and get it out in 12 months.” ▶ 30:12
Four nuclear executive orders (covering DOE, NRC, DoD and the supply chain), issued roughly a year before recording, created a major tailwind for the sector — a structural policy shift, not a one-off.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highnuclear executive ordersDOENRCDoD
So what: Confirms durable federal backing across agencies, lowering policy risk for nuclear and fuel-cycle investments through this administration.
“There were four executive orders covering DOE, NRC, DoD, and the supply chain that has really created a huge tailwind for the space.” ▶ 29:18
General Matter holds a $900M milestone-based, fixed-price DOE contract (drawn from a $2.7B congressional appropriation for HALEU enrichment), structured like NASA's COTS program with SpaceX — capital unlocked as build milestones are hit, not cost-plus.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highGeneral MatterDOEHALEUNASA COTSSpaceX
So what: Non-dilutive milestone capital de-risks the build and signals government conviction; the COTS analogy implies disciplined, performance-gated funding.
“Congress that appropriated $2.7 billion for Halo Enrichment. Um and we received one of those awards for 900 million.” ▶ 33:27
General Matter has an EXIM Bank LOI to wrap its enrichment contracts sold into Japan and Korea, pulling export financing forward to fund larger-scale build — with Japanese capital (hundreds of billions earmarked for US energy) as an additional pool.
data_point🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumEXIM BankJapanKoreaGeneral Matter
So what: Export demand plus EXIM/Japanese financing widens the addressable market and funding runway beyond domestic DOE support; track conversion of the LOI to firm utility contracts.
Supplying US enrichment to allied nuclear fleets is framed as a non-proliferation lever: if the US doesn't provide HALEU, allies building or restarting reactors will source it from Russia or China, ceding strategic influence.
thesis🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highRussiaChinanon-proliferationnational securityenrichment exports
So what: National-security framing supports continued bipartisan policy and financing backing for US enrichment exporters.
“If the US does not provide enrichment to these countries they will have to get it somewhere else... and that'll be Russia China.” ▶ 34:52
Demand timing is concrete: advanced reactors run initial test fuel from DOE through ~2028, begin single-digit commercial deployments 2028–2030, and ramp hard from 2030 — General Matter targets being online by end of decade to meet that scale-up.
prediction🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumadvanced reactorsDOEGeneral Matter
So what: Gives a datable demand inflection (~2030) for HALEU and SMR deployment to anchor investment timing in the fuel cycle.
“I think 2030 is where it really ramps... our commitment is to be online by end of decade.” ▶ 28:10
SpaceX execution playbook applied to hard-tech 4
The cost-plus government contracting model (Boeing) structurally rewards larger programs because pay equals cost plus a markup, removing any incentive against cost growth — versus SpaceX's clean-sheet, cost-adjusted-performance culture that treated time as the scarce resource.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highBoeingSpaceXcost-plus contractingElon Musk
So what: Explains why milestone/fixed-price contracts (now used by DOE) attract execution-focused entrants and out-compete legacy primes — a lens for evaluating hard-tech government-backed companies.
“The way to make the most money then is make the program bigger and bigger and bigger.” ▶ 4:07
SpaceX's core operating principle was an explicit cost-versus-time trade: spend extra money to pull in schedule only when it shortens the true critical path, because every day the company exists burns fixed cost ('the cost of time is greater than the cost of cost').
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highSpaceXElon Musk
So what: A reusable diligence heuristic for capital-intensive hard-tech: winners optimize critical-path schedule, not headline cost — watch how General Matter sequences its work streams.
“The cost of time is greater than the cost of cost.” ▶ 6:48
General Matter, like SpaceX and Tesla's Austin Gigafactory, keeps engineering-procurement-construction (EPC) fully in-house (~20-person team in Kentucky) rather than handing off to outside firms — treating construction speed as core IP, not a commodity.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highGeneral MatterSpaceXTesla GigafactorySaronicEPC
So what: In-house EPC is a recurring marker of fast-building hard-tech winners; outsourced construction is a red flag for timeline risk in capital projects.
Nolan characterizes General Matter as a Thiel-style 'complex coordination company' running eight parallel work streams (licensing, technology, construction, manufacturing) where any single failure delays the whole project — making coordination the core capability.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highGeneral MatterFounders FundPeter Thiel
So what: Frames execution risk concretely: the investment is a bet on coordination/management capability across parallel critical paths, not a single technical breakthrough.
“Complex coordination company where you're working on eight different work streams at the same time to go as fast as possible.” ▶ 30:40
Energy abundance, space nuclear & macro optimism 3
NASA (under Jared Isaacman) is targeting nuclear-driven ion space propulsion by 2028 and surface reactors on the Moon, which Nolan argues is the only sensible way to power the lunar dark side — opening a space-nuclear demand vector.
prediction🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumNASAJared Isaacmanspace nuclear propulsionMoon
So what: A speculative but real adjacent market for nuclear/space convergence; watch NASA nuclear-propulsion awards as an early signal.
“They're aiming for uh space uh ion nuclear driven uh space propulsion by 2028.” ▶ 36:40
Nolan's combined thesis: cheap abundant energy plus capable AI acting in the physical world drives broad cost deflation and a reindustrialized US economy, with the next 10 years seeing this 'take off' — an explicitly pro-growth, anti-doomer macro stance.
thesis🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: highenergy abundanceAIreindustrializationUS economy
So what: Captures the Founders Fund worldview tying together energy, AI and onshoring — useful for understanding the cohort of hard-tech bets this network is funding.
Nolan's earlier energy investing (Crusoe, Panthalassa for stranded energy supply; Radiant for stranded demand) led him to identify enrichment as the un-served gap — illustrating a stranded-supply/stranded-demand framework for sourcing hard-tech opportunities.
framework🗣 Scott Nolanconviction: mediumCrusoePanthalassaRadiantFounders Fundstranded energy
So what: A repeatable deal-sourcing lens (stranded supply vs stranded demand) and a map of Founders Fund's energy portfolio worth tracking.

Michael Howell / CrossBorder Capital 1 episode(s) · 26 insights

Institutional Crypto is Here: J.P. Morgan’s MONY Fund Breakdown36 min26 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dennis Crystalo, who leads wealth-management for JP Morgan's Kexis (formerly Onyx) digital-assets unit, details the bank's institutional tokenization stack: a >$3T-notional private Ethereum fork, the December 2025 'MONY' tokenized money-market fund on public Ethereum ($100M seed), JPM deposit tokens on Base, and a Citco-linked fund-flow app that tokenizes alts capital calls. Core thesis: tokenization's near-term prize is operational efficiency in alternatives distribution and idle-cash elimination, with deposit tokens (not stablecoins) winning institutional/wholesale payments and money-market funds the proven public-chain product.
JPM tokenization scale & strategic commitment · 4MONY tokenized money-market fund (public Ethereum) · 4Deposit tokens vs stablecoins · 4Tokenized alts & the Kexis Fund Flow / Citco app · 5Private vs public chains & multi-chain strategy · 3Future of wealth management & idle-cash elimination · 3Product-market-fit caveats & AI-crypto convergence · 3
JPM tokenization scale & strategic commitment 4
JP Morgan's Kexis (formerly Onyx) digital-assets unit has surpassed $3 trillion in cumulative tokenized notional value and processes roughly $5 billion of activity daily, evidencing institutional tokenization at production scale rather than pilot scale.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganKexisOnyx
So what: A bulge-bracket bank already moving multi-trillion tokenized notional sets the realistic baseline for institutional crypto adoption; watch Kexis volume disclosures as a leading indicator of TradFi-on-chain throughput.
“we just surpassed three trillion in total tokenized notional value” ▶ 1:57
JP Morgan frames tokenization and fintech as an existential competitive threat—Jamie Dimon's recent CEO/chairman letter cites the emerging threat from fintech and tokenization in its opening sentences—which is why the bank chose to build the capability in-house from 2014 rather than buy or partner.
causal🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganJamie Dimonfintech
So what: Top-down CEO framing of tokenization as a threat-to-defend implies sustained capital and reputational commitment from JPM, raising the bar for competitors that have waited on the sidelines.
“literally one of the first few sentences in there is about the emerging threat from fintex and tokenization” ▶ 2:20
Kexis runs on a private permissioned blockchain that is a fork of Ethereum stood up in 2014, and its solidity/Ethereum-based tooling materially eased the recent migration of select assets onto public chains.
framework🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highKexisEthereumSolidity
So what: JPM's private chain being Ethereum-compatible lowers its switching cost to public Ethereum, favoring Ethereum as the default institutional settlement layer over rival L1s.
“It's a fork of Ethereum that we stood up back in in 2014” ▶ 9:46
JP Morgan co-authored a Bain paper sizing the tokenization opportunity at $400 trillion and finds individuals hold about $150 trillion of global wealth with only ~5% allocated to alternatives, framing under-allocated alts as the primary growth vector for tokenization.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganBainalternatives
So what: The thesis pinpoints retail/mass-affluent alts allocation as the addressable market; firms building alts-distribution rails (admins, transfer agents, wealth platforms) are the second-order beneficiaries.
“individuals hold around $150 trillion in global wealth, but only allocate about 5% to alternatives” ▶ 24:08
MONY tokenized money-market fund (public Ethereum) 4
JP Morgan launched MONY, a tokenized money-market fund on public Ethereum, in December 2025 seeded with ~$100 million, structured with JP Morgan Asset Management and positioned as the firm's proven public-chain product targeting on-chain capital pools (crypto-natives, stablecoin holders/issuers).
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganMONYJP Morgan Asset ManagementEthereummoney market fund
So what: A GSIB launching a public-Ethereum MMF validates tokenized cash as the beachhead asset class; track MONY AUM growth as a gauge of institutional demand for on-chain yield instruments.
“in December we deployed a tokenized money market fund uh onto Ethereum. It launched with about 100 million of seed capital” ▶ 18:50
JPM's roadmap to make tokenized money-market funds superior to the conventional version rests on three features: 24/7 liquidity (redeem any time of day), minute-by-minute interest accruals, and tokenized-collateral use that eliminates double-pledging by enabling precise 24/7/365 movement.
framework🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highMONYmoney market fundtokenized collateral
So what: These features target corporate treasurers optimizing large cash balances and collateral desks; the collateral-mobility angle is the under-appreciated driver regulators are pushing.
“247 liquidity... hyper precise or minutely interest acrruals... this collateral use case” ▶ 20:06
US regulators are actively focused on enabling tokenized collateral, and a 24/7/365-movable tokenized money-market fund is well-suited to be pledged precisely and to resolve double-pledging of assets in certain transactions.
causal🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumtokenized collateralregulatorsmoney market fund
So what: Regulatory tailwind for tokenized collateral is a catalyst to watch; approval could unlock MMF tokens as mainstream collateral, expanding demand beyond yield-seeking.
“different regulators are very focused on enabling tokenized collateral and it makes a lot of sense” ▶ 20:30
JP Morgan plans to launch at least a few more tokenized money-market funds in 2026 and to expand the product beyond Ethereum to additional public chains.
prediction🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganMONYmoney market fundEthereum
So what: A pipeline of further MMF launches and multi-chain expansion signals JPM is scaling, not piloting; competing chains courting these deployments stand to gain institutional liquidity.
“we'll have uh you know at least uh at least a few more this year that we'll go live with and we're also looking to expand to other chains as well” ▶ 20:52
Deposit tokens vs stablecoins 4
JP Morgan launched its JPM deposit-token coin on Base in November and announced a Canton network integration, extending blockchain deposit accounts (a bank account on-chain) from its private network onto public networks.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP MorganJPM coindeposit tokensBaseCanton
So what: Deposit tokens on Base/Canton give JPM a bank-grade on-chain cash leg distinct from stablecoins; Base and Canton are infrastructure beneficiaries of GSIB deposit-token flow.
“we took this concept and extended it by you know putting these tokens uh onto onto base” ▶ 21:47
Crystalo argues deposit tokens and stablecoins are more complementary than competitive: stablecoin issuers will hold treasuries but also need a very liquid, eventually yield-bearing cash-like product in their capital stack, a role JPM's deposit token can fill with direct JPM bank-account on/off-ramps.
thesis🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumdeposit tokensstablecoinsJP Morgantreasuries
So what: Positions JPM deposit tokens as plumbing for the stablecoin ecosystem rather than a rival; implies stablecoin issuers become JPM customers, a quiet revenue channel.
“the stable coin issuers themselves, they're going to hold treasuries, but they probably also need a very liquid cash-like product in their capital stack as well. And this could play that role” ▶ 22:10
Crystalo predicts wholesale and institutional payments will move through deposit tokens rather than stablecoins because deposit tokens are the more scalable model, and explicitly picks deposit tokens to dominate institutional payments by 2030.
prediction🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highdeposit tokensstablecoinsinstitutional payments
So what: A bet that bank-issued deposit tokens, not Circle/Tether-style stablecoins, capture wholesale settlement; bearish on stablecoins encroaching upmarket into institutional rails.
“ultimately, you know, wholesale money will will likely move through deposit tokens more likely than stable coins” ▶ 22:51
Stablecoins and deposit tokens are fundamentally a distribution game where liquidity dominates, so incumbents strong at distribution (exchanges, DeFi) will keep winning and the market will trend toward monopoly/duopoly 'category killers' per use case rather than every bank issuing its own stablecoin.
framework🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumstablecoinsdeposit tokensDeFiexchanges
So what: Argues against a fragmented many-stablecoin world; favors concentrated winners like incumbent issuers, informing which stablecoin franchises to back.
“you'll probably start to see similar monopoly or duop like outcomes like you've seen already in the stable coin space” ▶ 23:50
Tokenized alts & the Kexis Fund Flow / Citco app 5
JP Morgan launched Kexis Fund Flow with Citco to attack alternatives operational inefficiency, tokenizing cash and fund interests so capital calls settle near-instantly: tokenized cash moves from the investor's brokerage account to the fund manager while the fund token moves the other way, automating wires and giving near-perfect transparency on who has paid.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highKexis Fund FlowCitcoJP Morganprivate equityprivate credit
So what: Targets the missing-DTCC pain in alts; fund administrators/transfer agents and distributors are the workflow disrupted, while Citco-type partners that integrate early gain an edge.
“there's no DTCC in the middle. There's no standards around how data is shared, how capital calls are processed” ▶ 12:56
In JPM's first tokenized private-equity capital calls, money moved from client account to fund manager 38x faster (a couple of minutes versus several hours) and the manual labor of file processing, mapping and reconciliation fell about 93%.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highKexis Fund Flowprivate equitycapital calls
So what: Concrete efficiency proof points (38x faster, ~93% labor cut) are the value prop that converts distributors; back-office reconciliation headcount at admins/wealth managers is the cost center at risk.
“money moved from client account to the fund manager 38 times faster... we were able to reduce that by about 93%” ▶ 26:39
Better blockchain-based data management lets JPM argue fund managers can drop the feeder-fund structure they used purely for convenience, saving end investors roughly 15–20 basis points in structuring costs.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumKexis Fund Flowfeeder fundsfund managers
So what: Removing feeder funds is a tangible cost saving (15-20bps) that incentivizes distributor adoption; feeder-fund service providers face disintermediation.
“that saves end investors you know 15 20 basis points in structured costs” ▶ 16:18
Crystalo positions the killer cross-asset use case as using a tokenized money-market fund as the funding source for a private-equity capital call on the same chain, eliminating the cash drag endemic to portfolios holding idle cash for unfunded commitments.
thesis🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highmoney market fundprivate equitycapital callscash drag
So what: Linking MMF tokens to PE funding turns idle cash into yield until the call hits—a monetary, not just operational, benefit that strengthens the MMF adoption case.
“taking a money market fund that's tokenized and using that as a funding source for a private equity capital call” ▶ 16:38
Crystalo treats institutional tokenization as a network-effect business that requires demonstrating benefits across fund managers, administrators (Citco, JP Morgan Security Services) and distributors before it scales—technology, not regulation or culture, is explicitly not the bottleneck.
framework🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highKexis Fund FlowCitcoJP Morgan Security Servicestransfer agents
So what: Reframes the adoption gate as network-building and stakeholder buy-in, not tech or rules; the binding constraint is the number of internal/external people to convince.
“The technology I don't think is the bottleneck. I think, you know, we need to scale it up, expand it out to other types of alts as well” ▶ 0:33
Private vs public chains & multi-chain strategy 3
JPM is chain-agnostic but use-case-driven: distribution-focused tokenization goes on public chains to reach crypto-native/stablecoin capital pools, while efficiency-focused alts use cases stay on the private permissioned network to avoid forcing clients to manage crypto wallets, extra sanction screening and transaction monitoring.
framework🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highKexispublic blockchainprivate blockchainwallets
So what: The private/public split clarifies where each asset class lands; wallet UX friction is the gating factor pushing institutional alts onto private rails for now.
“we separate the world into kind of the money crypto and the tech crypto piece” ▶ 8:36
Crystalo says there are too many chains and expects consolidation, but on public chains Ethereum is JPM's default starting point because it carries 60% of all stablecoins issued, deep liquidity pools, a large user base and a Lindy/decentralization advantage.
thesis🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highEthereumstablecoinspublic blockchain
So what: Ethereum's stablecoin liquidity moat makes it the institutional default; bearish on the long tail of L1s surviving consolidation, bullish on Ethereum as the deployment venue.
“You have on Ethereum 60% of all stable coins issued. You have a ton of users. You have uh very deep liquidity pools” ▶ 28:09
Crystalo is skeptical of cross-chain interoperability hype for real-world assets, saying he has not seen a phenomenal use case requiring atomic cross-chain movement and that moving assets chain A to chain B can be done several ways, leaving JPM in wait-and-see mode.
contrarian🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediuminteroperabilitycross-chainreal world assets
So what: Tempers the cross-chain/bridge narrative for RWAs; interoperability-bridge plays may be over-valued relative to actual institutional demand.
“I haven't seen and haven't heard a phenomenal use case around the need to go crosschain” ▶ 29:05
Future of wealth management & idle-cash elimination 3
Crystalo believes wealth will eventually be managed almost entirely on-chain, with the biggest missing piece being integrated (not self-custody) wallet solutions embedded in brokerage/custody platforms—he doubts investors will want to self-custody assets like their JP Morgan stock.
prediction🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highwealth managementwalletscustody
So what: Points to integrated institutional wallet/custody infrastructure as the next build-out; custody and brokerage platforms that embed wallets capture the on-chain wealth migration.
“the biggest thing that doesn't exist today is is is wallets” ▶ 29:34
Crystalo predicts instant settlement plus a broad set of on-chain asset classes will let managers deliver personalized portfolios at scale and eliminate idle cash—which he calls a 'secret tax' on managed portfolios held for unsettled trades—within roughly five years.
prediction🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highinstant settlementwealth managementcash drag
So what: Frames idle cash as a recoverable drag; instant-settlement infrastructure that frees cash is the value to capture and a metric to watch in tokenized portfolios.
“this idea of idle cash is going away” ▶ 31:02
Crystalo says tokenized PE access for the mass affluent arrives by 2027 'for sure', a more aggressive timeline than the 2032 alternative posed.
prediction🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highprivate equitymass affluenttokenization
So what: A near-term (2027) timeline for mass-affluent tokenized PE sets an investable horizon for alts-distribution infrastructure providers.
≈ “2027 for sure” paraphrase ▶ 35:06
Product-market-fit caveats & AI-crypto convergence 3
Crystalo cautions that tokenized assets on public chains beyond money-market funds still lack product-market fit: tokenized equities are an interesting global-access play, but tokenized alts (private equity/credit) on public chains had structural flaws in early iterations and have not yet attracted a meaningful bid.
contrarian🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumtokenized equitiesprivate equityprivate creditpublic blockchain
So what: A sober read from an insider: be cautious on public-chain tokenized-alts projects claiming traction; MMFs and equity-access are the validated categories, alts on public chains are not yet.
“for other types of assets like... alts that have been tok like private equity private credit funds that have been tokenized on public blockchains I don't think we're we're quite there yet” ▶ 32:54
Crystalo says early crypto-x-AI use cases were largely overhyped and are only now becoming more refined, while he expects eventual meaningful convergence between AI and crypto beyond simply using LLMs to write code.
contrarian🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: mediumAIcryptoLLMs
So what: Damps near-term AI-crypto narratives while keeping a long-term convergence bet; filters hype from substance for crossover-thesis investors.
“a lot of the early uh discussion on you know crypto x AI was probably overhyped” ▶ 34:13
JPM's intraday-repo 'digital financing' application has been at production scale for years, enabling wholesale intraday lending where borrowers pay only an hour's interest with no overnight capital charge because the repo is an instantly-unwindable intraday loan.
data_point🗣 Dennis Crystalo (guest, JP Morgan Kexis)conviction: highJP Morganintraday repodigital financingrepo
So what: Hyper-precise intraday repo shows tokenized liquidity already changing money-market mechanics; capital-charge savings are a concrete edge institutional borrowers can monetize today.
“clients they borrow for an hour they only pay an hour's worth of interest and there's no overnight capital charge because it's an intraday loan” ▶ 5:33

Asianometry · Jon Y 1 episode(s) · 26 insights

Taiwan's DRAM Failure38 min26 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
A history of Taiwan's two-decade, multi-billion-dollar failure to break into DRAM — Vanguard, TI-Acer, Nanya, Powerchip, ProMOS, Winbond, Rexchip and the failed Taiwan Memory Company — explaining why a foundry-strong island never became a memory power, and why DRAM's commodity bullwhip cycles, licensed (not owned) IP, and short-term bank-debt financing repeatedly washed out sub-scale players while Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron consolidated the industry to a profitable oligopoly.
Why DRAM is structurally different from logic/foundry · 3The bullwhip / chicken-game cycle as the core investor lesson · 4Why Taiwanese DRAM players structurally lost · 5TSMC, Vanguard and the foundry pivot as the winning move · 5Consolidation to a three-player oligopoly · 4The failed Taiwan Memory Company bailout · 2Nanya as the lone survivor and AI-cycle read-through · 3
Why DRAM is structurally different from logic/foundry 3
DRAM is a volatile commodity with economics nothing like logic chips: in its early decades the cycle ran roughly four good years to one bad year, and success required flawless R&D scaled every two-to-three nodes or you fall out of the race entirely.
framework🗣 Jon Yconviction: highDRAMmemorysemiconductors
So what: Memory is a capital-intensive commodity oligopoly; investors should treat DRAM names (Micron, Samsung, SK Hynix, Nanya) as deep cyclicals tied to the node-cadence race, not as steady compounders like a foundry.
≈ “DRAM is a famously ferocious industry, with economic dynamics nothing like logic.” paraphrase
In DRAM the majority of a chip's nominal accounting cost is depreciation (sunk cost) while actual cash cost is low, so once cash costs are covered producers have little incentive to cut output even as added supply crashes prices further.
framework🗣 Jon Yconviction: highDRAMmemory-makers
So what: Explains why DRAM downturns overshoot to the downside — the cost structure removes the natural supply brake, so investors should expect price crashes well below 'rational' levels before capacity discipline returns.
≈ “If those are covered, memory-makers have little incentive to cut back on production even if producing will cause prices to crash even more.” paraphrase
Unlike a pure-play foundry (TSMC) whose customers bring their own designs, a DRAM maker must own and design its own memory IP — making licensed-IP DRAM businesses structurally weaker than they appear.
framework🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTSMCDRAMmemory design IP
So what: IP ownership, not just fab capacity, is the moat in memory; a 'manufacturing-only' DRAM strategy is a red flag for durability and margins.
≈ “But memory-makers must design their memory, and it is a critical part of their success.” paraphrase
The bullwhip / chicken-game cycle as the core investor lesson 4
DRAM supercycles are repeatedly triggered by Windows OS upgrades that raised RAM requirements — Windows 3.0 (1990), Windows 95 (4-8MB), and Windows Vista (2006) each fooled makers into over-building fabs in the belief 'this time is different'.
causal🗣 Jon Yconviction: highMicrosoftWindows 95Windows VistaDRAMPC
So what: Demand-side catalysts (today: AI/datacenter, HBM) recur and reliably induce over-investment; the playbook is to fade peak-cycle capex euphoria, because the supply response lags and overshoots.
≈ “Such times often snooker memory-makers into thinking that things are different this time so they go build a bunch of expensive factories.” paraphrase
The mid-1990s DRAM build-out saw worldwide fab count leap from 73 in 1995 to 170 in 1996, with nine new DRAM fabs in Taiwan alone, and five more entering in 1997 — a textbook bullwhip that cratered the market.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTaiwanDRAM fabsPowerchipNanya
So what: Capacity decisions made years earlier keep arriving after prices have already turned; watch fab-count and capex commitments as a leading bear signal for memory pricing.
≈ “Worldwide, the number of fabs leapt from 73 in 1995 to 170 in 1996.” paraphrase
From 1995's $40 billion peak the world DRAM market collapsed to under $15 billion by 1998 — a >60% drawdown — as 4-megabit prices fell from $12 to $7.50 to $3.40 to $2.30, below Vanguard's cash cost.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: highDRAMVanguard4-megabit DRAM
So what: Quantifies the amplitude of memory cycles — peak-to-trough market value can fall 60%+ in three years, sizing the downside risk for any DRAM-exposed position.
≈ “By 1998, the value of the whole market had cratered to under $15 billion, down over 60% from the sweet summer days of 1995.” paraphrase
Semiconductor demand signals take time to travel up and down the supply chain (the 'Beer Game' / bullwhip effect), so end-user demand changes are amplified into violent over- and under-supply at producers like Samsung and TSMC.
framework🗣 Jon Yconviction: highSamsungTSMCsupply chainbullwhip effect
So what: A reusable mental model for timing chip cycles: the further upstream you sit, the larger the demand swings you experience — relevant to the current AI-driven memory shortage.
≈ “the time it takes for these signals to travel up or down the supply chain can cause a painful “bullwhip” effect.” paraphrase
Why Taiwanese DRAM players structurally lost 5
Taiwanese DRAM firms almost all licensed foreign IP (Oki, Mitsubishi, Toshiba, Siemens, Infineon, Elpida, Micron) rather than owning it — paying royalties that thinned margins and leaving them stranded when a partner failed to advance, as when Oki couldn't move Nanya to the next generation.
causal🗣 Jon Yconviction: highNanyaOki ElectricMitsubishiToshibaSiemensInfineonElpidaMicron
So what: Royalty-dependence is a recurring failure mode in commodity semis; favor memory players that own leading-edge process IP over licensees.
≈ “Licensing someone else's DRAM IP means paying them a percentage, cutting into precious margins.” paraphrase
Taiwanese DRAM makers funded their fab builds with short-term bank debt, unlike Samsung which could lean on end-product profits and chaebol cash inflows — making Taiwan the most fragile cohort in every downturn.
causal🗣 Jon Yconviction: highSamsungTaiwan DRAM firmschaebol
So what: Balance-sheet structure determines who survives a memory crash; integrated/diversified producers with internal cash outlast debt-funded pure-plays — a durable screen for cyclical survivors.
≈ “the Taiwanese funded their builds with short-term bank debt. A disastrous move.” paraphrase
Despite holding only 13-15% of world DRAM share, Taiwanese firms absorbed 42% of the industry's ~$10 billion in losses between Q1 2007 and Q3 2008 — grossly outsized losses relative to scale.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTaiwan DRAM firmsDRAM industry
So what: Sub-scale, high-cost producers bear a disproportionate share of cycle losses; market share alone understates a weak player's downside concentration.
≈ “Taiwanese DRAM firms took 42% of those losses despite having between 13-15% of world market share.” paraphrase
Morris Chang opposed both ITRI's and TI-Acer's DRAM ventures on principle because neither owned core memory technology — ITRI started from scratch and TI-Acer merely licensed TI's tech — and he privately judged the 'tragic outcome was almost inevitable'.
contrarian🗣 Morris Chang (via Jon Y)conviction: highMorris ChangTSMCTI-AcerITRITexas Instruments
So what: The most successful operator in the industry flagged the no-owned-IP model as doomed from the start — a high-credibility warning sign worth generalizing to today's aspirants (e.g. CXMT).
≈ “the "tragic outcome was almost inevitable".” paraphrase
Government designation of DRAM as a 'nationally important' field under the Two Trillion policy created an implicit bailout guarantee and moral hazard, encouraging firms to over-build on bank debt expecting rescue if they failed.
causal🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTaiwan governmentTwo Trillion and Twin Star policyDRAM
So what: Industrial-policy 'strategic' labels can distort capital allocation and worsen over-investment; an implicit-guarantee dynamic is a tell for eventual taxpayer-funded cleanups and shareholder wipeouts.
≈ “created an implicit government guarantee. Companies moved ahead with their plans thinking that the government would bail them out if they failed. A moral hazard.” paraphrase
TSMC, Vanguard and the foundry pivot as the winning move 5
TSMC's first CEO Jim Dykes refused PC-maker pressure to make DRAMs during the 1988 shortage and kept TSMC purely in logic — a discipline that preserved the pure-play foundry model that became the industry's most valuable franchise.
thesis🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTSMCJim DykesDRAMASICs
So what: Strategic focus (logic only) beat diversification into commodity memory; the discipline to say no to a hot adjacent market is itself a moat indicator.
≈ “Dykes refused, fully believing in the approach of staying only in logic chips.” paraphrase
TSMC absorbed the failed TI-Acer DRAM venture in 1999, converting its fab into Fab 7, and combined with the Worldwide Semiconductor acquisition this boosted TSMC's wafer capacity ahead of rival UMC — a major turning point in the foundry race.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: highTSMCTI-AcerFab 7Worldwide SemiconductorUMC
So what: Failed memory assets became cheap capacity that helped TSMC pull decisively ahead of UMC — illustrates how one player's distress is another's strategic accretion.
≈ “This purchase along that of another foundry challenger called Worldwide Semiconductor helped boost TSMC's wafer capacity ahead of rival UMC.” paraphrase
Vanguard, founded by Morris Chang via auction in 1994 to commercialize ITRI's DRAM project, lost $155 million in 1998 (a then-record corporate loss in Taiwan) before pivoting entirely to logic foundry by 2004 on Rick Tsai's advice that 'memory had no future'.
anecdote🗣 Jon Yconviction: highVanguard International SemiconductorMorris ChangRick TsaiITRITSMC
So what: The pivot from commodity DRAM to specialty/analog foundry was the survival path for Taiwan's memory exiters — a template repeated by ProMOS and Powerchip/PSMC.
≈ “Rick Tsai eventually privately told Chang that memory had no future and suggested that Vanguard pivot to logic foundry.” paraphrase
Vanguard recently licensed TSMC's power-GaN technology as TSMC frees up capacity for AI, and the two remain separate-but-allied with TSMC referring business and transferring process nodes to Vanguard.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: mediumVanguardTSMCGaNAI
So what: Vanguard is positioned as a TSMC-aligned specialty/power-semiconductor play benefiting from TSMC's AI-driven capacity reallocation — a second-order AI beneficiary.
≈ “Vanguard even recently licensed TSMC's power GaN technologies as the giant moves to free up more capacity for AI.” paraphrase
ProMOS and Powerchip survived the DRAM collapse by abandoning memory: ProMOS became a fabless designer after selling its fab, and Powerchip spun its fabs into logic foundry PSMC specializing in display driver ICs.
anecdote🗣 Jon Yconviction: highProMOSPowerchipPSMCdisplay driver IC
So what: Specialty logic (display drivers, power) was the escape hatch from commodity memory — relevant when assessing how distressed memory assets can be repurposed into viable foundry niches.
≈ “Powerchip spun off its fabs to create PSMC, a logic foundry. Their specialty is the display driver IC ... That basically saved their butts.” paraphrase
Consolidation to a three-player oligopoly 4
The DRAM industry consolidated from 20-30 companies historically to just three-to-five today, with 'only three really mattering' — the structural endpoint of decades of cyclical washouts.
thesis🗣 Jon Yconviction: highDRAMSamsungSK HynixMicron
So what: DRAM is now a rational oligopoly (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron); the surviving three should show better pricing discipline and higher through-cycle returns than the boom-bust era implies.
≈ “Once upon a time, the DRAM industry involved 20-30 companies. Today that has consolidated to three to five, with only three of them really mattering.” paraphrase
Micron consolidated the wreckage of Taiwan's DRAM industry — buying TI's DRAM business (1998), bankrupt Elpida for $2.5 billion (2012, which carried Elpida's Rexchip JV stake), the rest of Rexchip from Powerchip, and Inotera in 2016 — so the majority of Micron's DRAM is now made in Taiwan with 15,000 employees there.
data_point🗣 Jon Yconviction: highMicronElpidaRexchipInoteraPowerchipTexas InstrumentsTaiwan
So what: Micron's Taiwan footprint is the consolidated remains of an entire failed national industry; geopolitical/Taiwan-supply-chain risk to Micron is concretely concentrated, and Micron was the cycle's ultimate winner-by-acquisition.
“the majority of its DRAM is made in Taiwan.” ▶ 36:28
Korea's DRAM consolidation came via the Asian Financial Crisis forcing the merger of Hyundai and Goldstar/LG semiconductor divisions into Hynix (today SK Hynix), while Samsung and Hynix refused to cede an inch of share — out-burning the debt-laden Taiwanese.
causal🗣 Jon Yconviction: highHynixSK HynixHyundaiGoldstarSamsung
So what: The Koreans' willingness and ability to absorb losses to hold share was the decisive competitive weapon — a 'chicken game' that scale and deep pockets win.
≈ “with Samsung and Hynix refusing to give up even an inch of market share, there was only so much money the Taiwanese can afford to burn.” paraphrase
Europe's last serious DRAM maker Qimonda (spun out of Infineon to get DRAM volatility off the books) was cut off from sibling support and filed for bankruptcy in 2009 after a German government rescue fell through, with assets sold piecemeal.
anecdote🗣 Jon Yconviction: highQimondaInfineonSiemensGerman government
So what: Spinning a volatile cyclical out of a diversified parent removed its shock absorber and killed it — a cautionary tale on financial-engineering separations of cyclical units.
≈ “this cut off Qimonda from any future support from its siblings - making it vulnerable to a downturn.” paraphrase
The failed Taiwan Memory Company bailout 2
